### Post by slackthumbz on 2010-10-07
I finally got round to upgrading my netbook to 10.10 and I have to say I'm fairly unimpressed by the Unity desktop. 

First up, it feels kinda clunky, you have to wait for menus to load and I can't honestly see any advantage to the way it does things. It turns what could be a very small laptop into an appliancey consumer device and to me that just ruins a netbook.

Second, screen real estate, what a waste. Unity takes up a lot of space with that silly sidepane on the left and surely on a netbook you want to maximise your use of all available space? You could get rid of everything in the unity desktop and just provide a basic dock such as AWN or docky to act as the gnome-panel usually would or hell, just use a single gnome-panel at the top of the screen.

I know it's only two issues but they're annoying enough to make me use the normal gnome-session (oh yeah, in the netbook edition I had to manually install gnome-compiz and the various compiz fusion plugins just to get compositing working with desktop effects. WTF). 

Finally, and as far as I know this occurs in the normal desktop edition as well, you can't disable bluetooth via the panel without killing your wireless simultaneously. I've tested this on my netbook and my main machine (AMD64 box) and it occurs on both of them. Seriously annoying.

OK rant over :)

---

### Post by Chris1274 on 2010-10-07
> **slackthumbz said:**
> Second, screen real estate, what a waste. Unity takes up a lot of space with that silly sidepane on the left and surely on a netbook you want to maximise your use of all available space? 

Most screens these days are 16:9, so they're a lot wider than they are tall. The purpose of the side panel was to utilize the extra horizontal space in order to maximize vertical space.

That said, I don't really like the netbook edition either, whether the old one or the new one. I use the desktop edition on my netbook.

### Post by Mr. Picklesworth on 2010-10-07
> **slackthumbz said:**
> (oh yeah, in the netbook edition I had to manually install gnome-compiz and the various compiz fusion plugins just to get compositing working with desktop effects. WTF). 

That wouldn't have made a difference. You must have done something else, because Unity specifically uses the Mutter window manager, which always does compositing and has nothing to do with Compiz effects.



> **slackthumbz said:**
> Finally, and as far as I know this occurs in the normal desktop edition as well, you can't disable bluetooth via the panel without killing your wireless simultaneously. I've tested this on my netbook and my main machine (AMD64 box) and it occurs on both of them. Seriously annoying.

I have never seen that, either, and I suffer with nasty Broadcom wifi on my netbook. Sounds like you have a compatibility issue there, or just some funny hardware.

### Post by nrune on 2010-10-10
Uninstall unity and unstall this ppa  

sudo add-apt-repository ppa:netbook-remix-team/ppa 

and finaly 

sudo apt-get install netbook-launcher

Old launcher is back.  Good bye unity write when you get your act together.

### Post by Ozzy565 on 2010-10-11
Get my act together? I think my opinions about Unity are quite rational, and they seem to be shared by many others. If I thought it even had a future, when the bugs are removed, I would have said so. Unfortunately I think Unity is going down the wrong path in every way. 

The netbook interface was good because it had large icons and was simple. Perfect for a small screen. Unity is so far from it, with its clutter of annoying tiny icons and "smart" functions.

Sometimes the simple things are the best.

If Unity was anything good from the start, it would have been simpler than the old interface. It is not, it is more complicated, and unintuituve on top of it. They could have added options and intelligent functionality to the already existing netbook interface, in an intuitive manner, instead. Mac OS X has good intelligent search, but does not fill up the whole screen with it or push it on you when you do not want it.

Netbooks have limited screen size. Unity should have capitalized on this, and simplicity, instead of filling up the screen with "smart" options and increasing the amount of steps needed to do something.

I think Unity will be discontinued quite soon. They are going to receive a torrent of complaints. It is to Ubuntu what Vista was to Windows.

Thank you for the instructions. The repository does not seem to work, however:
"Failed to fetch [http://ppa.launchpad.net/netbook-remix-team/ppa/ubuntu/dists/maverick/main/binary-amd64/Packages.gz](http://ppa.launchpad.net/netbook-remix-team/ppa/ubuntu/dists/maverick/main/binary-amd64/Packages.gz)  404  Not Found"

### Post by Mikecore on 2010-10-12
solution - remove the Unity side panel its worthless. Just use the ubuntu menu button, It has a nice simple menu layout when you click on it. Then adjust Firefox. First remove the tab, bookmark, status tool bars this increases page view. Next move the navigation icons and url location bar up to the menu bar which also increases web page view area. And finally with the fact that Unity merges the window buttons ( close, min, max ) into the top panel bar you would gain even more view area. This is just my suggestion. I already do everything I listed but without Unity. To bad we couldn't adjust the look of Unity by letting the side panel be optional. Crap then I would have to be on the other side and say Unity is sweet. edit ( just realized with the auto hide function on the standard gnome bar I get the same effect as the window buttons being in the Unity bar. ) So I guess I'm back to "No need for Unity"

### Post by kerry_s on 2010-10-15
i just do my own using the standard gnome desktop.

dockbarx + compiz for the side panel, since it's a applet on the gnome-panel, it can be set to autohide

cardapio for menu

maximus to maximize & undecorate apps

xdotool used to run keyboard shortcuts from launchers

### Post by omskates on 2010-10-15
Found this nice userChrome.css script for more minimal vertical scrollbar in firefox:

Get "Stylish" addon if U don't have already.

Add to your userChrome.css or edit the example userChrome.css file in your Mozzilla Firefox .default folder.

```

/* hide vertical scrollbar */
notificationbox {
    overflow-x: hidden;
}
browser[type="content-primary"], browser[type="content-targetable"] {
    overflow-y: scroll;
    margin-right: -12px; /* 12px == width of my scrollbar */
}
```

Restart FF
Remove script if you don't favor it.

I've found that there is rarely any worthy content to the far right of most websites and when there is the addon "Default Full Zoom Level" in FF can remember the zoom out that fits; usually only out to 90% from 100%.

### Post by kerry_s on 2010-10-15
> Also, how do you get the workspace and show desktop icons(or launchers)?


use the main menu to create the launchers. then just drag & drop in dockbarx.

show desktop:
xdotool key Super+d

workspace(compiz):
xdotool key Super+e


i just wiped mine cause i was teaching a friend how to install ubuntu unity from usb. so i'm running the standard unity till he brings my usb key back.

ps: thanks, didn't know about "Window Applets".

### Post by ibuclaw on 2010-10-16
> **slackthumbz said:**
> 
Second, screen real estate, what a waste. Unity takes up a lot of space with that silly sidepane on the left and surely on a netbook you want to maximise your use of all available space? Whatever you feel comfortable with, I suppose. I personally hate dockys, they either get in the way of work, or don't integrate well with what I want.
> **slackthumbz said:**
> 
(oh yeah, in the netbook edition I had to manually install gnome-compiz and the various compiz fusion plugins just to get compositing working with desktop effects. WTF). 

Haven't used compiz in years... Other WM's deprecate the need for it IMO (and if a WM isn't composite-enabled, there's always xcompmgr). While compiz filled a missing gap in it's hayday, probably best if we just let the project die now...
Besides, why would you have heavy/dramatic composition enabled on a device that can't handle it?


> **Popoi said:**
> I feel this version like a lab experiment, It shouldn't be release yet.
In a way all Ubuntu releases are lab experiments.

### Post by mark1softie on 2010-10-17

### Post by Popoi on 2010-10-22
I didn''t like Unity, for every reason expressed here. But I want to sintetize some points and share brief impressions:
 
Global menu is nice, but it needs to improve interoperability with Firefox and OpenOffice (LibreOffice).
 
Sidepanel is not a bad idea at all, but maybe it should be smaller, it consumes too much space, in this case the medicine was worse than the illness.
 
Applications menu is really slow and buggy, we don't need 4 clicks to open a new program. The worst part is that Nautilus is so hidden, 20 seconds to reach a document. We want something fast and simple!
 
Unity as a whole is not versatile, it doesn't fit user view of her own desktop, it feels like a dictatorial desicion from developer, not the software libre spirit at all.
 
I'm using Gnome Desktop again, less space, but it works.

### Post by Red_Steve on 2010-10-29
The Thing with open source is that people tend to stick to what they already know and rely on. But with the freedom of FLOSS comes also the responsibility to maintain your system as you want it. Shifting defaults away from what you know simply scares people. That's what makes them complain.

I don't get that too.

As a long time Windows user just-recent-Ubuntu-turned it was even hard for me to get and accept that I myself are solely responsible to glue my system together, even while installing a precompiled distribution. Seems like this is exactly what makes used-to-gnome ubuntu users complain about the shift.

---

### Post by NightwishFan on 2010-10-29
Thing is they are not moving away from Gnome, just adding a new shell for it, replacing the gnome shell. Gnome 2 (panels etc) are still supported. Probably even off the standard Ubuntu live cd install at least until 11.04 and perhaps after.

They are basing Unity off of compiz, which I find to be an interesting and thoughtful move. It shows they hear what users are saying about mutter and the gnome shell, and want Unity to work on more hardware and faster. I heard that they hired a Compiz developer to help them work on it, which is good for compiz as well.

Also, even the Gnome Shell will be available for install if desired. It sounds like a solid future, it just depends on how Unity is recieved in 11.04.

The installation routines seem to have greatly improved with Meerkat, especially the way it starts the download and then asks some of the personal questions.  How this relates to this topic is:

### Post by GreatKeyHunter on 2010-10-30
[LEFT]From my point of view it makes perfect sense to move to unity. For one, since most modern screens/monitors now are wider and smaller from top to bottom, Unity takes advantage of those features by utilizing the width to its advantage.  Giving the user more screen space from top to bottom.  Second, from what i have read, gnome-shell is not compatible with compiz.  I my self love compiz, it is an awesome tool to pretend you are doing work on your comp.  Also, i'm willing to support the move to unity and help out finding any bugs possible.  From my point of view, unity will be one of the key features that will truly define the Ubuntu Desktop/laptop experience. 
[/LEFT]

### Post by fidamehran on 2010-10-31
I think, Unity should be included somehow in the CD. I don't think the available ISO should be bigger than 700 MB. CDs, I believe, are more solid when you want to read them in the ROMs. So, CD is better.

What Canonical can do is release the Unity as the DE after working thoroughly on it, so that it can maintain the speed of Gnome (we still want a 700MHz CPU with 256 MB RAM to be able to run Ubuntu).

As for the looks, people who prefer Gnome, can get a prompt after the OS is installed, asking them whether they want to go for Gnome. However, if they want to stay with Unity for some time and then decide, the prompt can sit minimized in the taskbar until the user decides.

Now, KDE and other DEs don't need to be included in the initial package. You have KUBUNTU, XUBUNTU and EDUBUNTU for that. All are open for you to download and install. Or you can always download a DE and use it in your current installation.

So, remove the fear of people who are afraid. We don't want to be confined in the past !!! We are the Ubuntu People !!!!!

What Canonical can do is release the Unity as the DE after working thoroughly on it, so that it can maintain the speed of Gnome (we still want a 700MHz CPU with 256 MB RAM to be able to run Ubuntu).

As for the looks, people who prefer Gnome, can get a prompt after the OS is installed, asking them whether they want to go for Gnome. However, if they want to stay with Unity for some time and then decide, the prompt can sit minimized in the taskbar until the user decides.

C'mon people!! Remove the fear of people who are afraid. We don't want to be confined in the past !!! We are the Ubuntu People !!!!!

---

### Post by 3Miro on 2010-10-31
Unity and Gnome-shell are both operate on top of Gnome. The current regular Gnome interface is being removed upstream by the Gnome people, so Ubuntu has to either move to Gnome-shell or perhaps a new interface altogether. IMO Gnome-shell is trying to cover a subset of power users, while Unity if trying to be as easy as possible for new users. I think it makes scene that Ubuntu is making their own interface, I just hope they manage to clean most of the issues with it for 11.04.

I am sure Gnome-shell will be included in Ubuntu as well, after all it is still Gnome.

### Post by vboxu on 2010-10-31
If you think unity sucks and liked the old netbook interface from 10.04 better, you can just 

sudo apt-get install  netbook-launcher-efl ubuntu-netbook-efl-default-settings

Log out and when you log back in you'll have a choice to used the "2D" netbook environment, which is the old one.

It seems to be the same as before except the app icons are twice as big and jump at you (expand) when you mouse over them.

Installing the old ui seems to use very little additional disk space, and I think ubuntu should have included it as an option by default.

I can see why they made unity the default for netbooks because they want people to try it.  But why not give people the old one as a fall back?

### Post by yoma1977 on 2010-10-31
If there is a reason to vote (as in gnome would go) i would totally vote against unity (based off 10.10 release).
 
I've turned my underused netbook into a smallish homeserver and first tried the netbook edition with unity. It had lots of issues (not taking clicks, indeed i like shortcuts on desktop, very bad vnc support, blabla, ..).
 
I then did away with it all, reinstalled from scratch starting with the server edition with no gui at all. Then I tried installing the kde desktop (which worked, but wasn't really that great and also had lots of clicking issues and a lot op menupopups i couldn't quite get rid of...)
 
Finally I changed the kde desktop for the gnome desktop and must really say i personally like that a lot more.
 
I'm not an expert by any means, i just thought let's give ubuntu a try and must say that i'm totally charmed by it.
 
I love it. Thank you canonical and all other developers for making a great piece of software that's turning underused hardware into a great homeserver at 0 cost.
 
It doesn't really matter what desktop gui they ship it with by default, as long as people get all the really important options in every flavour.
 
Maybe an integrated post install way to "switch to another desktop" easily for all users (including total noobs) would be at hand, with some kind of movie preview of how it would look like...
 
Anyway thanks again for a superb system!

### Post by CarpKing on 2010-11-02
I'm a bit torn.  I want Ubuntu to be a better upstream citizen, but TBH I'm somewhat skeptical about Gnome Shell.  On top of that, I know very little about what Unity is or what it will become when they modify it for desktop use.  

From my current perspective, I think my ideal desktop would be Gnome 2.x-style with AWN and a Mutter that behaves like the old Metacity except for having simple, elegant animations for things like resizing and minimizing.  Maybe Gnome Shell will approximate this if I install AWN, or maybe I'll like what they do with Unity.  People have been talking about a Compiz-based DE since Gnome Shell was first announced; maybe that's what Unity will become.  

I think in the end that I wish Ubuntu had worked with Gnome developers to influence the direction of the shell instead of making their own thing.  Now all that's left is to see how both projects turn out.

### Post by Merk42 on 2010-11-02
> **ubuntuman001 said:**
> just correct me where (if) i'm wrong:
> **ubuntuman001 said:**
> - UNE has always had unity, and at least for the foreseeable future, always willIt only had Unity as of 10.10, it had a different interface before, I'm not sure it had a particular name

> **ubuntuman001 said:**
> - gnome shell is gnome 3.0GNOME Shell is **one part of** GNOME 3.0

> **ubuntuman001 said:**
> - 11.04 desktop edition onwards is tentatively set to have unity as the default, but eventually will be based on gnome 3.0 (gnome shell?)GNOME 3, yes, GNOME Shell no. As I said before GNOME Shell is a part of GNOME 3. In Ubuntu's case that one part will be replaced with Unity though the others should remain.

> **ubuntuman001 said:**

### Post by t.rei on 2010-11-02
Actually even considered that unity aims clearly at touchscreens and possible future ways of human-computer-interfacing I am still going to give it a try once it becomes a little more mature. For now I stick to my working 10.10 as my testcomputer is unavailable. 

But considering that it's not even alpha I am a little excited what it will be like in a few months (considering how long kde4* has been unusable and how it still waits for a performance patch NOW (4.6) thats not much time!).

It might actually promote the way I interact with the computer as long as some cruicial parts work as well as they do in 'classic gnome' (I am one of those that remember the gnome 1 release ARGH! *smellsold*):
- multiple monitors (I cant see that in unity right now, so that will be interesting)
- presentations (mildly related to multiple monitors)
- keyboard shortcuts (probably no problem using compiz)
- grouping windows, but seperate IM chat windows (THATS rare - possible with cairo-dock, tho)
- ability to control with or without mouse as far as possible.
- THEMEING! :D

Really looking forward to see this **option**. If I don't get along with it, there will always be the trusty old gnome, or the spiffy kde, or the xfce or blackbox or fvwm :P.

I like how they take the shitstorm and sail it guns blazing! :D

### Post by sandman652001 on 2010-11-03
I have no problem with a shift away from Gnome, what I do have a problem with is a shift towards Unity! -There is just no nice way to say it .....IT SUCKS!!!.I would love to see a better and less tired interface for Ubuntu, but It would need to be a better interface not a worse one! Change just for the sake of change is never a good idea, and I have a feeling that Ubuntu is moving in the wrong direction. the people saying that "people that do not like it can just go back to Gnome" ignore the fact that interfaces that are not mainstream tend to be updated less often, and end up leaving us with obsolete systems.
My current attitude is to wait and see, if I like what I see(And I doubt it very much! I'm back to 10.04 on my netbook!) I'll stick with Ubuntu, If I do not I'll just look for an alternative, I do not mean this as a threat...just a statement of fact. I have been running Ubuntu almost from the very beginning And I have loved the ride so far,  but I'm a little disappointed that the heads of canonical have decided to ignore such a large percentage of their users!

---

### Post by Yougo on 2010-11-03
Good news:

-Canonical is not moving away from gnome :D
-Unity as it is, is not coming to the desktop. they're going to tweak it first, and THEN bring it to the desktop :D
-by far the most trouble people have with Unity, is actually Mutter's fault :neutral:
-Canonical is going to skip Mutter and use Compiz :D
-this is not change for the sake of change, it is change for the sake of not using Gnome-shell :D
-Ubuntu 10.04 will be supported untill 2013, and so will our dear precious panels :D

plenty of reasons to keep smiling.

---

### Post by forrestcupp on 2010-11-03
It's just that people use Ubuntu because it's known to be a great default Gnome distro.  You use Ubuntu because you like Gnome.  If you want KDE, you use Kubuntu.  If you want xfce, you use Xubuntu.  I know I can install KDE in Ubuntu, but if I'm going to primarily be using KDE, I'll install Kubuntu instead.

When KDE advanced to KDE4, Kubuntu advanced with it.  Ubuntu is a Gnome distro, so it should advance with Gnome.  If they want another choice with a default Unity, they need to come out with Unibuntu, and even put the majority of their focus on that if they want to.

### Post by Yougo on 2010-11-03
> **forrestcupp said:**
>   Ubuntu is a Gnome distro, so it should advance with Gnome.  If they want another choice with a default Unity, they need to come out with Unibuntu, and even put the majority of their focus on that if they want to.

Unity is not a DE. it is as much Gnome as Gnome-panel is. for now Ubuntu comes with Gnome. whatever panel/dock/shell configuration it is presented with, isn't specified. even if Canonical decides to go with a blank screen and a big red button in the center, it'd still be Gnome underneath.

and even then Ubuntu is the default. if they decided to make KDE the default DE, Kubuntu would just lose the K.

### Post by Red_Steve on 2010-11-03
Ubuntu even advances with GNOME! Unity will most likely use the new GTK libraries which are as integral a part of Gnome 3 as anything else could get. Ubuntu started to shift away from default gnome with the indicators that surely many if not most ubuntu users came to like and use. Now all they do is customize the default gnome further. So if you want the ubuntu package without the ubuntu flavour all you have to to is get rid of the indicators and in the future get rid of unity and install gnome shell. But I don't think that will happen THAT often as gnome shell is as foreign to someone used to the old panels as Unity ever could get.

### Post by h4xor66 on 2010-11-03
i'll tell you what i think ...

unity looks like a mcdonalds cash register with all it's bright colored cartoonish buttons ... it looks like they have dumbed down the interface so a monkey can hit a big button with his fist and get a peanut  ..... unity makes sense on a tiny netbook or a tablet computer , but for a desktop , for me a desktop with a 36 inch monitor it's just ridiculous .

i personally will not EVER be using it not even to give it a little try , i do not want, didn't ask for , and will NEVER use an OS that has a bunch of ugly buttons taking up the left side of my screen .... i don't care if it turns out to be the most functional UI ever created , it's ugly ... it is GOD AWFUL UGLY 

remember that you heard it here first ... this is canonicals first attempt to push us into cloud computing ... by making it more dificult to get to and use your powerful desktop programs .. they can start making little cloud apps that easily integrate into the unity UI and of course these apps will come at a price " help support ubuntu , purchase this app for .99cents "  thats the future of ubuntu , they are already designing the unity cash register and soon they will have you filling it with money 


but i want to thank ubuntu for forcing me to look else where ... im really liking fedora

### Post by NightwishFan on 2010-11-06
> **zer010 said:**
> Ok, I'm starting to get really curious about Unity. Since I am running 10.04, how can I install Unity beside GNOME? I did a search in Synaptic for "Unity" but it didn't seem to bring up the Unity DE.  
*Note: I just want to try it out and if I like it, I will probably use it in what ever release it becomes standard in. I'm still quite pleased with the old GNOME that I know and love.

To be honest I would wait for the 11.04 alphas and test it on a live cd, though if you want to use it on 10.04 use the netbook ppa:
[https://launchpad.net/~canonical-dx-team/+archive/une?field.series_filter=lucid](https://launchpad.net/~canonical-dx-team/+archive/une?field.series_filter=lucid)

To add it open a terminal and run:
```
sudo apt-add-repository ppa:canonical-dx-team/une && sudo apt-get dist-upgrade && sudo apt-get install unity
```

### Post by zekopeko on 2010-11-06
> **NCLI said:**
> To be fair, Apple didn't add it, they just bought NextStep.

This pisses me off just as much as those people who say that Microsoft has been working on Kinect for years, when they actually just bought a company which had developed the technology they needed.

You're right they did bought the company. And then they spent the next 14 years polishing it. Steve Jobs was the CEO of NextStep computers and when he came back he made Apple buy the company.

The same with IE. MS bought it but they spent the next decade and half building upon it.

So yes they do deserve the credit for pushing the initial tech forward.

This is [NextStep in 1995]("http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/3/3b/NeXTSTEP_desktop.jpg").

And this is [OSX today]("http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/8/82/Snow_Leopard_Desktop.png").

Pretty big difference.

### Post by cariboo on 2010-11-06

Coincidentally, this happens quite often with Microsoft and Apple.

By using some convoluted logic, you could say that the smaller company no longer exists, so the company that bought it can take credit for creating the technology. :)

> **zekopeko said:**
> You're right they did bought the company. And then they spent the next 14 years polishing it. Steve Jobs was the CEO of NextStep computers and when he came back he made Apple buy the company.

The same with IE. MS bought it but they spent the next decade and half building upon it.

So yes they do deserve the credit for pushing the initial tech forward.

This is [NextStep in 1995]("http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/3/3b/NeXTSTEP_desktop.jpg").

And this is [OSX today]("http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/8/82/Snow_Leopard_Desktop.png").

Pretty big difference.

zekopeko seems to understand what I wrote perfectly. Thanks, zeko!

### Post by Scoobin on 2010-11-07
Personally I have thought the existing Gnome 2 panel set up has been outdated at least since Windows 7 was released. By then both Mac and PC had moved to a simplified interface where the launcher was also the running task. I've been running Ubuntu mostly with AWN rather than Gnome panels for a year or so now.

Having heard about Unity becoming default in 11.04 recently, I thought I would find out about this interface. I've installed it for testing (doesn't work correctly, has video issues, perhaps mutter/compiz, but Canonical is saying they'll get it polished) and viewed some videos and must say I'm very impressed with the way it seems to function. 

My main concern is that there will not be enough options to customise but with this being Ubuntu's responsibility, they will be looking to keep evolving it into something exceptional. So I am optimistic and I think the Gnome 3 shell was going to be detrimental to uptake and continued use of Linux, due to the unusual way it operates and also how it runs sluggishly on good hardware (they may well turn it into a decent shell eventually, who knows)... At least I just couldn't appreciate it.

And by Canonical choosing something new it creates more choice in the GUI 'market'. That means if people want something different, they either use a different distro, or they install whatever is the current Gnome shell at the time. Yay for choice!


> **SonicSteve said:**
> My feelings are pretty simple;

1. I'm really excited to see what it will be like/ what they will do. 

2. I hope I like it. 

3. If I don't I'll likely find a way to change it. I use AWN after a few years of using the panels. Now I really like AWN and install it on all my Ubuntu machines. 

The beauty of Linux, if you don't like it change it.
Totally agree 100%.

> **ndefontenay said:**
> Outside of Europe, Japan and the US (including Canada) the internet is still crappy. Downloading a whole DVD would be excruciating!
Add Australasia and South Korea at least to that list :)

> **KingYaba said:**
> Unity is change for the sake of change.
Unity is change for the sake of improvement and moving with the times. Do you expect to be using Gnome panels in 10 years time?

### Post by czr114 on 2010-11-07
What are the implications for performance and resource usage compared to current GNOME?

Color me a skeptic, but I've seen too many good projects use a chance at reinventing the wheel to expand to fill available resources, while out-theming and out-widgeting the competition (but without offering any tangible improvement).

Likewise, reimaginings of UIs in both OSes and apps have a nasty habit of tossing aside what works for what's considered "edgy" or "buzz", as if somehow moving everything around and changing the interface for the sole sake of change offers any tangible benefit.

There's certainly room for improvement in GNOME, if that's the goal. I'd hate to see migration away from clean and efficient to something the likes of Vista or OSX.

For me, one of the principal advantages of OSS is that it frees programmers from the edicts of the marketing department. Much productive capacity is lost when programmers are stuck changing the window dressing, incrementing the version, and placing a new price tag on it.

### Post by Pedric on 2010-11-08
> **Scoobin said:**
> Personally I have thought the existing Gnome 2 panel set up has been outdated at least since Windows 7 was released. By then both Mac and PC had moved to a simplified interface where the launcher was also the running task. I've been running Ubuntu mostly with AWN rather than Gnome panels for a year or so now.

That functionality (launcher = task indicator) could easily be integrated into the GNOME Task List Applet. What you'd have to do is:

- make the window titles optional
  - possible options: never show, show if task is running, always show
- make the icons scale with the panel size
- make it possible to place launchers within the task list and for each running window, see if you can find it within the launcher list and if so, use that as a task indicator (by e.g. encapsulating it in a button so that it looks like current running tasks do)

That would get you Win7-MacOSX-Unity-like task indicators/launchers without sacrificing the power and flexibility of GNOME panels. For functionality alone, you wouldn't need to pull an entirely new shell out of your hat.

If I am a minority on this what is this from Phoronix:

> **By Phoronix's, Michael Larabel:** Let's hope that this Unity switch lasts as long as Canonical's affair with Yahoo when they became the default search engine within Ubuntu's Firefox, which was broken up by user outrage, but it's unlikely we'll see this happen with Unity unless they just push back the switch to Ubuntu 11.10. Doing so would at least allow their desktop interface to become more polished and also provide time for the open-source display drivers to further mature.

Source:[http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=ODcxNg]("http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=ODcxNg")

For the others who don't know how to switch back open terminal and type the following commands if you want gnome-shell:

sudo apt-get remove unity
sudo apt-get install gnome-shell

But it has grown up in to a great app, vastly superior to the old add/remove programs.

> If I am a minority on this what is this from Phoronix:

Phoronix is a bunch of hardcore geeks. Sorry to say, what they like for UI is irrelevant here. What canonical needs to target is mainstream users who use their pcs and dont spend half their days on linux fora. Let Arch or slackware or whatever target those and please allow ubuntu to gain mainstream acceptance by targeting ordinary mainstream users with their default interface and app selection. We will all benefit from that.

### Post by Nightstrike2009 on 2010-11-08
> **By P4man:** Phoronix is a bunch of hardcore geeks. Sorry to say, what they like for UI is irrelevant here. What canonical needs to target is mainstream users who use their pcs and dont spend half their days on linux fora. Let Arch or slackware or whatever target those and please allow ubuntu to gain mainstream acceptance by targeting ordinary mainstream users with their default interface and app selection. We will all benefit from that.

Phoronix and Distrowatch are well respected in the Linux community, and those aspiring to become "Hardcore Geeks" take them very seriously, Linux without "Hardcore Geeks" simply wouldn't exist, It was Richard Stallman (GNU) and Linus Torvalds (Linus Kernel) we have to thank for Linux, I think you should read up on Linux history before making comments like that in future, I got warned for much less than that comment.

I suggest is they are they so unwilling to learn knowledge and experience of Linux, Windows should suit there needs fine, there is no need for their "Conversion to the cause" by Canonical or anyone else in the Linux community. I left Windows to become involved, learn more and become part of the community and it took me years to learn that knowledge, Ubuntu should embrace new users but without learning and expereince its just "another windows" in my opinion and simply not worth migrating from that standpoint, may has well stick with windows.

I originally didn't use Linux mint, until I learned the "Backend" bits like how to use terminal in Ubuntu first, I took the "hard way" not the "easy one" and I am grateful for this experience, but they have gone to far with this in my opinion now.

### Post by janpol on 2010-11-08
> **P4man said:**
> Nightstrike, Im curious. Are you worried about you personally ending up with unity by default, or everyone else? 

If its you, in the time you wrote these posts, you personally could have switched to gnome2 about a dozen times. 

If you are worried about others, then try looking at unity through the eyes of a newbie, non expert. The kind of person that unlike you, doesnt know how to switch from one DE to another. 

I know for sure I am going to install it on my mother's laptop once it matures and she will love it. She is 75 and no exactly pc literate. She's been using ubuntu for years and still doesnt quite grasp the difference between  minimizing and closing apps, or very good at taskswitching etc. Her laptop is also only 1280x800 and gnome is horrible at that, especially with firefox and larger fonts, there is just no room left for webpages.  Unity is what will work for her and lots of others. Quite possibly myself included.
Both my mother and my girlfriend are windows users (and they are really basic users, they understand nothing about computers). I made em use Unity on a netbook (as a little experiment), they loved it, and they didn't need to ask me any questions about how to use it.

And I'm talking about the immature Unity that is released with 10.10 NE, which is obviously going to be different when it gets to Natty as the default desktop.

Wait until we get the final version in 11.04 before making apocalyptic announces. You also have to consider that is probable that Unity is going to be faster than Gnome 3 (Compiz vs Mutter), and the fact that Mark said Ubuntu is going away from X (maybe in four years or so, but those are really exiting news), I think Ubuntu is going to get lots of new users (from the Windows, Mac, and also from the Linux side). And this radical changes are really great for the Linux environment, right now, a regular user can't really point out the difference between the most popular distros. And last but not least, guys at the compiz project said that this announcement (remember that Unity uses Compiz instead of Mutter) gave them a new reason to work at it.

But, as always, only time will tell. For those who are ranting about Unity, you can still use gnome, or switch to another distro, but don't stress yourselves, Linux means freedom ;)

Ubuntu's mission is to get Linux to the masses, never forget that, if some users switch to another distro, you are still using Linux so, I don't think that's a bad thing for Ubuntu.

Regards!

---

### Post by 3Miro on 2010-11-08
The Gnome developers have decided to end Gnome 2 and go for Gnome 3. Gnome 3 comes with a new interface called Gnome-shell. Ubuntu can either keep dragging the old Gnome, move over to Gnome-shell or make its own Gnome interface of Unity. Dragging an old piece of technology is a bad idea, Ubuntu should move forward. Gnome-shell is aimed at more hardcore users, I know many people like it, but I find it very dysfunctional and I don't think it will work for people that are completely new to Linux. So the best option is to create your own interface - Unity. Everything else "under the hood" will be Gnome as well as most of the applications, the interface will be different.

### Post by Yougo on 2010-11-08
> **Pedric said:**
> That functionality (launcher = task indicator) could easily be integrated into the GNOME Task List Applet. What you'd have to do is:

- make the window titles optional
  - possible options: never show, show if task is running, always show
- make the icons scale with the panel size
- make it possible to place launchers within the task list and for each running window, see if you can find it within the launcher list and if so, use that as a task indicator (by e.g. encapsulating it in a button so that it looks like current running tasks do)

That would get you Win7-MacOSX-Unity-like task indicators/launchers without sacrificing the power and flexibility of GNOME panels. For functionality alone, you wouldn't need to pull an entirely new shell out of your hat.

I'm using the [DockbarX panel applet]("https://launchpad.net/dockbar") which prettymuch does what the win7 taskbar does. less animation though. the progress-bar-in-button thing would be nice.

if you still want this, but don't have a panel, i'm sure there's a dock out there that provides it. AWN is a nice option indeed

### Post by 3Miro on 2010-11-08
> **qamelian said:**
> Oh, I understand, but the "don't hate change" mantra gets hauled out every time someone objects to a change that Canonical makes. It gets old very quickly. I've read everything I can find about the plans for Unity's development, and can't find a single reason to prefer it over Gnome-shell. I'll test it like I test everything that Canonical adds to the Ubuntu distro, but that doesn't mean I need to fall in love with it. I have very practical reasons for maintaining a preference for Gnome-shell and nothing I've read thus far promoting Unity is likely to make me reconsider.

I find Gnome-shell very dysfunctional. It does great if you mostly keep two hands on the keyboard, however, as soon as you have to work with keyboard and a mouse or a mouse and a beer, GS gets very clumsy. In every current environment, I can open a large set of applications with one click, move windows to different workspaces with one move/drag, change workspaces, switch between opened windows even if they completely cover each other, access one click menu. This is only with a mouse and minimal move/click time. You can do all of that in GS, but only if you go over and over through the zoom menu and that is a lot of wasted time. I don't think it is a good interface for "everybody" especially beginners and that is exactly the main target of Ubuntu.

Correct me if I am wrong, I do want to know if I am simply not using GS properly.

### Post by czr114 on 2010-11-08
I never understood why they moved minimize/maximize/close to the left in Karmic to begin with. Most people are used to them on the right, because they're coming either from Windows, or from one of the countless other GNU/Linux variants which places them on the right. There's no functional difference between the two, so why not leave them in the place to which most users subconsciously and habitually look and move?

If we're going to do "different", it should be for the sake of improvement, not just to make the statement that we can buck the trend. Change for the sake of change just makes for an awkward transition period with no real benefit to show for it, other than maybe some buzz on blogs.

Hopefully this isn't another change for the sake of change reimagining.

### Post by nw2001 on 2010-11-09
This thread makes no sense.

In the same way you can distro hop, you can also just sudo apt-get remove unity && sudo apt-get install gnome2

Just because they are trying something new to make their target unit base be more comfortable.(Hint not hardcore geeks, rather people switching from Mac and Windows) does not mean they are making silly decisions. It means unlike every other distro that tried to get their attention, Ubuntu is adapting, even when DE devs wont.

Linux' strengths is its customizablity and sheer amount of choice. Unity just gives another one oriented to the new user, Something I dare say it(linux) currently lacks.

What we have now is not what we will get in 6 months or in 11.10. I expect unity will be a hell of a lot more fleshed out and better to use. The workspace switching mockups are amazing.

And last off, For anyone who is worried cause they are switching from gnome, Its not going to be the same gnome in a year or two anyways. Gnome3 is going to be VASTLY different in the same way as unity. All you do is delay the inevitable.:popcorn:

### Post by slackthumbz on 2010-11-09
> **Oxwivi said:**
> This: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=9522217&postcount=29](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=9522217&postcount=29)

Ubuntu is moving forward, if you want to keep things as long as they work then try Debian. It's the same as saying why bother with cars, horses are enough.

'Moving forward' generally implies making something better. This is clearly not the case with unity or gnome-shell. What they're doing is taking something like a transformer and taking away its ability to transform. All you end up with is a crappy car without the awesome robot inside.

If they're going to make for a good usable linux desktop it needs to retain the current level of flexibility that we have in gnome 2. The great thing about linux/floss is that it's customisable, as users we can tweak our desktops to suit us and not just accept what ever style of paradigm is inflicted upon us by a single vendor (ie, Mac OSX/Windows), what they're doing with Unity and Gnome-shell at the moment takes away from that and reduces the users control of their own personal style of interacting with their desktop. My desktop setup would probably be damn near incomprehensible to most people and many other users desktops would be confusing for me, but for each of us our own configuration works perfectly and that's the point.

> **By EvilErnie**:I am no uber-geek but I like to tinker but not devote a large portion of my life to learning the complete ins and outs of an OS, Ubuntu does give you that ability to scratch the surface and have a really cool tool that useful in everyday life but also if you want dive right in there.

Anyhow try them both out and make your own opinion up, I dislike Unity and will not use it, you may find it suits your needs, I am all for new users and user friendly but some effort should be made in learning linux for personal use, I don't want Ubuntu to become another "Utility OS".

PC's are like cars they need maintaining regularly and new parts from time to time, unlike your "Utility" TV which required no effort at all reather than to turn it on, It would be nice if PC's were "Utility" machines but sadly they require maintenance and effort. 

**I don't fear change I intend to become a "gnome-shell" user and just because gnome team is concentrating its efforts on making it stable rather than rush it out (Was expected in 10.10) doesn't mean it should be dropped as default too many things are rushed out on Ubuntu has it is, this appears to be a knee-jerk reaction to Gnome-shell not being ready in time IMO.**

### Post by TBABill on 2010-11-09
This is actually becoming a quite entertaining thread. Ubuntu devs and Marc Shuttleworth are working toward a goal of simplifying Ubuntu. Whatever it takes, whatever path it goes down, decisions have to be made. Anyone who has ever worked in an evolutionary line of work realizes there are risks involved with those decisions, but in the end there is a measure of success or failure. Which that will be is totally unknown to any of us, yet we dispute the thoughts of each other as if we do know.
 
Here's what I do know. Coca-cola changed the original Coke formula and it was perceived as a public relations nightmare, a company's failure to be in touch with the consumer, the destruction of an iconic product, blah blah blah. But what was not widely discussed was just how much public attention the company received as a result and how much the company was perceived in an incredibly popular light when they simply went back to making Coke as they always had and labelled it "Classic". And look at them today...strong as ever.
 
I'm not equating the decision to make Unity the default desktop to Coke's decision to change Coke. What I'm saying is the DECISION to try something new created a controversy and brought a great deal of attention to the company. Even if Unity is a bad decision, Ubuntu will gain an incredible amount of attention from the media, bloggers and users of other distros. So even if it drives people away over principles, how many will run toward it to try it out, to talk about it and to eventually further hype the distro? It's a win-win. If it fails, Gnome 2 or Gnome 3 or something else becomes the default. If it succeeds, then this whole thread was just fear of the unknown and we can reminisce about it later.
 
When Ubuntu or any distro takes risks and creates a flurry of buzz, a great deal of attention is paid to it and that will probably end in a greater number of total users than before those risks were taken. That's just my best guess, but history could likely repeat itself and we will end up with the benefits of the effort to create change. 
 
And if we don't like it, uninstall Unity, install Gnome. Big deal. I add ubuntu-restricted-extras, then remove the 32 bit Flash and open java for the versions I prefer. 3 minutes in Synaptic and I have what I want, which is all the OP wants as well. 
 
How about a suggestion to the devs? Has one been sent? Something like, "Since Unity will be new to non-netbook Ubuntu users, could an option be placed in the install sequence instead, allowing the user to choose Unity or Gnome 2?"

After a few minor adjustments to my work habits, I find it very difficult and unproiductive if I need to work in the typical Gnome 2 environment compared to Gnome-shell. That just happens to be my preference and doesn't necessarily have any bearing on your environment. Based on my usage of GS, in my opinion the old paradigm is not necessarily broken, but is isn't necessarily the best choice either. I expect to enjoy using GS for many years to come.

Wait, do you want PCs to become utilities or not? Which is it??

> **I don't fear change I intend to become a "gnome-shell" user and just because gnome team is concentrating its efforts on making it stable rather than rush it out (Was expected in 10.10) doesn't mean it should be dropped as default too many things are rushed out on Ubuntu has it is, this appears to be a knee-jerk reaction to Gnome-shell not being ready in time IMO.**
That is not what this is. Canonical disagrees with the way Gnome is taking Gnome Shell, especially things like thee exclusion of Zeitgeist. This is why Canonical has decided to build its own DE based on Gnome 3, it has nothing to do with the shell being delayed.

Also, Unity isn't being "rushed out." They're making it the default now so that it will receive a lot of testing, and be ready in time for 12.04. As I've said before; if you don't like sudden changes and unfinished or unpolished software, stick with the LTS', and use PPAs for the applications you want to have up-to-date.

The difference between you, and the many other Unity haters out there, and people like me and the many Unity defenders out there, is that while you have decided not to put any faith in Canonical, and assume beforehand that Unity Desktop is going to suck, we want to wait and see what happens before passing judgment. I'm not saying that I am 100% certain that Unity will not be crap, I just want to see it first.

### Post by Mr. Picklesworth on 2010-11-09
Gnome Panel has been living on minor bug fixes for a long time. It still has had the same deep, painful design problems it has always had. It's responsible for most of Gnome's slow login time, its keyboard accessibility is horrific, and it still uses the long deprecated Bonobo library. There are some nice applets out there, but Gnome Panel itself is a case study in how *not* to make an extensible interface.
(For what it's worth, Firefox's toolbars and XFCE's panels do way better on a few of these points).

Gnome Shell and Unity are starting small on the extensibility front because that's a sane way to do this. New extension points will probably be added as time goes on, but I doubt any of these will go for a generic one-size-fits-all applets type of thing.
Both of these are pushing the idea that applets aren't very important when we can just make normal applications fast, persistent and easy to reach.

### Post by cariboo on 2010-11-09
I use both gnome-shell and Unity on a daily basis, although on different hardware. Although the two look similar, they work in different ways. There are some things the gnome devs have done that I don't agree with, limiting  us to one desktop on startup, and I'm not really sure I like the new theme.

I'm running the latest version of both, gnome-shell 2.91.2 from the [ricotz staging ppa]("https://launchpad.net/~ricotz/+archive/staging") and unity 0.2.46 from the repositories.

I'm going to reserve my judgement on both, until we see a final release.

### Post by JoshuaRL on 2010-11-11
I've been running Unity as my primary DE for a about a week now to see how I like it.

Basically, I get what they're going for.  You can SAY that it's "OSX-dock-like" or "Win7-taskbar-like" but if you look at the design paradigms it's nothing like any modern OS accept itself.  Plus, with most computers today vertical space is much more premium than horizontal.  I often find myself feeling squished into a desktop by either the Windows 7 taskbar or the two bars of Gnome.  But use the same space on the left side and it doesn't fight anything.  You pretty quickly get used to that space just being an app drawer, and ignore its existence unless needed.

Which doesn't mean I love everything.  I had to dig around and install Thunar so I had a normal file manager.  The lack of Alt+F2 is maddening, and I really need the ability to add icons and widgets to the menu bar.  That and it needs to be a little bit snappier, as well as needing a specific place to mess with settings.  I kept looking for something like XFCE Settings and found nothing.

What I think they're going for something truly different and individual, and they're not scared to stick their necks out to do so.  I think that deserves praise and baited breath, not derision.  We'll see how it turns out, but can you name something that Canonical has done that hasn't been made into an awesome and useful part of Ubuntu within at least two years?  They have a pretty excellent track record in my book.

---

### Post by Red_Steve on 2010-11-11
And here we go again.

You know there is this thing called the "desktop paradigm" which was forced down so many throats by Windoze and OS/XYZ alike. Gnome with Gnome Shell is trying to switch away from this methusalemic paradigm in hope to reinvent the wheel just for the sake of change.

Yet there is this company canonical which offers a distribution called ubuntu. Ubuntu comes generally in two flavours. A normal version and a LTS version. Which means Long Term Supported. 
Canonical also want's to renew the paradigm but isn't as... determined to reinvent the wheel (I was going to say a.*.a.l. but that woulnd't be fair, eh?)
Thus they start to experiment with a new desktop environment to break away from this paradigm. they call it "unity". What a ironic name.
However - as with every other piece of software ever made, unity will not be perfect right from the start. Yeah, there will be caveats, yeah there will be roughness and tears and angry green nerds running around, desperatly wanting to smash things. But in time (read: for the next LTS) these issues will be mostly solved so that people who are not tht fond of aprubt or not that well thought through changes can swing from LTS to LTS in order to reap the ripe fruits of two years time of testing and developing.

In short: If you don't like canonical experimenting either stay with your current version for as long as possible or switch to an LTS and be merry!

---

### Post by toupeiro on 2010-11-11
The last thread I posted in got closed, and I really hope it doesn't happen here, so I am going to try to keep my passion at a simmer here.

I don't care about unity.  The more DE's are made optional is great.  That is one of the first things I loved about linux in fact, choice in DE (I liked windowmaker back then, I don't even think it exists anymore)

What I care about is wayland.  I could go on and on (which I did in another thread and it got closed), so I will try to be as to the point as I can be:

If you change the standard graphics server in your operating system, and your operating system is a distribution of many distributions and none of those other distributions are changing, you are not a cross-functional distro anymore.  Instead, you are now on a segmented path and once Wayland/X compatibility time is over, you are not cross-compatible, graphically with any other flavor of UNIX or Linux at home or in the enterprise.  Ubuntu will become a siloed variant of Linux, like OSX and IRIX are/were as UNIX operating systems. (IRIX primarily used something called DGL with it's X-server that made it incompatible with mainstream X.) For the dream of replacing X to work, you have to get the enterprise of linux AND UNIX to move, and there is absolutely no motion there.  If you don't have that, you are essentially making your distribution a somewhat proprietary offering at some point down your development path.  Thats a risky gamble.  Good luck!

---

### Post by czr114 on 2010-11-11
Methusalemic paradigm?

Certain things persist because they work. Other things persist because what they reflected when they were created is still true.

Great software is built by examining the needs of the userbase and tailoring features accordingly. The ultimate indication of that comes when something, be it a release, a branch, or a general concept can be finally marked as stable, freeing up the developer to innovate elsewhere. Only when a basic need or assumption changes does the design need revisited.

Of course, that often comes to an end when the restless need something newer to stay "hip" or "relevant", or as something to blog about.

If there's anything we should be questioning, it's the perpetual redesign paradigm. Can we honestly say that all the bugs, feature shortcomings, driver quirks, and performance bottlenecks have been eliminated in everything from the UI at the top, to the kernel at the bottom, such that interface wonkery is now the highest priority issue?

---

### Post by czr114 on 2010-11-11
toupeiro raises another excellent point. Breaking that level of compatibility and interoperability is a serious issue.

In general, excepting cases of project mismanagement, it's better to cooperate than compete.

We've got dozens of GUI archivers, torrent clients, calculators, download managers, and such - and almost all of them have issues. If duplicity there causes problems, imagine what happens with something as large as an X replacement.

---

### Post by Red_Steve on 2010-11-11
For something to get that old of age it has to offer an invaluable thing.
The DE just works and I don't see a need to reinvent it either. Yet I have no quarrels about trying something new as I certainly see myself not using the current DEs to their full potential yet am confronted with shortcomings the way I use them. Adding something new offers the chance to shift in a broader perspective about the DE and the way it could work.

### Post by Paqman on 2010-11-11
> **toupeiro said:**
> 
If you change the standard graphics server in your operating system, and your operating system is a distribution of many distributions and none of those other distributions are changing, you are not a cross-functional distro anymore.

Ubuntu is the throbbing 900lb gorilla of desktop Linux. Should Ubuntu look to the smaller distros for leadership, or should it lead the pack? If sticking with X is going to be an obvious problem in the future, and no one else looks like they're going to take the leap and sort it out, why not get the ball rolling? 

This announcement will force the other distros to at least hold the discussion internally about what their strategy is for X in the future. What comes out of those discussions will be quite interesting. Likewise, it'll be interesting to see the effect Ubuntu backing Wayland has on Wayland development.

Whatever happens, it's not going to do so quickly. This will be a gradual transition over several years.

I believe that having the same interface is holding ubuntu back because there is always "another" distro with similar features you can jump too. I believe the same with the other distros. Similarity is stagnation.

Ubuntu nor the other distros will ever make it big if they are all the same. The current situation in linux land is like car makers today. There was alot of companies, but they all made a truck a SUV and family van, and family car, and a fast sexy car. But they are all the same basically, just different colors and a different number of cup holder.

I say "bring on the Delorean!"

We need disruption in linux world. We need risk takers!

Ubuntu can be our Delorean! Willing to change how we look at cars!

Hopefully nay sayers won't fear the change and try to kill it like the real Delorean.

---

### Post by slackthumbz on 2010-11-12
> **PC_load_letter said:**

As for Unity, it's too early to tell, but it seems to be an improvement if they made it to work w/ Compiz. I was really kind of shocked when the Gnome project ditched Compiz (in favor of mutter) for the development of Gnome shell. What Canonical seems to be doing makes more sense IMHO.

Wayland doesn't have X's network transparency and for some of us (admins/coders who tend to work remotely) that is a killer feature. If I can't have that then I'll have to move to another distro because I require that functionality in order to do my job.

### Post by MasterNetra on 2010-11-12
> **Spice Weasel said:**
> I don't have a problem with Unity because it doesn't effect me. What I do have a problem with is fscking Wayland.

Ubuntu is the most popular free version of *nix. It should at least have some responsibility and care about compatibility with other systems. We don't have time to rewrite every single X11 app in existence. Lack of compatibility between OSes is what causes them to fail and make developers cry to sleep each night.

[http://blogs.computerworld.com/17303/ubuntu_abandons_x_server_for_wayland](http://blogs.computerworld.com/17303/ubuntu_abandons_x_server_for_wayland)

""... You won't need to give up X-based applications though to use Wayland. Shuttleworth also said, "We're confident we'll be able to retain the ability to run X applications in a compatibility mode, so this is not a transition that needs to reset the world of desktop free software. Nor is it a transition everyone needs to make at the same time: for the same reason we'll keep investing in the 2D experience on Ubuntu despite also believing that Unity, with all its GL dependencies, is the best interface for the desktop. We'll help GNOME and KDE with the transition, there's no reason for them not to be there on day one either." ...""

> **slackthumbz said:**
> Wayland doesn't have X's network transparency and for some of us (admins/coders who tend to work remotely) that is a killer feature. If I can't have that then I'll have to move to another distro because I require that functionality in order to do my job.

""... Someone going by the name Simon wrote on Shuttleworth's blog: "I understand network transparency isn't used by everyone - however, for some of us it's critical functionality. In my office, being able to ssh into a server or someone else's desktop to run development tools (e.g. a gtk-based code-review tool) is vital, and losing that ability *would* be a showstopper as far as Ubuntu desktops are concerned." Another writer on the blog, Diego, replied, "network transparency is beyond the scope of Wayland. You are completely free to implement wayland clients that use network protocols (not just X11: VNC, RDP, Spice&#8230;). In fact, it will possible to run X.org as a Wayland client. So, you will be able to run remote X11 apps on Wayland servers." ...""

""... You won't need to give up X-based applications though to use Wayland. ### Post by toupeiro on 2010-11-13
> **Paqman said:**
> Ubuntu is the throbbing 900lb gorilla of desktop Linux. Should Ubuntu look to the smaller distros for leadership, or should it lead the pack? If sticking with X is going to be an obvious problem in the future, and no one else looks like they're going to take the leap and sort it out, why not get the ball rolling? 

This announcement will force the other distros to at least hold the discussion internally about what their strategy is for X in the future. What comes out of those discussions will be quite interesting. Likewise, it'll be interesting to see the effect Ubuntu backing Wayland has on Wayland development.

Whatever happens, it's not going to do so quickly. This will be a gradual transition over several years.

uhh 900Lb Gorilla?!  I thought ubuntu was linux for human beings.. :P

Canonical's gorilla is more popular, but Redhat and Novell-Gorilla have the money and develop/contribute more.  Canonical can pound their chest all day, but they're not strong enough to deter the established enterprise players, and those players's contribute back more to linux development than Canonical does, ergo, this isn't going to force anybody to do anything.  If you don't work with them to collectively make this move, you're cutting your own throat.  It's no different than what Microsoft tried to do with Silverlight on the web, and now, they've announced that they're reducing its focus to only windows mobile applications.  **forcing** people to do things never works for the better, especially if the only place you have that kind of leverage is in your own imagination.  Redhat-Gorilla (bigger than canonical-gorilla) has tried for force open standards in media formats for years, and it has not swayed the people investing in multimedia production, at least, not by their actions. This is obviously at a much lower level than that.  

This is linux,  it doesn't really matter if you are running on a desktop or not.  What makes ubuntu a server or a desktop is 99.9% the default packaging on install, so if you're saying that breaking form from one of the most elemental and transparent pieces of the OS for the sake of a form factor demographic is good for the community and the Operating system, you've already lost in my opinion.

This means big money for companies that develop very expensive software for linux.  Companies like Halliburton, Paradigm, and Dynamic Graphics, are just a few in my shortlist that do expansive 3d modeling on X.  Do you really think Canonical is going to strongarm those developers to retool??  Do you really think they will want X to be a compatibility layer for a short timeframe, hindering their development paths?  :rolleyes: 

Ubuntu will lose corporate support in the enterprise is what I predict.  Losing Corporate support = lost dollars in canonicals pockets so they can pay their developers to keep advancing their Operating System because they aren't developing this change like a partner in the linux OS community.  This CAN be a good thing if it's handled right, but the way they are doing it, alone, is not well handled at all.  The more companies backing linux and UNIX distro's you get behind something like this, the more successful its going to be and the less likely it will be a completely wasted effort.  Most people look at this through such rose-colored glasses because ubuntu is so great that this change can't possibly be bad and that it's being handled fine. Those glasses need to come off and you need to taste the reality koolaid.  Canonical is not Microsoft.  There are no imperialistic entities in this dynamic of software, it's driven by a collective of millions of developers. This is bad, and primarily because of the way it's being handled more than the potential, or lack thereof, that Wayland might have to replace X if it gets enough support behind it.

### Post by Scoobin on 2010-11-13
> **Pedric said:**
> That functionality (launcher = task indicator) could easily be integrated into the GNOME Task List Applet. What you'd have to do is:

- make the window titles optional
  - possible options: never show, show if task is running, always show
- make the icons scale with the panel size
- make it possible to place launchers within the task list and for each running window, see if you can find it within the launcher list and if so, use that as a task indicator (by e.g. encapsulating it in a button so that it looks like current running tasks do)

That would get you Win7-MacOSX-Unity-like task indicators/launchers without sacrificing the power and flexibility of GNOME panels. For functionality alone, you wouldn't need to pull an entirely new shell out of your hat.
I agree. Which begs the question -- why hasn't this been done -- not as some plugin but officially, even if just as an option, but as part of Gnome? This would have expanded the use-by date of Gnome 2 and perhaps we wouldn't be using Unity. Alas, Gnome has chosen to create a new interface (Shell) and the Ubuntu team's vision of their GUI has digressed too far from that of Gnome to continue with the same interface.

Then there is the file menu intergration (think Mac) and also the way the menu works on Unity.

With the Gnome folks heading for Gnome Shell though Gnome 2 I guess will be left for those who a) detest Gnome Shell and b) want their system to run on lower end systems.

> **sandman652001 said:**
> Actually if you bothered to read my whole post you would know that I'm not against change!
What makes change useful is improved usability and a more intuitive interface all of witch Unity is not!
I welcome change even radical change,but not if that change makes my computer harder to use. I'm not saying do not get rid of gnome, what I am saying is do not replace it with Unity!
?? Check again you'll see I was replying to Kingyaba not you. For the record though I did read your posts. You can hardly blame the Ubuntu team for ditching the interface when Gnome themselves have basically obsoleted it with the development and soon-to-be released Gnome Shell. So Unity is not for you. That's unfortunate. It's not perfect by any stretch, but to me it seems the most logical evolution of the modern desktop GUI that I've seen (perhaps with exception of the new menu system).

@Red_Steve - good point about LTS versions. 10.04 is a pretty solid release which could serve well I think until the next LTS.

### Post by toupeiro on 2010-11-14
> **Paqman said:**
> Lots of widely-deployed Linux projects only have one developer. Compiz, for example.

With all due respect, Compiz is a window manager, NOT a hardware abstraction layer.  And yes, it does matter, for compiz cannot exist without the abstraction layer, and X is the most versatile abstraction layer for windowing that has ever been made by the sheer number of operating systems that implement it as their core framework for graphics and all the software developers which develop for it.  It takes a lot of developers and support to keep something with that broad of a scope moving forward, and Wayland isn't there.

---

### Post by Paqman on 2010-11-14
> **toupeiro said:**
> Wayland isn't there.

Of course not, nobody's suggesting otherwise. To quote the SABDFL himself:
> There are issues with Wayland, but they seem to be solvable, we’d rather be part of solving them than chasing a better alternative. So Wayland it is.

Several options were evaluated, including continuing development of X. Wayland was picked as being the most attractive option. That wasn't because it's 100% ready right now. This is a long-term project.

### Post by czr114 on 2010-11-14
To everyone suggesting we wait and see, the concept is already there and laid down. Yes, they will polish around the edges, but the core idea is already out in public for review.

I use a standard GNOME panel at the top for my most commonly used app launchers, monitors, and trayed applications. At the bottom, I have an autohiding task bar. It's efficient, productive, and conscious of screen real estate. I can't find a limitation with that, whereas the Unity screenshots clearly seem anti-productive and clumsy.

I'm curious what the supporters would suggest exactly that I wait for to see.

I've echoed a concern about the lack of named workspaces with different panel settings; i.e., one for personal, one for productivity, one for software development, etc. That seems like the sort of UI improvements that would help people get things done more efficiently, instead of all these mashups seemingly as change for the sake of change, or some sort of emulation of other desktop environments less suited for power users.

### Post by czr114 on 2010-11-14
> **MasterNetra said:**
> *points to his post that occur just before the quoted.*
What's your point with the wait and see approach? What new information is going to emerge about the general concept of Unity that might completely vindicate the concerns I posted?

I've seen this discussion continue on this forum, others, and a host of blogs, and the two sides seem to be "GNOME 2.x is sufficient and functional" and "Unity looks cool and change is good." It makes me question if certain upheavals are being made for the right reasons.

It always helps to know your market, and desktop GNU/Linux serves a distinct market. The concerns there aren't going to be the same as a 7 or OSX demo unit at Best Buy.

It'd be unfortunate to see a lot of developer man-hours go into change for the sake of change, only to see most of the base revert to GNOME 2.x by manual means after the "new" feel wears off, when there is so much on the roadmap elsewhere. It'd also be unfortunate to see GNOME 2.x support slip in future releases should it get labeled as "unimportant" by the types who have been preaching against anything seen as "old" as if it were broken, and nobody should want to use it anyway.

### Post by Scoobin on 2010-11-15
From Wikipedia:
> The Wayland project was created in 2008 by Kristian Høgsberg, who was then working for Red Hat. His stated goal was a system in which "_every frame is perfect_, by which I mean that applications will be able to control the rendering enough that we'll never see **tearing**, **lag**, **redrawing** or **flicker**".
If they can't even develop X to remove the above issues it does little for Linux in the area of graphics, including applications, demos and games. Here we have some nice graphics such as Compiz and yet we are getting these niggling little irritations that haven't been removed at least in the 5 years I've been using Linux. In this age of fancy graphics you want a smooth graphical experience if you are going to win those with an eye for detail.

I know little of Wayland, except the above; and I like Kristian's goal there. What I do know is that I don't like X a whole lot.

---

### Post by toupeiro on 2010-11-15
> **Scoobin said:**
> From Wikipedia:

If they can't even develop X to remove the above issues it does little for Linux in the area of graphics, including applications, demos and games. Here we have some nice graphics such as Compiz and yet we are getting these niggling little irritations that haven't been removed at least in the 5 years I've been using Linux. In this age of fancy graphics you want a smooth graphical experience if you are going to win those with an eye for detail.

I know little of Wayland, except the above; and I like Kristian's goal there. What I do know is that I don't like X a whole lot.

Um, they can and have developed X to remove the issues above, if the software is quality enough it can take advantage of it!  I support 3 visualitation labs doing this at 3840x2160 with software that runs only on X, all with no tearing.  Also, you're probably using a Geforce card.  Guess what, a card that is made to leverage DIRECT-X will not work as well as a card optimized for OpenGL (Quadro).  You don't even need a top of the line quadro card to outstep a top of the line Geforce card on Linux..  What you're saying is sort of like blaming the starbucks coffee company because the 18 year old behind the counter doesn't know how to make coffee.

People often dislike what they don't understand.  I never said Wayland COULDN'T replace X someday, with enough development behind it.  I don't even dislike the technology.  I dislike the manner in which it's being slapped in place prematurely with little to no cooperative effort, given X's very wide and versatile usage.  I'm saying it's not there yet and until it has a real chance at getting developers behind it, not just for itself but for software to leverage it, it's wasted cycles, and people/companies who write good graphics software on Linux  are not going to tolerate a HAL compatibility mode for a HAL unless there is a damn good reason.  So far, all I see is the usual "canonical can do no wrong" Zealotry towards ending something that has worked great for over 20 years, and has continued to develop and evolve over that period of time and is WIDELY adopted and supported.  I've yet to hear of one quality reason why Wayland is the right thing to do right now in the scope of all the reinventing it's going to do for graphical API's on linux, and the cross-platform compatibility divide it's going to create.

There are millions apon millions of dollars of development of graphics software that supports billion dollar industries, written for X, and all of those companies pay for enterprise support.  It's a stupid business move to risk compromising that, putting aside the fact that it's a strong-arm tactic that hasn't been thought through very well considering the nature of FOSS development. 

Neither NVidia nor ATI/AMD's drivers support Wayland yet, Yet, most people think that it's still a GOOD thing to inject into canonical's flagship OS in 2011?  If you're really gung-ho about replacing X, you better let Wayland build a little more steam.  Right now, it certainly does NOT have enough.

### Post by Mr. Picklesworth on 2010-11-15
> **toupeiro said:**
> People often dislike what they don't understand.  I never said Wayland COULDN'T replace X someday, with enough development behind it.  I don't even dislike the technology.  I dislike the manner in which it's being slapped in place prematurely with little to no cooperative effort, given X's very wide and versatile usage.  I'm saying it's not there yet and until it has a real chance at getting developers behind it, not just for itself but for software to leverage it, it's wasted cycles, and people/companies who write good graphics software on Linux  are not going to tolerate a HAL compatibility mode for a HAL unless there is a damn good reason. &#8230;

That's why [the stance]("http://www.markshuttleworth.com/archives/551") is more like: &#8220;we know Wayland isn't there yet, so we're going to help get it there in order to ship it at some point in the future.&#8221; Sounds like *exactly* what you are advocating here. It's just that Canonical and Red Hat (Fedora) have said from the onset that this is what they are doing. THEY are taking this on; they aren't going to wait for mysterious strangers to do it for them.

Having two companies focusing on Wayland will help a lot for its long term survival, too. Hopefully it will gain a wider community in the process so the project can stay neutral and diverse.

And we have pretty much killed HAL, though I think you mean X in that last sentence ;)

Maybe I have a very different view of what development is for something this core, because it usually doesn't include a global deployment at this much of an infancy stage.

### Post by NightwishFan on 2010-11-15
I am not involved in development, though I can surmise from comments from the developers so far that this will not be the default for a while. Also when it is, what is stopping Xorg being available just as it is now, just not as the default?

### Post by toupeiro on 2010-11-15
> **NightwishFan said:**
> I am not involved in development, though I can surmise from comments from the developers so far that this will not be the default for a while. Also when it is, what is stopping Xorg being available just as it is now, just not as the default?

Really, because there is a finite number of developers out there and *disclaimer, point of possible conjecture* GENERALLY, developers do not have to completely rewrite their application calls to make something that was written using KDE to work in Gnome.  _The user may have to load additional libraries to be sourced by the application, but the application framework, the X calls, are all the same._  There will not be this option with Wayland.  People will have to write for Wayland Natively, or else they are still writing for X, and you can't delude yourself into thinking it's a great thing to run both forever.  This will change the way all software which draws a GUI is written for Linux, which begs the question, which distro's, and what about UNIX?  How much segregation is this going to create, how much interoperability is this going to break in the long term, and the better question is, **is it worth it?**  Having seen how solid X can be, with no tearing on 3d models that are upwards of 40GB a piece, I think X has been going the right direction, making solid progress, ESPECIALLY with the development of MPX, and this is an ill-planned move that will cause more disruption than "unity." /punintended
