### Post by toupeiro on 2010-11-15
> **Mr. Picklesworth said:**
> Ah, got it. Silly me; I probably think [Bonobos]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bonobo") should be deprecated, too :b

There is absolutely nothing about 11.04 in Mark's post, though. Maybe it was misreported somewhere in an effort to write words. The closest thing to a timeframe is probably this: [http://www.markshuttleworth.com/archives/551#comment-339255](http://www.markshuttleworth.com/archives/551#comment-339255)

&#8230;which sounds like Fedora's roadmap as well: something developers can easily play with in six months, a closed loop in a year, and indefinite time after that making the thing solid before really considering it for inclusion by default.

I guess one of the areas I am confused about is the comment Mark makes where he says "It's possible to get amazing results with X, but it's extremely hard."

Well lets have a serving of perspective with that statement.  Look at the state of your GPU developers drivers and look at their roadmaps.  Only now, after YEARS of the community screaming for better support are you going to get a catalyst suite of drivers and software that comes marginally close to NVidia's unified driver development for Linux.  NVidia driver developers work closely with the x.org team to troubleshoot issues and have resolutions rolled up into either their drivers, or an x.org patch depending on the nature of the bug.  There are great working relationships there.  The Only way Wayland is going to make any ground is with the open source drivers which lag terribly far behind the vendor drivers.  Yet, somehow this approach is going to be *easier*?  I just don't follow the logic.  X may harder than Wayland to develop for, I couldn't say, but I do know that users and companies will not tolerate a setback of ten+ years in graphics evolution on Linux, and given how long it's taken the state of linux graphics to get where they are, the proprietary drivers may take that long to catch Wayland support up to X.  

If there is truly going to be an indefinite hold on making a switchover, that does make more sense.  What I read in a few places was that the cutover was slated for 2011, and as early as 11.04, and that is too soon.  There are MUCH bigger interoperability issues to worry about with this change than a fractional FPS change on a 2d window when you drag it from left to right.

---

### Post by MasterNetra on 2010-11-15
> **czr114 said:**
> What's your point with the wait and see approach? What new information is going to emerge about the general concept of Unity that might completely vindicate the concerns I posted?

I've seen this discussion continue on this forum, others, and a host of blogs, and the two sides seem to be "GNOME 2.x is sufficient and functional" and "Unity looks cool and change is good." It makes me question if certain upheavals are being made for the right reasons.

It always helps to know your market, and desktop GNU/Linux serves a distinct market. The concerns there aren't going to be the same as a 7 or OSX demo unit at Best Buy.

It'd be unfortunate to see a lot of developer man-hours go into change for the sake of change, only to see most of the base revert to GNOME 2.x by manual means after the "new" feel wears off, when there is so much on the roadmap elsewhere. It'd also be unfortunate to see GNOME 2.x support slip in future releases should it get labeled as "unimportant" by the types who have been preaching against anything seen as "old" as if it were broken, and nobody should want to use it anyway.

My point is simple wait until the darn thing comes out and try it then, if you still don't like it fine, whatever that's your preference but hating on something or in this case a version of something that's not even ready (and by ready in this case I mean pre-UI freeze and/or pre-final release) is both premature and senseless. For all you know those concerns might no longer be valid with 11.04 release. If by release they still aren't, fine leave if you want. No one's stopping you, I'm just asking to give the blasted thing as chance in its improved form before you condemn it.

### Post by toupeiro on 2010-11-17
> **zekopeko said:**
> Go actually [read the blog]("http://www.markshuttleworth.com/archives/551") before continuing to talk rubbish:



No mention of having Wayland BY DEFAULT. Only that it is going to be available for testing.

Zekopeko, seriously, take your own advice...  Go actually read my posts before trolling them.  I did read his blog after the first person linked it, I read that quote, and I think 6 months to a year before it's "widely useful" is pretty rose colored.  I don't find that realistic on the basis of the breadth of X.org's scope of oses and applications.  Yes, my initial information on what was released from a computerworld.com (*I think it was computerworld, can't remember now*) posting was wrong about Wayland replacing X as early as 11.04, and I noted that.  That said, I still believe Wayland's practical, limited, usefulness could be more visible in 4 years so people are actually writing stuff for it, but I say that with a lot of scepticism right now since practically nobody is.  I see no value of any consideration for it to be deployed before that happens unless it get a sudden surge of development and support.  That is purely my opinion based on the state of affairs today, and I backed it up with true and existing technology and resource gaps. So what makes you the appointed expert to call this rubbish?  You're still going to be running an X-server for the vast majority of your applications to be compatible for probably the next half-decade at least, so what is Wayland actually providing other than overhead?  It's ridiculous to think a project this small will ramp up in development so fast as to even be "widely useful" by his projections, considering the lack of developers, drivers, and people writing native applications for it, versus the near 3 decades of development behind X, putting aside when they decide to make it default. 

Before you go calling my posts rubbish, maybe you should consider your own sources.  High end visualisation on X is a part of what I do to pay my bills and put food on the table.  I work with it on a daily basis, and have gotten deep in the weeds with X more times than I care to admit. It doesn't mean I know everything there is to know about it,  but I think it does make me well versed enough in the subject matter to share what I feel are pretty legit gaps in a roadmap away from X. (Which, I am not opposed to if it's done right and is truly a *nix replacement as it needs to be.)  

If you want to insert something constructive instead of linking the same blog for the 4th time, I welcome it.

I don't find that realistic on the basis of the breadth of X.org's scope of oses and applications.

You're cherry picking. "Widely useful" was in a context. AFAICT Shuttleworth doesn't think that people are actually going to use Wayland in production environments but in a year the bits will be there and packaged for people to do wide testing and file bugs.

> Yes, my initial information on what was released from a computerworld.com (*I think it was computerworld, can't remember now*) posting was wrong about Wayland replacing X as early as 11.04, and I noted that.  That said, I still believe Wayland's practical, limited, usefulness could be more visible in 4 years so people are actually writing stuff for it, but I say that with a lot of scepticism right now since practically nobody is.

That's why Shuttleworth is saying that the ecosystem will start to move in 4+ years. 4 years in FOSS is a LOT of time.

Fedora is already targeting Fedora 15 for Wayland landing in the repos.

> I see no value of any consideration for it to be deployed before that happens unless it get a sudden surge of development and support.  That is purely my opinion based on the state of affairs today, and I backed it up with true and existing technology and resource gaps. So what makes you the appointed expert to call this rubbish?  You're still going to be running an X-server for the vast majority of your applications to be compatible for probably the next half-decade at least, so what is Wayland actually providing other than overhead?  It's ridiculous to think a project this small will ramp up in development so fast as to even be "widely useful" by his projections, considering the lack of developers, drivers, and people writing native applications for it, versus the near 3 decades of development behind X, putting aside when they decide to make it default.

Your skepticism is unwarranted. Intel, Nokia,Canonical and Fedora are interested in using Wayland. That is a lot of resources right there. I think that Wayland is going to land in production environments after LTS 12.04. We'll also have more powerful computers in the future so running X as a Wayland client isn't going to be a noticeable overhead. You're also ignoring the fact that existing drivers are starting to use the same technologies that Wayland is going to use so there will be a lot of code reuse.

### Post by toupeiro on 2010-11-20
> **Docaltmed said:**
> And there we have the cause of the whole *sturm und drang* in a nutshell. The guy has a knowledge base that he doesn't want to see devalued.

Can't say I blame him, but that's the way of the world, my friend. Learn Wayland and find bliss. And profits.

As I said, I have no issues learning something new, and I have no issues replacing X someday, *if* it's a true cross-platform replacement.  This is a much better example of cherry picking. You can't think of this type of a change so one-directionally, replacing X has MAJOR widespread affect.  The value of my knowledge isn't sourced in X by a very long shot.  Depreciating X won't change the value of what I do, it will just make it more difficult across platforms -- or, it will simply eliminate distributions which use Wayland from the enterprise scope until the development base has matured for it.  That is what remains to be seen. Adaptation is great, if it's really the right technology and the right time.

As for me cherry picking, Call it what you want.  Yes, I focused on that one component in that response, but I have commented on many, many others in this thread.  And in this same thread I've been accused of cherry picking AND being all over the place.  Quite the contrasting positions to take.  Everything other than the comment about enterprise support is back by true and known developmental and technology gaps.  That is not so much cherry picking than it is pointing out the painfully obvious from a development standpoint.  What it really sounds like to me is, if shuttleworth is saying it *must* be the right thing to do.  If I wanted dogmatic development, I'd invest my time, money, and skills in Apple or Microsoft..  X being used and developed by every major flavor of UNIX and Linux in the world is a cornerstone to what FOSS stands for.  Wayland can get there, but it's not there yet.  My skepticism isn't about whether or not it can get there, its about whether or not it will get there *in time for what Mark has planned.*  Because it's such a core framework of UNIX and Linux GUI's, this is something you don't necessarily want to rush, and 4 years is NOT enough time.  As I said, this is much more than just an "application." You can make Wayland as solid as stone, and if nobody is writing for it, putting drivers out for it, then its totally pointless.  Your code reusal statement will mean a lot more when it's not vapor.  Vapor != ready to replace X in 4 years.  I'm not ignoring it, but there is no vested roadmap in what you are saying from ATi and NVidia yet.  As far as Wayland changing to support existing drivers (which is much more probable), that *sounds* great, but again its not there! If it gets there in 4 years, than in 4 years it will only just be ready to truly start developing against.  That seems quite different than what is planned at present IMO.

### Post by whistlerspa on 2010-11-27

It's also frustrating that file management can't be undertaken when I open the files menu until I do an extra click on a file icon at the top right and want to copy or paste say between folders, which is made more difficult by only being able to have a single window in a single workspace. I also prefer my applications in sorted drop down menus rather than the solid mass of 'Applications' as in Unity. All in all I think that Unity is a retrograde and faddish desktop interface. I guess it's emulating cell phone menus but I don't want my computer desktops to look like cellphones.

### Post by EricDP on 2010-11-30
I think some (most) of the people posting in this thread have missed the point. Just because it's called netbook edition doesn't mean you must use it if you have a netbook. If you're using your netbook just like a full size laptop or desktop, then use the desktop edition for full functionality and customize the appearance.

The point of the netbook edition is that many people who buy netbooks do so to have a small, portable web device. They use it for casual browsing, messaging, and listening to music, and very little else. For that purpose, unity is excellent. It's much better than moblin (which is faster, but uses too much vertical space), and way better than Windows 7 Starter, which, like moblin, fails to be optimized for a small, wide screen.

Personally, I have reduced the side bar to about six things - the things I use regularly. I love the fact that my browser goes from the very bottom of the screen to the very top, with no panels on top or bottom. And I can see what apps are running because the side bar shows me with the arrows. And if a background app has a message, the icon animates... everything I need is there and visible.

It doesn't do everything. But for my purposes, it's the perfect interface for my small-screen portable device.

---

### Post by czr114 on 2010-11-30
Autohiding GNOME panels are great for the small screens of netbooks.

Since multitasking isn't as heavy on netbooks, the bottom taskbar panel can be autohidden with little readjustment frustration.

The top can also be autohidden, if you're not relying heavily on notifications or applets.

The Unity concept seems clunky. Too bad they're not ironing out performance, stability, and hardware compatibility issues, instead.

### Post by ninjaaron on 2010-12-03
Eh, I've never been a big fan of gnome. I interface with the computer using mostly Cairo-Dock, gnome-do, and the terminal. The actual gnome panel and menu interface is a real drag, and I do everything I can to get it out of the way. It looks atrocious, and it's not particularly efficient. I also keep all the icons off my desktop already, and have for years. I hate the clutter. The only thing on my desktop is the cairo-dock and a clock widget.

On the other hand, Unity, as it is in 10.10, isn't quite there yet as a desktop environment, though one can certainly get to all the same utilities and all through gnome, just takes a bit of doing, especially if you want to launch a nautilus browser, which you pretty much need to do with Unity in it's current state. It also doesn't support key-bindings very well yet, or at least I haven't found a way to add them, and I can't have that. I tend to navigate my computer more with the keyboard than the mouse, and nothing pisses me off like a system without good support for key-bindings (though I should mention that the first nine icons in the 'dock' are automatically bound, which is better than nothing). I'm also playing with Jolicloud at the moment, and it's the same story, if not worse. However, I' probably going to get a netbook with a touch-screen soon, so I want to familiarize myself with the system.

And then there is the glitchy behavior...

However, despite the shortcomings in the current version of Unity I think I "get" what Canonical is trying to do, and I have complete faith that they with do it within the next year, even if it's not fully realized by 11.4. Unity is simpler and more elegant, and I think more intuitive. The integrated search is golden. If they could get it to pop out some Google results at the bottom of the page it would be damn near perfect.

The single menu bar is also genius. It really has a positive effect on the screen economy. I really hate looking at bars everywhere, and seeing the panel stacked on top of the application title, stacked on top of the application menu, stacked on top of any tool bars drives me absolutely nuts. You're looking at half a screen of gray bars, with a little sliver of content in the middle. I want a screen full of content, not interface. I have my panel auto-hidden, and I have all my regular apps set up with the most minimalist possible interface. Ridiculous as it may sound, that's why I use Chrome instead of FF (ok, the speed might also have something to do with it). Unity gets it all on one bar. Why hasn't anyone else done this yet? Apple got the job half-done years ago, but Unity has finally perfected "bar economy." (I must say that Jolicloud does quite well in this department as well)

I'm not a huge fan of the side bar, as you might imagine after that bar rant, but it's not terrible either. I think they are really trying to get into the touch market right now, which is the right choice, and that bar is made for touching. The only thing that could make it better four touch would be the option to collapse and expand it with a button. It may even be that they are trying to produce a viable free and open platform for tablets and smart-phones, and this is the beginning of that. I'm all for it. Still in a traditional interface with keyboard and mouse/trackpad, auto-hide has to be there, especially if they are concerned with optimizing . Luckily, it already there now in the newly released alpha.

Finally, despite all my attempts. I've never had any success making gnome really beautiful in Ubuntu, though it does look pretty good in Linux Mint. Unity already looks better than Gnome ever did, and the way the developers are approaching the graphical interface with Open GL, and this sort of 'luminous glass' type look puts Ubuntu in a position to compete in looks with Aero and Aqua, with just as much flash and style, but without copying in the least. The integrated menus are a joy to use (minus the lack of context menu. WTF?).

Unity is bringing something totally new to the desktop interface, and the giants are bound to copy it (it's already in the works, for the latest version of Aqua, actually). I mean, you do have sexy linux desktops out there like vistubuntu... I mean KDE, but they aren't really pushing innovation in a big way. I really tip my hat to Canonical for having the guts to lead the way in creating a new way for users to interact with their computers that will ultimately be much more elegant and efficient, even if there are some bumps in the road right now.

Well, got to go. My disk-image of 11.04 alpha just finished downlaoding.:twisted:

---

### Post by 3Miro on 2010-12-03
With the first 11.04 Alpha available, I installed it to check out this Unity thing. There were long discussions, but only now we can see what it is all about. I made a stand alone install on its own partition on a multi-boot machine (I am still using Grub from Gentoo). The CD installer works under Gnome 2 and the first boot of the system takes you to classic Gnome 2 interface; only after I installed the proprietary Nvidia drivers did I manage to get Unity (this may differ on Intel or ATI cards). Check the screenshots attached.

The first impression is that Unity is incomplete, but that is to be expected from Alpha 1. Many things may and probably will be changed for the final version. The worst thing right now is the lack of applications menu. There are also bugs, I could neither start the Software Center (I ended up using Synaptic), nor could I get the compiz cube effect to work with Unity (compiz and Unity crashed and I had to reboot).

- The first thing that catches attention in Unity is the dock on the left. This is very similar to what you get from Mac or Windows 7 or Awn. It shows a list of open windows and you can right-click to select which ones you want to "Pin to Launcher" and use as permanent shortcuts. The last button is an applet that gives you the compiz Expo plugin (Meta + E by default on older versions). There is also the trash, but no other applets yet.

- If you have two windows open (say in Firefox) and you click on the icon, you get a view of all of them to select from, similar to the Static Application Switcher compiz plugin. Check out the second screenshot, those are zoomed out versions of the FF windows.

- The Ubuntu icon on the top left is supposed to bring up the menu, however, right now it only open Nautilus at "/usr/share/applications". I am pretty sure the menu will come later.

- To the right of the Ubuntu icon you can see the "File/Edit/View" menu for Nautilus. The menu is now not on the window, but at the top like on Mac. This works for Nautilus, Image Viewer, Gedit, but not FireFox (note the Firefox menu is at the FF window). I don't know is all applications will go the Mac way or there will be mix of the old and new ways.

- At the top right of the top panel, you get the classic Network Manager, Sound, Mail, Clock, Status applets. This looks a lot like Gnome panel, however, it is not Gnome panel. You cannot add/remove or even move the existing applets. I am sure more options will be added later.

- Unity uses compiz window manager, so you get the standard compiz effects and functionality, however, I couldn't get the cube to work (probably a bug).

- 11.04 comes with Beta version of Firefox 4 and Linux kernel 2.6.37, but those are separate topics.

The rest is the same, Totem Move Player, Termimal, Appearence and Settings and so on. I have not tried many other applications.

I have not used Gnome-shell for some time, but I wonder is Unity and GS will be that different after all.

---

### Post by whalogreg on 2010-12-03
First off, I wasn't sure where to post this, so if it must be moved I understand. Second, I don't want this to come off as a rant, but more of a search for enlightenment and explanation/an output of my opinion. 

As for what I've seen about Unity so far, it almost baffles me why a default ***desktop*** would use what seems to be a 'netbook optimized' environment. Unity seems to be a wonderful step forward for netbooks, but I find it to be more troublesome for every day use on a desktop with a normal size screen. 

The global menu is one of the things I've always disliked about Macs. Say I have a few applications open and tiled for quick access to them, ***and not maximized***, the one I want menu access to is on the right side of my desktop, I have to bring that window into focus and then travel across the screen to get to the menu. And then repeat over and over to simply access menus. It seems to be making my desktop harder to use, a worse flow.. I don't need to reserve the screen space (again, not on a netbook), so this just seems like an annoyance. 

And as far as the launcher on the left side of the screen... It's taking things formerly hidden in a menu and permanently displaying the un-needed entries on screen, and in my opinion it is just an eye sore of a window list, or better yet, (as of now) a dumbed down version of a dock, in which case, i'd rather have a choice of my own, and be able to easily theme it/customize it to match my gtk theme, or just install one I like and leave it as it came. 

I don't like the full screen lists of big icons for (again in my opinion) another poor version of a menu system if you are not on a netbook with a small screen. There are so many better ways of accessing apps and documents in a cleaner/sleeker manner.. Cardapio, Synapse (with zeitgeist! yay), Docky, Cairo Dock, AWN all take care of what needs to be done here (and what the launcher does), and are easier and more efficient in my opinion, not to mention they hide and take up LESS screen space. And are all ***easily installed*** and more importantly ***easily customizable***. 

Other concerns are the panel and compiz (not a bling issue for me, more utility). I list these as concerns, because I don't know if these issues are planned to be taken care of or if they even are issues at this point.. 

To my understanding, you cannot add indicators or other applets/launchers to the panel... huge downside for me. And now that unity is a compiz plugin, I'm not as concerned, but still hope that the utility and window management will remain fairly similar to the way they are now..

Now, I know I can still use the normal Gnome desktop environment if I choose, but my concern is, that now that Unity will be the default desktop environment, Gnome will most likely no longer be maintained and developed by Ubuntu, so is my current desktop where the line ends for me if I don't like Unity?

Unity is cool... yes. But seems (in my opinion) to be a regression as a non-netbook desktop..

Am I just being suborn?

---

### Post by mpt on 2010-12-03
> **whalogreg said:**
> The global menu is one of the things I've always disliked about Macs. Say I have a few applications open and tiled for quick access to them, ***and not maximized***, the one I want menu access to is on the right side of my desktop, I have to bring that window into focus and then travel across the screen to get to the menu. And then repeat over and over to simply access menus. It seems to be making my desktop harder to use, a worse flow.. I don't need to reserve the screen space (again, not on a netbook), so this just seems like an annoyance.

In [a message on the Ayatana mailing list last month]("https://lists.launchpad.net/ayatana/msg04064.html") I considered what happens when you take the global menu bar to a larger screen:

> So, what’s relevantly different for notebook and desktop computers, compared to netbook computers? Two things: they have a larger screen, and they’re more powerful.

That larger screen slows down the action of travelling to a menu. But it doesn’t slow that action down five-fold. It doesn’t slow it down nearly as much as putting menus inside the window would.

The greater power means you’re more likely to be running multiple programs, which makes a global menu bar both more and less compelling than on a netbook. It’s less compelling in that you’re more likely to have to click in a window before using its menus. It’s more compelling in that it avoids the problem Microsoft discovered where people were confusing menus from background windows with menus in the active window.

I’m not really involved in the design of the launcher or the Dash, so I’ll leave that to the people working on those parts.

> **whalogreg said:**
> To my understanding, you cannot add indicators or other applets/launchers to the panel... huge downside for me.

Whether a program provides its own status menu is up to the individual program. See [Indicator-Weather]("https://launchpad.net/weather-indicator"), for example.

> *And now that unity is a compiz plugin, I'm not as concerned, but still hope that the utility and window management will remain fairly similar to the way they are now..*

What do you mean by “utility management”?

> *Now, I know I can still use the normal Gnome desktop environment if I choose, but my concern is, that now that Unity will be the default desktop environment, Gnome will most likely no longer be maintained and developed by Ubuntu, so is my current desktop where the line ends for me if I don't like Unity?*

Ubuntu is continuing to use Gnome. And Gnome 2 will be available as [the 2-D fallback in 11.04]("https://blueprints.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+spec/other-dx-n-2d-experience-fallback") for those who can’t run Unity.

### Post by Sophont on 2010-12-04
> **whalogreg said:**
> 
As for what I've seen about Unity so far, it almost baffles me why a default ***desktop*** would use what seems to be a 'netbook optimized' environment. Unity seems to be a wonderful step forward for netbooks, but I find it to be more troublesome for every day use on a desktop with a normal size screen. 

The global menu is one of the things I've always disliked about Macs.

You are not alone. Exactly my thoughts.
I'm fine with Unity and it's global menu on Netbooks. Small screen - mostly only 1 app active and that one will be maximized.

On my big screen Laptop/Desktop I always have several apps open - rarely is even one of them maximized and the global menu is a PITA in this environment. And - yes - it's to me the most annoying thing when I sit in front of a Mac.


> **rg4w said:**
> 
It actually takes measurably less time and effort to access a menu when the menu bar is detached from the window as long as it's against an edge of the monitor:
[http://www.asktog.com/columns/022DesignedToGiveFitts.html](http://www.asktog.com/columns/022DesignedToGiveFitts.html)

I knew somebody would bring up Fitts Law.

Perfect App applying Fitts Law: One Big Button that covers all the screen. Can't beat the time to hit hat. Very easy to hit - even with no mouse movement at all.

There was never a noticable difference to me to either hit a button or menu on a (semi-)random place or just have it on a global menu.
But having to click another app first before I use it's menu - that I notice every time. Plus I can see it before I even start moving the mouse.

Apple is very stubborn about their early design mistakes and tries to make them look like the greatest idea ever. And Apple fans always find apologies for those decisions.
That's why Apple users had to replace silly 1 button mice for over a decade. Having a second mouse button instead of hold key and click on mouse was always a better idea to get to context menus.
And on a big screen it's better to have the menu with the app it belongs to instead of ripped off and put someplace else - which then totally changes depending upon which app is active.

Let's rip out the toolbars too and put them on a sidebar - surely better according to Fitts Law.
Status Bar - can't have that on the app itself - let's have a global status bar on the bottom. While we're at it - there's still that title bar on the app - surely there must be a better place than the app it belongs to, to place it instead.

I can see why those responsible for these decisions want a consistent desktop - from Netbooks to Workstations. But there *are* different restraints on these various kinds of machines. And what makes sense on one can be bad on the other.

Please - just give me an option to keep my menus on my apps - thanks. I already have a script for fixing gconf settings so I get the update icon instead of the UpdateManager jumping in my face and restore the editable path to nautilus. One more setting to keep sensible menus won't kill me. But if global menu  becomes not only the default, but the only way - I'd have to look for another distribution. Which would be sad after being happy with Ubuntu for half a decade (moving the window icons left didn't bother me and I loved the move to PulseAudio from the beginning). Also I have to find one that doesn't do Gnome Shell either. Sigh.

And to the designers behind this - just get a Mac. ;-)

### Post by idi0tf0wl on 2010-12-04
Here's a thought:

I've decided (after a bit playing with it just now) that one of the main reasons I can't get into the side dock on a real machine is it's size. I run an awn dock on the left of my screen, running up from the bottom and changing its height dynamically. It's fixed to the bottom, with the trash there in the bottom left (I swear, each new Ubuntu copies my latest desktop layout so closely...even the damn terminal just above!), but with a much more manageable feel than pretty much the same thing with Unity. I enjoy the fun colors and animations, but the translation (esp the scale!) is simply off for large screens... like the translation of the application launcher to better hardware. One a netbook, it all feels tight and punchy, but on a desktop or on a laptop proper it feels like a toy.

When I have the time, I'm gonna jump around with the Natty alpha and see if there's anything being worked on in this regard. GNOME is great and robust, but it is sort of a misnomer, its footprint being as far from petite as it is.

### Post by BuddyThirteen on 2010-12-04
So some people are like "The devs did studies, so we should trust them. They know what they're doing." Even when half the actual users are saying loudly that this is terrible.

No, I don't know that I'm not in the minority, but except for a few people in this thread who are very trusting of whatever the developers do, and some reviews on sites that praise how nice Unity is for netbooks, nobody seems to want this on their desktops. So it's a comparatively vocal minority.

I like my screen clean, I don't want that ugly thing on the side. Does it auto-hide? Probably, but it seems so clunky and unnecessary. What is it, really? A launcher and window switcher? I have the apps I use the most already pinned to the top panel, and I have the taskbar to switch windows. I find that usable and nice. Why would I want to implement something that makes that task less intuitive in favor of eye candy? (Well, eye candy for some--like I said, I think it's ugly.)

I have the apps I use the most already pinned to the top panel, and I have the taskbar to switch windows. I find that usable and nice. Why would I want to implement something that makes that task less intuitive in favor of eye candy? (Well, eye candy for some--like I said, I think it's ugly.)

I've been keeping tabs on this thread since its first post, since then i found no reason to reply until you. Your post is full of "i want" "i don't want" you you you. 

Still waiting to see your stats on how many people hate Unity by the way.

To Matthew:

> **mpt said:**
> In [a message on the Ayatana mailing list last month]("https://lists.launchpad.net/ayatana/msg04064.html") I considered what happens when you take the global menu bar to a larger screen:



I’m not really involved in the design of the launcher or the Dash, so I’ll leave that to the people working on those parts.



Whether a program provides its own status menu is up to the individual program. See [Indicator-Weather]("https://launchpad.net/weather-indicator"), for example.



What do you mean by “utility management”?



Ubuntu is continuing to use Gnome. And Gnome 2 will be available as [the 2-D fallback in 11.04]("https://blueprints.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+spec/other-dx-n-2d-experience-fallback") for those who can’t run Unity.

In Mark's quote, it mentions Microsoft's discovery of confusing different window's menus. I personally have never had this problem, and have never once heard of someone complaining about this on this forum, or anywhere else. And I also don't understand the part of the quote which states 

"That larger screen slows down the action of travelling to a menu. But it doesn't slow that action down five-fold. It doesn't slow it down nearly as much as putting menus inside the window would." 

I'm not questioning it, I just don't understand how it slows it down putting as putting menus inside the window.

As for indicators in the panel, I was talking more about putting custom launchers and adding things such as Cardapio to it, not indicators ***from*** other programs.

And regarding the "utility" and "window management", I was referring to plugins such as 'scale' and 'ring switcher' which I'm sure will not be an issue now, so no point in going on about them.

And to clarify about the choice of default environment, my concern lies not in the ability to choose, but the fact that (as in KDE) it not being the default environment, it will not receive as much development and attention, and will loose out on much. I understand that Gnome is going in a different direction anyway, so this is not only an Ubuntu concern, just a concern in general.

To lucazade:

I'm sorry if this came off as (or turned into) a rant thread. I stated in the initial post, that it was not intended as such. I was just looking for clarification on things, and to voice my opinion on them. I don't want this to make me seem stubborn or self-righteous. It was an honest quest for information on why the change when some things seemed TO ME, to be a step backward. I feel that I am not hindering developers from doing their jobs, just inquiring and voicing an opinion. I cannot provide a study, I understand, and am not demanding any change. I saw you also said it is a 'simplistic approach to a complicated problem'. I'm not criticizing, but what is this problem(s)? I genuinely don't know what it is, at this point, that made Canonical choose to go in this direction. Again, I'm not intending to rant, I just wanted to have some insight on these decisions, that is all..

### Post by rg4w on 2010-12-04
> **whalogreg said:**
> And I also don't understand the part of the quote which states 

"That larger screen slows down the action of travelling to a menu. But it doesn't slow that action down five-fold. It doesn't slow it down nearly as much as putting menus inside the window would." 

I'm not questioning it, I just don't understand how it slows it down putting as putting menus inside the window.
Fitt's Law:
[http://www.asktog.com/columns/022DesignedToGiveFitts.html](http://www.asktog.com/columns/022DesignedToGiveFitts.html)

Roughly: "The time to acquire a target is inversely proportional to the size of the target and the distance to it."

So yes, because menus are farther away at the top of the monitor they do take more time.  But because they have the natural backstop of the monitor at their top their vertical size is effectively infinite, since you don't have to slow down to avoid overshooting.

---

### Post by rg4w on 2010-12-04
> **Sophont said:**
> There was never a noticable difference to me to either hit a button or menu on a (semi-)random place or just have it on a global menu.
But having to click another app first before I use it's menu - that I notice every time. Plus I can see it before I even start moving the mouse.
I can appreciate that, but the benefit of having menus available in windows which aren't in front is limited to layouts in which you arrange your windows tiled to keep their menu bar visible.  In many arrangements the frontmost will window will obscure the menu bar in any background window requiring an extra action anyway, whether it's sliding the top window over to access the menus of a bottom window, or clicking the bottom window to bring it to the front.

> Please - just give me an option to keep my menus on my apps - thanks.
It's my understanding that Gnome will still be available in Ubuntu to those who prefer it.

And if the whole thing's a deal-breaker, it's not like Ubuntu is the world's only Linux.

### Post by madjr on 2010-12-04
this thread is pretty pointless.

This is not like when **Vista** where you DIDN'T have a choice.

Right now both the **classic** and unity are included.

going back to the classic is as easy as choosing "*classic desktop*" on the *login screen* or turning off the compiz plugin.

The main reason behind unity is to reduce the fragmentation of the interface between ubuntu light, netbook and desktop.

It will be default, but you can change it back to w/e you want.

Even the buttons on the left were a baby step to unity. It was not to copy mac, but for a seamless transition between normal size and maximize windows controls to the panel.

So it was either unity or gnome-shell.

This is a step forward for ubuntu to differentiate itself, compete with the top players and bring linux to the masses, like android is already doing in the smartphone department.

### Post by mpt on 2010-12-04
> **Sophont said:**
> I knew somebody would bring up Fitts Law.

Perfect App applying Fitts Law: One Big Button that covers all the screen. Can't beat the time to hit hat. Very easy to hit - even with no mouse movement at all.

Slippery slope fallacy.

> *There was never a noticable difference to me to either hit a button or menu on a (semi-)random place or just have it on a global menu.*

It&#8217;s always less noticable to the person whose brain is occupied in trying to acquire the target, and more noticable to the person timing them.

> [i]Apple is very stubborn about their early design mistakes and tries to make them look like the greatest idea ever. And Apple fans always find apologies for those decisions.
That's why Apple users had to replace silly 1 button mice for over a decade.[/i]

This is not the Apple Forum and I am not a fan, but both your premise and your conclusion are incorrect.

To demonstrate that the premise is incorrect, an assortment of examples spread over 25 years: Apple added arrow keys to Mac keyboards, replaced the derided hockeypuck mouse design, killed off the Cube form factor, introduced the possibility of closing windows without bringing them to the front, made it possible to interleave windows from different applications, abandoned brushed metal, and in Lion will apparently replace the old zoom box in title bars.

I know your conclusion is incorrect because for three years I worked in Internet cafes, where I often had to tell customers, &#8220;Oh, it&#8217;s not working because you&#8217;re using the right mouse button. Remember, use the left mouse button for everything.&#8221;

> *Having a second mouse button instead of hold key and click on mouse was always a better idea to get to context menus.*

Not that it&#8217;s relevant to this discussion, but a second mouse button, if you had one, always *did* open any context menus.

> *And on a big screen it's better to have the menu with the app it belongs to instead of ripped off and put someplace else - which then totally changes depending upon which app is active.*

Why is it better?

> *Let's rip out the toolbars too and put them on a sidebar - surely better according to Fitts Law.*

Yes it is, but only if (a) there is only one toolbar, (b) it needs to be used frequently, (c) there are few enough buttons that they fit on the side of the screen, and (d) none of them require a horizontal orientation (like a font menu, for example). For example, with a tall enough screen, it&#8217;s possible to make Photoshop&#8217;s palette faster to use by making it a single column down the side of the screen.

> *Status Bar - can't have that on the app itself - let's have a global status bar on the bottom.*

Why?

> *While we're at it - there's still that title bar on the app - surely there must be a better place than the app it belongs to, to place it instead.*

Why?

> *I can see why those responsible for these decisions want a consistent desktop - from Netbooks to Workstations. But there *are* different restraints on these various kinds of machines. And what makes sense on one can be bad on the other.*

That&#8217;s true. If Unity doesn&#8217;t adapt appropriately to different screen sizes, it will fail. That doesn&#8217;t necessarily mean that the menu bar is one of the things that should change.

> **dino99 said:**
> - its too early for real installation: only work with Humanity right now (will see at end of Alpha cycle for better usability)

Humanity? Do you mean the icon theme? Or what are you talking about?

> *- touch screens are not so numerous yet on the market (nobody knows how big it will be )*

Unity is not designed for touch screens. If it was, it wouldn&#8217;t have menus *at all*, regardless of where they were placed.

### Post by mpt on 2010-12-04
> **whalogreg said:**
> In Mark's quote, it mentions Microsoft's discovery of confusing different window's menus. I personally have never had this problem, and have never once heard of someone complaining about this on this forum, or anywhere else.

By &#8220;I&#8221; I meant me, not Mark.

In Windows 95, the menu bar in an unfocused window looked exactly the same as the menu bar in the focused window.

In Windows 98 and later, Microsoft changed the appearance of menu bars so that they always look disabled in background windows, even when they aren&#8217;t. No other controls had this effect, just menus.

Why? The only reason I can think of is that Microsoft discovered people were accidentally using the menu bar in background windows when they meant to use the menu bar in the foreground window.

> [i]And I also don't understand the part of the quote which states 

"That larger screen slows down the action of travelling to a menu. But it doesn't slow that action down five-fold. It doesn't slow it down nearly as much as putting menus inside the window would."[/i]

[rg4w]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10199056&postcount=46") has explained that one.

> *As for indicators in the panel, I was talking more about putting custom launchers and adding things such as Cardapio to it, not indicators [I]**from*** other programs.[/i]

Whether Cardapio integrates into Unity&#8217;s menu bar is up to the Cardapio developers.

Canonical chose the one less traveled by, and that has made all the difference, if you ask me.

Well, Gnome Shell is in the Software Center. I don't see the point in creating a separate distribution for it, since the underlying framework is exactly the same.

> That doesn't impact resentment towards the ivory towers telling people what they're supposed to like, or buzz-hungry users with bad attitudes towards anyone who is fine as is, and who has no use for the next greatest thing.
I would agree with you if the Gnome panels were being removed from the software center, but they aren't. They're available in the default install, you don't even have to look at Unity!

### Post by czr114 on 2010-12-04

### Post by NCLI on 2010-12-04
> **czr114 said:**
> The UI paradigm shift and the ongoing code development/improvement are not one in the same. We can have the latter without scrapping the classic UI. That was an available choice, which was not made due to the push to change up the UI for the hell of it.
Let me try to make this understandable with one of my(awesome) analogies :D

Imagine that you have built a house. It's a small house at first, but adequate, and quite nice. However, more people move in, and they have different requirements. Some want a bigger living room, some want a room just for eating, some want a bigger bathroom, some want an observatory, etc. 

There was no plans for all this development when you built the house, so it all ends up being a huge mess, with some parts of the house showing cracks from all the stuff that has been added.

Now, you could spend a lot of time and money on maintaining that house, adding additional support struts, and maintaining the old, crumbling parts.

Or, you could sit down with everyone, and make a good, doable plan for a new house, which would have all of the things people need in it from the beginning, and it even designed so that new rooms can easily be added without compromising the original structure.

Gnome chose the latter, have fun with your old, crumbly house.

### Post by czr114 on 2010-12-04
> **NCLI said:**
> Obviously, but why is offering a new vegetable to you for free, while still keeping the existing choices, such a bad thing?

The existing choice is no longer trendy enough for further refinement and optimization, and may eventually lose compatibility with apps dependent on a new framework, not a new UI.

> **NCLI said:**
> 
It's certainly a simplification. I have installed Ubuntu for several people, and generally, the less they understand computers, the more they like it. That's a good thing if you ask me, since us experienced users can always just install our preferred DE.


We'll get back to this point in a minute.

> **NCLI said:**
> 
```
FF4 will be better than FF3. Ext3 was a better general-purpose filesystem than ext2. Unity is simply different.
```Why do you call them better? Isn't that just your personal opinion? Unity is faster, lighter(Compiz Unity) and doesn't rely on deprecated frameworks. It also changes the UI significantly, but so does FF4, and many people rebel against that as well, because *they don't like it*. That doesn't mean it's bad, not design is for everyone, that's why we have so many different DE's.

Ext3 introduced journaling, better indexing, and increased size limits. That's a clear improvement for general purpose filesystems.

FF4 is introducing hardware acceleration where there was none. (The UI can be put back to 3, but it's not a terribly drastic change)

Those are examples of improvement coming without any rational argument for the old branch. There is a rational argument against a goofy dock, special effects, eye candy, and an abstracting menu, making the new branch subject to taste and preference in addition to raw benchmarks.

> **NCLI said:**
> 
Not sake for the sake of change, but more choices for the sake of more choices.


In this case, the choice comes at the expense of depreciation.


> **NCLI said:**
> 
Now you're just being plain stubborn. Gnome 2.0 will still be there, and if people want it, someone will maintain it.


It's being depreciated in the long run in favor of the OSX-ization of the DEs.

Once again, any benefits from changes under the hood do not require forcing a new UI paradigm.

> **NCLI said:**
> 
You may not want Unity, but that doesn't make it true for everyone.

Did I say that? Refer to several points above.

> **NCLI said:**
> 
Bull****.


Is the concept not demonstrated in those screen shots and the previews?

> **NCLI said:**
> 
Hey look, [autohide]("http://http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GwlXZr-3DRo"). What were you saying about "screenshots being enough"?


So the goofy, ****ed up dock is only visible when a mouse is over it? Genius!

GNOME panels are much slimmer by default, and can also be autohidden when every pixel counts. Your straw man autohide claim carries no weight.

Autohidden anything on the sides can be a huge nuisance, especially for multi-monitor setups. The top isn't as bad, and the bottom is rarely a problem. Why? The sides of the screen act as useful stops for the mouse cursor. Either said autohidden element snaps out immediately, causing a nuisance to stray mouse movement, or it is set to snap out after a delay, which inhibits fast access.

Believe it or not, there is a damn good reason for having a thin, fixed panel at the top to launch applications, monitor the system, and hold the tray, and a thin, autohidden panel at the rarely-used bottom to act as a taskbar.

> **NCLI said:**
> 
Source please.

When has a proliferation of eye candy and effects ever not consumed more resources than a simple, functional build?

> **NCLI said:**
> 
How is one click to access any application(After adding it to the launcher) "getting in the way of quick access"?


No, that's the problem with screwy menus. The dock suffers from a different set of usability problems.

For what you're looking for, a thin, unobtrusive top panel, devoid of fancy effects, is also a highly effective launcher for fast access.

Rather than adding in support for workspace-specific panel options in G2, the entire interface is being gutted.

> **NCLI said:**
> 
No, but you do have to drive it to know its handling, seat comfort, noise level, etc.

You're taking the analogy too far. The point is that certain glaring problems are sufficient to disqualify something from consideration without needing to evaluate minutiae.

> **NCLI said:**
> 
No it isn't, it's a fact.


No, it's a straw man. You're minimizing real issues with this course of development while simultaneously implying that a depreciated branch can be run in perpetuity with no ill effects.

The opportunity cost of the new UI was tangible, meaningful improvements in the current branch.

> **NCLI said:**
> 
So can Unity, your point being?

The point was further down, but I'll spell it out here: just because a user can still obtain and build G2 doesn't mean that the user won't be troubled for having been the opportunity cost of the OSX-ization of the interface.

> **NCLI said:**
> 
I would agree with you if the Gnome panels were being removed from the software center, but they aren't. They're available in the default install, you don't even have to look at Unity!

They're not being removed, just fading into the long tail, as the plan stands now.

I'm hoping for a coding insurgency to fork the project.

### Post by qamelian on 2010-12-04
> **3Miro said:**
> Compiz is not part of the Gnome project, does that mean that Ubuntu has not been using Gnome for quite some time now?

The shell is a very small part of the DE. Unity is a variation of Gnome even if it is not part of the official Gnome project.
WHat you said here makes no sense, at all. Of course, compiz has nothing to do with Gnome. Gnome allows you to run any number of different window manager on top of it. Metacity is just the default WM, but you could just as easily revert to Sawfish which was the default before Metacity. The window manager is not necessarily a part of Gnome any more than the shell/GUI is. Unity not in any way, shape, or form a "variation of Gnome". It is a separate project running on top of Gnome. 

As to the shell being a very small part of the DE - try telling that to the average end user who essentially only sees the shell. From their perspective, the shell is often perceived as the entire DE. It is the part the interact with after all.

### Post by NCLI on 2010-12-04
> **czr114 said:**
> The existing choice is no longer trendy enough for further refinement and optimization, and may eventually lose compatibility with apps dependent on a new framework, not a new UI.
Not if the community keeps the old Gnome compatible with the new.

> Ext3 introduced journaling, better indexing, and increased size limits. That's a clear improvement for general purpose filesystems.
Unity will introduce better easier file and information management with Zeitgeist, a more robust replacement for gnome-panel, improved application handling and launching, and a much faster way of accessing settings and applications.

> FF4 is introducing hardware acceleration where there was none. (The UI can be put back to 3, but it's not a terribly drastic change)
This is rather ironic coming from you. The UI in Unity isn't that different either, and, wouldn't you know it, can be changed back.
> Those are examples of improvement coming without any rational argument for the old branch. There is a rational argument against a goofy dock, special effects, eye candy, and an abstracting menu, making the new branch subject to taste and preference in addition to raw benchmarks.
And those rational arguments are? 

Anyway, there are plenty of complaints about the tabs moving to the top and the refresh/stop button merging into the Awesomebar(Their name, not mine), and some have certainly gone to great lengths posting what they would say is rational arguments. Ext2 to Ext3 is an unfair comparison, since there is no UI, which is what the majority of this discussion is about.
> In this case, the choice comes at the expense of depreciation.
So did switching from Gnome 1 to 2, or from KDE 1 to 2, and 3, and 4.

In the case of KDE, KDE3 is still being kept alive by the community, which is what I think is awesome about Open Source: The community can just take over.
> It's being depreciated in the long run in favor of the OSX-ization of the DEs.
You didn't present an argument against my claim that someone will take over the maintenance if there is a demand for it.

```
Once again, any benefits from changes under the hood do not require forcing a new UI paradigm.
```
No, but it sure as hell is a good time to do it.

> Did I say that? Refer to several points above.
Yes you did, and you do.
> Is the concept not demonstrated in those screen shots and the previews?
Yes, the concept, not the feel, the execution, or the workflow.
> So the goofy, ****ed up dock is only visible when a mouse is over it? Genius!
Hello personal bias.
> GNOME panels are much slimmer by default, and can also be autohidden when every pixel counts. Your straw man autohide claim carries no weight.
Ah, but it does. You claim that Unity take up more space than Gnome 2, I've just demonstrated that it doesn't have to. Granted, the panel can't be hidden as of now, but there's no reason to think that functionality won't come, at which point Unity and Gnome 2 will take up exactly the same amount of space: None.

> Autohidden anything on the sides can be a huge nuisance, especially for multi-monitor setups. The top isn't as bad, and the bottom is rarely a problem. Why? The sides of the screen act as useful stops for the mouse cursor. Either said autohidden element snaps out immediately, causing a nuisance to stray mouse movement, or it is set to snap out after a delay, which inhibits fast access.
Did you even watch the video?? Just like Gnome Shell, the Unity dock only pops up when you mouse over the top-left corner of the screen, not the side.

Again, what were you saying about screenshots being enough?

> Believe it or not, there is a damn good reason for having a thin, fixed panel at the top to launch applications, monitor the system, and hold the tray, and a thin, autohidden panel at the rarely-used bottom to act as a taskbar.
And you're free to do that, either by continuing to use Gnome 2, or by switching to xfce.

> When has a proliferation of eye candy and effects ever not consumed more resources than a simple, functional build?
In the case of Unity, that's how it is. As opposed to you, I actually use the thing on a daily basis, and it uses less memory than Gnome 2 on my netbook.

Again, again: What did you say about screenshots being enough?

> No, that's the problem with screwy menus. The dock suffers from a different set of usability problems.
Which you have yet to outline.

> For what you're looking for, a thin, unobtrusive top panel, devoid of fancy effects, is also a highly effective launcher for fast access.
So, you combine window management, one-click application launching, and notification area in one gnome panel??

Please post a screenshot of that, it sounds incredibly messy.

> Rather than adding in support for workspace-specific panel options in G2, the entire interface is being gutted.
What do you mean by workspace-specific panel options??

> You're taking the analogy too far. The point is that certain glaring problems are sufficient to disqualify something from consideration without needing to evaluate minutiae.
Again, bull****. The analogy is fitting, how the car drives is way more important than how its dash looks.

> No, it's a straw man. You're minimizing real issues with this course of development while simultaneously implying that a depreciated branch can be run in perpetuity with no ill effects.
A deprecated branch can continue perpetually with no ill effects if a group dedicates itself to maintaining it, yes.
> The opportunity cost of the new UI was tangible, meaningful improvements in the current branch.
I can reverse this one too: The opportunity cost of maintaining the old, messy code was UI and feature stagnation.

> The point was further down, but I'll spell it out here: just because a user can still obtain and build G2 doesn't mean that the user won't be troubled for having been the opportunity cost of the OSX-ization of the interface.
It's absolutely true that Gnome 2 hasn't been receiving a lot of love lately, but there's no reason why any Gnome 2 loving developer couldn't have submitted patches independently. I wonder why byfar most of them wanted to move on....

Oh right, because it's a mess.

> I'm hoping for a coding insurgency to fork the project.
I'm not sure insurgency is the word here but yes; so do I. I quite like Gnome 2 you know.

> **czr114 said:**
> Are you daft?
Stooping rather low now, aren't we?
>  I've pointed out **several times that the substructure changes _are severable_ from the visual changes**. There was no need to force a new interface to realize those benefits, yet the devs are ensuring that said benefits only accrue to those switching UIs.
Yes, they could have just rebuilt gnome-panel for gnome 3, but A LOT of people have felt that Gnome has been stagnating, which is why they decided to design a new, fullly thought-out interface from the bottom up. Canonical disagreed with their direction, and did the same thing, but in their own way.

All the while, gnome-panel works perfectly well under gnome 3 for those who want it.

> Complicated is your term. I would call them organized and hierarchical, and fast and clean, which are both good for those applications not used enough to be made into a panel shortcut.
To open CCSM in Gnome 2, I have to click System, hover over Preferences, sort through a list of 15-10 settings managers, and click. That's two clicks, some precision mousework, and a lot of information processing by my brain, Of course, I could add a launcher to my panel, but if I were to do that for all of the applications I use regularly, it would be a mess.

In Unity, I just click on Applications in the launcher, start typing Compiz, and appears, and I click it. The computer has done all of the information processing for me. Also, if I use the application often, I can pin it to the dock, which is much better at handling multiple application launchers than the current panel, and I will be able to launch it with one click in the future.

> Bells and whistles? Bang for the buck? Nonsense.
I didn't use the words "Bells and whistles", why would you write that?? :huh:

What I mean by "Bang for your bug" is that you get more features per hour, and less time spent on figuring out the huge old codebase.

> 
No, the appropriate analogy would be that, going forward, anyone who doesn't want to farm in seclusion will be forced to like asparagus. That's a **** poor way of doing things when G2 is fine, many people have no problem with the interface, and all that eye candy is simply not needed.


> How many more times must I say depreciation?
Just as many as I must say "the community can maintain it" I'm afraid.

> False. They simply chose not to keep the clean, simple panels on the new subsystem.
Clean and simple? Compared to Unity? With Application launchers, window management, and everything in one panel, "clean and simple" sure isn't the words I would use.

I understand now that you meant for them to just rebuild the panels under Gnome3, but the current panels are Gnome3 compatible, so what are you complaining about? It's already there, they just decided to do something new as well, instead of hacking away at something representing a design paradigm the developers no longer like.
> Hopefully there will be an insurgency and fork. Too bad certain forces have chosen to waste time on glitz instead of real functions.
There are plenty of functionality improvements in both Unity and Gnome Shell, as I have pointed out earlier, but "glitz" is also important if you want to have a chance of conquering the desktop market.

> 
  ExcludingKDE, which is worse in many regards:

I'm not ignoring anything, just hoping to avoid a top-down forced diaspora.
Again, no one is forcing anyone to do ANYTHING. The developers who gave you something for free have simply decided that they don't want to work on that particular thing anymore, and want to give you something new for free, while still letting you use the old thing, WHICH YOU GOT FOR FREE TOO.
> I'll be forced to select one of those options in time. Too bad they aren't as mainstream.
It doesn't sound like you like mainstream much though.
> Which tells me you don't give a **** about how others want to use their computers when it conflicts with your supreme opinion of what a UI must be.
What's with all the curses? I do care, but I just don't want people like you holding back UI development. I especially find it incredibly arrogant of you to complain about something you get for free, and will still have, for free, and which you atre free to form a team to fork.

I've realized that you're just posting the same stuff over and over again, so until you try Unity, and work with it for at least a week, I'm not going to respond to you, as it seems to be a waste of my time.

[COLOR="Red"]**_[SIZE="4"]TO EVERYONE ELSE:[/SIZE]_**[/COLOR] Feel free to reply to whatever czr114 replies to this post, I retire from this particular argument for now.

### Post by PC_load_letter on 2010-12-04
Wow!!! Some people take their UI a bit too serious, but that's fine. I just have a few things to say about Unity as I tried it from the Live USB of Natty Alpha1 on my ancient Thinkpad T43.

**The good: **
- Looks nice, and the rendering is very snappy.
- The workspace switcher is nice, but I could always do it w/ CTRL+ALT+ arrow keys, nothing new here for me. 
- Unfortunately, as much as I want, there is nothing else to add here at this point.

**The bad:**
- How do you launch applications with this thing (ones that are not pinned)? When I click the Ubuntu logo I get a folder full of all the installed apps. This is NUTS!!!! 
In many cases I look for the app I want by searching in the category, now it seems that I have to remember all the names of the apps I'm using? I don't think this is more productive at all.
I launch the apps that I use frequently w/ Gnome-do (or Synapse), will Unity have something similar?

- There is no auto-hide option, not one that I can see, there is not even a right-click menu so far, but as I understand correctly this will be implemented.

- With the top bar now having the menu options of the active app, I guess I will not be able to autohide it, like I do now w/ Gnome-panel, right? 

For me, there is nothing that the Unity UI adds here. It actually makes things a bit more difficult.

---

### Post by Spr0k3t on 2010-12-04
My opinion on this... I have tried it on alpha1 since the release and I have to say I hate it so far.  I tried the hidden panel option but unity still dealt to intrusive.  With multiple monitors and sessions going, the global menu has me constantly hunting trying to figure out if I have the right app active to make amendable needed through the menus.  So I did some playing... removed the global-menu, turned off unity, reconfigured the menus, ahhh... no more feeling like I'm playing with a Mac.

### Post by NCLI on 2010-12-04

> **PC_load_letter said:**
> **The bad:**
- How do you launch applications with this thing (ones that are not pinned)? When I click the Ubuntu logo I get a folder full of all the installed apps. This is NUTS!!!!

In many cases I look for the app I want by searching in the category, now it seems that I have to remember all the names of the apps I'm using? I don't think this is more productive at all.
Try using Unity 10.10 to get an idea about how applications are handled.
> I launch the apps that I use frequently w/ Gnome-do (or Synapse), will Unity have something similar?
Nope, just the dock.
> - There is no auto-hide option, not one that I can see, there is not even a right-click menu so far, but as I understand correctly this will be implemented.
Right-click works for some, but not for everyone(It doesn't work for me either). Auto-hide is already there though. You just have to install Compizconfig Settings Manager from the Software Center, find the Unity plugin, and check the autohide box.
> - With the top bar now having the menu options of the active app, I guess I will not be able to autohide it, like I do now w/ Gnome-panel, right? 
Not currently, no.

This is still Alpha you know ;) Start doing serious criticism like this around beta time.

### Post by castrojo on 2010-12-04
> **PC_load_letter said:**
> 
- How do you launch applications with this thing (ones that are not pinned)? When I click the Ubuntu logo I get a folder full of all the installed apps. This is NUTS!!!!

> 
I launch the apps that I use frequently w/ Gnome-do (or Synapse), will Unity have something similar?

Basically. You'll hit the super (windows key) and there will be a search thing there that you can start looking for apps in.

> There is no auto-hide option, not one that I can see, there is not even a right-click menu so far, but as I understand correctly this will be implemented.

It's there but not turned on yet (afaik it will be on by default). Here's how to turn it on: [http://askubuntu.com/questions/9865/will-the-unity-launcher-auto-hide](http://askubuntu.com/questions/9865/will-the-unity-launcher-auto-hide)

>

It's not finished. :)

### Post by idi0tf0wl on 2010-12-04
It cracks me up reading everyone bitch about Ubuntu's "OSX-ization." Maybe y'all forgot to take notice of this phenomenon, but Ubuntu has been doing extremely well since they decided to get out of the "old white people who make charts" demographic people were starting to associate with everything non-Apple. You might have also noticed that Apple sales have been on FIRE for a good minute. People like things to be crisp, attractive, and intuitive. Apple has been charging a premium for this sort of UI (or at least the illusion of such) and making more than enough money to pay for all of Steve Jobs' fancy turtlenecks for a while...

If you think Linux is something that deserves to be looked at as something other than Linux (which, in case you're completely oblivious to the unenlightened's opinions, is as a joke), let me ask you this: What the hell exactly is the problem with making more people WANT to be running Linux? That should be glorious! You don't like it? Switch distros or fork it yourself (remember? Linux is open source!) and stay back in the dark ages and cling to your little exclusionary club of 1337 h4x0rz and go on in the night on your BBMS about the good ol' days of Linux when it was difficult to use and virtually no one did.

To me, that's not what FOSS is about. Ubuntu's just one flavor, baby. Turns out, perhaps inexplicably for some, people are finding that flavor to be increasingly delicious with each new iteration.

And there is nothing wrong with that. Thanks for the eyes.;)

### Post by thomas144 on 2010-12-05
I use a laptop, and I don't really like Unity, either. I don't really like changes in GUIs.
Here are a listing of the reasons I dislike Unity:
--No Applications fold-out menu. This menu was slow to use, but I open up all my regular programs with keyboard shortcuts. The Applications menu laid everything out in an organized manner on the once-in-a-blue-moon occurrance when I did need it.
--The menu bar is at the top of the screen instead of on top of the window. This Mac-like interface might be good for netbooks, but for someone like me with a laptop or desktop and who usually has several windows open, it's much less than ideal.
--When I have to look for a specific program that's not on the left panel, I have to click the Ubuntu logo on the top left corner of the screen, which opens up another window in which it's hard to sort through all the applications so that I can find the right one. This would take much longer to find the application I want.
--The bar on the left takes up way more screen space than the GNOME Panels.
These are all things I dislike. So, I respectfully disagree with everything Unity has to offer. It's just not my thing. At least for now, I have the option to use the conventional (or "Classic") desktop, but when they take that option out, I'll either install GNOME manually, or switch to another Linux distribution. PLEASE keep the option to log into the regular GNOME for a very long time. If you do that, I'll be satisfied.

---

### Post by 1roxtar on 2010-12-05
> **TenPlus1 said:**
> Doesn't Natty have the Gnome Classic Desktop in the GDM selection ? so you can use a standard gnome desktop instead of Unity ?

Yes.  I have been testing Natty Alpha 1 on a supported and unsupported computer.  If your computer is unable to use 3D effects and Unity, you will get a pop up telling you that it is switching to the 2D or Classic version and will do it automatically.  You will then be booted into the regular Gnome desktop.  I, for one, am looking forward to seeing how Unity develops.  Whether we all like it or not change IS coming to our Linux desktops.  

Official Gnome is moving to the Gnome 3 interface and may thereafter stop supporting the "classic" version.  I'm sure we will see forks of classic gnome sprouting up nevertheless.  Ubuntu is moving forward and Gnome is moving forward.  Nay-sayers need to stop crying about it and move forward too.  Either by accepting and adapting to the changes or by finding another distro or variant that will better suit your tastes.

---

### Post by ndefontenay on 2010-12-05
I installed Ubuntu Desktop on my computer and heard about Unity so installed it too making it Netbook remix in the process.

I loved it right away.

I'm missing a few things but I know they are due for the next release so I'm patient.

If I want gdm it's just a click away when I log in...

The concepts are great but at the moment it's a bit buggy.

Here's a list of things:

1) Netbeans and some other random applications can't be docked
2) One of my application icon stopped showing in the dock after I docked it. Leaving just a blank square that I couldn't click.
3) Browsing documents is great but somewhat limiting. For the moment, all we can do is click on said document and read it. (Workaround, I've docked the File Manager but it defeats the purpose. Note: I do the same thing in Win7 at the office)
4)The global time is missing. I love to be able to set my time to a different, pre-defined country. With family and friends around the world it's super important. (I saw drawings that made my jaw drop for the next version. Can't wait for this one :)

And that would be it I think. For the rest, browsing has become easier. When I work, I switch easily from one application to another. (Web Development)

I'm very open to what Unity is bringing to this community. The  development team has done amazing work in such a small time frame. They had to leave things behind but there's more to come.

Excited and impatient.

Microsoft doesn't give you a choice, Canonical gave you a choice from the start.

### Post by toupeiro on 2010-12-07
<Opinion>
Unity is a ridiculous perversion of desktop real-estate if you have anything larger than a 14" screen and/or a resolution at or greater than 1152x864. (God forbid dual screens.) Truthfully, I didn't even like it on my Acer One Netbook...In other words, for desktops, it's another solution in search of a problem. I will clutter my desktop with things I specifically care about, or not. Thank you. drive through.  

No, no amount of slide decks are going to change that.

I've heard a lot of Devs and read a lot of case studies, but I've yet to see one overly joyous reviews from any USERS living with unity on a one to two screen linux system and hear about all the time and troubles the interface has solved.  Nothing 5-star worthy as to make it something that should be the first look and feel every user gets.  I hear it's pretty (occasionally), and then shortly after, I hear buggy.  Where do functional benefits fit into pretty and buggy? 11.04 May be alpha, but unity is not.  This feels like more ivory tower development with a blatant disconnect with the userbase.

</Opinion>

### Post by whalogreg on 2010-12-07
> **lucazade said:**
> Global menu and dock are not invented by Apple.

[U]Amiga Workbench global menu (1985)
[/U]
[U]NeXTSTEP Dock (1989)
[/U]
[http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/it/thumb/6/62/NEXTSTEP_3.3.png/800px-NEXTSTEP_3.3.png](http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/it/thumb/6/62/NEXTSTEP_3.3.png/800px-NEXTSTEP_3.3.png)

I don't think I heard anyone claim they were **invented**
by Apple.. Mostly referring to them as 'Mac-like' because they are used by Macs, and they are the trendy thing currently (and just because it's trendy doesn't make it flawless, I have friends with Macs that don't like the global menu much at all).

Secondly, everybody is saying "what is your solution?" I think, in my observation, people have been stating their solutions. No global menu, a more traditional style menu (and by traditional I don't mean ancient, you can still be inventive with having a menu instead of icons just floating around the entire desktop), and an opt-out of the 'side-bar' launcher or customize it, instead of just auto-hiding it.. I don't think every time I want to access the menu and accidentally hit the corner I'd like to have an eye-sore take up my entire left-hand side of the screen. Again, I am taking the liberty of speaking for the group there, so I just may be wrong. It doesn't seem like these things would change unity all that much, just make it more usable as a work-station/normal desktop/multi-screen desktop. I will be fine with Unity if I have the option to switch these things, just as I have the option to do the global menu/netbook interface in general, with classic Gnome.. I think that is one of the key reasons people (or at least myself) like Linux, if you don't like it you have the option to change it. And if everyone keeps saying 'just use classic Gnome', I will, until it's not supported/developed any more, then I'd like to move onto Unity or something else **if** I have the option to make it work well and efficient enough for me. If I want to be locked into something I don't like, then why not just keep Windows on any new laptop/desktop I buy in the future, and not go through the process of installing a new OS..

### Post by lucazade on 2010-12-08
> **toupeiro said:**
> Here's a suggestion:

Stop making change for the sake of change and make it for the sake of productivity!

GNOME2 is no more maintained.

> **toupeiro said:**
> Where's the outpour of positive feedback for this UI to decide it's ready to be the primetime UI on the flagship Desktop OS from canonical?  

I'll quote Jason Smith, Docky and Unity author [OMGUbuntu]("http://www.omgubuntu.co.uk/2010/12/intellihide-added-to-unity-launcher/"))

"Things to keep in mind:

- Programs like Unity are 90% support libs, 10% interface code
- The support libs in Unity are unchanged from Maverick
- Natty is just an update to the interface code (to make it fast and nice)

In other words, I think we can bring maturity to Unity compiz much faster than we could to say Unity mutter due to not having to write a large body of underlying work as well."

"The new Unity theme is amazingly similar to some of the latest docky themes actually. We intend to move to having Unity draw itself using svg's, the same way Docky does. We couldn't use Docky because we wanted to have the full integration of the window manager. Docky being stand-alone will always be limited in what it can do for window management.

I wish I could tell you that there were plans to add additional placement options, currently there are not. I do believe that in the future, as Unity matures and we nail the defaults, we will begin to add those options. I believe this is the path good software takes, it took me many releases before Docky could be placed anywhere but the bottom of the screen. Unity isn't to that stage yet."

More than anything, I like the cloud profile approach of Jolicloud.  It's a great little OS.
