### Post by toupeiro on 2010-12-08
> **lucazade said:**
> GNOME2 is no more maintained.



I'll quote Jason Smith, Docky and Unity author [OMGUbuntu]("http://www.omgubuntu.co.uk/2010/12/intellihide-added-to-unity-launcher/"))

"Things to keep in mind:

- Programs like Unity are 90% support libs, 10% interface code
- The support libs in Unity are unchanged from Maverick
- Natty is just an update to the interface code (to make it fast and nice)

In other words, I think we can bring maturity to Unity compiz much faster than we could to say Unity mutter due to not having to write a large body of underlying work as well."

"The new Unity theme is amazingly similar to some of the latest docky themes actually. We intend to move to having Unity draw itself using svg's, the same way Docky does. We couldn't use Docky because we wanted to have the full integration of the window manager. Docky being stand-alone will always be limited in what it can do for window management.

I wish I could tell you that there were plans to add additional placement options, currently there are not. I do believe that in the future, as Unity matures and we nail the defaults, we will begin to add those options. I believe this is the path good software takes, it took me many releases before Docky could be placed anywhere but the bottom of the screen. Unity isn't to that stage yet."

OK... so will gnome suddenly be sans UI without unity?  No, it won't..  Gnome has a very sound [roadmap]("http://live.gnome.org/RoadMap") already in place with plenty of development to guarantee its milestones and end goals.

So to summarize your single quote for the outpour of support for unity, this is what I interpret: Unity has some cool functionality similar to docky, it integrates better than docky, but it also has significant roadmap work to go before its as flexible as it needs to be, but I have hope for it!!

Yep, sounds like a great time to implement it, before the roadmap is even mature...  Nice quote you selected.  This justifies and supports it's integration now, in your opinion??  :confused:

I'm confident they could bring a new interface that would meet everyone priorities and tastes. :)

You answered part of my question.  I didn't say the project has no justification.  I am saying that the integration PRIOR this currently vaporware "new interface that could meet everyones priorities and tastes" is wrong .  Unity today doesn't cut it for desktop for MANY, MANY people, not just us multi-screen folks, and unless they do an easy year+ worth of work in the next 4 months,(given that you quoted things they absolutely need to make it flexible aren't even on the shortlist) then whats the point.  His quotes all but certified Unity is not ready **now** to be used for desktop class.  It should be optional until ready, then default, like their approach with Wayland. (and I say that very generous amount of scepticism on the presumed ready timeframe for Wayland.)

### Post by Spr0k3t on 2010-12-08
> **lucazade said:**
> I hope globalmenu could be disabled to integrate menu inside application.. Is it possible to disable on Osx?

It's not possible to disable the global menu on OSX, however, you can extend it using a 3rd party tool called DejaMenu.  That's about as compatible as you can get in OSX.

### Post by dje on 2010-12-08
I have yet to have a good experience with Unity, the Maverick version was abysmal on my netbook (eeePC 701) mainly due to performance issues but also horrendous usability issues. I realise that they are improving performance for this new version but even the latest build I have many issues with.

As much as this interface can be argued to make sense on a small screen (IMO it still doesn't) it makes no sense on large screens and multiple monitor setups. The interface is jarring and clunky with the gigantic sidebar which has no text labels (big problem for me). IMO the dual slim panel setup of Gnome 2 is sleek and efficient.

The global menu is just a terrible idea, it is a usability nightmare for me. I do not want to travel across the entire screen (two with dual monitors) to get to the file menus. Honestly I cannot see any advantage to moving the file menus out of the window and onto the panel.

The indicator applets, as they are in Lucid are bearable, however I do not see why they are integrating the music controls into the sound menu. In my mind a volume menu is for controlling volume, not music playback. The new network indicator is also currently a nightmare with all left + right click options from the old version lumped into one gigantic list that fills the screen with hundreds of text labels.

The proposed menu system also worries me, if I want to start a program currently I can add a nice compact icon to my panel or navigate quickly through the compact 'Applications' menu. I don't want my screen to be taken up by huge icons and labels just to start a program.

To those saying that it doesn't matter as you can continue using Gnome 2, well yes you can for now. However, Unity is giving a glimpse of the direction Ubuntu wants to take and I, for one, do not like it. I will probably stick on Lucid until 2013 or until I see a suitable replacement distro/DE/window manager. Unfortunately for me, the decision to make Unity the default environment is one of many reasons that have recently started to push me away from Ubuntu, which is a shame as it is my first and only distro since 7.04.

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2010-12-08
> **toupeiro said:**
> <Opinion>[Snip....] for desktops, it's another solution in search of a problem. [......Snip]
</Opinion>

If by problem you mean 'having a single desktop that will scale well from small screens to large screens', then yes that is a 'problem' it solves. ;)

If you are a user that only cares about your desktop system, then maybe you don't see the point.

For some users and more so for developers, having some thing that scales well and can provide a little more consistency between different devices with different screen sizes is desirable. 

For OEMs that are looking for something for both netbooks and desktops it's probably more desirable to have a single environment that scales well, especially when it comes to the question of tech support.

> **lucazade said:**
> GNOME2 is no more maintained.

Gnome 2 will continue to be maintained for some time, there are too many applications that use it, it just won't get more than bug/security fixes.

If what you really meant to say is 'Metacity+Panel is no more maintained', that's not correct either, they will continue to be maintained, and it doesn't seem likely that either the panel or Metacity will depend on any Gnome 2 stuff. 

How long Metacity+Panel continue to be maintained is a totally separate question as to how long Gnome 2 will be maintained. Sawfish continues to be maintained after the Gnome project switched away from it as the default window manager, but there is not as much reason for Metacity to survive long term if it only gets used by those that can't run Mutter+Panel since they are visually the same and Metacity hasn't really seen any use to speak of outside of being the Gnome window manager. Much depends on how willing the developers are to continue porting between Metacity and Mutter and/or how willing people are to step forward and maintain Metacity independently of Mutter.

> **lucazade said:**
> Global menu and dock are not invented by Apple.

[U]Amiga Workbench global menu (1985)
[/U]

[U]NeXTSTEP Dock (1989)
[/U]

Don't know if I would be so quick to throw NeXT stuff out there as an example of 'not created by Apple'. Technically it's a correct statement, but NeXT was started by Jobs after he was kicked out of Apple and it was the hiring of Jobs and the NeXT developers at Apple that led to OS X.

Later, Seeker

---

### Post by simpleblue on 2010-12-08
When I walk into a computer store I can see that the laptop (and even netbook) section is much larger then the desktop pc section. I think this is because these days people want to be able to bring their computers with them. It's likely that the desktop usage as we know it will continue to decline as more and more people get laptops and netbooks. This being considered it makes sense to develop an OS that can accommodate these changes and be built with the laptop in mind. No use taking years of developing to find out that the OS is outdated.

The  [default Ubuntu 10.10 screen]("http://echoblogger.com/images/Ubuntu-Maverick-Meerkat_10.10.jpg") seems about 10 years out of date to me. I'm actually embarrassed to show my friends because it may reaffirm their belief that Linux is behind in technology. Yes, I love it, and it's quick and 'practical', but I don't think it's not what most newbies would prefer. These days most OS's use nice big icons as tabs for applications, not text with a small 25x25 logo (or whatever size it is) at the bottom of the screen. It's the big flashy animated icons and the quick sleek graphical effects that make the system look so sexy and be so fun to use. I think many people undervalue the importance of the default look of a system.

I remember when Windows first started coming out and many DOS fans resisted saying that DOS was more quick and that the keyboard shortcuts were faster, it took less memory..., and while this is true, DOS was losing ground. Would I not be correct to say that I think many of the Linux people that are upset with these changes are perhaps a little more experienced then the general user? Many of these users seem to prefer a very compact and generic look that is vastly outdated and unsuitable for newbies.

If we continue to go the route we are going with the Linux desktop (gnome 2.3xx) then we will continue to see the same results, which are not in our favor.

---

Here is a list of how Unity could be improved to be usable for me, of course I can change some of them myself (window buttons):
[LIST]
[*]Allow moving of the sidebar
[*]Allow resizing of the sidebar
[*]Allow applets to be added to panels and/or sidebar (tomboy etc.)
[*]Allow text labels on sidebar
[*]Removal of global menu or make it optional
[*]Return to nm-applet rather than the network indicator or at least clean it up
[*]Allow for the classic menu system
[*]Add previous options of panels (autohide, opacity)
[*]Window buttons on the right hand side :P
[*]Music controls return to their own indicator
[/LIST]

### Post by seeker5528 on 2010-12-09

I have a 20" LCD at home (4:3 aspect ratio) and a 17" CRT (16" viewable area) at work, using Unity in Natty.

Not really much else to say at the moment since other significant features are not implemented yet.

I was watching this episode of Vanishing Son the other day and it started out with this story.

> This old man had a horse he kept penned in a field behind his house.
One day the horse escaped.
His neighbors all came over and said "That's bad." and the old man replied "How do you know?. 
The next day the old man's horse returned bringing a bunch of wild horses with it. 
His neighbors all came over and said "That's good." and the old man replied "How do you know?".

That's kinda where Unity is at right now 'How do you know?'.

> **Merk42 said:**
> Haven't you followed the thread? Unity is terrible and wastes space with things people would interact with rather than leave all that space empty so it doesn't obstruct the cat picture on my desktop.

Well, it doesn't waste space for my cat picture, but here's a shocker, we ARE giving suggestions for ways we think it could be better.. It's space issues are more of a usability issue, but thanks for the sarcasm, that's productive... ehem.. 

In my opinion it is only awful in terms of how difficult it will make my computer to use.. 

A global menu makes me travel a long distance with my cursor (yes I gave the global menu a shot in normal Gnome), especially when I'm running both of my monitors (which is quite often). A lot of the time, to simply make it to my current window's menu, I have to lift my mouse at least one time (if not more) and re-position it and move it again, to make it all the way to the other monitor (or other side of the screen I'm on) to do a simple menu task. Now I'm taking more than twice as long to do simple tasks. And as for keyboard shortcuts, I still am taking more time initiating the menu with a keystroke, moving over between 1 and 5 or 6 times to get to the correct drop down menu, and repeating vertically. Still takes longer than a short mouse trip. And I've also heard the argument that people mistake menus of windows behind the current focused window as the menu they want to access.. I say that could easily be solved by color/saturation/grey-ing it out..

As for the menu system, at least as for what I have seen, instead of having a list-style (personally I like Cardapio) where I can simply browse in an organized manner, I have to again travel across my screen to do simple tasks. Also, if one would want to have a view of an opened window while viewing the full screen menu, it becomes near impossible.

The launcher is not as much of a usability thing, I'm not afraid to admit that. It's just a less customizable dock.. which I wouldn't mind, but (to my understanding, and correct me if I'm wrong) it pops out when you hit the top left corner. So if I do want to access the menu, and accidentally hit the corner, I have the thing popping out at me.. That is simply an aesthetic issue for me.. (I hope I'm still allowed to have opinions, I don't want anyone to think I'm entitled to anything here)

As for the panel, I'd like to be able to add things to it (again for usability, not just aesthetics) such as custom launchers and maybe even an alternate menu. I only say this because I've tried the netbook edition in Maverick and it would not let me add anything to it. I am sure this will not be the case in the long run, so I'm not too worried about it. 

So in summary, (and from looking back on the posts here) there are really only a few issues that are being addressed here. Unity has a ton of potential, just three or so usability issues for a number of us. I'm sorry that offends so many people. 

And by the way, the community is what I used to like about open source so much. I've read a lot on these forums over the past couple of years. A lot of people stating opinion, and a lot of people responding and explaining to others, why things are the way they are instead of just arguing points over and over, not to mention a certain feeling of ideas being thrown off one another to find a good outcome, instead of people being sarcastic and rude and discouraging them if they feel a certain way. So thanks everyone... :-/

---

### Post by toupeiro on 2010-12-11
> **seeker5528 said:**

For some users and more so for developers, having some thing that scales well and can provide a little more consistency between different devices with different screen sizes is desirable. 

For OEMs that are looking for something for both netbooks and desktops it's probably more desirable to have a single environment that scales well, especially when it comes to the question of tech support.

That seems to be the opinion of everyone in this thread who has actually used it **to scale.**

### Post by seeker5528 on 2010-12-11
> **toupeiro said:**
> Except, that it DOESN'T scale well, at all!  And the people at this end of the scale are the ones telling the devs and advocated that say it does, who obviously never used it at that scale, that it *really doesn't.*  And no, that is not just *my* opinion.  That seems to be the opinion of everyone in this thread who has actually used it **to scale.**

If you are looking at Unity in Maverick, that was designed specifically for netbooks and not to scale the way it works to desktop use, I could see how you might think that.

Based on what's current in Natty.

The appmenu stuff is only recommended by the Unity package, so you are not forced to use unified menus if you really want the application menus to be in the applications window, I doubt that's going to change. Not really a scaling issue anyway.

If I click an icon on the launcher for an application that has multiple open windows I get a visual representation of those windows so I can click the one I want. Doesn't seem to be any scaling issues with that idea.

If I click the pager icon, I get a visual representation of my desktops so I can choose the one I want. Good for the desktop, by default it will probably be like it is in Maverick when running on a netbook.

If I remember right from the discussions during the Maverick development cycle, apps run full screen, so that would be another scaling option where by default they do windowed mode on desktop machines and fullscreen on netbooks.

Later, Seeker

### Post by kaldor on 2010-12-14
> **samalex said:**
> Honestly I think OSX is a beautiful OS, so though I don't see many similarities between Unity and OSX (other than color scheme) I wouldn't have a problem if they favored one another.

In the past, I used to post against people saying Lucid looked like a Mac (it didn't) because, aside from buttons on left and wallpaper being purple, it didn't look like OS X.

Unity, however, does feel similar to using OS X. The Launcher (dock renamed ;) ) Works very much like OS X's dock. But, I think I like Unity more when it comes to Window management.

On OS X, your dock gets cluttered up when you minimize things; every window minimized becomes its own dock icon. This is fine for 1 or 2 windows, but when you are using Firefox, OpenOffice, Preview, IRC, Skype (and each conversation window) and others all at once... minimizing is messy.

On Unity, they all minimize into their running application's icon; no clutter.

### Post by veggen on 2010-12-14
Dunno... Unity just looks unwieldy to me. No bitching here, just an attempt of an objective look. You click on the corner of the screen, items appear on the middle! That's ok for the small screen where everything is clustered together but imagine the same on a 24" screen. Your menu will *always* appear 12" away from your pointer?! How's that good design?
I see no efficiency improvements, just eye candy targeted to Win7 and Mac lovers.
I love Linux. That means I'm ok with the command line if that's a faster way to do something. Does anyone really think I'm the right audience for this new-age eye candy? It's not more efficient (meaning it requires more clicks and mouse movement), thus I don't like it. I don't care if my box looks hip to newcomers or not. I don't care about "converting people", I'm no cultist, so spare me the "it will attract more people" argument. Leave 3D and bells&whistles to those who want them and leave default vanilla.

Let's be fair, desktop OSes today aren't at all expensive, so being free (as in beer) isn't much of an argument any longer. Being free (as in freedom) never meant much for a broad audience. The real reason so many of us chose Linux in general and Ubuntu in particular was that it didn't require beating into submission  before it becomes usable. With Ubuntu I never had the feel of fighting with the system like I did with every Windows after XP. That's why I love it. Unity on the other hand has every single characteristic that gives me this feeling. In short, it's someone else's vision of usefulness, simplicity and what not, but not mine.
Yes, I can remove Unity and install something else. In theory. In practice, I'll be too scared to try it. I'm in no way proficient enough with Ubuntu to attempt that. Plus, as many noted, can we really expect this option to exist for a long time?

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2010-12-15
> **veggen said:**
> Dunno... Unity just looks unwieldy to me. No bitching here, just an attempt of an objective look. You click on the corner of the screen, items appear on the middle! That's ok for the small screen where everything is clustered together but imagine the same on a 24" screen. Your menu will *always* appear 12" away from your pointer?! 

Have you used it?
And if so did you use the version in Natty or the one in Maverick?

What are you clicking on in the corner that causes something to open in the middle of the screen?

In Natty, on a larger screen, new windows show in the upper left unless there is already an open window, in which case it will either be placed in an area that is not covered or placed overlapping but offset from the already open windows.

Dialog boxes may appear in the center of the screen, but that is a whole different thing, how often do you have to go directly from a dialog box to a menu?

Is the sensitivity/acceleration on your mouse set so low that it takes *that* long to move the mouse cursor half way across the screen?

I understand if you as an individual don't like it, but it doesn't really seem all that unwieldy to me.

For my usage, my individual open windows are usually taking up a big chunk of the screen anyway, so the menus are usually only going to be 2, 3, 4 inches away from the top of the screen anyway and in spite of the fact that the menus may be farther away I find it to be about the same or quicker to target the menu I want with the unified menu than when they are in the application window.

OK there is the case where you may want to click on the menu of an application that is not in the foreground, but for me personally that is not something I intentionally do that often. More of a personal preference thing than a usability thing.

Later, Seeker

### Post by seeker5528 on 2010-12-16
> **veggen said:**
> Natty, pre alpha. When you click the applications menu (in the top left corner by default), the available items appear in the middle of the screen. I found [this]("http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tXVJWWm4juY") YouTube video demonstrating exactly what I meant. Check around 0:40.

Don't know where that application menu came from, unless it was from the version of Unity that was built as a Mutter plugin, and not the current stuff that is built as a Compiz plugin.

The early Compiz version of Unit didn't give you anything when you clicked the Ubuntu logo in the corner and currently when you click it it brings up a file manager pointed at '/usr/share/applications/' just so you have something until the dash gets implemented. Maybe the dash is far enough along that you get something different when you pull from the source tree and compile, currently (at least today) Unity itself is newer from the PPA.

Looks like the PPA has the necessary stuff to try the newer Unity on Maverick as well.

The Desktop-Unity is separate code from Netbook-Unity, it's being completely rewritten. So, not all features are present.

### Post by me4oslav on 2010-12-19
Ok,this is gonna be one really long post.
Gnome 2:
Great and stable desktop enviroment,which is highly customizable,sadly it bores me,cuz no matter how much I tweak it,it is always gnome-ish.
Gnome-shell:

### Post by Kalimol on 2010-12-19
Wait, why can't you switch between Unity and Gnome now? I thought they both just showed up as Session environments - all the tutorials seem to treat Unity that way.

It's a serious no-sale for me, given that I've become very attached to a Mac-style one-panel + dock layout - both set to autohide, because I don't see any sense in them taking up screen space when I'm not using them. 

I haven't used a button window switcher in ages, and I don't even use the dock for switching, most of the time - just the Ring Switcher or Scale. Both of those things are faster than *any* kind of buttons, because one is a keystroke and the other has *huge* target areas, and neither one requires any screen space at all, so I really don't get the point of that grand ugly bar on Unity's left. I do appreciate that they finally *put* it on the left, which makes a great deal of sense - when I still had always-visible panels, they were vertical - but *Christ*, even *Windows* still allows you to auto-hide the Taskbar.

Anyway, I do hope Ubuntu continues to support Gnome. It's ugly and clunky out of the box, and without the option of the Compiz Fusion plugins and Cairo-Dock (or AWN, or Docky, etc.), I would never choose it over Unity, from what I've seen. But I *do* have those options. = )

### Post by Kalimol on 2010-12-20
Yeah, I don't get that, either. I'm biased because I *need* autohide, psychologically - most people would *not* be bothered by the simple idea of a bit of *anything* hanging around all the time - and the left edge has always been the most sensible place for a panel, absolutely. I also admit that my hidden upper panel gets in the way of some things - I have to think to slow down to hit menu items and the window management buttons, if I use them at all (I'm a keyboard freak.) The combined upper panel makes a lot of sense.

The argument I'm seeing, and I fully agree with it, is that Unity makes Ubuntu special. It gives it a unique and integrated UI that can be compared and contrasted with OSX and Windows. It gives it brand identity, not in a down-your-throat advertising way, but in the sense that *the product is the brand*. I like that.

I don't *really* see myself switching from Gnome, though. Unity isn't *pretty* in the sense that I mean it - I have Cairo-Dock and a very pretty monochromatic icon pack, and it's set to stay behind, rather than purely autohide, so it looks really damned inviting and 3D sitting out there over my desktop. = ) I also don't think of application switching as something that should be taking up *any* of my screen space - I use the dock for launching, but not so much for switching. I use Alt+Tab, set to the Ring Switcher, or Scale, for which, unlike Unity's expo-style switcher, I don't have to click on anything.

I don't think any OS would ever make any major UI advances if it were left up to a vote - people don't work that way. I also don't think I'll be switching from Gnome in 11. = )

Edit: Then I tried it. I was wrong. When this is finished, with auto-hide and skins and things? I can get behind it. NBR-type features like the Applications and Places dashboards are really ... kinda nice.

Well, going back now. = )

---

### Post by chuckp on 2010-12-20
> **Harry33 said:**
> Well If you had installed Maverick, you would not have there Unity by default.
Or, do you mean the netbook edition?

Unity is default in Natty Narwhal 11.04, which will be published on April 2011.
You will have an option to use the classic gnome too (Gnome-Panel), with menus, panels, applets and all, in Natty.
And yet you will also have an option to use Gnome-Shell.
So this is freedom of choice.
Actually, I installed the desktop edition and it did install Unity as the default, and I couldn't find any way to get rid of it.  I did try installing Gnome and Gnome-panel from Synaptic, didn't do anything.

I finally wound up installing Kubuntu 10.10.  At least now I have a usable desktop.

With Unity, there is no way (that I could find) to set up additional desktops, access any program not on the desktop, or do _anything_ that wasn't enabled as an icon on the desktop.  Those functions may be there, but I'll be damned if I could find them.  Like I said before, I'm not a comp-sci major, I'm self-taught with all the hassles and pitfalls that come with it.  I'm fortunate that I have a friend who can help me sort out the issues that I don't understand.

Now that I've switched to Kubuntu, I'm not sure I'll switch back, although I _might_ give Natty a try.  As far as I'm concerned, Unity belongs in the Trash, not on the desktop.  Thumbs ***down*** on Unity from me.

### Post by Kalimol on 2010-12-20
Merk42: someone was actually complaining that Unity had broken something, and I've seen several people asking how to get rid of it. Since I posted that, though, I finally updated to Maverick and tried it out, and I certainly didn't have any problems deselecting it as the default session.

It's in a silly place now, without autohide or skins, but it's very quick and seems quite useful. I don't *really* imagine myself using it, but it's worlds better than going back to Mac or Windows, and it kind of makes Gnome feel a little ... old. I mean, there's that realization that sets in that this is what operating systems look like now, post smart phones.

Gnome Shell *is* a separate issue, although there are obviously some similarities. It'll inevitably have more freedom in terms of customization, and to be fair, it still has something very much like a panel, even if it's not a Gnome Panel, which OSX's clearly is not, so. = )

---

### Post by MZ250Supa5 on 2010-12-20
I moved from Windows XP a couple of years ago and chose Ubuntu because of the Gnome desktop, I actually like the existence of panels, (mine are set to autohide, as I don't like a cluttered desktop). I also like having the option of setting up extra workspaces, something that's long overdue in Windows. It may be that the current Gnome desktop is a bit spartan in its appearance, but I like that, though the switch from the brown default to the present purple is questionable, (though brown wasn't really appealing, but at least it wasn't blue, which is something that really puts me off other desktop environments... It's, erm, too much like another well known desktop).  As some have hinted, the desktop environment can be made look very flashy indeed, with Compiz Fusion, (not a favourite of mine, I much prefer the KISS principle, (Keep It Simple Stupid).  Much change in IT, and in general seems to be of the change for change's sake philosophy, which makes no sense at all to me. If it isn't broken, why fix it?  

What I don't need is the seemingly constant dumbing down of things that don't need dumbing down, and that's what is happening with Unity. I do see a need for a less cluttered approach for netbooks, and especially mobile phones, and I'm glad to see that Linux is being increasingly adopted in that field, (Android, and the forthcoming MeeGo), but does a mobile phone actually need a graphical interface? But then, I'm hardly with the programme, (or is that program?), as I use an eleven year old Philips C12 mobile phone, simplicity itself, and very intuitive, but then Philips got intuitive technology sussed many years ago - with my phone I never needed to even read the user's handbook!  Another poster suggested that Gnomebuntu would be a good name for a variant of our beloved OS, I suggest Dumbutu as an alternative, if somewhat facetious moniker for Unity.  

Another poster suggested that the adoption of Unity was a sneaky way of forcing us to use cloud computing, and making us pay for apps.  I already make donations for software that I try and that I like, and I'm unlikely to be dazzled by an array of shiny apps that do all sorts of weird and wonderful things, though I do like a few widgets that are available for the panel, (though that silly goldfish and the cartoon eyes do make me wonder... What use are they?) As for cloud computing, I suppose it's to be expected from the likes of Microsoft, after all they are unashamedly after money, but I have other concerns too, cloud computing offers an even greater opportunity for the corporate world/government (increasingly the same thing) to invade our privacy and control our lives even more than they do already.  Sure, I can see the the positives of this kind of development, but the minuses far outweigh the potential benefits in my opinion - maybe I'm just too cynical? 

However, I do use a few cloud type offerings, Ubuntu One being an example, very useful for sharing stuff with people, but I find the default, (and free) 2 GB of storage more than adequate, and will probably never increase my space there.

Finally, I have tried Unity on my laptop... for about two hours, and then promptly reinstalled normal 10.10 again.  It was an experience somewhat like using Viewer 2 in Second Life, and those of you familiar with that metaverse will know that serious users mostly detest Viewer 2.

I have heard some people suggest that Gnome-shell is even worse an experience than Unity, (is this possible?).  Given that all this is open source stuff, maybe some kind souls will fork Gnome 2 and carry it on?  I for one hope so, as I like the intuitiveness of Gnome, and though it's not perfect, it suits me, as I actually like to use my computer, not just be the proud owner of something flashy that I cannot do anything with because everything is hidden.

---

### Post by Finalfantasykid on 2010-12-20
Unity is looking pretty sick to be honest (except for the global menu, that should belong on a netbook only.  Thankfully I think it is fairly simple to disable this).  I am using the git Gnome Shell right now, and it is at least a lot better than it used to be, but it has a very long way before it would be able to be adopted as a mainstream desktop environment.  At the moment it is lacking many basic features that Gnome 2 has.  Worst of all is development is very slow.  I think Canonical has done more in the past 6-8 months with Unity than the Gnome developers have since Gnome Shell was first announced (2 years ago?).

Having said that, I still think that Canonical should have gone with Gnome Shell because then alot of the work they did with Unity could have gone towards Gnome Shell, which might have actually made it decent to use and better than Gnome 2.  But I think Unity is going to be pretty solid for 11.04, since from what I am seeing it is looking really sexy, and is more usable than Gnome Shell.

EDIT:  Also I should point out, that in many ways Unity is the right way for Ubuntu to go.  Ubuntu is now really unlike any other distribution, and will make it unique, not just some other Gnome distribution.

If nothing else, things like Unity help give the devs and the community new ideas and new sources for inspiration, and that can't help but be good in the long term.

> **c00lwaterz said:**
> yesterday I downloaded the netbook version of ubuntu which has unity. when i try it via virtual box it says "no driver detected for unity", then it loaded on the original interface. 

when I check and search on google, it says that it needs 3d support. my laptop is toshiba m900 with ati radeon hd 4500 series. 

I wonder why it won't work when it is designed for netbook.

Because it's designed for netbooks, not notebooks. Yours is a notebook. Netbooks usually (99% of the time) have integrated Intel graphics, which are supported out-of-the-box on Ubuntu.

> most netbook has no dedicated video card and mine has video card and why it won't work.

You need to install the proprietary ATI driver for your ATI graphics card - get an internet connection, refresh the package list and go to System > Administration > Additional Drivers (Hardware Drivers on 10.04).

---

No, sorry. I have a 2.35GHz AMD Sempron
1GB RAM (256 for graphics)
NVIDIA GeForce6600/nForce405

And Unity is really slow. Running Unity and Firefox (Minefield or 3.6) at the same time causes the system to slow to a crawl. The entire computer freezes up on a website like [http://design.canonical.com](http://design.canonical.com) (just an example, eg Facebook and GMail work properly)

In all honesty, I'd put the minimum requirements at about my system's configuration, exchanging the Sempron for a newer Atom or Neo, and the graphics, probably ION or similar. Lightweight browsers like Midori are decent, and Banshee seems to run okay.

### Post by cariboo on 2010-12-29
I really wish people would stop spreading mis-information, Unity is built on top of gnome, all your favorite gnome programs will still work. Unity is just a shell, you don't have to use it, you can choose the Classic desktop from the login screen, and get the old fashioned 2 panel interface.

Gnome 3 and Gnome-shell will not be in the repositories when Unity is released, as the devs are too busy to devote much time to getting it ready at release time. There is a ppa located[ here ]("https://launchpad.net/~ubuntu-desktop/+archive/gnome3-builds") for those that want to give Gnome 3 a try.

### Post by qamelian on 2010-12-31
As has been said over and over in other threads, Gnome is still there and is not going away. The only thing that is changing is the default GUI shell that sits on top of Gnome, and then only if your graphics processor can run it. You will still have the option of running the classic Gnome desktop and will be able to select it from the login screen.

### Post by Merk42 on 2010-12-31
> **qamelian said:**
> It isn't necessarily premature. By following Unity's development and  reading on future plans for it, you can certainly extrapolate from there  whether or not it is like to meet your needs. So far, everything I have  seen only reinforces my initial negative opinion. The more and longer I test it, the more I hate using it.I feel the same about GNOME Shell :p




Opinions aside, here are the facts:
[LIST=1]
[*]GNOME will still be in 11.04 and later, the only part that won't be by default* is the GNOME Panel UI.
[*]The GNOME Panel UI will be available optionally, by choosing it at the log in screen
[*]In the long run, the GNOME Panel UI will be discontinued in favor of GNOME Shell, this change is due to GNOME, not Ubuntu.
[*]GNOME Shell will not be available as a package in 11.04**, though you should still be able to build it from source
[/LIST]

*as long as your 3d hardware supports it
** as of this writing

---

### Post by qamelian on 2010-12-31
> **Merk42 said:**

[LIST=1]

[*]GNOME Shell will not be available as a package in 11.04**, though you should still be able to build it from source
[/LIST]

### Post by Merk42 on 2011-01-02
> **ivarn said:**
> Ok so I will now have both Unity and Gnome to choose from instead of just Gnome. That's nice I guess.
But I have a few questions about Unity.
> **ivarn said:**
> Can I disable the ugly sidebar dock and use the desktop as usual?
If you mean 'as usual' as being using the panels, then no, might as well just use the Classic Desktop
> **ivarn said:**
> Can I use panels instead of that ugly file menu at the top of the screen?
Other than the right hand side, I don't understand how the file menu relates to the panels.  If you mean you don't like the global menu aspect and instead want the menus in each window, then no you can't do that.
> **ivarn said:**
> Will Compiz still work?
As Unity **requires** Compiz, yes. Though some settings may cause a conflict, like Unity wants to make a desktop wall, so it conflicts with desktop cube.
> **ivarn said:**
> Will Nautilus still be the file manager?Simply put, Yes
> **ivarn said:**
> Also, I have the panels down on the screen so do I have to get used to having the menus at the top from now on?Again, I'm not sure what specific thing your'e talking about, nothing has moved from the bottom to the top (from default panel to default unity)
> **ivarn said:**
> And when I upgrade from 10.04 to 11.04 (if I don't do a fresh installation), will that remove gnome?
GNOME will not be removed regardless. Unity is still GNOME, it is simply the UI part of GNOME that is different. As such, no it won't remove GNOME, even if you did a fresh installation you'd still have GNOME.



Keep in mind **the panel UI is eventually going to be discontinued by GNOME** so in the long run you'll have to either use Unity, GNOME Shell, or a completely different DE (KDE, XFCE, etc).

### Post by efflandt on 2011-01-12
Unity has a vertical launcher panel to the left with some icons.  It can be set to autohide, so it does not get in the way when using other applications full screen.  And window controls and menus for (most) full screen applications end up in the top panel instead of a separate menu bar in the application.  So it really does not take up more screen real estate when used that way.  And some applications (like Hulu Desktop) can use the entire screen when maximized, without any panels at all.

You can add icons to the Unity panel for apps you use frequently by opening the app, right clicking on the Unity icon for it and checking "Keep in Launcher".  You can bring up the hidden Unity panel by moving your mouse cursor to upper left screen corner.

But if you do not want to use Unity, you can use a "classic" desktop that looks just like Maverick.

If you try Natty, don't expect everything to work perfectly yet.  It is not even alpha2 yet and is months away from release.  Although, I have been using it daily with usually nothing more dramatic than intermittent appearance glitches.

### Post by jerrylamos on 2011-01-14
> **Temüjin said:**
> IMO, the classic gnome desktop with two horizontal panels (or a KDE double-wide taskbar) isn't good for widescreen use. It takes up too much valuable vertical real estate. I'm not sure why you're saying you've got a "WIDE" screen and then complaining about a design that saves your more limited vertical space.

I don't have any use for the bottom gnome panel so I delete it.  That means losing only one line at the top.  Only thing I occasionally use there is the trash icon which I add to the top panel.  I switch between windows with Alt-Tab.

Sometimes I use tiny core, which has a unity like set of thumbnails at the bottom which squirm, squiggle, and enlarge and show title when the cursor passes over.  Reminds me of Apple.  When I start internet swiftfox I can select the whole screen.  That is, the entire screen.  My applications can seamlessly overlay the thumbnails.  

Alt-Tab and a drop down window appears so I can select other applications, system functions, etc.  Proof positive, no permanent panels needed at all.

So far at Alpha 1 Unity takes a lot of real estate out of the left side of the screen regardless.  I don't have it up at the moment because it will never run on two of my four test pc's because of some complaint about hardware not capable. My Thinkpad T40 should run it but Unity can't even start without crashing.  

Unity will run on the fourth pc for a few minutes before Compiz crashes.  Do note, Natty Compiz crashes on that pc in both gnome and Unity.  There's a new login option "classic" with "no effects" which does not use Compiz.  Runs fine for hours.

Tiny core doesn't do shading or exploding selections like Unity.  From my user standpoint, tiny core runs on all the pc's and has an Apple like squirmy enlarging set of symbols with appearing/disappearing titles.  Unity won't run by design on two of them for lack of ability to do shading and explosions.  On the other two which have the hardware there are Alpha type bugs which may or may not get fixed by release time.

?

Jerry

### Post by psimonsen on 2011-01-15
> **dino99 said:**

As I understand plymouth brings you a nice looking boot up at the expense of foregoing native graphics support once you are up and running.

The point I was trying to make was that Canonical in my view should focus on getting an overall attractive platform (eg. the apps). 

Concerning unity the common understanding is that a) unity will be the new default and b) it doesn't bring any goodness. I am seriously afraid that it is a very bad move to default to unity in 11.04 or any other subsequent release for that matter. I know there are alternatives, but any new prospective novice user will be stuck with unity, which in my view will reflect badly on both Ubuntu but also on Linux as such, since Ubuntu has the market and mindshare it does. For that very reason I hope Canonical changes it's mind on unity.

### Post by Maximus559 on 2011-01-28
They say not to look a gift horse in the mouth. However, given that Ubuntu is largely a community-driven project, I feel like I should speak up on this issue.

With the release of Ubuntu 11.04 around the corner, I'm getting more and more uncertain about the future of this distribution. Specifically, I'm concerned about Canonical's choice to replace Gnome with Unity as the default window manager. To be fair, I've only been using Ubuntu since 9.10 came out. However, in retrospect, I'm glad I found Ubuntu when I did. If I was a Windows user looking to try Ubuntu today, and I found some screenshots of 11.04, I would probably lose interest very quickly.

The problem is Unity. Wacky color schemes, window controls on the left, universal menu bar, app launcher that you can't get rid of... all these things would be enough to scare me away. Of course, as a current user, I know that there are other window manager options for Ubuntu. If and when Unity becomes the default, Kubuntu and Xubuntu will probably be joined by Gnubuntu. However, people who are new to the Linux world don't have this knowledge. They just see what comes with the distro by default, and if they don't like what they see, they won't try it.

Besides what I've already mentioned, these are my complaints when it comes to Unity. First of all, it sure looks like Canonical is trying to pander to current Mac users. By doing that, they are alienating 90% of the computer-using public and targeting the 5% that is least likely to switch to Linux. Not the smartest move if you want to gain mainstream acceptance. Secondly, Unity also looks like it is designed for tablets. That's great, except I'm not using a tablet. I don't want my computer to look and behave like a tablet. I want it to look and behave like a computer.

Now, if Unity was offered as another option for tablet users, I'd be all for that. The more options, the better. Just please, *please* Canonical, don't make it the default. You need to keep recruiting Windows users, and so far Gnome has done a good job at facilitating that. Basically, my point is this: 

**Don't mess with success**.

That is what you have here, Canonical. The most successful Linux distribution of them all, if I'm not mistaken. Don't go and screw it up for some petty or arcane reason.

I doubt that anybody on the development team will read this, but if Unity does drive Ubuntu into obscurity, at least you won't be able to say you weren't warned.

---

### Post by km6xz on 2011-01-28
> **mikewhatever said:**
>  How does Ubuntu become main stream and not a niche OS for power users with their stupid power toys? How does Ubuntu differentiate itself from hundreds of other Linux distros or makes itself more attractive to OEMs?.

How about both? A nice preview of each in the install process that allows the new user to choose since they are both easily changed shells?
That would continue Ubuntu's reputation for being the distro for everyone. 
I knew nothing of Linux, other than it was for hard core geeks, until I tried the super simple install of Ubuntu and found it was even more compatible with my hardware than Windows Vista was. I saw a demo of KDE on the web and changed soon after but switched back to Gnome a few months later. If a complete Linux newbie could have no problem it is certainly feasible to cater to both power and iPad life-stlye users in the same distro by offering easy selection between the two, or 3...

I think that if given the capabilities of both user groups, if only one shell is to be included, it should be the one appealing to the least advanced users. The advanced users know they can change and probably do often anyway. A splash screen showing both during the set up with a selector would be even better.

I switched my whole office over to Ubuntu, 28 desktops and Ubuntu Server, and have never had such a reliable system. Nothing ever crashes, the server has only been turned off, for moving to a closet, once since last spring, no viruses. The office workers are certainly not digitheads, yet some have switched to KDE on their own after initially complaining that the new OS was scary because it was not Windows. Getting people who are not geeks to try it is more important than being familiar to geeks, who know very well what the package can do.
Personally, offering such a UI as Unity will help spread the acceptance of a new class, previously far outside the Linux universe, of user who just might progress quickly to being power users with the UI being up to them and their imagination.

### Post by cascade9 on 2011-01-28
In general, I agree with you Maximus559. 

The visible signs of move toward 'macness' started with 10.04 and the moving of the buttons to the left, so that there could be room for a feature that still hasnt arrived (and AFAIK wont be in 11.04 either). 

But you've got to put these things into context. Have a look at what has led to unity. 

Ubuntu was one of the most gnome-centric distros around. Gnome 3 seems to have broken the gnome release cycle, which ubuntus release cycle was based around. Then there is the general disquiet over gnome 3, which isnt just in the ubuntu world. 

Then you've got the new ubuntu contributor agreement. I'm not quite as cynical as Bradley Kuhn, and I wouldn't go anywhere near as far as to use the headline he used, but I sure think that they are leaving the door open to the possibly of open core in the future- 

[http://www.ebb.org/bkuhn/blog/2010/10/17/shuttleworth-admits-it.html](http://www.ebb.org/bkuhn/blog/2010/10/17/shuttleworth-admits-it.html)

BTW, before anybody drags up what was said by Shuttleworth at UDS ("We have absolutely no plans for any proprietary extensions to Unity") that doesnt rule it out in the future. Nobody would be buying into unity at this stage anyway... 

So they have the option for open core, a release shedule thats lost a significant part of the reasoning behind it. Add into the mix a at least one person at canonical is a fan of macs, the *ugh* success of the ipad/iphone, and the number of manufacturers who are getting into android etc,. 

Now ask yourself......is it really that suprising?

### Post by rg4w on 2011-01-28
Rather than suggesting that Ubuntu looks like one OS or another because of specific UI elements, it may be more useful to examine *why* those elements are in place.

About the only two things that could be consider "Mac-like" in Unity are the shared menu bar and the left-hand window controls.

The latter _[has been discussed before]("http://www.markshuttleworth.com/archives/333")_ so we needn't rehash it here.

As for the shared menu bar, please keep in mind that most displays these days are widescreen, and with Gnome 2's default panels being at the top and bottom they consume real estate where's it's most precious, reducing the amount of available vertical space dramatically.  Given that email, web browsing, and document editing comprise such a large part of the time people spend with their computers, reducing the useful vertical space has been a tremendous setback (though it did look kinda cool to be so ultra-widescreen <g>).

And FWIW, note that the bottom is where Apple places its Dock by default, so moving away from that to a left-side launcher is hardly "Mac-like".

But whether you leave the bottom panel in place or move it to one side, look at the total amount of screen-inches that are currently unused:  there's the top panel, then the window title bar, then the menu pane within the window, then finally any leftover space is used for the most important stuff, the window's content.  Lots of sacrifice going on there, and most of it is just blank pixels with no utility or even feedback, just unused space.

So to me it seems a big step forward to take a fresh look at how things are laid out and explore ways to make much better use of available real estate.  By combining elements into formerly-unused space, IMO Unity does this better than any option before it.

But also note that comparisons between Unity and Gnome 2 as evidence that Mr. Shuttleworth has gone on some sort of solo Mac-crazy binge overlook what the Gnome team has been up to.

Gnome Shell (Gnome 3 to some) is much closer to Unity in many ways than it is to Gnome 2.

Check it out:
[http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell/Design/](http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell/Design/)

The Ubuntu team has definitely taken a distinct path with Unity, but in some of the most salient ways Unity is no more dramatic a departure from the old Gnome than the new Gnome itself.

So far I've been very happy to see this sort of deep rethinking of the desktop experience from both teams, so valuable for moving Linux forward.

Sure, it's a gamble.  What isn't?  There's a positive relationship between risk and reward. :)

And sure, it doesn't look like Windows.  But as so many here are quick to point out, Ubuntu != Windows.

While details like left-hand window controls may superficially appear to risk slowing adoption among Windows users, I would suggest it's actually quite beneficial in that it helps manage expectations for switchers:  if Ubuntu looked too much like Windows, users would be confused every time they discover differences, leading to an ever-greater ubiquity of questions like "Why doesn't my EXE run?".

By making Ubuntu distinct, users are better oriented to expect that it will be a different experience on every level, one that I feel is much better even if it takes a little time to adjust to.

### Post by Maximus559 on 2011-01-28
[QUOTE=mikewhatever]If the current implementation of Unity is not good, what do you propose?[/QUOTE]

I propose that the current interface based on Gnome 2 is just fine and should not be changed. If the discontinuation of Gnome 2 forces a change, the new interface should look as much like the old one as possible. So yes, possibly Xfce or something similar.

[QUOTE=mikewhatever]How does Ubuntu differentiate itself...[/QUOTE]

Apple has been trying to do that for years with its quirky UI design elements. Look how that's turned out for them in terms of market share. (In the PC market, that is.)

[QUOTE=mikewhatever]...your personal petty preferences.[/QUOTE]

My preferences may be petty, but I'd wager they are also widespread. For better or for worse, human beings tend to shun things which are unfamiliar. Linux is already unfamiliar to most people. Making the UI even more foreign does not ease the process of mass acceptance.

[QUOTE=bizz101]Maybe Unity could fix bug #1.[/QUOTE]

Maybe. Like I said, I have no problem with Unity being one of the UI options for Ubuntu. What I'm afraid of is that Unity as the default might keep Windows users away, simply exacerbating bug #1.

[QUOTE=rg4w]And FWIW, note that the bottom is where Apple places its Dock by default, so moving away from that to a left-side launcher is hardly "Mac-like".[/QUOTE]

I would argue that the simple presence of something like the dock is enough to set off many users' Mac alarms. Doesn't much matter which side of the screen the launcher is placed on. If it behaves in a way that is unfamiliar to most Windows users, then there is a problem.

[QUOTE=rg4w]But as so many here are quick to point out, Ubuntu != Windows.[/QUOTE]

True, but there's no reason why Ubuntu can't be very similar to Windows in most respects. Change for the sake of change is just stupid.

> ...leading to an ever-greater ubiquity of questions like "Why doesn't my EXE run?".

With Unity, the questions may change to things like "Where's the iTunes Store?". No matter how Ubuntu is designed, somebody is always going to be confused in the beginning. The best Canonical can do is present as little confusion to as many people as possible.

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-01-28
Unity is something that Ubuntu can use to brand itself as a unique OS. If it's going to continue being *the flagship Linux distro*, the Linux that people switch to from Windows, it needs that. It's much better at managing screen space than Windows 7 and faster to navigate, and the new users who want to feel cool for using Linux are going to recognize that. 

I already *have* a left-side dock, left-hand controls, and an appindicator tray that shares space with the title bar, all in Gnome Classic with tools that are older than Unity. Like just about anyone likely to see this thread, I know what I'm doing and can tweak these things. Unity isn't about function - it's about adoption and branding. Ubuntu is still going to be an easier switch from Windows than Mac, which remember hasn't really been the case until 9.04 or so; it's also going to have an identity of its own and obvious, visible perks.

And isn't anyone else glad they've final dropped the idiotic dual panels on default? Does *anyone* still use that layout?

> Apple has been trying to do that for years with its quirky UI design  elements. Look how that's turned out for them in terms of market share.  (In the PC market, that is.)

And how well was Gnome Classic working out again?

> I would argue that the simple presence of something like the dock is  enough to set off many users' Mac alarms. Doesn't much matter which side  of the screen the launcher is placed on. If it behaves in a way that is  unfamiliar to most Windows users, then there is a problem.

Have you seen Windows 7? Gnome Classic is the only system *not* to use a dock right now.

> True, but there's no reason why Ubuntu can't be very similar to  Windows in most respects. Change for the sake of change is just stupid.

And change for the sake of efficiency, innovation, and distinction is not.

### Post by oldmankit on 2011-02-07
I thought I'd try Unity ahead of time, to see what these major changes that are going to take place look like.

I installed a package called 'unity', logged out and then logged back in, this time to Netbook Remix I think.  I haven't used it before.

It's kinda exciting, as some of the changes are things I've wanted to do with gnome anyway, but never got round to.  However, at the moment it's completely unusable for me.  I'm sure they're all solvable though.

The first major problem is speed.  I have a laptop which runs the desktop way too slowly.  Just clicking on the icon in the top left of the screen, which brings up a searchable menu, takes several seconds.  I don't want to wait for several seconds when the applications menu loads instantly in Gnome.

Which is where things got interesting.  How do I change any of the settings in Unity?  I went to appearance > visual effects, but it says this is disabled because it's using mutter.  Fine.  How to change mutter settings?  I've searched and have no idea!  I need to tone down the nice visual effects, I mean right down, so that it's as fast as possible.  Function for me is first, frills second.

The second problem is minor.  Something in Unity has hijacked (I'm using deliberately emotive language) keyboard shortcuts I use all the time, and I want to edit (actually, remove) these shortcuts. Most importantly, I use the windows key to start many applications and manage windows, but these are used to do something with the panel on the left (which I really like, by the way).

There's a lot of heat going on the net about 11.04, and I for one am glad to see some bold changes in Ubuntu.  If I end up disliking it, well dammit I'll just find another distro!  Why the need for the anger?!

### Post by cariboo on 2011-02-16
Keep in mind that the interim releases between LTS versions is where most of the experimentation is taking place. If you want mostly stable releases, stick with the LTS versions.

As far as Natty being buggy, we've had it to easy over the last few releases, they have mostly worked out of the box, with only a few hiccups. Unity is a major change and we should expect quite a few problems during the testing cycle.

Natty shouldn't be used on production systems, or even if you expect to have a system that runs smoothly for day to day usage. I see far to many people using Natty as their only working system. You should always have a backup install of the previous version, just in case things go badly.

That being said, I run Natty on three systems that I use daily, it hasn't always been a smooth ride so far, but for the main, I find Natty/Unity works well for me on well supported hardware.

### Post by Mr. Picklesworth on 2011-02-16
> **frankzen said:**
> Bugs I can deal with, but when developers email me to say they don't plan to fix any more bugs in the Intel-Video drivers, I think it's time to quit. It was suggested I deal with upstream. Guess I simply don't have the time to deal with upstream...and all the regular bugs the developers are working on.

That sounds like a pretty good idea, to me, unless these issues are specific to Ubuntu: Intel's driver problems *are* usually upstream. A distribution does not have the specialised resources to fix every driver it ships. If Intel's drivers are having problems on your hardware, you're going to find these problems consistently across distros until they are fixed upstream, or fixed in one distro and pushed upstream.

Also, please keep in mind that driver problems are really common at this point because of the latest xorg landing and all that stuff. Give it a month and it will all settle down.

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-02-16
> **frankzen said:**
> My wife's 5 year old 2.6 GHZ/1 GB MB runs Windows7 fine.

If you bought something decent 5 years ago, it's reasonable to expect it to run current stuff. 

On the Linux side, I would say as much as 8 years ago.

In both cases maybe needing a memory upgrade to *really* run decent with the current crop of stuff.

But even you *did* buy a decent computer, that doesn't necessarily mean the hardware is well supported. 

The amount of work any one distribution vendor can afford to put into working on drivers is going to be limited. For the majority of hardware if some issue isn't fixed by the people working on the driver, it's not going to get fixed, in which case the best you can hope for in a distribution is to disable feature X that triggers the issue or fallback to some other alternative that works.

Development is not going to stand still based on cheap/unfortunate/unlucky hardware purchases people made or inability/lack of desire of people to upgrade their hardware. That doesn't mean people won't have choices that allow them to run something more current while still sticking with their old hardware for whatever reason.

Later, Seeker

---

### Post by 37fleetwood on 2011-02-16
Very interesting read. I would like to add a non technical perspective.
it interests me to find that there are people who are trying to be state of the art on old equipment. it's like finding that your new Hydrogen fuel cell won't fit into the old Pinto.
The way I've always looked at it, if you want all the newest hottest stuff, you have to expect to have a system that can handle it, if you want to run older equipment sacrifices may have to be made. It's not like the older versions stop working just because they're testing a new version. I have an older limited computer that's still running 8.10.
I guess in the end, what I'm getting at is, the alternative is trying to get Windows to run at all regardless of your system, makes Linux in general, and Ubuntu in specific, amazing to me. never would I have thought there would be an os that would run as well on older equipment as Linux does. we're all a bit spoiled by what we have.
when I switched over to Ubuntu a couple years ago I considered the cost of a new video card a great deal compared to all the costs involved in buying Windows every time they had a new version out, not to mention the cost of software, and the hassle of dealing with viruses and antivirus software. just thinking about it still traumatizes me.:lol:

### Post by rebootme on 2011-02-16
I think Natty is a courageous undertaking.  It deserves all the support we can give it.  Unity is the most significant rethinking of the desktop interface in a long time.  Personally, I love it.  For those with different tastes or older hardware there's the classic desktop or Unity 2D.

Yes, it's buggy.  It's supposed to be.  It's an alpha release of a non-LTS release!  If your requirements for stability make it unsuitable fall back to 10.10 or even 10.04 LTS and wait for Natty to be released.

It is unfair and counterproductive to roast the alpha for being exactly what it is supposed to be.  Right now, Natty is for test pilots ready for frequent ejections out of a flying ball of flames.

### Post by dnewkirk on 2011-02-17
Although I completely understand your plight, it may not be quite that bad. I've found the 8000 series for nvidia to have the best driver support at the moment. One can purchase an 8400GS card for as little as about $30 at online retailers such as Newegg. These cards should be supported for a long while yet, won't break the bank, nor is it likely to require a new power supply in the computer or other components. Other than that, it really is a matter of preference as to what features you want present on your desktop-and much of this can be changed either by tweaking Ubuntu or moving to another distro as a last resort. Every distro has its directions and associated politics. The hope though is that people can become familiar enough with linux to redesign their install in the way they want it. It is linux after all :)

No, it's just an indication that your hardware is problematic, and time-consuming to support. Just Google the name of your GPU, there have been many issues with it in the past as well, and now the developers are going to move on.
> Look, I know Natty is Alpha - I know Alphas are generally full of bugs - it just naws at me when developers say "too bad buddy- deal with upstream cuz we don't want to (can't?) deal with intel-video anymore"
There are only so many developers. When one old GPU which not many people use is responsible for a lot of bugs everytime they change something, they eventually stop supporting it to develop drivers for newer, easier hardware.
> To those suggesting I fall back to earlier releases, I already run Maverick and Debian Sid on this machine.
Just stick with Maverick 'till 11.04 lands, then upgrade and use Unity 2D or Ubuntu Classic.

> **frankzen said:**
> When a decision is made to drop the Gnome3 desktop (even before it's released) and replace it with Unity, I would describe that as a "direction".
Yes, but not a direction that ups hardware requirements in comparison to what is dropped(Gnome Shell). Also, the Gnome 3 desktop isn't dropped, just Gnome Shell. Everything else is still Gnome 3.

> This whole discussion started with me asking a question about Grub2, as part of a post about my problems with Natty. It was entitled " Dropping Natty"...because at the time that's what I was planning.
Since then I have been mostly told to 1) upgrade, 2)stop complaining ..it's an Alpha, 3)some have rushed to the developers defense, and 4) and some have agreed with me, Natty is very buggy on their hardware, for whatever reason(s).
When you post a thread stating that you are disappointed with a release, you can expect them to be interested in why. When you then post the reason, you can expect them to reply to that as well. If you'd simply posted a thread titled "Getting rid of Natty?" asking: "I am planning to uninstall Natty(Too buggy for me right now) but I am wondering what will happen to the grub2 boot loader natty installed in the MBR. I guess when I install another distro on /dev/sda3 it'll be replaced/wiped out ??", everything would have been fine. 

However, your post was written in a very negative way, and people get annoyed by that, especially when talking in the development subforum.

> **frankzen said:**
> In this context it means I was planning to replace Natty with something else.....Among the other distros I run is Maverick...which the last time I checked is also Ubuntu.

The plan to replace Natty was posted in the context of a question about Grub2, because I had read a report Natty's Grub2 installation was problematic.

So, no, I am not leaving. I'll still be around (yeah I know..damn).
Maverick and Natty are two **versions** of the same **distro**(Ubuntu). Quite an important distinction. What made me reply like this was certainly the fact that I thought you were moving to another distro to solve the problem, which wouldn't make sense.

Be careful with words on the Internet ;)


> **frankzen said:**
> I ran NVidia a few years back but got sick and tired of the hassles every time the kernel updated.

Some say Radeon is a good card for Linux...others disagree and say ATI...I guess you pick one and live with it.
Radeon is made by ATi :p

### Post by Jonny87 on 2011-02-20
OK I'd like to start by saying that the following is just my opinion and my opinion alone (though I welcome others to post their opinions on the matters discussed).
Also if anyone has any user friendly solutions to any of the following please post them. I'm aware that Natty is still in development and that some things may change, I just feel like posting my opinion to date for any one trying to get an idea on whether or not they should try Natty.

I have been using Ubuntu since 9:10, so I've come quite familiar with it and have been a big fan. This may change if the current Natty is where Ubuntu are planing to head. I have just upgraded to Natty today, and my opinion is mixed.

I love the fact that Ubuntu are going for a new look and way of doing things. I believe it's exactly what they need to separate them from all the other GNOME based distros that all look much the same on their default install. I would like to continue to see a difference in future releases.

However there are a couple of minor things that bug me and that I would like to discuss. The first is the new global menu style. I love this idea and loved it when I tried the notebook edition (with unity interface) back on 10.10, so much so that I was looking for a way to get it in the GNOME desktop (couldn't find away though). What I don't like about it tough is that when you click on a category such as "Find More Apps" it drops out of the menu and opens a nautilus window with all the app launchers. The reason I'm not too fond of this way of doing things is that, to me, it seems kind of sloppy, plus it creates an extra window to close once your done selecting your app. The only way around tis seems to be to use the "Applications" launcher on the left launcher panel. Though as for the actual global menu itself, I love how it looks though I preferred it in the 10:10 notebook edition, where it took up the whole screen and changed as you clicked or typed.

The other thing that bugs me is that there seems to be no way to customize the unity panels as in shift them to a different side or the way the look (i.e make them transparent or give them another color from your current theme). And aside from removing launchers on the launcher panel on the left and side there seem to be little to noway of changing the behavior of it. This disapoints me as one of the things that made me so fond of Linux over MS Windows was the ability to customize almost everything.

Well thats my opinion so far, let me know what you think. I'm still undecided if I'll stay with the unity on natty, I'm considering the following options;

[LIST=1]
[*]Install GNOME desktop.
[*]Try Installing and trying GNOME Shell.
[*]Staying on 10:10 for a while.
[*]Possibly in future changing to Mint or Debian.
[/LIST]
For now though I'm happy to keep testing Natty on my laptop, but will be some time before I change my desktop from 10:10 (my laptop is like my testing station, my desktop is my main workstation that I can't afford to have to many issues with).

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-02-21
> **Jonny87 said:**
> I agree, I believe that for Ubuntu to be able to compete with Windows and Mac it has to be as user friendly if not more, but be unique in the sense that it doesn't look like a Windows or a Mac OS, or even most Linux distros.

I think being different just for the sake of being different does not do anything to benefit any Linux distribution, more so when you are comparing one Linux distribution to another than when comparing them to Windows or Mac.

The one big, good, reason to do things different is to make the system more usable, and I think Unity takes a good step in that direction, much more so than Gnome Shell.

I expect good things out of Gnome Shell in the future, but I think it will take multiple iterations to get there once there has been a release and the developers start getting a wider range of feedback from users on general look and feel, layout, task switching, etc... Seems like quite a few people are making judgments about Gnome Shell based on screenshots of the Activities UI, but that doesn't really tell you that much in regard to what it's actually like to use. 

I think Unity is at a pretty good place now as far as layout and the look and feel go, so it's more at a place of refining things generally speaking but the big things seem to be completing the functions the dash is expected to provide and providing system configuration stuff if that's not something that will be provided through the dash. I have not run the earlier version of Unity before it was ported to Compiz, so I don't have that as a gauge to compare where the dash was then to what we have now, but I'm not overly concerned that time is too short to get the important stuff done before Natty gets released.

Later, Seeker

---

### Post by rg4w on 2011-02-21
> **seeker5528 said:**
> The one big, good, reason to do things different is to make the system more usable, and I think Unity takes a good step in that direction....
I used to gripe here about the lack of usability research info being published about Unity, but in recent months we've begun to see some trickle out, most notably this one:

[http://design.canonical.com/2010/11/usability-testing-of-unity/](http://design.canonical.com/2010/11/usability-testing-of-unity/)

It's great to see efforts like that going into to something this important, and very encouraging to see the sober recognition there of many areas that still require significant design work.

I'd certainly love to read more articles like that, and I think it would help all of us better appreciate the process.

Unity isn't just based on hunches or Mark's personal preferences, but seems to be an earnest effort to move Linux usability forward for the 21st century.  It's far from perfect and much remains to be done for the first version in April and the more complete version in November, but it looks like it's off to a good start.

### Post by Dragonbite on 2011-02-22
I've grown happier with Evolution recently with the "Express" (email only component) version available.  IT loads a lot quicker, but is still integrated with the rest of Evolution if I want.  Make a launcher for ```
evolution --express
``` but this only works for 10.10+.  I added this to my main menu under Internet, and left the full version under Office for the times I want to see/edit my calendars.

Fooling around with Unity 2D, it is starting to grow on me.  Sad thing is the style is supposed to be good for smaller screens, but the sidebar and top bar takes up MORE space than the two panels on top & bottom!

When Ubuntu/Canoncial do drastic changes like this (Unity,UbuntuOne, etc.), I usually am hesitant on it but after a while I think it's a great move.  So now I am trying to hold off on judgement until I see how it is supposed to work and give it a try to integrate it into my current work flow.  

For example, UbuntuOne I love, and hate.  I love how it makes things easy, and the concept of it all.  What I hate is that I jump around distributions and U1 is only for Ubuntu (and Dropbox lacks some of the key features I like about U1).

So we'll see about Unity.

---

### Post by Dragonbite on 2011-02-22
> **frankzen said:**
> Bugs I can deal with, but when developers email me to say they don't plan to fix any more bugs in the Intel-Video drivers, I think it's time to quit. It was suggested I deal with upstream. Guess I simply don't have the time to deal with upstream...and all the regular bugs the developers are working on.

Yes, I have not been enamored with the response with this issue yet.

> **cariboo907 said:**
> Keep in mind that the interim releases between LTS versions is where most of the experimentation is taking place. If you want mostly stable releases, stick with the LTS versions.

If frankzen is talking about the i915/855 Intel issue, I understand his frustration.

Ubuntu 10.04 was the first one this whole issue came out where it left the system completely unresponsive and useless.  Then there were patches, which are no longer supported.  I don't know if 10.04.1 (or was is .2 which just came out?) includes a fix or not.

Ubuntu 10.10 brought some functionality, but not all. Desktop effects, and thus Unity 3D, are not available and at this stage likely to never be touched.  I am grateful for the Unity 2D but it still isn't the same.

Ubuntu 11.04 is going to have to be a "wait and see", but signs are not pointing to a positive experience so far.

The upstream, though, isn't doing anything about it. It has been up to the individual distributions to do anything about it since it showed up in Ubuntu 10.04, Fedora 12 and openSUSE 11.3!

Mind you, though, that Fedora HAS applied the "big hammer" patch and Fedora gives me full capability; desktop effects, external monitor and webcam/video.  It doesn't work for everybody (for somebody in the openSUSE forum with similar but different specs it doesn't work).

OpenSUSE 11.4 applies some patches and is kinda working but it doesn't sound like it's much better than 10.10 is at this point.
