### Post by artwich on 2011-02-22
I do not like Unity.

Reasons, you say? Here are a few just to get started:

[LIST]
[*]Maximized windows look mismatched with title bar, menu button and panel in one.
[*]Little to no visible order, esp. in "Applications".
[*]"Files & Folders" shows no immediate way to open a folder at a chosen path, or navigate to it.
[*]Little possibility to customize.
[/LIST]

Maybe I'm just too stupid to find out how to do these properly. Who knows? But even if so, it just goes to show that unity isn't self-explanatory - something which is, frankly, a must for a desktop environment.

I had high hopes in the 10.10 netbook edition, which were shattered. I had hoped the 11.04 alpha 2 (which I now use) would be better. It wasn't (and no, I don't mean because of bugs). I am now using the "classic" desktop.

Unity is, I imagine, quite nice for tasks which generally require little interaction with the desktop environment, as the latter is specifically designed to be unobtrusive. I find it hard, however to undertake say, administration tasks in unity, as, in my opinion, it simply doesn't give enough flexibility.

~Art

---

### Post by Dragonbite on 2011-02-22
You know what's funny? The sidebar and top bar actually seems to take MORE space than just a bar along the top and bottom.

The benefit to the smaller screens is that the launching buttons are larger and easier to click. This will be especially beneficial when the touchscreen tablets come around.

I've been using the 2D version for a little while now and it isn't bad overall.  I hope the final version's sidebar is a little more customizable than what I've seen so far (being able to add/move/remove icons).

The workspaces have some room to improve. I expect them to work like the virtual desktops do now, but it seems you have to go through somersaults to get something like Firefox on more than one workspace.

I may give it a try on the Cr-48 later, which is a widescreen as opposed to my current laptop which I'm using Unity 2D on.

On my desktop's widescreen monitor I haven't bothered with Unity, I just added a panel on the side and added what application launchers I want there and that works perfectly fine for me.

So I put myself down as "Neutral" because it may be like most of the things that come out with Ubuntu; at first it seems like there are negative things about it but in the end it works and is a nice benefit to using Ubuntu (such as the notification area and UbuntuOne).

### Post by pi.boy.travis on 2011-02-22
Greetings Comrades. Just thought I'd weigh in om something the FOSS Community has been ablaze with for some time now. These are simply my opinions, but I believe they are well-informed opinions.

In regard to Unity. I feel that everyone should at least give it a fair try. If Natty won't boot satisfactorily on your machine, try it with the 10.10 Netbook Remix, bearing in mind that in Natty's final incarnation it will be very different. Try to look past the bugs for a bit, and instead evaluate only the new desktop paradigm Unity represents. I think Unity is one of many signals that indicate a change in the way people think about personal computers. Ten years from now, what we think of as a "computer" will in few aspects resemble the machines we are using right now. Like any change, it will have it's difficulties. But it's going to proceed anyway, and we, as users, will likely be better off in the end. Think about the cell phone you carried five years ago. It probably was a lot different and a lot less capable than the one you use today.

I admire Canonical's decision to depart by choosing to develop Unity over implementing the GNOME Shell. Isn't that what FOSS is all about? When you disagree, diverge. Thats why GNOME (and most other projects) came to exist, branching off due to differences.

As for Unity itself, I like it so far. If it crashed less (It's in alpha, remember) and provided more customization options (I'd really like to be able to resize the icons), it would be a superb shell/desktop environment/what have you.

</speech>

### Post by aRagnis on 2011-02-23
Let's say I have open two instances on firefox. Both windows are maximized. I curently have open firefox instance #1.
Now i open gedit, wich is also maximized.
Now when I want to open up firefox instance #2:
...**In Unity** i have to first click on firefox icon on the dock. Then I have to click it again. Then i have to move my cursor all the way to the other end of my screen and click on the instance 2. = **3 clicks.**
...**In gnome** I just have to move my mouse to the bottom panel and click on firefox instance 2. = **1 click.**

As somebody said before, why do you want to make it look like a tablet?
If I would want a useless toy, I would buy an iPad.

### Post by ErikNJ on 2011-02-23
My biggest concern with Unity is that it's straying so far from upstream and using compiz instead of mutter.  For some reason, compiz gives me occasional redraw issues where some widgets look funky or are missing - mutter has yet to give me any issues.  So, in my case, unless the compiz craziness gets sorted, I will need to use "classic" gnome or stick with a distro that uses mutter.  Mutter seems to me to be a cleaner compositor than compiz.

Anyway, I will still likely give Natty another shot down the line - maybe the compiz redraw issues I see will be better sorted.

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-02-23
I don't know how to answer this poll. I briefly tested Unity early on and didn't care for it, but it was basically a half-functional mockup at that time. I now use Avant Window Navigator in a purely Unity-style layout because it really is an ideal workflow. The lack of customization in Unity is awful for me, but for the average user, it wouldn't matter, and Unity is a very attractive, distinctive, and navigable layout. 

With the lefthand panel on auto-hide, as soon as that's enabled, and the merging of the panel and menu bar on maximized apps, it's a very pixel-thrifty layout, no different from what I have now. A maximized window takes up the entire screen, and there's no vertical space wasted even with non-maximized windows, because the menu is in the panel. That's just sensible. Pixel for pixel, it's the same as having a wing panel. 

Unity is far more functional than the old two-panel layout, because it's actually designed for widescreen monitors where vertical space is at a premium. I've never left the panels on the top and bottom like that, even when I was new to Linux. It just doesn't make sense. It's not about desktops vs. netbooks - it's about aspect ratios. And of course, Unity is simply built around a newer way of thinking about navigating a PC. Things like the pinned applications, the use of Compiz's scale, and the desktop search *should* be default features.

As I said in the other thread, new users shouldn't be required to build their interfaces from the ground up. 

I love Unity, but I also wouldn't ever use it. Can I get a poll option for that? = )

---

### Post by TBABill on 2011-02-23
> **cariboo907 said:**
> As of right now, none of the restricted drivers. Nvidia or ATI/AMD work in Natty, but that isn't Ubuntu's fault, as we are waiting for the manufacturers to come up with drivers that support the latest version of Xorg.
 
Unfortunately this is the way of the world. We build something new so we need new things to support them. In that example, xorg needed to be updated to probably support newer video cards. So the move was made and all of a sudden someone had to support the new xorg with new drivers that are compatible. But the new xorg can't support the old driver. At some point those manufacturers have to make a decision *WHEN *their product will no longer be supported. They no longer make money off those aging products and supporting them indefinitely becomes financially difficult to justify.
 
I see it all the time in my line of work. Obsolescence is a fact of life and sometimes the pain is worse than other times. When it comes to our personal possessions, obsolescence is expensive and sometimes not easy to overcome. Even if it is not money some people just love their particular item and do not want to replace it regardless of whether it is obsolete or not. If it works, why change it?
 
I can feel for the OP and his concerns are justified. Moving to the latest and greatest indefinitely will always result in a piece of equipment no longer being supported. It's inevitable. At some point you are forced to decide on new hardware, a new DE, or even a new distro. Keeping up becomes impossible with older gear and Linus Torvalds has spoken about the size of the kernel at length. To keep it somewhat under control, although it keeps growing, older equipment gets dropped from support to make room for support for newer hardware.
 
The beauty of Linux lies in the ability to find distros with older kernels and software versions that can support that older hardware for years. But expecting the latest and greatest distro releases to support the same hardware indefinitely is simply unreasonable since, at some point, it will have to be dropped in favor of the newer goodies.

---

### Post by pi.boy.travis on 2011-02-23
As an experiment, I compared Unity and GNOME3 side-by-side, using the Natty daily image and the most recent GNOME shell build. I honestly think Unity is simpler. It took me a bit to get my head around Gnome shell, I think Unity is a much better implementation of a similar set of concepts, and I personally think it'll be more approachable for new users. Unity may have initially been designed for small displays and tablets, but I find it elegant to use on my 21-inch monitor, I only wish I could give it a spin on my four-display rig. . . It would be awesome if the global menu propagated for active windows across each display. [-o< I think once people get their heads around Shuttleworth's (and his new design team's) ideas, they'll warm up to the changes

### Post by handy on 2011-02-23
> **pi.boy.travis said:**
> Very, very well said.

Thanks.

I read your signature; I like Arch & Openbox & will most likely never use Unity. :)

With the range of desktop systems & window managers available to us (let alone distros, BSD system & beyond), that all really exist due to the great variety of preferences & needs that distro/BSD developers/users have, has created the situation where we the users are the beneficiaries of multiple blessings, & usually for free too.

We still have to learn how to use & configure whatever GUI front end that we have chosen to use, so that takes a little time in research & practice.

Some people see things superficially & think that what they see is the limit. Mostly we have to get out our spade if we want to see what is underneath. So often it takes next to no time & effort to find that something is exactly NOT what we thought it was. 

A quick 5 minutes of search & read will set a member of the Ubuntu dis-Unity groups mind at rest. 

I see so many threads on the topic that are mostly started by people that really don't know what they are talking about. They don't listen when they told & so many don't bother to get their hands dirty on this topic. It seems to be some kind of unified dis-unity club! :lolflag:

**The answer is that the user can very simply choose to use Unity or not when they log in.** 

How people can find that to be a problem I don't know? 

For those that really don't want Unity to reside on their system then they can delete it if they must. Though I expect that few will bother & those that feel the need to won't have any trouble following the steps required to do so, which can be found here:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10104104&postcount=1](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10104104&postcount=1)

Anyway, that's it for me & the Unity discussion. It will continue to go on & on until it finally fizzles out as the practical reality finally dawns on the vocal minority, a minority who are in reality just people chasing their tail or should it be tale? :) Because they haven't got anything better to do. Though I would have thought 5 minutes of research would have been a better use of their time. ](*,)

The thing that "scares" me are bug like [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnome-shell/+bug/690045](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnome-shell/+bug/690045), which are marked as fixed for 11.04 which means even if I want to run gnome3/gnome-shell on it I will have to jump through hoops to do it assuming I can make it work without breaking any libs.

People should have choice and the I do not think is normal that because 2 people in the Ubuntu development think gnome3 should be black list and is not ready that makes it true. Comparing the the 2 (unity, gnome3) now gnome3 is the more ready and polished one while unity looks like it will only be ready maybe in 11.10.

And if Mint Linux can make Gnome3 work in their distro based on Ubuntu why can't ubuntu do it ?

### Post by Nightstrike2009 on 2011-02-24
In my own opinion I dislike Unity with a passion, but there are options available;

1) The obvious leave for another distro which could be fraught with problems I myself have tried Debian, Kubuntu and Mint.  Mint seems to be the most user friendly alternative :-)
2) Stick with Ubuntu 10.04LTS until its support runs out (2-3years) and review the situation. 
(My current situation)
3) Upgrade to 11.04 at the end of April and gut Unity out of it using the terminal command line.

Has stated before I dislike Unity with a passion and will not use it under any circumstance, but if it attracts more users to Linux then despite my own opinion on it it is ultimately a good move.  The rest of us have the know-how to remove it at will.

Personally I think too much media attention has been wasted on Unity, the advancement of Linux is the important issue not another Gnome Vs Unity Vs KDE Vs Xfce Vs Gnome 3.0 farce. :-(

> And if Mint Linux can make Gnome3 work in their distro based on Ubuntu why can't ubuntu do it ? 

I also belive that Ubuntu 11.04 has the neccesary underpinning framework to remove unity and install Gnome 3.0 into it if thats what you are looking for (Unity seems to rely on some Gnome3.0 technology to work).

### Post by Shining Arcanine on 2011-02-24
> **Maximus559 said:**
> They say not to look a gift horse in the mouth. However, given that Ubuntu is largely a community-driven project, I feel like I should speak up on this issue.

With the release of Ubuntu 11.04 around the corner, I'm getting more and more uncertain about the future of this distribution. Specifically, I'm concerned about Canonical's choice to replace Gnome with Unity as the default window manager. To be fair, I've only been using Ubuntu since 9.10 came out. However, in retrospect, I'm glad I found Ubuntu when I did. If I was a Windows user looking to try Ubuntu today, and I found some screenshots of 11.04, I would probably lose interest very quickly.

The problem is Unity. Wacky color schemes, window controls on the left, universal menu bar, app launcher that you can't get rid of... all these things would be enough to scare me away. Of course, as a current user, I know that there are other window manager options for Ubuntu. If and when Unity becomes the default, Kubuntu and Xubuntu will probably be joined by Gnubuntu. However, people who are new to the Linux world don't have this knowledge. They just see what comes with the distro by default, and if they don't like what they see, they won't try it.

Besides what I've already mentioned, these are my complaints when it comes to Unity. First of all, it sure looks like Canonical is trying to pander to current Mac users. By doing that, they are alienating 90% of the computer-using public and targeting the 5% that is least likely to switch to Linux. Not the smartest move if you want to gain mainstream acceptance. Secondly, Unity also looks like it is designed for tablets. That's great, except I'm not using a tablet. I don't want my computer to look and behave like a tablet. I want it to look and behave like a computer.

Now, if Unity was offered as another option for tablet users, I'd be all for that. The more options, the better. Just please, *please* Canonical, don't make it the default. You need to keep recruiting Windows users, and so far Gnome has done a good job at facilitating that. Basically, my point is this: 

**Don't mess with success**.

That is what you have here, Canonical. The most successful Linux distribution of them all, if I'm not mistaken. Don't go and screw it up for some petty or arcane reason.

I doubt that anybody on the development team will read this, but if Unity does drive Ubuntu into obscurity, at least you won't be able to say you weren't warned.

If you do not like the new default, then do not upgrade, change it when you upgrade, switch distributions or make your own distribution. If you really do not want them to change, perhaps then you should be asking for them to stop making new versions. That would ensure that there is no change.

---

### Post by Lucradia on 2011-02-24

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2011-02-24
Here's the bottom line- most people who will be trying Linux on their own for the first time are willing to deal with technical hurdles, and will probably think it's fresh and interesting. It likely won't give them a horrible impression of Linux.

For people who aren't doing it on their own, it's up to the person installing it for them to explain any changes or to switch to the old interface. That's why Canonical has LTS releases.

This doesn't mean I support Unity or not, I'm just stating the fact that you'll have to deal with it or get involved. It's your source code, you can do what you want with it, but please respect the consensus among the community if you pretend to represent them.

Just like you choose Xfce, Gnome, or KDE, so too will you be choosing from Gnome-Shell and Unity in the future. I think it's important that we respect and propogate the projects that have brought us this far, and with Unity you have meaningful connections to OpenGL, Qt, and GTK.

So, in essence, Unity could end up benefitting many corners of open source, if we decide to show respect and support each of them with meaningful bug reports and not ignore them out of a botched sense of nationalism for the UI of our choice. The lines are blurring, people. Let's make sure we're supporting open source doing its best, not just whatever looks best to us.

### Post by Shining Arcanine on 2011-02-24
I would also like to add that GNOME has not been the default desktop environment on Ubuntu for a long time now. What has been the default desktop environment is Canonical's heavily patched fork of GNOME, which they wanted the GNOME people to adopt. The GNOME people refused, so now they are pursuing something else.

Anyway, if you want to run GNOME on Ubuntu, then it will have to be compiled from the upstream sources. It is not possible to get true GNOME on Ubuntu otherwise.

### Post by Lucradia on 2011-02-24
> **ki4jgt said:**
> Sorry for the addition here, but the save button for the edit isn't working on chrome

***EDIT: My dad even made me switch his computer back to Windows after trying to use Unity and me telling him this is where Ubuntu was going. I'm going to keep Ubuntu and try to learn to use it, but it's losing it Windows like feel when Unity comes into the picture.

You can always roll a mini.iso:

[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Installation/MinimalCD](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Installation/MinimalCD)

Do the **Command Line Install**.

Then, when you get to the command line:

sudo apt-get install gnome-core gdm xorg

You can install anything else later when you login to the gui. Please note: You will not have flashy metacity themes, gnome icon packages, or anything fancy (Firefox, compiz, simple-scan, etc). (gnome package would have more of that, but if I recall, it's broken.) To install restricted drivers from ubuntu's repo, add **jockey-gtk**

[http://packages.ubuntu.com](http://packages.ubuntu.com) is your friend.

### Post by Naggobot on 2011-02-25
> I, for one, have not heard anyone saying "Wow, this new Unity interface is awesome!"Let's fix this

Wow, this new Unity interface is awesome!

Now that is out of the way :P

More seriously. I do not know if I am majority or minority but when 10.04 will get to end of life I will be happily switching to Unity and I will give it a fair try. I feel that removing horizontal clutter from the screen is progress.  Portion of laptops is increasing and on a laptop default Gnome with two horizontal bars is not best possible. First thing I do is hide the lower bar and I use a lot of full screen mode in Firefox. On my work computer with two 22" screens (and wxp) I have about 6 cm wide task bar ***between*** the two screens, positioned on the secondary screen. This actually works great. I have noticed that I do not need 3 cm gray area on the side of the page while using text editor or pdf reader.

Btw. One of the reasons it is difficult to read from a screen is that the text lay out is often too wide. Try sometime reducing the width and reading then. Or Just like the editor window of this forum which is only 1/3 of the width of my laptop screen is easier to read than the preview with full width. 

There is a general rule that when something changes the noisy minority will complain and silent majority will accept. Remember the 3/11 rule. In net it is probably more like 3/110? I wonder if there is any data on this?

Personally my worry with Unity is that will they get possible relevant bugs out during the 10.04 LTS support. That is the main reason for me why I will stick with LTS. Unity is probably a big change on code level so I fear that first adopters will have it rough compared to us a waiting freeloader like me.

### Post by billdotson on 2011-02-26
I'm not going to read everything that has been said thus far, but from what I've seen about Unity so far, I don't think I will like it. Like the OP said, it seems like it is made for a netbook or a media tablet. When I think of a netbook or a media tablet I think "restrictive".

What they really should have is a choice on an install DVD where it shows you what KDE, Gnome and Unity looks like and how each one of them works and lets you choose what you want. Along with that option, they should include options like: "Do you want your window buttons on the left side or the right side?" No user should be expected to change things like that by running terminal commands or picking through user settings. 

Answering the question though, I'm not sure is Unity will kill Ubuntu or not. The way I see it is the vast majority of computer users are not extremely or even fairly tech savvy and the simplified interface would attract them. I don't necessarily think it is bad that Ubuntu is trying to incorporate elements that make things like Apple products enjoyable to many people. Having a simplified interface, an application store, etc. is attractive to a lot of computer users. The Software center, Unity and those other things may not be for me (Synaptic, and sometimes the terminal is what I usually prefer) but I can't say for sure if the number of people those things will turn away will be greater than the number of those brought in by them.

I am glad Canonical is at least trying to get Linux OSes more of a user base. The way it is now, barely anyone uses Linux, let alone knows about it. For most people, they thing is they really don't need it either. For most people Windows and OS X meet their needs perfectly fine. Lots of people don't use anything other than a web browser, a music playing program and some office software on their computers. 

Also, most people that I know of are at least somewhat accustomed to the idea that software is just something you have to pay for sometimes. Look at the people spending a bunch of money on security suites at places like Staples, or buying software that allows them to burn CDs.
Plus, the cost of OS X or Windows doesn't really affect a whole lot of users. With OEMs like Dell or HP, Microsoft makes the OS part of the purchase fairly cheap. I'm not sure how expensive OS X is in regards to a Mac price, but I'm sure it is discounted. When Dell sold Ubuntu PCs (do they still do that?) I remember the price of one being pretty much the same as a Windows PC. What do you think a "normal" consumer is going to do if they see this thing that they have almost no idea what it is versus Windows, the OS that all their programs already run on and they know how to use (at least marginally)? Obviously, most are going to stick with Windows. 

Linux OSes need something special to bring people to them. OS X brings people to it for all sorts of reasons: the design of the hardware that comes with it, the relative ease of use, the graphical whizbangedness (I just invented that word, that's right). GNU/Linux needs reasons to bring normal computer users to it, not just tech savvy folks. Not everyone is going to go with the freedom aspect of an OS(most people just use a web browser, office software and multimedia software and don't care about modifying it), so there need to be some very distinct other reasons for people to use it. If Canonical can make Ubuntu super easy to use that is one plus for it.

---

### Post by Harry33 on 2011-02-26
> **jcphil said:**
> Most of the time, Unity hasn't worked for me. When it did work, I hated it. And it seems like Natty has only made it difficult to experiment with Gnome Shell, which looks more interesting to me.

But these are not ready yet, of course there are bugs, some of us even do like to resolve them.

One more thing.
You want something to work reliably.
Well that is exactly what Canonical does.
Gnome-shell was not pulled in, because of high risks of it not being fully ready for Natty.

### Post by NightwishFan on 2011-02-27
I switched to Debian, but not for any Ubuntu failings, it just seems to be a lot more conservative (even in Testing) and that suits me magnificently. What I do not understand is the fascination with Mint. When asked what distro I use and I say Debian, people think I instantly mean Mint Debian. I am not sure they should even be allowed to call it "Debian Edition" if it does not comply with the DFSG. As for normal Mint, I can see if they are more conservative it might have less Ubuntu specific problems, but as a whole it should not be faster than a similar package set on Ubuntu.

That being said I have nothing against Mint, it is just not for me. If I want ease of use and company support I will use Ubuntu. If I want a universal OS I will use Debian. :) I have room to recommend both.

Now, Gnome 2 I am glad is being re-done however I really feel that the Gnome Shell is not ready. Sure Gnome 3 is fully usable, unlike KDE 4.0 was, however the Shell itself is a bit lacking. It has some excellent ideas though. I am actually hoping for Unity eventually being packaged on Debian (I really hope they do). :)

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-02-27
> **tgm4883]Wouldn't that be resolved by an 'apt-get install gnome2' or something to that effect?[/quote]

[QUOTE=cariboo907 said:**
> Unity is a compiz plugin, all you have to do is disable it and select the Classic Desktop from the session menu, as Merk42 said in post [#41]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10499634&postcount=41")

Actually selecting the Classic Dsktop, disables the plugin for you.

I'm not sure whether you were responding to tgm or to me, so I'll try to clarify, and if it's unnecessary, ignore it. = ) I do understand that Unity is implemented through Compiz and that all of Gnome (except the panel) is already running underneath, and that said Gnome environment is fully capable of running said panel regardless. That is, theoretically, one would not even need to switch sessions to get to the classic desktop - it'd be as simple as compiz --replace && gnome-panel . But if the Gnome folks decide to deprecate the classic mode entirely, then the panel goes away, permanently, and there's no way to bring it back in. I was saying that I thought some folks were worried about *that*.

As I said, as long as the Unity plugin can be turned off, I don't care for my personal uses one way or the other whether the classic panel is retained.

### Post by Bazon on 2011-02-27
Some Things I really don't like in Unity (10.10 netbook edition):

**1. Bad Window / Workspace management**
I'm used to have six workspaces (2 private, 4 job), each for different purposes. When I'm on a specific workspace, I don't want to be distracted by anything on the other workspaces. No problem in Gnome.
But what it's like in Unity?
(a) **The left launcher-bar shows applications running on ALL workspaces.**  (including annoying private ones I don't want to see when I'm on one of my job workspaces)  and there seems to be no way turning this off. :-(
(b) Worse than that, there is **no easy way to open a new window of a running application on another workspace**: When clicking the launcher, no new window is opened, but I get on another workspace there the program is already running in a window. I really hate this!
(c) So the only way to put a new window to a new workspace, is to open a new window with the file menu in the existing window. so I have to go back to a workspace I don't want to go. I like to keep focused on my actual task, you now? 
The I have to move that specific window in the workspace overview, which is really hard to find, because from distance many windows look nearly the same...

**2. What should replace Gnome-Panel Applets?**
I really love to see what my computer is doing right now, so **I want to have the Gnome-Panel-System-Monitor**. And some other things for the Gnome Panel. But: No way in unity, the configurable panel is gone! :-(

**3. I think, Alt+F2 is mentioned yet...**



Stealing opportunities from the user is really not a good idea. I hope, Gnome will kept in, or maybe I have to move to another distribution.... :-(

---

### Post by pafufta503 on 2011-02-27
unity might be the move that canonical needs to gather a bigger userbase.  there are some prevailing paradigms for new computer users, and also less knowledgable and older demographics (grandparents for example).  the layout of android and iphones is a new paradigm that might replace the window/desktop paradigms.  

for a person like myself, and my best friend, gnome2 represents the pinnacle of a desktop/window paradigm, nearly every aspect is fully customizable to any concievable need. whereas unity/gnome3/os x/chrome os/netbook os's are made to provide an easy and straight forward access to the computer, and in such a way that is similar to a mobile OS.  this is a good thing for mobile phone users learning computers as they do not need to change their perspective or paradigms to learn their computer.

i have convinced my mother to switch to ubuntu, and when i finally get around to doing this i am going to start her off with unity or gnome 3.  her ability to think in a highly abstract way with computers is extremely difficult, and she needs an interface that can predict her needs and won't require customization for optimal performance and ease of use.

so me, i'll stick with gnome2 for now, i might try a more minimal DE/WM in the future.  for the older generations, and younger kids i think this is a good change and will help keep ubuntu competitive with the proprietary OS's.

---

### Post by CarpKing on 2011-02-27
> **Copper Bezel said:**
> Canonical's track record is already better on this in the sense that Unity at least still supports Compiz, though. Gnome Shell is already the bit where the devs decide to break a staple of the Linux desktop. ; )

It's not a case of Unity "still" supporting Compiz.  If I understand correctly, the new version of Unity is essentially a Compiz plugin, meaning that you won't be able to use it with Metacity.  This track record is thus *exactly the same* as Gnome, where the new Shell is essentially a Mutter plugin.  

I guess it would be nice if Gnome-Shell wasn't so tied to its window manager, but it doesn't bother me too much because Mutter is making Compiz irrelevant by absorbing its useful features.

The button on the left thing has been done to death, if you don't like it go to the configuration editor and change it back to the right. It is trivial (I figured that out after installing Ubuntu for a week)

The change to Wayland is much bigger. That I am worry about, I hope they don't rush it until they make sure there is proper hardware support (like propiretary graphic drivers) It is probably too soon if they want to make it default in 11.10 or even 12.04.

### Post by Vaphell on 2011-03-03
unity is more than ok for netbooks and low end laptops where vertical space is at premium. Screens are getting wider but the height stays the same or even shrinks in mainstream devices, so it makes much sense to put UI elements on the side. Pretty much all of the internet is optimized for 1024px wide content so standard 1366x768 wastes approx 30% of screen estate on nothing.
In fact i did the same thing in W7, moved that fat bottom panel to the left so more stuff can fit at once in webbrowser and in office apps.

### Post by slackthumbz on 2011-03-03
> **maqtanim said:**
> I did not use unity that much so far, I am still using Lucid, but I personally like the idea of the Unity! :P 

In netbook, this interface really saves a lot of vertical spaces. And to my point of view, a dock is needed to pin all the frequently used applications for shortcut access. And unity is using Compiz as a window manager. 

Overall I am not scared of Unity rather I am thrilled! But I donot have any intension to dump my current LTS before 12.04! Hope (and believe) that, within this span of time, Unity will be grown into a more mature and popular shell!

Unity uses Mutter as its window manager. I think you'll find that if you install the 10.10 netbook remix and start up a Unity desktop there'll be no compiz running. I had to install compiz and the gnome integration manually to get desktop effects and a decent gnome desktop on my netbook.

The inability to autohide the side bar was a ridiculous waste of screen real estate.. actually just read the first few pages of the Unity MEGA thread in recurring discussions: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1695432](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1695432) 

I'm sure it'll be better in Natty and I'm interested to see how much it's improved by then but its first outing in 10.10 was abominable.

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-03-03
> Unity uses Mutter as its window manager. I think you'll find that if you install the 10.10 netbook remix and start up a Unity desktop there'll be no compiz running. I had to install compiz and the gnome integration manually to get desktop effects and a decent gnome desktop on my netbook.

You're using Unity 2-D, whether you know it or not. Something in Unity doesn't like your graphics card. Compiz is the default and Mutter is the fallback.

And yes, I agree, I wouldn't use it until the panel intellihide is implimented, although I'm quite happy with AWN and won't migrate regardless. Still, it's a simple interface that makes sense for new users, which the two panels never were,

---

### Post by beew on 2011-03-03
> **maqtanim said:**
> 

I think it is not a good idea to have menus instead of icon. If the menu-submenus are added, the dock will be more cluttered. The present looks of unity is really neat and clean. But ... yes ... it should be more configurable.


I find the icons too big. I have tons of stuffs installed it is always easier to go through a menu than wading through a bunch of icons, especially if they are huge,--it would make sense for tablets and devices ( only a few apps installed, you want to use your finger to start them instead of the keypad) but it doesn't seem to make sense for desktops or even laptops where users use the keyboard and the mouse . 

Right now I use the Cairo dock, there is an icon which when you click will show the application menu, I don't see how that would clutter the desktop, basically you just need to add an option to use something like that instead of the "Applications" launcher that opens a window full of icons jumping at you.

The Cairo Dock is 3d and it has animation, the Unity dock is basically flat. Also as noted in my last post at this point there is no nice window decorator in Unity comparable to emerald with transparency effect etc. I love eye candies but actually don't find unity that beautiful as it is comparing to what I have already got and much less usable. But since I am not upgrading to Natty (my Maverick box has been installed for barely 3 months!) that is not my immediate problem, hopefull it will be more enhancement and added functionality and options by the time 11.10 comes out.

I think incorporating Compiz is a big plus in relation to gnome-shell though.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-03-03
> **beew said:**
> How do you do the 3d cube thing in Unity? Tried the live usb, doesn't seem to work. There is no option in enabling visual effects in Applications > Appearance.

Currently I use emerald as window decorator and it is beautiful, I know that emerald is depreciated and will not be working with Unity anymore, does anyone know if there is work in making a replacement? I like the transparent window border, the gtk-window decorator always strikes me as extremely ugly with the solid black window borders.

That's because the tab has been removed. Compiz is enabled by default because unity is a compiz plugin. To set the affects install compizconfig settings manager, and all your old favorites be be available.

BTW Unity 2D was created using QT libraries, there is no mutter involved.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-03-03
Keep in mind that the interim releases between LTS releases, are only for trying out new stuff. If you want stable and bug fixes, stick with the LTS releases. If you want the latest released programs, use the interim releases, but be aware they are only supported for 18 months, and most can't be considered really stable.

### Post by NightwishFan on 2011-03-03
I took a look at that review of Fedora 15 and to be honest it just seems like FUD. That is certainly not the final state of the Fedora desktop, as Gnome 3 itself is just now freezing for release. (If it IS the final for the desktop please forgive my optimism) I assume they will polish it out just fine as Fedora always has the slickest and most polished Gnome (I think even more so than Ubuntu as it follows regular gnome more closely).

As for the Ubuntu Classic Desktop, it is great. Try it. :) It uses the global menu and the indicators by default just like unity. The only potential downside is that (by default mind you!) it only uses a single menu, and not the applications/places we are used to. All 3 menus are now contained in a single logo. I will admit it looks slick and does not lower usability enough for me to not use the setup.

I heard talk that Gnome 3 itself is making a gnome 3 panel for desktops without acceleration, that resembles the gnome panel but uses gnome 3 technology. I could be wrong, so it might be best to check the Gnome 3 Myths. I *believe* the Ubuntu classic panel is much like the one we have now in Lucid/Maverick.

I do like Unity, and to be honest I plan on using it. It will also save my grandma from having 300 firefox windows open due to her launching a new one every time she minimizes an old one. :)

It might seem a little locked down, but I believe it has the potential for a lot of customization in the future, or at least hope. Because that is that way it should be in my opinion.

Do not get me wrong I like the gnome 3 shell as well, but Unity seems more simple and complete, and less messy at a glance. :)

---

### Post by CaptainMark on 2011-03-06
Ive installed the Natty Alpha 3, ive never used unity before and ive gotta say I dont like it, its slow, restrictive, awkward and seems generally a real step back.  But hey I will let it have its fair trial before discarding it. One of the things really bugging me is that the launcher menu only lets you open one window, and if i want to open for example two file managers to drag and drop files between two directories, i have to use the unity launcher to open one, then go to the file menu (which auto-hides :mad:) and open a second, does any one have a quicker way???

Im curious to see what others think of unity because in my opinion its only good side is that it looks nice but with a huge sacrifice on functionality (did apple design it?)

---

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-03-06
> **CaptainMark said:**
> Ive installed the Natty Alpha 3 [...]

One of the things really bugging me is that the launcher menu only lets you open one window, and if i want to open for example two file managers to drag and drop files between two directories, i have to use the unity launcher to open one, then go to the file menu (which auto-hides :mad:) and open a second, does any one have a quicker way???
Quicker way...

First, don't hide the launcher.  I leave mine visible all the time.  This solves MANY problems!

Secondly, all you have to do is click the file manager icon.  Then, Crtl-N.

You can open as many as you want, here are four (Crtl-N x 3)...  

Also, You can use Superkey-2 to open the file manager.  

So, Superkey-2, then Ctrl-N will do the job too (no mousing necessary).

Unity is very versatile!  Give it a chance...    ;)

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-03-06
> **CaptainMark said:**
> okay th superkey functionality is okay i guess, but for the past 2 years ive used the superkey for something else and didnt want to have to give it up [...]
You can map the keyboard any way you want!

You can keep your Superkey, if you wish.

### Post by wedderburn on 2011-03-06
been a long time GNOME user ( since 2.4) I chose Ubuntu because it was a pretty stock standard version of gnome without much cruft, with this latest divergence to unity is anyone else out there considering finding a distro that is closer to the vision the GNOME developers have?

### Post by 3Miro on 2011-03-06
> **wedderburn said:**
> been a long time GNOME user ( since 2.4) I chose Ubuntu because it was a pretty stock standard version of gnome without much cruft, with this latest divergence to unity is anyone else out there considering finding a distro that is closer to the vision the GNOME developers have?

Ubuntu moved from standard Gnome long time ago. It was one of the few distros to come with Compiz by default as opposed to the native Metacity. They also have the entire sound plugin.

On the other hand, apart form the window manager and sound applet, Ubuntu still ships with Gnome. The difference isn't as big as some people would think.

You are also aware that Gnome itself is going through a major revision, right? If you like the current Gnome 2, then take a look at XFCE. Gnome-shell is nothing like Gnome 2 (in fact Unity is smaller change to Gnome than Gnome-shell is).

---

> been a long time GNOME user ( since 2.4) I chose Ubuntu because it was a pretty stock standard version of gnome without much cruft, with this latest divergence to unity is anyone else out there considering finding a distro that is closer to the vision the GNOME developers have?

what do you think gnome is?
unity is still gnome, but the desktop ui is unity.

if ubuntu was following the gnome developers, you would be using gnome-shell.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-03-10
> **jermza said:**
> Next month, Unity launches.  I'm quite excited to get it, but I just have a few questions.

1.  Because Unity is so different to Gnome, will a lot of my software stop working properly?  (Docky, for example). 

Docky still works fine here, I've been using it since the toolchain was uploaded

> 2.  Which is better?  A clean install or a normal upgrade when Ubuntu prompts me?

In this case it may be better to do a fresh install including /home/$USER, as some of your old settings may screw things up.

3> .  Is Unity just an interface variation (like KDE)?

Unity is a compiz plugin, so it won't work on a system that can't do 3D

> 4.  Can Unity's top panel be minimised into unexpanded mode?

The top panel is hard coded, becuase of the global menus, at this point it isn't customizable.

As I stated earlier, Unity is a compiz plugin, so all the apps you always use will work without a problem.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-03-11
> **jermza said:**
> Thanks for the great reply.

I sure hope the top panel is worked on soon.  Using a screen with a high resolution, it's irritating with a top panel that stretches across the top (and tiny icons on the far right).

Just a question regarding the clean install, how will it affect my current situation; I have two hard drives - one running Ubuntu only, and the other storing all my files.  (I have the menus mapped so that Ubuntu doesn't use the drive on which it is installed.  Problem is, I can't remember how I did this and am worried about what will happen when I do a clean install.  Any advice?

I'd suggest backing up both your /home directory and /etc, as that is where the majority of the configuration files are stored. The way your partitions are mounted is located in /etc/fstab, so make sure that you back that configuration file up, a couple of times. :)

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-03-11
> **cariboo907 said:**
> [...] it may be better to do a fresh install including /home/$USER, as some of your old settings may screw things up.[...]
When I upgraded from A2 -> A3, I did a sudo-fresh install.

That is, I went through the standard Live-CD drill, but I told the installer to NOT format the partitions -- that's all.

Amazingly, the installer then  asked me if I wanted it to reinstall the existing packages (never seen that feature before) -- I answered Yes --and it properly installed all of the officially supported programs.  I didn't lose hardly anything, and for the most part, nothing was screwed up.

The exception was, as stated elsewhere in these forums, I had to reauth some of the repos, I had some double entries in sources.list, and so forth, but the proggies (themselves) worked just fine.

For the most part, almost everything was reinstalled by the installer, except 3rd party non-sense -- and even then -- the config files were still there, making installation uber simple.

I agree that a fresh install is probably best, but the Natty installer is pretty slick IMO.  You might want to give it a chance to sort things out, for you, before using the nuclear option.

Just saying...  ;)

---

I started thinking about how the default install looks.  Yuck!

Does anybody run the default Ubuntu Gnome shell (and brag about it).  I judge the answer is NO!

So, what are we in love with?

We are in love with what WE have done -- our own work, on the desktop, e.g. the changes that WE have made to Gnome.

Personally, I think the default Unity interface is butt fugly, just like Gnome, but...

Nothing keeps you from customizing the look n' feel of Unity.

Here are examples of what I've done with Gnome and Unity -- neither one looks like the default install...


[INDENT]**Ubuntu 10.10 / Conky 1.8.1** (Click to expand)

[[IMG]http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-10-mar-2011(600x480).png[/IMG]]("http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-10-mar-2011.png")[/INDENT]



[INDENT]**Ubuntu 11.04 / Conky 1.8.0** (Click to expand)

[[IMG]http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-10-mar-2011(2)(600x480).png[/IMG]]("http://vindsl.com/images/vindsl-desktop-10-mar-2011(2).png")[/INDENT]


I'm equally happy with both of them... but, that's just me.

The bottom line is:  if you don't like the way Unity looks, change it into something that you DO like!  ;)

Exactly and my problem is that Unity is not as flexible to configure. For example, I use the Cairo Dock which is better looking(with 3d effects) and have way more functions than the Unity bar (a real desktop switcher, menu options etc) I can also move it to the side like Unity.  I can't use this with Unity  anymore because two docks would be ridiculous. 

The good thing I like about the old gnome is that it is modular so it is a lot more flexible to add and remove features. KDE is not modular, and now Gnome is trying to go the same route with the Gnome shell. I don't really care of "consistency" at the cost of removing flexibility. With a modular design, you can still strive for consistency if that is important for you.

I too think that out of the box Gnome is butt ugly, I never use the default look (I couldn't understand folks who get upset with Unity and threaten to switch to Mint with that Windoze 98 wanabe look)

### Post by irrdev on 2011-03-19
I think Unity is actually a very smart move. Yes, it has several similarities to Gnome shell, but the differences are apparent enough if you try both of them out. For me, however, the greatest difference is the direction of both projects. The Gnome project really seems to lack focus, clearly-defined goals as well as a target audience. In other words, the Gnome project feels like a DE made by developers, without consideration for what they're trying to achieve. Unity, however, is obviously geared towards the sole purpose of making Ubuntu more mainstream and also incorporating support for a wider range of devices and screen sizes. I fully support that. You say you don't like KDE? Well, ironically enough, Unity now has the same project goals and leadership behind it that KDE did with the 4.x release. There will be many bumps in the road ahead, but I think it's a smart decision in the long run.

---

### Post by gerowen on 2011-03-19
As much as I love Ubuntu, I personally feel that one of its weaknesses is the requirement to have an entirely new release every 6 months, which often bring major changes.  People are making debs for Ubuntu and want to work with you, they're managing repos for Ubuntu versions of their software, but when you change your gameplan every 6 months, you're going to start scaring people away.

If it aint broke, don't fix it.

### Post by Random_Dude on 2011-03-19
> **zika said:**
> Try FluxBox...

I did.
I found it actually easier to configure than openbox, but then I got some GUI tools that help to configure tint2 and openbox menu, which now makes openbox easier to configure than fluxbox.

I also like to try tilting window managers, but I haven't found a perfect one yet.
scrotwm: I found it the easiest to configure. I also like how the windows behave, the simplicity of the keys and dmenu. However, it doesn't have a taskbar, which I really miss.

awesomeWM: Task bar works great, but I don't like how the windows behave, it's hard to configure the menu, it's hard to configure the keys (I still can't change the volume through key shortcuts), I can't integrate dmenu or change the theme (this last one I haven't tried much). 

wmii2: Haven't messed around much, but it looks promising. I love how you can change the windows from tilting to floating and resize them while on tilting mode using the mouse. However, no bar.

Trying out this stuff really makes me see how user-friendly is the default gnome environment is.

Cheers :cool:

### Post by gerowen on 2011-03-20
> **ikt said:**
> Are you saying that having a ~1% market share for desktop linux is not broken?

Do you know what the no.1 bug for ubuntu is?

I'd be willing to say that the biggest reason for desktop Linux's small market share isn't Gnome, it's a couple of things.

1) Microsoft's corporate contracts and deals.  For example every non Apple PC you buy in the store will have a copy of Windows pre-installed.  This means that every person who wants to go into a store and buy a computer will buy a copy of Windows, and will probably continue to use it unless they did research on their own and learned about Linux and choose to use it, because most desktop PC users don't care what OS they use as long as it works.

2) Fragmentation.  With Windows, you can predict, with a great deal of accuracy, what your target audience has in terms of software capabilities.  With Linux you've got hundreds of distros that are all just a little different, dozens of desktop environments, rpm packages, deb packages, etc.  There isn't enough of a push to standardise things in Linux, so it makes it hard for developers to make quality software because if they build it around one platform, it may take some tweaking and additional software to get it running on another one.  Ubuntu doesn't help this with releasing a whole new version of their OS every 6 months, instead of just patching/updating stable ones in place.  People have to make new repos, update their old ones, rename their packages to match the new cutesy animal name for the new release, etc.  Don't get me wrong I love Ubuntu, it's stable, FOSS, secure, and easy to use, but I'm not afraid to point out potential flaws.

Unity is guilty of contributing to my #2 point.  It's just ANOTHER ui, like we didn't have enough already.  Let's just stick with "Gnome" and push for fixes and updates to that instead of forking it off into a whole other project.  Fragmentation will be the death of desktop Linux.

---

### Post by NCLI on 2011-03-20
> **gerowen said:**
> As much as I love Ubuntu, I personally feel that one of its weaknesses is the requirement to have an entirely new release every 6 months, which often bring major changes.  People are making debs for Ubuntu and want to work with you, they're managing repos for Ubuntu versions of their software, but when you change your gameplan every 6 months, you're going to start scaring people away.

If it aint broke, don't fix it.

And this is why we have LTS releases. If you dislike frequent change, stick with them.
> **gerowen said:**
> I'd be willing to say that the biggest reason for desktop Linux's small market share isn't Gnome, it's a couple of things.
It's definitely not "Just gnome", but having an attractive UI is a big part of getting new users. Just look at OSX.

> 1) Microsoft's corporate contracts and deals.  For example every non Apple PC you buy in the store will have a copy of Windows pre-installed.  This means that every person who wants to go into a store and buy a computer will buy a copy of Windows, and will probably continue to use it unless they did research on their own and learned about Linux and choose to use it, because most desktop PC users don't care what OS they use as long as it works.
Very true, but you forget that Unity was actually partially creted because OEMs were asking for it, so it helps solve this problem. Besides, if people like Unity when they see it on laptops, demand will slowly rise.
> 2) Fragmentation.  With Windows, you can predict, with a great deal of accuracy, what your target audience has in terms of software capabilities.  With Linux you've got hundreds of distros that are all just a little different, dozens of desktop environments, rpm packages, deb packages, etc.  There isn't enough of a push to standardise things in Linux, so it makes it hard for developers to make quality software because if they build it around one platform, it may take some tweaking and additional software to get it running on another one.
That's pure bull. There's a big push towards stadardisation, it's just still underway.  However, as a developer, you usually just worry about writing it so that it complies with the fd.o standards, which is what Ubuntu and the other major distros strive to comply with, and let the distro packagers worry about packaging for the indiviudal distros.

GNOME seems to be fighting this push towards standards right now, but hopefully a solution will be found soon.

> Ubuntu doesn't help this with releasing a whole new version of their OS every 6 months, instead of just patching/updating stable ones in place.  People have to make new repos, update their old ones, rename their packages to match the new cutesy animal name for the new release, etc.  Don't get me wrong I love Ubuntu, it's stable, FOSS, secure, and easy to use, but I'm not afraid to point out potential flaws.
Wait, you were talking about standards, right? Standards don't change often, and Ubuntu's release schedule have no effect on that. Also, noone has to manually rename and repackage packaes, that's all handled by the automated build services, so that is bull.

> Unity is guilty of contributing to my #2 point.  It's just ANOTHER ui, like we didn't have enough already.  Let's just stick with "Gnome" and push for fixes and updates to that instead of forking it off into a whole other project.  Fragmentation will be the death of desktop Linux.
GNOME was unwilling to cooperate with Canonical and accept input from the Ayatana design team while designing GNOME Shell. Since Canonical disagrees with the direction GNOME Shell is taking, and couldn't affect the design process, they had to design their own UI.

So while it definitely would have been better for them to cooperate, that just isn't going to happen anytime soon.

Also, you seem to be confused about what "fragmentation" means. Different UI's != fragmentation. Fragmentation occurs when the different UI's have different standards for interactions inbetween applications, and beween applications and the UI, forcing developers to write different implementations for each UI.

Nowadays, largely thanks to fd.o, most of these things follow common standards, forexample the new notifications standard KDE and Canonical have developed through fd.o, which GNOME chose not to implement, sparking the current disagreement between the three big DEs(Unity, Shell, KDE).

For the record, Unity follows the standards.

---

### Post by beew on 2011-03-20
> **gerowen said:**
> I'd be willing to say that the biggest reason for desktop Linux's small market share isn't Gnome, it's a couple of things.

1) Microsoft's corporate contracts and deals.  For example every non Apple PC you buy in the store will have a copy of Windows pre-installed.  This means that every person who wants to go into a store and buy a computer will buy a copy of Windows, and will probably continue to use it unless they did research on their own and learned about Linux and choose to use it, because most desktop PC users don't care what OS they use as long as it works.

2) Fragmentation.  With Windows, you can predict, with a great deal of accuracy, what your target audience has in terms of software capabilities.  With Linux you've got hundreds of distros that are all just a little different, dozens of desktop environments, rpm packages, deb packages, etc.  There isn't enough of a push to standardise things in Linux, so it makes it hard for developers to make quality software because if they build it around one platform, it may take some tweaking and additional software to get it running on another one.  Ubuntu doesn't help this with releasing a whole new version of their OS every 6 months, instead of just patching/updating stable ones in place.  People have to make new repos, update their old ones, rename their packages to match the new cutesy animal name for the new release, etc.  Don't get me wrong I love Ubuntu, it's stable, FOSS, secure, and easy to use, but I'm not afraid to point out potential flaws.

Unity is guilty of contributing to my #2 point.  It's just ANOTHER ui, like we didn't have enough already.  Let's just stick with "Gnome" and push for fixes and updates to that instead of forking it off into a whole other project.  Fragmentation will be the death of desktop Linux.

3) technical support. If your windows system breaks chances are it would be easy for you to find someone to trouble shoot it, maybe a guy from the store but most commonly just someone you know. But if you run Linux it would be quite difficult (most people don't want to wade through forums looking for answers, and even if they find the answers to their problems they may not want to go through the trouble of implementing the solutions themselves)

4) hardware support. Unity may not be that much of a problem but it could be a huge problem if ubuntu decides to rush Wayland.

5) brand recognition. Not just for the OS itself, but also the software that runs on it. There are good open source replacement for almost anything you can find in Windows or Mac  which are just as good or better (except maybe for some specialized things like Photoshop which most users don't use the  advanced options anyway) But there is a problem of recognition.

Moving to a  shinny  new UI addresses none of these, though I have actually no problem with shinny and cool new UI. I like cool and new design,  I just don't think it has a lot to do with market share and in the case of Unity, my problem is with its lack of flexibility and usability, not that it is new.

 Also, we can't "stick to gnome" (you mean the gnome 2 panel because Unity still runs on top of Gnome) because Gnome itself has changed to Gnome Shell which is similar to Unity in appearance except IMO  even worse.

---

### Post by CraigPaleo on 2011-03-20
> **gerowen said:**
> As much as I love Ubuntu, I personally feel that one of its weaknesses is the ***requirement*** to have an entirely new release every 6 months, which often bring major changes.  People are making debs for Ubuntu and want to work with you, they're managing repos for Ubuntu versions of their software, but when you change your gameplan every 6 months, you're going to start scaring people away.

If it aint broke, don't fix it.

> **NCLI said:**
> And this is why we have LTS releases. If you dislike frequent change, stick with them.

Exactly. LTS are supported for 3 years on desktops and 5 years on servers so there is no requirement to upgrade every 6 months. Both 8.04 and 10.04 are still supported. Even the the non-LTS releases are supported for a year and a half. So, it's really only temptation, curiosity and impatience that requires one to upgrade every six months. :D

### Post by beew on 2011-03-20
NCLI

> It's definitely not "Just gnome", but having an attractive UI is a big part of getting new users. Just look at OSX.I am not sure what is Macs actual market share. MS puts it at around the same as Linux actually (the 1% figure for Linux is controversial and probably a gross underestimate,  there are numerous threads on UF addressing that)

 Macs has certain advantages which Ubuntu can never have no matter what UI it uses. 

1)100% hardware compatibility because OSX only works for hardware custom made for it.

2) Marketing hype. Apple markets itself as a lifestyle thing, it is the brand that appeals, not necessarily any special feature of the OS itself. Now in my university almost everyone has a Mac (their parents pay for it most likely) and it is super uncool to use Windoze. But what do these kids use the Mac for? Basically just document, spreadsheet, listening to music, going online ...the usual stuffs, why do they need a computer that costs twice as much ? It is style and peer pressure. (Of course a few use Linux and that is super cool :))

3) Marketing hype
....
infinity) marketing hype.

So it would be very simplistic to think that you can suddenly increase your market share by using a new UI.Mac users are not the type who enjoy looking under the hood and making  changes to their OS. They want a package. They wouldn't want to go  through forums to get things to work. It is a completely different  mindset from most Linux users. You can't make Ubuntu like Apple by just changing its UI (actually I don't even find the Apple UI that pretty, I have a very customized Ubuntu laptop running full Compiz. According to everyone I show it to, it beats Apple any day in look)

### Post by beew on 2011-03-20
> **CraigPaleo said:**
> Exactly. LTS are supported for 3 years on desktops and 5 years on servers so there is no requirement to upgrade every 6 months. Both 8.04 and 10.04 are still supported. Even the the non-LTS releases are supported for a year and a half. So, it's really only temptation, curiosity and impatience that requires one to upgrade every six months. :D

No, it is the need to get updated software that would compel someone to upgrade every 6 months. It sounds good and nice that LTS is being "supported" and by that you will be using antique software (3 year old) which is probably quite poor in usability and lacking many new features,--open source software has much more frequent release cycles than commercial software,-- so who cares if it is supported by Ubuntu?

You can use ppas (I do) to keep your software up to date but this is not the recommended method by Ubuntu. The officially recommended way to upgrade your software is to do them all at once every 6 months along with the OS. This I find insane and it creates a lot of instability and uncertainty.

### Post by uRock on 2011-03-20

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-03-22
> **Mark76 said:**
> Move the launcher bar to the right hand side [...]
Personally, I don't like that idea.  That's where I keep my Conky output!

The dilemma is this... 

Left-hand works fine, IF YOU DO NOT AUTO-HIDE IT.  But, auto-hide is the default.  BAM!

The fix is simple.  Don't auto-hide the dashboard -- end of problem!

Save the right-hand side for Conky!

> **blwegrzyn said:**
> I don't know whose idea was that, but unity interface is a total failure.  It is not intuitive, does not have any options and feels like step backwards.[...]
The second part of the equation is this...

Everyone is used to RIGHT-CLICKING items, and being met with a list of (more or less) useless options.

When you right-click the Unity Dashboard, nothing happens!

Of course, this doesn't mean you cannot change things, but simply, right-clicking gives the appearance (and misperception) that no options are available.

In a perfect world, the Unity Dashboard would NOT auto-hide, and when you right-clicked it, the CCSM Unity Plugin would appear.

I'm sure, in time 'they' (the devs) will figure this out, but in the meantime, a little extra effort is involved by the (assumed) advanced user.

Seriously, when Canonical says a release is an Alpha, they mean it.

I suspect the beta will be much more to the AVERAGE user's liking.

And, the final will be a treat...

The Alpha period is when 'they' mess with (not only) the package, but with your mind.

The impetus is upon YOU to make Chicken Salad out of chicken (feces), and let them know your likes n' dislikes.

That's what I like about threads like this.  It's YOUR opportunity to make a difference, but...

Taking your ball and going home doesn't prove anything except, you're a poor sport.

Soooo, enjoy the ride!

Pretty soon (in 2 months) Natty is going to be about as exciting as shaving, or brushing your teeth.

Then, we can all come to these threads and complain about "Oneiric Ocelot"... Which, undoubtedly, will signal the end of the world, as we know it.

Natty will be looked upon with favor, and everybody will be lamenting it's passing. 


Anyway,rant over.  Carry on...  :D

> 2) Marketing hype. Apple markets itself as a lifestyle thing, it is the brand that appeals, not necessarily any special feature of the OS itself. Now in my university almost everyone has a Mac (their parents pay for it most likely) and it is super uncool to use Windoze. But what do these kids use the Mac for? Basically just document, spreadsheet, listening to music, going online ...the usual stuffs, why do they need a computer that costs twice as much ? It is style and peer pressure. (Of course a few use Linux and that is super cool :))
WHy shouldn't Ubuntu be able to do the same thing, with enough resources? False.

> 3) Marketing hype
....
infinity) marketing hype.
That's bull. Mac is more than marketing hype. It's a simple, pretty secure system, with strikingly beautiful hardware design, and with the entire system and most of its applications following very well defined guidelines for design, behaviour and interaction.


> So it would be very simplistic to think that you can suddenly increase your market share by using a new UI.Mac users are not the type who enjoy looking under the hood and making  changes to their OS. They want a package. They wouldn't want to go  through forums to get things to work. It is a completely different  mindset from most Linux users. You can't make Ubuntu like Apple by just changing its UI
NO ONE is saying that Unity alone will make this "The year of Linux." What it does do is get us one step closer to that day. 

> (actually I don't even find the Apple UI that pretty,
Ok, but most people do.

> I have a very customized Ubuntu laptop running full Compiz. According to everyone I show it to, it beats Apple any day in look)
The key is in what you said there: "Highly customized". We need Ubuntu to "beat apple" out of the box, not after extensive customization. Of course, you should be allowed to customize all you want, but the default look should be beautiful out-of-the-box for as many people as possible.

> **azurehi said:**
> Guess I will stop my whining and live with 10.04 for the remainder of it's life and/or keeping looking for other distros.  I posted a topic in Beginner's asking what hardware would be best for 11.04 and beyond...there appears to be no definite answer.
If the problem is drivers, moving to another distro doesn't solve the problem.

### Post by NCLI on 2011-03-22
> **beew said:**
> Most people don't even know about Linux. Publicity has nothing to do with UI.
You can't do nice commercials if you don't have a nice UI. You'll get bad reviews if you have a bad UI.

Both commercials and reviews created publicity.


> Yeah well I would like to know what well known PC are made for Linux (system 760? Most people haven't even heard of it. )
There is plenty of [Ubuntu Certified hardware]("http://www.ubuntu.com/certification/") out there, so your point is moot.
> 
Unlike MAc, Linux is not inherently tied to any hardware, it is its strength, but it also means that Ubuntu (or name your favourite distro) cannot provide the level of support and babysitting that Apple does (and Linux is free so it doesn't come with customer support) . This is important for many non tech savy users, it has nothing to do with UI.
Of course it can. The difference is that with OSX, you're forced to use Apple's hardware. With Ubuntu, you can buy a [certified device]("http://www.ubuntu.com/certification/"), or you can build your own, though you'd probably want to look at [the list of supported components]("http://www.ubuntu.com/certification/catalog") before doing so.

> It is not doing it, maybe it doesn't have enough resource? Would you rather pay for its advertising budget to mount an Apple style marketing campaign, say by paying $100 whenever you download a live iso?  I wouldn't.
Yes, yes I would. Not by force mind you, but by donating. I've donated several thousand dollars to GNOME over the years(Though I'm probably going to stop donating to them, but that's for another thread), bought lots merchandise and cd's in Canonical's online store, pay for an extra 20 GB in UbuntuOne, and almost always buy music through their store.

> But in any case, it has nothing to do with UI.
YES.IT.DOES.
> Maybe to you. How many Mac users know about hardware design to know if it is beautiful or not?  Simplicity will come with Linux if you don't have to fiddle around with drivers and worry about hardware compatibility, while it is not Linux's fault it has nothing to do with UI.

You don't have to know *anything* about design to know that you think something is beautiful. That's just stupid.

If you look at something and think "Wow, that's pretty", you think it's beautiful, and you're just as qualified to think so as the art critic standing next to you thinking "Wow, that's butt-ugly".

In this case, there are more people thinking "Wow, that's beautiful", which is what I said.
> 
And by the way, Linux does not design its hardware, beautiful or not. So again it proves my point that it has nothing to with the UI.
I wasn't just talking about hardware, OSX is also considered by many to have a beautiful UI. 

However, I specifically mentioned hardware because part of what is holding Ubuntu back is hardware, in stores, looking incredibly pretty and running Ubuntu. THAT is what makes most people switch.
> As for security Linux is just as secure as Mac if not better.
Certainly.

> How do you know?  If I want an interface that looks like a Mac why wouldn't I buy a Mac in the first place? If i am too cheap then perhaps I wouldn't care so much about look? (Win XP still has the biggest market share and it is butt ugly)
When did I say that Unity looks like a Mac? There a similarities, yes, but it handles, very, very differently.

I just said a beautiful UI, not necessarily in the same way the the OSX UI is beautiful.

Also, there are plenty of reasons not to want a Mac, numero uno for most people being price.

Oh, and the main reason why XP has such a huge marketshare is probably that businesses are still scared of upgrading after the whole Vista debacle. However, what I'm talking about here is not businesses, who only care about efficiency. I'm talking about ordinary people walking into a store to buy a new computer.

> Aside from the look what about usability? With Unity you need to know and remember many hotkeys to do common things efficiently on a desktop. Do you think this is a selling point to people who are used to point a click?
Bull, I very rarely use hotkeys in Unity, other than alt+F2, but I used that just as much in Gnome 2.

>  Mac allows the user to move the dock anywhere (as do docky, Cairo dock and Awn) but not Unity, how is that for customizability? I am all for new interface and innovation, but it is a step backward if it comes at a cost of functionality.
Functionality first, then comes customizability. It's already quite customizable, though you are correct that it currently cannot be moved. However, this is only the first iteration of the design(They had so much trouble with mutter in 10.10 that I find it unfair to count it.), and by the time we reach 12.04, it will be ready.

EDIT: 
> **beew said:**
> These are OEM. But the hardwares are not custom made for Linux like the Apple hardwares are custom made for the Mac.

> **lucazade said:**
> Apple hardwares are common pieces and also overpriced!
Linux doesn't need custom made hw.. it can run also on my microwave hoover!

> **beew said:**
> No disagreement with you there.
Wait, what!? I don't think you get exactly what lucazade is saying here beew, so I'll make it clear: A mac is simply normal components put into a very well designed case, with working drivers written by Apple. The chips inside a Mac are no different from the chips inside a normal PC, it's just been selected by Apple to be used in its products. It's normal RAM, normal CPUs, normal GPUs, etc.

### Post by jerrylamos on 2011-03-23
Yes, running Natty Alpha 3 + updates on 4 test pc's.

Unity 3D on a Compaq Presario that's fast enough and late enough to handle Unity 3D compiz.  Looking for Alpha bugs example Compiz crashes.

Unity 2D on a desktop 2 gHz and on a 1.5 gHz thinkpad T40.  Neither one will run Compiz ultra fancy "eye candy" code.  I don't much like Unity however I'm running it on these pc's which CD Live puts into Classic mode.  As it stands Unity 2D is not on the install CD's.

Unity 3D and Unity 2D on a Acer Aspire netbook.  With Unity 3D, Compiz uses up 3% go 7% processor cycles when I'm doing full screen applications with nothing Compiz is doing visible.  Why is Compiz running at all with nothing to do?  I want those processor cycles for my applications.

Current vague impression Unity 2D faster and crisper than 3D, however I haven't done any measurements.

Now for easy quick fast function Gnome desktop clearly superior, however I'm running Unity anyway.

Jerry

### Post by Dragonbite on 2011-03-24
> **BigCityCat said:**
> Has anyone considered using Jolicloud as an alternative. It's essentially Ubuntu that focuses on the cloud, but from what I can tell you can do everything you do in ubuntu. Has a really sleek interface. It's designed for netbooks but I use it on my laptop and it works really nice.

[http://www.jolicloud.com/](http://www.jolicloud.com/)

After using ChromeOS for the past few months, I'm mixed on the Jolicloud.

On the one side Jolicloud offers the advantage of providing the power of local applications as well as easy file management.

On the other side, these apps are not accessible from alternative computers unless you use the Cloud-based apps and it looks to me like Jolicloud is trying to blur the lines between the two so you're not 100% sure if it is local or in the cloud.

With ChromeOS I know it is 99% in the cloud (and getting better each time) and thus is accessible from anywhere (or any Chrome/Chromium browser).

Can Jolicloud be merged with UbuntuOne so that which is done locally is sync'd online automatically?  Even Box.net or Dropbox would be good alternatives.

### Post by Lucradia on 2011-03-24
I used Joli OS. Doesn't have a trash system; no terminal; all of everything is basically in the cloud. You cannot install other repos either. You must install apps via the browser.

I switched to Xubuntu, immediately after. Also, you cannot delete your Jolicloud Account, nor remove devices once you pair them with Jolicloud.

### Post by BigCityCat on 2011-03-24
> **Lucradia said:**
> I used Joli OS. Doesn't have a trash system; no terminal; all of everything is basically in the cloud. You cannot install other repos either. You must install apps via the browser.

I switched to Xubuntu, immediately after. Also, you cannot delete your Jolicloud Account, nor remove devices once you pair them with Jolicloud.

Not true. You have to click on the folder local apps and then the folder more apps. Then nautilus opens and all the apps that come with ubuntu are in that folder. Also you can add ppa's by using the terminal but they have to be copy and pasted into the software sources file which is located in a different location (forget where). I'm using firefox 4 from the stable ppa in jolicloud right now.

### Post by BigCityCat on 2011-03-24
> **Lucradia said:**
> Would you kindly tell me, step by step, to find local apps, if it's removed from the jolicloud apps page that shows up when I first login to Joli OS?

The first desktop you see when you log in where all your web apps show up. At the bottom of that screen and in the middle should be a couple of dots depending on how many app you have installed. Click those dots and there is an icon called Local Apps. After you click on that it has another folder that says More Apps. When you click on that nautilus opens and all the local apps are located in there.

You have to be using the installed version of jolicloud. You can't be using it through a browser to access local apps.
