### Post by beew on 2011-03-26
> **Random_Dude said:**
> This thread reminds me of the time when everyone went nuts over the change in the placement of the window buttons back in 10.04. :lolflag:

This is a very shallow observation. The button change is trivial and you can change it back with a click in the gconf-editor.

Unity  however is a major overhaul of the UI and it goes beyond cosmetic changes of the interface.  There are many loss functionalities and regressions at this point and it is natural for people to complain (and I think this is expected from Canonical and it probably wants to get the feed backs that way else why make Unity the default when it is obviously not ready in that it still lacks so many features)

I know the classic desktop is one click away, but unless someone forks it it will die eventually (probably very soon). Since Unity is the future face of Ubuntu users may as well trying to get the features they want in Unity instead of switching to the classic desktop.

P.S. Even though I have some problems with Unity at this point it is still much better than the Gnome-shell and I actually am thankful that Canonical has taken the bold step to do their in house development, it does offer more choice to the users.

---

### Post by blueridgedog on 2011-03-29
What we have now, is not what we had before.  As those that come along wish to add their own innovation to the art that is Linux, we should not cling so strongly to that innovation that we added ourselves.  We have helped create a dynamic and evolving form of computing.  As that form evolves, we should take pride in the audacity and vision of those that push it forward.  Let it grow!

Most of my experience with today's up and coming computer user is all about searching versus rigid structure.  Unity, Gnome3, KDE 4 all build upon the fact that a generation of searchers is upon us and the future is in a fluid OS that is more based on what you want to do than what you know how to make it do.  

The future is different than the present and it will be made by those that have the drive, will and courage to make it happen.  I love seeing the passion of the Linux community now and the places it is going.  Would I take Linux in these directions?  No, but I thank the powers and spirits that be that others have that drive.  I may not like Unity or XXXX but I love what it represents (and I like being pushed out of my own personal comfort zone!).

### Post by d1g1t on 2011-03-30
@NightwishFan: I'm sure they'd like to know what users think before final release. :P

I switched back to the classic desktop. The UI/UX change was pretty frustrating
* I couldn't use most of the panel applets anymore
* The application shortcuts I had in my panel previously now takes a lot of space in the launcher, and I need it to scroll to select the open windows
* I'm pretty used to using Alt+F2, typing out half a command and hitting enter. On unity, I need to use the mouse to select the program I want to run.

New users will probably like Unity a lot more than older users :P

### Post by Sophont on 2011-03-30
> **blueridgedog said:**
> I bought a mac about a year ago and really had a hard time with the global menu.  However, after using it for a while, I realized that it was efficient to have it in one place and what I did not like about it was that it was simply different that what I was accustomed to for decades.

I have to disagree with the global menu being efficient.

You got used to it and it works for you - great. No problem.

And let's be real neither pro or contra global-menu proponents will be able to argue that that daily productivity will be much affected either way. It's not a big deal beyond a few seconds of extra clicks and/or mouse movements per day.

But here is why I can't accept the more efficient argument: You have several open windows. With global menu you always have to click on the next app to activate it, then go up to select from menu. With local menu you just go to app and directly click on the menu. Plus you can see your menu choices all the time. And the extra pixel area this takes up is irrelevant on a modern monitor (my position changes with Netbooks - I have much less problem with global menu there). And that's why "Fitts Law" is silly and BS.

Obviously this issue affects people differently depending on whether their workflow is either using a single maximized window almost all the time - or constantly juggling several windows in windowed (parts of full screen) state. But people who tend to use maximized/fullscreen windows all the time already have a de-facto global menu.

I can work with a global menu - it doesn't really hinder my work much. It's just a constant small annoyance. The lesser of 2 solutions. And except for Netbooks there is just not a good reason to choose the lesser option.

---

### Post by Sophont on 2011-03-30
> **handy said:**
> If you don't like unity, then choose to use Ubuntu's version of classic Gnome when you log in.

It you don't like having to make an extra click or so to start your system the way you want it to be, then find the how-to to make it start as classic Gnome by default.

I've posted it somewhere (not this thread) in the past.

Obviously I felt like visiting the storm in the tea cup again. :rolleyes:

I understand were you are coming from.
But defaults matter.

Having to choose Classic is not the problem. It will be just one more of a handful of options I select after a new install. So far no problem really.

But what do you think will happen to "classic" after all major distros went either Gnome Shell (sigh) or Unity (mostly fine with me - except for the damn annoying global menu).

When classic is no longer widely used/tested/developed for it *will* degrade over time. It will get less attention and no focus. Bugs will creep up and the lack of TLC will make it look and behave less well with every upgrade-cycle.

Stuff like notifications will be optimized to look and behave great with the GnomeShell/Unity - while classic will be an after-thought. Bugs will not be marked critical/blocking and linger more for the "deprecated" old UI.

It's not about having to make a quick option change after an install once in a while. And it's very different from button positions on the title bar (left or right never bothered me to begin with and is not a fundamental change).

After every install I set Toolbars to only show Icons, Update-Manager to show as Icon instead of just popping up the full window, remove the bottom panel (never understood why Default Gnome uses two panels - I put window switcher on top panel and drop the bottom panel - done), get Nautilus to show editable paths, etc... That's all small change and never got a single complain out of me.

### Post by Sophont on 2011-03-30
> **beew said:**
> But the thing is they DON'T HAVE TO COPY. Now my 10.10 box has full Compiz with the cube and 3d switcher. These features have been the trade mark for Ubuntu for quite sometimes if you look at how many Youtube videos are made. These features are better than anything you can find in Windows and Mac and if you like a dock add dockey, awn or the Cairo dock. Anyone of those are more functional, more customizable and better looking than the Unity bar.

But now with Unity the Compiz cube doesn't even work (can be enabled but doesn't work properly) and some smart-a** lectured me on some bs about design paradigm on the other thread (because he doesn't use it) and the 3d shift switcher is no longer 3d.

It is one thing to borrow quite another to give up your own strength in order to become a bad imitation. If these so called "new features" are so important to new users why wouldn't they just get a Mac or Windows?

This.
Compiz and the cube brought a ton of new users who tried out Ubuntu/Linux for the first time just because they saw a cool video on Youtube (which is awash with Compiz show-off videos and comparisons with Windows) while XP was getting stale and Vista drove users away from Windows.

Gnome-Do (plus docky) is also one of the best things that ever happened to Linux (don't care that it was inspired by Quicksilver). Combining a shiny icon bar with optional fast text selection.

Between those innovations Linux had a distinctive, cool and efficient interface.

Check out classic and classic w/o effects from the login screen. A few easy tweaks mentioned here also:

### Post by peacewithall on 2011-03-30
> **Sophont said:**
> 
But here is why I can't accept the more efficient argument: You have several open windows. With global menu you always have to click on the next app to activate it, then go up to select from menu. With local menu you just go to app and directly click on the menu.

This is one of the pains of using Unity, and what I'm about to say has probably already been said many times, which means maybe it is a problem. I can't see why a non maximised window loses its menu. For example if you are editing a script or maybe a document, and you are using a couple of other open documents as reference, or to quote sentences etc., you have to click on that window, but instead of staying on that window, you have to move the cursor to the top of the screen to access its menu, and this has to be done each time another window menu needs to be accessed. 

Editing in applications such as GIMP, where it may be required to quickly move between windows, and open, new files in the chosen window are also equally effected. I've seen the arguments such as learning short-cuts, and thats great, but that type of argument is questioning the need for a menu at all?, and I think we do need application menu's:P.

I understand a maximized window integrating its menu within the panel though, saving space. But there really should be an option at least, to stop the menu grabbing behaviour on non-maximised windows in my opinion.

### Post by Mozai on 2011-03-31
I guess I'll add my experience to the heap.

- The sliding dock is distracting.  I'm confident I'm not the only one who feels this way: mammal eyes track motion automatically.
- How on Earth do I tell it to change the auto-hide behaviour?  Where's the "settings" button?
- It imported the launch shortcuts from GNOME panel.  good, good.  But when I click one of those launch shortcuts, the side-panel highlight a  different icon somewhere else, and that other icon gets the little arrow for a running task.  (I'm willing to accept "you're not a typical user" for this quirk.)
- I miss having a hotkey that I could use to type in a command and not have to worry about adding 'nohup' before and '&' after.
- Why is my name in the upper-right corner?  Why can't I get rid of it?  And why is it asking me about changing my 'available/invisible/busy' status?  I found out this is used for the package 'empathy' but I don't have it installed, at all.  It must assume that I'll never not use it, since there's a chunk of the interface that's always reserved for it.

Here's my biggest beef:
- I click on the 'Terminal' icon, it starts a nice 80x24 instance of 'gnome-terminal'.  I click on the 'Terminal' icon again, it brings that first instance to the foreground.  So... how do I start a second terminal?  Must I do something very different than how I started the first terminal?

Something I'd like to see:
- an "introduction to Unity for GTK/KDE/xfce users."  Unity's interface has different "vocabulary" to do things, which I haven't seen anywhere else; it would be nice to have a phrasebook so I know how to say "maximize this window" in Unity-language.  The following are just a few things that surprised me during my first hour:
 -- dragging an icon OFF the dock to move it to a new place ON the dock
 -- the idea that you can't just add arbitrary launch icons to the dock, you can only turn task icons into launch icons while the task is running
 -- maximizing a window by dragging it's titlebar up to the top panel, 
 -- left-click-and-hold-and-drag to scroll the dock icons up and down despite the absence of any scrollbar or other indicators of scrolling behaviour
 -- what the difference is between a little triangle on the right side of an icon, and on the left side of an icon

I'll be switching back to what they call "Ubuntu Classic."  I'm sure I could learn the new way Unity does things, but it seems limited, and it makes assumptions about me (like "you will always have empathy installed" and "you will only ever have one instance of any program running") that I'd rather not have to overcome.

---

To open a second instance of a program, just middle click the icon.

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-04-01
Heh!  This is where (almost) everyone is making a mistake, including the devs.

NEVER HIDE the icons on the left (however you describe them - Launcher - Dashboard - whatever)!

Look, all you have to do is install CSSM (this should have been been done by default), then:

[LIST]
[*]Go to the Ubuntu Unity Plugin 
[*]Hide Launcher: Never
[*]Backlight Mode: Backlight Always Off
[*]Launch Animation: Pulse Until Running
[*]Urgent Animation: None
[*]Panel Opacity: 0.0000
[*]Launcher icon size: 32
[*]Fade Animation: Fade on bfb and Slide
[*]Dash Blur: No Blur
[/LIST]

All of a sudden... you will love Unity!  ;)

BTW, implement these settings, and the icons will take up very little real estate, plus everything will work.

### Post by NexusN on 2011-04-02
I am sorry but I don't know where I can express my opinions on the Ubuntu 11.04 Unity, so I open a thread here to talk about it.
If you find it unsuitable to be post here, hope you would kindly relocate it to the correct board.
Thank you.

On trying the ubuntu 11.04 beta today by upgrading from 10.10(x64),
I took a glance on the unity bar.
Thanks to everyone who put efforts on it, the bar is not bad, but I think there are room for improvement.

First, on opening a new app(say a Chrome Browser), on pressing the opened apps icon of Chrome, I cannot minimize it and that is inconvenient. Also, I cannot switch between opened browsers by pressing the icon.

Second, on more launchers added to the bar, some of them get flipped to save space while it slows down the visual effects much on my computer(Core 2 Duo T6500, NV GT130M). ON deceasing the number of icons to an extent that no flipped icons are spotted, it loads faster.

Third, for many launchers on the bar, I have to drag it or scroll many wheels to move down, that is a hard work when I want to access some launchers at the bottom. Maybe it can be changed to touching the top or bottom of the unity bar to make it scroll(higher scroll rate is preferred).

Sorry for plenty of my words, I don't mean to rude but I just want to share some experience on it.

Thank you for your reading.

---

### Post by GnuDragon on 2011-04-02
The unity global menu top bar seems just like the Apple bar on my Macbook only not as polished in its implementation; there's a few things that don't quite work and auto-hiding behaviour (of the dock) is plain weird.

Coupled with the combined task bar/dock, it feels Mac-ish but just not as well implemented. It's not smooth or unified and the UI gets in your way in a way that Snow Leo's doesn't; Snow Leo just blends into the background and you forget it's even there. Hope they get it fixed up for release.
 
I note that compiz 'window scaling' task switcher (with the thumbnail grid) is almost the same as Expose (apple task switcher), too but doesn't have the ability to sort by app, or show only windows from certain apps. I have that turned on because i simply can't use the alt+tab "line up all the windows in a row and hope you can somehow find the right one to click on". I need my mini-thumbnail grid to quickly select the right window ;-)

The app menu is quite bad actually, the way it only shows so few apps on the screen and have to click to find others. Not that it's bad but there's better ways of dividing up that menu. That's easy to fix by editing start menu categories though.

Kinda annoyed they didn't make their own UI and just copying Mac OS one - and not properly, it's "clunky" which I can't explain but that's how it feels. All we need now is Coverflow view for folders but I guess that one will have to wait for the outcome of the patent case? :-)

Still at least Ubuntu understands what to do when you want to copy a folder over another folder of the same name - it merges the contents instead of over-writing (Apple weirdness, and it won't be 'fixed' until Lion)

Summary - turn on compiz's window scaling task switcher and have you a mac os beta UI :) or just something like looks similar but feels clunky - also you don't of course have the massive amount of hidden shortcuts mac  os has, instead those are all on menus which makes them feel less  streamlined.

---

### Post by ankspo71 on 2011-04-02
Hi,
If you install compizconfig-settings-manager (ccsm), you will find a plugin for Unity inside it. This is where you can configure some settings for Unity. You can change the size of the icons on the Unity dock and if you make them smaller that might help with your 'second' and 'third' observations. (smaller icons = no flipped icons and no scrolling or dragging).
Hope this helps.

PS. You can find most discussions about Ubuntu 11.04 and Unity here:
_[Ubuntuforums.org > Development & Programming > Natty Narwhal Testing and Discussion]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=394")_

### Post by shipdit on 2011-04-02

### Post by Jay Car on 2011-04-02
> **shipdit said:**
> "...Although I wonder sometimes if all those choices are a bad thing. 

Here's the thing about choice (from my own little-ol-lady viewpoint). With so many people coming from a system of minimal-to-no choices, suddenly being surrounded by choices of every description can feel very overwhelming. I know this because that was how I felt at first. The ability to chose and change things was a stunning revelation. 

If I hadn't already understood the basics of why moving away from Microsoft products was important to my small business, that sense of being overwhelmed with too much choice might have stopped me in my tracks...

...and it MAY stop others, at first, but the most important thing they'll take away from the experience is the new knowledge that there ARE choices.  That may be something they never knew before. That knowledge will likely bring them back, one day, to try it out again. 

The hardest part of making changes in our lives is often the need to unlearn old habits.  The fear of choice is an old habit that really isn't that hard to break. It just goes with the new territory.

> **shipdit said:**
> I mean, if Ubuntu created one desktop (not borrowed someone else's) and then worked at it until it was solid and usable and addressed the majority of users' wants and needs...why, they might just pick up some permanent users. 

Except for the "one desktop" thing, which would be silly, I believe they've already done the rest, and continue to do so. I don't always like the changes they make, but I ALWAYS like that they are willing to try new things.  And I like being able to chose whether to use those changes, or not. I'm also happy to be able to change my mind and give the new stuff another go.  Those choices are where the value (and fun!) resides, for me. 

> **shipdit said:**
> I'd love to see Ubuntu solidify into the OS that it has the potential to be.  Save me from having to buy these $2,000 laptops (although, in all fairness, Ubuntu would have to be REALLY GOOD for me to give up OS X).  

Still, one can hope.  :)

Well, to be honest, your apparent fear of "too much choice" is the only thing that's rationalizing your $2000 expenditure...some people like to brag about how much money they spend, others like to brag about how much money they save.  It really has nothing to do with Ubuntu's potential. I believe it's already "REALLY GOOD"...Personally, I'm happier to spend my $2000 on donations to organizations that help create all that overwhelming amount of choice (that you wish would go away).

I guess it's just a matter of priorities. My own priority is freedom of choice.

---

### Post by rcayea on 2011-04-02
> **cariboo907 said:**
> To those that say Unity is clunky, how about telling us what you find clunky about it. It may be that you just don't know how to use it properly.

"those" would be me. 

What I find clunky is that the navigation isn't always responsive the way a gnome desktop or another desktop may be. Using the word clunky to me means I am trying to say that the desktop feels, at times, that it is like a set of gears. The gears work, slow down, stop, no wait, they go full speed, slow, fast, stop. It just seems to be like that from my experience.

---

### Post by shipdit on 2011-04-02
> **Jay Car said:**
> 
Except for the "one desktop" thing, which would be silly...

Let me clarify.  I mean one desktop environment.  Ten DE's means ten times the bugs.  Don't believe me?  Go install KDE.  I'll wait.  :)

> **Jay Car said:**
> 
Well, to be honest, your apparent fear of "too much choice" is the only thing that's rationalizing your $2000 expenditure...some people like to brag about how much money they spend, others like to brag about how much money they save.  It really has nothing to do with Ubuntu's potential. I believe it's already "REALLY GOOD"...Personally, I'm happier to spend my $2000 on donations to organizations that help create all that overwhelming amount of choice (that you wish would go away).

I guess it's just a matter of priorities. My own priority is freedom of choice.

Not bragging.  I love my MacBook.  Construction-wise, it's the best machine on the market (aluminum body, multi-touch trackpad with gestures, etc).  OS X is a damn fine OS, too.

But I'm not bragging.  And I don't fear too much choice.  But I think you'd have to be dense to think that too much choice is a good thing.  It bogs the user down in an endless quagmire of possibilities, when what he or she ought to be doing is putting his or her computer to work rather than endlessly customizing it.  

As for your presumption...it offends me.  I don't wish choice would go away. I'm not saying all these DE's should disappear.  I'm saying Ubuntu needs to pick one and then work it until it's stable and awesome.  KDE and Enlightenment and XFCE can stay and do their thing...but if Ubuntu is going with Unity, it ought to go with Unity and nothing else.  No switching back and forth to classic mode.  Making every new version of Ubuntu legacy compatible is a hindrance.  It's why Apple is gaining more market share every day, despite their outrageous prices:  they're willing to let go of the old in favor of the new.  

I like saving money as much as the next guy, but if I have to endlessly customize/fix/debug my computer, I'm not saving money.  Sure, the OS is free...but I'm wasting my time as a beta tester, reporting bugs that never get fixed and struggling constantly with an OS that can't make up its mind.  

I doubt I'll persuade you to at least see my point of view (even if you don't agree with it), and that's fine.  I was just giving my opinion.  

And you know what they say about opinions, right?  They're like butts:  everybody has one, and most of them stink.  Including mine.  And yours.  

:)

EDIT:  And as for my "$2,000 expenditure"...I'm happier with my MacBook than I ever was with Windows or Linux.  But still, spending two grand on a laptop pisses me off.  If Ubuntu could get its stuff together, I'd be back in a minute.  At least dual booting, anyway.  :)

---

### Post by cariboo on 2011-04-02
> **rcayea said:**
> "those" would be me. 

What I find clunky is that the navigation isn't always responsive the way a gnome desktop or another desktop may be. Using the word clunky to me means I am trying to say that the desktop feels, at times, that it is like a set of gears. The gears work, slow down, stop, no wait, they go full speed, slow, fast, stop. It just seems to be like that from my experience.

I wasn't pointing at you in particular, there are others that have said the same thing. I think a lot of what people consider clunky, is trying to use Unity and Gnome-shell the same way they've used computers since Windows 95, that just doesn't work anymore. the UI is changing on all platforms, and trying to make it work the same way as it always has doesn't work anymore.

I fix WIndows problems for a living, I find it's a lot easier when you are helping someone over the phone, to ask them to click Start and type explorer in the search box, than it is to ask them to click Start-> Accessories->Windows Explorer. In a lot of cases the reaction is "cool, I didn't know I could do that". Unity and Gnome-shell work much the same way.

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-04-02
> **cariboo907 said:**
> The UI is changing on **[COLOR="Red"]all platforms[/COLOR]** [...]
This is sooooo true!

Fact NOT opinion:  Smart phone users are more numerous than computer users, by many orders of magnitude!

"So what?" you might ask...


The UI on computers and smart phones are melding together.  Soon, they will be indistinguishable.

Computer UIs are looking more like smart phones, and smart phones UIs are becoming more like computers.


Somebody asked about my Conky (above).  You might be surprised to know...

The majority of fonts I used were stripped out of the Android SDK, e.g. they are smart phone fonts.  They scale beautifully!  LoL!


Anyway, regardless of what OS you are using, it WILL start looking more n' more like a cell phone, as time goes on -- guaranteed!


Get used to it...  :D

### Post by dash86no on 2011-04-02
Upgraded to 11.04 last evening. 

Was keen to give Unity a good run to ensure I could make fair comments, but it annoyed my so much I went back to gnome after 30 minutes. 

Since I put program launching icons in the top gnome bar, and auto-hide the bottom bar, I'm pretty sure I'm making better use of my 1366 x 768 screen space already. 

I suspect the Ubuntu project leaders have it as their ultimate mission (whether they are conscious of it or not) to replicate the mac experience with Ubuntu. 

Thing is, I'm not poor, and if I wanted a Mac I'd just go out and buy one. I use Ubuntu because I look for more from my computing experience than a dock and some pretty icons. I suspected this was (Mark's?) plan back when they moved the close/min/max buttons over to the left of the windows, and at the same time changed the default theme to be purple--which didn't really bother me--but this move with trying to rip off the silly doc in unity is a step too far. 

You can never out do apple at being apple. I think the strengths of Ubuntu are speed, community spirit, ability to customize, the depth/breath of the repos, speed of evolution. 

I suspect the goal of Ubuntu is to finally be able to monetize their captive audience through selling music, games, software, movies, and other media through the Ubuntu One store. I sincerely hope that they succeed in this mission, as they have thus far created a great product and it's not sustainable to keep giving it away for free with no financial return. Like I said, I'm not poor, and would actually pay for Ubuntu (anything up to 50 USD I reckon; maybe 100USD for the server edition.) per release. I know that's against their ethos, though, so I have been keen to pay Canonical back through other means--but really, is anyone actually using the Ubuntu One store? I tried to search for a few mainstream things and came up blank earlier today. 

My dream is that I can click on an "Ubuntu One" icon, search for something like "cars 2", click "yes" to some 3USD option to rent for 48 hours, then watch it through banshee or some other media player. Or purchase the latest slim shady album for 7USD. My dream is that Canonical can make the shopping experience as smooth as Amazon or apple itunes so that I can start using them as regularly as I use Amazon. (I don't use itunes as I dislike apple, although you have to respect what they've done with that). 
However, Ubuntu One is currently a LONG WAY from that goal.

ETA: Is it actually Ubuntu One? I know that was the file sharing thing they came up with. I wanted to pay them for that but I seem to remember that it was only 10GB or something like that on offer so didn't bother. I am a user of AWS though so if a company is willing to do it right I'm willing to pay for the cloud. 

I thing Canonical need to hire a few marketing people who know what they are doing and start branding this a little better. I read the other day that the online music biz was worth 80 billion dollars--it would be great if the Canonical guys could start benefiting from a little of that.

---

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-04-04
> **Derspankster said:**
> Banshee will not play network media so I have no use for it even though it's now the default player now.[...]
I can't really say that I understand this statement.  Define "network media".  By my definition, it works fine.

Banshee et al. had a daunting task on their hands, and I think they've handled it quite admirably!

David Nielsen, aka gnomeuser, has been active, open, and honest, in these forums, regarding the Banshee player.

He has clearly stated that Banshee will probably remain broken, until 11.04 final is locked-down -- The reason being, Natty is a moving target.

You need to remember, Natty is a dev release!

Ubu dev releases are NOT for the faint of heart.

If something isn't working in Banshee, make a bug report.  Don't stick your head in the sand.

### Post by sjosul on 2011-04-05
On the whole I quite like unity, though I too have reservations:

1. If the main taskbar (unity menu) is to be on the left then leaving desktop icons aligned to the left also is very sloppy - icons automatically placed on the desktop are part covered by the taskbar
2. The home button opens nautilus at the home folder, great. But once Nautilus is open, clicking the launcher doesn't give any options to open another instance
3. The search-based approach to application management works fine for me, but I lost some of the more obscure apps because their name and "role" or metadata name do not coincide (type crossover reaching for the series of apps linked to crossover linux and nothing; type cx and there it is...)
4. When compiz inevitably crashes, it would be best if Gnome took over, instead of leaving a dysfunctional desktop

I do like:
1. Lenses - they are only starting to emerge, but they promise much
2. I actually like the differences with OS X - I think the unity taskbar is cleaner-looking
3. I like the emerging compiz settings options (I brought back wobbly windows and set some desktop corner hotspots to show expose for instance)

---

### Post by ankspo71 on 2011-04-05
hi,
> **sjosul said:**
>  The home button opens nautilus at the home folder, great. But once Nautilus is open, clicking the launcher doesn't give any options to open another instance.

The middle click can open the additional home folder windows. The left click is reserved for exposing multiple windows across the screen like a window chooser. So if you middle click 3 times on your home icon to open 3 home folders, left click on that home icon again to switch between those 3 home folders. The clicks feel like they are backwards to me, but it's a nice feature I think. It's strange how firefox has a right click option for "open a new window" when the other icons don't though.

---

### Post by Dragonbite on 2011-04-05
I hope the Unity 2D is getting some lovin' too.  Since my hardware doesn't handle 3D (unless there have been changes fixing that) that is my only choice outside of classic KDE or Gnome 3 on Fedora. 

I'm using Unity 2D on my laptop now and it works for the most part, and I am withholding judgement so far (since it's 10.10's version).  

I couldn't pin Netbeans to the left bar, but I could pin MySQL Administrator.  Don't know if it is a Java thing or not.

It still doesn't seem efficient for non-widescreen systems. The little gained on the bottom is counterbalanced by the amount lost on the side (maybe if auto-hide is available?).  Some programs also don't scale well and run off the side of the window (hoping there's a horizontal scrollbar).

On my desktop widescreen system I don't have Unity, but I do have my icons loaded on the left side in a panel similar to Unity. One that system, so long as windows are not forced to be maximized, I think Unity would work just fine.

### Post by ErikNJ on 2011-04-05
While I like the design of Unity, I wonder how it will work when Ubuntu catches up with Gnome 3.  How is its dependency upon Compiz going to work when Gnome 3 uses mutter.

I can't help but fear that in the future, Unity will need continuous patching to keep up with the changes in Gnome.  Or, is the plan to just stick with Gnome 2.32 and Compiz forever?

...my one design complain - why do the menus for non-maximized windows still need to be on the top panel?  To me, that only makes sense for a maximized window.

---

### Post by Mozai on 2011-04-05
> **ErikNJ said:**
> 
I can't help but fear that in the future, Unity will need continuous patching to keep up with the changes in Gnome.

Keeping up with the Joneses is a recipe for failure.  I should hope Unity plans to forge ahead with their own design innovations.

> **ErikNJ said:**
> ...my one design complain - why do the menus for non-maximized windows still need to be on the top panel?  To me, that only makes sense for a maximized window.

That's only because you're used to applications having it's 'menu bar' piece in the main window.  If you were a Xerox/Lisa/Apple/OSX user, you would be more comfortable with the bar at the top automatically adjusting to the application you're using, so you don't have to mouse around or aim to get to the menu bar -- just roll up and there you are at the screen's edge.  (A well-known regret of Microsoft Windows 95 design was having it's 'start' button a few pixels away from the corner, so you couldn't just roll into it.  This is what happens when you try to imitate something without understanding it.)

People using GNOME have already been using the top bar as the menu bar for applications, with the 'Indicator Applet Appmenu' applet for gnome-panel.  This isn't something that got dragged in just for the sake of Unity.  (I'd be using it myself, except it doesn't seem to work all the time for Firefox & The GIMP.  Waitin' on bugfixes. )

---

### Post by blueridgedog on 2011-04-05
> **azurehi said:**
> The following discussion regarding 11.04 is very interesting and explains why I will not use Unity.

If you look at all of the author's posts/articles:

[http://search.theregister.co.uk/?author=Scott%20Gilbertson](http://search.theregister.co.uk/?author=Scott%20Gilbertson)

You can see a general trend that the most recent commentary is an extension of.  Some of the criticisms are valid.  For the price of a download and copying an image to a USB flash drive, you can have your own real world experience and make up your own mind about it.  

Throwing rocks at beta products or expecting an open source release to be tested and debugged to the extent implied is just hard to understand.  This is Linux.  This is us...we make this.  How did we end up with the expectation that releases have to be perfect?  

The rapid release schedule is intended (as I see it) to drive innovation.  The downside is that some (most?) releases fully mature after they are in wider use.  How else can you define what needs to be without a few thousand users making suggestions and working to chart a path toward fixes and improvements?  Input will be made, ideas will flow and Unity will develop, before and after some arbitrary date.

If bug fixes and stability are your cup of tea, there is Debian.  If you see features you want but can't find in a distro, roll your own with your own window manger, window decorator, dock and application collection.  If you want be part of a fast developing and highly integrated distro, there is Ubuntu.

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-04-06
> **ErikNJ said:**
> While I like the design of Unity, I wonder how it will work when Ubuntu catches up with Gnome 3.  How is its dependency upon Compiz going to work when Gnome 3 uses mutter.

Gnome 3 doesn't use Mutter, Gnome Shell uses Mutter. If you want the "Gnome 3 experience" as it seems it is being called these days, then you choose Gnome Shell. I'm kinda wondering, since I started seeing that term being used, if we are going to start seeing 'Are you experienced?'/'Have you ever been experienced?' stuff promoting Gnome 3 after it's release.

If you don't care about the Gnome 3 experience, there is a Gnome 3 version of the traditional panel, which is what distributions will probably ship with for people who want mutter/metacity+panel or need Metacity. Gnome panel has had work to bring it more in line with the panel in Gnome shell, so while it will look look like the old panel, you may not necessarily have all the old stuff depending on what you use.

Things seem to be a little broken in Debian ustable+experimental at the moment (where I have the Gnome 3 stuff installed) so I'm not sure exactly what functions you are supposed to have with the Gnome 3 version of the gnome-panel, currently I have a custom .xsession that starts gnome-settings-daemon, gnome-panel and fluxbox. The right click on gnome-panel doesn't show me a context menu so currently stuck with menu, clock, and notification/tray area on the top panel and have the task list applet and pager on the bottom panel.

If Gnome 3 had been more stable during the Natty development cycle, Unity and Compiz probably would have used more of it.

Up to now having the Ubuntu release schedule synced to the Gnome release schedule has not been a significant issue because the changes from one  Gnome release to the next are not all that drastic. With Gnome 3 however the amount and significance of the changes in the gnome 3 stuff made it too volatile to try to port Unity to when you consider the amount of effort going into Unity. The volatility of the Gnome 3 stuff relative to the Gnome and Ubuntu release schedules also created a huge amount of uncertainty as to whether the Gnome 3 stuff would be sorted out soon enough to allow the Ubuntu devs to sort the ditribution integration related issues by the time of the scheduled Ubuntu release.

Later, Seeker

```
moses@deunan:~$ apt-cache search unity-2d
libunity-2d-private-dev - Unity 2D shared library - development files
libunity-2d-private0 - Unity 2D shared library
libuqpanel-dev - dummy transitional package for libuqpanel-dev
unity-2d - Unity interface for non-accelerated graphics cards
unity-2d-launcher - Unity 2D Launcher
unity-2d-panel - Unity 2D Panel
unity-2d-places - Unity 2D Places
unity-2d-spread - Unity 2D Spread
libuqpanel0 - dummy transitional package for libuqpanel0
unity-2d-default-settings - Transitional package for upgrades to the new unity-2d package
```

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2011-04-06
> **paul_h_ward said:**
> I too have one gripe (unless someone who reads this knows how to fix it) - I HATE THE TASKBAR AT THE TOP

Whoa, there shouldn't be a "taskbar" at the top. There's a global menubar at the top, which also holds indicators and window management buttons when the current window is maximized.

There's a launcher/dock thingy on the left side.

But definitely no "taskbar" in Unity.

**To the OP:** You can minimize windows in Unity. You can also look at what windows are open for each specific application - just click the application's icon in the launcher twice, and the windows will expose themselves. You can also right-click the icon and the popup menu will show what windows are available.

### Post by Lucradia on 2011-04-07
> **Mark76 said:**
> Other desktops are available (3 in fact), and I don't see any of them going down the Unity/Gnome Shell root in the foreseeable.

More desktops than three are available, if you don't mind mucking about in CLI before you use xorg.

You only take into account XFCE, KDE and LXDE, the main DEs. What about noDE? Such as having a window manager + PCManFM?

Such as Openbox, Fluxbox, etc. Though, if you need fancy 3D effects, you'd be using emerald+compiz instead, as compiz can't play with any WMs except KWin and Metacity. (I've tried the whole making compiz execute a different WM through its config; never worked, always took away the window borders.)

ubuntu is more or less a respository of distributions, rather than a single distribution though. However, unlike Ubuntu, Fedora actually does GRUB right when I install it side-by-side. With Ubuntu, I have to recover GRUB, since Ubuntu can't detect Windows 7 properly (In fact, Fedora doesn't either, it uses the Recovery partion as Windows 7. If I try to change it to the real Windows 7 partition, it will just hang on the windows logo, forever.)

### Post by Dr.Geekit on 2011-04-07
** Hmm wonder why my post got moved here???? that's odd


This is my own random thought of what i'd like to see and if anyone would like to partake feel free.

With the new "unity" interface being all flashy and new, i'd like to see them update a few other aspects of gnome to sorta fit in with Unity.  Right now unity sort of looks like when an ugly girl puts on the right makeup to make itself look pretty.  It seems to me like that they get this great idea but fall just a few feet short of actually matching everything up.

My first idea on this is a basic one, a simple one, and of course it's my opinion and others may not agree but its very simple.

The screen savor comes on and your computer locks.  When you come back and move your mouse you get a sudo login screen where you enter your password (not sure the proper name for that).

That screen reminds me of windows 98.  IDKY, but it does.  With a nice fluffy splashy feature rich desktop, and then bam, something looks 20 years old.  It just makes me think that they spent all this time on the big picture, but over looked the little experiences.

I've sorta felt this way about the generic look to the windows theming.  Yeah the tool bar is awesome, and cool, and then.... here comes the rest the window, looks generic.  I know I can skin them to change the color but personally I don't think changing the color is enough.  Maybe I just need to dig more into skinning the system with themes and stuff.  But that takes allot of work and deciding and soon and so forth and truthfully I'd like to just install and go.  Build as I go.

Don't get me wrong but I love ubuntu, some of the themes I see are awesome.  There's always someone who put allot of work and customization into it.  But not everyone is artistic, no matter what choices I make I always see someone's who's I like better.

I think this is something that Mac and Windows has on Ubuntu/Linux in general.  As their OS's progress so does the artistic quality of their default system.

IDK just my thoughts.

What would you like to see upgraded by default as far as ascetics?

---

### Post by ePierre on 2011-04-07
Hello peeps,

as long as Unity and Gnome3 were legends (I mean, at alpha stages), I didn't really pay attention to any of them, enjoying my Ubuntu and its Gnome desktop environment.

But since Gnome3 has been officially released, I grabbed a LiveCD copy and tried it for an hour.

Then I decided to do the same with Ubuntu 11.04 beta1, since it comes with Unity.

I was expecting a lot of differences, but after an hour of use for each systems... I can't really tell.

Both have very minimal interface, both have the snap window function (if you grab a window to the left, right or top of the screen)...

If I really had to chose as of now, I would pick up Gnome3 because it feels clean and polished.

Which brings me to the question of this post: **Why did Canonical decide to make their own Desktop environment?** I couldn't find a clear answer anywhere...

Thanks for your answer!

P.S.: this question is NOT a troll, I would just like to know.

P.P.S: Apparently I'm not the only one wondering --> [http://www.techworld.com.au/article/382495/gnome_3_will_ubuntu_reconsider_/](http://www.techworld.com.au/article/382495/gnome_3_will_ubuntu_reconsider_/)

---

### Post by clgy15 on 2011-04-07
Iv noticed alot that there is a clear divisive line between those that like Unity and those that are against it. There seems to be no middle ground. It certainly looks like Ubuntu will lose some of its most avid followers over a design change. The two arguments seem as this (tell me if im wrong)

For Unity: The functionality is essentially the same, its a cool new design and I cant wait to try it out. Those who are against it are just worried about the big switch from the long standing gnome 2x. 

Against Unity: Compiz compiz compiz oh where is my compiz? I lose my functionality, I dont like the new design, I dont like the new toolbar thing and I want my gnome 2x.

Now im not trying to support one or the other and im most certainly not trying to offend anyone. But a change had to come at some time.

Anyways heres a great solution. Either you dont have to upgrade to the natty or ubuntu could publish the alternate cd to include Gnome 2x in the desktop enviroment option. Which they probably will. Yes yes of course its not the same since ubuntu will no longer be actively developing on gnome 2x. But even gnome is moving away from gnome 2x with 3 that just came out. Yes ubuntu probably went a little fast with such a change. I give you that.

Its not the end of the world :popcorn:

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-04-07
> **ErikNJ said:**
> That leaves the question of how well will Compiz play with Gnome 3?

If you are using Compiz you are not using Gnome Shell or Mutter, so no conflict there, the rest is just porting from the Gnome 2 stuff to the Gnome 3 stuff.

Some of the Gnome 3 stuff is already being used.

Some of the stuff was using GTK 3 earlier in the release then reverted to using GTK 2, I think largely because of the work involved in porting the Ubuntu default theme to GTK 3 and not wanting to tackle that on this development cycle with all the other stuff going on and as a result of that concerns of how it would look if you have a bunch of stuff using GTK 3 themes and a bunch of other stuff using GTK 2 themes when the themes are different. 

Later, Seeker

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-04-07
> **NightwishFan said:**
> All of Gnome 3 except the Shell is the same as ever, just newer. If you can avoid using the shell and find an alternate launcher compiz will work fine. This is what Unity is doing.

That not true....

Apparently there is a new documentation format.

Gsettings replaces gconf.

Some of the other things in this porting guide.....

[https://live.gnome.org/DevGnomeOrg/Gnome3PortingGuide](https://live.gnome.org/DevGnomeOrg/Gnome3PortingGuide)

Some of the things mentioned in the porting guide were a part of Gnome 2 and suggested for use in place of things that were scheduled for deprecation in Gnome 3 other things made their way into distributions and started being used that may not have been intended to be used with Gnome 2.

I think gsettings falls into the later category, it's been included in Ubuntu, I think for at least a couple of releases now, and some things are already using it.

Later, Seeker

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-04-07
> **ePierre said:**
> I was expecting a lot of differences [between Unity & Gnome3] but after an hour of use for each systems... I can't really tell. [...]

Which brings me to the question of this post: **Why did Canonical decide to make their own Desktop environment?** I couldn't find a clear answer anywhere...

Thanks for your answer!

P.S.: this question is NOT a troll, I would just like to know.

P.P.S: Apparently I'm not the only one wondering --> [http://www.techworld.com.au/article/382495/gnome_3_will_ubuntu_reconsider_/](http://www.techworld.com.au/article/382495/gnome_3_will_ubuntu_reconsider_/)
Long discussion, but the bottom line is...

It comes down to 2 major differences:

[LIST=1]
[*]Gnome3 doesn't have AppIndicators.  Unity does.

[*]Gnome3 lacks strong, mature, technical leadership.  Unity doesn't.
[/LIST]

If sticking push-pins in your eyes, and running blindly into traffic sounds appealing -- Gnome3 is for you.

If you prefer knowing if you're connected, what the time is, your wifi is solid, your battery is about to die, you have mail waiting, somebody is trying to chat with you, etc. etc. -- Unity is the way to go.  ;)

### Post by mw13068 on 2011-04-09
I've been using Ubuntu for a number of years, and I downloaded 11.04 beta and installed it on an extra drive to check it out.

Unity is awful. It's a terrible UI for anyone who actually knows how to use a computer. What makes it particularly heinous to me is the removal of the right-click context menu from the UI components. Who the hell wants to wade through 8 layers of "beautiful" MacOS-style screens to find (or not) some configuration program to change the icon used on a launcher button?

It seems like Ubuntu really wants to be an iPhone or an Apple UI now. Good luck with that.

I realize that unity can be disabled in favor of regular Gnome (which is an excellent and simple GUI), but the decision to make Unity the default is ridiculous. I'm going to revisit Debian rather than upgrading my Ubuntu system to this Unity travesty.

---

### Post by capngloval on 2011-04-10
I am just one of a growing movement of people that will instantly leave Ubuntu and use anything else that still gives us gnome.

Unity is a horrible replacement.

Unity currently does not allow me to change my theme, a big problem for me, becaus the buttons should be hard and fast on the right, that's where they belong.

I love Ubuntu, but If I have to i will install it, and rip it straight down to x, and forcibly install gnome on it, but since I am lazy enough not to do that, i'll probably go elsewhere, maybe PCLinuxOS.

Our entire linux club in town is planning to leave Ubuntu, save one, and that's because she uses mepis, not Ubuntu.

Please give us ear and keep unity only as an option, and not the default.

Their maybe better options than gnome, but it isn't unity. Unity doesn't even compare.

I hope I didn't sound too flaky, but talk is that gnome is going the way of the dinosaurs in Ubuntu, and it's replacement is like taking the family car and trading it in for a go kart.

Not a decent trade. Gnome is only about 1 million times better in practically every way.

I actually removed unity from my netbook version of ubuntu, and my netbook runs faster and is more usable.

-Denny

---

### Post by Philsoki on 2011-04-10
Hey Denny. They haven't removed GNOME from Ubuntu at all, only GNOME Shell. Unity is  just a shell that "sits" on top of Gnome 3. I'm not sure how closely your LUG follows the goings on in the Linux world, but Gnome themselves are in the process of discontinuing the Gnome 2.x you know and love. The panels are being replaced in favour of a Shell UI.

You'd be better off moving to Debian which will have the original Gnome 2.x in it's repo's for years to come. (Though, I think Ubuntu might as well). And there's always Linux Mint with the "Mint Menu."

Cheers.

and would hopefully keep the user base we have - without upsetting anyone .......

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-04-10
capngloval, there's seriously nothing to freak out about. 

* If you've played with Unity at all on your 10.10 machine, understand that what you were seeing was a early mockup using a completely different backend from Unity proper.

* Unity doesn't replace Gnome. Think of it as another blend of Gnome, like the Classic Desktop, which is managed through Compiz instead of using the Gnome Panel.

* The old interface still ships with in Natty, and even within Unity, the buttons can be moved to the right, although this won't apply to maximized windows, which don't have any decorations at all and have their buttons supplied by the panel.

* Xubuntu *is* Ubuntu. You're not talking about "switching to a different OS with a different DE", you're talking about "switching to a different DE." If you installed vanilla Natty and xubuntu-desktop, you'd end up with exactly the same thing. You could also do this now on your 10.10. Ubuntu, Xubuntu, and Kubuntu all share the same repos.

* Threatening to "leave" is a little childish, because you're presumably the only person in the thread who cares what OS your LUG uses, but I am concerned that if you're not all that well versed in how these elements of the OS operate now, you're only going to have more difficulty switching to another OS. Ubuntu is going to have the most support, both in terms of supported software and in terms of technical support. If you're worried about the Gnome Classic desktop being discontinued at some point in the future, try out some of the other DEs available. KDE's looking fairly shiny. = )

---

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-04-10
For Starbase1 in #7:

You can still switch between desktops using the mouse wheel - you just have to enable it.  (It's off by default because it doesn't play nice with some laptop trackpads.)
In CCSM, go to Viewport Switcher, and click on the Desktop-based Viewport Switching.  You can enable it there.  I prefer Next/Previous to Left/Right since the former wraps around while the latter does not.

A note of caution however: It is not a good idea to try enabling the cube in a unity session.  I kinda killed mine trying it out!

---

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-04-10
I think by 'taskbar' he meant panel.  As far as I know it's not possible right now to move it or customize it.  I'm hoping that will change at some point.  
A big difference is the use of the indicator-[] system for deciding what goes on the panel - for example, to get a weather report you have to install indicator-weather.  Others are becoming available, but aren't in the repos yet.  (I think there's an indicator-system out there if you like having a CPU monitor in the panel.)
One tip: if you're used to having applications with icons in the notification area, you can get them back using the dconf-editor.  There's a 'whitelist' for the unity panel.  (You can set the whitelist to 'all' if you're aren't feeling overcome by systray clutter.)

---

### Post by Dragonbite on 2011-04-11
> **NightwishFan said:**
> To be honest I would be surprised if down the road Debian did not package Unity. 

If it is any indicator, Fedora AND openSUSE spent some time trying to package Unity for their distribution but stopped. I think it had more to do with Unity being under such heavy development that it was too much work to try and keep up with it.

There's no use in looking around for who is going to continue to offer Gnome 2, ... it's a goner.  The only way to "extend" the life of using Gnome 2 is to use a ditro that doesn't change often (like CentOS/Red Hat) or stick with LTS (10.04 supported for another 2 years!).

Xfce and KDE may get some benefit from this, just like Gnome got benefit when KDE went to 4 but I think eventually things will settle down and Gnome, Xfce and KDE's usage will end up right about where it is now.

### Post by 3Miro on 2011-04-11
You can change the theme and button positions of Unity. Unity is nothing more or less than a side dock, top panel and new menu for Gnome. Unity is Gnome ...

You still have the regular Gnome preferences as well as CCSM for the more advanced options. Also, check the side of my windows buttons (select appearance, select "clearlooks", select "customize", select "ambiance" for both controls and windows borders, takes 10 seconds tops).

### Post by Dupointx on 2011-04-11
Well after waiting some time, I finally decided to give Unity a try. I would usually wait till a release to do this, but with all the talk going around I couldn't resist downloading the beta and giving it a spin. So here is what I think about Unity. [This is in no particular order btw, just wrote things down on a note pad as I thought it.]

Launching a second instance of an app from the dock is tricky. I was lucky to read that you need to use the middle mouse button to do it, but I was left wondering "what are you going to do if you have a laptop and only left-right buttons?". I tested it out and didn't find any combination of alt ctrl or super + click would work.

The auto-hide feature of the global menu was a pain. I'm a right handed person and it's stupid that to do anything you have to constantly put the courser in the top left. Why was it even important to use a global menu anyways? I don't see the benefit. If anything it makes it harder to use apps by constantly having to deal with that bar up there.

The search for app thing that you get by clicking on the Ubuntu icon needs to be trashed, and something new needs to be put there. Actually just turning it into a combined launcher with menus would be 1000 times better. But, just to point out how bad it is I'll be spending some time talking about it.

   -The size of it and the icons is massive! What an eye sore, and this is on a 14 inch laptop. The Firefox button was like 3 inches by three inches. Overall the size was just way off, reminded me of using a netbook OS. There's no reason to hog the screen like that.

   -You can't keep the search screen open and change workstations. Once you do, it closes. So much for using multiple spaces at a time.

   -I couldn't figure out how to add/remove from the shortcut screen that starts by default. That would be pretty important to me to save time since having to even open that time trap of a interface is a waste.

   -I didn't like it tracking my app usage. I find this to be very intrusive to my privacy. If I'm going to use Ubuntu in the future there needs to be a way to turn that feature off.

    -The install apps that you see at the bottom of the search feels like a trap. I even mistankenly clicked on one and got taken to Software Center trying to launch a terminal. If I want apps I'll search. You don't need to advertise apps to me, thanks.

Un-hiding the dock while an app is in the way was somewhat buggy. It would act like it wanted to un-hide, but it wouldn't do it unless I stuck the pointer ALL the way to the top and left of the screen. I understand why you want it to be hard to un-hide since it's on the left, but it could be easier.

While on the subject of the dock, I found it to have very few, actually that's being generous; NO customization options. I couldn't even take the trash can, and other icons off of it. I want the dock to be how I want it! Give me control please.

Changing workstations was quite stupid. I liked having the desktop panel applet better. Having to click like 5 times on a desktop to go there is uncalled for. It should only take one single click, and it should be fast.


Well, that's my 2 cents on Unity. If I were to give it a letter grade right now it would be D-. It's failing to pass, but it is usable (better than some other DEs by far).

I'll be trying Gnome 3 (shell) next .):P

[EDIT]: just downloaded Fedora 15 alpha to try Gnome 3 shell. Well it was a no go i guess? I got a desktop with panels. I guess my Intel HD graphics are not good enough for Gnome 3 shell. What a shame, I really wanted to try it. I guess it is time to start looking for a new DE then. Maybe Unity will be up to speed by the next LTS.

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-04-11
[QUOTE=Krytarik]That means that it's exactly the same as in current releases (10.04, 10.10), at least as of now![/QUOTE]

Yeah, Canonical's been focusing on Unity quite a bit, to the point that 11.04 doesn't even come with a new default theme - the elements of the UI that aren't Unity have barely changed a jot.

[QUOTE=capngloval]This was a heads up for the Ubuntu crew, and somewhere someone didn't see what it was about.[/QUOTE]
Maybe you misunderstand something - this isn't a forum of Canonical employees or devs. We're all just enthusiasts, the same as your LUG, with a few devs lurking here and there.

There's a lot of controversy over the Unity interface, and a lot of it's based on incomplete information or misinformation. There are also a number of heated arguments on the subject online, including some within this forum.

Regardless of whether you're voicing your own opinion or expressing that of your group, you're expressing some very strong feelings on the subject, and whether they're yours or someone else's, they're overreactions in several ways, as noted in this thread. As for your feelings about Ubuntu, I generally agree - I think it is, hands down, the best operating system available, and I too want to see it succeed. Where I differ is in that I don't see Unity as a threat to that.

For the purposes of full disclosure, I don't and don't intend to use the Unity interface, either, as I feel that there are other more mature projects that offer similar or greater functionality. Regardless, I think Ubuntu is making a good move in shipping a more attractive default desktop than was previously possible.

### Post by nothingspecial on 2011-04-11
> **zika said:**
> Question:
On net there is ([http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/xfce](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/xfce)) tutorial how to add Xfce to Your setup. But (as far as I remember) isn't it enough to install xfce4 (with apps that it depends on) instead of whole xubuntu-desktop? ...

```
sudo apt-get install tasksel
sudo tasksel
```

Navigate with the arrow keys, find ubuntu-desktop and press space to deselect it

Find xubuntu-desktop and press space to select it.

Press Tab to get to the ok button

Press Enter.

When it's done reboot.

---

if i had any kind of wish it would be to add some kind of indicator that would hold oll the old sys tray apps like fusion icon and such. i wont complain about  ubuntu but i cant see going out of my way to mount  other drives when i could just access them from a running app like mount manager. sure i could mount by hand but  really?  i am too spoiled by  those little gnome applets  you could put in the panel.. even killing an app was fast with a click. now i have to make an xkill script with an icon and put it on my desktop. i dont want a desktop  filled with icons.

Launching a second instance of an app from the dock is tricky. Why was it even important to use a global menu anyways? I don't see the benefit. If anything it makes it harder to use apps by constantly having to deal with that bar up there.
 
this is something ive chewed on since the  alpha.. so last night i sat down and opened apps , moved them around and toyed with the  main menu. heres the scoop- it takes  my mouse 1/2 inch of movement from wherever it is to the global menu(chosen at random times while working). my belly moves more than that when i breathe. i am really begining to think people are just afraid of change because  this is no different than any other time when you have  an app fullscreen on any computer running any os. its even sillier when the app is  not maximized and contains its own menu(this will probably change). i mean really, 1/2 inch most of the time. at worst 1 inch at bottom of the screen. is this is why  "clear coke" didnt make it (asside from the fact something that tastes like soda pop  can not be clear).

> -You can't keep the search screen open and change workstations. Once you do, it closes. So much for using multiple spaces at a time.
 
not sure why you would bother  wanting to keep it open but what about an add-on search. it would stay open in the launcher and you could click it to view it whenever. something like catfish or even gnome-do. im sure there are  plenty..
 
> -I couldn't figure out how to add/remove from the shortcut screen that starts by default. That would be pretty important to me to save time since having to even open that time trap of a interface is a waste.
 
will have to check this out tonite.. could be useful :)
 
> -I didn't like it tracking my app usage. I find this to be very intrusive to my privacy. If I'm going to use Ubuntu in the future there needs to be a way to turn that feature off.
 
you can turn this off(reporting) in synaptic or update manager. might not be what you are looking for though.. i could be wrong but unity relies strongly on a good search function to find apps in a variety of ways. turning this off might break things.
 
> -The install apps that you see at the bottom of the search feels like a trap. I even mistankenly clicked on one and got taken to Software Center trying to launch a terminal. If I want apps I'll search. You don't need to advertise apps to me, thanks.

it might have bugged me too but then i realized it seems to list  known apps  based on things i do. in that respect, and for all the new users out there i think it is clever and has a place. you are using software freely and written by devs who can use this  to promote their works for free or profit. my advice is not to look that far down..
 
> Un-hiding the dock while an app is in the way was somewhat buggy. It would act like it wanted to un-hide, but it wouldn't do it unless I stuck the pointer ALL the way to the top and left of the screen. I understand why you want it to be hard to un-hide since it's on the left, but it could be easier.
 
check gconfig or compiz to see if this has been made easier to do or modify. it was a pain for me too but recently got more solid and easier to do. i might have changed a setting or 2 or 10.. 
 
> While on the subject of the dock, I found it to have very few, actually that's being generous; NO customization options. I couldn't even take the trash can, and other icons off of it. I want the dock to be how I want it! Give me control please.
 
check compiz plugin unity, gconfig. i was changing options before i knew there was a plugin to do it. i even  fired up gimp and made some launcher  changes graphically. its fun and  you will learn  much.
 
> Changing workstations was quite stupid. I liked having the desktop panel applet better. Having to click like 5 times on a desktop to go there is uncalled for. It should only take one single click, and it should be fast.
 
if you mean screens, i thought you could  click the  launcher  icon and select the screen just like the old days. same  number of clicks. also gconfig should be tweakable to set up key bindings for this, maybe..
 
 
> Well, that's my 2 cents on Unity. If I were to give it a letter grade right now it would be D-. It's failing to pass, but it is usable (better than some other DEs by far).
 
I'll be trying Gnome 3 (shell) next .):P
 
[EDIT]: just downloaded Fedora 15 alpha to try Gnome 3 shell. Well it was a no go i guess? I got a desktop with panels. I guess my Intel HD graphics are not good enough for Gnome 3 shell. What a shame, I really wanted to try it. I guess it is time to start looking for a new DE then. Maybe Unity will be up to speed by the next LTS.
 
 
this is why unity wins in my opinion. gnome had  much time to hear the complaints of users and one was  "what about those with  less than optimum graphics". they ignored  everyone. unity comes in 2 flavors- 2d for low end cards and 3d. i thought the 2d  looked good and this is what won my favor- thinking about the users more than the next guy. good luck with the hunt.
frodo

### Post by CraigPaleo on 2011-04-11
> **frodowiz said:**
> 
 
 

 
this is something ive chewed on since the  alpha.. so last night i sat down and opened apps , moved them around and toyed with the  main menu. heres the scoop- it takes  my mouse 1/2 inch of movement from wherever it is to the global menu(chosen at random times while working). my belly moves more than that when i breathe. i am really begining to think people are just afraid of change because  this is no different than any other time when you have  an app fullscreen on any computer running any os. its even sillier when the app is  not maximized and contains its own menu(this will probably change). i mean really, 1/2 inch most of the time. at worst 1 inch at bottom of the screen. is this is why  "clear coke" didnt make it (asside from the fact something that tastes like soda pop  can not be clear). 
 

 I hardly ever use full screen and when I do, it's usually to view videos. That's about it. If I had a doc or my browser open to full screen, I'd literally have to keep turning my head to read. I often have multiple windows spread out on the desktop as it's easier to work between them. 

 One thing I LOVED about leaving Mac for Ubuntu was that I no longer had to go all the way over to the menu every time I brought a new window into focus - just click and the menu is already right there. 

It may be resistance to change for some but I'm one person who was once used to global menus and will never go back. I welcomed that change. :D  

Why punish us when it really doesn't effect those who always use full screen? It seems like change for the sake of change.

### Post by NCLI on 2011-04-11
> **Dupointx said:**
> Well after waiting some time, I finally decided to give Unity a try. I would usually wait till a release to do this, but with all the talk going around I couldn't resist downloading the beta and giving it a spin. So here is what I think about Unity. [This is in no particular order btw, just wrote things down on a note pad as I thought it.]

Launching a second instance of an app from the dock is tricky. I was lucky to read that you need to use the middle mouse button to do it, but I was left wondering "what are you going to do if you have a laptop and only left-right buttons?". I tested it out and didn't find any combination of alt ctrl or super + click would work.
You need to click the left+right ouse buttons simultaneously, or do a three-finger tap. I agree that this isn't the best design decision, so please do report a usability bug.

> The auto-hide feature of the global menu was a pain. I'm a right handed person and it's stupid that to do anything you have to constantly put the courser in the top left. Why was it even important to use a global menu anyways? I don't see the benefit. If anything it makes it harder to use apps by constantly having to deal with that bar up there.
Join the Ayatana mailing list or IRC and try to convince the designers to put the menus back in the windows then. I've been trying for a while, but more voices speak louder.

> The search for app thing that you get by clicking on the Ubuntu icon needs to be trashed, and something new needs to be put there. Actually just turning it into a combined launcher with menus would be 1000 times better. But, just to point out how bad it is I'll be spending some time talking about it.

   -The size of it and the icons is massive! What an eye sore, and this is on a 14 inch laptop. The Firefox button was like 3 inches by three inches. Overall the size was just way off, reminded me of using a netbook OS. There's no reason to hog the screen like that.
Touchscreens. I agree that we should be able to customise it though.

>    -You can't keep the search screen open and change workstations. Once you do, it closes. So much for using multiple spaces at a time.

It's not really a search screen, it's a launcher. But if you think this is a problem, report a usability bug and get it fixed.

>    -I couldn't figure out how to add/remove from the shortcut screen that starts by default. That would be pretty important to me to save time since having to even open that time trap of a interface is a waste.
It's easier to just add something to the launcher. When an application is running, just right-click it's icon. You'll be able to make it stick around even after closing it.
> 
   -I didn't like it tracking my app usage. I find this to be very intrusive to my privacy. If I'm going to use Ubuntu in the future there needs to be a way to turn that feature off.
How is it intrusive to your privacy to have Ubuntu display locally stored statistics about application usage? It's not like it uploads them to some central server.

>     -The install apps that you see at the bottom of the search feels like a trap. I even mistankenly clicked on one and got taken to Software Center trying to launch a terminal. If I want apps I'll search. You don't need to advertise apps to me, thanks.
I sincerely disagree. Working with several different computers running 11.04, I'm often in dobt as to what applications I have installed. If I want to launch a specific application, I appreciate being able to install it just by searching for it like I would if it was already installed.

> Un-hiding the dock while an app is in the way was somewhat buggy. It would act like it wanted to un-hide, but it wouldn't do it unless I stuck the pointer ALL the way to the top and left of the screen. I understand why you want it to be hard to un-hide since it's on the left, but it could be easier.
That's not a bug, it's a feature(tm)

> While on the subject of the dock, I found it to have very few, actually that's being generous; NO customization options. I couldn't even take the trash can, and other icons off of it. I want the dock to be how I want it! Give me control please.
As of now, you can't remove the trashcan, and you have to uninstall the individual lenses in order to remove them. I agree there should be a way to add and remove those without uninstalling, and since ou obviously do too, report a bug.

Most customisation options are only accessible by installing CCSM. Obviously, this is obviously not final behaviour.

> Changing workstations was quite stupid. I liked having the desktop panel applet better. Having to click like 5 times on a desktop to go there is uncalled for. It should only take one single click, and it should be fast.
Report a usability bug.
> [EDIT]: just downloaded Fedora 15 alpha to try Gnome 3 shell. Well it was a no go i guess? I got a desktop with panels. I guess my Intel HD graphics are not good enough for Gnome 3 shell. What a shame, I really wanted to try it. I guess it is time to start looking for a new DE then. Maybe Unity will be up to speed by the next LTS.
If you bother to report these as bugs, you probably won't have to wait for the next LTS.

On net there is ([http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/xfce](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/xfce)) tutorial how to add Xfce to Your setup. But (as far as I remember) isn't it enough to install xfce4 (with apps that it depends on) instead of whole xubuntu-desktop? ...

yes, 
```
sudo apt-get install xfce4
```

worked for me and even co-exists with Ubuntu Classic.
I can log in to either at gdm and their individual settings seem (so far ) to be independent of each other.  :D

The option to install Xubuntu desktop wants to remove Gnome desktop which was unacceptable for me so simply installing xfce4 worked well.

### Post by Dragonbite on 2011-04-12
Does this help anybody?

> [[COLOR="Blue"]**Get your Compiz cube back on with Unity in Natty**[/COLOR]]("http://www.omgubuntu.co.uk/2011/04/compiz-cube-natty/?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed%3A+d0od+%28Omg!+Ubuntu!%29&utm_content=Google+Reader")
I think it is fair to say that the Desktop Cube has been one of Linux’s iconic features. The cube provided both an easy way to visualise workspaces as well as a nice bit of eye-candy when using it.

However, in the current beta of Natty Narwhal, the cube is not available. The Unity plugin requires the “largedesktop” feature which is provided by Desktop Wall, an alternative workspace switcher which conflicts with the cube, and as a result trying to enable it results in the following message. Also, if you disable Unity, unsurprisingly Unity will die.

So what can you do?

Initially Unity depended on the wall plugin directly. Due to a bug report, they have changed this requirement to simply “largedesktop.” As a result it is possible to have Unity and Cube running without modifying the Unity plugin, previously you had to remove the Wall plugin requirement from Unity in the xml file....

... and it goes on explaining how to make it work.

### Post by Johnsie on 2011-04-13
Unity has no 'show desktop' button and there doesn't seem to be a way to open more than one instance of nautilus quickly. I also cannot quickly click my nautilus shortcuts without first going to the home directory (That's an extra click). To fix these issues I pressed alt-f2 and entered the following:

> gnome-panel

I keep both the panels at the bottom. It's a much more convenient way to open applications than with that part-time sidebar. When the sidebar is away I don't remember where the icons are, so when it reappears I need to find it again. This is just frustrating.

Unity is definitely not ready to be the default UI in Ubuntu.

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-04-14
> **cariboo907 said:**
> Here's another hot tip, to get to the Unity settings page if you have compizconfig-settings-manager installed, press Alt-F2 and type:

```
about:config
```
Bwahahahaha!  Good one!  :D

"CompizConfig Settings Manager closed unexpectedly" or whatever.

One of the few fails I've had with Unity.

All I do, in this case...

[LIST]
[*]Open CCSM.
[*]Right-click the CCSM Icon (in the Launcher Panel).
[*]"Keep in Launcher"
[/LIST].
It's amazing (to me) how few proggies I use, on a regular basis -- and I probably could be considered a power-user, whatever that is.

I "Keep" the proggies I use (on a regular basis) in the Launcher Panel, and they only take up 75% of available vertical space, in the Launcher.

I've actually *thought* about "Keeping" bogus proggies, in the Launcher (things I don't use, on a regular basis) just to see how it would be, if / when I needed to scroll the Launcher Panel.

Anyway, the larger point is, there are many, many tips n' tricks in Unity, that have NOT been documented yet.

I *believe* this is because, Unity is brand new, in beta, and a moving target.

When things are finalized (or shortly before) I'm sure the "doc committee" or whomever, will fill us in, on all the options.

If not...

Er.  I don't even want to think about that possibility.  :)

### Post by Dragonbite on 2011-04-14
> **Joe of loath said:**
> I know on multitouch stuff, two fingers is right click. Maybe three fingers is middle click?

Alternative multitouch commands...

[LIST]
[*]1 finger for click
[*]elbow for right-click
[*]forehead to reboot (doh!)
[*]swipe nose for next page
[*]swipe other direction to wipe off nose smudges to go back
[*]***** to turn off parental controls (or to block/un-block ****** pop-up ads)
[*](butt) cheeks to start video chat (we already do butt-dialing on our phones ;) )
[*]right ear to increase volume
[*]left ear to decrease volume (could be accessorized with bluetooth earrings)
[*]heavy shaking to clear screen (a 'la etch-a-sketch)
[/LIST]
