### Post by Johnsie on 2011-04-14
How are people going to remember all of these special moves? It's like Street Fighter 2. You have to do a sonic boom to open something ;-)

Day two of using unity was a bit of a failure. I ended up running gnome-panel again and using that. I have alot of ssh bookmarks in "Places" that I need to be able to access quickly. I'm the programmer at my company and people are often calling me about random things. I need to be able to get into my stuff pretty quickly.

In Gnome I have shortcuts on my panel, on the desktop and in places/nautilus. This gives me the ability to open things quickly and respond to peoples questions.


Today I decided to mess around with the sidebar settings. I managed to turn off autohide which was good, but it was a bit of a mission to find out how to go about doing that. With gnome I just right click the panel and click properties, but with Unity I needed to randomly go into the depths of some compiz settings. Usability fail IMO.

While in the Compiz settings I decided to re-activate the cube and get my wobbly windows back. Activating the cube broke Unity and I had no window borders. I can't remember how I fixed it but I think it involved deleting hidden folders from my home folder.

On the positive side the middle-click thing worked and I was able to get the wobbly windows back.

I still feel very claustrophobic in Unity and really miss all my lovely shortcuts.

### Post by michael-wd21 on 2011-04-14
I've been using Gnome since it was only available in source form. I have been using and advocating Ubuntu since 2005.

I am now using Xubuntu.

Disclaimer: I am, perhaps, a gnarly curmudgeon.

I should probably say something useful.

The gnome desktop in 10.10 was, for me at least, pretty much perfect. I mean, there were little things that could be tidied up probably, I didn't like the way notifications "revived" themselves when you hovered over them, for instance, but nothing major.

Unity strikes me as fashion-driven. It's not clear to me what it fixes. It breaks all kinds of things for me, for no payback.

Having tried it, hated it, then read that "classic" is to be removed in 11.10 I immediately thought "where do i go now, I want my PERFECT FUNCTIONING SYSTEM to last for ever."

I installed Gnome 3 from PPA and tried Gnome Shell. I hated that for all the same reasons.

And now I am on XFCE. It is less pretty than "classic" gnome but it is much faster and performs almost identically for my purposes. I think I may be happier on the sidelines, away from where all the "cool stuff" is happening, because guess what, the cool stuff seems to also be vapid, dumbed down, lowest common denominator stuff.

I suppose I will be happy with Ubuntu for as long as XFCE is available. But I do a lot of advocacy for Ubuntu, installing it and supporting it on friend's computers, and now I'll be a bit of a phony as I no longer know or care about what the hell is going on in the mainstream of the project.

PS when I sit down at a Windows PC the first thing I do is set everything to classic mode to make it look and behave as close to windows 2000 as possible. I guess I think what freaked me out was reading that classic would not be an option in 11.10. It just seems crazy.

---

### Post by robert shearer on 2011-04-14
> **michael-wd21 said:**
> 
And now I am on XFCE. It is less pretty than "classic" gnome but it is much faster and performs almost identically for my purposes. I think I may be happier on the sidelines, away from where all the "cool stuff" is happening, because guess what, the cool stuff seems to also be vapid, dumbed down, lowest common denominator stuff.

You have eloquently summed up the feelings and experiences of many established Ubuntu users faced with the Unity desktop migration.

Yes, I too lament the potential loss of the knowledge base from the help forums.

Whether that has been factored in to the Unity decisions or not time will tell, though judging by the recent suggestion by the admins here to remove archived posts from public view it may be have been.

### Post by azurehi on 2011-04-14
> **michael-wd21 said:**
> I've been using Gnome since it was only available in source form. I have been using and advocating Ubuntu since 2005.

I am now using Xubuntu.

Disclaimer: I am, perhaps, a gnarly curmudgeon.

I should probably say something useful.

The gnome desktop in 10.10 was, for me at least, pretty much perfect. I mean, there were little things that could be tidied up probably, I didn't like the way notifications "revived" themselves when you hovered over them, for instance, but nothing major.

Unity strikes me as fashion-driven. It's not clear to me what it fixes. It breaks all kinds of things for me, for no payback.

Having tried it, hated it, then read that "classic" is to be removed in 11.10 I immediately thought "where do i go now, I want my PERFECT FUNCTIONING SYSTEM to last for ever."

I installed Gnome 3 from PPA and tried Gnome Shell. I hated that for all the same reasons.

And now I am on XFCE. It is less pretty than "classic" gnome but it is much faster and performs almost identically for my purposes. I think I may be happier on the sidelines, away from where all the "cool stuff" is happening, because guess what, the cool stuff seems to also be vapid, dumbed down, lowest common denominator stuff.

I suppose I will be happy with Ubuntu for as long as XFCE is available. But I do a lot of advocacy for Ubuntu, installing it and supporting it on friend's computers, and now I'll be a bit of a phony as I no longer know or care about what the hell is going on in the mainstream of the project.

PS when I sit down at a Windows PC the first thing I do is set everything to classic mode to make it look and behave as close to windows 2000 as possible. I guess I think what freaked me out was reading that classic would not be an option in 11.10. It just seems crazy.

I am alternating between Unity and Classic.  I usually like to try New "things" and find Unity to be suitable (eventually) for touch screen devices.  I do Not like the extra "work" and searching I must do with Unity to achieve what I can do so much more easily with Ubuntu Classic (in Natty and even more easily in 10.04 or 10.10). 

I believe the top and bottom panels in Classic take up less space than the launcher in Unity with it's Huge buttons.  And everything is available through icons on the panel and drop-downs...so much more customizable than Unity.  I feel "left behind" by Unity and what is to come with 10.10 and after.  

I could not install Natty on my old computer because my video card is no longer supported.  I dual booted with my XP computer but may install XFCE or even Linux Mint Debian Edition, Gnome or XFCE.  Too bad Shuttleworth and his Devs are taking this approach.  What of those Many whose hardware will not support this?

---

### Post by cariboo on 2011-04-14
> **Johnsie said:**
> How are people going to remember all of these special moves? It's like Street Fighter 2. You have to do a sonic boom to open something ;-)

Day two of using unity was a bit of a failure. I ended up running gnome-panel again and using that. I have alot of ssh bookmarks in "Places" that I need to be able to access quickly. I'm the programmer at my company and people are often calling me about random things. I need to be able to get into my stuff pretty quickly.

In Gnome I have shortcuts on my panel, on the desktop and in places/nautilus. This gives me the ability to open things quickly and respond to peoples questions.


Today I decided to mess around with the sidebar settings. I managed to turn off autohide which was good, but it was a bit of a mission to find out how to go about doing that. With gnome I just right click the panel and click properties, but with Unity I needed to randomly go into the depths of some compiz settings. Usability fail IMO.

While in the Compiz settings I decided to re-activate the cube and get my wobbly windows back. Activating the cube broke Unity and I had no window borders. I can't remember how I fixed it but I think it involved deleting hidden folders from my home folder.

On the positive side the middle-click thing worked and I was able to get the wobbly windows back.

I still feel very claustrophobic in Unity and really miss all my lovely shortcuts.

For ssh shortcuts in Launcher, I picked up this tip from [Jorge Castro's blog]("http://castrojo.tumblr.com/post/4536294249/more-quicklists"), just right-click the launcher to see the quicklist.

```
[Desktop Entry]
Version=1.0
Name=Remote Servers
Comment=Login to my servers
Exec=gnome-terminal --disable-factory --sm-client-disable --class=remoteserver
Terminal=false
X-MultipleArgs=false
Type=Application
Icon=utilities-terminal
StartupNotify=true
StartupWMClass=RemoteServers
X-Ayatana-Desktop-Shortcuts=Server1;Server2;Server3;

[Server1 Shortcut Group]
Name=SSH into Willy
Exec=gnome-terminal --disable-factory --sm-client-disable  --class=remoteserver -x ssh -t willy
TargetEnvironment=Unity

[Server2 Shortcut Group]
Name=SSH into Likely
Exec=gnome-terminal --disable-factory --sm-client-disable --class=remoteserver -x ssh -t likely
TargetEnvironment=Unity

[Server3 Shortcut Group]
Name=SSH into Chilanko
Exec=gnome-terminal --disable-factory --sm-client-disable --class=remoteserver -x ssh -t chilanko
TargetEnvironment=Unity
```

### Post by ZarathustraDK on 2011-04-16
I think there's an unneeded commotion over unity at this point in time. Natty is not a LTS-release, let us cherish the opportunity to experiment and make mistakes while we can, I'd say.

Another thing that I think gets an unwarranted amount of attention is the common criticism that basically goes "It's not like Gnome 2, ergo it sucks". Usability and interface innovation is not about copying what's true and tested, or necessarily taking the bits that work and use them in a new paradigm. It's about making an interface that people with no prior experience in a given interface can use better than they would the old one. So bringing your baggage from Gnome 2 or some other DE is not going to help in that regard. I wouldn't look to flint and tinder if I were to design a lighter, I wouldn't keep "horses" in the back of my mind if I went about to make an automobile.

Old stuff gets chugged out the window when you go about aquiring something new, if you don't like there are plenty of plain Gnome-distros out there. I, for one, want to see unity live and learn; whether it fails or succeeds it will be invaluable as a lesson or or resource, respectively, for the open source community as a whole. We all want ponies, sometimes you get it dead and you learn why that is so.

---

### Post by rg4w on 2011-04-16
> **ZarathustraDK said:**
> I think there's an unneeded commotion over unity at this point in time. Natty is not a LTS-release, let us cherish the opportunity to experiment and make mistakes while we can, I'd say.
I've long advocated that the most prominent Download link at ubuntu.com be for the LTS, and those who want anything more exotic will know to use the option control provided to get what they're looking for.

But unfortunately the LTS is the hidden option, and the current non-LTS release is the one most people will download because they don't have the experience to know otherwise.

The LTS designation therefore has almost not practical benefit for anyone other than professional sysadmins at large orgs, since the average joe will just download the default.

With that in mind, I would respectfully suggest that the commotion surrounding this experimental and somewhat unfinished release is indeed warranted.  

If the main Download link changes, I'll change my tune accordingly.

---

### Post by Flash858 on 2011-04-16
Let me elucidate the point I made earlier: Unity and Gnome 3/shell are REGRESSIONS. Neither make ANYTHING easier, rather the converse is true. Both create unnecessary layers on the desktop, and in so doing, also take the customization out of Linux. You can tweak a FEW things, sure, but nothing like what you can do now with Ubuntu/Gnome 2.32. 

This is Mac-like thinking. You do not get to actually decide for yourself how you want to interact with your desktop, rather you are just there to share the experience, and hope for the best.

I do NOT want a walled garden, I do NOT want a global menu. The entire concept of the global menu is the most ludicrous thing ever invented in the history of computing (apart from perhaps, Microsoft Bob ;) ) because it is inherently inefficient ergonomically.There are twice as many mouse and keyboard interactions to achieve the same result as there would be in Gnome 2x, XFCE, or even in Windows. It is just ICKY ICKY ICKY.

Also, when I said "Appley", it is obvious that Ubuntu has been taking design queues from OSX for the last couple of releases. The indented text, the buttons on the wrong side, the color scheme, and now the move to a global menu, replete with a cleverly disguised albeit moved to the left Mac Dock and a complete rip off of Expose (I love Expo, and in fact all of Compiz, and consider it essential to my desktop, as it allows me to do things MUCH more efficiently, and I do have to admit that unlike some, I never tire of the eye candy. In fact, if Compiz made their own desktop, I would race to it in a heartbeat - but the desktop switcher in Unity is a complete clone of Expo). 

Apple has the occasional good idea on the desktop (like the dock), but they always execute it in a way that is counter-intuitive TO ME. Incidentally, if you want to see a desktop that achieves all of the goals of Gnome 3 and Unity, take a look at Jolicloud (Now JoliOS). It uses easily accessible big icons, and IOS type pages built on top of Ubuntu, and is IMHO, the best netbook interface ever made. I never cared for UNR, nor any of the respins born out of it (eeeUbuntu, Easy Peasy, etc.).

Anyway, I HAVE to use a mac at work, and have been for nearly 2 years, and I hate every moment that I am in that environment. While I understand it thoroughly, and can do anything I need to do with it, it is wholly illogical and counter-intuitive TO ME, and slows me down considerably. Additionally, OSX is far LESS stable than my Ubuntu computer at home, which is not as new, and never, ever crashes (and it is not just me - everyone at work is fully up to date, and there is at least one crash a day on fairly new macs - it ain't all it is cracked up to be).

 I have been using Ubuntu since Dapper, so when I see it evolving from something I gleefully embrace into something I despise on every conceivable  level, it give me cause for concern.

I will use Unity a bit, as I will give Ubuntu every opportunity to convince me Unity is better (or at least usable), and I am certainly hopeful that it one day will evolve into something that suits me, as I do understand that it is in its infancy.

 I have Beta 2 installed on a partition, so for the next 18 months, or as long as I can keep using the "classic" desktop, I will be using Ubuntu. I have 3 other distros installed on other partitions however, so am hedging my bets. If Unity does not change, I will likely just switch to Xubuntu, as I am an Ubuntu loyalist, though I will keep my eyes open.

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-04-16
> **VinDSL said:**
> Basically, everyone was biatching out Mark Shuttleworth for saying the devs were trying to make Unity a zero config install.

Damn it, you made me have to do research!!! :twisted: :p

There is a big difference between saying zero-config *install* and zero-config *experience*.

And the actual term used was "zero-config experience".

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/unity/+bug/748739/comments/2](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/unity/+bug/748739/comments/2)

As it relates to Unity, the number of things you *need* a config tool for should be pretty small.

Icon size on the panel. Why should we need a config tool for that as opposed to just being able to grab the edge of the panel and drag it to the size we want?

Icon size in the dash. Why should we need a config tool for that as opposed to just having a view option in the dash itself visible in the place we actually want change it?

There is room for flexibility, while still sticking to the idea of "zero-config experience".

For the Compiz stuff there is CCSM, was CCSM included in older releases or did the people that wanted the extra configuration have to install it?

For general stuff (screen resolution, wallpaper, etc...) you have the stuff in the control-center, which is still installed by default, correct?

If CCSM were to be included by default, then you increase the probability of stuff like in the following thread.

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1729088](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1729088)

Later, Seeker

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-04-17
> **ELD said:**
> Been using it all day, far more stable than it was a couple weeks ago.

Started to really love it, need an easy way to add stuff to the unity bar though.
Here's what I do...


**The easy part.** :)

Right-click on your Desktop -> choose Create Launcher...

The Create Launcher screen should look familiar, if you've ever added a Custom Launcher to your (Classic) Gnome menu system.

When you're done, you will have a Custom Launcher sitting on your Desktop.  Double-click it, and make sure it works.



**Now, here's the tip n' trick: MOVING the Launcher to the Unity Bar.**  :D

What I do is (once I know the Launcher works):

[LIST]
[*]Open Nautilus
[*]Navigate to your Home Folder (make sure it shows hidden files)
[*]Navigate to your .local/share/applications Folder
[*]Drag and drop your Launcher from your Desktop to .local/share/applications
[*]Then, drag and drop your Launcher from .local/share/applications to the Unity Bar
[*]Finally, delete your Custom Launcher from the Desktop (if it's still there)
[/LIST]


LoL!  I *know* that sounds contrived, but it's the easiest method I'm aware of, in the current state of Unity.

Do it a couple of times, and it will become as second-nature as shaving, and brushing your teeth.  ;)

### Post by castrojo on 2011-04-18
> **Mozai said:**
> Problem I have with Unity: If I launch multiple x-terminal-emulator with different parameters (like 'ssh to host Alpha', 'ssh to host Beta and use blue text so I know it's server Beta I'm looking at'), the icons on the Unity dock for windows and launchers are identical.  What's more, when I start any xterm, all the 'xterm something' launchers are highlighted as if I have all of each open, when I only launched one.

You need to set a --class value for it so it doesn't get matched. Plus you can add your SSH hosts to a quicklist, check this out: 

[http://askubuntu.com/questions/34597/how-do-i-make-a-custom-launcher-for-terminal-applications](http://askubuntu.com/questions/34597/how-do-i-make-a-custom-launcher-for-terminal-applications)

### Post by Mr. Picklesworth on 2011-04-20
> **jdunn said:**
> Does the program icon have to be in /usr/share/icons?  Does the program have to have a link in the usr/bin directory?  I tried using absolute paths in the .desktop file but that didn't work.

**never mind**  problem solved.

You'd be better off adding it to ~/.local/share/ (/icons and /applications). Applications look at those places before looking at the system locations. That way you can override existing files, dpkg is happy, and your own changes are all logically attached to your own user account.

### Post by marin123 on 2011-04-21

---

### Post by Daedal on 2011-04-22
> **marin123 said:**
> i dont know if this was asked before, so here it goes :)

i want to have a different folder instead of home folder in launcher, for example, i access Downloads way more than home. it would be great if i could put downloads in there.

i think the best way would be to make a new custom launcher to use instead of the one that's there now.  not sure if it's just a bug or what but for some reason i couldn't get the static lists i added to the /usr/share/applications/nautilus-home.desktop to actually work.

open your home folder and create a new folder, name it whatever you want. open the folder create a new file.

paste this into the new file.
```
[Desktop Entry]
Name[en_US]=Downloads Folder
Comment=Open your downloads folder
Exec=nautilus Downloads
Icon=user-home
Terminal=false
StartupNotify=true
Type=Application
X-Ayatana-Desktop-Shortcuts=Home;Documents;Music;Pictures;Videos;


[Home Shortcut Group]
Name=Home Folder
Exec=nautilus 
TargetEnvironment=Unity

[Documents Shortcut Group]
Name=Documents Folder
Exec=nautilus Documents
TargetEnvironment=Unity

[Music Shortcut Group]
Name=Music Folder
Exec=nautilus Music
TargetEnvironment=Unity

[Pictures Shortcut Group]
Name=Pictures Folder
Exec=nautilus Pictures
TargetEnvironment=Unity

[Videos Shortcut Group]
Name=Videos Folder
Exec=nautilus Videos
TargetEnvironment=Unity
```


save the file and rename it to NautLaunch.Desktop

right click the current home launcher button and uncheck "keep in Launcher"
now just drag your NautLauch.Desktop file onto the laucher and you should be good to go. 

the new icon will open the downloads folder when clicked and when right clicked will open a list menu with the options to open the home, documents, music, pictures, or video.

I like Unity but think why many don't is becaus of the radical change in apperence/experience, Apple Mac OS X Cheetah like-it-or-not was released 11 years ago and is visually still the same as the upcomming OSX Lion yet many under the bonnet improvments for productivity and user experience have and are being implemented and it's the same with KDE and Windows to a degree, It just goes to show you can't underestimate the power of a stable environment.
Maybe .

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-04-24
> **NormanFLinux said:**
> Great screenshot! You're running Unity 3D?
Thanks!  And, yes, I'm running Unity 3D.

I'll have to say, they've done a nice job with Compiz!  Compiz used to be a toy.  They've turned the eye-candy into something useful.

OO will be even better...  ;)

BTW, in the Workspace Switcher (pictured above) you can drag n' drop programs from one workspace to another, without leaving the switcher.  Makes it a simple job to organize them.

Once you have everything where you want them, you don't need to return to the swtcher (unless you want to).  When you click the open icons on the Unity Panel, it automagically flips to that workspace. And/or you can use keyboard shortcuts - your choice.

You wouldn't believe how fast you can navigate Unity, once you get used to it - even with a dozen (or more) programs running!  :)

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-04-24
Have you looked at the list of [keyboard/mouse shortcuts]("http://askubuntu.com/questions/28086/unity-keyboard-mouse-shortcuts")?

I've found multiple windows fairly easy to deal with.  The default settings let you use Shift-Alt-Up to expose all the windows on the current desktop, and switch between them, while Super-W does the same, but shows windows from all desktops.

One thing I'd like to see is an option to not use the global menu for unmaximized programs.  On my netbook the global menu is fantastic, since it saves space, and most windows tend to be maximized anyway.  On my desktop, if I have a small window (for something like solitaire, for example) open in the bottom-right of my 24-inch monitor, it's a pain to have to go all the way to the top-left to access the menu.

---

### Post by NormanFLinux on 2011-04-24
It won't be available in Natty on low powered computers which will default to the Classic GNOME desktop. You'll have to install the 2D repository and then install it on your computer. It runs nice and smooth and the only thing missing is the compositing engine that you have with 3D.

I should mention that its now possible to run the compositing engine with 2D with the help of a Tweak tool:

[IMG]http://marianochavero.files.wordpress.com/2011/04/app.png?w=587&h=420[/IMG]

If you have limited hardware, you can now attain near to the 3D experience every one else will have!

### Post by samriggs on 2011-04-24
I have been using Ubuntu for over 2 years and enjoyed it thoroughly UNTIL IT HAPPENED! :(
I took 11.04 for a spin and was extremely disappointed. 
Tried to create a theme to find out untiy does not recognize gtk and panels it falls back to the default or your limited to what you can do, then found out it's hard coded (a nightmare for theme developers)
the side menu is nothing more than a shortcut menu when you click on apps you get a big black screen that does not show all you want, it adds a install more apps layer (WHY?) the navigation is worst not easier, its a bloated mess that is not needed at all but it is eye candy that desperately needs more work done it (for me though its totally useless), install the netbook version on my sisters netbook she hates it, she wanted to delete a file or go to the download folder to get to the files like normal she had to do it through the trash (totally ridiculous) she wants it gone. My wife checked it out, she wants the classic or forget it, so shes switching now. My cousin is switching also after this.
All I can ask is WHY WHY WHY?
You had a great thing going until unity divided it.
Anyhow I would like thank ubuntu for getting me interested in linux and getting me involved, I did enjoy it up until unity divided my love towards ubuntu and now I must divorce myself from it. 
I know I am not the only one as I've been reading in a lot of places that people who loved ubuntu are disappointed and now leaving it because of unity and the useless interface they added.
Hopefully they will bring it back to the way it was one day.
I'm sure a lot will like the new format, for me though it is not for me at all or anyone in this house.
Sorry I have to go because I really did like this os and the community.

Take care all

### Post by Mozai on 2011-04-24
> **NCLI said:**
> The doc package will be there in time for release.

Looking forward to seeing the 'doc' package show up, so I can read about Unity and find out how I can use it, instead of clicking around in the dark, or dealing with incomplete instructions (like what I mention below).  I'll file the usability bug when I can afford the time to swap my interface from GTK back to Unity to write down the steps-to-replicate.

> **castrojo said:**

Set it where?  xterm doesn't have a '--class' parameter, neither does uxterm, lxterm, koi8rxterm, nor gnome-terminal (these are listed by 'update-alternatives --config x-terminal-emulator'). Arrrrgh.  I'm willing to DIY, but I need documentation.

### Post by NormanFLinux on 2011-04-24
Unity 2D is a QT version of Unity shell for low-powered computers and netbooks.

3D is the default Unity shell that will run on laptops and desktop PCs.

3D is available in Natty Narwhal if the operating system detects you have the appropriate graphics card installed.

If you run Meerkat Maverick, it is available in the repository under Unity.

2D can be installed from the Unity 2D PPA repository. Do a search on Google for Unity 2D PPA.

---

If you run Meerkat Maverick, it is available in the repository under Unity.

2D can be installed from the Unity 2D PPA repository. Do a search on Google for Unity 2D PPA.

not sure if you mean a PPA for Maverick for Unity2d ??

For Natty simply install unity-2d from the repo/Synaptic/Software centre.

### Post by Garthhh on 2011-04-25
I use workspaces much like user accounts
my wife's stuff is on the 1st workspace by agreement
mine on 2nd, VM's, remote desktops on 3rd
bittorrents on the 4th
I can multitask with just windows on any old OS,the extra level of nesting makes a big difference in my work flow
we both know where things will be on either computer
I'm not the only one who uses workspaces in this way

I'm not going to stomp my feet & storm off to a different distro quite yet:P

I'm not in a hurry to muck around with configuration files & on the terminal to get things looking the way I like

I'm confident that a bunch of the GUI things will also be more fully developed by the time 12.04 rolls around

the beta's in between LTS's are just that **Beta's**

### Post by aaaantoine on 2011-04-25

### Post by cimh on 2011-04-26
> **castrojo said:**
> The requirements for Unity aren't any different than what they've always been to run a normal Ubuntu desktop.

My hardware doesnt agree with you. 11.04 struggles on my 2yr old 1.6g Asus netbook and is heavy on power. 1 year ago ubuntu had a 10s boot time target on netbooks. Now that has gone(along with low power consumption). When I last tried 11.04 the boot time had doubled from 10.04 and it was too unresponsive and power heavy to be useful. So I haved moved to peppermint as it has a sub 20s boot, its responsive, solid and low on power consumption. 

I'm happy with innovation but 2 things bug me.

1. Its a shame to see the 10s boot abandoned because many netbook users appreciate fast and frugal os's. 

2. It is a big risk to put unfinished software (unity) in such a prime position in a main release - it will win some people over - but others like me will be put off.

I accept we are still talking about pre-release software

cimh
eee 901

### Post by castrojo on 2011-04-26
> **Mozai said:**
> Set it where?  xterm doesn't have a '--class' parameter, neither does uxterm, lxterm, koi8rxterm, nor gnome-terminal (these are listed by 'update-alternatives --config x-terminal-emulator').  Or maybe you assumed I'd always be using ssh in every terminal?  ... no, there's no '--class' parameter for /usr/bin/ssh either.

It's in gnome-terminal, do a gnome-terminal --help-all:

--class=CLASS                   Program class as used by the window manager
--name=NAME                     Program name as used by the window manager

---

### Post by castrojo on 2011-04-26
> **cimh said:**

> It is a big risk to put unfinished software (unity) in such a prime position in a main release - it will win some people over - but others like me will be put off.

Your original claim was that Unity had "hefty power requirements", which isn't true, it runs at 60fps on a Dell Mini 10.

If I can run it on a Samsung N150 Netbook, the requirements couldn't be THAT hefty...

### Post by Dragonbite on 2011-04-26
> **beew said:**
> What I found is that Natty boots quite fast enough, it is slightly faster than Maverick from power on to login screen (slightly less than  20 seconds) but it takes a long time for Unity to load after log in  so you are staring at a blank screen for another 15 seconds so so after login  (it may be shorter but psychologically it feels like a long time .  maverick from power on to fully usable desktop takes about 25 seconds

Try KDE.. those times sound about right or faster than booting up KDE on my laptop. I don't have times, but I definitely notice a quicker startup with Gnome 2.x & Unity 2D than KDE 4.5

> **beew said:**
> From what he says his netbook is quite capable of running a full 3d desktop with Compiz over the old gnome 2.  If he is not able to run a 3d desktop now because of unity it is a loss of functionality.Why should he want Unity 2d?

Huh.  I didn't see any reference to that in that post.

Anyway, if it is Unity then should be able to go to Classic Gnome and have it work fully, shouldn't they?  If Gnome can and Unity cannot in 11.04 then that's Unity.  If neither can, then it is Ubuntu or the drivers.

I have Intel video on my laptop and it really sucked to find out that the drivers regressed and did not work, even more when I found out other distributions (Fedora, openSUSE) coming out around the same few months also had that issue and even more so when I found out that it isn't going to be fixed in the kernel so it is up to each individual distro to come up with a fix.

### Post by hornedfiend on 2011-04-26
I'm actually impressed with Unity. It's something new, something different from all the rest of the distributions out there.
Linux has always been plagued by fragmentation and that's one of the reasons which still drags it down, in comparison with windows and mac os. I like the fact that Canonical are finally bringing stability to this platform.
And about the actual unity interface, I find it sufficiently functional for my needs and it can only get better with time, so I think we should give it a shot.
I'm seeing a few bugs also, but nothing that couldn't get fixed with a few updates. I'd rather see the final version installed on my laptop on the 28th than later and with a few minor improvements.

### Post by Starks on 2011-04-26
Unity works and adds value to Ubuntu. If it didn't, I wouldn't be using it over Classic.

That doesn't mean that it isn't quirky or have some downright stupid behaviors at the moment.

### Post by Horseboy on 2011-04-26
I've been a huge fan of Ubuntu, it's desktop and it's ease of use. I started out with Ubuntu in 2007/2008 and fell in love with the GNOME desktop. But now that's all changing. I along with the majority of testers don't like Unity (and I got "majority" from all the threads/posts in this forum). So here are my thoughts/questions:
[SIZE=4]
**1. It was rushed.**[/SIZE]

Unity has plenty of bugs, and with a release date of the 28th, these bugs will remain. I've encountered very odd behavior with the Unity launcher. All due respect to the devs, it seemed thrown together. Two more days until release, and Unity is nowhere near stable. That isn't good.

**[SIZE=4]2. Why replace GNOME with Unity anyway?[/SIZE]**

I read somewhere that it was Canonical that came up with Unity. Apparently, they disagreed with what GNOME 3 was becoming and what it's interface looked like and decided to go their own route and create their own unique desktop. In the end, it's about what the community feel, not Canonical. Why replace GNOME 3's shell with a buggy, unstable shell? Pointless. GNOME 3 is a heap better than Unity, and that's what most of the community think as far as I can gather.

**[SIZE=4]3. Unity was an interface for the Netbook edition, keep it that way.[/SIZE]**

People don't need huge icons (talking about the launcher) unless they are visually impaired, and in that case they should use the magnifying tool. If Unity really wants to make use of space, it should start by doing away with the unneeded giant icons. However, this type of thing would be suited to Netbook users (which I'm guessing was the idea behind it when they put it in the Netbook edition).


They are my thoughts, you may agree, you may disagree, but please... Respect my thoughts and don't start to post things like "Show respect for the devs". I respect the dev's work and time to code Unity, but I simply think that it won't go down well with many Ubuntu users, like over-salted soup. And I've seen a few people saying "I'm going off to use Fedora" or the likes, well, that's the truth, users will start to move to other distributions.

---

There are a lot of reasons to create Unity, it is not about appearance of gnomeshell like you said. Read indicators, notifyosd and other software made in canonical in the past and not accepted in gnome upstream (also as external dependency).
A poor roadmap and timeline of gnomeshell that had a boost in development only in the last months.
The poor performances of mutter instead of Compiz and other things (give a read at Shuttleworth blog for more info)

> **3. Unity was an interface for the Netbook edition, keep it that way**

People don't need huge icons (talking about the launcher) unless they are visually impaired, and in that case they should use the magnifying tool. ### Post by NightwishFan on 2011-04-26
Natty is certainly stable. It is a frozen time based release. It will have few if any infrastructure changes after it is released. Will it have bugs? Certainly. I hardly think Ubuntu will be released with release blocking issues. Time will tell.

Though there is really only one positive thing to do if the Ubuntu release has many usability issues or obvious problems; Take it slower the next time around and learn from our mistakes.

Angry flame wars achieve nothing. If you want to leave Ubuntu please feel able to. We are all users of free software no matter what we use. There is even open source for Windows and Mac. If you still like Ubuntu, think of what you like or would like in Unity or how you can continue to use Ubuntu. Rather than focus on what is wrong or how you cannot use it.

That is not to say you have no right to criticize. If Unity and Ubuntu are falling short of your expectations you have a right to say so. Such opinions should be respected and discussed. The problem is when opinion is stated as fact.

### Post by Horseboy on 2011-04-27
> **lucazade said:**

Have you ever seen Linus asking for kernel features to the community? no, never.. 
because users don't usually know what they want! Developers are instead professional.

You're reply seems to be making the point that compared to the Ubuntu developers, we, the community, are small. That's not true. Yeah, no arguing over there regarding Gnome 3! :rolleyes:  Believe me, Unity is not unique in this, Gnome 3 is getting it's collection of hate-mail too.  

One difference is Gnome is larger than just one distribution so there is more "I don't like it, but the developers have chosen" where with Unity is is "Canonical doesn't listen to us!".

While some people see Canonical doing what they want as a bad thing, I see it as a good:[LIST]
[*]Canonical is making decisions, sometimes hard decisions, with long-range goals in mind.
[*]They make decisions based on knowing what the market holds, what companies and customers want and what developers can or will be able to produce.
[*]They can make changes based on ideas, not the emotional clamor by the community.
[*]Their innovations and work puts them at the level of being compared with Microsoft and Apple.  
Ubuntu has pushed Linux into recognition unheard of outside of the server room, since it came out in the mid-2000s.  Linux has been around a long time before that.
[*]They can listen to the community, but listening does not mean obedience.  
Does Apple, Adobe & Microsoft do what the people tell them they want? Does Red Hat do what the _community _wants them to? No, they do what their _customers _want and remember, as a community user you're getting a "free ride", you aren't paying their bills.
Or another way to think about it is, you listen to your children, but do you obey them without question?
[*]Projects considered good are adopted, others are lessons learned.
Maybe that's why so many distributions are spun off of Ubuntu, to easily incorporate these innovations.
[*]Ubuntu is like the radical artists of the Linux world, where their art (distribution) produces thought, reflection and often influences change in the world. 
[/LIST]

In the balance of "giving what people want" and "innovation" there will always be arguments.  Despite Steve Job's reality-warping field, Apple products are not immune to controversy.

---

### Post by _outlawed_ on 2011-04-27
> **Horseboy said:**
> Canonical is turning into Microsoft, going their own route and not thinking about what the community want.

Microsoft uses feedback from its users to improve their OS, which is one of the reasons why Windows 7 was born, just as Canonical does. They only just offering a little something extra (Unity) for those that like new things.

The Windows community was asking for something powerful, reliable and secure...and what was born out of that? Windows 7.

People wanted a Windows release with the performance of XP and the stylish eye-candy of Vista, and out came Windows 7.

People wanted to stick with Ubuntu Classic in 11.04, and Canonical allows you to change to it willingly.

I am not leaning to one side, I support both Microsoft and Canonical both. Microsoft made an extremely good product (Windows 7) and the same applies to Canonical (Ubuntu).

### Post by Johnsie on 2011-04-27
> I really don't see how a user can do a better job than a developer.
They have hands and eyes like us, they will use natty like us and they will be able to fix and improve things. Something a user cannot do.


I'm the full-time programmer in the company I work for. If I wrote software that only suited my own requirements then I would lose my job pretty quickly. The job of the developer is to create software that the end user can use effectively. They need to meet the needs and requirements of the end user. Developers and end users have some needs in common, but it would be foolhardy for any developer not to listen to his/her end users. If I didn't listen to my users they would just start using other software that they like better and then there would be no reason for me to be here.

The same is true for Ubuntu, the end user matters (alot).

### Post by vigyani on 2011-04-27

I downloaded the latest Daily Build of Ubuntu 11.04 (April 27th 2011) and did a fresh install on Acer Aspire 4710 laptop.

Played around with it and my first impressions (may be incomplete):

4. Only if one knows what to look for, you can search it.

regards

### Post by NormanFLinux on 2011-04-27
Its basically a search/launcher - you find applications in the global menu and you should be able to drag and drop them on the scrollbar launcher. The scrollbar launcher is the replacement for the now deprecated GNOME bottom panel and Ubuntu Netbook Remix Favorites tab.

---

### Post by vigyani on 2011-04-27
Exactly.

One can search for something only it is known. 

How do you search for something, you do not even know it exists?

---

### Post by NormanFLinux on 2011-04-27
Click the Ubuntu Dash button and scroll through the menu. The same thing can be done with files and folders. You can also do a *wildcard* search by typing the first two or three letters of what you're searching for to obtain results closest to your search.

The menu is hidden by default and pops up only when you need it. The idea is to avoid clutter. The trade off with Ubuntu Netbook Remix is to avoid having to wade through tabbed screens to find what you want to add to your Favorites.

### Post by Roasted on 2011-04-28
> **GnuDragon said:**
> The unity global menu top bar seems just like the Apple bar on my Macbook only not as polished in its implementation; there's a few things that don't quite work and auto-hiding behaviour (of the dock) is plain weird.

Coupled with the combined task bar/dock, it feels Mac-ish but just not as well implemented. It's not smooth or unified and the UI gets in your way in a way that Snow Leo's doesn't; Snow Leo just blends into the background and you forget it's even there. Hope they get it fixed up for release.
 
I note that compiz 'window scaling' task switcher (with the thumbnail grid) is almost the same as Expose (apple task switcher), too but doesn't have the ability to sort by app, or show only windows from certain apps. I have that turned on because i simply can't use the alt+tab "line up all the windows in a row and hope you can somehow find the right one to click on". I need my mini-thumbnail grid to quickly select the right window ;-)

The app menu is quite bad actually, the way it only shows so few apps on the screen and have to click to find others. Not that it's bad but there's better ways of dividing up that menu. That's easy to fix by editing start menu categories though.

Kinda annoyed they didn't make their own UI and just copying Mac OS one - and not properly, it's "clunky" which I can't explain but that's how it feels. All we need now is Coverflow view for folders but I guess that one will have to wait for the outcome of the patent case? :-)

Still at least Ubuntu understands what to do when you want to copy a folder over another folder of the same name - it merges the contents instead of over-writing (Apple weirdness, and it won't be 'fixed' until Lion)

Summary - turn on compiz's window scaling task switcher and have you a mac os beta UI :) or just something like looks similar but feels clunky - also you don't of course have the massive amount of hidden shortcuts mac  os has, instead those are all on menus which makes them feel less  streamlined.

They didn't copy the Mac UI. At all, in fact. While the global menu is similar to Mac's, it's not the same. Likewise, the entire idea behind Unity if ANYTHING is a clone with minor tweaks of Gnome Shell, NOT Mac.

And this is coming from a Mac user...

I've used Ubuntu since late 2005. I've always heard Mac this, Mac that. It looks like Mac, it acts like Mac.

It never has.
It never will.

They've always had their own independent agenda. There are only so many different things you can do with an interface in a limited amount of space. If Ubuntu changed the icons to the left to better prepare themselves for their next interface, so be it. That was their agenda to prepare for their implementation in the future. That doesn't mean they copied Mac by any degree of the imagination.

But I'll be fair. The global menu? Yes it's very similar to my Mac's. In some ways, I prefer it. But at the same time, I don't hate/love either one. They're different while being similar. Other than that, it's a very, very unique atmosphere and very different from Mac.

### Post by dash86no on 2011-04-29
OK, had another go with Unity, and I wish to retract my earlier statement that I thought it was a bad idea. 

Unity is a fantastic interface. Full credit to the guys over at canonical. 

The problems I was having were due to 1) Me not knowing what I was doing and 2) The Beta 11.04 I was running was buggy. 

Now the final release seems to have stabilized Unity on my system, and I read the following guide: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1742326](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1742326)

I am very much enjoying the Unity experience. If you use the keyboard shortcuts you will soon be on your way to enjoying the interface. 

Another brilliant move by the Canonical guys. I'd love to see these guys sell a ton of tablets/netbooks/enterprise support licenses/media through Ubuntu One and make some money deserving of the fantastic products they continue to bring to the market.

### Post by SunnyRabbiera on 2011-04-29
After many years off and on Ubuntu, its time to quit it.
Unity just plain sucks, its a uncustomizable mess that you cant configure the panel or the dock the way you want them.
Gnome shell sucks, again its a uncustomizable mess that you cant configure or customize much.
With ubuntu abandoning the classic gnome desktop in 11.10 its time to end this relationship.
I have now permanently moved to openSUSE KDE, its not perfect, but at least it works and is not under the whims of people who want to change things just for the sake of change.
I think Ubuntu will loose over half its users with the move to unity, it was too new to push out people this soon and its too much built for a notebook to make it a decent desktop experience.
I dont know what happened on the day that both the Gnome or ubuntu developers say to themselves "lets just screw around with a good thing, make it unusable and non customizable!"
To hell with it.
Use openSUSE
use Mandrive
Use Pclinux
use Linux mint
hell even use windows as even though it sucks too at least it can work and you can tweak it a little.
use anything not using unity or gnome shell

### Post by disabledaccount on 2011-04-29
I don't think that Unity is bad (as an idea for new GUI), but I can agree that it could be the biggest mistake to drop gnome "old" interface so early.
I've just tested 11.04 and I'm not impressed of what I've seen:
- Unity is in fact so much different that many peoples won't like it.
- "old" GUI is broken in many aspects (eg. sliders, broken themes).

In general, Canonical went wrong way - Unity should be optional in this release. Moreover I suppose that Unity was forced as primary interface just to avoid implenting Gnome shell - but Canonical fallen in their own trap of too frequent releases and fixed deadlines - Unity is not ready yet, while fallback gnome is broken.
It could be that 11.04 becomes the worse desktop in Ubuntu history.
Besides if they remove old interface, then they should add support for old gfx hw in unity (vesafb?) - otherwise they will lose a lot of users with old laptops/desktops.

### Post by beew on 2011-04-29
> **aguafina said:**
> LoL it's just a menu, it was there before only one inch lower.

If you gonna hate on something about Unity don't let it be something as trivial as the menu position.


It is not trivial if you have multiple non maximized application windows open. It is also not "trivial" that the menu is disembodied from the application window and sticks to the top, it  just makes no sense if you have a big enough screen. 

The global menu itself is easy to remove, but there are a lot of design and functional issues which arise because of the global menu and removing the global menu doesn't solve it (strange menu bugs like applications open full screen without buttons, top panel not supporting applets, wndows behave strangely when maximizing/ restoring etc) That brings to another point, Unity is not modular, you can remove the global menu but in a sense you can't.

### Post by BigSilly on 2011-04-29
I'm using KDE4.6.2 as well as Gnome 3. They're both awesome.

It's kinda sad reading here that so many people are finding change so difficult to adapt to. The whole picture of using Linux for me is constant change. It was a massive change for me when I switched from years of Windows use to Linux all those years ago, way harder than a change to the DE, however radical that might seem initially. I think if I hadn't properly committed to it, I would have just stuck with Windows. I'm glad I stayed with Linux, and that huge change to how I use a computer proved a massively worthwhile effort in the end.

I think Gnome has needed change for some time. It was slowly becoming just another DE, and Gnome is better than that. I was struggling to see the difference between Gnome and XFCE etc, but now Gnome has come up with a radical environment that I have really taken to and to me it's cemented it's future. That is a good thing. Ubuntu has done the same thing with Unity.

### Post by scorp123 on 2011-04-29
> **BigSilly said:**
>  It was a massive change for me when I switched from years of Windows use to Linux all those years ago I use Linux since 1996. Way before GNOME. Way before KDE. When they were first introduced around 1998 they were a welcome change, giving us features we didn't have yet. I welcome such changes.

But with GNOME 3 and Unity .... :confused: Sorry, why fix something that isn't broken in the first place??? :confused:

Thanks but no thanks. I don't have the time to tinker around with these new braindead concept studies. **I have work to do. _Tons_ of work in fact.**

I haven't decided yet what I will do .... maybe simply remove GNOME altogether and go for KDE instead? That shouldn't take too long and I can go back to work.

> **BigSilly said:**
>  It was slowly becoming just another DE And so??? :confused: What's wrong with that? Don't fix it if it ain't not broken.

> **BigSilly said:**
>  but now Gnome has come up with a radical environment  Too radical for me, I find it utterly unusable. I decide where I place my windows. If I want a window to maximize I will do that myself. If I want a window out of the way I want to be able to park it anywhere where I decide, even at the top of the screen --- but that doesn't mean that I want it to automagically maximise!!! That's so utterly brain-dead. This noob-GUI POS is a total turn-off. :-&

At least I know how to help myself and what to do about that. But these changes were so totally not necessary. I really have better things to do than distro-hopping because some idealistic dreamers felt like changing the GUI into some noob-friendly utopia. For me the GUI is just a tool. It has to work. It doesn't have to be radical. If it looks the same over the course of 10+ years ... PERFECT! That's what I want so I don't have to adjust every damn time I work with a different OS. GNOME 2 looks totally the same be that on most Linux distros, BSD releases or commercial UNIX variants such as HP-UX or Solaris ... this is *EXACTLY* how it should be. So I don't have to care about the OS I am working on, I know exactly where to find which app and how to work with this thing no matter on which OS I am ...

Nice job breaking this :(  And Canonical wants Ubuntu to be seen as OS which is *"ready for use in professional environments"*????  Yeah right. LOL.

### Post by TeoBigusGeekus on 2011-04-29
I have finally understood Canonical's intentions with unity.
Guys, they did it to educate Ubuntu users; the whole mess of unity, the inability to find the app or place you want immediately, the lack of customization, etc. have only one purpose: to make Ubuntuers use the command line!!!
Yep, that's right. I've been struggling to understand why they released such an immature, horrible product so early in its development stage; it didn't make sense at all.
But, finally, it all became clear: Canonical wants us to start using the command line!!!
What did you do when you were first greeted by unity? The same thing that I did: ohhh shittttt, where's the friggin terminal?

Well done Canonical!!!

---

### Post by Mark76 on 2011-04-29

### Post by disabledaccount on 2011-04-29
It's second day while I'm trying to use Unity (just rebooted back to 10.04) - I must change my previous statement - Unity is good for phones (mobile devices generally).
Every, even simplest action takes more time and displaying 10 huge icons on 24" display is just... ineffective (I'm trying to say it gently).

> **BigSilly said:**
> It's kinda sad reading here that so many people are finding change so difficult to adapt to. The whole picture of using Linux for me is constant change.

I think Gnome has needed change for some time. It was slowly becoming just another DE, and Gnome is better than that.
Yes, linux is changing constantly. But changes are not better just because something has changed - there are good directions and complete mistakes. Unity for desktop is complete mistake. Unity/Gnome shell for mobiles is what was missing and expected to be developed.

Gnome was stable, clean, intuitive, proffessional-looking environment - now it becomes a phone GUI ...just great.

---

### Post by ZarathustraDK on 2011-04-29
I just don't understand this burning need to complain. Natty has been out for 1 day, 1 DAY, and people starts ragequitting because it's different.

Take a step back, you're using a different UI, it means you have to alter your workflow, you have to CHANGE what you are used to doing, get comfortable with it to the degree that you no longer draw upon the experience and expectations of your former UI's paradigm, and THEN you can complain.

You do not get that from 1 day of experiencing the final product anymore than I become a professional racedriver from getting a joyride in a Ferrari when I'm used to driving a KIA.

Unity has its flaws and stuff that could be done better, but I'd withhold my verdict of Unity until the next LTS is a reality. The fact that there are issues is why Natty is not a LTS.

That was my grumpy rant. No hard feelings ;)

### Post by robert shearer on 2011-04-29
> **ZarathustraDK said:**
> I just don't understand this burning need to complain. Natty has been out for 1 day, 1 DAY, and people starts ragequitting because it's different.

Take a step back, you're using a different UI, it means you have to alter your workflow, you have to CHANGE what you are used to doing, get comfortable with it to the degree that you no longer draw upon the experience and expectations of your former UI's paradigm, and THEN you can complain.

You do not get that from 1 day of experiencing the final product anymore than I become a professional racedriver from getting a joyride in a Ferrari when I'm used to driving a KIA.

Unity has its flaws and stuff that could be done better, but I'd withhold my verdict of Unity until the next LTS is a reality. The fact that there are issues is why Natty is not a LTS.

That was my grumpy rant. No hard feelings ;)

Many of those disaffected have been using Unity not for **1 day** but as testers for several weeks/months.

I've seen innumerable postings in the testing forums telling them not to criticise  an  alpha/beta release but to wait and see how great it will be in final form.

Well, now they have and  they have their opportunity to post their thoughts and comments, that's what a forum is about.

Now were going to see posts saying not to criticise this release but wait for LTS... ?  

(not personal, just pointing out the irony of it all from the sidelines :) )

cheers, Bob.

### Post by geekman462 on 2011-04-29
Here's what I did think when ubuntu updated to 11.04 :
"Pretty !"
"Cool more screen estate !"
"the menu is a bit slow where do you configure it ?"
"'system settings' below 'turn off' ? What's next, an emergency shutdown on the blade of a lawnmower ?!"
"no more inhibit applet ? Damnit ! i use it to only allow the screen turning off after the computer do its RTC bootup"
"Wait 4 clicks to change workspace ?! Oh, no, only a left followed by a RIGHT click ?! What was wrong with clicking directly on the wanted workspace ?!"
"Slowwwwwwww menu, please come up. Pretty please ?"
"Ok, time to use keytouch, and learn the keyboard shortcut to switch workspace. The least they could have done is add a quick tutorial on the desktop"
"Damnit, all the friends I installed Unbuntu for will call me because nothing works anymore ! Time to unplug the phone !"
"Wait, i have a 1920*1080 22' screen : WHY U NO SHOW ALL APPS ?! 5 more results, and you can't show it ?! How the **** do i remove that useless downloadable apps crap ?!"
"But i want chrome icon to be the first ! Why don't you allow me to do so ? *sob*"
"Well better keep it to get used to it, but i do hope its going to morph into something usable soon ; the screen estate thing is neat, at least"

### Post by FreeTheBee on 2011-04-29
I guess unity might work on a netbook or tablet, but  on my 23" screen it took about an hour before I switched back to gnome2.

I'm kinda curious if there is a relation between screen size and unity opinion.

### Post by Roasted on 2011-04-29
When I began testing Gnome Shell and Unity a while back, I hated it. I hated both of them passionately.

Over time, they grew on me significantly.

Gnome Shell still has a ways to go in the usability department. It's very sexy, but after using it in production for a few hours (let alone days, weeks, etc) the zoom in/out to switch apps (yes I know about alt-tab) began to wear on my eyes heavily.

So while I like Gnome Shell, it has a ways to go. Meanwhile, we have Unity, which was also beginning to grow on me. Likewise, it too began to show its benefits in usability in the production environment. And let me just say... DAMN I'm loving it now.

### Post by Mozai on 2011-04-29
> **NCLI said:**
> The doc package will be there in time for release.

Whelp, 11.04 was officially released.  Still no documentation for Unity.  /usr/share/doc/unity/ is empty except for changelog.Debian.gz and copyright, and there is no 'unity-doc' package.

Am I the only one gobsmacked that the makers of Unity never thought to include instructions on how to use it?

I expected something like this:
```
moses@deunan:~$ ls /usr/share/doc/grub2
AUTHORS              copyright       NEWS.gz  THANKS
changelog.Debian.gz  NEWS.Debian.gz  README   TODO

```
(the README for grub2 ain't much, but I was able to use it to write a sox script to preview the GRUB_INIT_TUNE feature, and it told me how I could add non-default features like changing font sizes)

Instead this is what I see:
```
moses@deunan:~$ ls /usr/share/doc/unity
changelog.Debian.gz  copyright

```

So if I want to find out how to add a launcher to the tray, I have to click around at random, or hit the forums and hope some kind soul did the clicking around at random and stumbled on the right answer before I asked.

Gonna go file a usability bug.

EDIT:  The bug for missing documentation was filed on package 'unity' 12 months ago, 2011-07-02.
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/600875](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/600875)
Last comment that isn't a "me too!" was 2010-12-21.

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2011-04-29
> **otherethe said:**
> I dont know about you guys but i hate this new look and fill of the desktop and I thank god i found out how to change it back to classic gnome if i wanna spend 20 hours trying to find stuff or if i wanna Look at a netbook type screen i'd go buy a freaking netbook sorry im sure they put in alot of hardwork and this new Desktop they made would prob go good on a netbook but good lord keep classic gnome as def from now on it took me for ever to find out how to get back to classic even tho it was super simple you guys put wayyyyyyyy to much gui into this Most of us use linux to get away from the gui not to jump knee deep into it Let me know your fillings on this matter guys but let me know your thou on this new desktop GUI they got going here My tho is I HATE IT

Full-stops would've made it easier to read, especially for people who don't have English as their best language.

Unity is built around search. Use it. Instead of navigating the lenses to run programs that you know, hit Alt-F2 and just start typing. Once the program has been run once, you can pin it to the launcher and launch it with a single mouse-click.

How long have you been using Unity? An hour or two? Use it for a week or two. You'll get used to it; in fact, you'll probably like it. There are a couple of corner cases where Unity is not appropriate, such as dual monitors; but Unity actually works better on a big desktop screen than on a netbook because you can fit in more icons on the launcher, and you don't lose precious vertical space on a widescreen monitor.

It's not possible to keep "classic Gnome" forever - it's been abandoned by its developers. Either Unity or plain Gnome 3 are the way forward, and plain Gnome 3 is not much of an option for Ubuntu because it means chucking out a lot of their now-mature Ubuntu-specific code.

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2011-04-29
> **GuiGuy said:**

No it was not. You've only been using the thing for a day. I've been using Unity for months. Unity is very usable, and it's not Unity's fault if you don't want to actually get used to it. I'm using it on netbook and desktop, and I prefer it on the desktop actually.

All Unity's settings are in one place: Compizconfig Settings Manager. If anything, Classic Gnome has "poorly documented" settings - a lot of the useful ones are in gconf-editor.

### Post by Mozai on 2011-04-29
> **GuiGuy said:**
> Windows users moving between XP and 7 feel at home almost instantly.

AH-HA-ha-ha-ha, you must live in a very different place than I.  Windows users moving from XP to Vista or Win7 (usually against their will) has been the biggest source of people asking me "so, how can I use that Linux-a-ma-jig thing I see on your computer?"  

Changing the interface paradigm is an ubiquitous barrier to getting done, which causes frustration and upset users.

At least with Ubuntu/Unity, the login screen(*) gives you the option of falling back to a more familiar interface.  When Mac users moved from System9 to OSX, and when Windows users moved from XP/2003 to Vista or Win7, they were forbidden from using the old paradigm.

(*:gdm or slim.  I'm guessing you wouldn't install kdm unless you intended to always use KDE, and I don't know anyone who still uses the stock X11 xdm anymore.)

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-04-29
This behavior can actually be modified in CompizConfig as well, so that the dock only shows when you hover the upper left.

> Docky can, not sure abot the others, but I thiink Cairo and AWN have those options too. It's called "window dodge".
CD and AWN have it as well and have for some time. Technically, it's the "Dodge Active Window" or "Intellihide" behavior. It's especially nice combined with AWN's Transparency hide, where the dock becomes a just-barely-visible overlay instead of disappearing completely, so that you can see you click target while you're moving the mouse toward it.

> Docky's creator is also the guy who wrote the Unity plugin.
This explains a lot. Docky's pretty sleek and popular but fairly simplistic compared to Cairo Dock and AWN. By design, but still.

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-04-29
There are instructions for disabling the global menu [here]("http://www.webupd8.org/2011/03/disable-appmenu-global-menu-in-ubuntu.html"), if it bothers you too much.  I haven't tried it yet myself.  Like I said, I really like the global menu on a netbook, where it frees up precious vertical space.  On my desktop with a 24" monitor, I like it for maximized windows, but not for small windows, especially for windows near the bottom of the screen.

I never have too many open windows on one workspace, so it hasn't taken me too long to get used to it.  The above link tells you how to disable the menu for specific programs too - so if you're wasting time with Aisle Riot running down at the bottom of the screen you can let it keep its menu.  In the link I gave there's also a link to a mailing list discussion; it sounds like this is not yet settled among the devs.  It's possible that in a future Ubuntu we'll see an option to have the global menu in use for maximized windows only, which could be useful.

### Post by Mozai on 2011-04-29
> **NCLI said:**
> Press F1, or use the Dash to search for "Help"

Back again, after a good solid 'apt-get update && apt-get upgrade && reboot' for the sake of hygiene.  And I re-enabled my F1 key.

**Ubuntu Desktop Guide** this is faster than the GTK Help I'm used to seeing when I hit F1.  Hey!  This is the same /usr/bin/yelp I was using with 10.10; I wonder why it's faster now?  This looks really good, and it still works when I'm disconnected from the Internet.  Good stuff; this addresses my concerns about a lack of documentation.


I'm going to give Unity a good try.  First thing I gotta do is get rid of that autohide that's making the tray... uh, dock?  launcher?  Microsoft calls it a 'tray', Apple calls it a 'dock', GNOME calls it a panel, Unity documentation calls it a 'launcher' or 'dash' at different places in the documentation. They even refer to it by both names on the same page:
```
Ubuntu Desktop Guide » Use the Launcher to start applications › Customizing the Launcher »
To add an application to the launcher for easy access:
Drag the application's icon from the dash onto the launcher.
...

Ubuntu Desktop Guide » Overview › The Desktop »
Dash
 The dash is the list of your favorite applications that is shown on the
 left-hand side of the activities overview. Applications that are
 currently running are also shown here. The dash is sometimes referred to
 as the dock.
```
... so to add an application to the launcher (that thing on the left side), I'm supposed to drag it from the dash (that thing on the left side) to the launcher (that thing on the left side).  [sound of head hitting desk]

ANYways, whatever it's called, it's sliding in and out every time I change focus between windows.  Motion in my peripheral vision makes my reptile-brain focus on it, so I keep looking away from whatever I'm working on.  Gotta fix that.

wait, no, nevermind, bigger problem: the panel (menubar? whatever) that thing that's the 1/2 centimeter at the top of my screen just went entirely black.  Still response to mouse clicks, if I can guess where the buttons are.  And two of the Unity ?lenses? the launcher/dash widgets (GNOME calls them 'panel applets') for the applications button, and the Files & Folders button just vanished off the launcher/dash while I was writing this.  I saw them blink away while I was writing this paragraph about the top bar thing turning black.  asdfjkl, it's like Unity is falling apart before my eyes, this is nuts.

I can't recommend this to my 57-year-old mom; she's used to GNOME, and she'll find this more frustrating than I do.  I can't answer the questions she will have before I can answer my own questions.


I will say the documentation is there, and the interface for the documentation is pretty good, better than what I could've done on my own, and better than what I'm used to with GNOME.  Kudos to the Ubuntu-doc team... just gotta iron out wrinkles.

### Post by el_koraco on 2011-04-29
> **Copper Bezel said:**
> 
This explains a lot. Docky's pretty sleek and popular but fairly simplistic compared to Cairo Dock and AWN. By design, but still.

Cairo is a hog. AWN is sweet, but there's a lot of tinkering going on for my tastes. Let's start a dock flame war! 

By the way, a lot of the Unity launcher is a kind of replica of the Docky behavior, only ported to Compiz directly. The guy said he that during the negotiations for Unity he didn't wanna just port Docky to Ubuntu, because that would constrain it. I do think some of the ideas will be going back and fourth in the future, which is cool for everybody concerned.

### Post by JayKay3OOO on 2011-04-29
I remember 2 or so years ago when I first moved to Linux and had to get used to gnome that it took a while to get used to and i've never really liked it, always tweaking and trying to find 'the right look' and never really getting it.

Hence the reason I now run Joli Cloud on my laptop. However, it does not support the pae kernel and the desktop does not scale well for screens much larger than 15.4inch.

I'm sure that I'm in the minority here, but while unity is not perfect, it's certainly a fun step in potentially the right direction and will probably make the operating system more attractive for new users which i'm sure is the aim.

It's not perfect, but it's nice to have a change for the desktop edition as there as so many waves of distributions that all look the same, but carry different names.

### Post by jdunn on 2011-04-29
I have only three hangups with Unity: 1) The Applications menu shows "frequently used" and "apps available for download" whether I want to see them or not. 2) A small number of applications don't play well out-of-the-box with Unity, particularly TrueCrypt which relies on a status-bar icon but there's a workaround to fix it. 3) Creating an application menu item is currently a hassle...requires a text-editor to create a .desktop file.

Overall, I like Unity. The launcher is easy to use and pin apps to. it makes sense to have the launcher on the side because most displays are widescreen with less vertical resolution than horizontal. The launcher also hides if you expand an app to full-screen but moving the mouse to the extreme-left unhides it momentarily. My favorite part of Unity is the Workspace Switcher...moving apps between workspace desktops was never easier.

---

### Post by mutantstargoat on 2011-04-29

### Post by lovinglinux on 2011-04-29
> **Copper Bezel said:**
> This behavior can actually be modified in CompizConfig as well, so that the dock only shows when you hover the upper left.


CD and AWN have it as well and have for some time. Technically, it's the "Dodge Active Window" or "Intellihide" behavior. It's especially nice combined with AWN's Transparency hide, where the dock becomes a just-barely-visible overlay instead of disappearing completely, so that you can see you click target while you're moving the mouse toward it.


This explains a lot. Docky's pretty sleek and popular but fairly simplistic compared to Cairo Dock and AWN. By design, but still.

I use Kwin, not Compiz.

### Post by Mark76 on 2011-04-29
I dutifully upgraded and the first thing that happened when I rebooted was a "signal out of range" box moving around the screen.

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-04-29
I filed a wishlist bug on launchpad during development to request some sort of editor like alacarte that could be used to customize the Applications lens. What I'd like to see is some sort of nesting, so that if I had lots of games installed, for example (as I do) I could sort those games into various categories.  I'd like an applications place where you first see the different software categories (Accessories, Games, etc), and then by clicking on a category see the applications in that category or, where applicable, a list of subcategories - sort of like what you see in the software centre, only with the ability to sort things to your own tastes.

### Post by Modanung on 2011-04-29

Is there **any way** I can get rid of:
- The *'Launcher'* (AAAAARGH!@$*?)
- The fusing of my menu bar
- The Configuration Screen

- Selected few neatly ordered icons
- Right click on any bar to add or remove elements (where'd the weather go?)
- Clear menu's with easy access
- Scrollbars
- Smiley face

...**without degrading** to a previous version. I'm hoping it's a well hid setting.
Seriously, what happened? You don't lure people away from windows by imitating them, if that's what someone was trying. I can't see any other reasons to copy bad ideas.
I am very disappointed and slightly frustrated. Thank you.

### Post by blinkylights23 on 2011-04-29
Seems like there are a lot of really nice features in Unity. I really like the type-to-find thing in the Dash, and I like having the taskbar in the top panel. The dock/launcher does some cool things, and I'm interested in the promise of app-specific quicklinks on the launcher icons.

It's not ready for regular use, though. The Dash would make a really nice compliment to Main Menu, but it doesn't quite work as a Main Menu replacement, IMHO. I'm also confused by why the Dash hides most of my application icons in order to make room for "frequently used" stuff that's already on my launcher and a list of Mahjong and Bible memorization apps some algo thinks I might want to install. Also confused about why I can't just turn that stuff off. And really - *must* Dash be full screen?

Anyway, looks very promising but there are enough usability problems and obviously missing features that I'm going to have to check back in with Unity at some future date. Like maybe when it has some configuration options.

### Post by NCLI on 2011-04-29
> **Mozai said:**
> Back again, after a good solid 'apt-get update && apt-get upgrade && reboot' for the sake of hygiene.  And I re-enabled my F1 key.

**Ubuntu Desktop Guide** this is faster than the GTK Help I'm used to seeing when I hit F1.  Hey!  This is the same /usr/bin/yelp I was using with 10.10; I wonder why it's faster now?  This looks really good, and it still works when I'm disconnected from the Internet.  Good stuff; this addresses my concerns about a lack of documentation.
I be lazy right now, so I'm not gonna Google a link for you, but OMGUbuntu had an article about this new version of yelp being a huge improvement a few months back.

> I'm going to give Unity a good try.  First thing I gotta do is get rid of that autohide that's making the tray... uh, dock?  launcher?

I dobt that this will be in the documentation, since it's still a bit experimental, but you have to install CompizConfigSettingsManager to modify most of Unity's settings.  > Microsoft calls it a 'tray', Apple calls it a 'dock', GNOME calls it a panel, Unity documentation calls it a 'launcher' or 'dash' at different places in the documentation. They even refer to it by both names on the same page:
[code]Ubuntu Desktop Guide » Use the Launcher to start applications &#8250; Customizing the Launcher »
To add an application to the launcher for easy access:
Drag the application's icon from the dash onto the launcher.
...
This is the Dash:
[IMG]http://2.bp.blogspot.com/_5bdO0FMWXa8/TUvy8IGsLfI/AAAAAAAAAh4/dbF-qag09TI/s400/Screenshot.png[/IMG]
This is the Launcher:
[IMG]http://lh4.googleusercontent.com/_1QSDkzYY2vc/TXlFcCAaAFI/AAAAAAAADak/19ngeIP6gKo/s400/unity-launcher-resizable.png[/IMG]

> wait, no, nevermind, bigger problem: the panel (menubar? whatever) that thing that's the 1/2 centimeter at the top of my screen just went entirely black.  Still response to mouse clicks, if I can guess where the buttons are.  And two of the Unity ?lenses? the launcher/dash widgets (GNOME calls them 'panel applets') for the applications button, and the Files & Folders button just vanished off the launcher/dash while I was writing this.  I saw them blink away while I was writing this paragraph about the top bar thing turning black.  asdfjkl, it's like Unity is falling apart before my eyes, this is nuts.
alt+F2, then write "ubuntu-bug unity"

> I can't recommend this to my 57-year-old mom; she's used to GNOME, and she'll find this more frustrating than I do.  I can't answer the questions she will have before I can answer my own questions.

Then for the love of God, don't! I'm not ever planning to upgrade my grandmother either. My own mother has taken well to it though, so maybe you could give it a try once 12.04 rolls around?

### Post by kiridude on 2011-04-30
I don't really like Unity, not cuz of some familiarity with the old, but because it's clunky and un-intuitive. In Gnome, I had one little icon in the corner of my screen from which I could access anything on my computer. I like simplicity, minimalism, and effectiveness. Why would I want a bunch of clunky icons on my screen that offer nothing more than before? In fact they complicate things.

I also hate how the right click has been disabled. WTF? I did not get apple when I decided to drop Windows years ago for that reason. I have to do research for an hour to figure out how to edit Unity's setup. No good.

Another basic qualm: cant see the windows I have open. No Good. 

I simply don't have the time (or desire) to spend hours figuring Unity out. Gnome is intuitive and one can figure alot of stuff out easily. Not so with Unity.

With that said, I'm a big fan of Ubuntu. I just think the Unity thing needs to be re-thought a bit.

---

### Post by underling08 on 2011-04-30
I finished the upgrade to Natty today, and although I usually look forward to Ubuntu upgrades, this one turned out to be a total reversal of what I expect from Ubuntu.

IMO, Unity is awful. It seems to sacrifice the control and usability of GNOME for some idealistic sense of simple aesthetics that, while admittedly pleasing to the eye, my lack of ability to easily access system settings or the settings for Unity to fiddle with the way it looked made me physically nauseous. I just don't think Unity is ready for use, as others have said, and personally I'll be sticking to GNOME, which I know and love inside out. Maybe the change was just too shocking to me, but the rudimentary style of Unity took away what has always felt like the POWER behind running Ubuntu on my machine.

GNOME for life!
