### Post by RobOrr on 2011-04-30
To me, unity is a poorly implemented version of my pre-upgrade desktop environment.

I had a customised dock using the docky package from the repos, and the gnome-do package was more lightweight, and required less input to load the requested programs than this new type locator function. I'll be switching to a different distro probably - I like to have a pain-free upgrade and if I have to faff around every release uninstalling unity and installing and re-customising gnome then Ubuntu's more trouble than it's worth I'm afraid.

I think this was a pretty bad time to implement unity in the main release, it should have been an opt-in install rather than an automatic until it was a lit less intensive on resources - a lot of people I know use Ubuntu because of its ease of use whilst remaining fast. Unity has made my ageing computer noticeably less smooth.

### Post by Jan Sloep on 2011-04-30
I completely agree. **A desktop computer is a desktop computer. Not an oversized smartfone. **

What moron thinks that on a desktop computer it is more productive to be able to open just one window at a time? What idiot thinks that a panel at the left side of the screen with big mickey mouse icons takes less space than a top side panel that is there anyway, but which makes it impossible to add icons to? 

Yes, ofcourse this is fine or even necessary for a smartfone or a netbook but not for a desktop. But even so. I would not think about putting this unity garbage on my android smartfone, where my icons are spread in 3 x 3 columns over several workspaces, next to a panel at the bottom of my screen. Even on a smartfone, unity would be garbage. 
Beside that. Unity is much too late to even have a chance to ever be able to compete with android or iphone.  

**A desktop computer is a productivity tool**, not a toy or a thing to quickly look up some information or to send friends a short message.

I have a big 26" screen where I can put all the shortcuts I need in the top panel. I use the grid function of compiz to split my screen with one keystroke in 2 or more windows, so in one window I can have my browser to search for the information I need, while working in my word processor in the other window. I even have a second 19" vertical screen next to my big screen, in which I can put other documents or pdf's as reference material. And even then, I still have to switch to another workspace from time to time.

So what do I do now, as also gnome3 is the same kind of garbage as unity and gnome2 will probably be fased out with Ubuntu 11.10?

First of all I will not upgrade to Ubuntu 11.04 and wait and see. And hope that there will be that much resistance against Unity and Gnome3 that Gnome will keep supporting Gnome2 as an alternative to Gnome3.  

And if this does not happen within a year the only option left will be that I switch either to KDE or even Windows (which never in my live I thought would ever happen, but it is a clear sign of how desperate I am)

To my opinion unity and gnome are a disaster for the Linux community as well, as they will divide instead of unite. And far from good enough as an alternative for either windows, osx, ios or android. 
Also for the developpers unity and gnome3 will be a disaster as the philosophy behind unity and gnome3 is too much different from, for example KDE, whatever Mark Shuttleworth says.

I have been an Ubuntu user and admirer of Mark Shuttleworth for many years now, but it looks like that is going to be different from now on. The introduction of unity and the end of gnome2 is one of the saddest things that happend to me in my life. It almost feels as if I am betrayed.

But one thing is certain. I will never touch Unity or Gnome3 again.

### Post by NormanFLinux on 2011-04-30
There are three steps, find your applications, add them to the scrollbar launcher and run them when needed.

The scrollbar launcher in Unity keeps everything ready when you need them. A conventional dock is more customizable and you can add one in Classic GNOME or add applets to the panel like you did before.

### Post by janpol on 2011-04-30
What I like about Natty:

.Looks OK (looks better than GNOME, but not as good as KDE, but this is a matter of personal taste only).
.When you press the windows key, a menu pops up, and you can search anything from there (I loved this feature)
.Works great (for my hardware at least, YMMV)

What I don't like:

.The show stopper for me: The global menu when you use minimized windows (like the terminal). When I open something minimized, it really bothers me that the menu appears at the top of the screen (far away from my window when I use a large screen). If there is a way to change this behaviour, please tell me :)
.I can't right click on a usb drive and format it, like I used to do on 10.10 (and I use this a lot).
.The default theme bored me (usually I don't like dark themes). It was cool on 10.04, it still looked nice in 10.10, now it's boring :P (but you can change this easily). 

So, I still love Ubuntu, but now in it's KDE flavor ;). If those nasty global menus go away (on minimized windows only ofc) I might use Unity as my default desktop, but till then, I'm loving KDE 4.6 (only missing the Software Center).

### Post by l3vity on 2011-04-30
I have only relatively recently joined the Ubuntu bandwagon. My first system was Maverick, and I thoroughly enjoyed the transfer. I found my productivity greatly improved and performance was much better than previous. I have used it as my sole OS for the last 4 months now, before upgrading to Natty. I had previously set up Docky and it ran similar to the Unity bar.

My first thoughts on unity was "where's my app menu gone" as I generally used this for launching most applications. Now in order to browse my applications it does generally take a little longer. However, I have found myself preferring the keyboard options instead, the SUPER button search is great, i use it to run pretty much all programs not on the dock (to save space)

I also love the global menu. I know a lot of people are complaining about it, but I like it. Besides, there are ways to turn it off, it's just not exactly obvious.

At first I also thought that it was completely uncustomisable, however, after reading a few blogs I have managed to create custom launchers using the quickmenu function. By right clicking my home menu, i can select which folder to start from, similar to the places button was.
I have also included a "work" launcher that clicking opens gedit, but by right clicking it I can launch several different applications, netbeans, my course notes folder, libreoffice, and a few others. This works quite nicely, similar to a Drawer on gnome panels, just without the icons.

The only downside is you cannot remove certain menus, such as the workspace switcher, which I never use, I've always used keyboard controls.
Also, it would be nice to re-arrange the indicators (I like having mail and chat next to each other, and time in the corner next to shutdown etc)

So all in all, I quite like Unity, however, I think if I had a touchscreen, it would be miles better. I say keep it.

### Post by braden7 on 2011-04-30
The new interface is garbage. It looks like some idiot-proofed embedded consumer device. The long-standing DE design ideas which have been with us for years are there for good reason - they work. Panels, taskbars, and menus have been refined to be productive and useful.

I know this might be terribly uncomfortable for the hipsters and graphic artists, but the purpose of a computer is to get things done, not look cool as it gets in the way.

3D acceleration for shiny icons and transitions? Sorry, I use a computer to get things done from within applications, not sit there staring at the DE itself. At least it isn't as bad as KDE4 which forces annoying, time-wasting transitions and fades on everything, on top of the need to cram heavy graphics into every unwelcome corner.

**Fortunately there is a way to take out the trash. Open up Synaptic, find *unity*, and rip the crap out. The nuisance will be gone and you will be back to a functional Gnome 2. I just got done testing this in a Virtualbox, and fortunately, the devs didn't make the entire system depend on Unity. I'll be upgrading to 11.04 shortly, forgetting that goofy experiment ever made it onto the ISO.**

### Post by braden7 on 2011-04-30
> **arnelcolar said:**
> I think its faster to open applications in Unity compared to what we are used to. If I want to look for an application, just type it in. Way faster than locating it inside a menu. Plus you don't need to download optional dock because it comes installed.

This I think revolutionizes the way we look at desktops and in Linux. A unique concept that only those who accepts change will be able to harness Unity's full potential.

The fastest way to open applications is by pinning them to the Gnome top panel, which is already there, and had extra space anyway. For the couple of most-used applications, this is a winning strategy. My nine most used applications are pinned right to an overhead panel via small, unobtrusive icons, eliminating the need for nested menus and goofy search bars entirely, but without the resource waste which comes with an add-on dock.

Figure that at least 80% of your application launches will come from a small set of applications.

### Post by garydtaylor on 2011-04-30
Part of me likes it, part of me doesnt.  Not much help if you are trying to decide based on what we post here -so, here is what I do and don't like.


Likes:
----------
1. Being able to do so much with just the keyboard.  I hate knowing exactly what I want to do but having to go via menus to find it.
2. Switching between windows when you can't really remember what you have running under what is made easy.  
3. Searching for files
4. Whilst not part of unity as such, I love dim inactive windows.  This made me switch to KDE in previous versions of ubuntu, but I always ended up back in gnome, so it obviously wasnt that important !
5. The intelligent hiding - i.e. get out of the way if I am in the way, otherwise show itself.

Dislikes
------
1. I switched from Mac OSX because of poor window management with 'Spaces' ad the dock.  I want each 'space' or I believe they are called desktops in linux - to have its own dock.  Almost the equivalent to it being its own machine !!  So, when I click on google chrome and there is no google chrome running in that desktop, I want it to open one, not jump to an already open instance in another desktop and lose track of where I was.  That is what OSX does and it drives me mad.

2. Occasional loss of keyboard shortcut 'Super + Space' which I believe is supposed to be used for the main search box.


So, I am now about to re install as I messed up my system installing gnome 3 (which looks better in my opinion, but I am now too afraid to try it again).  I will then stick with unity for now and use'Cairo Dock' as well

### Post by ghostborg on 2011-04-30
Overall it seems pretty good.
I am not a big fan of the Apple way of the file menu which how I work makes me move the mouse around more. Especially when you have several windows open, I first have to look up to see which window has focus then click on the window to give it focus and then drag my mouse all the way over to the top left to use the file menu. On larger high resolution panels this can be a bit more work than the old way. It's not a show stopper of course but just my preference.
I liked Docky for the Zooming eye candy but stopped using it because it would be useless to have two launchers.
I do like having the Unity launcher on the side because with a wide screen panel more height real estate is nice.
The workplace switcher does not show me at a glance which one I'm currently in.
I miss adding applets to the top panel, one of my favorites was the system monitor which had a small graph showing me how hard my cpu was working. I liked looking up at it and knowing when some cpu intensive process was done in another workspace.
I like how it hides when a window bumps into it or maximized which is nice if I have Virtualbox maximized.
Overall, I think it's a keeper and I'm sure things will be figured out by the community. I have not given Gnome 3 a spin yet and am curious if I would like that better.

### Post by MugOTea on 2011-04-30
> **themainliner said:**
> Why is EVERYTHING so much harder?

I want to use Synaptic, where is it? 

1) Ubuntu button 2) More Apps 3) All Applications 4) System 5) Click 11 more results 6) Synaptic Package Manager! Oh yeah this is more efficient...


I wasn't expecting unity on the upgrade either, but I have to defend it here, try:
<super> | Type: syn | Click Synaptic Package Manager

I found it much quicker than System > Administration > Synaptic Package Manager

They also included a launcher icon for the software centre which is the same thing really.

There's some cool features and eye candy with Unity, it has a few teething problems too but that's to be expected, that's what user feedback and updates are for.

I say, give it a go and give canonical a chance to fix the bugs and read / digest the user feedback... they've not let us down too bad in the past!:guitar:

### Post by Rinzwind on 2011-04-30

### Post by Ken UK on 2011-04-30
Ok so lets see, I want to change the appearance of my desktop so:

[LIST]
[*]Click Applications
[*]Type 'appearance' (or at least start to)
[*]Either click enter or click on it
[/LIST]

Am I missing what all the complaining is about? How is that difficult or slower? Infact if you use things regular you miss out the typing in the middle!

I have 2 x 24" monitors @ 1900 x 1200 and I find Unity and the new bar at the top fine. It makes my already big display bigger, I get much more on and the new drag drop thing with the windows which is the same as windows 7 is great, it even works on dual display so I can quickly have 4 things even spread across my screens.

As for the bar at the top, sure I admit you have to wonder upto the top with your cursor but at least with dual display if its something on the right screen it detects it and you use the right top bar and the left top bar for the left screen instead of going from the bottom of the right screen upto the top of the left. I really appreciate these small things that have been thought of and am impressed!

If moving your cursor so far is a problem you are either new to using a mouse or have your mouse sensitivity to minimum because you have no coordination and in both cases I say "bless you, get more practice". I do it without thinking and its nothing to me, but then again Im not lazy.

---

### Post by angryfirelord on 2011-04-30
I really don't have anything too positive to report on Unity.

-First, for some reason it insists on using OpenGL extensions. If this was an interface for netbooks, then the developers should have realized that putting extra strain on the graphics card will only eat away at the battery and make it run slow. My netbook only has a GMA 950, so you can certainly see the slowness. 
-Second, the buttons really make no sense. I still consider a menu-driven interface the best way to find and run applications. If you need to execute something quickly, that's why there are desktop icons. 
-Minimizing some apps works fine. Other apps disappear and clicking on the icon launches a new instance of that app.
-Finally, the whole thing looks like a cheap ripoff of the Mac OS X dock and toolbar up top (with the way the application's name and options appear on the toolbar). I hated OS X's interface because it seemed to take me forever to find things.

I understand Canonical's need to do something different, but releasing slow and buggy software is no excuse.

---

### Post by braden7 on 2011-04-30
> **Throne777 said:**
> I think you'll find it comes out as mostly negative, mostly because people who have a problem with something tend to be a lot more vocal than those who don't.

I think you'll find that is because those who are adversely impacted by something like this are hurt to a greater degree than those who are helped find benefit.

The upside isn't that strong, but the downside is huge.

Somebody who likes it will say "cool" and move on. It isn't as if this is their first GUI desktop, after years of text only. Somebody who can't tolerate it will be moving on as well, to an entirely different system.

### Post by truck87bp on 2011-04-30

Work Space switcher needs to be locked at the top of the Icon Bar so its always in the same place.

My experience with my Netbook wasn't bad but not having the full Compiz compliment on the Live CD SUCKED for both my Netbook and Desktop. Leave the office program off the CD and replace it with Compiz. Without Compiz the testing was a total waste of time.

I will keep a vigilant eye on the progress of Unity but will **_NOT_** be installing it on my main machines until it has been tailored more to fit the Ubuntu Community Experience.:popcorn:

### Post by Fury5000 on 2011-04-30
As an IT Pro dealing with users Windows problems I convert as many as I can to Linux.  Ubuntu being my top choice for compatibility and support.  With this latest release I truly can no longer recommend Ubuntu to the average new to Linux user.   The simple and intuitive interface is no longer the default and would just leave the new user with a very “Vista like” experience followed by aggravation and frustration.  My personal experience with the new interface was severely disappointing.  Being an “expert” in Ubuntu Linux, “I” could not figure out how to turn off this new interface and had to google where the elusive “classic mode” toggle was.  Then when back in classic mode I found the “invisible scroll bars” still in effect.   Another feature that just boggles my mind.  Its like hiding the buttons to the elevator in a public building.  Why add even more confusion for the user by hiding things to gain literally 3 millimeters of desktop real-estate.  So, I went to turn off compiz thinking this feature must be part of that interface to find “desktop effects”.. missing/hidden.   Then the “expert” had to google where that feature went.  Ok, it was back out at the log in page as “classic without effects”.   Amazing.   Came back on to find the invisible scroll bars still “ever so slowly fading in” further slowing my productivity.   Then I had to google how to remove this feature via the terminal commands.   For a moment I was satisfied that I had at least a working 11.04 retro environment but there was no way I was going to talk a new user through the steps necessary to make this new Ubuntu usable to the novice.   Hearing that the classic mode will be removed at a later time has closed the book on Ubuntu as the linux distro of choice for the conversion of new users.  It saddens me that new users will come to find this version as there first taste of Linux and will quickly go back to windows never knowing that it used to be as good and as simple as 10.04.

---

### Post by ully-mick on 2011-04-30
Tried it, don't like it, removed it. back to classic Gnome for me. Gnome 2 might not be pretty in it's basic form, but it's highly customizable.

I have a 22" 1680 x 1050 screen. I don't need or want a scrolling side dock with all my programs uncategorized. I currently use awn at the bottom with 10 of my most commonly used programs. any other I occasionally use are in the applications menu. 

I don't like the community toolbar, it seems to be designed for full screen use. I don't use programs in full screen. I often have 3 or 4 windows open. when I no longer need 1 of the back windows I can click the cross to close it, with this I first have to bring it into focus to get it's toolbar, then close it.

I'll stick with Gnome 2 until the next release. then I'll try Gnome 3 (assuming Unity can be removed from it,) and see if that is more customizable. otherwise it Kubuntu, Mint or whatever else appears in the meantime.

### Post by beew on 2011-04-30
> **3rdalbum said:**
> People have very quickly forgotten that there were constant complaints that Gnome 2 "looks like it comes from the mid-1990s".

Unity has been written from scratch, and the basic usability of it has always been implemented before customization options. I don't expect it to be as customizable as Gnome 2's shell, but it's already got some good options.

Well then don't make it the default with so much fanfare and instigate people to upgrade if it is not ready. It is as if Canonical cannot wait to make a bad first impression.

But you are not entirely on the mark though, as some of the lack of customizability is by design (zero configuring experience?) It is one thing to say that customization will come later and we only have to be patient, quite another to create a locked down UI which is intentionally not customizable. 

I like innovation and pretty desktop, but not all changes are for the better and we shouldn't like all of them just so that we don't get labeled as being "afraid of change", this is just common sense. 

Vanilla Gnome 2 does look Windows 9x-ish (and Mint makes it even more so with the Mint menu) But with a little customization (Compiz, emerald, a dock ..) It can look very modern and  slick, my 10.10 laptop looks great beside the mac book pro and the mac book pro can't do the cube, let alone win9x. You can even make g2 looks like Unity, but it is a lot more usable. Change by all means but if you end up losing functionality and options that is a regression.

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-04-30
Wow ... I tried, I mean I really tried ...
Two beta versions and the actual bonafide 11.04 release ...

The pros about Unitiy is that it does exactly what it's meant to do. It works beautifully for all of those people who spend more time with their cell phone apps then a computer and it works just as beautifully for anyone who's never had a computer before, at least that's my opinion. I would have expected the setup to be as simple as Win7 or Linux Mepis ver. 11 by now, but ah well. Those are two great examples of how easy an installation could/should be. :confused:

But for me, as a power user with dozens of applications that are set up "just so" on four different partial (reduced size) panels on my screen, Unity is rubbish. I could write the many things down that I found disappointing, irritating, hard to find, impossible to find, but I'm not going to do that because it would take way too long. Switching to the Classic Desktop during login wasn't really an option for me either because some of my backup apps. clearly did not want to function correctly anymore. Then, after restoring my Ubuntu Tweak settings ... which caused the system to crash out completely over and over ... I decided that that was it for me, switching back to 10.10 instead. That took all of 45 minutes and everything is back the way it was. Thank goodness ... :)

I have to agree with what a lot of people who've posted on numerous forums had to say. It should have been the other way around ... Classic Gnome should have been the default and Unity should have been the thing to try out, to decide if it's what one wants to use. It's much worse than switching from Ubuntu to Kubuntu, even for a new user. I suspect that the new Gnome which will be released with Fedora 15 or the next version of Ubuntu Mint will be the next Linux Distro for me.

It saddens me that I'll probably say good-bye to Ubuntu forever within the next few months, but my Unity experience was simply unacceptable, terrible would be more apt. Since Unity will remain the standard desktop and since I believe that the Classic Gnome Desktop is not an option as a result of too much quirkiness, I don't see what other choice I have? It's a shame that Unity could not be more complete, more functional for hands-on users, and more importantly, that it had to be forced onto everyone before it was 100% ready. Clearly, Unity still has quite some growing to do. :(

Oh, and BTW, four different computers, three of them different manufacturers, and all of them with Wifi problems after using Ubuntu 11.04 ... problems that either never existed, or were very simple/quick to correct, with Ubuntu 10.10 ... Tons of forum posts on the Internet about that too. In my opinion, Ubuntu 11.04 was not an improvement, certainly not for me and our computers. :(

BUT ... The fact that Linux rocks as no other OS can, keeps me happy & optimistic.
Take care and enjoy your Weekend.

:guitar:

### Post by ottosykora on 2011-04-30
I am also too much shocked by the unity.

The designers have been using too much of 'some kind of fruit company' (Forrest Gump) before, so now they think everybody in the world needs big colorful icons, computer has to do and think instead of the user. 
Yes it looks and work more like a smart phone then anything else. 

Funny is the explanation on the website of unity.
It says, well if you give it to a newcomer to computers , he will learn it fast.
If you have some computer experience, you should also try to learn it, it will make your life better.

Well this is similar as when we in central europe will try to tell the british: drive on the right side of the road, this is more easy, more natural, more simple.
The british or other commonwealth will tell us, no drive on the left side of the road, you will definitely feel more safe and better.

Both are right *from their point of view* but say nonsense if observed from the other side.

If someone has 20 year of driving experience on the right side of the road, it is an advantage for him , his reflexes are fully connected to this side driving and forcing him to change, will definitely not making him feeling more safe or happy. 

So comparing young child sitting at a computer first time and an experienced user who's brain is making certain associations based on previous experience during his work at a computer is not very sensible. It is an idea of typical sales promoters or similar.

Actually more worse then the design I found the compulsory use of the logging subsystem zeitgeist which will be part of almost all new linux GUI. That all the colorful fun can not live without this information collection engine is very sad, as we  are then here not much different then using products of 'big competitors'.

Have just noticed, that my old debian is still being updated, still all works, so will probably use this in future if the unity etc becomes compulsory here.

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-04-30
That's true, these days a "Power User" is less someone with a terminal and more someone with "extreme multitasking" abilities via GUI. Word processing, spreadsheets, graphic art, photo editing, video editing, website design, presentations ... I do all of that and more professionally ... just not with the terminal.  ;-)

Some of the people who commented here apparently didn't notice that I did try out the Gnome Classic Desktop ... which caused problems, in particular with some of my previous 10.10 backup applications. Eventually I managed to totally wipe out my 11.04 installation, while using the Classic Desktop mode, after retrieving my Ubuntu-Tweak backups.  :confused:

There were also ATI driver issues, the Wifi issues, and other minor quirks which ended up in a really bad overall experience over a period that spanned a few weeks. _I have no intention of ever switching back to Windows_ but I did have to install Win7 for my wife on a work computer from her employer ... and was astounded by the ease of the installation. Since Mark Shuttleworth (also) strives for Ubuntu to be "Human i.e. People friendly" I'd think that ease of installation would be one of the absolute top priorities there is? IMO both Mint and Mepis are more people friendly as far as the installation process is concerned.

My comment about leaving Ubuntu in lieu of another Linux distro came from the fact that I'd read somewhere that eventually Gnome would be abandoned altogether, once Unity has truly defined itself and made it's mark on Ubuntu. Without Gnome, and with no desire to use KDE, I'd then be forced to move to another distro such as Fedora 15. I like Suse too, and a few other distros. Need a little bling and 3D too though ....

IMO the 6 month release date _is at this point_ turning into a mistake. Ubuntu has gone very very far in the past 3 years alone. Now it's time to RC test new releases for at least 3 full months before throwing things out that clearly need more work ... while at the same time proclaiming "Ubuntuness for all the People" out there. A lot of those people don't have the patience to deal with any problems, most especially not problems that require any kind of hands on fiddling, no matter how small. PEACE.

---

### Post by bowens44 on 2011-04-30
I wanted to like Unity but after playing with the release for several hours it became apparent that I will never like it.

That ugly launch bar with giant icons welded to left side , that awful menu that makes finding applications a nightmare and especially the global menus nonsense make unity, for me,  completely unusable. 

If I have my browser in the lower right corner of the screen, where I usually keep it, why would I want to have to click on the browser and then move the mouse all the way to the upper left hand corner of the screen to get to my bookmarks. Of course I then have to move back to the lower  right to scroll. It is the most ridiculous thing I have ever seen. Maybe if the global menu was only used when an app is full screen it would have some use. As it is, it's just silly.

I honestly can not see a single thing in Unity that can be considered an 'upgrade'.

They seem to have taken what was a nearly perfect distro (10.10) and destroyed it. Unity seems to be geared toward grandmothers, young children and the visually impaired. It is really not a desktop environment  suitable for anyone who is more then a causal computer user. 

It looks and feels like a toy, not like a computer.

Yes I know I can boot into gnome if I choose. Luckily, I installed this  on a spare computer.....

---

### Post by Ron Jones on 2011-04-30
Upon upgrade from 10.10 to 11.04, I could not get Unity to work. So, I've been stuck with Ubuntu "Classic." While classic is attractive and functional, I would like to try Unity for a few weeks, just to make sure I give it a fair shake.

If I can ever get it up and running I'll be too invested at that point to just abandon it without a thorough test. :lol: 

> **buster2209 said:**
> Unity is trying to be like Macs but if I wanted a Mac, I would have bought one.

*While Macs are a work of art*, at $2,000+, I couldn't justify it even if I just HAD to have one (my wife just wouldn't understand spending that much on a metal/plastic box with a tv screen attached to it). Thankfully, their corporate philosophy (secretive & tyrannical) makes it easy to avoid.

> **themainliner said:**
> I'm also a little bored by the fanboys' who feel any criticism of their new tech can be brushed off with stating the opinion that Unity is 'modern' and only two days old.

I had to chuckle at that one. The Fanboi is present in every tech crowd. Usually parked downstairs in his mommy's basement, pontificating on things about which he has no real understanding ("Linux Rox, windoze sucks!"... "Android is better than iPhone"... and on and on it goes).

If it were mission-critical, I'd probably care more. And stick with tested and proven technology like Gnome 2, until Gnome 3 and Unity were mature and stable. But if that were the case, I'd be a business user.

In the final analysis... tinkering with Linux is cheaper, and more constructive than drinking.

### Post by NadirPoint on 2011-04-30
Some people don't have very wide comfort zones and immediately panic when confronted by something they don't understand.  We get comfortable exercising authoritarian control over the computer and fool ourselves into believing we dominate every last CPU cycle.  Then all of sudden there's a new UI sitting on the screen and it's as if in the blink of an eye we know nothing and have no control.

Pretty earth-shattering for some people, I suppose.  But if they took the time to learn a few new tricks they would soon be pleasantly surprised at how much BETTER it actually is.

Nothing ventured, nothing gained, as the old saying goes.

### Post by beew on 2011-04-30
> **NadirPoint said:**
> Some people don't have very wide comfort zones and immediately panic when confronted by something they don't understand.  We get comfortable exercising authoritarian control over the computer and fool ourselves into believing we dominate every last CPU cycle.  Then all of sudden there's a new UI sitting on the screen and it's as if in the blink of an eye we know nothing and have no control.

Pretty earth-shattering for some people, I suppose.  But if they took the time to learn a few new tricks they would soon be pleasantly surprised at how much BETTER it actually is.

Nothing ventured, nothing gained, as the old saying goes.

Well that is a strawman argument, while "some people" like you described exist, most people give you very specific reasons why they don't like Unity after using it for some times.

I see a pattern here, this is how the Unity enthusiasts try to dismiss critics: either they are fear of change, or fear of the unfamiliar, while never addressing the actual arguments. It is easy to write off your critics by labeling them as irrational, that way you don't have to actually tell us why the new design is supposed to be better.

I can live with Unity, though not crazy about it at this stage, I think it has potential if they do it right, but what I can't stand is the propaganda and condescending BSes.

As someone has said on the other thread, this is a tremendous insult to many Ubuntu users, who have given up the comfort zone of Windows and have been sticking to Ubuntu for all the changes since the early days when most hardware would need a lot of tweaking to work if at all.

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-04-30
> **Roasted said:**
> Gnome Shell still has a ways to go in the usability department.

I think the usability of Gnome Shell is OK, it's the suitability of purpose that's a question.

There are significant trade-offs in efficiency that I find it hard to compensate for, in spite of the fact that 'Alt'+'Tab'+mouse is nicely implemented.

In Unity the trade-offs seem much more logical to me, I can still navigate between apps with multiple windows on multiple desktops quickly and navigate through the categories of installed applications quickly using only the mouse and there are lots of shortcuts for keyboard commandos.

I'm not a keyboard commando, so I have not really investigated what Gnome Shell provides in that area.

Later, Seeker

### Post by manzdagratiano on 2011-04-30
Last I checked, Windows takes about 40 minutes to install (I have never known how Vista/Win 7 installation proceeds). Ubuntu takes FIVE minutes to install, with the greatest of ease, on my Sony Vaio. The new installer is way simpler than anything else I have seen so far, so I don't really see cause for complain here.

Regarding problems with WiFi, I have a broadcom card on my Dell Vostro that has caused me to learn a great deal of gymnastics to get it to work perfectly on multiple distros. If there are problems with such things, that is what the community is there for - it has certainly been to my benefit in the past.

Ubuntu is not about the shiny new UI or Gnome - it is an excellently put-together distribution, that offers innumerable choice to shape it the way anyone wants. I often use Ubuntu with Ratpoison - works like a charm, even though it is not an official *Buntu variant. Canonical will offer what they by and large feel the average user wants, but nothing stops anybody from customizing it to the way they want. Even Fedora shall offer Gnome Shell now - I am sure they have a ton of users complaining about how it is so horrendous and how Gnome2 was better.

---

### Post by beew on 2011-04-30
> **NadirPoint said:**
> And these reasons you speak of seem to revolve around the theme of not being gnome"ish" enough for various reasons, which I dismiss by definition.  Expecting familiarity will certainly result in disapointment..

..I saw Unity for the first time less than 24 hours ago, and almost  immediately became completely enamored of it in the 2 short hours I had  time to look around a do a few things.  Surely I have not yet scratched  the surface.



That is another strawman.

Posters on many threads since alpha testing have been saying how many more clicks to take them to accomplish the same tasks, for example, and it is a fact that you can no longer put applets such as temperature monitor on the top panel (or anywhere) unless you go for third party tweak. It is a loss of functionality. There are other points which I won't go into, you can look up the Natty development forum. There is a point often made by Unity enthusiasts who have tried it for more than 24 hours, which is that Unity is very keyboard friendly, in other words, it would work well with hotkeys. The implied flip side is that it doesn't work so well if you are point and click user instead of a keyboard jockey who remembers all the obscure key combinations.

I would put those as more thoughtful opinions than your less than 24 hour infatuation.

BTW, your argument against customization is beyond absurd. If Unity works for you without any tweaking, good for you, it doesn't prove that it also works with others, especially by your own admission that your experience with Unity is somewhere around 24 hours, and your preference is not the basis to deny customizable options for others.

*While Macs are a work of art*, at $2,000+, I couldn't justify it even if I just HAD to have one (my wife just wouldn't understand spending that much on a metal/plastic box with a tv screen attached to it). Thankfully, their corporate philosophy (secretive & tyrannical) makes it easy to avoid.



I had to chuckle at that one. The Fanboi is present in every tech crowd. Usually parked downstairs in his mommy's basement, pontificating on things about which he has no real understanding ("Linux Rox, windoze sucks!"... "Android is better than iPhone"... and on and on it goes).

If it were mission-critical, I'd probably care more. And stick with tested and proven technology like Gnome 2, until Gnome 3 and Unity were mature and stable. But if that were the case, I'd be a business user.

**In the final analysis... tinkering with Linux is cheaper, and more constructive than drinking.**

I absolutely love this!!

### Post by Throne777 on 2011-04-30
> **braden7 said:**
> "Acquired taste" is a euphemism for "unpleasant."


Err, no it isn't? It means that it's not something you'll pick up first time.
Take rum. I'm a big fan of rum. But when I first started drinking it I didn't really care for the flavour or what type of rum, just so long as it went with coke and got me drunk. Now I appreciate the different dark rums, can appreciate the subtle differences between different brands and so on.
Mathcore is another good example. It's a type of music that does, at times (most times...), sound like dissonance, but that's half the point (on a first listen you can pretty much guarantee you'll just hear a blur of loud sounds that don't seem to be in any form of rhythm whatsoever). It's actually pretty technical metal & I find great reward in really getting to know the music after repeated listens(take Dillinger Escape Plan's EP 'Irony Is A Dead Scene', for example). It's almost definitely an acquired taste, but that's not a bad thing.

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-04-30
> **manzdagratiano said:**
> Last I checked, Windows takes about 40 minutes to install (I have never known how Vista/Win 7 installation proceeds). Ubuntu takes FIVE minutes to install, with the greatest of ease, on my Sony Vaio. The new installer is way simpler than anything else I have seen so far, so I don't really see cause for complain here.

Ubuntu is not about the shiny new UI or Gnome - it is an excellently put-together distribution, that offers innumerable choice to shape it the way anyone wants.

I've found the installation times to vary greatly from one computer to the next, often not even having anything to do with the hardware, seemingly anyway. Win7 installed so quickly on that computer from my wife's work that I was downright shocked at the speed and that happens to be the truth. On the other hand, I installed Mepis on a system with quad core processors and 6 GB of Ram, the installation completed very rapidly and very similar to Windows7. But when I tried to install Mepis on a dual core 64bit machine with 4 GB of Ram ... the process took so long that I left to drink a cup of coffee, no kidding. I've installed Ubuntu on dozens of machines during the past couple of years and experienced exactly the same thing. On one machine with tons of RAM and disk space it installs quickly, and the next installation seems to take considerably longer. I'm pretty sure that graphics drivers and/or Wifi chips are at the heart of those slower installations. On my Dad's 1.7 Ghz. intel with 2 GB of RAM and 256 MB integrated intel graphics chip, the installation & performance of Ubuntu was so unbelievably dismal that I switched his system over to Xubuntu after a couple of weeks.

I'm not sure that I agree with that "offers innumerable choice to shape it" because when I first got seriously involved with Linux about 3 years ago, it was the "credo of Ubuntu" the OS for Humans, the "human factor" if you will, that impressed me so much that I decided that that would be the Linux distro for me to really get involved with, in my endeavor to get away from Windows. I can't imagine that I'm the only one who was motivated by that. To me, first and foremost, Ubuntu will always be for humans, for togetherness, for unison ... to fully complete something for everyone to use ... which to me means that Ubuntu, unlike most other Linux distros, is not meant to be changed, shaped, altered, by just anyone since it is designed to be for anyone i.e. everyone.
Well, whatever, I guess just like the "poweruser thing" everything is open to individual interpretation.

### Post by beew on 2011-04-30
> **Ken UK said:**

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-04-30
> **ankspo71 said:**
> Hi WinRiddance,
Have you thought about staying with Ubuntu 10.10 at least until 11.10 rolls around? It will give you some time to see what other distributions are planning to do with Gnome. Hi. Yeah, of course I'll be sticking with 10.10 for awhile and checking other things out too. I haven't had a chance to test drive Gnome 3 yet (am downloading the latest Suse now), but after reading over dozens of blogs and forum comments, stacking Unity against Gnome 3, and after my own personal experience with Unity, I've come to the conclusion - wrong as it may ultimately prove to be - that Unity is more for the new cellphone and iPad like user, whereas Gnome 3 seems to be more geared towards professional computing without the newest computer users in mind (uh, oh, hope I don't get crucified for that remark).
Obviously only time will tell, but I think that I'll be liking Gnome Shell better than Unity.

Thing is, I've built, repaired, upgraded, and sold literally hundreds of windows machines in the course of apx. 10 years, even selling them nationwide online, so switching to a new OS version when it's released is almost compulsory for me. I know, it's crazy, but it's almost like I can't help myself. Oooooh, new version, gotta check it out, eh? I never felt that way with windows ... probably because it took so darn long to have new versions and SP1, SP2, SP3, SP4 upgrades sure as heck weren't anything to get excited over. Hah, maybe that's what I need, something like Debian or another distro that only has a release every couple of years or so ... :D

Have a great weekend everyone. That's it for me on this post. ;)

### Post by manzdagratiano on 2011-04-30
I commend you for your deep sentiment - I now understand what Ubuntu means to you. I must admit that that was not the viewpoint I took when I adopted Ubuntu - to me it was a door to freedom, that led me to shed all throes of serfdom and welcome a world where I was all-powerful, and could do anything I wanted... and such I have largely done.

My goal recently has been to merge my experience with five major distributions into one - Ubuntu, Arch, Debian Sid, Slackware and Gentoo - and what I learn on one I have tried enabling on another, and have had promising results so far. My point of view now is that any distribution is just a set of core system files and its associated package management scheme - and other than that there are no boundaries. This way, I treat them now on an equal footing with each other, and that is what gives me pleasure. Five different viewpoints, and yet the same goal achieved in the end - that is a thing of beauty.

---

### Post by euthyfro on 2011-04-30
I've been using Ubuntu since Dapper Drake, though i still feel like a noob in most computing areas. I've upgraded to Natty & must add yet another HATE IT! to the pile for Unity.  Call me outdated but I own a computer (not a laptop or smartphone or netbook or tablet or what have you), and have all the complains that have already been aired here.  
  The system/cpu/network usage monitor and calendar as well as my handy weather applet not being there was the 1st sign of trouble, also galling was that i keep my movies & mp3s on a separate partition (i don't have to explain myself!) w/links to their folders as desktop icons.  All i had/have to do before/in classic to access them was go to Places & find my 150 gb file system listed, click it & it's desktop icon sits on screen next to the links to the respective movies & mp3s folders.  Contains of partition easily usable until next restart, when i'd just click thru places again.  
  Unity required me to hunt through nautilus for it only to get a "permission denied" when i tried viewing the contains of the partition folder. Even after getting root privileges to open the partitions folder there were no items in it anymore w/vuze telling me "data missing" on the stuff i had been uploading this morning.
  As for all these awesome keyboard shortcuts i'm reading about:  sorry, i lost the use of my right hand after a stroke so holding more than 2 keys down at the same time or using the key board & mouse at the same time is out of the question and doesn't make this monstrosity any more user friendly.
  So, i know there are guides available to address these technical concerns if i so choose to invest more time in learning a completely new system, but 1 of my primary reasons for making the switch those many years ago was i liked the utilitarian look and feel of Ubuntu and Unity comes across to me as a low-rent rip-off of the overly stylised OSX and Windows 7 whom i've come to view as mortal enemies.

---

### Post by manzdagratiano on 2011-04-30
All in all, I must say that from the screenshots and the philosophy layouts I have seen for Gnome 3, it seems to me that Gnome3 and Unity are not all that different, which perplexes me even more. Today I logged back into Unity after two weeks, and in the meantime I have been reading about the philosophy of Gnome3 - how menus and clustering are ancient paradigms and need to be done away with, and everything should be search-able instantly, etc. Isn't that what Unity pretty much strives to do anyway? Maybe it's just me, but I'll stick with Unity on Ubuntu, and when Gnome Shell lands up in Debian Sid or Arch, I will stick with that there too. I loved Gnome2 these past three years after breaking away from KDE - which I still think is bloated - but I also believe that when times evolve, one must evolve with them. I think people dislike Unity because it is a new principle - I am sure there are many who dislike Gnome Shell compared to Gnome2. But once people get used to these new ideas, they just might never wish to go back.

---

### Post by acplusplusprogrammer on 2011-04-30
Hello folks,
    I haven't any real problem but I'd like to start a serious discussion with all the Ubuntu Forum about Ubuntu 11.04.
    I've started to use Ubuntu since the 8.04 release and I've been updating it since then. I simply LOVED it. Why? Only one reason:
- It is FAST, SIMPLE and ESSENTIAL

Now, I've tried the new Unity interface and the Gnome 3, and I simply don't like it. Why?
1) 4 clicks to open Gimp or any other software and a LOT OF RESEARCH. Before I just used the Gnome Menu and 1 second I opened the software I wanted to open
2) 2 clicks to open the Document Folder or any subfolder (before I just needed to score the menu)
3) I use Ubuntu in my company, we use it for everything (Openoffice, GIMP, APtana, InkScape as well as Skype , GFTP etc...etc...etc.....). Now, if the interface changes so much it will be really difficult to let my co-workers accept such a big change.....again.....
4) A desktop, where you work with FILES....well, you
- needn't BIG icons
- needn't a iPHONE style application menu (it's a desktop with a keyboard and a MOUSE and with a different logic).
5) When you open many applications it gets disorganized on the left bar.....

So....I'm not closed minded, I've been trying it for a whole day.
I just know that it takes LONGER to make things and it's more disorganized.

My conclusion is that:
IF
I won't be able to use a Gnome2 style user interface in the long run (I've read
THEN
I need to switch to another distribution where I can


Simple and clear....I really hope Ubuntu team will understand what normal user think.

Give your opinion, this is a wonderful operating system but this "new direction" is worrying in the long run

### Post by Ken UK on 2011-04-30
> **beew said:**
> Who the hell are you to tell what people should or should not put on the panel? Other than weather applet cpu usage and temperature monitors happen to be useful for checking hardware performance. Now these you can put on the panel before, now the panel is still there but you can't put these indicator anymore so it is a loss of functionality.

Please tell us how losing a functionality = improvement.



That is not a real menu, if you click a category you don't get a submenu with a icon which you can launch with one click, but a dash that takes up the whole screen or at least 3/4 of it, thus very disrupting of what you are doing, and you then have to either type in a search box (again an interuption of work flow if you are multitasking) or worse, click "show all" and scroll through a bunch of icons to find your application. 

Well if they don't intend you to use the scroll bar then why the dash in the first place? If you use only the search function you don't need the dash that takes up the whole damn screen, so the design itself is inconsistent.

Listen Im not trying to say you are wrong but I want specifics so I can consider them myself. Sure I like how minimalistic gnome is with its simple menu etc. maybe you lost a feature for now, maybe they will bring it back in some other form later? but overall it has much more functional, its new and needs time to really get anywhere near its potential. Sure I would like a weather icon and cpu usage etc. I use to have that on docky but to be honest it wasn’t the end of the world when I realised that was gone along with other things for now. Sure I would love it back but at the end of the day Im not sure what you use your computer for but none of the work I do requires it.

Unity is just a next generation dock with alot of potential. The problem I have with docks in their standard form like OSX is that they take up too much valuable space on the bottom. Even the new Windows 7 super bar doesn’t seem generations ahead of it (although I haven’t used it yet).

I admit the space the full out panel takes up could be distracting for some, however I didn’t think about it as it doesn’t effect me as much as others because I run at 1900 x 1200 resolution so it only takes up maybe a third of my screen and I have 2 at this size so it seems small to me.

Maybe I would need to see someone using Unity who uses the mouse purely on a smaller screen to see the problem because I still don’t see it. I dont know most of the names of the apps on my computer or what I have so even if I can't type some vague word to try and locate it I just pick a category and have a look. Do you really open and close apps so regular that having the panel come up really puts you off what you are doing?

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-04-30
> **acplusplusprogrammer said:**
> [LIST]
[*]I've started to use Ubuntu since the 8.04 release


[*]I simply LOVED it. Why? Only one reason: It is FAST, SIMPLE and ESSENTIAL


[*]I'm not closed minded, I've been trying it **[COLOR="Red"]for a whole day[/COLOR]**.


[*]Give your opinion, this is a wonderful operating system but this "new direction" is worrying in the long run
[/LIST]
I've used Natty / Unity for a few months now...

At first, Unity was rough as a corn cob.  LoL!  When Canonical says Alpha, they mean Alpha!  :D

Truthfully, for a while, I didn't think they would pull it off -- but the devs listened to the community, and things started falling in place, between Alpha 3 and Beta 1, e.g. the middle of March.

After you LEARN Unity -- once you wrap your mind around it -- Unity is as "FAST, SIMPLE and ESSENTIAL" as a machine gun.  Believe me, on this!

You just need to give YOURSELF some time.  You aren't going to become a Unity Power User in a day, a week, or a month.  It's a fresh start, a new idea, and will prove to be a steep learning curve (for advanced users).

As an aside, OO is going to be MUCH better NN.  I can't wait until the toolchain is uploaded!

madjr, I think you are right about the menu. This change is the worst thing about Unity, IMO. The typing of the app can be a nice short cut, but too much of the old menu is missing in the new one and I never thought the old menu was bad to start with.

I like the new launcher bar on the left, as the task bar tended to be information overload. That said, there should be an override to allow the a second copy launch if you want (right click, 'launch another' or some such). Not every app has an 'open new window' option and having to use the ubuntu menu (dash?) to open a second copy isn't user friendly. I do like the little dots for multi-running apps though. The way it works is a good basis though.

The invisible scroll bars are growing on me though and it saves a good few pixels, without losing much functionality (ok, you can't page down as easily, but it's a positive trade off, IMO). However, if you do a search and you have a full timer cursor, the scroller doesn't work at all (a bug, I assume).

Also, those of us who use follow mouse focus will find the top menu to be broken, unless the window is maximised. I like the additional screen space, but without click to focus, both optimisations don't work, but one of them does (combining app bar when maximised is good) The following fixed this, but it should be automatic when on follow mouse (otherwise, it's a bug):

sudo apt-get remove indicator-appmenu

I miss the top bar applets (I forget the formal name), but new indicator apps seem to be appearing to fill this gap.

So, IMO, it needs some more polish. It feels a bit 2 steps forward, 1 step back. Some areas are better, others are worse. With a bit of polish, I think most areas could be better for both amateurs and professionals alike. I get the feeling some of these things should have been resolved before release, but I'm hoping some will be patched soon and the others will be fixed in 11.10.

---

### Post by braden7 on 2011-04-30
> **ZarathustraDK said:**
> I just don't understand this burning need to complain. Natty has been out for 1 day, 1 DAY, and people starts ragequitting because it's different.

Take a step back, you're using a different UI, it means you have to alter your workflow, you have to CHANGE what you are used to doing, get comfortable with it to the degree that you no longer draw upon the experience and expectations of your former UI's paradigm, and THEN you can complain.

You do not get that from 1 day of experiencing the final product anymore than I become a professional racedriver from getting a joyride in a Ferrari when I'm used to driving a KIA.

Unity has its flaws and stuff that could be done better, but I'd withhold my verdict of Unity until the next LTS is a reality. The fact that there are issues is why Natty is not a LTS.

That was my grumpy rant. No hard feelings ;)

Why should the DE dictate the workflow? That's absolutely bass ackwards. The point of the DE is to enable and compliment the workflow.

Not only is Unity a completely unnecessary change for many users, bringing a completely unnecessary transition period, the benefits just aren't there on the other side.

The basic concept of Unity isn't going to change, neither are its core features, and neither are my needs. How would using it for a month be any different than using it for the 15 minutes it takes to figure out what the hell it is, how the hell it got there, and how the hell to get rid of it?

I don't have to eat turd sandwiches for a week to know I don't like turd sandwiches.

### Post by JKyleOKC on 2011-04-30
You might take a look at Debian Squeeze. That's what I'm testing as my possible escape route once Lucid LTS is replaced by something much less capable of being customized.

Since Ubuntu is based on Debian, lots of things look and feel exactly the same. What's missing are some of the little touches that make Ubuntu more friendly to newcomers, and some of the GUI front-ends to system administration tasks such as dealing with user profiles and the like. However I suspect that those capabilities exist, and just are not in the default installation...

My test box is an ancient machine with only 512 MiB of RAM and around 30 GiB of disk space, so performance obviously isn't going to be comparable to my production systems that are on much more modern hardware, but even with such a miniscule amount of resources, it's enough to let me get the hang of working with it -- and I've still got two more years of support for Lucid LTS. By that time I ought to be comfortable with Squeeze and can become an ex-Ubuntuian (along with many others who apparently don't like the direction the distro is taking).

---

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-04-30
[QUOTE=braden7]Why should the DE dictate the workflow? That's absolutely bass ackwards. The point of the DE is to enable and compliment the workflow.[/QUOTE]

Your habits are based on your experience with the system you're used to. Every UI has "a workflow", which may be very constrained or very open or anything else; Unity attempts to offer a relatively narrow but well-thought-out one, as does OSX, while other UIs are a little broader but perhaps a little less fluid in actual use.

The things that you do in Gnome 2 are simply the habits you've formed in that environment according to the limitations of the software that existed there. A change in environment (should you choose to change environments) does, naturally, require new habits.

### Post by SonicSteve on 2011-04-30
Here's my $.02

I sympathize will Billsnice, I'm not sold on Unity yet either. However I'm willing to give the Dev's time to tweak it and add features to it. I don't like the default Icons or theme. What I expect is that over time these will be configurable with new unity theme programs. 

On the other hand Gnome 3 is on the horizon and Distros' won't be able to stick with Gnome 2 forever. So this leaves the question of Unity or Default Gnome 3. 

I plan to stick with 10.10 for a while and watch with great interest to see what feature changes come next. The beauty is that this is Linux and if you don't like it, change it. Even if 11.10 won't support Gnome classic, there will be an Ubuntu derivative that will. Having said that it may not be that hard to install pure Gnome on 11.10, and change the login session.  

Lastly I have installed 11.04 on spare box in my office, I've found ways already to tweak it so that I may be able to accept it.

---

### Post by alguin2 on 2011-04-30
**Unutty** is a nice try, but it misses the point:

[LIST=1]
Ubuntu users want the choice and flexibility; but ramming this not-ready-and-not-well-thought out interface is really bad, and is one of the reasons why many are not willing to take the leap to Linux.   

[*]**Unutty** should be a choice, instead of an unpleasant surprise upon completion of an upgrade. For those experienced with *nix systems, it may be a simple nuisance;  for others, it can be a harrowing experience. 

[*]Linux is **not** MacOS and should not try to emulate a half-baked imitation of Aqua.  I hate to say it, but Microsoft has done its research, and has put together an intuitive interface in Windows7 that's easy to migrate to from the previous XP interface.  


[/LIST]

Is Unity perfet?  Heck no - it's new.  And it's new to us  all new things take a while to mature and be accepted.  Nobody is dictating anything - all they are doing is saying - "hey, we've got this idea here we really do think can go someone, but we need more input from you users out there.  The things you are used to, including the "old" desktops, are still there and you can of course use them.".

I think they have the beginnings of something here.  I've run into some things that are what I would call "broken", but I would assume there will be a list of fixes coming out sometime in the next couple of weeks at the most, so I'm not going to report them until things settle in for a while.

I see Unity as a mix between Gnome and KDE (I don't know - is that where the name comes from?).  In time I think it will be good.

---

Pointer:  DMZ (Black)[/INDENT]

[INDENT]Hide Launcher:  Never
Backlight Mode:  Backlight Always Off
Panel Opacity:  0.4000
Launcher icon size:  32[/INDENT]

**Widgets visible on right-side (combination of):**
[INDENT]Conky
conkyForecast
Lua[/INDENT]

### Post by Johnsie on 2011-05-01
> people are just straight out "No its different, not using it".

I'm not sure this is the case. For many people there are important usability issues:

1. It takes alot more effort to open applications. Unity makes a meal out of the simple task of opening apps.

2. The 'Places' menu is gone....This means you need to go into nautilus to access your nautilus bookmarks.

3. If you had your computer set to auto-login and you update from 10.10 to 11.04 and there are any graphics driver issues then your computer will not start.  


For me personally 1 and 2 will slow my productivity down permanently because I will need to do extra work to do simple tasks. In work I need to be able to do things quickly at the drop of a hat so this is something I need to consider. Number 3 was fixable but left me without a computer for several hours while I was getting help.

### Post by firepages on 2011-05-01
Assuming even that unity is nowhere near as bad as many (many) comments here.... if I worked for a large Redmond based company I would be laughing my socks off just about now.

Try explaining to the management of some SMB's the answers posted here .. e.g `**you will get used to it**` when they complain that none of their staff can understand the new interface.

**Yes **I know we can switch to classic , but you **know** thats a cop-out, most development will be made for unity not for classic (I know its not that simple or that different) unity will be the face of ubuntu, the old face of ubuntu would be recognizable to 90% of computing public, unity .. less so, in fact less unifying methinks :)

### Post by michaelzap on 2011-05-01
> **manzdagratiano said:**
> Whilst people are ranting about Unity here, they might consider looking in the Arch forums: Arch just got Gnome 3 yesterday (practically today), and the reaction to Gnome 3 is pretty much the same as the reaction to Unity here.
Uh. I see a whole lot of positive feedback on Gnome 3 in the Arch repos. Nothing at all like the reaction to Unity here...

I've tried them both, and although there are a number of superficial similarities (dash on the left, Gnome Do-like launcher, etc.) they are totally different user experiences. Gnome 3 feels like a well-integrated, thoughtful experiment in user interface design, whereas Unity feels like a jumbled, confusing mess.

Resource-wise they are also dramatically different: My laptop uses about 220 MB of RAM running several browser tabs in Gnome 3, and there's never a millisecond of delay when I switch workspaces, etc., whereas Unity is yet another slab of bloat on top of Ubuntu's already hefty haunches.

I think that the global menu is one of the otherwise good ideas that just should not have been included in Unity. It goes against the whole idea of removing distractions so that the user can focus on one task at a time, and it's poorly-implemented to boot. Major UI elements should not jump around - they should be where the user expects them to be instinctively.

And people who say that both Unity and Gnome 3 are not for everyone are absolutely correct. If you have multiple monitors, or you need to monitor a number of things at once on a regular basis, or if you have a small screen with a crappy touchpad, then you're probably not gonna be that happy with either of them. I'd add to that the many people here who would prefer to not be part of a user interface experiment and would rather set up their own user interface exactly the way that they like it, since neither of these allows for very much customization at the moment.

I may eventually end up using Gnome 3, since I felt that it worked well for me and actually seemed designed with my workflow (multiple workspaces with different grouped tasks, lots of keyboard shortcuts, and frequent need to tile two applications) in mind. But until there are stable packages in the Debian testing repos and more ways to customize it, I'll continue happily using Xfce. People who are unhappy with the Unity/Gnome 3 experiments should definitely check Xfce out (try a Crunchbang or Linux Mint Xfce Live CD for starters), and people who think that Unity and Gnome 3 are two sides of the same coin should download the Fedora image from the gnome3.org site so they can experience the difference for themselves.

### Post by tthrock on 2011-05-01
OK...  I just put the hard drive with Ubuntu 10 back in my laptop.  I had put a 100GB drive in to test Ubuntu 11.  I went through the "Power Users Guide To Unity" mentioned in this thread, installed all my applications and used it for a few days.  While Unity has some good features, I will not use it.  I do not desire having an "app" based OS.  Making Ubuntu like an ipad definitely appears to be their goal.  They must have a money making scheme behind this tragedy.  It is not ready for release that is for sure.  It is not efficient to work with either.  My wife uses Windows 7, my son uses Windows XP, and they both have an ipod.  After going through the new Ubuntu with them they both said it is like their ipods.  Ubuntu is trying to jump on the ipod /ipad / Android boat.  My wife said it is to cumbersome and my son said it "weird" (he is 10 yrs old).  He also begged me to put his old hard drive back in his laptop (lol he wanted XP back lol - wife wanted Windows 7 back lol - and I wanted Ubuntu 10 back).  So all hard drives were put back in tonight.  On the bright side my 8 yr old daughter likes Unity.  Unfortunately her laptop will not support it (barely runs XP).  She wanted to keep my wifes laptop leaving Ubuntu 11 on it (NOT).  I am a Windows computer/server tech, my work laptop is Windows 7, and I work on 2000, XP, Windows 7 workstation.  I personally have used Ubuntu since version 8 for my home laptops.  After testing Ubuntu 11 I have to say this is the worst operating system interface I have ever worked with.  And I have worked with a lot over the years.
Change is part of my life (I work on computers...) so that isn't it for me...  Unity is just bad...  I had more control over my user "experience" in Windows 3.11; and usage flow (work flow) was smoother... Sad...  
Unity is taking Ubuntu backwards.  That is my "professional" opinion.  But hey, that will be good for business (I work for a Microsoft based company - meaning we sell/support Windows servers and workstation).
30% of my work time is spent getting rid of Malware lol.  Thats why I use Ubuntu on my personal laptop.  Guess I have a few years till I have to find another good OS before Unity is permanent.
I just do NOT want an IPAD operating system on my laptop.   I am sure the makers of Ubuntu have considered their user base loss will be high but they will gain some of the users back when they release the touchpad based on Ubuntu Unity.  And their will be plenty of community based "apps" ready to be released with it.

### Post by manzdagratiano on 2011-05-01
I don't know about positive feedback (ironically, it is one of the topics I am working on at the moment) - most of the threads I read were full of woes about how bad the Gnome guys will learn now that they did not learn from the mistakes of KDE4, etc etc.

I shall indeed be posting a comparison of both Unity and Gnome Shell - I have yet to try Gnome Shell out at all, but I HAVE to say that Unity is certainly NOT a jumbled mess. In fact, I can see that I can switch applications faster, search for them faster, and I haven't missed a single thing so far after having configured the indicator panel to put wicd and Dropbox back in - except for Sticky Notes from gnome panel, which I have yet to find a substitute for (no I will not install tomboy, and that's not because of Mono). The whole philosophy is not without cause.

In fact, I am PRETTY sure that both experiences are, on the average, going to be similar, like I stated above - they are both similar paradigms. I think it shall be unjust to praise one and diss the other.

### Post by michaelzap on 2011-05-01
> **manzdagratiano said:**
> In fact, I am PRETTY sure that both experiences are, on the average, going to be similar, like I stated above - they are both similar paradigms. I think it shall be unjust to praise one and diss the other.

You haven't tried Gnome 3 yet, so you are prejudging apples and oranges. I think that if one is judging the two based on whether they succeed at improving the ability to focus on one task (or group of tasks) while making switching among them intuitive and fast ("getting the desktop out of your way"), then Gnome 3 is the clear winner. Unity seems to have no overriding design principles, which is why it feels like a jumble and confuses users (wait the menus are up here now...).

I'm not claiming that Gnome 3 is perfect or that Unity is hopeless, but I think that this is one of those occasions where the you can clearly see the difference between two similar projects based on whether or not they heeded the old maxim of "form follows function."

> **barneystroud said:**
> I need advise on what distro would best suite me out of Fedora or Suse or should I go Kbuntu or Xbuntu?

If you don't like Gnome 3 either, then neither Fedora or OpenSUSE is a good choice (in their Gnome editions anyway). They're both moving to Gnome 3 now. I'd recommend that you try a good Xfce distro, which for me is either Linux Mint, Crunchbang, or plain old Debian (these are all Debian-based, so the change should be relatively painless).

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-05-01
> **firepages said:**
> Assuming even that unity is nowhere near as bad as many (many) comments here.... if I worked for a large Redmond based company I would be laughing my socks off just about now.

Try explaining to the management of some SMB's the answers posted here .. e.g `**you will get used to it**` when they complain that none of their staff can understand the new interface.[...]
I work for a somewhat large, "Redmond based company".

We're down to about 35,000 employees, these days.  Most of them have a computer sitting on their desk.

The funny thing is...

Back in the last century, we were running Windows 3.1.  Later, they converted to Windows 2000 Pro.  It took several years.  The switch was easy, but...

About 6 years ago, they converted to XP Pro, and ppl are STILL complaining about the 'new' interface.  LoL!

I don't know if ppl realize it, but in 2011, smartphone users (worldwide) outnumber computer users by several orders of magnitude.

Desktop computer users are a dying breed, and so is GNOME 2.

It was a brilliant move for Ubuntu to differentiate itself from the stodgy old DEs of yesteryear.

Unity is a good start, IMO.  

Just saying...

### Post by sumadartson on 2011-05-01
> **acplusplusprogrammer said:**
> Hello folks,
    I haven't any real problem but I'd like to start a serious discussion with all the Ubuntu Forum about Ubuntu 11.04.
    I've started to use Ubuntu since the 8.04 release and I've been updating it since then. I simply LOVED it. Why? Only one reason:
- It is FAST, SIMPLE and ESSENTIAL

Now, I've tried the new Unity interface and the Gnome 3, and I simply don't like it. Why?
1) 4 clicks to open Gimp or any other software and a LOT OF RESEARCH. Before I just used the Gnome Menu and 1 second I opened the software I wanted to open
2) 2 clicks to open the Document Folder or any subfolder (before I just needed to score the menu)
3) I use Ubuntu in my company, we use it for everything (Openoffice, GIMP, APtana, InkScape as well as Skype , GFTP etc...etc...etc.....). Now, if the interface changes so much it will be really difficult to let my co-workers accept such a big change.....again.....
4) A desktop, where you work with FILES....well, you
- needn't BIG icons
- needn't a iPHONE style application menu (it's a desktop with a keyboard and a MOUSE and with a different logic).
5) When you open many applications it gets disorganized on the left bar.....

So....I'm not closed minded, I've been trying it for a whole day.
I just know that it takes LONGER to make things and it's more disorganized.

My conclusion is that:
IF
I won't be able to use a Gnome2 style user interface in the long run (I've read
THEN
I need to switch to another distribution where I can


Simple and clear....I really hope Ubuntu team will understand what normal user think.

Give your opinion, this is a wonderful operating system but this "new direction" is worrying in the long run

Eh, I don't know. I'm a very keyboard centric user and there are a couple of awesome choices in unity that make my life much easier. An indication that for this interface, the designers looked beyond 'Can grandma get it in five seconds?' and will take a more expert user into account as well (the future tense is intentional here, it's not all there yet). This is something Apple consistently gets wrong, and makes OSX a mediocre gui at most.

For instance, look at what happens when you press meta (windows key)  the different apps on the Launcher get a number and you type the number to launch the app. Instant keyboard shortcut! Want to launch an application? Hit meta, type the name and go. Home folder? meta-1. Files in general? meta-f.

I would still prefer some more and easier locatable customization, or at least clear indications of how to set certain things. How do I customize my top bar? How do I change the location of launcher? Right-clicking either of them does nothing, and I haven't figured this out yet. Also, how do I add a folder to launcher?

So, not satisfied yet, but I have hope. As long as the interface doesn't only focus on the 'does grandma get it' philosophy of UI design and makes customization clear, I have great hopes for it.

EDIT: just found out the integration of help and apt. Great!

### Post by themainliner on 2011-05-01
Three days; two camps.

In the **Unity Haters** camp.
I hate this, I can't do anything! Everything is unintuitive, this interface is designed for touch screens. Why is it following a more MAC-like model? Can some please fork Gnome 2.x?

In the **Unity Apologists** camp.
"Oh look, 15 pages of crying." Unity is modern and kewl! I love MACs anyway. You're all just afraid of change!

I admit that yesterday I felt angry and frustrated, Ubuntu is a fantastic project that has changed the playing field on which discussions about usability and suitability for new Linux user is held. Ubuntu is stable and workable out of the box and turned me around from being a Gnome sceptic and dragged me over from SUSE.

So after a little ranting, I quickly bore with the two entrenched positions forming around Unity. We need to mingle. I love change and I love new tech. I have no definable comfort zone, if I feel comfortable I install another distro and struggle to bend it to my will.

I broke myself trying to bend Unity and customise it to work in a manner I found valuable. I do understand that (even) Unity has potential to become my new shell of choice. Those who are pro Unity need to understand that being dismissive and condescending to those of us who are struggling and initially unhappy with Unity is not going to make us like it any more. 

Some patience, hand holding and the acceptance that Unity may not be perfect for everyone **yet** will take you further. I'm not a Unity Hater, I am a Unity Sceptic but I installed 11:04 and I am ready for a challenge is anyone ready to show some leadership and help me take my first steps into adoption?

### Post by BigSilly on 2011-05-01
After a very successful few days with Gnome 3 and Opensuse, I decided to try out Ubuntu Natty with Unity. My first impressions weren't great. Compared to Gnome 3 it's very complicated. There seems to be multiple button clicks to get to the simple menus I could get at very quickly with Gnome 3. It really is a DE designed for netbooks, and I'm not sure I want that for my desktop. Gnome 3 seems to have an elegant solution that could fit any device, not just netbooks or tablets.

But these are my initial impressions and I'm going to stay with it a while. It's not broken or nasty, and it is very new and unique, and hence very exciting. This is a good thing as Ubuntu is trying to find new users and broaden it's appeal. Underneath Natty itself seems very nice and stable, and I'll give it some time and see how I go.
