### Post by k4ff3 on 2011-05-01
I want to share a rather touching story with you guys. As a disclaimer I want to say that I really appreciate the hard work and dedication of the Ubuntu Developers. I sincerely appreciate your efforts, and therefore I find it a bit hard to criticise your work. But I will do it anyway.

[For those of you that are in an efficient mood, you will get the sense of the story by reading the text in bold.]

It was a sunny day and the birds where singing outside my work-desk. I could not enjoy this, however, since I had a really important deadline to make. I needed to finish a web development project, and I needed to do it fast.

While booting up my lappy I got great news: Ubuntu 11.04 is released! Yay! *Update*.

Booting in again. However eager to work, I decided that I wanted to explore my new neat distro. And what an adventurous experience that was. I will present here some of my findings.

My docky dock has been replaced by a new one. That's fine, it's cool. Until I found out that **I can not move the dock**. I like to have the taskbar at the bottom. However **I can't reposition the taskbar either**. In fact, **I can't even right click on the taskbar**. And **right clicking on the dock gives only two useless alternatives**. Are you kidding me? While trying to figure out a way to move the dock, I found that **I need to search for 'System Settings' in order to view the Control Center** . What a nuisance. **Where did the nifty menu with Settings, Administration and Appearance go**? It didn't take long until I realized that **the size of the Control Center window was larger than my screen size**. Further more, **it was impossible to move the window around**. Not only that, it was **impossible to resize the window**. Hence, the bottom icons in the Control Center where out of reach for me. In fact, **all windows opens behind the taskbar by default**, so that the top buttons of every window is hidden.

By this time, my girlfriend could sense my agony, so she (unknowingly) poured some gasoline on the fire and suggested that I could use her XP-loaded laptop instead. Hell'no!

Through intense concentration and with an IQ a couple of standard deviations above the rest of the population, I found out that I could resize by pressing alt + 3rd mouse wheel, and move the window by dragging and alt+clicking. Sweet. I felt a bit like Marco Polo when he was able to communicate with the tribes of Asia for the first time.

In the meantime I had opened up an instance of Chrome, which itself was not an easy task since **the icon was hidden amoung multiple libre office icons**. I was amazed of the fact that **the dock has no kind of hierarchy**, so that you can't group things for example. **The dock is a mess**. However, next I realize that **Alt+Tab doesn't work**. The keyboard shortcut I use the most is malfunctioning! Really?! 

Let's use the 'Ubuntu Classic Mode' instead. No, Impossible: **The login screen is bypassed** and I can't find the settings to edit to make it appear at start-up.

My girlfriend then asked me again if I was sure that I did not want to borrow her archaic computer. No I said! I put on some comforting music instead, to ease my mind and relax a bit. But no. I ended up almost getting an **epileptic seizure from that dang visualization effect which can't be turned off**.

Breathe. Relax. OK. Let's work the old fashioned way instead: up with the terminal! It's Vim-time! A sense of nostalgia spread out to my limbs.

DANG! What is it now? **I can only have one instance of the terminal open at the same time**?! No matter how many times I clicked at the icon in the dock, one terminal was the maximum. After a period of problem solving and intellectual affairs, I finally managed to find a fix: create a keyboard shortcut to open the terminal.

Ok, Ok. It's all good now. Let's work! I was mildly unbalanced in my mind at this stage, due to the fact that I had wasted such a substantial amount of precious time; a whole day in fact. I connected a dual monitor like I always do with my precious lappy.

BAM! *BLACK*

What happened?? **The screens went black after connecting the dual monitor**! No response.. Unplug the VGA cable: No response.

BAM! *BLACK*

What happened?? It was my temper this time. I got so angry that I slammed my fist into my desk at full force. It was a dramatic scenery: I actually missed the desk and hit my wireless mouse instead.

Despite me having the muscles of a true academic, I must have hit hard: The scroll-wheel ricocheted off from the mouse by the impact and bounced into a glass of water standing next to my dear lappy. 

Nooo! The world started to move in slow motion: The glass began to fall over. The latency in my neurons wasn't low enough! I didn't manage.. to save.. my... lappy.. *Sigh*. Water was all over the place.

Am I dreaming? Is this a nightmare? I hope so. It must be! It isn't.

After all the fuzz, my girlfriend approached me again, somewhat terrified and cautious. She left her computer at the chair next to me. She didn't say a word. I didn't say a word. I started to work.

Retreat! **Saved in Action, by Windows XP SP1 and IE6**.

---

### Post by Ken UK on 2011-05-01
> **k4ff3 said:**
> I want to share a rather touching story with you guys. As a disclaimer I want to say that I really appreciate the hard work and dedication of the Ubuntu Developers. I sincerely appreciate your efforts, and therefore I find it a bit hard to criticise your work. But I will do it anyway.
 
[For those of you that are in an efficient mood, you will get the sense of the story by reading the text in bold.]
 
It was a sunny day and the birds where singing outside my work-desk. I could not enjoy this, however, since I had a really important deadline to make. I needed to finish a web development project, and I needed to do it fast.
 
While booting up my lappy I got great news: Ubuntu 11.04 is released! Yay! *Update*.

Booting in again. However eager to work, I decided that I wanted to explore my new neat distro. And what an adventurous experience that was. I will present here some of my findings.
 
My docky dock has been replaced by a new one. That's fine, it's cool. Until I found out that **I can not move the dock**. I like to have the taskbar at the bottom. However **I can't reposition the taskbar either**. In fact, **I can't even right click on the taskbar**. And **right clicking on the dock gives only two useless alternatives**. Are you kidding me? While trying to figure out a way to move the dock, I found that **I need to search for 'System Settings' in order to view the Control Center **. What a nuisance. **Where did the nifty menu with Settings, Administration and Appearance go?** It didn't take long until I realized that **the size of the Control Center window was larger than my screen size**. Further more, it was** impossible to move the window around**. Not only that, it was **impossible to resize the window**. Hence, the bottom icons in the Control Center where out of reach for me.
 
By this time, my girlfriend could sense my agony, so she (unknowingly) poured some gasoline on the fire and suggested that I could use her XP-loaded laptop instead. Hell'no!
 
Through intense concentration and with an IQ a couple of standard deviations above the rest of the population, I found out that I could resize by pressing alt + 3rd mouse wheel, and move the window by dragging and alt+clicking. Sweet. I felt a bit like Marco Polo when he was able to communicate with the tribes of Asia for the first time.

In the meantime I had opened up an instance of Chrome, which itself was not an easy task since the icon was hidden amoung **multiple libre office icons**. I was amazed of the fact that **the dock has no kind of hierarchy**, so that you can't group things for example. **The dock is a mess**. However, next I realize that** Alt+Tab doesn't work**. The keyboard shortcut I use the most is malfunctioning! Really?!
 
My girlfriend then asked me again if I was sure that I did not want to borrow her archaic computer. No I said! I put on some comforting music instead, to ease my mind and relax a bit. But no. I ended up almost getting an epileptic seizure from **that dang visualization effect which can't be turned off**!
 
Breathe. Relax. OK. Let's work the old fashioned way instead: up with the terminal! It's Vim-time! A sense of nostalgia spread out to my limbs.
 
DANG! What is it now? **I can only have one instance of the terminal open at the same time**?! No matter how many times I clicked at the icon in the dock, one terminal was the maximum. After a period of problem solving and intellectual affairs, I finally managed to find a fix: create a keyboard shortcut to open the terminal.
 
Ok, Ok. It's all good now. Let's work! I was mildly unbalanced in my mind at this stage, due to the fact that I had wasted such a substantial amount of precious time; a whole day in fact. I connected a dual monitor like I always do with my precious lappy.
 
BAM! *BLACK*
 
What happened?? **The screens went black after connecting the dual monitor**! No response.. Unplug the VGA cable: No response.
 
BAM! *BLACK*
 
What happened?? It was my temper this time. I got so angry that I slammed my fist into my desk at full force. It was a dramatic scenery: I actually missed the desk and hit my wireless mouse instead.
 
Despite me having the muscles of a true academic, I must have hit hard: The scroll-wheel ricocheted off from the mouse by the impact and bounced into a glass of water standing next to my dear lappy. 
  
Nooo! The world started to move in slow motion: The glass started to fall over. The latency in my neurons wasn't low enough! **I didn't manage.. to save.. my... lappy..** *Sigh*. Water was all over the place.
  
Am I dreaming? Is this a nightmare? I hope so. It must be! It isn't.
 
After all the fuzz, my girlfriend approached me again, somewhat terrified and cautious. She left her computer at the chair next to me. She didn't say a word. I didn't say a word. I started to work.
 
Retreat! Saved in Action, by Windows XP SP1 and IE6.

Just to quickly help you out with some bits along with anyone else reading:
[LIST]
[*]**All system settings** from appearance to network connections can be accessed by clicking the on/off button at the top right and selecting the last option 'System settings' but remember if you want something just start to type! I want sort out my network connections "network" etc.

[*]For **moving a window around** without using the window decoration at the top hold down Alt and drag it with the mouse button.

[*]**Alt + tab works** for me so its just your installation with a problem.

[*] I've had **no problems with an external monitor** in my laptop so sorry to hear you have had problems.

[*] For multiple terminal instances either **right click on a terminal window** you have already and select 'open terminal' or just select it from the lens again by typing in terminal or whatever.

[*]The idea with the dock is you place in it what you want to stay there and if you have multiple instances of the same application such as multiple FF windows you **double click on the icon in the launcher** and it arranges them so you can choose which one you want to focus on.

[*]Also remember that the idea in the launcher being vertical is that all displays are wider than taller and often your **vertical space is more valuable than horizontal**. Sure it would be nice to change it but that would be defeating the point and I can hardly see that one thing being significant enough to not use Unity/11.04.
[/LIST]

I hope that helps you have a better experience, it just takes abit of time and calmness to figure some of these things out.

---

I have had my own frustrations with previous changes like the lack of a proper location bar in nautilus (Ctl-L) and many other minor gripes but I have never felt that I had reason to vent in a forum over Ubuntu's evolution .... until now.

I have no problem with new ideas but the unity interface is not a simple change, it is a major change in the look and feel which probably annoys regular users more than casual browsers but they will just think its android anyway :( (I like android BTW~)

Many here say `OK use another distro`, thats not the point .... Debian 6 is looking better than ever.... but it won't ever be Ubuntu, Ubuntu was always that special cup of something that would never scare you, but  just look at the size of this and other threads here and elsewhere ... something went wrong, remember Vista? hopefully the developers will and hopefully we wont have 2 years of pain before they fix it.

If progress is Mr Jobs 'my way or the highway' then Ubuntu has finally peaked.

> **themainliner said:**
> Three days; two camps.

In the **Unity Haters** camp.
I hate this, I can't do anything! Everything is unintuitive, this interface is designed for touch screens. Why is it following a more MAC-like model? Can some please fork Gnome 2.x?

In the **Unity Apologists** camp.
"Oh look, 15 pages of crying." Unity is modern and kewl! I love MACs anyway. You're all just afraid of change!

I admit that yesterday I felt angry and frustrated, Ubuntu is a fantastic project that has changed the playing field on which discussions about usability and suitability for new Linux user is held. Ubuntu is stable and workable out of the box and turned me around from being a Gnome sceptic and dragged me over from SUSE.

So after a little ranting, I quickly bore with the two entrenched positions forming around Unity. We need to mingle. I love change and I love new tech. I have no definable comfort zone, if I feel comfortable I install another distro and struggle to bend it to my will.

I broke myself trying to bend Unity and customise it to work in a manner I found valuable. I do understand that (even) Unity has potential to become my new shell of choice. Those who are pro Unity need to understand that being dismissive and condescending to those of us who are struggling and initially unhappy with Unity is not going to make us like it any more. 

Some patience, hand holding and the acceptance that Unity may not be perfect for everyone **yet** will take you further. I'm not a Unity Hater, I am a Unity Sceptic but I installed 11:04 and I am ready for a challenge is anyone ready to show some leadership and help me take my first steps into adoption?

+1

### Post by Welly Wu on 2011-05-01
I launched Synaptic in Ubuntu 11.04 64 bit Natty Narwhal and I installed the Unity 2D shell interface. I like it a lot. It makes for finding software applications and launching them very easy along with managing multiple windows. I think it will help me to be more productive in using my computer.

The reason why I installed the Unity 2D interface is because I have an ASUS N61JV-X2 notebook PC and it comes with an nVIDIA GeForce GT 325M GPU with nVIDIA Optimus which is not supported by nVIDIA. So, I rely upon the open source nouveau graphics driver which does not support the OpenGL hardware acceleration required for the Unity 3D shell interface.

Unity is a much better idea in execution than GNOME 2.x or GNOME 3. The Dash feature is quite good too.

### Post by Ken UK on 2011-05-01
> **k4ff3 said:**
> Thanks. I fixed alt-tab problem by assigning the key combination in the "Keyboard shortcuts" utility.
 
And thank you for pointing out that I can access System Settings by clicking the off-button. A ridiculous feature, but thanks for pointing it out.
 
Another bug: When I move windows around by clicking on the titlebar, the windows get stuck when it travels from one monitor to another. How do I fix that?
  
The most annoying problem is that the titlebar of windows hides behind the taskbar. How do I fix that?
 
And I want the taskbar to auto-hide, how do I do that?
 
And I want my taskbar at the bottom, not the top. How do I do that?
 
And I would like to use Docky instead of the default dock. How do I remove the current dock?

I want to be able to resize my windows without having to click Alt, how do I fix that?

Not sure what you mean by some of your terminology like the title bar (the decoration on the top of each application windows?) hiding behind the taskbar? you mean the launcher aka The dock on the left?

Sounds like your having afew problems I never had from default. I did have the problem a couple of time where the window decoration when going full screen would hide behind the global menu at the top but only as one offs, its a bug that needs to be fixed.

To autohide you need to use the CompizConfig settings manager to set the hide option to autohide.

Its not a typical dock like docky, its a launcher and has been specifically designed to be on the side for a purpose. You cant change it and I dont think there is any intention to change it because although it might be your personal preference it has no functionality benefits.

The default 'dock' IS the desktop environment so if you dont like it use Ubuntu classic and add docky. Its not just some add on but is the actually user interface.

You shouldn’t need to use Alt to resize your windows? Its only used in the odd occasion where a window decoration goes outside of the screen space so you can drag it back in. What resolution are you using?

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-05-01
> 
And I would like to use Docky instead of the default dock. How do I remove the current dock?
set the Unity Dock to Autohide and then add Docky

compiz >>> unity plugin >>> then autohide the UNITY dock

( docky may already be installed - but if not do the following )

sudo apt-get install docky

alt + f2 

docky

### Post by el_koraco on 2011-05-01
> **k4ff3 said:**
> Will the new desktop switcher still be available in Classic Ubuntu mode?

install ccsm. Go to systzem, preferences, simple compiz config settings manager. The last tab is called "edges". Chhose one of the edges, and select expo view. Bring your mouse to the selected screen edge. Voila. 

The workspace switcher is just a button for the the Compiz expo plugin.

### Post by toupeiro on 2011-05-01
> **smellyman said:**
> same people will be whining when Debian and Mint give up gnome 2 and go 3......

even though 11.04 has classic Gnome, it is still more fun to whine and complain.

I have no problems with supporting the mainstream direction of a DE.  Unity is not that.  Unity was a small resolution/netbook tool bastardised for desktop form factor solutions.  Despite my previous experience with it, despite every attempt at trying to raise concerns about it, I still tried it from beta 2 until now, and its just horrible.  I'm not mad, but Unity and the following decisions to replace X with with Wayland have thrown up a huge fork in the road.  I've already made the recommendation for all the people I support who do not like this change (by my accounts 112 people so far) to consider Mint, Fedora, or Debian: a.k.a. a distribution who supports the full DE and X.  Canonical obviously thinks they have enough clout to change the development of applications for x.org over the next 5 years and I think that is an absolutely absurd decision.  In the meantime, I don't want the added bloat of wayland wrapped around X until they realize this, or simply don't and continue on their merry way continuing to ignore the pulse of their community on these issues.

Its not an anger thing for me, but it is heavy disappointment in the handling of ubuntu.  I'll probably always still run it on a few machines, but I will not be able to run as my primary OS or support it from that standpoint for people who use Linux professionally much longer.  People always tout that there are other options than ubuntu.  Nothing could be more true.

---

### Post by michaelzap on 2011-05-01
> **k4ff3 said:**
> The most annoying problem is that the titlebar of windows hides behind the taskbar. How do I fix that?

I believe that he's talking about the new "global menu". When you maximize a window, its menus get merged with the top panel. This is indeed confusing and inconsistent behavior, especially if you've moved the minimize/maximize/close buttons to the right (since they'll switch to the left when maximized).

There are several posts here in this thread about how to disable the global menus, so please search for that and you'll be able to turn off this "feature".

### Post by ndefontenay on 2011-05-01
The last thing we can expect from an Operating System trying to break up with standards set a long time ago, is to work the way you expect a UI to be.

Simple things makes a difference. Having the task bar on the left requires a bit of time to get used to.

Somebody discovering computers and UI, won't be saying it was better before and will probably get used to it faster than current gnome 2.x super users.

It's a good start for a first release. There's not too much complaints considering it's only a few days since Ubuntu 11.04 is out.

It's also hard to judge the success of a release on a support forum because it's usually the place where people come when they have a problem.

Some things are fixed, some new things are still not working.

With Maverick Meerkat, I couldn't post pictures to an existing facebook album. Now I can.

The calendar and events in the time area is a really cool addition as well.

Customization is not big yet, but I've managed to reduce the size of my icons in the task bar already.

I've discovered that Unity support lenses. Plugin like apps, pretty much like in firefox, which allows me to view feeds from gwibber from a UI similar to Unity's file manager (icons for applications and files & folder at the bottom).

To make the most out of Unity, I've read about shortcuts and Super + 0-9 is really nice :) My top 10 applications are now always placed first.

Unity is definitely more sluggish than gnome 2 but then there was no OpenGL before unless I used compiz.

I'm sure it will only get faster and better.

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-01
> **rdingraham said:**
> Used Windows for 20 years, then Ubuntu for the last 5, but I am gone if Unity is the future. OpenSuse, Fedora, Mint... anything would be better. Someone should tell Shuttleworth that a desktop computer is not a smart phone or a tablet. Ubuntu has a history of releasing essentially beta software, while ignoring chronic problems (like sound issues), but this release is the last straw. All kinds of things broken. No Menu? Who wants to have to use the keyboard to find an application? Ridiculous!

**Absolutely positively correct !!!**

The first thing that I said when I first tried Unity was "Oh my gosh, they're turning Ubuntu into a cell phone" because that was my instant first impression. I can see Unity with phones and on tablet PCs, but definitely not on production user computers. You can talk all you want about the nifty little keyboard functions and I'll be right there to tell you that my Mom and Dad, both Seniors in their 70s, each with their own computer, will NEVER learn those kinds of things. That's how I got them interested in computers, by being able to find and click on darn near everything almost instantly.

No matter who you were, young or old, computer proficient or not, first time linux user or a windows user, the old (10.10) quickly allowed anyone to comform their systems to their own needs. The ability to conform Ubuntu (LINUX FREEDOM) easily and quickly by anyone has been drastically reduced with Unity. Hmmmm, seems a bit more like ... WINDOWS ... now, doesn't it?

BTW, **Classic Gnome is not a fix!** It wouldn't run some of my backups, gave me more headaches than I've had with Ubuntu 9.10 or 10.04 or 10.10 and eventually totally hosed my system when I tried to include UbuntuTweak backups to my classic desktop. _SO STOP SUGGESTING_ that the Classic Desktop is an "instant fix" because it's not.

### Post by beew on 2011-05-01
> **bowens44 said:**
> Yes, that was another ridiculous thing the devs decided to do. Moving them back to the right is the first thing that I do with any new Ubuntu installation.

Moving the buttons to the right is easy because the option is there but for many Unity "features" there is no option to change. It is not the same thing.

BTW while I don't care about whether the buttons are on the left or the right,--I use both on different machines,- where are they now? There is no more option to move them to the right because maximized windows now take up the top panel where the buttons are fixed on  the left.  You can kill the global menu and change the buttons to the right on unmaximized windows, but on maximizing they switch to the left (so the buttons are switching from left to right and back depending on whether you maximize or not, in effect that means you have NO choice to put your buttons to the right)

### Post by georgelappies on 2011-05-01
The thing I dislike the most about unity is the fact that you do not have a taskbar any more. I have several documents of the same type open simultaneously. With unity I can no longer quickly switch to a specific one with one mouse click. I need to move my mouse to the left to unhide the dock. Then search for where in the doc my application is then click on it to show me little windows of all the different docs of that app that is open, then I get to select the one I want.

Its happened more then once with unity that I would try to open an already opened application. 

Using xfce xubuntu now and loving it. Will not use ubuntu with unity any time soon as it makes my work more difficult and gets in my way instead of helping me.

Because something is new and uses opengl doesn't necessarily make it better. Take the simple philosophy of small commands with the ability to be piped together that is the foundation of unix and thus Linux. Nothing newer that is better has been released IMO. 

If it works leave it, or atleast leave the option open to the users to decide what to use...

### Post by Blasphemist on 2011-05-01
> **lucazade said:**
> Simple, who hasn't read hardware requirements!
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/DemystifyingUnityGraphicsHardwareRequirements](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/DemystifyingUnityGraphicsHardwareRequirements)

I think this link hasn't been found and looked at enough as it could greatly simplify the descisions ahead of time about whether or not your gpu will work well with natty. Especially this:

> [B]How do I know my GPU can run Unity?
There is a test program in Natty for that. Run this command: 

/usr/lib/nux/unity_support_test -p 

It will tell you if your system has the required capabilities for Unity. This is the same program we run at boot time to decide if Unity will be started or if a fall-back should run instead.[/B]

Here is the result from my modest laptop.

> $ /usr/lib/nux/unity_support_test -p
OpenGL vendor string:   Tungsten Graphics, Inc
OpenGL renderer string: Mesa DRI Mobile Intel® GM45 Express Chipset GEM 20100330 DEVELOPMENT x86/MMX/SSE2
OpenGL version string:  2.1 Mesa 7.10.2

Not software rendered:    yes
Not blacklisted:          yes
GLX fbconfig:             yes
GLX texture from pixmap:  yes
GL npot or rect textures: yes
GL vertex program:        yes
GL fragment program:      yes
GL vertex buffer object:  yes
GL framebuffer object:    yes
GL version is 1.4+:       yes

Unity supported:          yes

### Post by Docaltmed on 2011-05-01
One thing that I've been using for a while, but I have found works great with Unity, is the windows picker. I have it set so that when I throw the cursor to the bottom right, I get the windows picker screen, and then I just click on the thing I want to do. 

This is *much* better than using a task bar, especially when you have multiple windows of the same application running. 

The other thing I do is have it set up to have a clear desktop when I throw the cursor to the lower left corner. Then the Unity launcher unfolds, and I can roll out something new.

With those two things enabled, Unity simply rocks. Best release yet.

Perhaps that is not how unity was designed, but none the less people ARE having to do this.  I'm having to do this on multiple machines with identical configurations as mine. I have a stake of several years into supporting and propagating ubuntu to people, non-profits, and for-profits.  Perhaps "just leaving" is not the option I wish to take, but its not out of the question if the community has essentially lost its power to contribute and/or voice issues or concerns that are met openly.

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-01

Perhaps that is not how unity was designed, but none the less people ARE having to do this.  I'm having to do this on multiple machines with identical configurations as mine.  You won't know the answer to this issue, because you won't spend any time researching it, as I am doing.  Therefore, you have absolutely no basis whatsoever to call this issue FUD.  It's happening.  Perhaps if you weren't being so dogmatic about supporting unity by concept alone you'd realize how many issues it has.  I'm glad its doing everything *you* want it to, but clearly that is not the case with everbody, and if our only option, based on moderated feedback is to find something else, we will.  Unfortunately, that message carries a monetary penalty to canonical in many cases.  As a moderator, I'd think you'd care about such things.  That is where my comment came from.  I have a stake of several years into supporting and propagating ubuntu to people, non-profits, and for-profits.  Perhaps "just leaving" is not the option I wish to take, but its not out of the question if the community has essentially lost its power to contribute and/or voice issues or concerns that are met openly.

I remember when I first moved to Ubuntu. 8.10 had just been released a week before (though I didn't know that), and there I was with an HP Tablet with a scrambled vista and Intrepid half-installed.

Once I got things up and running, there were still some mighty hills to climb. Touch screen sucked, rotation sucked, stuff Just Didn't Work.

But I kept my eye on the fora, googled a lot, filed bugs on launchpad like it would buy me lunch. And over the course of the next week, month, 2 months, the bugs got fixed, I discovered work-arounds, others shared their workarounds with me...

And that's pretty much how every release has gone for me ever since then. That's *our* part of the job -- file the bugs, add to the wishlists, actually DO THE WORK that makes this a free gig for everyone. I couldn't program my way out of a paper bag, but I sure as heck can explain my problems in excruciating detail to any dev that wants to listen.

And they aren't listening here, and moaning and groaning on Ubuntuforums, as emotionally satisfactory it may be, isn't going to get your problems solved OR get Ubuntu moving in the direction you want it to. Go to launchpad, get on IRC, hang out with over there for a while, and you'll make a difference.

### Post by michaelzap on 2011-05-01

I haven't seen any interviews that explain why the Ubuntu team decided to create Unity in the first place, given that Gnome Shell was already on the way and Unity is so similar in its design. Can anyone provide a link to an article somewhere with insight on that? To me Unity just seems like a bad copy of Gnome Shell.

Compiz is an amazing toolbox, and I think that almost all of the good things about Unity are to its credit. 

Having two brand new default interfaces that are so similar in both their advantages and their flaws launch at the same time disperses everyone's energy and development time and encourages forks.

### Post by BigSilly on 2011-05-01
> **Blasphemist said:**
> I very much like this and wish some that have been dissing the release had seen this. I think it might have helped.

I think one of the big problems I have with Ubuntu and Unty is not some much the DE but how it was communicated. When Gnome 3 came out, they set up a website with very friendly and professional videos explaining the new environment and how to use it. I was very impressed with this. It seemed a very good way to go about things. By contrast there has been nothing about Unity to compare, so many users have had to use Google, which is all well and good but I just feel Unity could have been communicated better. No matter, I'm here now. :)

> **michaelzap said:**
> I haven't seen any interviews that explain why the Ubuntu team decided to create Unity in the first place, given that Gnome Shell was already on the way and Unity is so similar in its design. Can anyone provide a link to an article somewhere with insight on that? To me Unity just seems like a bad copy of Gnome Shell.

Compiz is an amazing toolbox, and I think that almost all of the good things about Unity are to its credit. 

Having two brand new default interfaces that are so similar in both their advantages and their flaws launch at the same time disperses everyone's energy and development time and encourages forks.

These were exactly my feelings too as I was playing with Gnome 3 a while ago. I just didn't see the point of spending all that effort on Unity when Gnome 3 was so great. However, I can kind of see why Ubuntu wanted to differentiate and have it's own identity, though I still feel they might have been better served with Gnome shell. Only time will tell whether they are different enough to warrant their existence. To me, though their ideas are similar, they are both done in very different ways.

### Post by scratman on 2011-05-01
I don't like Unity.  It might be the best thing that ever happened to Ubuntu, but I don't like using software that slows me down/feels like an Alpha release/crashes my computer!

Seriously, I don't find the location of active windows intuitive, I don't like pressing Alt+Tab to switch windows to have unhelpful icons rather than the helpful icons I had in 10.10.  When I switch tabs I get the default icon for the software, with no text beneath, which is a problem when I have 4 or 5 instances of the same piece of software open simultaneously.

I don't like the way that Ubuntu is stable using Gnome, but logs me out randomly when I use Unity.

I don't like not having a panel at the bottom of my screen telling me what programmes I have running.  And it's not like I gain useable space through this, because the Autohide option on Gnome meant that this space was always being used for running programs unless I moved the mouse to the bottom of the screen, when I got useful information about running programmes.

I don't like the combination of grey and black for the top toolbar and the icons/buttons on the top toolbar.

I don't understand why my menu/locations buttons are by default in the middle of a menu on the right side of the screen, rather than somewhere intuitive, like the corner of a screen.

There are other issues, but these are the ones that really irritate me.  They slow me down, and I do not want to use an interface that has this effect, so unless and until Unity becomes more usable and efficient for me than Gnome, I will not be logging into Unity sessions on my machines.

---

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-05-01
> **uRock said:**
> Most other gnome distros will be changing to GNOME3 which isn't much different from Unity.

On the face of it yes, but [this article]("http://www.techrepublic.com/blog/opensource/gnome-shell-vs-ubuntu-unity-which-desktop-wins/2291") on TechRepublic has some interesting points as to why Gnome3 might be a better choice.  

In addition the author (of the article) Jack Wallen has his own Poll on Unity, but the question is slightly different to the one here.  Interestingly the 25/25/50 split also occurred in his poll, but with over 50% saying they preferred Gnome3 to Unity, the other two quarter splits (actually 23% each) saying they preferred either Unity or something completely different.  See thumbnail below.  Unlike our poll, that one is still open and has over 4000 votes; not an insignificant number.  I encourage everyone to add their 2c.

I also disagree with the comment made here that "Gnome3 is not ready yet".  Unity appears to have more than just few holes and is also "not ready yet".  I understand the need to change since the old Gnome will be unsupported, but I'm not sure why it was decided to spend time developing Unity rather than Gnome3.  From an outsiders point of view, surely a group effort in that direction would have been more productive.

> The link in my signature may be helpful.

Unfortunately no, since I've decided to stick with 10.04 LTS until something demonstrably better comes along.  As I keep telling people there's nothing wrong with that in a production machine...  it's the LTS release after all and that's what it's for.

With classic Gnome there were dock type programs written for those that like them (I do not).  I am sure that we'll see drag and droppable window switchers to replace the missing bottom bar in Unity and Gnome3.  With this and some decent configurability and reliability I'm sure either one will make a decent desktop.  Keyboard shortcuts are all very well, but there also needs to be an in-your-face obvious way to do things as well, like right-clicking on the desktop to make a launcher or change the configuration.  This was one of the things that made classic Gnome a friendly place to be for both new users and the old hands alike.

> 
Merged with Unity Mega Thread

I got dumped here from what I thought was a legitimate discussion on Unity.  You might have waited a week or two before classifying it as a "recurring topic".  This isn't just a religious discussion, people really do have concerns.  I suspect sweeping it under the carpet won't make it go away, and the background noise won't stop until they have a UI that they can use that way they want.

Regarding the option to switch to a different desktop release or even a different distro: Yes that's option, and one I investigated.  The fact is though, that I like the Ubuntu community and the support level for the mainstream releases is unmatched by anything else.

Chris

### Post by lebm on 2011-05-01
> **Guillaumeb said:**
> Hello, 

I work as a technology journalist for the French magazine Clubic.com. I am currently trying to figure out the first reactions of the Ubuntu community to the Unity interface.

I was wondering if any of you would be likely to answer this poll. It only takes a few seconds. Oh and feel free to leave a comment.

Basically Now that 11.04 is published i would like to understand how Unity has been received by the users.



Thank you very much

Well, I don`t like Unity. I tried it for a few days and decided to go back to Ubuntu classic. I like to see at a glance what applications are opened and I hate auto-hide panels. Another more serious problem. I convinced my mom to use Ubuntu when she bought her computer. All she wanted was to write a few docs and browse some sites. It took a while for her to learn even such simple tasks, if I upgrade her computer I will have to make Ubuntu classic default for her, she will have a hard time to learn Unity. I think this will happen to many non geek people. I used to brag to Windows people that when I upgrade my Ubuntu, nothing changes  ! I mean, except for some improvements and few changes here and there, everything else seemed the same on the interface, which is a good thing ! This is not the case with this upgrade if one decide to use Unity. Unity may be a good choice for the small netbooks screen (for the record, I prefer Gnome on my 10" netbook), but not on my wide screen 19" monitor. Canonical should have spent its time improving the already good Gnome interface.

### Post by lcason on 2011-05-01
:confused:

I've always had positive results with Ubuntu upgrades, so I didn't hesitate to upgrade to 11.04. Wasn't too happy with the initial experience on the new Unity interface, but decided to give it a few days before making final judgement. I hate pop-up tool bars, so I figured out how to set the display state to static. But when I sat down to pay bills just now, I noticed that the Compiz Fusion screen manager doesn't recalculate the remaining screen size. So CTRL+ALT+4 for a left half-screen overlaps with CTRL+ALT+6 for a right half-screen. Yuck! 

So, I figured that there must be some simple way out of here, to put me back to a more conventional UI. Hmmm, here's something. "Disable Unity Plug-in". Sounds reasonable. Take away the plug-in, and things should go back to what they were before the plug-in twisted them up.  #*$%&$!!!!  Nope!  What you get is a UI with **NO UI**!!  Nice going guys.  Tried a restart just to see if something would check to see that there's a blank desktop and help me out. But no help.

Needless to say, I'm seeing red. This is the first time in many years that I've felt like I was on the wrong OS. This is the first time since running Ubuntu that I've felt like I've been hoodwinked by some marketing-driven rush to market--for what??

OK, I know I'm over-reacting a bit, so I'll try to calm down and get to the point of my post...

QUESTION: Is there some way from a terminal shell to get some kind of UI working again after making the mistake of disabling the Unity "Plug in". ?  Right now my "cure plan" is to reinstall 10.10, but I was hoping for something a little less dramatic and time consuming.

Thanks,

Lee

I've always had positive results with Ubuntu upgrades, so I didn't hesitate to upgrade to 11.04. Wasn't too happy with the initial experience on the new Unity interface, but decided to give it a few days before making final judgement. I hate pop-up tool bars, so I figured out how to set the display state to static. But when I sat down to pay bills just now, I noticed that the Compiz Fusion screen manager doesn't recalculate the remaining screen size. So CTRL+ALT+4 for a left half-screen overlaps with CTRL+ALT+6 for a right half-screen. Yuck! 

You should really report a bug on this.

> QUESTION: Is there some way from a terminal shell to get some kind of UI working again after making the mistake of disabling the Unity "Plug in". ?  Right now my "cure plan" is to reinstall 10.10, but I was hoping for something a little less dramatic and time consuming.

Thanks,

Lee

Sure, just run "gnome-panel". Then you can start CCSM and re-enable the Unity plugin, or log out and switch to Ubuntu Classic.

### Post by cloyd on 2011-05-01
I haven't read the entire thread . . . there is quite a bit here. However, I installed Natty yesterday. I have an netbook running Lucid . . . tried the live flashdrive and it just wouldn't boot . . . got a snowstorm where I should have had a login screen. However, Natty worked on my larger laptop . . . and I think I'm going to like it. True enough, I don't have my precious cube. But the cube really didn't do anything but look pretty.  However, the way the multiple desktops work is really useful and easy. At first, I thought it was hard to find my application in the menus . . . until I learned to use the search bar . . . and it is fantastic. Still have conky.  Miss a few of my panel apps, but have conky doing the same thing. I think the only thing I really am going to miss is having my minimized applications in the bottom panel. But I think I may get used to it and like it better. Once I found my way around, most of the changes have been useful for doing work . . . but not for eye candy. 

If I had read the entire thread, I might find an answer to this . . . why the name Unity? Because the netbook and desktop OS have been merged?  

Why the push to Unity? Perhaps, simply to have an interface that is unique?  
Will this interface be more adaptable to tablets? I wonder . . . that would explain the move in this direction.  And, at first glance, the thing looks more like a smart phone interface.

Nothing vital and living stays the same. Change is essential for things that are viable. I've loved Ubuntu and Gnome. I don't always like change. I'll keep Lucid on my netbook. The LTS version will probably be supported longer than the little netbook lasts. However, I'll have Natty and Unity on the machine that I do most of my real work on. I think it may make some things easier.

### Post by TR82 on 2011-05-01

First impressions ? It's radically different, this Unity thing. 

That's a diplomatic way to say my new desktop environment looks absolutely horrid. The icons in the Unity bar are far too big for my liking, the whole bar's too wide for me. Why can't I make the bar & the icons narrower/smaller ? And place the whole thing on the bottom of the screen like a dock ? And why's there still a bar on the top of the screen ? There's so little in the top bar thingy ... couldn't that all have been integrated into the Unity bar and the top bar completely dispensed with ?

You know, it wouldn't have been so yucky if I could customise the whole thing. It's because I can't change it that I hate it.


The workspace switcher looks rather nice but requires far too many clicks to switch from one space to the next. And where's my desktop cube gone ? In fact, where's the compiz manager thingummyjig gone ?

Grrr ... I can't find a damn thing LOL ... it's almost as bad as going from Windows to Ubuntu waaaay back.

Nvrm. Will stick it out and report back after 24 hrs of trial and error.

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-05-01
> **lcason said:**
> :confused:

I've always had positive results with Ubuntu upgrades, so I didn't hesitate to upgrade to 11.04. Wasn't too happy with the initial experience on the new Unity interface, but decided to give it a few days before making final judgement. I hate pop-up tool bars, so I figured out how to set the display state to static. But when I sat down to pay bills just now, I noticed that the Compiz Fusion screen manager doesn't recalculate the remaining screen size. So CTRL+ALT+4 for a left half-screen overlaps with CTRL+ALT+6 for a right half-screen. Yuck! 

So, I figured that there must be some simple way out of here, to put me back to a more conventional UI. Hmmm, here's something. "Disable Unity Plug-in". Sounds reasonable. Take away the plug-in, and things should go back to what they were before the plug-in twisted them up.  #*$%&$!!!!  Nope!  What you get is a UI with **NO UI**!!  Nice going guys.  Tried a restart just to see if something would check to see that there's a blank desktop and help me out. But no help.

Needless to say, I'm seeing red. This is the first time in many years that I've felt like I was on the wrong OS. This is the first time since running Ubuntu that I've felt like I've been hoodwinked by some marketing-driven rush to market--for what??

OK, I know I'm over-reacting a bit, so I'll try to calm down and get to the point of my post...

QUESTION: Is there some way from a terminal shell to get some kind of UI working again after making the mistake of disabling the Unity "Plug in". ?  Right now my "cure plan" is to reinstall 10.10, but I was hoping for something a little less dramatic and time consuming.

Thanks,

Lee

[COLOR=Red]**ALWAYS BACK UP IMPORTANT DATA - BEFORE TRYING things just to be safe ...... **[/COLOR]

you could do

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install aptitude
sudo aptitude update

Desktop 1 ..... with the kde login ......
sudo aptitude install kde
sudo aptitude install kde-full

That one gets you KDE working

or 

Desktop 2 .....

sudo aptitude install lxde 

( lightweight desktop environment .... may fire up some errors )

Desktop 3 .....

sudo aptitude install E17 

( not real useful a bit outdated )

Desktop 4 .....

sudo aptitude install E16 

( very old one .... but love it .... adds to almost anything )


Not sure if they will all work for you or not ..... but they do for me ....

gets me out of tricky situations sometimes ....... but you could try one first before
reverting to 10.10 

Unless anyone else knows a easier or better way  ..... to get UNITY back for you ......
I did see a thread somewhere ..... 

might be worth searching if UNITY is what you want maybe .....

unity --reset

But it does not sound that way ..... so thats why I give the options above ..... a few choices

Hope they help a little .... to make your day better .... ;)

Make sure you have room ..... for whatever you decide to put on .... KDE is quite big ....
The rest are relatively small though .....

or you could try in my signature .... with Gnome-Shell .... experimental though ....
Although again it works ok for me ....

[COLOR=Red]**ALWAYS BACK UP IMPORTANT DATA - BEFORE TRYING just to be safe ...... **[/COLOR]
but as you did say you were going to go back to 10.10 ..... you must have a backup.

### Post by toupeiro on 2011-05-01
> **NCLI said:**
> You do realise that Fedora also plans to switch to Wayland, right?



This is nowhere near a "plan"  It's an analysis and testing and at this point.  Redhat understands what doing so will mean to RHEL, and the billions they pull in revenue for support. 

I also know Fedora plans on using gnome shell.  a Full DE is not a term I coined...  A Full DE is the end to end, as intended, desktop environment and all components therein.  As a user, use what you want, but for a company who contributes very little back upstream to gnome for the amount they leverage it, its a bit disheartening.  Wayland as an X "replacement" is pure smoke and glass because its NOTHING without applications to run on it.  Linux caters to applications in scientific disciplines alone that cost more than most people could imagine from a development and support standpoint. Redhat is smart enough to realize this, which is why they haven't fully committed to it.  Fedora has an upstream responsibility to RHEL, and Redhat is the parent of both.  They are watching Canonical and assessing waylands potential.  If app developers and corporations who put out applications writing for X get behind wayland, I can totally see this happening, but it wont be in RHEL until that happens, and therefore it will not "switch" from X in fedora either.  What you will have by "switching" any sooner than that are wrappers for everything in the graphical stack.  This is NOT better...

---

### Post by Tyrgle on 2011-05-01
> **Tyrgle said:**
> My review of Unity:

Linux Mint seems to be a decent OS.  The most notable thing about it is a different color scheme, and an annoying main menu.  Luckily, the main menu button was easy to delete.

As was Ubuntu.

Ok, maybe that was a little content free.  Let me give a quick rundown of the 11.04 user interface.  I was an Ubuntu user since Dapper BTW.

(This on a Dell Mini 10v Ubuntu Edition, yes, it shipped with Ubuntu)

[LIST]
[*]Installed 11.04, everything went fine.  Rebooted.
[*]Boot and login took longer than usual, no biggie, probably updating files.
[*]Rebooted after it settled down.  Boot was ok, login still took forever.  Unity thrashed my disk a lot.  The UI was slow and jerky.
[*]Tried Unity a bit.  Couldn't stand any single aspect of it.
[LIST]
[*]I don't like docks.  They have no relation to my actual open windows, since they just indicate programs, not windows.
[*]The dock is terrible.  It has zero customizability, is slow, and ugly.
[*]Putting the menu bars at the top is fine, I always preferred that style.  Making the menu bar pop up on mouseover?  Not so much.  Putting the close button up there right next to it?  That stuff you're smoking, it can't be legal.  The whole point of an embedded menu bar at the top is so you can be sloppy with the mouse.
[*]The popover apps interface is inconceivably awful.  I tried poking around in it, and it literally made me want to smash the laptop.  You randomly click around looking for what you want, poking around in the ugly and badly organized menu.  After you do this for a bit, you accidentally click slightly off, and the interface disappears.  You reopen it and it's not where you left it.  This happens every time because you're using a touchpad.
[*]The theme is absolutely terrible, just like it's been since Lucid.  You can't tell the focused window from the unfocused one.
[/LIST]
[*]Ok, I logged out and logged in again with classic.  Classic logs in quickly, unlike Unity (even after a cold boot).  Unfortunately, classic is totally broken in 11.04.
[*]Compiz is horribly misconfigured:
[LIST]
[*]The panel slides when you move viewports.  Had to manually reconfigure it.
[*]The grid widget means that placing windows doesn't work.  You drag them around and they randomly jump around the screen if you get close to an edge, or maximize themselves.
[*]ccsm jumps above and below the screen, and basically doesn't work on the netbook.  Spent about an hour hitting random buttons trying to find what to kill to fix compiz.
[*]Finally got compiz to not go insane constantly.  However, it started crashing every time I open a menu in chrome.
[/LIST]
[*]Rebooted to see if it was an Xorg bug.
[*]Went back to Unity for a few minutes to give it another shot.  The horror of the apps etc. was shocking.  Unable to turn off any terrible features.  Hell, couldn't even *find* the preferences in the app overlay.
[*]Went back to classic.
[*]Compiz went into infinite crash-restart cycle.
[*]Walked into other room, and got a Linux Mint USB stick.
[*]Held down power button for 5 seconds, inserted USB stick, and reformatted computer.
[/LIST]

Unity final score: Game over.

---

It's not hard to start typing "settings" into the search field to see where to go to make changes. Once you find the app you are looking for you can just drag the icon to the panel if you plan to use it often enough to warrant it.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-05-01
> **BigSilly said:**
> OK, not read the whole thread (obv.), but I have something I'm not quite clear about. I keep reading that the next release will not have any Gnome components, and that Unity will completely replace it. Is this true, and how will that work? Will there still be Nautilus, for example, and Unity on top of things like that, or are the Ubuntu devs intending to completely usurp the Gnome applications? I'm especially curious about this as obviously Gnome 2 will be deprecated by the time of the next Ubuntu release, so there must be some plan...?

Or have I totally gotten the wrong end of the stick? :D

Yes you do have the wrong end of the stick, Oneiric will be using gnome 3 libraries and programs. Unity is just a shell on top of gnome. When Oneiric is released we should have the choice of using:

[LIST]
[*]Unity 2D
[*]Unity 3D
[*]Gnome-shell
[/LIST]

Whether the classic desktop is kept around will be decided at [UDS-O]("http://summit.ubuntu.com/uds-o/")

### Post by Tyrgle on 2011-05-02
> **uRock said:**
> It's not hard to start typing "settings" into the search field to see where to go to make changes. Once you find the app you are looking for you can just drag the icon to the panel if you plan to use it often enough to warrant it.

I find Unity very easy to use and have been asked to install it on other peoples' systems.

Or to put it another way, the organization and presentation of the data is so bad that the only way to navigate it is to know the name of the program you want and to use a search engine.

This isn't an argument in its favor.  In fact, this problem is probably the primary argument for why a GUI might be superior to a CLI: the CLI requires memorizing a bunch of arcane keywords to search for, while the GUI lets you discover what you need.

Unity: The downsides of a CLI, combined with the bloat of a GUI.

---

### Post by Mr. Picklesworth on 2011-05-02
> **NCLI said:**
> > What? I can&#8217;t change my window manager?

This is the case for both desktops. You can not change your window manager. Say you prefer Emerald over the standard window manager&#8230;if you&#8217;re using either GNOME Shell or Ubuntu Unity, you&#8217;re out of luck. One of the draws of the Linux desktop has always been its flexibility. If you&#8217;re using either GNOME Shell or Ubuntu Unity, kiss that flexibility goodbye.I'm not sure on this one actually, has someone tested this?

That is true. Both environments are built around their respective window managers, much as Openbox or Fluxbox or Wmii are window managers foremost. There is a really good reason for it, and the bottom line here is simple: if you want to be a software developer, be a software developer. (Or if you want a different shell, use a different shell; X session types are wonderfully flexible. I have one for Wine that works brilliantly).

---

### Post by Robvdl on 2011-05-02
I don't like Unity at all, I really tried it for a while, but it feels way too clunky for me.  Maybe it's great for new users, but I absolutely hate it as a long time Linux power user.  I can't stand the global menu and close buttons on the left, it's starting to feel way too much like a Mac.  I also can't stand the overlay scrollbars (ugh).  Another thing I wasn't happy with is when they removed icons from menus and buttons by default a while back, they were a great visual queue to find menu items faster, so why hide them???  It makes no sense to me.  If you turn menu and button icons back on, the desktop looks nicer to me too, menus don't look so bland.  I used to love Ubuntu, and I still do, but lately I have not been happy with some of these changes.

What I currently do straight after installing Ubuntu:

Switch back to classic gnome and remove unity
Put close/minimise/maximise buttons back on the right
Re-enable menu and button icons
Remove overlay scrollbars

I am going to give Gnome 3 a go some time soon, to me it looks a lot more attractive than Unity. Despite the good and bad reviews out there, I am willing to give it a good go.

### Post by acplusplusprogrammer on 2011-05-02
I've decided to give Unity another chance, but it simply doesn't work:

-** 4 click to open a preferred application** (for example, to open Gimp I have to: Application, move the mouse to the extreme end of the screen, select graphics and then I can click on Gimp.....much easier with Gnome 2)
- **SLOWER** and **UNSUPPORTED**
-** Fancy!** Hey hey, I need my PC to work not to feel cool opening fancy panels. I'M NOT STARTING MY PC TO FEEL: "HEY, I HAVE SO MANY APPLICATIONS....."
- **And if I open many applications** (as I usually do)......I have to wait that the fancy application bar on the left scrolls all the windows one by one....before I just needed to point my mouse on the bottom bar and **click!**
- **Disordered**. And not because I'm not used to. now I have 4 launchers on the top bar (Firefox, Thunderbird, Terminal and Skype). They are the things I use more and I simply "move and click" without scrolling results. 
-** The LEFT panel isn't clever**: I have a wide screen (an many have), so to move the mouse to the left takes longer than to move the mouse to the top
-** I don't want launchers on my Desktop, I just want ordered and fast menu**


Said all this, I have almost the SAME criticism for Gnome 3.

I understand that doing so Canonical is trying to become more appealing for people like my brother (18 years old, iPhone oriented, grown with Windows XP, VISTA, and 7 and with that kind of environment)......

BUT

I can assure that those people:
- **Don't really care **and they will never use 1 single minute of their life **to understand** **the terminal, the shell and everything**. And if you use Linux, soon or later , you are going to use it
- Can I play Rome Total War? Empire Total War? Call of Duty? Bad Company?** NO!!!!** So you will never get their attention, that's all. But the risk is_ to lose the people you've got with your great work_.


So, I understand the will to take a biggest market share, the will to give a modern look. But simply, if I'm selling a High-Quality Chocolate, traditionally made and with a sophisticated taste I can't make it appealing for those who eat Nutella, adding more and more fats!

I simply hope that by the 12.04 (when the support for my 10.10 - a very good Ubuntu version - expires) at least both Canonical and Gnome, have decided to give the possibility to choose and ALWAYS choose, what interface to use.

OTHERWISE

I don't really see the point of using Linux instead of Windows 7 (preinstalled on my PC...)

### Post by NCLI on 2011-05-02
> **toupeiro said:**
> This is nowhere near a "plan"  It's an analysis and testing and at this point.  Redhat understands what doing so will mean to RHEL, and the billions they pull in revenue for support. 
Just like Canonical then. Obviously they're not switching unless it works properly.
> 
I also know Fedora plans on using gnome shell.  a Full DE is not a term I coined...  A Full DE is the end to end, as intended, desktop environment and all components therein.  As a user, use what you want, but for a company who contributes very little back upstream to gnome for the amount they leverage it, its a bit disheartening.
So if I install Gnome 2.XX, remove the panels, epiphany and nautilus, and use Nautilus-elementary and Docky instead, I'm not using a "Full DE"? If that's true, I have never used a full DE.

> Wayland as an X "replacement" is pure smoke and glass because its NOTHING without applications to run on it.  Linux caters to applications in scientific disciplines alone that cost more than most people could imagine from a development and support standpoint.
And the people using those applications probably won't use Wayland for many years to come.

>  Redhat is smart enough to realize this, which is why they haven't fully committed to it.  Fedora has an upstream responsibility to RHEL, and Redhat is the parent of both.  They are watching Canonical and assessing waylands potential.  If app developers and corporations who put out applications writing for X get behind wayland, I can totally see this happening, but it wont be in RHEL until that happens, and therefore it will not "switch" from X in fedora either.
You usually don't write applications for X, you write applications for GTK, Qt, java, etc. Which means that if those toolsets are updated to support Wayland, all applications which use them magically will as well.

> What you will have by "switching" any sooner than that are wrappers for everything in the graphical stack.  This is NOT better...
And other than in your fantasy world, no one is going to do that.

### Post by Nisitiiapi on 2011-05-02
After trying to fudge with Unity for a few days, I ended up reverting back to gnome. Unity has some hope, but right now, it's not ready to replace gnome.  It needs additional features and options, for sure.

The applications menu is annoyingly time consuming.  Too much trouble to just find an application that should be readily available.  It needs an option to display the menu as a list (sort of like Cairo Dock has) as well as with smaller icons.  Also, there needs to be an option to disable the "here's some applications you could install."  If I want to install an application, I'll do it and don't need the clutter when I'm just trying to find an application.  Same thing for "favorites."  You should be able to turn that off.  An application menu should be allow a short and smooth step to open an application.  Unity is two to three steps -- click and click and scroll; or click, leave the mouse, type a search, hope for no typos, too much time later finally finding what you wanted to open.  Makes opening an application a pain and slows down productivity, hinders usability.  Finally, having Application groups when there's no installed applications under that group is a waste and clutter (Education and Scientific).  The gnome method of hiding those groups unless and until an application under those groups is installed is much more clean and easier to use.  But, I suppose that ties into the "don't show me suggestions to install, I have work to do."

I looked forward to being able to use the Super key to start up applications without having to create my own keyboard shortcuts, but don't like that I'm stuck with numbers.  There should be an ability to designate letters for launchers.  Not for applications that show up just because they're running, maybe, but for anything that's selected to stay there, you should be able to easily designate a Super+key combination (such as Super+F for Firefox).  Trying to remember the order of the icons to get the right integer to open a program is pretty useless, particularly since you might change the order sometime.

The universal menu is nice for maximized windows and the small extra amount of desktop space is good.  But, for non-maximized windows, it's just annoying and a pain.  If I have nautilus window open in the bottom right-hand corner of my screen, it's a waste of time to take my mouse all the way up to the opposite edge of the screen just to activate the menu.  There should be an option to disable the universal menu for non-maximized windows.  Again, it makes the interface inconvenient and counter to efficiency and productivity.  I'm more of a keyboard user than a mouse user, but even hitting just "Alt+F" to get the file menu is not clean -- if my focus is in the bottom right-hand corner of the screen, moving it to the opposite corner to view the file menu is inefficient.  Since the top "bar" of Unity cannot be used like a panel to add items to (other than notification area items), it ends up becoming wasted space, negating some of the space saving of the universal menu.

My overall experience with Unity was very frustrating before I finally decided I actually need to use my computer for work and went back to a gnome session.  I tried to make custom lists for the launchers and followed instructions from others perfectly with absolutely no effect whatsoever -- on two separate clean installs.  The lack of ability to easily add custom launchers is a big downside.  I use a few VMs for testing different browsers in different OSs, testing web sites on a local web server, and some other things and like to create launchers in the menu for them to bypass having to open VirtualBox.  I tried to add those launchers to Unity with no real luck.  I finally got a running VM to show up separate from the VirtualBox icon, selected to keep it there and it just disappeared on reboot.  Another loss of functionality and efficiency.

Between the apparent bugs and the total lack of options for customization, I find Unity not worth the time or trouble right now.  Although I am switching back to a standard gnome session for natty, I do hope that Canonical does get Unity to be something useful, efficient, and customizable to meet each user's unique needs.  Natty overall feels too much like a release candidate that was put out as a final release.  Due to a bug in Natty with the Application Switcher that screws up window focus to the point the OS becomes unusable in a production environment (that bug took a day and half to track down the cause), I almost went back down to 10.10 just to make sure I could work today (and I still may since I'm not a huge fan of the shift switcher and the static application switcher is incredibly slow in Natty with a good 2 second delay before it even comes up).

I always look forward to new Ubuntu releases and I was particularly looking forward to Natty and trying the new Unity interface.  I was hoping it had been significantly improved since it was introduced for the former Netbook version (which I also felt needed further development and was hoping the last 6 months would provide that).  I am disappointed that Unity still seems very immature and, in my opinion, too immature to be the default interface for Ubuntu right now.  The limitations and lack of easy configurability and customization to a user's needs compared to gnome and other user interfaces should've kept it off desktops for now.  I think a better plan would have been to keep it with a Netbook version where the machine itself is designed for limited uses until Unity reached sufficient maturity to make it a useful interface for a desktop computer.

I know lots of people are enjoying Unity and I'm glad.  It certainly has potential and I hold out hope for it to grow into a great, unique interface, but, for me and my purposes, it's not ready for prime time (so to speak).

---

### Post by diablo75 on 2011-05-02
I like it!

I wish I had the time and energy to read every post before mine but I just got off work and managed to get Ubuntu working after a quick fsck repaired some file system errors that existed before (but didn't immediately effect) the upgrade.  Fortunately it was very easy to fix (selected "Reboot to fsck" in recovery mode).

Anyway I wanted to leave some comments about what I think about Unity.  I've been using GNOME since I started with Ubuntu 7.10, and dabbled with KDE on multiple occasions coming away from it disappointed by something about it and overall far more comfortable with GNOME.  I recently checked out the netbook version of Ubuntu 10.10 which comes with Unity and I had mixed impressions at the time.  It wasn't my computer so I didn't give it much time.

Now that I've got it on my own system I'm writing into here as I get used to using it.  I'll try to spend a half hour now trying it out, doing things I would normally do if I were busy with a lot of different kinds of work.  I've already started and I've immediately noticed one very annoying thing:  the auto-hiding bar on the left seems to want to hide almost immediately after you mouse leaves it.  This might have been a "feature" that was intended to reduce the time-out if it detects the user is trying to perhaps click on something very near that edge of the screen and getting into a little bit of a thumb-war with the bar and the cursor over a thin sliver of pixels.  I don't know if this is a real feature though; I have noticed the behavior has returned to normal.

One thing that I got frustrated about while using that netbook I mentioned earlier was the fact that it wasn't very apparent where the System/Administration menu/items had gone.  I just now found them in the shut down menu and I have to say... this is the perfect location for that stuff.  The thing I've noticed and LOVED about Unity is that it is really trying hard to appeal to users who want an interface that's minimalistic and sleak, and I mean that in an aesthetic sense, not a system resource usage sense.  They took the dock bar from Mac and the Search box from Windows (or KDE, whatever), and I like both of these features.

But another thing about this that has to be chewed on is what happens when you click on an icon for an application you've already opened, perhaps intending to open a second instance of it.  I suppose I could fuss about that; a lot of people do.  But the I started to think, "Why and when have I needed to do that?  I mean, I know how to open another tab in Firefox.  And if I'm using a word processor and want to create a new document, I just click "File>New Document".  But the thing I like the most about trying to restrict the user to running one Windows per program is that when you click the red X button, it DOESN'T close it.  Ever close Firefox by accident because you had so many different instances of it running that you mixed them up and closed the wrong one?  It's actually smarter to just get into the habit of using one window + tabs that never get lost or mixed up or closed by accident.

The scroll bars are trolls from reddit with that big grin [IMG]http://3.bp.blogspot.com/_jALkHCV9Hgw/TN0HgHT55HI/AAAAAAAAAIk/PO5VxUyetQU/s1600/Trollface%252Blittle.gif[/IMG].  You'll see it appear, go to grab it and sometimes it'll just disappear and stay gone even if you hover your mouse over where it was, and remain gone even if you click and pretend it's just gone into phantom mode... it really is gone.  This needs to be more "play proof".

Alright, I've gotta get some sleep.  I like most of what I see and think I'm gonna love it once the rough edges are softened out.  I also think people going to the wayside are over-reacting.  Unity is far more pleasant to look and in my opinion look like an interface that is very much geared towards the new user.  This will gain many more new users than it will lose in the long run.

Edit to add a couple of complaints though.  I have an external hard drive attached to my system right now, as well as a Windows partition Ubuntu is parked next to.  How to I browse these resources?  Where is the file browser?  I don't want to "Find files" all the time, I would also like to browse them if I want.

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-02
> **fabricator4 said:**
> 
I got dumped here from what I thought was a legitimate discussion on Unity.  You might have waited a week or two before classifying it as a "recurring topic".  This isn't just a religious discussion, people really do have concerns.  I suspect sweeping it under the carpet won't make it go away, and the background noise won't stop until they have a UI that they can use that way they want.

Regarding the option to switch to a different desktop release or even a different distro: Yes that's option, and one I investigated.  The fact is though, that I like the Ubuntu community and the support level for the mainstream releases is unmatched by anything else.
Chris

Yup, I feel the same way and I felt the same way about my thread too, which actually had more to do with different variables of Unity, Ubuntu, Gnome, and other Distros, as opposed to being a rant or a rave on Unity. As far as other distros are concerned, after almost 3 years of Ubuntu and now feeling as though "True Linux Freedom" is being removed via Unity, I've decided last night that I'm ready for a change.

Last night I installed Debian 6 and was literally blown away by the "total" similarity between Debian and Ubuntu. As a matter of fact, importing my Thunderbird accounts & emails as well as FF settings and numerous other applications was no problem at all. I'm almost willing to bet that I can get my Debian Desktop to look and function identical to my Ubuntu desktop ... at which point I have to scratch my head and ask ... what's so awesome about Ubuntu in the first place if Ubuntu was built off Debian to begin with?

I'm using the Gnome Desktop, I'm using all of my former apps, System administration is virtually identical, System preferences are virtually identical, default file manager is identical, accessories, games, synaptic package manager, the software center, both panels with identical manners of customizing them, the themes as in Ubuntu ... IT'S ALL THERE. Granted, you do have to jump through some tiny hoops to be able to view all types of video/mp3 formats and I'm still fiddling with my Nvidia which is proving to be a bit of a pain (nothing to get too excited about though), but aside from that I'm pretty much already running my former Ubuntu ... On Debian version 6.
Compiz you ask? Yup, that's not a problem either with Debian, and neither is KDE if you want it.
Debian is also reported to be rock solid, totally stable, and they have a good forum too.
Definitely wouldn't recommend such a change for total noobs though.

Since I also endorse Ubuntu and its humaness/user friendliness I'll continue to use it on a virtual box via Debian which will prevent having to use the dual-boot menu at all. That way I can still evolve with ubuntu to help others, but remain happy with my "old stuff" for a long time since Debian doesn't use 6 month release cycles which I've always considered to be way over the top. Yeah, 6 months of development is fine, but then EVERYTHING should come to a screeching halt, followed by 3 months of testing, updating, repairing ... before the "final release" is actually unleashed to the masses. At least 75% of real Ubuntu problems stem from the fact that there's no "extended stop & fix" period before the release.

### Post by YesWeCan on 2011-05-02
Interesting Wikipedia article: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unity_(desktop_environment](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unity_(desktop_environment))

> On 25 April 2011, the eve of the release of Ubuntu 11.04, reviewer Matt Hartley of IT Management criticized the decision to lock Ubuntu users into Unity by making it hard install alternative desktops, such as GNOME 3 and by not providing information that other options exist. Hartley said, "I know of many people who feel strongly that Unity is the next logical step for Ubuntu. And it's entirely possible that the Unity desktop could be well received by most people. That's something we'll have to wait and see how it turns out. From my perspective, however, I think it's not only going to be a massive disaster for the existing user base, but I'm skeptical as to the value Unity will deliver in the first place. Then again, the same could be said about the default shell provided by GNOME 3 on other Linux distros. Speaking for myself, I'll almost certainly be selecting the Xfce desktop, as I've had enough of GNOME and Canonical. The dumbing down of the Linux desktop environment is bordering on insane."

The Unity shell works fine for a small laptop maybe, but desktop wise I prefer the old GNOME panels.

### Post by ZarathustraDK on 2011-05-02
> **NormanFLinux said:**
> Unity 2D runs with no issues.

It should have been the default UI in the first place not afterthought in the Unity project.

I'd disagree. Onboard acceleration is becoming standard pretty fast these days, so a minimum of 3d is to be expected.

Yes I know, there are people out there with boxes worthy of a museum, or who got that old stowaway pc from their parents, or who was trying to refurbish a batch of ancient pc's.
Still, it's not all a shiz'n'giggles decision to go for a 3d-accelerated desktop. For one there's the matter of aesthetics which is pretty straightforward. Second, by utilizing the graphics-card for drawing your desktop, you offload the pressure on the CPU. Try open a sysmon and move a window around in a 2d desktop, notice the spike in cpu-usage when doing it. That's not there when running a composited desktop.

Cheers.

### Post by Peter09 on 2011-05-02
UI designers have long recognised that the mouse/menu system is not the best way of driving a computer. Navigating a mouse to a small target, moving over multiple other targets before clicking requires concentration and coordination. Just watch a beginner try to find a menu target from a big menu.

Power users have for a long time deferred to keyboard shortcuts, command lines and searches to perform these tasks, even in MS Windows. They do it because it is quicker and easier - it allows the user to concentrate on the task in hand.

What Unity does is move focus away from the mouse/ menu towards an easy to use and easy to remember system of shortcuts.  Allowing casual users to easily adopt the practices of Power Users.

If you are going to enjoy Unity you have to change you way of working somewhat to fit in with this concept. Once you do so it all comes together.

List of Unity Shortcuts

[http://www.ubuntugeek.com/list-of-ubuntu-unity-keyboard-shortcuts.html](http://www.ubuntugeek.com/list-of-ubuntu-unity-keyboard-shortcuts.html)
