### Post by Mad-Halfling on 2011-05-02
I was really looking forward to Unity but am bitterly disappointed with it.  It's very unstable and buggy/glitchy and doesn't seem at all to be at the release candidate level of stability and polish I have come to love and expect from Ubuntu/Canonical.  Also, reverting back to GNOME doesn't work anywhere near as well as should be expected.  If you want to get certain things enabled, you have to end up hacking in the shell or config files - surely this goes against the "Linux for human beings" ethos?  I reverted back to GNOME and then tried to enable my compiz cube (until I had lost it I didn't realise how much I actually use it from a functional point of view), but this absolutely hosed the window manager - I windows didn't have title bars and I could resize/move them, tuning on the window decoration displayed a title bar but it was completely non-functional and I still could resize or move windows.  I've worked out how to get the cube enabled in Unity with config file hacks, but come on, this isn't good.

If I disable window snapping (I don't like, nor use it) then this hoses my top bar - it disappears and won't come back until I log out then back in - the same applies when I enable wobbly windows.

Sometimes when I move the mouse to the left hand edge to reveal the launcher bar, it doesn't appear and I have to move the mouse away and back a few times before it eventually appears.  Other times, after it appears, as soon as I move the mouse away from the edge of the screen (to click on a launcher) the bar immediately disappears again.  Another problem is when I launch an app that isn't on the launcher and it appears at the bottom of the list, when these get "flattened" as the bar is full, sometimes activating the bar doesn't unflatten them and I can't activate them using the mouse (nor click anything else on the bar) - interestingly this seems to happen more with my touchpad and the only way to fix it was to plug in a physical mouse.

This is dreadful and these problems were occurring before I had tweaked stuff (all happened in the first 1.5 hours of use) so it was a "vanilla" upgrade from 10.10 and not something I had messed about with.

### Post by BigSilly on 2011-05-02

### Post by MasterNetra on 2011-05-02
I don't mind Unity, screen space is saved with global menu, and while the extra 1-2 clicks for accessing a app is a annoying negative I've already gotten over it. However that said I may return to 10.10 not because of Unity's interface but because natty, for me at least, has plenty of random bugs that seem to come and go. From a vanilla install I had a issue with wallpaper reverting back to default at log out/restart after fully upgrading. This, however, went away after having it shutdown for a while then booting back up.
It also has for seemly random reasons decided to logout on me while i was doing something (crash and logout probably) and when I log back in and do the same thing its fine.
Another problem is that I have a second monitor that I use. Sometimes natty lets it function, sometimes its so badly glitched that you can't see a app window you move into it. There doesn't appear to be a reason for this. 
All in all, interesting idea, but I'll wait until 11.10 to try again, maybe it will be as stable as 10.10 if not more so for me.

> **YesWeCan said:**

But I understand that Gnome3 uses Gnome Shell and Mutter (is that right?) and Unity uses Compiz and possibly some part of Gnome Shell (?). Unity & Gnome 3 are very much different. From a GUI perspective Unity is mostly a inteli-hide dock on the side with fancy right click menus plus a global menu.

Gnome 3, while yes it does have a dock on the side it goes further then that.

### Post by Algus on 2011-05-02

I guess I understand the philosophical reasons for the switch from GNOME to Unity.  It isn't just Ubuntu though.  I feel like it is this general trend in many Linux distros as well as Windows to try and create a Mac-like desktop experience because of how well Apple has marketed their products.   I don't see how it is better, just bulkier.  I don't need big tiles to click on to get to my frequently used programs and it is more work for me to clear out the rubbish that they think I need. Still, if Unity helps bring new people to Linux, I'm all for that.  I'm convinced that Ubuntu can and should be a viable alternative for people who aren't techies but don't necessarily want to be in the OS X or Windows environment.

### Post by YesWeCan on 2011-05-03
> **MasterNetra said:**
> Unity & Gnome 3 are very much different. From a GUI perspective Unity is mostly a inteli-hide dock on the side with fancy right click menus plus a global menu.
I decided to burn a fedora 15 (beta) CD and try out Gnome 3. It ran fine on my system, unlike 10.04 Unity which rejected my system, and it seemed to look just as nice and be reasonably intuitive. Gnome 3 looks even more like my iPad.

It's funny to see this on fedora. I have always thought of fedora as the ultimate geek-linux, and to be very hard to use unless you wrote linux. If fedora 15 is anything like the fedora website then it looks inviting but when you try to use it for something practical you probably won't be able to. :P

So I am still seeking advice on what features of (a fixed) Unity make it worth Canonical breaking away from Gnome and reinventing the wheel? Or are there other reasons for doing this?

Meanwhile I am wondering why my desktop PC will be better off with an iPad interface. I am struggling. Ok, there is a nagging feeling that I have a stone-aged mentality and that perhaps by logical extension I should decry windows entirely and advocate CLI. But I think this is unfair because the point is really about keyboards and mice, and small screens. Tablets don't have mice and don't have efficient keyboards. That is why Apple and others use these large icon renditions with flat menu structures. But the, as some have said, clunky icons are not there because they are better in all circumstances; rather, they are a touch-screen solution to hardware that lacks a keyboard and mouse. I am yet to be convinced that large, flat icon menus are better even with a keyboard and mouse.

So, I come back to wanting Ubuntu to maintain two GUI styles - a really efficient and speedy one for those with full hardware and a more Telly-Tubbies one for those on the move with only finger control. Whether these two are concurrent or selectable or whatever is not that important to me as long as I get the most efficient one for my hardware. So this rumour that Canonical is not going to offer optimisation in the future alarms me.

I also worry about Ubuntu in general. It seems to me there is enough crucial work for Canonical to be getting on with aside from reinventing Gnome 3. The bug list, even for the relatively reliable 10.04, is huge. There are many applications I would like to see added and/or fixed. The linux kernel has a huge amount of support, that's why it is so reliable. The linux apps do not. It is as much the apps that make the OS attractive as its core reliability. Most people would prefer fancy and reliable apps over fancy desktops. You want a really impressive 3D experience? - buy a game.

### Post by Canecon on 2011-05-04
Wow, I didn't expect  to see so many complaints about Unity. I understand people don't like changes, but I'd suggest everyone give it a try. There are several good tutorials on customizing the launcher and using shortcuts. Some people don't realize that:
1) You can add, remove, change the order of applications in the launcher. In reality most people use <10 applications on a frequent bases=>the launcher is not cluttered and you can access you favorite programs in just one mouse click. It takes  several days to get used to using icons in the launcher, at the end of the day it feels very natural. 
2) Using shortcuts is simply great. Spend two minutes on learning them and maybe like me you'll feel that you productivity actually went up with Unity!
I've been using Fedora for the last two years, but I believe that Ubuntu 11.04 is better that Fedora 15. That's why instead of complaining about Fedora I simply installed Ubuntu on my computer. There are many good distros around, I'm sure  everyone can  find the one he/she likes. 
My congratulations to Canonical on another great version of Ubuntu.
P.S. Somehow many complaints remind me of Windows users bashing Linux. The arguments are very similar.

### Post by Perosteck on 2011-05-04
> **daz4126 said:**
> 

So what does Unity offer that is genuinely new, original ... and more importantly - better?

DAZ

It is a new global interface concept based on a clearer (default) desktop and an accessible search function.

From the Unity help file:
[SIZE=2]
[/SIZE] [SIZE=2][COLOR=#000000][FONT=Ubuntu][COLOR=#3C3C3C]"Ubuntu 11.04 is the first release of Ubuntu to feature *Unity*, a completely reimagined user interface. Unity is designed to minimize distractions, give you more room to work, and help you get things done."[/COLOR][/FONT][/COLOR][/SIZE]


Minimize distractions and room for work. That's why its config options are not immediately accessible from the first page, so to speak. And that's also why you can't move the launcher panel, because then the concept goes to hell. Unity is meant to be a plugin for gnome3. Use it or don't. But don't expect gnome3 to be like gnome2. Gnome3 is more like Unity. Soon most people will use gnome3 or unity and all the tried-and-true gnome2 nostalgia is over.

### Post by greybirds on 2011-05-04
It's pathetic, frankly. Undoubtedly it will improve with time, but the ways they've had to twist and break GTK and Gnome makes me suspicious it will ever be stable and useful. It should never have been released at all in its current state, let alone pushed as default. My personal experiences thus far:

1) It's buggy. The window decorator sometimes goes into a cycle of crashing whenever I click the mouse. Within five minutes of booting into it after installation something crashed and took the entire interface with it except the mouse; I had no options at all but to hard reboot. It's been well over a year since X crashed like this pre-11.04.

2) It makes bad use of space. For all the discussion about how important screen real estate is, Unity somehow managed to almost fill the dash on my 1080 monitor with just stock icons on a default install. I was able to wedge two additional icons in before things started happening down at the bottom. Of course, this is because the icons are gigantic, and there is a way to resize them, but it's not at all apparent and wouldn't let me shrink the icons below 32 pixels. And of course, the icons are gigantic (and you can't make them not gigantic) because Unity wants to work with touch screens, but I don't *have* a touch screen.

Frankly, shoving everything into the dash and giving it a full-size icon is wasteful and ill-conceived. It's also bad design; those icons fall into functional categories but there is no distinction provided. Fully half the icons in the dash would be better placed elsewhere; it would help conserve space and organise the interface. 

3) It's arbitrary. I can't move things. I've been able to place things where I wanted them, for whatever reason I wanted, for years now, and suddenly this is verboten. 

4) I had to reboot my entire computer in order to get normal scrollbars back. To me this screams "we've had to hack into things all the way to the bone to make this look like a Macintosh", and that scares me because it's possibly destabilizing GTK and Gnome. And again, they're making me accept this hack designed for a touch screen when I have a desktop. So I'm risking my apps misbehaving or crashing so someone else on a table can see two extra characters per row on a web page. 

5) It over-ruled my Compiz settings without asking or even notifying me. This is unforgivable, since I've tweaked them over years of use to work exactly how I needed them. It's also unprecedented as far as I can tell, since I've always been asked whenever a change was being made by the installer. When I realized they were just flat gone, I tried to restore them from backup but it didn't help; it turned out someone, somewhere, decided to change where Compiz stores its settings, so now my backups are useless. Even though I'm not using Unity I can't use Gnome the way I want unless I (1) create a script to migrate my settings over or (2) rebuild the settings for all 30-odd plugins option by option. 

The last item is what killed my goodwill to 11.04. Until then I was ready to accept this as a work in progress, but I'm not sticking with an upgrade that manages to take functionality away *and* make me redo every tweak I've ever set. I distinctly remember the developers saying that Unity wouldn't be the default for people upgrading and that the scroll bars would be easily reverted. Both have proven false, which makes me suspect they're not working towards a goal but struggling to keep all the hacks and grafts from falling apart, slipping behind in their development schedule as a result and having to take shortcuts and cut out features. I use Linux for stability; Unity isn't stable. I moved to Mint as a stop-gap and am considering what distribution to migrate to long-term.

And on that subject, your poll needs another answer: "It made 11.04 so thoroughly unusable for me I changed distros."

---

### Post by Lucradia on 2011-05-04
> **lemonberry said:**
> Another one here switched back to Ubuntu 'Classic'...

You won't be able to switch to gnome2 soon though, in an upcoming release. However, whether or not ubuntu packages a new gnome2 package after gnome 3 releases as the new gnome meta, is up to ubuntu, not gnome. Canonical would have to have devs to specifically update code in the gnome2 components in case any issues major enough arise if that were the case, as gnome, itself, would not support an old release, unless it had reason enough to, which I don't think it needs to update an old release.

This change will bring about a filter between the people who just follow ubuntu as a cult, and those who really want to use linux as it is. For me, I'd use debian instead of ubuntu.

### Post by YesWeCan on 2011-05-04
[http://computergyan.wordpress.com/2011/04/09/gnome-3-vs-unity-preview/](http://computergyan.wordpress.com/2011/04/09/gnome-3-vs-unity-preview/)

It seems to me that Shuttleworth couldn't persuade the Gnome folks of some details and so embarked on a wheel re-invention and pushed it, unfinished, out the door coincident with the Gnome 3 release so as not to "miss the boat".

I'm still none the wiser about what Unity brings to the table that justifies this huge amount of effort on the part of designers and this waste of effort on the part of the Ubuntu user base as they try to debug it, not to mention any damage to the reputation that Ubuntu has gained as a reliable and user-friendly distro.

I'm all ears. I have tried Gnome3/Shell out and it seems perfectly adequate to me, and pretty much a finished product. The DE is the least of Ubuntu's problems - there are bigger fish to fry towards the goal of widespread uptake.

> **YesWeCan said:**
> I have no expectation either way. This isn't the point I am trying to make. I am questioning what the current motivations are behind design decisions for Ubuntu.

Being a member of staff, would you help me by explaining who it is intended to please by Canonical releasing 11.04 with a half-baked (by most accounts) notebook GUI and who it is intended to please by disconnecting Ubuntu from Gnome in future releases?
Ubuntu is not being disconnected from Gnome, it is being disconnected from GNOME Shell. Unity is a shell for gnome, it's just not Gnome Shell.

> These decisions certainly aren't pleasing me (and I am in the majority judging by this poll so far). But I don't expect them to as I am not paying Canonical for my copy of Ubuntu and because I don't believe this is really a "community" project in an idealistic sense (although that has been a nice fantasy to dwell upon).
You'd be right there. However, the open source community has never been, and will never be, a democracy. It is a meritocracy. If this was a democracy, Gnome 2.00 would have been voted down, and Gnome 1.XX would still be what we use.

> My mistake. I am using Ubuntu and Chrome so it must be one of those that does not recognize this obscure word. The parallel is that the word is bizarre and obscure and originated from your boss and not from the community.
You really think that it's overstepping his bounds for a man who has invested most of his fortune in a project to be allowed to choose the codenames?
> **eentonig said:**

That is NOT usabilty! Usability is not to force people to do things your way, usability is when people find something easy to use in their own style. Why can't Unity support keyboard shortcuts AND normal mouse behaviour AND mouse gestures so people can choose how they interact with it?
File some bugs asking for these options to be added. The developers are generally positive to suggestions.

I have some issues with your post though. First of all, all change is hard. When you're used to an interface you've used for many years, like Windows, and switch over to something radically different(OSX). You're going to feel lost.

> **cmedley said:**
> I love how people say "you'll get used to it."  If I had cancer I am sure I would get used to that too.

It shouldn't be in th middle of your screens. It will always appear on the monitor set as primary, just change that setting to get it out of the way.

> I tried the Ubuntu Classic, but had big problems with the title bars disappearing and crashing.  Before someone tries to blame my hardware, It was rock solid under 10.10.  I tried both an upgrade and a clean install with the same results.

> **daz4126 said:**
> Can anyone say what Unity actually brings to the party?
A new UI design, with a faster way to launch applications, no cumbersome menus, and a clean design.

> As far as I can see all of the 'neat' Windows tricks are just Compiz plugins configured in a certain way. This can be done in Classic.

That leaves the Launcher, which is really just a poor man's Cairo Dock with no configuration options. Cairo Dock is also available in Classic.

So what does Unity offer that is genuinely new, original ... and more importantly - better?

Yes, you can add a dock, install gnome-do, remove the lower panel, and setup all the neat window management tricks Unity uses. Unity is about bringing it all together with the right defaults, in a clean, efficient design.

> **fireflower said:**
> "what the **** am I seeing"
"maybe it's better I just have to learn"

Please report a bug for your gaming issues.
> **realzippy said:**
> This always used to be easy.open terminal,type part off app name,TAB,enter.

Check link in my sign to see what is ***real*** easy.
What you need to enter in the terminal is not the name of the application, it is the name of the launcher located in /usr/bin. Besides, the application will close if the terminal is closed, meaning you'd have to open many terminals to launch all of your applications.

Searching in Unity's Dash brings up results which match both the name of the application, and the name of the file located in /usr/bin.
> **Lucradia said:**
> You won't be able to switch to gnome2 soon though, in an upcoming release. However, whether or not ubuntu packages a new gnome2 package after gnome 3 releases as the new gnome meta, is up to ubuntu, not gnome. Canonical would have to have devs to specifically update code in the gnome2 components in case any issues major enough arise if that were the case, as gnome, itself, would not support an old release, unless it had reason enough to, which I don't think it needs to update an old release.

This change will bring about a filter between the people who just follow ubuntu as a cult, and those who really want to use linux as it is. For me, I'd use debian instead of ubuntu.
Debian will also drop Gnome 2.XX. No one wants to maintain it, it's a huge pile of old code, which relies on quite a lot of deprecated libraries.
> **YesWeCan said:**
> [http://computergyan.wordpress.com/2011/04/09/gnome-3-vs-unity-preview/](http://computergyan.wordpress.com/2011/04/09/gnome-3-vs-unity-preview/)

It seems to me that Shuttleworth couldn't persuade the Gnome folks of some details and so embarked on a wheel re-invention and pushed it, unfinished, out the door coincident with the Gnome 3 release so as not to "miss the boat".

I'm still none the wiser about what Unity brings to the table that justifies this huge amount of effort on the part of designers and this waste of effort on the part of the Ubuntu user base as they try to debug it, not to mention any damage to the reputation that Ubuntu has gained as a reliable and user-friendly distro.

I'm all ears. I have tried Gnome3/Shell out and it seems perfectly adequate to me, and pretty much a finished product. The DE is the least of Ubuntu's problems - there are bigger fish to fry towards the goal of widespread uptake.
You're largely correct. Canonical chose to move away from Gnome Shell because none of the issues raised by Canoncical were being taken into consideration. This isn't just about the Shell, there have been big problems with the cooperation for quite some time now.

Also, that Unity and Gnome Shell look very similar is not just due to Canonical borrowing design ideas, the Gnome team has as well. There was no dock in Gnome Shell before Unity was announced to be the next default in Ubuntu.

---

### Post by Docaltmed on 2011-05-04
> **YesWeCan said:**
>  And if Unity will be much better than Gnome Shell then why rush out an unfinished Unity? Wait until it is finished and fit for use and then folks will still change to it from Gnome Shell because it has compelling advantages.

Since I began using Ubuntu, at 8.10, I can't remember a single release that wasn't "unfinished" in one form or another. That's because the users are actually part of the development process, and the developers depend upon our feedback from the response on various configurations and with various users, to iron out the bugs. 

Same thing on Gnome Shell. You think that's a finished product? Heck no! I mean, I like it a lot, and was actually using it on a production system ages ago, but it still needs a *huge* amount of polish. I have no criticism of anyone who chooses either DE, but this Canonical rage seems a bit excessive to me. Canonical is a far cry from MS or Apple. Nobody is forcing your hand, here, except for the cold, robotic hand of advancing technology.

### Post by firepages on 2011-05-04
> So, what you're saying is that Unity sucks because it's too different. Not a valid criticism.

but it **is **(a valid criticism)**,** and thats the point many here trying to defend unity are totally missing, it is too different compared to previous changes, many reasonably seasoned ubuntu users like myself have been used to a steadily improving stable familiar platform that we use in work and home life with minor gripes and small changes that made us smile or frown, the unity interface is **so** different that threads like this exist.

many here are suggesting it just needs polish ... the ability to not move the menu , or have good enough graphics to enable compiz to at least partially customize it is not a bit of missing polish, its a big rusty dent.

Yes we can all `move to a different disto` , but thats a cop-out. Whether you like unity or not (I like it on my netbook BTW) you are fooling yourselves if you think that we are all nay-saying stuck in the mud blinkered conservatives who just dont like change, we are regular run of the mill everyday users who mostly don't care if its gnome1/2/3 or pixie 5.6 behind the scenes as long as it provides a consistent usable interface so we** don't** have to go around distro shopping with every release.

Prior to Ubuntu I would change distros more often than my socks.

### Post by NCLI on 2011-05-04
> **YesWeCan said:**

Anyway, this is dodging the issue. Googling "ubuntu 11.04 review" brings back quite a lot of results. Ignoring the people reviewing the beta, I can find only one review really criticising Unity(The one you posted). The rest of the professional reviewers(I'm being generous by including the Register in that category) are largely positive, which is why I said "good work finding that review".
> 
Ok.
I guess I would be more sympathetic to this outcome if Unity showed compelling benefits over Gnome Shell. Then I could say - the Canonical vision is much better for users than that of Gnome, so the time, pain and defocus on other Ubuntu issues is justified.
What are these benefits that justify Unity and its premature introduction?

Ubuntu has been in development for ~2 years, including brainstorming. Gnome Shell has been in development for 6 years. That they are largely equals at this point is the main reason why I think Canonical will soon have a much more compelling environment. 

Also, Canonical seems dedicated to following the standards decidded upon through FD.org, whereas GNOME has many time shown that it really doesn't care, and would rather go it's own way.

> PS: And if Unity will be much better than Gnome Shell then why rush out an unfinished Unity? Wait until it is finished and fit for use and then folks will still change to it from Gnome Shell because it has compelling advantages.
Because people would then have to adapt to change twice. Considering the response we see to the release of Gnome Shell and Unity now, that doesn't seem like a very good idea.
> **realzippy said:**
> **@NCLI**

*A new UI design, with a faster way to launch applications,..*
please specify why apps now can be loaded faster?
Check the link in my sign to know what is fast.

But hey, I see no reason why we can't both be satisfied, so I'd suggest filing a feature request for mouse gesture support, or writing a blueprint for a discussion at UDS-O.

> *I can now work faster, and more efficiently, than I could using Gnome 2.XX. Again, personal opinions FTW!*

I have the idea that you never exploited the possibilities of
Gnome2/Compiz/Avant/Easystroke....
Yes, yes I have. Compiz is Unity, so that is irrelevant. I can do pretty much everything you can do with the mouse in Easystroke with the keyboard in Unity, and the rest just comes down to preference.
> **firepages said:**
> but it **is **(a valid criticism)**,** and thats the point many here trying to defend unity are totally missing, it is too different compared to previous changes, many reasonably seasoned ubuntu users like myself have been used to a steadily improving stable familiar platform that we use in work and home life with minor gripes and small changes that made us smile or frown, the unity interface is **so** different that threads like this exist.
So we should have never moved away from Gnome 1.XX?

> many here are suggesting it just needs polish ... the ability to not move the menu , or have good enough graphics to enable compiz to at least partially customize it is not a bit of missing polish, its a big rusty dent.

Unity is a Compiz plugin, what are you talking about?

> Yes we can all `move to a different disto` , but thats a cop-out. Whether you like unity or not (I like it on my netbook BTW) you are fooling yourselves if you think that we are all nay-saying stuck in the mud blinkered conservatives who just dont like change, we are regular run of the mill everyday users who mostly don't care if its gnome1/2/3 or pixie 5.6 behind the scenes as long as it provides a consistent usable interface so we** don't** have to go around distro shopping with every release.
Then you should *really* stick to the LTS releases. Frequent change and experimentation is the entire reason why we have nonLTS releases.

> **YesWeCan said:**
> Sigh. I have read many posts here from much more experienced Ubuntu users than I and the message I am reading is that 11.04 is the most unreliable release so far.

So I ask again, what is the benefit of Unity, over Gnome Shell, that justifies this?
That it is controlled by Canonical, which have shown me that they work faster than the Gnome team, and adhere to common standards.
> 
Unity is causing a disunity by being a maverick DE. Fine if it gives a compelling benefit. Does it? The only strong benefits I have read about are political.
Compared to Gnome Shell as of this moment? Not much I can say there, as testing Gnome Shell extensively is still on my to-do list(I used it before the dock was added and some buttons removed). Considering their respective pace of development though, I'd say quite a lot.

Just to clarify: I am fully aware that the Gnome team not only had to design and implement the Shell, but also maintain and improve the entire Gnome stack. I also don't really care. What I care about is how rapid I feel development is advancing, and if Canonical can work faster with Unity because they don't have to work much on other things, that doesn't change the result.

### Post by NadirPoint on 2011-05-04
> **realzippy said:**
> Please specify.
Two things stand out right up front.  The launcher now allows me to not only launch the apps I typically added to the panel previously, but also to monitor their running instances at a glance and instantly navigate to any of them with one click.  That is easily an order of magnitude better than Gnome panel.

The workspace wall manager is now just that - a real workspace management tool, as opposed to merely a button-click switch.  Zooming in and out to multiple workspaces and being able to see the workspace content in a zoomed out mode as opposed to deciphering what might be contained in a tiny icon is light years more efficient, faster and intuitive, to me at least.

### Post by beew on 2011-05-04
> **NadirPoint said:**
> Two things stand out right up front.  The launcher now allows me to not only launch the apps I typically added to the panel previously, but also to monitor their running instances at a glance and instantly navigate to any of them with one click.  That is easily an order of magnitude better than Gnome panel.

I have been doing that for ages with awn and the cairo dock and both have more options and are better looking.

But how many applications can you fit on any dock without looking really crowded? If you only use the same few applications it is fine, otherwise the dock alone is not enough. So I only use the dock for the most frequently used applications. Otherwise there are buttons for menus in both AWN and Cairo and they are a lot more efficient than finding and opening applications with  the dash.

> 
The workspace wall manager is now just that - a real workspace management tool, as opposed to merely a button-click switch.  Zooming in and out to multiple workspaces and being able to see the workspace content in a zoomed out mode as opposed to deciphering what might be contained in a tiny icon is light years more efficient, faster and intuitive, to me at least.I am doing that in 10.10 with Compiz and there are many ways to zoom in and zoom out to windows in the same workplace too (most of them don't work in Unity)

the workplace switcher in the Cairo Dock can actually switch workplace instead of just exposing them (and take another click to choose the workplace)

I dfon't know about others, but I don't really care for the gnome panel for its own sake (i use mostly the cairo dock anyway) but I like it because it is customizable and modular so the possibilities are endless and you don't have to use the vanilla setups. Unity may have better defaults (debatable) but definitely a lot more limiting at this stage (Nothing seems to be movable/removable, but that will change in 11.10)

### Post by bro on 2011-05-04
Unity crashes regularly, I dislike the global menu, have always disliked on macs to. It creates long distances to travel over the screen, puts tasks outside of your working window, it's just not intuitive. 

I also don't like that I can't switch easily with several windows of the same application, since I don't have buttons for each instance. 

The behavior of the sidebar showing not entirely but moving in a bit as I move my mouse to the corner is just anoying. Only slightly annoying is the fact that it shows only after pushing the left edge of the screen. 

I should be able to change the possition of these buttons. Nobody needs to dictate that I like vertical screen space more then horizontal. 

I want intellihide behavior the way Docky does. 

Will I use it ever? I'll try it again in 11.10 but have low expectations, Gnome3 looks better. It might be more mature in 12.04 or 12.10. As long as it's hiding the menu options until I hover over...please...

### Post by Exodist on 2011-05-04
IMHO, I wasnt really excited about it at first. But I do see great potential in it. 
So instead of complaining what is "cant do" or what I "dont like". I am going to help assist on the project and submit suggested changes, modifications or to help complete features that would make it a great UI; instead of just complaining about it.

Everyone can contribute to make Unity better even if you cant program or draw. Beta testing features consistently and submitting constructive feedback can help with over all stability and friendliness of the UI by a huge amount. Just complaining that you dont like it does nothing to help anyone.

Cheers,
Exo

### Post by glitterball on 2011-05-04
After 5 years with Ubuntu and Gnome, Unity got me to move to Kubuntu. To be honest, the default Kubuntu UI is pretty close to how I had Gnome working for me anyway (menu, window list, system tray on a panel at the bottom).

The worst thing for me was the global menu on a multi-monitor setup - that more or less made it unusable for me.
Second was the lack of customization options - that's why I use Linux in the first place.

It's one thing moving things around (e.g. placing the minimize, maximize, close buttons on the left), but it just annoys people if there are no options to change these elements back to the way that they are used to working.

Q. Why do we still use QWERTY keyboards when a more efficient layout could be used?
A. Because there would be too much disruption if we all had to learn something new.

### Post by michaelzap on 2011-05-04

> This is too much clutter. I mean why would you need two docks? 

If the Unity dock is as good as the Cairo Dock I shouldn't need the latter, but it is not so in Unity if I want the functionality of the Cairo Dock I would need to install the latter, but if I install the Cairo Dock why would I want to keep the Unity dock?? But I have to because at least in 11.04  I can't remove it!

+1

On my most recent Ubuntu Gnome install (Maverick), I ran an auto-hiding AWN dock at the bottom of the screen and used Compiz to cycle through windows and workspaces quickly (with a workspace switcher on the AWN dock also just in case I was drinking a hot cup of coffee at the time and felt like using the mouse instead of a keyboard shortcut). That worked very well for me, and was functionally equivalent to what I would get with Unity. Except for one major difference: I set up my system to work exactly as I wanted it to, not the way that the UI forced me to.

The other thing that I noticed is that Unity takes far more resources than my previous setup did, and I like its visual effects a lot less.

### Post by toupeiro on 2011-05-04
> **NCLI said:**
> Just like Canonical then. Obviously they're not switching unless it works properly.
Canonical has already made commitment to the switch.  Redhat is assessing it.  That is NOT a plan to switch, that is R&D..

> So if I install Gnome 2.XX, remove the panels, epiphany and nautilus, and use Nautilus-elementary and Docky instead, I'm not using a "Full DE"? If that's true, I have never used a full DE.

Again, I don't really care what *you* do, which is exactly what I said.  As a user, do what you want, but as a distro, which has enterprise customers, be practical in your environment and offer the full DE by default.


> And the people using those applications probably won't use Wayland for many years to come.
Then whats the point?  Clearly, its not ready.  Let it mature, but don't make your customers who don't want nor need it bear the abstraction bloat of that testing unless they volunteer to do so.

> You usually don't write applications for X, you write applications for GTK, Qt, java, etc. Which means that if those toolsets are updated to support Wayland, all applications which use them magically will as well.
Wayland is still an abstraction layer that does not fully support all calls to these libraries, and it leverages X HEAVILY today to fill the holes...  your applications will magically fail if wayland doesn't have X to wrap around unless ALL of that code changes.  You need to understand the graphical stack on linux better before making statements like this.  I made a career out of supporting it.  Anything that makes a call to X today needs to be recoded.


>  And other than in your fantasy world, no one is going to do that.

Whatever.  I've seen the Wayland documentation illustrating the full stack.  this is EXACTLY what it is...  They are wrapping their abstraction layer around the X abstraction layer to have full compatibility.  This is non-fiction, Drama is down the hall. :P

---

Better rely on Linux Counter, cause it asks that (What distro are you using?) [http://counter.li.org/reports/machines.php]("http://counter.li.org/reports/machines.php")

**Distribution**
122300 registrations entered 125344 values
[U]
Distribution -       Count -       Percent[/U]
arch linux -       1528 -       1.25% 	
archlinux -       786 -       0.64% 	
centos -       1816 -       1.48% 	
**debian** -       19393 -       15.86% (+1.48 + 0.56 = **17.9%**)	
debian lenny -       1810 -      1.48% 	
debian squeeze -       682 -       0.56% 	
fedora core -       7560 -       6.18% 	
gentoo -       7304 -       5.97% 	
kubuntu -       2025 -       1.66% 	
mandrake -       2946 -       2.41% 	
mandriva -       2440 -      2.00% 	
red hat -	       5420  -      4.43% 	
s.u.s.e -       9035 -       7.39% 	
slackware -       7785 -       6.37% 	
**ubuntu** -       31643 -       **25.87**% 	
Others -        23169 -       18.94%

Of course this is not exact and reflects only 125344 counts and we now much more people uses Linux! Enterprise versions may be quite under-rated there, but not only. Some popular distros are not listed (Mint, Mepis, PCLinuxOS, Knoppix, Puppy...).

### Post by angryfirelord on 2011-05-05

### Post by DIFTOW on 2011-05-05
I can not remember the exact version of Ubuntu I started on. I think 6.0.
Had the best theme though, the old safari theme with the cool looking bird.

Anyway, I've tried every version of Ubuntu since then and other than missing that old awesome wallpaper with the bird, I've been very pleased with the updates.

With Unity in 11.04, my feelings are mixed. While there are some cool features in Unity, I feel like navigation is very slow.

It adds a search function in a more "obvious" manner than previously, and while it is nice to have that, it only is good if you know what you're looking for.

Unity's start menu begins with 4 navigation options, and then shortcuts.

First off, it takes up the entire screen and everything is gigantic as if its meant for a small display or someone with terrible eyes who should think about glasses.

Secondly, it groups all programs into two categories. Media apps, and internet apps.

With the amount of space this launcher takes up, you'd think you could get more options from the start than that.

And why is everything 'app' now.

Between the navigational interface and the word 'app', I instantly am reminded of a smart phone interface like Android or iOS. I do not want Ubuntu to become a 16:9 1080p cell phone screen. Please D:

The classic interface has a much more compact, and easier way to navigate installed app_***lications***_ ;)

The shortcut bar, isn't too bad. Should be customizable (position) and toggleable though.

I don't mind the menu of the program/application running to integrate with the top bar, which is kind of Macintosh like. I'm not a fan of Mac, but it does save a little space in the height.

Honestly, my only issue with Unity is the launcher. I don't like it. It feels like it was made for an iPhone. v_v

If I could have all the Unity features, minus the launcher and replace it with the classic launcher.. I'd be quite happy.

Or at least make the Unity launcher less wasteful on space, and have more options upon first screen.

The sidebar and desktop can have shortcuts, so I don't think we need shortcuts in the launcher.

EDIT: Unity's launcher currently requires me to navigate through several interfaces just to access a category that lists what I'm looking for.

With the amount of space the launcher takes up, even on a smaller display like 800x600.. there would clearly be enough room to list every single application installed in an expanded tree view all on one page.

"More apps" is just absurd. Literally a giant icon that acts as a shortcut to basically one program.. Ubuntu Software Center.

"apps for download".. Do we really need that if we can just click Ubuntu Software Center if we want to download programs?

---

### Post by Shibblet on 2011-05-05
I have to say that I wasn't a big fan of unity in 10.10.  But now with this release in 11.04, it has changed my mind quite a bit.

My biggest irritation was the fact that you couldn't make the loader disappear.  Now it happily moves out of the way, or only shows up when you want it to.

There are a few things about Unity that I am still not crazy about.  Most of those are just re-learning a new interface.  I do not like the buttons (Exit, Minimize, and Maximize) on the left hand of the windows.  I have never been able to get used to it, even after trying for quite some time.  Fortunately Ubuntu-Tweak ppa:tualatrix/ppa allows me to change that, even in Unity.

My only other issues with Ubuntu seem to be program based.  Not really with the OS itself, but with it's choice of packages.

I don't like Empathy or Evolution.  There are too many problems with them.  Now, I know I can choose a different program like Pidgin and Thunderbird, and I usually do.  The only issue is the menu still has the "Chat" and "Set up Mail" options sitting there doing nothing.  I'd like an easy way to remove them.

I am still having a problem with mplayer... This issue has been around since 10.10 came out, and apparently NO ONE knows how to fix it. 

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1592917](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1592917)

So, I can't say Unity "Rocks", but I do like it.

---

### Post by mercurycc on 2011-05-05
Just like you, I started with Ubuntu 6.06.  Everything was just cool until Unity.

The best part that is missing in all of OS X/Unity/gnome 3 is the task bar.  I really like it because whatever you need will always sit at this fixed location, and once you launched something else you can switch back to it really fast.  I do not really understand how gnome3 would claim a visual switcher is faster.

When I use Windows shell/OS X/gnome2/3 I can easily go into my work and completely ignore the existence of the desktop environment.  For some reason that I cannot specify, I feel Unity is always in my way.

Tried to switch to gnome3 because there are still a lot of stuff I loved in Ubuntu.  Gnome 3 PPA isn't very stable though.  A apt-get upgrade can wipe out gnome.

I still have the chance to go back to gnome 2, but since gnome team is not going to develop it there isn't much reason not to take a step forward.  Desktop is a big thing for me.

Maybe Fedora 15 or Kubuntu.

### Post by DIFTOW on 2011-05-05
> **mercurycc said:**
> Just like you, I started with Ubuntu 6.06.  Everything was just cool until Unity.

The best part that is missing in all of OS X/Unity/gnome 3 is the task bar.  I really like it because whatever you need will always sit at this fixed location, and once you launched something else you can switch back to it really fast.  I do not really understand how gnome3 would claim a visual switcher is faster.

When I use Windows shell/OS X/gnome2/3 I can easily go into my work and completely ignore the existence of the desktop environment.  For some reason that I cannot specify, I feel Unity is always in my way.

Tried to switch to gnome3 because there are still a lot of stuff I loved in Ubuntu.  Gnome 3 PPA isn't very stable though.  A apt-get upgrade can wipe out gnome.

I still have the chance to go back to gnome 2, but since gnome team is not going to develop it there isn't much reason not to take a step forward.  Desktop is a big thing for me.

Maybe Fedora 15 or Kubuntu.

Well, the sidebar still does act like a taskbar, and I was able to use it to navigate but.. I do prefer Windows style taskbar.

If it wasn't for the option to disable Unity, I would have had to switch distros.

What I have been hearing is that Unity is intended for compatibility with netbooks and touch screens. Touch screens are over-rated, and netbooks have tiny displays.

Why should the interface of a desktop be forced to appeal to a netbook? Netbooks are tiny, they need their own interface.. there is no global solution. They suck, and can't handle a real desktop interface.

Touch screens, especially on smaller screens.. require big icons or big selection areas to avoid mistakes.

But what is the benefit of a touch screen, if you still need to type?

On screen keyboards... a complete joke, and so much for preserving screen space when one of those giant things have to come up just to type.

"but DIFTOW it is so cool.. I can open a file by touching the icon with my finger"...

Oh yeah really.. open Half-Life 2 and load the game. Let me know how that goes without a mouse. ;)

It is all a stupid trend, just like the 3-D craze.

Bottom line, they need to keep the classic style around permanently, and also make it the default for desktops.

People who don't like this Unity interface who aren't good with computers, are going to have a hard time figuring out how to change it to classic.

#1, they won't even know the option exists until they actually find it.

#2, the option doesn't appear in places existing computer users would think to look.

### Post by DIFTOW on 2011-05-05
> **sartic said:**
> Just say it. Unity sucks :)

It sucks.

Make it customizable. Make all of its features toggle-able.

Do that, and Unity is fine with me, but for new users who will no doubt have trouble figuring out how to change the UI.. make classic or easier.

If the program menu in Unity ([http://www.linuxfordevices.com/images/stories/ubuntu_1104_dash_shortcuts_sm.jpg](http://www.linuxfordevices.com/images/stories/ubuntu_1104_dash_shortcuts_sm.jpg))  is going to be default, than make that first screen better.

Media Apps, Internet Apps, More Apps, Find Files... Stupid setup, on top of the overly sized icons and the even more wasteful empty space in between.

We don't need _more apps_. People are fully capable of accessing the software center from a normal menu system or the sidebar shortcut.. or just using a web browser to download programs. We don't need a giant icon on the start page just to launch basically one program.

We don't need "find files", especially since there is a search bar directly above.
And the sidebar takes care of this as well. If you must have a find files in this menu.. does its icon really need to be this big and take up this much space?

The shortcuts below that are retarded if we already have shortcuts on the sidebar and desktop.

My god, who has a desktop just for web browsing, photos, music and email?
Lets get real here, stupid shortcuts. The sidebar does the job way better.. pick one, cause we don't need both.

What about videos, video editing, photo editing, games, game development, 3d modeling, programming, chat/call? Oh, I suppose no shortcuts for those things :3

How about if the default view MAINLY shows all installed programs, with small icons.

Can have 4-5 columns there as categories with a scroll window below for each.. listing immediately all the programs.

Maybe not the best idea I could come up with in a few minutes, but certainly less wasteful of space. Less pages to navigate through.

### Post by DIFTOW on 2011-05-05
I did this in photoshop really REALLY quickly, to illustrate how I feel the launcher should be.

Keep in mind I did this in an 800x400 image to demonstrate how it can fit even on smaller displays.

So there could be more columns, more spacing, icons to go along, etc...

[IMG]http://root.miriscus.com/unityfixed.png[/IMG]

You can clearly see that all your applications are immediately there for you to access.. all on one.. shall i say.. page?

You can instantly switch what is in the display to system related applications.. or to a device browser (HDDs, partitions, USB drives, etc)

This would just be a quick launch page. If you clicks the categories, it could bring up an expanded grid style view.

And "all programs" would do the same, but without categories.

Could customize how things are displayed in window, just like in Windows explorer.
Tiles, grid, icon size, details.. etc

This could also be horizontal instead of vertical.

It is nice when you can get what you're looking for fast, without having to create shortcuts or use shortcuts.

And this being all on one page to me, seems easier for people who have no computer experience, than the system Unity currently employs.

Anyway, take into consideration the layout I did there for the initial first page of the launcher. Remember, the scrolling can be nicer than that, and it can have icons too. There can be multiple columns of icons, grid view.. in each scroll area.. and bigger.. all depends on the display size.

> Also, Canonical seems dedicated to following the standards decidded upon through FD.org, whereas GNOME has many time shown that it really doesn't care, and would rather go it's own way.
SO Gnome, which is the leading DE (it and KDE), is going its own way. Your point?

> Because people would then have to adapt to change twice. Considering the response we see to the release of Gnome Shell and Unity now, that doesn't seem like a very good idea.

> Then you should *really* stick to the LTS releases. Frequent change and experimentation is the entire reason why we have nonLTS releases.

> That it is controlled by Canonical, which have shown me that they work faster than the Gnome team, and adhere to common standards.
I agree that this is a control issue. The antithesis of linux.

I also question whether Canonical should be applying its efforts to developing new DEs when it could be applying its efforts to making the Ubuntu experience much better, especially with regard to applications support and quality. Just look at the size of the bug list. It seems like a flight of fancy to focus on a DE when this is not the main purpose of an OS.

I would like to "choose" to disable the launcher. I can't... YET! :P

### Post by lukejanicke on 2011-05-05
After 1 week with Unity on my Lenovo S10-3c, I have to give it the kick. It LOOKS nice and seems to have some good features if you open the hood a little. However...

1. It killed my wireless network adapter. I had a workaround for 10.10 but now I'm screwed.

2. You're stuck with the default top bar/panel. I don't use half of that stuff and I want to customize the rest. But sadly it seems to not be a customizable Gnome "panel" anymore.

3. I don't like being forced to use a non-customizable left-side menu bar. I prefer bottom of the screen. Mac got that one right. And I don't want Docky/Awn running side-by-side with Unity menu.

FINALLY, and although no Internet is a show stopper, THIS is my biggest complaint...

4. If you're going to deeply integrate a search system for getting around the OS, there are two things you MUST include:
 A. By default, show results for Apps, Files AND Folders.
 B. If there's multiple folders/files with the same name in search results, indicate their locations so I know which one is the one I want.

Sheesh!

Nice effort taking a new direction but I'm gonna have to kick Unity and get Gnome 3.

### Post by sw22 on 2011-05-05

### Post by satanselbow on 2011-05-05
> **VinDSL said:**
> 
Is this what you're trying to accomplish?!?!?

Nope! Cos I now have no dash!

The (very simplistic) way I see it is this.

Unity has 2 components. 

#1 The Dash - which I like and find useful... even though typing "nautilus" or "Home`"draws a blank which is a bit weird... there are others noticable by their absence

#2 The Launcher - which I do not like. For my purposes (this is my machine still right?) It is in the wrong place and is wholey unnecessary given the general good function of the dash.

On one hand Unity is moving towards a more keyboard orientated way of working - with the dash - on the other it is providing a point and click dock - the launcher.

Something for everyone then right? Fair enough - but why is there no separation or ability to turn one or the other off? A pretty simple ask, really.

From and aesthetic design geek point of view the launcher and dash are not well integrated so why not make that separation part of the config - handing the "choice" back to the user with the ability to use one or other - instead of the current all or nothing which leaves you with Ubuntu Classic or hacking on Gnome 3 and the further issues that may bring.

Hey - it's an infant system rushed out to meet a preset deadline so something had to give - with this release it was a degree of choice in some of the most basic configuration options. 

The tweakers will rise - as they are already - and inherit the desktop ;)

Actually I feel Ubuntu is trying to save the desktop ui. You want to argue being forced and not listening to the user base, check out the GNOME team. They have a decade long track record of intentionally not listening to anyone at all and removing features because they was to complicated for someone with an IQ of 11.
Unity is still very new, but your options really should be listed as Unity, or GnomeShell. Gnome2 will still be around for a while, but only for legacy issues and for the server market until another rock solid server gui is released.

True Unity may not be for you at the moment. I will admit I am still very attached to classic Gnome2. But right now Unity devs are keeping it simple to keep the bugs down. More features and options will be added in time when they are stable enough. Just enjoy Gnome2 until you feel you are ready for Unity or find someone that pleases your taste. :-)

### Post by ghostborg on 2011-05-05
You can adjust the Unity launcher size through CompizConfig Settings Manager. Category Desktop in the Unity plugin and under the Experimental Tab. There is a slider for Launcher icon size.

I really miss how I had my top panel setup. My main problem is with the top panel takeover.

### Post by YesWeCan on 2011-05-05
> **Exodist said:**
> Actually I feel Ubuntu is trying to save the desktop ui. 
What do you mean by "save it"? Save it from what? To me the DE is the floor of my room. It's nice if it has an attractive finish but I don't care that much as long as it is solid and flat and the things I do care about...the furniture (applications)...are reliably supported.

I can understand the need to have a modified DE for small screen/no keyboard/no mouse hardware devices, such as tablets. So let's have a tablet DE option with touch-screen optimisation.  I am totally in favour of this if it gets me closer to having Ubuntu on iPad one day. :)

But leave the PC desktop alone. It is fit for purpose. Concentrate on improving the furniture.

### Post by michaelzap on 2011-05-05
> **handy said:**

It is incredibly difficult to gauge what the vast majority of Ubuntu users who aren't complaining on the forums are experiencing.

The number of people complaining about Unity seem to be a very small percentage of Ubuntu users.

### Post by ully-mick on 2011-05-05
The Unity concept isn't new. I started with Ubuntu 6.10. before that I was using xp with Astonshell. For those that don't know, Astonshell is a shell replacement for windows explorer. see examples here ([http://themes.astonshell.com/themes/page1.html](http://themes.astonshell.com/themes/page1.html)) it had sidebars, top or bottom bars. the sidebars could be either left or right, both or neither. the could have menus, submenus or just icons. it was fully customizable. unfortunately that seems to be whats lacking here.

A thing that puzzles me is in compiz settings under desktop, the unity plugin allows you to pick left, right, top,bottom or none for the side bar. but which ever you pick it is always on the left. so maybe thats a hint at the future.

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-05-05
> **Guillaumeb said:**
> Basically Now that 11.04 is published i would like to understand how Unity has been received by the users.


Well if you like application docks, haven't got a clue about object oriented GUI's and only use your computer for browsing and mail, you will probably think that Unity is great.

Unfortunately I hate docks (tried them and deleted them previously), use my computers for intensive photo editing, Desktop Publishing, Web site Management as well as the usual browser and mail.  My intensive use of the computer means I often have several applications and several data folders open at once, often working on multiple images or documents at once, and I appreciate an object oriented GUI that gives me options on how I work depending on what I am doing. In particular, the ability to open a minimised or hidden application by dragging a data file to the bottom task bar to open the application and drop the file into it or the document that is there.

For me, Unity was a complete train smash.  I can only hope that Gnome3 works out better and is a more mature product, however it will need considerable changes to suit me.  I don't generally waste time tweaking or playing with a GUI - if it's a good GUI then I think it should be very usable out of the box and give users options in how they want to work.

Chris.

### Post by michaelzap on 2011-05-05
Wow - Linux Insider pretty much tears Unity to shreds:
[http://www.linuxinsider.com/rsstory/72383.html](http://www.linuxinsider.com/rsstory/72383.html)

> "Ubuntu would have been far better off simply making a few add-ons to Gnome instead of spending piles of development time on yet another GTK-based desktop that really doesn't bring anything special to the table other than a lack of customization options," said Thoughts on Technology blogger Jeff Hoogland. "But hey, maybe some people are into that sort of thing."

> "Canonical has said they see the new interface attracting new users from that other OS," Pogson pointed out. "I see it as a betrayal of the tens of millions of current users. If we wanted a corporation to yank us around by the user interface, we would stick with M$ or Apple."

> In short, "what were they thinking?" Hudson mused. "Instead of trying to mimic Apple's 'Think Different' slogan from 1997, perhaps Canonical should go back 90 years to IBM's 'Think!' and work their way up?"

This included the application suite of software normally complimented on a Gnome based release. Being that many would not use them anymore (or as much) and would move to other things. Yes XFCE is a great option and it also uses the GTK. Unity feels more like an approach to keep everyone on GTK UI based apts that they have grown accustomed to, while at the same time still moving forward with improving on the Gnome2.0 base. Becoming one day the Unity DE and not a Unity mod of Gnome.

IMHO :-)

Exo

### Post by BertN45 on 2011-05-05
It is a promising new Gui, but it still needs work. Compared to the change from XP to VISTA, this change has been managed better.  Unavoidably it as some of the same issues, like:
- increased memory requirements
- decrease of responsiveness
- less tweaking opportunities
- more bugs also in other areas, because the main attention during testing has been the new desktop.

And of course most people don't like changes. It is the default human reaction, but Gnome 2 has been declared dead, even by the Gnome developers themselves. 

The Unity decision has been taken some time ago, so let's stop crying over spilled milk and follow the example of one of the English prime-ministers who said something like: Right or wrong, Unity!
Personally I think it is a small step backwards, to enable Ubuntu to make some big steps forwards in the coming releases.

### Post by FlameReaper on 2011-05-06
With the launchers having the ability to be hidden, that's a +1. That surely helps save some screen space with the title bar, menu and close/minimize/maximize buttons now integrated into the panel. 

But for me, having to wait for the launcher to come up when I hover the mouse to the left of the screen seems to be more painful than plain Docky (which in my opinion is what the Launcher seems to imitate in functionality), so the waste of screen space comes up again with my decision to unhide it.

Also, there should be a way to bring back tray icons and other panel stuff (I use the PanFlute applet way more than anything else in the panel, for example) in Unity. I have a lot of programs running in there and since I'm using SCIM and not IBus (for some reason, I just can't get to like IBus) the program sets itself in the panel tray and this doesn't show in Unity. Same goes for some other programs such as Audacious, Deluge and XChat as well.

Also, some tips for those who want to revert to their GNOME 2.x desktop with the default GNOME panels etc.

Once you run Unity, you'll be stuck with it even when you log in back to the non-Unity UI, especially if you have Compiz's Unity plugin enabled.

1. Disable it in ccsm (CompizConfig Settings Manager). Depending on your graphics driver etc your Unity UI might go funny, but don't worry.
2. Go into the terminal and kill these processes:
- unity-applications-daemon
- unity-files-daemon
- unity-panel-service
- unity-window-manager
3. And from the same terminal log out using the ```
gnome-session-save --logout
``` command.
4. Login back into Ubuntu Classic. Your ye olde panels etc will be back without Unity's UI interfering.
5. Beware as this will not make your Unity launch when you enter the default Ubuntu session (which is Unity). You might want to open up ccsm in terminal and re-enable the Unity plugin, and/or have a Unity launcher available on the desktop beforehand.

### Post by Bazon on 2011-05-06
Unity feels crippled. So many things which worked before are not possible anymore, not even as an option!

That is:

**Separation of windows by workspaces**
I'm used to use different workspaces for different contexts, but that does not work any more: The launcher displays representations from all workspaces:
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/689733](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/689733)
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/683170](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/683170)
The way Unity works may be useful if you used workspaces for separating applications before, but not if you use workspaces for separating contexts, e.g. having several pdf documents on several workspaces: Clicking the evince icon always shows all of them.

OK, super+w shows only windows from the current workspace, but how should one select one of them if you can't distinguish them as you can't read the content?
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/734253](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/734253)

**GUI elements lost function**
Where have all the right click context menus gone? Before, that was an easy way to set the properties of the selected element. But sadly, the approach in Unity seems to be a "configuration free" (M. Shuttleworth) interface. Meaning: You don't have properties any more as you can't configure things any more.

Not even the way things always worked before, as clicking an Icon to minimize a window:
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ayatana-design/+bug/733349](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ayatana-design/+bug/733349)

Or using middleclick-paste (or at least Ctrl+v) to paste in to Alt+F2:
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/736222](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/736222)



So for now, I only use Unity on my non-productive system, and there with the fantastic Cairo-Dock which gives you really great amount of options and therefore opportunities. 

On my productive Systems (including my netbook), I use 10.10 or 10.04 with Gnome 2 which simply works better.

### Post by NadirPoint on 2011-05-06
I would view this as a malformed perception:
> **Garthhh said:**
> it's shortsighted to ignore the rank & file in an effort to attract the Ipad crowd
Based on the following points:

1) People that post on this forum represent a small subset of all Ubuntu users.

2) People who post emotional, virile, and/or negative comments represent and even smaller subset of that crowd.

   2a) People with strong emotional responses to topics of their liking are more likely to post at all, as opposed to those with middling or positive feelings.

So at the end of the day you will find the group with strong anti-Unity sentiment is really a tiny fraction of the concerned user base - NOT the "rank and file."

### Post by canadianwriterman on 2011-05-06
**[SIZE="3"]Why I don&#8217;t like Unity[/SIZE]**

While I&#8217;ve test-driven many GNU/Linux distros, I&#8217;ve been a devoted Ubuntu user since Hoary Hedgehog was released 6 years ago. Ubuntu is my full-time desktop on both my MSI Wind netbook and my Zareason laptop.

I&#8217;m also a devoted GNOME user. The smooth, soft, roundedness of buttons and dialogue windows is just &#8220;comfy.&#8221; It&#8217;s like slipping into a pair of old, comfortable slippers. I&#8217;ve always believed that the gods at Canonical have always made changes that made my use of Ubuntu easier and more enjoyable (perhaps with the exception of moving the window controls to the left side, but even that I&#8217;ve come to accept). Then along came Natty, with its Unity interface, and my belief in the work of the gods has been shattered.

I&#8217;m a pretty advanced user, and I know there are workarounds to most of my issues with Unity, but I&#8217;m a bit of a purist. I believe that a distro should just work the way you want it to out of the box. If not, then the distro is not for you. Anyway, here are my issues.

**Overall desktop look and feel**
Perhaps it&#8217;s my age (59), but the Unity interface looks like a toy. I&#8217;m not a smart phone or tablet user, so I&#8217;m not comfortable with that &#8220;iconized&#8221; look. It seems okay on my netbook, because it already looks like a toy anyway. But on my laptop, it just looks silly. I know I can revert to the &#8220;Ubuntu Classic&#8221; interface, but it seems like a workaround that defeats the whole purpose of progressing to a newer version of Ubuntu. Also, I understand that Classic view will not be maintained after Natty.

**The Launcher bar**
Aside from its overall toy-like look, the actions of the launcher bar are just plain annoying. Every time I move my mouse to the far left, for example to the Firefox back button or the Evolution send button, out pops the launcher. It gets in the way all the time. It means always having to be extra careful of where the mouse pointer goes. Yes, I know I can change the settings to lock it in place, but it&#8217;s a workaround and it seems to defeat the intent of the designers and it means I have to look at those icons all the time.

**Launching apps**
There are now several ways to launch apps, but none is as convenient as the traditional application menu. I either have to click through several windows of giant icons to get where I want, or type the name of the app. It&#8217;s a real problem trying to remember how to spell Rhythmbox, to remember that the system process monitor is HTop. Limiting the number of icons to the &#8220;Most Frequently Used,&#8221; &#8220;Installed&#8221; (and then clicking again if it&#8217;s not one of those shown) and &#8220;Apps Available for Download&#8221; is a waste of space and inconvenient. Putting icons for all the apps I&#8217;m likely to use means a big accordion-fold stack of icons to sort through. It means finding apps is not as quick and easy as it used to be.

**Global Menu**
Most of the time, the Global Menu sits on top of the program name, partially blocking it. The menu items can blend into the program name. That means extra attention must be paid to &#8220;see&#8221; what you want to click on. That&#8217;s extra effort and a waste of time. Also, when using programs that appear in a small window that may be anywhere on the screen (such as Pidgin), I find that I have to move the mouse out of the active window, to the top of the screen, then back to the open window again. More extra time and effort. 

**Overlay scroll bars**
These sometimes appear where I expect them to be, sometimes not. They take extra effort to ensure the mouse is in exactly the right place when trying to click on them, or they just disappear. Sometimes it takes several tries to get and lock onto the overlay scroll bars.

**Weather indicator**
Personally, I love having the temperature and weather icon permanently on the top bar. It&#8217;s an effortless, always-visible reminder that I&#8217;ve come to depend on. It will not show on my laptop (oddly, I can get it to show on my netbook). I&#8217;ve installed &#8220;indicator-weather&#8221; and it is a running process, it simply won&#8217;t appear on the top bar. I know there have been issues with it for some people, and has been discussed several places in the forums, and I&#8217;ve followed all the recommendations, but nothing works. It just won&#8217;t appear.

**What to do?**
As I said, I can use a lot of workarounds. But then, what&#8217;s the use of using a distro that just doesn&#8217;t work for you out of the box? The design decisions have been made with other types of users in mind. Should I just stick around to be faithful, or look elsewhere. While I&#8217;m still using Natty in all its Unity &#8220;glory,&#8221; I&#8217;ve also installed Kubuntu alongside it. It looks great, but I&#8217;m not a big KDE fan. It doesn&#8217;t have the smooth simplicity and comfy feel of GNOME. And some of the apps have an overall jarring look (such as Amarok) that I find unappealing. 

I feel like a bit of an orphan wandering around looking for a new home. How do you feel?

---

### Post by Paulgirardin on 2011-05-06
Unity is excellent.

I installed yesterday.After tweaking it to my taste - making the top
panel fully transparent and making the launcher hide at all times ,I find that it is very good.
 
I'm tending to use the keyboard to open all files and apps as the search ability is so fast.
 
I have added a small Cairo - Dock to the right boarder .This has added
functionality as the Cairo workspace switcher operates on mouse over with the scroll wheel and Tomboy notes opens on mouse over,I also can close apps with this extra dock.
I have also added the shutdown applet to the Cairo-dock.

If you don't want to use the launcher,make it hide all the time and launch via the dash.
People have commented about the number of keystrokes it takes to open some things,but I find that once the dash has learned your favorites apps or files is usually a minimal number of keys away.I find it is much faster that the menu/mouse method.

Based on the following points:

1) People that post on this forum represent a small subset of all Ubuntu users.

2) People who post emotional, virile, and/or negative comments represent and even smaller subset of that crowd.

   2a) People with strong emotional responses to topics of their liking are more likely to post at all, as opposed to those with middling or positive feelings.
```

```
### Post by Sean Hayes on 2011-05-07
I agree. Unity is supposed to unify the desktop environments across desktops, notebooks, tablets, netbooks, etc., but the problem is it gives my 16" laptop screen the usability of 10" netbook. Interfaces for these devices should be different because they have different capabilities and different use cases. This new interface may be better for casual users who need to quickly browse with Firefox, but for power users and developers who frequently use a large variety of applications it's suboptimal.

I also don't like the auto generated list of MRU programs. In Classic if I use something often enough that going through the menus pisses me off, I can just add a launcher to a panel and it always stays in the same place, but MRU lists constantly change. It's just another solution in search of a problem.

### Post by michaelzap on 2011-05-07
> **canadianwriterman said:**
> I feel like a bit of an orphan wandering around looking for a new home. How do you feel?

You might want to give Xfce a try. I've switched my main system to (Debian-based) Crunchbang Xfce, and it's essentially a faster, more efficient Gnome 2. It doesn't have all of the bells and whistles of Gnome (although I'm not missing anything), so try it and see what you think.

Linux Mint Xfce might be a better option for you than Crunchbang because it uses the testing repos (Crunchbang uses stable), which means you'll get Xfce 4.8 right away (which includes some major improvements, such as automatic network share browsing like Nautilus has).

Linux Mint's next Ubuntu-based release (out this month) will feature a "classic" Gnome desktop, so you could use that for a while and see what comes down the pike in the next six months.

I definitely understand why you feel a bit like an orphan. Gnome is changing to Shell (which I think is better than Unity, but it shares many of the issues that you don't like about Unity), so pretty soon Gnome 2 will be no more. Hopefully you find that you really like Xfce and don't miss the old Gnome, cause you can never go Gnome again (painful pun intended).
```

```
### Post by robert shearer on 2011-05-07
> **canadianwriterman said:**
> **[SIZE="3"]Why I don&#8217;t like Unity[/SIZE]**
-snip-

**The Launcher bar**
Aside from its overall toy-like look, the actions of the launcher bar are just plain annoying. Every time I move my mouse to the far left, for example to the Firefox back button or the Evolution send button, out pops the launcher. It gets in the way all the time. It means always having to be extra careful of where the mouse pointer goes. Yes, I know I can change the settings to lock it in place, but it&#8217;s a workaround and it seems to defeat the intent of the designers and it means I have to look at those icons all the time.


Just a little research would have found you the solution for this...

install compiz-config settings manager, open it and find the Unity plugin.
There in the 'reveal mode' select top left and deselect left.
Now the launcher won't show when you mouse **far left** and will show only when you mouse **very top left corner**.

Simple eh.

and while you are there change the icon size to small and it won't look so 'toy-like' or stack so much
```
