Making a statement based on the one year time frame while ignoring the part where he indicates he would like to be proven wrong in the 6 month direction, but more likely he is wrong in the opposite direction is what makes it out of context.

I understand 'exactly correct or accurate' as *the* definition of 'dead on', I don't understand *your* usage of the term. 

Later, Seeker


For someone who focus so much on context, you are sure taking me out of context...

> If it ends up in scope for 12.04 (by projection, the next LTS), and everything in his post from a timing perspective says it certainly might, then I think I'm pretty dead on...

Lets take the first word, ***IF***.  If is the beginning of a *conditional statement.* Another word, following the comma, is ***THEN,*** which generally starts a resultant set of actions or statements.  If you read the _whole sentence_, it doesn't imply anything to be exactly correct or accurate on its own, it implies that I **_think_** that most of what I've researched and talked about will happen, **_if_** certain conditions are made to exist.  I can always be wrong, just like Mark or anyone else can, and I'll have no issues admitting if I am wrong when/if the time comes for this matter.  If anyone is taking people out of context, its you.

As I said, I have made a career out of supporting high end graphics solutions on linux.  I have to care about the technicalities over the opinions.  With X, and with anything for that matter, that means working with a lot of third party companies on how they leverage the current graphics stack on linux as well as open source toolsets built for the stack.  I've read many whitepapers on wayland.  And aside from everything I said about its relation/isolation to X, it also has extremely limited vendor hardware support.  The timeframes Mark is shooting for for a widely usable solution are simply unreasonable.  Just because canonical is behind it doesn't mean hardware vendors like ATI and NVidia, or software vendors which do high end graphics on linux like Dynamic Graphics Inc, or the project behind tools like blender, have put rewriting their code to make Marks opinion blog a reality over the primary function of their toolsets..  I've said in many threads (probably now all merged into this one) That wayland needs a tremendous amount of support from the software AND hardware world to succeed.  It doesn't have that today.  The cart is way before the horse on this project.  To answer your question, yes, I do have the wayland source code, understand how its structured, and know how to compile it.  Unfortunately, I am having hardware compatibility issues with actually trying to use it. You are more than welcome to it yourself. [GIT repo]("http://cgit.freedesktop.org/wayland")

NCLI> I've done research on the matter, and taken time to understand the issues, as it relates to trying to legitimately support wayland in a world of development on X.  Shuttleworth may have the final say in ubuntu, that has absolutely nothing to do at all with Waylands readiness...**_ I'm not saying it will NEVER happen_**, I'm simply saying Shuttleworths timing is way off.   If, as you so arrogantly put it, my issues were dealt with, I really wouldn't have anything to say.  If you choose not to research the issues for yourself, or respond anymore to me, thats your prerogative.  Lets resurrect this thread around ubuntu 12.04 and see where things are... ):P

---

### Post by ottosykora on 2011-05-07
Actually I can not understand why the linux folks try to contruct something like unity. So far I had mostly the impression they are so keen on command line work...

But the unity is some kind of product not for serious work, it is just result of some work done by the smart phone generation. Smart phones are also just gadgets for facebook generation and not for anything else.

I am not sure why there are some configuration tools available only when one asks and searches for, why they are not delivered with it? One idea is, that we are not supposed to change anything , we should get used to this smart phone, the big brother simply wants it so, who ever it is. 
Or why we have the build in advertising engine in it? OK, at the moment every time one tries to open an app, advertising for number of apps will appear. Reminds me very much of googles advertising on almost any website. Difference: on websites you can get rid of it, here this is OS, we can not stop it.

The system is running 'zeitgeist', a powerful logging engine, collecting everything the user does with its computer to a big database. If we disable this, the whole operating system becomes useless.
No, don't tell me this is for users convenience. Someone out there is hot after users behavior, his style of work, what software he uses, which websites he visits, what mails he does sent, which picts he watches on his computer most often, what music he listens to....

Was this not what we all criticized on the big 'competitors' systems? Apple is criticized for storing gps data of the smart phone, so who is pushing open source community to produce a gps interface to zeitgeist logging engine as well? Who is interested so much where a user is doing certain task on his PC so much that he might even be able to indirectly force open source folks to develop such feature?
Who is the one now wanting all that being also available from linux users?

> Install compiz-config settings manager, open it and find the Unity plugin.

Now the launcher won't show when you mouse **far left** and will show only when you mouse **very top left corner**.

Simple eh.

and while you are there change the icon size to small and it won't look so 'toy-like' or stack so much

### Post by Nick3193 on 2011-05-07
> **canadianwriterman said:**
> **[SIZE="3"]Why I don’t like Unity[/SIZE]**

...

**Launching apps**
There are now several ways to launch apps, but none is as convenient as the traditional application menu. I either have to click through several windows of giant icons to get where I want, or type the name of the app. It’s a real problem trying to remember how to spell Rhythmbox, to remember that the system process monitor is HTop. Limiting the number of icons to the “Most Frequently Used,” “Installed” (and then clicking again if it’s not one of those shown) and “Apps Available for Download” is a waste of space and inconvenient. Putting icons for all the apps I’m likely to use means a big accordion-fold stack of icons to sort through. It means finding apps is not as quick and easy as it used to be.

**Global Menu**
Most of the time, the Global Menu sits on top of the program name, partially blocking it. The menu items can blend into the program name. That means extra attention must be paid to “see” what you want to click on. That’s extra effort and a waste of time. Also, when using programs that appear in a small window that may be anywhere on the screen (such as Pidgin), I find that I have to move the mouse out of the active window, to the top of the screen, then back to the open window again. More extra time and effort. 

...

**Weather indicator**
Personally, I love having the temperature and weather icon permanently on the top bar. It’s an effortless, always-visible reminder that I’ve come to depend on. It will not show on my laptop (oddly, I can get it to show on my netbook). I’ve installed “indicator-weather” and it is a running process, it simply won’t appear on the top bar. I know there have been issues with it for some people, and has been discussed several places in the forums, and I’ve followed all the recommendations, but nothing works. It just won’t appear.

...

I feel like a bit of an orphan wandering around looking for a new home. How do you feel?

This sums up very nicely my initial impressions of Unity.  The lack of customization options is shocking to me.  One of the great benefits of Linux (in my opinion) has always been that an experienced user can get it to be exactly how they like it.  Everything could be tweaked, and usually pretty easily.  This can make it hard on newbies since the number of options can get overwhelming, but to solve that by removing almost all the customization?  What is this, Windows?  I shouldn't have had to download a special tool just to resize the icons in the launcher.

Not only do I miss the weather indicator at the top of my screen, but other useful applets I had there.  Mouse following eyes (very helpful with a big screen and small cursor), system monitor to quickly see if something is hogging resources, etc.  Any way to get them back?

The application launching is going to be the biggest pain from what I see. It would really help if one could easily remove apps from the provided lists.  I also see several application listed that are no longer installed and multiple links to the same application.  It found the old menu entries for them and now they clutter up the long (alphabetically sorted) list of all my applications.  And, as someone else pointed out, it's a real waste of space to show me apps available for download.  If I want them, I'll find them, and what I've seen so far I don't want.

But I have faith that there will be improvements.  Unity is a step backwards, but it will get better.  Hopefully quickly.

### Post by Ludd1te on 2011-05-07
I realize it's still a work in progress (and the constant evolution is one of the strengths of Ubuntu and its community), but . . .

The Bad
--------

- I use dual monitors, and shuffle windows around a lot; I've yet to figure out how to move them under Unity.  Alt-F7 doesn't move them, and I can't grab the menu bars to drag them.  Maybe one can, but I haven't after more trying than it should take.

- When windows (or dialogue boxes) open, they open behind the menu bar, cutting off the top of sometimes important messages

- Yeah, weather, system monitor, etc. _somewhere_ if not in the menu bar

The Good
---------

- That said, I do like the more efficient use of space in having the menu bar use that top space.

- Using, e.g., Alt-F2 and then a few key strokes to find a program is nice--if they're not to be easily found elsewhere (searching the Unity bar is a lot of mousing)

### Post by tizza42 on 2011-05-08
I've been an Ubuntu enthusiast since Karmic Koala.  I've been in the IT industry for decades - just laying the foundation for what I'm about to say.

I think the move to Unity is presently providing a very poor user experience.  I have had to battle with several instances of quite strange operational changes.

I'm also not convinced that the switch from OpenOffice to LibreOffice has been well timed or tested.  Because I have to use Microsoft Office at work, it is necessary for me to use a solution that can handle Microsoft files - like .docx - correctly.

When opening a docx file with LibreOffice I find so many formatting inconsistencies that it's pretty much unusable - whereas OpenOffice appeared to have far less shortcomings in this area.

In summary - I believe that Natty Narwhal has introduced many usability problems which may have a detrimental affect - particularly on new users checking it out.

There needs to be some focus on at least the 2 areas I mentioned above:
- stability of Unity Windows management, and
- LibreOffice support for the newer Microsoft Office file formats

I'd like to hear how other people have faired with Natty - or is it just me?

---

I always do a fresh install, no upgrades, and use the alternate install CD.

### Post by Sand &amp; Mercury on 2011-05-08
First thoughts using Natty:

1: Oh my god this is utterly hideous.

2: Where are all the options, and what happened to 'Places'?

3: Where's the menu bar?

Initial tests confused the hell out of me; suddenly I had no idea where to go for anything or what I was looking at much of the time -- in comparison, while pure GNOME took some a bit of getting used to when I first tried settling with Ubuntu back in the 7.04 days, the only things I was learning was the concepts of packages instead of EXEs and such -- not grappling with how to start the file manager or even change basic settings.

Also the very first thing I'm greeted with upon starting 11.04 for the first time was a message using the fallback GTK theme saying "You don't have the necessary hardware to run Unity" -- when AAMOF I definitely have more than enough in that regard, it's just the noveau driver doesn't cut the mustard for it yet. It all made sense to me, but for a new user with a brand spanking new laptop such a message would be really misleading and even a bit demeaning.

Ubuntu's still a serviceable distro under the hood but at the moment it's like opening the door to your nice new Ford only to find half the dash is missing and they've swapped the accelerate and brake pedals around and the ignition is under the seat, and the bonnet and windscreen are caked in pigeon crap. Only with some considerable rearranging and cleaning up do you end up with something you can really enjoy using, and there's a lot of people who just aren't gonna bother. I certainly didn't and I'm quite happily typing this post on Windows 7 without any temptation to give it a second glance whatsoever.

I have installed it on an Intel i7 with Nvidia graphics, an Intel Atom Mini-ITX board, an older AMD laptop, and a VIA C7 board all with no problems.  I always do a fresh install, no upgrades, and use the alternate install CD.
I'm sorry but why "upgrade" to 11.04 at all if you are gonna have to "tweak" it so much?

11.04 and more precisely Unity, is hard on resources and it takes away customizability which is one of the things I love the most about Ubuntu. It felt Like the latest release of MS Windows.

  After 4 days of agony I went back to 10.10 , considered going back to 10.04 but figured, if Ubuntu is bent on pushing Unity I will just migrate to a different Distro when support for 10.10 ends.

---

### Post by FlameReaper on 2011-05-08
I'm getting more curious:

So apparently we have waves (read: waves) of people who don't like Unity. I'll frankly state that I'm neutral but I thought it was a refreshing move, if nothing new at all.

Possible reasons I can think of why there are lots of dislike:

1. Very little to no flexibility of customization, changing the GTK themes, window decorators etc won't even cut it to begin with (joke mode on: HARDCORE MODE PLEASE), it needs to be able to be tweaked as users please. Not to mention I noticed Emerald is a bit broken in 11.04 with every attempt to launch it ends up with a segfault, and I prefer that I won't have to compile from git; since the ports git uses to pull from git repositories are blocked in where I am (campus).

2. Very taxing on hardware, even KDE looks like a speedy champion (Yes, you'll never believe it) compared to it

3. Just outright looks unpleasing to look at (now that's quite a very high emphasis on aesthetics for some people I guess)

However, from what I'm seeing it, it's as if there's this impression Ubuntu ditched GNOME 2.x altogether and made it compulsory for everyone to use Unity, I don't know how I got this conjecture but it's just what I thought. Well, it's a good thing you can still login to the good old plain GNOME desktop, isn't it? Or maybe there's something else? At least you have your customizations you thought you were going to miss in Unity.

Simply put, Unity is but an option, you can either choose to log in into it or leave it. I used to uninstall Unity but then again after figuring out things I got it installed again. And everything turned out fine for me after that.

... With Ubuntu going into Unity and there's GNOME 3 where I'm noticing there's quite some dislike for it (I used to not like it, but now I'm rather curious to try it again) although not as vocal as Unity. Perhaps I guess someone (you better hope it will happen) will go and do a GNOME 2 Legacy version for the sake of users who don't want to make the jump.

### Post by DZ* on 2011-05-08
> **powerpleb said:**
> Yes, but he was wondering, as am I, why it says "Activated but not in use".

I solved that by adding
```
alias nvidia nvidia-current
``` 
to /etc/modprobe.d/nvidia-graphics-drivers.conf

Some people would rather have workarounds available for them rather than figuring a workaround themselves. and if I were to follow the so-called User-Centered System Design (UCSD) principle this is already a minus factor in GUI design by miles.

And it seems that they didn't manage it. Some lived through it while some others just gave up. 

It's like Canonical set up a new maze game, and see how users cope with it. And the results were pretty predictable enough: some loved playing Canonical's maze, others were stuck while some others say that the maze game sucks, they wanted something else or just don't want it at all.

But then when someone gives you a game that doesn't mean you have to play it right? At least you won't disturb those who liked it and want to play it again.

### Post by Ludd1te on 2011-05-08
To answer to my own post (yeah, I guess I talk to myself in public too much), I _was_ able to move windows, etc. after going into compiz and check Move Windows, etc.  Odd that they had become unchecked.

Note that with the menu bar replacing the usual place to grab a window, one has to use key combos (by default Alt-F7) to move maximized windows for some programs.

Anyway, I admire the ambition and innovation that went into Unity.  It was just a big jump into somewhat untamed lands just when Ubuntu was gaining real street cred for being accessible by GNU-noobs like me.

I enjoy learning new things and am very grateful to benefit from other's laudable ingenuity (like I could EVER build something like Ubuntu!).  But I do still have to work on this machine--and on more than re-learning an interface while I patch up and in things I relied on to help me get things done.

### Post by neu5eeCh on 2011-05-08
> **kostageas said:**
> I said I liked Unity.  I said that the people that hated it were smoking something and that this was a great step forward, and it was.  I still love the concept, but I found a major flaw that's hard for me to live with.  I use more applications than will fit on the side dock thing, daily.  And it is a huge pain in the *** to click the ubuntu logo menu thing, and SEARCH for it.  I miss the three menus.  Unity is pretty damn cool, but this . . . sucks.

What I didn't like about the "side dock thing" was the following: I use the all-in-one sidebar add-on and I'm used to having it on the left side (so it isn't activated when I mouse for the scrollbar on the right) and minimized.

With the "side dock thing" glued to the left, every time I moused over for all-in-one, the *** "side dock thing" would pop up. Unity is a work in progress and it needs **lots **of work.

---

### Post by robert shearer on 2011-05-08
> **VTPoet said:**
> What I didn't like about the "side dock thing" was the following: I use the all-in-one sidebar add-on and I'm used to having it on the left side (so it isn't activated when I mouse for the scrollbar on the right) and minimized.

With the "side dock thing" glued to the left, every time I moused over for all-in-one, the *** "side dock thing" would pop up. Unity is a work in progress and it needs **lots **of work.

That is so easy to remedy.
Install compiz-config settings manager and open the Unity plugin within it.

In  'Reveal'  select top left and deselect left.
Then the launcer ('side dock thing' to you :) ) will only show when you mouse **right into the top left corne**r.
Now you can mouse left anywhere else for your 'all-in-one sidebar' (whatever that is...;) )

cheers Bob.

---

### Post by michaelzap on 2011-05-08

On both systems I'm experiencing far more trouble with LibreOffice than I do with oO. I've also seen totally whacked Microsoft .doc and .docx formatting in LibreOffice that I don't recall ever seeing in oO.

So I really want to use and support LibreOffice, but there are some pretty serious issues with it at the moment that make it borderline unusable for me.

### Post by powerpleb on 2011-05-08
My major gripes with Unity/Natty are the following bugs:
I can't use the numbered shortcuts until I've opened a program that isn't on the dock.
Tiling shortcuts leave little gaps between windows (doesn't effect functionality but looks shoddy).
Sometimes windows seem to partially move across into different desktops, frustrating the use of numbered shortcuts.
Gapless playback seems to have disappeared in Banshee and Rhythmbox.
Periodic freezing and graphical weirdness that comes with having to use compiz.

Other than them, I have to say I really like Unity. I hate menus, I've always been a keyboard shortcut and search bar kind of guy.

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-09
To avoid those "every 6 months" releases which always appear to present tons of problems that require corrective action ... often not even completed by the next release 6 months later ... while now stuffing unwanted Apps a'la Microsoft into my Desktop Environment, I've decided to start working with the core of Ubuntu instead, this being Debian. I am totally amazed how incredible & stable Debian is. I can continue to use Gnome 2 for the next 1 - 2 years while getting used to Gnome 3 on my own terms/time after it's been completely polished up. The only difference that I can tell between Debian and Ubuntu, is that my Debian 6.0 actually appears to work smoother and in some instances a tad faster than Ubuntu. Compiz 3D works beautifully too. From now on I'll be using Ubuntu in a Debian Virtual Box ... check out the screen shots ....

Word of warning though, installation of Debian 6.0 works best if you already have your partitions ready to go, one for the system, one for your data, and a small swap partition. Took me 3 days total to get everything, and I do mean everything set up 100% perfect (had some xserver & driver issues) but now I'm happy as a clam!

.

---

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-05-09
> **WinRiddance said:**
> To avoid those "every 6 months" releases
[...]
Environment, I've decided to start working with the core of Ubuntu instead, 
[...]
amazed how incredible & stable Debian is. I can continue to use Gnome 2 for the next 1 - 2 years while getting used to Gnome 3 on my own terms/time after it's been completely polished up.
[...]

To a large extent you could say the same about the LTS releases of Ubuntu.  If you stay with 10.04 LTS you have support until April 2013.  The next LTS is due out April 2012 which gives you a whole year to evaluate it before changing.

It would take a truly outstanding six monthly release to coax me away from LTS as my main OS. I do have a couple of partitions set up for testing new releases and different distros so I'm not against change as such.  The best thing about the LTS is that the improvements like better hardware support does filter back down from later releases - the wireless network in my Eee PC works better now than it did a year ago for example.

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-10
> **fabricator4 said:**
> The best thing about the LTS is that the improvements like better hardware support does filter back down from later releases - the wireless network in my Eee PC works better now than it did a year ago for example.

Chris

Well, that better hardware support is exactly what I've been waiting for, ever since I started with Ubuntu back during version 9.04 and then upwards. Don't get me wrong, the compatibility is pretty darn amazing, but I've installed several versions of Ubuntu on 25 to 30 machines and have never had a flawless installation. One way or another, there was always some fiddling involved. Even worse, sometimes a newer release would have problems on the same machine that didn't experience a particular set of problems with an earlier release. Wifi, need I say more? How can that be? Whatever happened to "backwards compatibility" which is supposed to be standard?

As far as Debian is concerned, just the initial CD installation was a bit more frustrating. Not a big deal though. Then the xserver/graphic chip ... what a pain in the butt, just like ATI chips with many if not most Linux distros. Part of my frustration also came from me screwing up some things as a result of lacking knowledge. Can't really blame Debian for that, right? But you know what? At the end of my 3 days I had better speed & stability than I've seen with Ubuntu in a long time, no kidding. Granted, Debian has Iceweasel as opposed to Firefox and Icedove as opposed to Thunderbird, but those are basically mozilla created clones just so they're branded to Debian. Importing my bookmark backups, email accounts, and passwords was no problem at all. The performance difference was actually noticeable right from the get-go. It's a sheer pleasure to work with Debian, at least in my opinion.

You can only improve things so far before you cross the line and start regressing. I believe Ubuntu started that regression with version 10.10 and it's become full-blown with the newest version. All I can say is ... stop rolling out every 6 months and take a whole year for once so all of those hundreds of quirky things that people have been living with for years get fixed once and for all. Besides, it's not really "Linux" anymore if you start dumpings things on people by default, whether they want it or not. Keeping Gnome 2 shouldn't be the option ... **it's getting rid of Unity that should be optional**.

---

### Post by roachk71 on 2011-05-10

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2011-05-10
Unity will shake things up a lot, but I think that anyone who Unity works for now won't have to worry for the foreseeable future. Ie. I can't think of any hardware issues you'd have in the future if you already have OpenGL 2 support on Compiz- there's not a whole lot beyond that, visually. Maybe 10 years from now I'll be eating my words, but even if you can't deal with it from a hardware perspective, Xfce4 isn't all that different from Gnome 2, and it may have a few suprises you'll be delighted with as well.

I also don't expect we'll go very long after 11.10 without a popular Gnome 3 derivative. It seems inevitable to me- heck, I can put it together in an afternoon if no one else is up for it.

### Post by Applegeek on 2011-05-10
Here's the deal:  When you want to make a major design revision it has to be:  Faster, Cheaper and Better.

Unity is not faster, especially on a netbook where its a pain to scroll anyway.  And you may or may not have a middle mouse button.

Unity isn't better:  Nothing is categorized?  Maybe I don't see how to do it but why do I have to see all programs (mis-named "Apps") in one giant pile??  Why is it offering me stuff to install that I don't want?  I can't seem to turn that off.

Its also inconsistent:  If you set windows theme to have control boxes in the upper right, then when you maximize (under Unity) they jump to the upper left...Who thought this was a good idea??  Keep it consistent!  Don't make me look for control boxes each time.  Leave them where I put them, please!

I'm not sure how to categorize cheaper, except if my time is valuable this sure wasted a lot of it.  I tried as along as I could stand it, about 3 hrs, so that is time totally lost exploring something I had hopes for.  Oh Well.

I am really, really disappointed that Unity is set for the default manager, it took just a bit to realize the reliable classic Gnome interface was available - which works fine.

All i would suggest when releasing the experimental stuff:  Make the experimentals an option - don't default to these controls if the new interface isn't ready for prime time.

---

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-05-10
> **WinRiddance said:**
> Don't get me wrong, the compatibility is pretty darn amazing, but I've installed several versions of Ubuntu on 25 to 30 machines and have never had a flawless installation. One way or another, there was always some fiddling involved.


My experience of installing LTS is quite different.  My main problem is that I run through a KVM switch for my main box, and it doesn't pass the monitor EDID information correctly so Ubuntu (and Windows) can't set the correct screen resolution without help. For 10.04 LTS my older hardware is no problem, and if a few things are a bit quirky on my laptops and netbooks it doesn't change the fact that they are perfectly usable and I'm much happier running Ubuntu.  Similarly I've converted quite a few people to LTS simply because it was a 20 minute install and the system just worked the way it was supposed to.  In a dozen installs I've not had one system that was not stable and usable.  It does seem that is going to change however, with my older more useless graphics cards not really being suitable for future releases.  I will still install and recommend 10.04 LTS for people who want a change from their current bug and trojan ridden OS.

>  
You can only improve things so far before you cross the line and start regressing. I believe Ubuntu started that regression with version 10.10 and it's become full-blown with the newest version. 

You could also say that that's where we are in the development cycle.  It's always been that way, and always will be while the 6 month development cycle is being followed.  Just because it's the latest release doesn't mean you have to upgrade your main workhorse to it - the exact opposite if anything.  I'd only be disappointed if the next LTS release was not a successful one.

> 
All I can say is ... stop rolling out every 6 months and take a whole year for once so all of those hundreds of quirky things that people have been living with for years get fixed once and for all.


The intention of the LTS release is for those who want the Debian-like stability.  If you stop following the six monthly releases you don't have to keep finding fixes and workarounds if you don't want to. Plus, enhancements to modules do filter back, so it actually gets better over the course of its supported life, not worse. In this respect the LTS release benefits greatly from the impetus of the energetic development cycle.

>  Besides, it's not really "Linux" anymore if you start dumpings things on people by default, whether they want it or not. Keeping Gnome 2 shouldn't be the option ... **it's getting rid of Unity that should be optional**.

I agree with you, but the old Gnome is dead as far as new Ubuntu releases go.  10.04 LTS is good for a few years yet.

It's a shame that the Gnome3 devs and Canonical couldn't find some way to work together - I'm sure a collaborative effort could have achieved much greater things.  Not much ubuntu there eh?

Chris.

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-10
> **bouncy93 said:**
> I don't know if this is the right place to ask, but I have a problem with alt-tab switching. I am with Ubuntu 11.04, using Unity, card nvidia9500(512MB) and CPU Intel5200(2x2.5gh). I use Static Application Switcher(from ccsm) but it's extremely slow. When I alt-tab it takes over a second to switch to the next window. I tried to disable Static Application Switcher and enable only Application Switcher but it's the same - slower than my grandma. I searched the net but I couldn't find a solution. Any help would be good. Thanks :)

Using proprietary drivers? If you have an Nvidia or ATI control panel try to change the HZ setting of your display. If it's set to Auto change it to 60 instead. If it's set to 60 change it to Auto instead. What you're describing is exactly what happened to me. I saw it on someone else's ATI laptop and just recently I experienced the same thing on my desktop system with Nvidia graphic drivers. Heck, my Nvidia drivers were the linux recommended ones ... :confused:

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-10
> **fabricator4 said:**

It does seem that is going to change however, with my older more useless graphics cards not really being suitable for future releases.  I will still install and recommend 10.04 LTS for people who want a change from their current bug and trojan ridden OS.

**[COLOR=DarkRed]4. [/COLOR]**It's a shame that the Gnome3 devs and Canonical couldn't find some way to work together - I'm sure a collaborative effort could have achieved much greater things.  Not much ubuntu there eh?

Chris.

[COLOR=DarkRed]**1. **[/COLOR]Well, I wasn't saying that they systems weren't usable. All of them were perfectly usable after some fiddling, and in a few cases with a lot of fiddling. That's exactly my point though, just about anyone can set up Windows 7 these days but the same **still** doesn't hold true for Ubuntu. All it takes is an installation without working WiFi and bamm, off you go on another machine looking for solutions. That's unacceptable for an OS that's based on "Humanity & User Friendliness" and it's unacceptable because it's a problem that persists with every version of Ubuntu, even LTS releases. Just look at the forums, other linux forums too ... WiFi and Graphic drivers are an ongoing issue. Sure, you and I can deal with these issues, but what about my Mom & Dad or any other Windows user with little to no computer experience who wants to make that switch? Using Debian for the past week has taught me one thing in no uncertain terms ... How to scratch my head and ask myself what all of the Ubuntu hype is all about? **Did you see my earlier screenshots** ... ???

[COLOR=DarkRed]**2. **[/COLOR]The 10.04 LTS release was so hyped up that even some news channels were talking about it days before the actual release. Finally someone for MS to seriously contend with. Well, from my point of view there really wasn't much difference between Ubuntu 9.10 and 10.04. Matter of fact, 9.10 is the one that worked the smoothest for me "right out of the box" so to speak. After 10.04 was released you could read in the forums how many people were disappointment and that 10.04 did not meet the hype or most people's expectations.

[COLOR=DarkRed]**3. **[/COLOR]I haven't used Debian long enough yet to talk about stability, but I'll say this ... I've had Ubuntu crash or freeze on me so often with newer as well as older machines that I started installing the "force to quit app" on the panel by default as soon as I finished an installation. _Ubuntu is perfectly fine without propritary graphics drivers_ but guess what, that's what most people want. These days everyone wants to play the hottest games or use 3D this and 3D that. It's the graphic driver issues and WiFi issues with every version of Ubuntu that's made me wonder when this "Human OS" is finally going to turn human enough to wipe out just those two particular problems? Again, look at the forums, heck just this forum is enough ... literally thousands of posts with graphic driver issues. **Makes Windows and Mac look so much better** to anyone who doesn't know any better ....
My primary disappointment with Ubuntu stems from the fact that I've been waiting close to 3 years for graphic driver and WiFi issues to go away, so I can tell everyone who will listen to me how "mind boggling awesome, cheap, safe, and reliable" Ubuntu really is. But I can't do that because at this point I don't think that those issues will ever get resolved. Forget about you and me, I'm thinking about the Ubuntu aspect of it, the "humanity" of an OS that should be simple enough for anyone to use. Well, let's keep one thing in mind here. Anyone means everyone and **everyone includes people who've never had a computer nor tinkered with one before.** For those people, graphic driver issues are problem #1 from the get-go becaue of what is or isn't being displayed on the screen, and WiFi connectivity issues are problem #2 since you can't upgrade or update anything without that connection. I've honestly had better experiences with Linux Mint, (no kidding changed a couple of systems over to Mint because of ongoing Ubuntu troubles).

[COLOR=DarkRed]**4. **[/COLOR]Yeah, you can say that again. This too soured Ubuntu a little bit for me becaue all of a sudden we're getting away from the "Collective Linux Effort" and turning it int something that has much more of a propriatary feeling to it.

**As far as sticking with 10.04 LTS is concerned**, go ahead and tell someone that who wakes up one morning to find an Update Manager on their screen with the button ... NEW VERSION AVAILABLE, INSTALL NOW. Many people click on that without any thoughts or worries, only to find out that that was a huge mistake. In the past almost 3 years I've rebuilt at least 5 systems where that happened ... an innocent upgrade prompt that turned into a massive fiasco. Tons of forum posts about those kinds of issues too ...

I loved the whole thought of Ubuntu and this awesome OS for the world. But before Canonical can get serious issues resolved "for all of the people" (the human ones out there) once and for all, we're already being hit with Unity. That's not the sign of the times, at least not to me. From where I'm sitting that seems more like a sign of ego, not being receptive or willing to admit that it's time to slow down and make things perfect as can be **before moving on** to the next big thing. Well, hmmm, guess what? Debian calls itself the Universal Operating System now. Perhaps they'll get some of those issues resolved before Canonical does, and if they do, wouldn't that make Debian, then, even more human too? Talk about Ubuntu ....

PEACE !!! ):P

### Post by getut on 2011-05-11
I think both Ubuntu with their Unity and Gnome 3 are throwing advanced users of higher powered machines under the bus.

The inability to configure and tweak Unity and Gnome 3 guarantees that those cannot be used unless you use them exactly how the developers intended and no other way.

I use dual screens in a top and bottom configuration. To be more specific, I use a laptop on a docking station with a second monitor sitting on top of the dock visible over the top of the laptop monitor. Unity and Gnome 3 with the FORCED top panel sits right in between my two screens. In their infinite wisdom they have mandated that it be there and only there. Also, global menu is horrible with multiple displays.

I have NO PROBLEM with any default setting that Ubuntu Unity or Gnome 3 choose to set. But taking away configuration options so that they can ONLY be used the way they want you to use them goes against everything that linux and open source stands for.

I do a ton of installs and get 10-15 people a year to give a go to Linux. I will be recommending and installing Kubuntu for people from this point forward.

### Post by YesWeCan on 2011-05-11
There is the question of setting the right expectations.
There is a community desire for accessibility and reliability.
Then there is what Mark Shuttleworth is up to.

To understand where Ubuntu is headed we have to understand what motivates MS. He is a mid-thirties over-achiever who has a massive personal fortune though selling a company to Verisign. What makes people like him get up in the morning these days?

Would being the benevolent facilitator a loss-making distribution of a very reliable and very easy to use, basic OS be enough? Would the size of the community (the Club) of users be enough? Would allowing it to be evolved democratically by the needs of the users be satisfying?

I don't think so. I think he wants to be another Steve Jobs.

Whether this turns out good or bad for us community folk has yet to be seen. But at the end of the day, you get what you pay for.

### Post by YesWeCan on 2011-05-11
Part of the conflict is that the expectation of "ubuntu" has been set. This is a brand promise of a product for We The People, an antidote to the abuses of the incumbent, profiteering corporations.

So when Canonical does something against, or fails to do something towards, the "ubuntu" vision we get upset, even though we have paid no cash for the product. Not forgetting that many folks have paid a high price in time and effort to support others and give feedback to Canonical.

The premiss that Canonical is dedicated to the "ubuntu" vision is an illusion, of course.

Still, I am grateful (to lots of organisations and Canonical and this community) because I find Ubuntu useful and it is free. :)

### Post by egervari on 2011-05-11
I don't know if Unity is Ubuntu specific or not... and I think it needs some work before it's ready to go prime.

I really tried to use it for a long time, but in the end, the default behaviour is just not working for me, and I find it oddly restrictive and weird. There's actually a lot of really nice things about it. It behaves like windows 7 in many ways... but I think copying the windows 7 bar would be have been a bit better. I certainly find it more user-friendly and less restrictive than unity.

I just thought I'd throw these usability problems out there so that if anyone cares, maybe they can think about fixing them?

1. Programs like RubyMine and IntelliJ IDEA are grouped together in the same launcher icon - not separate icons like they should be. Once you open one of these programs, you cannot use the launcher to open the other one - you have to manually find it and open it. Kind of defeats the purpose of having them both on the launcher :(

2. Switching between 2 windows is actually more of a pain now than ever. Alt-tab has this .5 second delay to it... that I honestly really appreciated the window task bar at the bottom - it was rapid and immediate.

3. We can't easily edit the application icons like before. For example, I spent 10 minutes clicking and right-clicking... and I couldn't figure out how to change the script an icon should run. This is pretty easy to do in 10.10... but I just couldn't figure out how to do it using unity. I think allowing people to add/edit these icons easily is a pretty high priority item.

4. I think the second biggest problem for me was splitting the application menu bar (Like _F_ile, _E_dit, etc.) to the top made things more confusing than ever. You see, you're placing the menu options in a different context than the program itself. Sure it works for maximized windows, but sometimes you want to layout your screen with a bunch of smaller windows non-maximized windows - especially when you have terminals, pdf files, a web browser and an IDE running at the same time!

Maybe this design is simpler for non-programmers, but I found it really getting in the way. I tried and tried to get used to it... but it was a no go for me :(

5. Related to point 4: Whenever RubyMine was moved out of its maximized position (by dragging it accidentally), the application itself would lose its outer window border. What I mean is that the minimize/maximize buttons would disappear and I would not be able to click and drag the window anymore. The solution was to reboot :( This is a productivity sink.

6. The default auto-hide behavior on the side launcher is actually really annoying. I accidentally triggered it numerous times. I found out how to turn this off and make it activate by mousing over to the top/left of the screen... but I generally would have liked the bar to be at the bottom like before anyway. By putting it at the top/left, it just made me slower at activating new programs and switching between them.

After problems 3-6 happened enough times, I am now using classic. I think I'll wait another 6 months before I give unity a shot again.

I do really like the ability to type a program's name and have it come up like in Windows 7... that's a great new feature. Keep that in there ;)

Anyway, I hope this feedback helps.

---

@winriddance: the default in LTS releases is to prompt on a new LTS being released, NOT a new six-monthly version. So the scenario you present won't happen unless the user has already fooled around with the setting.

### Post by horsemanoffaith on 2011-05-12
It's amazing to me how many people don't like the Unity desktop. I have installed 10.04, 10.10, and 11.04 (not at the same time) on my laptop dual-booted with Windows, and I have to say I have been impressed with Natty Narwhal. When I had 10.04, I installed AWN and Cairo Dock, and BOTH of them were highly annoying to me because they got in the way or they didn't work as well as I felt they should. With the launcher, I can have my windows maximized, and the launcher doesn't interfere with my windows. I have to admit that it's a little bit harder to get to my programs, and that's my biggest beef... it takes me longer to launch a program if I don't have that icon on my launcher. Still, it's an acceptable compromise. I've had far FAR fewer problems with this version of Ubuntu than any other. In every other install, I've had to do multiple re-installations to fix problems that cropped up (some of which I admit I caused myself). The biggest issue I had in 10.10 was the pictures not loading into my e-mail in Evolution. I haven't had ANY problems at all since I've loaded 11.04. Everything has worked well, with one small exception, and that's the system-wide equalizer for PulseAudio. If I enable it, it just doesn't work. The other beef that I had was I wasn't warned that my video card wouldn't support Unity prior to loading it on my older desktop. I have since loaded the 2D version of Unity, and my desktop is working well. Overall, I give Ubuntu 11.04 a thumbs up... and I definitely do LIKE the Unity desktop! Keep it up, developers... you're doing great!

One small note... one thing that I did not like when I changed from 10.04 was the Ubuntu Software Center becoming the default program for adding/removing software. It is WAY too slow compared to running it with the archive manager or with the terminal. When I loaded 11.04 to get Google Chrome installed I had to do it through the terminal to find out why it didn't install correctly... this is one thing that I would like to see fixed!

---

### Post by El Zoido on 2011-05-12
IMHO Unity is better than the standard Gnome2 desktop.
But - I was anyway used to mod the old desktop layout according to my preferences.

So far I quite like the new main menu, which I find much better than the old one, even if you have to click one or two times more on occasion. But the integration of keyboard search and Zeitgeist is great.
The Launcher(/Dock?) on the other hand has been set to autohide and I'm back to using Docky which I prefer for most of the stuff I do with an launcher/dock.
Should I need the Unity dock I can access it easily, otherwise I can use Docky.

I guess it may improve a lot over the next few releases, so I'm cautiously optimistic about Unity. However, the same can be said for Gnome Shell, so I still wonder if it was really a necessary step to go for Unity instead of Gnome Shell.
Well, we'll see and I hope that with the next release I'll be able to easily install both - as long as I have the flexibility I'll stay with Ubuntu (and if not, there's always remixes or if everything else fails other Distros)

---

### Post by NCLI on 2011-05-12
> **horsemanoffaith said:**
> It's amazing to me how many people don't like the Unity desktop. I have installed 10.04, 10.10, and 11.04 (not at the same time) on my laptop dual-booted with Windows, and I have to say I have been impressed with Natty Narwhal. When I had 10.04, I installed AWN and Cairo Dock, and BOTH of them were highly annoying to me because they got in the way or they didn't work as well as I felt they should. With the launcher, I can have my windows maximized, and the launcher doesn't interfere with my windows. I have to admit that it's a little bit harder to get to my programs, and that's my biggest beef... it takes me longer to launch a program if I don't have that icon on my launcher. Still, it's an acceptable compromise. I've had far FAR fewer problems with this version of Ubuntu than any other. In every other install, I've had to do multiple re-installations to fix problems that cropped up (some of which I admit I caused myself). The biggest issue I had in 10.10 was the pictures not loading into my e-mail in Evolution. I haven't had ANY problems at all since I've loaded 11.04. Everything has worked well, with one small exception, and that's the system-wide equalizer for PulseAudio. If I enable it, it just doesn't work. The other beef that I had was I wasn't warned that my video card wouldn't support Unity prior to loading it on my older desktop. I have since loaded the 2D version of Unity, and my desktop is working well. Overall, I give Ubuntu 11.04 a thumbs up... and I definitely do LIKE the Unity desktop! Keep it up, developers... you're doing great!

One small note... one thing that I did not like when I changed from 10.04 was the Ubuntu Software Center becoming the default program for adding/removing software. It is WAY too slow compared to running it with the archive manager or with the terminal. When I loaded 11.04 to get Google Chrome installed I had to do it through the terminal to find out why it didn't install correctly... this is one thing that I would like to see fixed!
The Software Center will receive a hefty upgrade in 11.10, and should be able to deal with installing .deb files a lot better after that.

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-12
> **3rdalbum said:**
> @winriddance: Unity is workable, but it will get better. It will be a lot better when wed look at it in six month's time.

I've never doubted or disputed that but what you just said has been exactly my point and that of many many others in this mega-thread. Unity was still far too much in its infancy to be released this early and this thread is pretty much proof of that. It'sd not a thread about people hazing Unity, but largely a thread with tons and tons of compaints from people who've actualyl tried using Unity and weren't happy with the results.

I find it quite disconcerting that Mark Shuttleworth/Canonical took that step away from Gnome3 which really says a lot as far as the Worldwide Linux Community is concerned. Like it or not, you can't take away that "proprietary Microsoft like feeling" that was created with the introduction of Unity. It's a big reason for me to stop using Ubuntu because I want total Linux freedom and not partial Linux freedom. That's my feeling about that anyway.

I'm sure that Unity will be substantially better in 6 months, but will I still be able to do what my screenshots show without logging into the "Mercy Ubuntu" with the Gnome2 desktop? That's what I want and that's what I prefer. If Ubuntu takes that freedom away from me with Unity then Ubuntu becomes no different to me than any other handful of distros out there ... Mint, Mepis, PCLinux OS, Fedora, and so on. To me personally Ubuntu, the human Linux OS, stopped being "Ubuntu" when it suddenly and inexplicably became restrictive ... **I want complete control** of my desktop, will the next version of Unity have that (without MercyGnome version 2.0)?

.

---

### Post by Dragonbite on 2011-05-12
> **WinRiddance said:**
> I find it quite disconcerting that Mark Shuttleworth/Canonical took that step away from Gnome3 which really says a lot as far as the Worldwide Linux Community is concerned. Like it or not, you can't take away that "proprietary Microsoft like feeling" that was created with the introduction of Unity. It's a big reason for me to stop using Ubuntu because I want total Linux freedom and not partial Linux freedom. That's my feeling about that anyway.

Unity, from what I have heard, is open source and other distributions did try adding it to their own repositories but dropped it.  I assume it may be because it is moving too fast, and once it gets settled down may be picked up again.

Opensource mantra "release early, release often" seem to be what they are doing with Unity.  Get it out into the "wild" and let people use it, hack it, and provide feedback.

I'm surprised UbuntuOne isn't making Ubuntu more Microsoft-esque since no other distributions are able to connect to it at this time.  If you want cross-linux and even cross-platform you have to go with some other online file sharing product such as Dropbox.

Unity = different default shell for GNOME - BIG DEAL, this doesn't have anything to do with freedom, please explain your claims. Also, you have complete control over Ubuntu, you can change anything you want.

Canonical ships Banshee now as the default music player, but let's say you liked Rhythmbox, so you go and install it and you won't make a big deal about it. Now, it's the same with Unity, you can use GNOME2, you can also use the new GNOME shell, hell, you can even use KDE (I do) or XFCE.

Ubuntu gives you the freedom to do whatever you like, Unity is just the default looks of the main spin. 

You want complete control? you have it.

### Post by getut on 2011-05-12
> **janpol said:**
> Unity = different default shell for GNOME - BIG DEAL, this doesn't have anything to do with freedom, please explain your claims. Also, you have complete control over Ubuntu, you can change anything you want.

Canonical ships Banshee now as the default music player, but let's say you liked Rhythmbox, so you go and install it and you won't make a big deal about it. Now, it's the same with Unity, you can use GNOME2, you can also use the new GNOME shell, hell, you can even use KDE (I do) or XFCE.

Ubuntu gives you the freedom to do whatever you like, Unity is just the default looks of the main spin. 

You want complete control? you have it.

I think you are misunderstanding winriddance. Ubuntu is FORCING people to change with unity.

With Gnome 2 and KDE, they support nearly any usage model imaginable. With Unity and Gnome 3 a usage model is enforced and is "take it or leave it". I find that new behaviour infuriating.

For example like I said in my post:

Dual monitor users are disregarded with unity. An enforced top and side panel with freaking huge icons. If you run dual monitor in a top and bottom configuration and want to move the panel to the bottom you can't do it.

If you run windows anything other than full screen their saying bye-bye.

A good desktop is FLEXIBLE and lends itself to being used in new and wonderful ways that no one intended. Unity is thumb management by force.

I've gone KDE and not looking back. I can think for myself thank you very much Steve Jobs and Mark Shuttleworth.

---

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-13
> **collisionystm said:**
> If they had gone with gnome 3 there probably would not have been a 'classic' mode. Can you imagine the uproar? Have you even tried gnome 3? Its worse than unity IMO.

I think we were anything but forced. They tried out unity....big deal. Atleast they had the courtesy to include a 'classic' version.... hence still leaving you the option to enjoy the ubuntu environment you were comfortable in and transition to something new at your own pace.

**Including the classic mode was nothing but self-preservation.** Can you imagine the hundreds of thousands (if not more) of users i.e. WORD OF MOUTH ADVERTISERS who might have jumped ship immediately if there had been no classic Gnome version included?

Of course people are being forced to switch because Mark Shuttleworth made no beans about the fact that Gnome will be abandoned altogether. So come Ubuntu 11.10 or 12.04 it's pretty unlikely that there'll be any kind of Gnome available at all. Being forced as far as software terminology is concerned, means with little to no time to adapt and that's exactly what happened. What should have happened, was a much slower transition, giving people time to test and play with Unity while still using Gnome, instead of the other way around. That's what a lot of people are in an uproar about, the fact that Gnome was literally yanked out from under their feet overnight without any consideration. That's just plain ol' callous behavior, simple as that ... the kind of behavior you'd expect to see from, um, who comes to mind? Oh yeah, Microshaft.

There's absolutely nothing "Ubuntu" about how that was being handled. Luckily for Canonical and MS, I seriously doubt that the majority of Ubuntu users actually know and understand what Ubuntu **really** means or how the name for Ubuntu came to be. I think MS himself has forgotten what it means too. Part of the uproar also has to do with "being blindsided" into thinking ... to many people for several years ... that they finally had the most incredible free OS experience available, only to find themselves confronted with such a radical change after years of being happy with what they had. At least the Gnome3 developers are having the foresight to do what Canonical should have done, by making Gnome3 available far far in advance so people can get used to it before they actually have to use it.

---

### Post by Blodskaal on 2011-05-13
I had my normal Ubuntu interface customised quite a bit, and when I first looked at Unity I noticed a lot of things I didn't want, and a lot of things not that I had added in the classical interface.
Then I tried to customize Unity and couldn't find out how (No context menu seriously!?).
I started trying to figure it out. First I realised that I had to click a non-descriptive icon to open a window that covered the entire freaking screen, to type the name of the application to get the launcher to show up and then click it.
I realised that normally I would select it:
1. From the applications menu, requiring some mouse use.
2. Type the name directly into terminal (which I have bound to <Mod4>R, an artefact of my windows days) and hit enter.
Unity required me to switch from mouse to keyboard and back to mouse, and filled my entire screen while I did so.
It was in the way, and unwieldy to me.
I logged out and logged in to the classical interface, which luckily retained all my customisations that where gone in Unity.
I am happy with the classical interface.

---

### Post by Crempel on 2011-05-13
As one of the many KDE guys who had migrated to Gnome (Ubuntu) after KDE 4 came out, and also having found Kubuntu to be somewhat of a "poor cousin" I went back to an excellent KDE-Distro (Mepis 11). I have run Kubuntu 11.4 under VirtualBox and found it very good. It took me some research to find out how to install Ubuntu (Unity) in VB. I finally managed to do it: UNITY ROCKS! I have yet to spend some more time with it, but, gee, whhat a totally different approach to a Linux desktop!
Come 10.11 it should be near perfect, and that after such a short time! For interest sake, am running Unite (VB) with 1.5 gigs of Ram on an AMD dual core with 2x2.9 Mhz, the NVIDIA card is set to just 64 MB.

I suppose it would be too much to ask for the forks to be part of a more comprehensive marketing effort, the differences between the different members of the Ubun family isn't real clear without some digging...   
The amount of choice is intimidating to potential converts
most of the information on the home pages are geared towards enthusiasts.
If the goal is to capture a larger market share, further product development isn't as important as the marketing, which is scattershot at best

I just found out that we live in a very exciting era with all the changes, yeah Unity wasnt my thing but hey there is so many more out there.
Unity opened also my mind to try the other family members of Ubuntu, and we have everything at the moment, Gnome 2, Unity, Gnome Shell, Xfce, LXDE, you can also install Openbox, Xmonad, Enlightenment, you can build whatever you want, so it is just Linux, but the flag ship is Unity. Now, I really think Unity might be good for all the new comers and for the hardcore Linux freaks/powerusers we can make what we want of Ubuntu and I like the Ubuntu base and community.
Now I could not decide what to use, KDE or Xfce, I like them both, before I never would try them, but it seems in the end on my machine Xfce is the winner. :)

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-05-13
> **athenroy said:**
> I'm not a Linux purist, but having tried some early Red Hat free versions back in the early 2000s Ubuntu and Unity are light years ahead.  For me it works fine and is relatively easy to understand.  :)  **Perhaps some users are afraid the "mystery" of Linux is vanishing and soon anyone will be able to use it!** [...]
True!  But, I *think* there's more to it, than that.

I've run (probably) 100 different distros.  It was my hobby, for a year or two.  That's all I did - ate - worked - slept - and installed different distros in my free time.  I couldn't get enough of it...

Personally, I *think* some ppl are resentful of all their hard work going down the drain.

It's like the devs keep moving the goal post, farther down the field.

They *feel* like a carrot is being dangled in front of them, but the devs keep it just out of reach.

Personally, I enjoy a challenge.  I can't stand it when "things just work". That's why I l-o-v-e Ubu dev releases.  Breakage gives me a warm n' fuzzy *feeling* inside! 

I know, I know... it's probably a deep-seated psychological defect, caused by my parents getting divorced when I was 5 years old, blah, blah, blah.  Suffice to say, chaos and mayhem are my two best friends.

The problem is, most ppl aren't built this way.  

Soooo, while ppl like me (and maybe you) find Unity new & exciting, others see their virtual lives flashing before their eyes.

Anyway, they'll get used to it.  Or, not.

You can lead a horse to water, and all that sort of stuff.

But, when they get thirsty enough, they'll drink the kool-aid!  

It's their conventional human nature coming out - that's all.  :D

### Post by santq on 2011-05-13
I just upgraded and my first reaction is.. Whoa, it's great!

I had beforehand read bunch of negative previews about the new interface and I was ready to change to OpenSUSE, but now it seems to me the previews were more of unfounded hatred towards change than reasoned arguments.

I was angry when Canonical changed the window buttons from right to left, because it made no sense. It was just annoying gimmick that forced people to relearn the hard-wired fact that windows can be closed from the top right corner. But the design solutions done in Unity just make sense. From the beginning when I started using Linux desktops, I thought the user interface was outdated. Now we finally have much more logical desktop interface and I'm only left to wish for W7 type navigation bar for Nautilus.

I have encountered a few small bugs already, but that much was to be expected. I tip my hat off to Canonical. Great job!

**edit**: Okay I must point out it seems I can't customize anything on my desktop, which is annoying (I can't even add cl switches to launchers!). I hope the regression is fixed in the next release.

### Post by janpol on 2011-05-14
> **getut said:**
> I think you are misunderstanding winriddance. Ubuntu is FORCING people to change with unity.

You know that Ubuntu is also Kubuntu, Xubuntu, etc... right? Mark Shuttleworth is also supporting those, so if you are moving to KDE and using Kubuntu, it's still Ubuntu you know?

Your are misunderstunding me, what I wanted to say is that Ubuntu is not Unity, Unity is just a shell for GNOME. You are free to download any flavour of Ubuntu, or even download the default and then go with GNOME classic or even install GNOME3 or whatever you like.

I'm currently using Kubuntu 11.04 and really happy with it (and Xubuntu on my netbook), and that still makes me an Ubuntu user (and a Linux user for that matter). 

Linux is about choice :) and in this case the user still has the choice (to even go with another distro). Did Ubuntu came with your laptop and you paid a licence for it? No, most likely is that you have downloaded it and installed it, you **_choosed_** to do that. Even if you have bought a laptop with Ubuntu preinstalled, you haven't paid a licence and you are even encouraged to try any Ubuntu flavour you like (or any distro). Nobody is even going to try to convince you to stay with Unity, just go and be happy ;)

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-15
> **VinDSL said:**
> 

If Gnome 2 was so great, why did they need "literally thousands of people around the world" explaining it? All the ppl that are ever going to use Gnome 2 are using it now.


Okay, I've built, sold, and upgraded literally hundreds of computers, sold them online too for quite some time so I daresay that I have tons of experience where new users are concerned, or even not so new ones who are switching their OS for one reason or another. Having "converted" more than a couple of dozen people to Ubuntu personally, primarily on systems that I built, I'm here to say that I have yet to have one single person who's not happy as a clam with their Ubuntu setup. So to me, the adage that "as many are using Gnome2 with Ubuntu as there will ever be" is just baloney since most computer users are not technicians, nor are they interested in fiddling. For most people, Ubuntu 10.10 with Gnome2 would have worked for the next 10 years ... just like WindowsXP Pro worked for 11 years (so far) ... and actually continues to work, even for many businesses.

So getting away from average users ... the majority of worldwide computer users ... we're down to the professionals, technicians, builders. etc. like myself. And from that point of view there have always been two things that have greatly bothered me about Ubuntu, and I say "greatly" because they're things that I believe could easily have been fixed by now (during the last 3 years).

**1.** The installation not being a no-brainer, i.e. the fact that you can't give an Ubuntu CD to just any user and say "here, put that in your drive and try it out" which IMO would have had far more people using Ubuntu by now. Recently I installed Win7 on a machine and couldn't believe how simple that was. Recently I also installed the latest Linux Mepis and couldn't believe how simple that was either. So if Mepis could do it ... so could Ubuntu ... but they didn't. First and foremost the OS should be simple enough to install that anyone without any knowledge about partitioning should be able to do the job. Shouldn't need to know anything about hardware either, just like with Mepis, have a program that determines if your machine can run the OS, or not.

**2.** Then, number two, the endless fiddling. Oh, believe me, Ubuntu is awesome once everything is running flawlessly. But I'm here to tell you that in over a couple of dozen installs none of them were truly flawless. Wifi issues here, Graphic driver issues there, Permission problems, etc. So even if #1 was as simple as Win7 or Mepis installations, you'll still deter others by #2 if their system doesn't run perfectly right from the start.

So in my opinion, those two points are **far more** important to getting those new users for that 200 million goal, than any of this other BS (Unity) which was intoduced before #1 and #2 were ever fixed. That's probably my biggest beef with Ubuntu, the fact that truly important issues have still, after several YEARS not been perfected, yet it was time for a whole new desktop experience already. Well, as long as #1 and #2 aren't fixed for everyone ... and believe me, it can be done ... you'll never see that 200 million goal reached. It's absurd, nothing less.
Sounds like some serious ego combined with wishful thinking ....

### Post by beew on 2011-05-15
It is not so much the interface, but the usability and (some really show stopping) bugs. My main beefs

. Slow boot time.

. It still randomly logs me out when I press "open file" in Smplayer or "open new tab" in Firefox (it is rare but does happen).

. finding and opening applications with the dash is a pain. All th ey need is to provide a logically organized menu system that enable you to find applications and start them with 2 clicks, AWN and cairo dock can do it, I don't see why the Unity bar can't (I know about search, but why need the dash at all if they intend people to use search and what if people can't remember the application's name or don't feel like typing?)

.The global menu. It is easy enough to remove but its implementations generate weired menu bugs and renders the top panel uncustomizable. Some of these global menu induced bugs are
1) Applications that open maximized without maximize/minimize/restore buttons (e.g LibreOffice)
2) Switching between maximized and restore sometimes make the button disappear (e.g Smplayer)

. awkward ways to switch between open windows and between desktops.

.if an application window is open clicking the Unity launcher does nothing (whereas in all other docks and the gnome panel this would minimize the window)

.unmovable dock and ack of customizability in general.

A pretty UI means nothing if it is awkward to use. A good UI is something that integrates smoothly with your workflow that you don't even aware that it exists. But Unity is going the opposite way in that it is as if it tries to insert itself before you in every opportunity to remind you how brilliant its design is,--e.g the dash. This is epic fail imo.

None of the above is unfixable, but what I find most upsetting is the attitude of some Unity supporters and developers. Instead of acknowledging your points and saying something like xyz maybe good but we can't get around to it yet, very often they would just try to lecture you why you **shouldn't** want feature xyz because they know what you should want as a user. If you don't you need to retrain yourself to suit Unity because according to their theory it is so much better so it has to be and to hell with actual user feedbacks . The worse part is to change the intended users in an argument just for making excuses. If you complain lack of flexibility
they would tell you that Unity is supposed to be a friendly UI for the "average user" (TM)  who don't need that many options. If you say it takes too many clicks to do simple things they would tell you you should memorize obscure key combinations as if the "average users" suddenly have all turned into professional typists and keyboard ninjas.

> **WinRiddance said:**
> **1.** The installation not being a no-brainer, i.e. the fact that you can't give an Ubuntu CD to just any user and say "here, put that in your drive and try it out" which IMO would have had far more people using Ubuntu by now. Recently I installed Win7 on a machine and couldn't believe how simple that was. Recently I also installed the latest Linux Mepis and couldn't believe how simple that was either. So if Mepis could do it ... so could Ubuntu ... but they didn't. First and foremost the OS should be simple enough to install that anyone without any knowledge about partitioning should be able to do the job. Shouldn't need to know anything about hardware either, just like with Mepis, have a program that determines if your machine can run the OS, or not.

**2.** Then, number two, the endless fiddling. Oh, believe me, Ubuntu is awesome once everything is running flawlessly. But I'm here to tell you that in over a couple of dozen installs none of them were truly flawless. Wifi issues here, Graphic driver issues there, Permission problems, etc. So even if #1 was as simple as Win7 or Mepis installations, you'll still deter others by #2 if their system doesn't run perfectly right from the start.

So in my opinion, those two points are **far more** important to getting those new users for that 200 million goal, than any of this other BS (Unity) which was intoduced before #1 and #2 were ever fixed. That's probably my biggest beef with Ubuntu, the fact that truly important issues have still, after several YEARS not been perfected, yet it was time for a whole new desktop experience already. Well, as long as #1 and #2 aren't fixed for everyone ... and believe me, it can be done ... you'll never see that 200 million goal reached. It's absurd, nothing less.
Sounds like some serious ego combined with wishful thinking ....


But aren't these misgivings more to do with Ubuntu than with Unity?  You yourself say that these problems existed before Unity.  FWIW, my Natty installation was a breeze.  From what I've read, I may have been lucky, but my experience was that it was a "no-brainer."

For point two, you can't really compare Windows and Ubuntu in working with hardware, since Microsoft benefits from the fact manufacturers will bend over backwards to help make Windows compatible.  Canonical can't depend on that.

### Post by jhonan on 2011-05-16
It would be interesting to understand Canonical's business reasoning behind the move to Unity default. I would hope there *are *business reasons, and this is not just the result of a falling out between Gnome and Shuttleworth.

We know that Canonical make their money from offering support and related services off the back of their Ubuntu and derivative offerings. We also know that most of their big customers will be using LTS 10.04 and unlikely to even think about upgrading until the next LTS release.

Google and Apple have split their OS offerings to cater for specific customer+device segments, they realised that a 'one size fits all' generic OS doesn't work. People have different needs in an OS. From the 'uber-techie tweak everything' user to the 'I just want something that works' iPad user. Take a look at how Google created ChromeOS specifically for the netbook market, instead of attempting to shoehorn Android in.

Canonical have not successfully addressed the slate/casual/smartphone segment. They have Ubuntu One sitting in the background, so they can make some revenue from that, but only when they've made inroads into that segment.

Unity has some similarities to the iOS/Android UIs, I can see how it could evolve into a slate OS, and how it might be a suitable OS for the casual netbook user, with some more polishing.

I don't think Unity is intended for the more technical or power user, and I honestly think Canonical would be shooting themselves in the foot if they pushed this as the default desktop in the next LTS with no fall-back. Think of the increased cost-of-ownership, training etc. the corporate support customers would incur if this was the case.

Thankfully, Canonical have more than one string to their bow; Ubuntu, Kubuntu, Xubuntu, Edubuntu, and probably Lubuntu (soon, hopefully) - I think defaulting Unity in 11.04 and unleashing it on the general non-LTS population is a cheap and risk-free way of Canonical dipping their toe into the iOS/Android segment in preparation for a push on Ubuntu One.

---

### Post by alonewestand on 2011-05-16
I was to understand that the whole point of Linux was freedom.  Freedom to do whatever you wanted with your operating system to customize it to tailor and fit your every need.  Ubuntu is probably the most popular distro, so this should be the shining example of such a concept.  Yet I see this new bar on the side of my screen that I can't move.  Wait that can't be right, I could move it in Windows, surely Ubuntu--freedom and customization king--can move it?  No?

Really?

So what I've read is that the Ubuntu developers have not allowed moving this thing deliberately by design.  They want it right there and that's how they want people to experience it.  They want it near the Ubuntu button and believe moving it would degrade the experience of the user.  Well I'm here to say: We don't *care* what you think.

Do not get me wrong, I appreciate all of your hard work and effort.  You are providing a free platform to countless people and it is top notch.  I wish you nothing but the best in everything all of you do.  However, it is not your place to absolutely dictate what the user wants to do with something so simple as a launch bar.  I should be able to move the launch bar.  I should be able to disable it.  I should be able to set it aflame and make it sing the Hungarian National Anthem!

No, I do not ask that you give me the means to do this, but merely allow me the freedom without having to delve into The Matrix just to get the blasted thing down there at the bottom of my screen.  I believe you should allow Ubuntu users the freedom that they come to Linux for, and that I believe is one of the main reasons you all work on this project to begin with.

Thank you, and good day.

---

### Post by edw666 on 2011-05-16
Frankly I do not like the new look!  It looks bulky and currently customizing is a joke!  Maybe for the upcoming pad computers the recangular look makes sence but not on a mouse operated pc. 

Anybody saying that you have to get used to it accepts that the current design is not intuitive!  In addition the menue being displayed in the upper line is just plain stupid!!!  The design is a half-hearted copy (does not work for all applications) of one of the poorer  Apple OS features.   

The whole issue reminds me of Microsofts idea to impose the ribbon on all Office users. Both ideas to simplify things and to get the most needed things together in a more ergonomic way is good!  But not being able to customize them pulls the whole concept down!!!  

On top - I do not understand that the best known eye candy of Ubuntu  - the Cube - is not working without a major workaround even though the Compiz interface is used for this idea!  

There have been so many docks and things around so that anybody in need of something like the Unity buttons could have installed it by themselves!  No need to make this idea a standard design feature!

All in all - in my opinion - this is a step in the WRONG DIRECTION!  Please reconsider and give us something that will make us proud to use Ubuntu!

> Dude, you are *so* outta luck! Word on the street is that Compiz will be losing the flaming windows option soon.


News flash!  Shippit is back in business: you can now order a free can of lighter fluid and a box of matches for the purpose of flaming windows.  It will take 10 weeks to arrive and may be opened and searched by customs, and may be out of date by the time you receive it.  Mark Shuttleworth has suggested shipping the lighter fluid disguised as a miniature (as in those tiny bottles of alky hol) and the development team is working on a miniature that hides itself under the postage stamp when customs officers come near, but will coyly reappear when the consignee waves his finger over it or alternatively, simultaneously taps the postage stamp while holding the package by all four corners and whistling "Nearer my God to Thee".

This ploy is probably also doomed to failure, since everyone knows that those miniatures are always empty, even though you can never remember opening and drinking them.
 
> 
Kidding aside, I imagine the lack of flexibility is only a temporary situation. Frankly, except for moving it, there's not a whole lot you can't do with the launcher than you could do with any other dock. Well, except that 3D shelf thingy, that was cool, too.

I can think of a few other things:

Be able to change the background colour, change the transparency...

Ditto for size

Right click on an icon and be able to select from running instances of the same program (Now there's thought!)

Be able to drag a data file over the icon and have it maximise/bring forward the running application so I can drop the data file into it.

Better still, be able to drop the data file onto the icon and have it open the application and load the data file. (What a novel idea! an object oriented GUI.  Who'd a thunk.  Oh yeah, Microsoft and IBM did it 20 something years ago.  Should the loss/lack of a true OOGUI really be consider a step forward?)

The whole thing is fascinating really; I'm waiting to see what they do next.  The look of shear terror on my face is purely for the benefit of the paying customers.  :lolflag:
