### Post by gunterb on 2011-05-16
I am somewhat surprised at the mostly polarised responses.

People have different working styles: some like it more hierarchical, others graphical, others maybe something else again. Trying to force one way down everyone's throat doesn't seem sensible to me. It makes more sense to me to give users a choice, or even better, make it customisable. 

My own, limited experience so far: I have 2 PC's set up with 10.04. and 2 with 11.04. I use 11.04 when I want to learn about it, figure out HOW to do things in it, when I have time. I use 10.04 when I want to get things done, as it frustrates me having to spend minutes in 11.04 what to get started what I do in seconds in 10.04.

Thing is, I realise not everyone thinks like me. 11.04 might suit some people better. Who think different, or have a preference for other applications.

So, again, give us a choice, or make it EASILY customisable. I really don't want another fecking Windows.

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-16
> **VinDSL said:**
> Don't give up on Unity so easily!  It just needs a little fiddling...  :D
Here's what I've been working on recently (a mixture of Unity, Classic, and Compiz):[INDENT]**Gnome2 Panel Overlay on Unity DE (no loss of functionality on either)**[/INDENT][INDENT]**Gnome2 Panel Overlay (bottom panel showing) on Unity DE**[/INDENT][INDENT]Could you guys live with that?!?!?  :)[/INDENT]The fiddling is the thing that I believe a lot of us are really sick of. I believe there are tons of people who finally gave Linux a shot because of Ubuntu, because it was so gosh darn user friendly, but there's almost always "fiddling" involved, in one way or another, to get Ubuntu working 100% perfectly. With every release a higher degree of perfection with less fiddling is (at least from my point of view) expected, only to be suddenly confronted with the current 11.04 Unity release. I'm not talking to those who install Ubuntu once or twice and it's awesome, I'm talking about people who install it on dozens of different computers. You can tell by reading the hundreds and hundreds of comments here that a lot of people are just frustrated by this new change which nobody asked for.

Had Unity been introduced more publically (with internet pages by Ubuntu), and had the final design been more like your last two screenshots by default, I'm sure at least 50% of all of the comments in this thread wouldn't even exist. Shame on Canonical for stirring the pot like this, without any regard to the many dedicated users who'd be negatively impacted by this new flavor of soup. Shame on Canonical also also for not having at least a lengthy, srollable introduction with hints appear immediately after the installation of 11.04 to help not just new users, but also long term ones be able to better integrate themselves into NUbuntu. Considering the amount of time & intelligence that's behind this OS, I can't help but think that MS had everything designed like that on purpose, just to see what kind of reaction it would cause. Heck, he's filthy rich and quite often people like that tend to behave somewhat eccentric, right?

### Post by Crempel on 2011-05-16
I had read so many negative reports that I hardly dared trying it out- well, what a surprise, it's great! I am basically a KDE person but the concept of UNITY is awesome. It leaves Gnome for dead, BUT: And this is a big but, it took me several days to get it to work properly on a mid-stream desktop: ADM dual core 2.9, 4 G of RAM, Nvidia 7200 (!) Gpu. On Vbox it worked, in real life it didn't, then reinstall a few times, finally use the (not recommended) Nvidia driver 173...
How would a Linux novice have coped with this?
So the feeling is mixed: Great desktop, best I've ever used - just the concept how things are achieved/done, but then it does not (and I don't seem to be the only user who says that), it does NOT in many cases "work out of the box".
To cut a long story short, I really look forward to 11.10. And, finally, compared with KDE 4.0 when launched, Unity is by comparison up to the 4.2 level already; well done!:D

---

### Post by absinthe on 2011-05-16
I'm worried that people are confusing what Ubuntu is all about. Ubuntu is built on Linux sure, but it is by no means the end all be all expression of it. Rather it is a simple desktop that is readily available to all for free. Simple. Free. Easy. Fewest strings attached as possible. 
Now, if you are into tinkering, fussing, customizing, and generally lording over your system (like I used to be) perhaps you should give a different expression of Linux a shot. Some distro's are meant for the more...mad professor types among us.
I think it's important to understand that Unity IS in fact a continuation of the goal of Ubuntu = to be simple, easy, and free. I thank the computer gods for Ubuntu every day because it is so darn easy.

In the past when I had the time, and inclination to fiddle more I used Arch, Gentoo, and other distro's that thrive on customizing and a more...I dunno, modular way of working with your computer?

Now having said that...what do I think of Unity? I think it's an awesome start! I tend to agree that it was released a smidge to early, and should've waited another release, but it's a fascinating amalgam of modern computer interfaces. Like it or not portable pc's are becoming the norm and desktops are quickly becoming a thing of the past. Unity is an interesting expression of this new way of working.

Wow, I did not mean to create such a cumbersome wall of text.

### Post by Danny Dubya on 2011-05-19
If Shuttleworth wants the launcher to always be connected to the Ubuntu button, then can the Ubuntu button be moved to the right so the launcher follows it, and if not, then how come?

---

### Post by Larkspur on 2011-05-19
> **Danny Dubya said:**
> If Shuttleworth wants the launcher to always be connected to the Ubuntu button, then can the Ubuntu button be moved to the right so the launcher follows it, and if not, then how come?

I believe there's talk of an option to "mirror" the interface for cultures who read right-to-left, so technically yes.  Otherwise, the button and bar can't be moved, because of the way it's been designed: the left hand of the screen is about your use of the computer, the programs you run and so on (notice that all the functions of starting and stopping programs are focused on the top left corner), and the right is about the outside world (connection to the internet, email/messaging, the time, and finally, leaving the computer).  Moving the bar to the bottom (as many people want to) would break this, ahem, "unity" of design.

This is just guesswork, BTW.

---

### Post by decoherence on 2011-05-19
I'm forcing myself to give Unity a chance. Things I've noticed so far:

1. No obvious way to add widgets to the 'panel'. I'd like to see the weather and CPU load up there. This may already be answered but I searched and couldn't find (just a bunch of 'do you like unity?' threads ;) )

2. I lose track of windows. I was one of those guys who would set the task bar to show all windows, set alt+tab to show all windows, etc. I found gnome shell's overview function to be a reasonable replacement but I haven't figured out how to get the same functionality from unity. Perhaps I'm just 'doing it wrong' -- if anyone has any suggestions, i'd love to hear them.

3. putting some applications in full screen mode sometimes seems to force them to stay in the foreground. this smells like a bug and doesn't happen consistently

4. sometimes the dock won't disappear. again, doesn't happen consistently and smells like a bug.

5. the 'press windows key and start typing' is nice. that's one of my favourite features of gnome-shell so it's nice to see it in unity.

6. i don't care for the universal menu. i have to move my mouse to see what menus are available. i HATE having to move my mouse. --correction, i just discovered that pressing alt reveals the menu items. ok, that's not so bad i guess seeing as you have to press alt to drop them down anyway.

7. gnome-shell is prettier imho

8. if I want to open up a second terminal window, i can't seem to do it by clicking the icon in the dock (rather, it takes me to the currently running instance. that's kind of a feature but it'd be nice to have a right-click menu option that will let me start a second instance). this is not a huge deal -- i just start it up using the windows key. it's just a bit weird how 'launchers' become 'switchers' when the application is already running. i guess that's how the OSX dock works but i don't much like that interface.

so all in all, i'd say that so far unity isn't blowing my mind. mostly due to losing track of windows and not being able to customize the 'panel.' if anyone can show me how to get around this, i'll be much happier with it. 

gnome-shell, on the other hand, totally blew my mind when i first started using it. imho it is much sexier than unity... at least, when it works!

unity seems like a reasonable way of doing things and perhaps with a bit of refinement could be an excellent user interface.

### Post by el_koraco on 2011-05-19
> **decoherence said:**
> 
8. if I want to open up a second terminal window, i can't seem to do it by clicking the icon in the dock (rather, it takes me to the currently running instance. that's kind of a feature but it'd be nice to have a right-click menu option that will let me start a second instance). this is not a huge deal -- i just start it up using the windows key. it's just a bit weird how 'launchers' become 'switchers' when the application is already running. i guess that's how the OSX dock works but i don't much like that interface.



1. They're called indicator applets. Do a google search, you'll find those you've listed. 

2. As a workarround, go to ccsm and find the "Scale" plugin. Set it to an edge of the screen. When you put the mouse there, it will list all the non-maximized windows. Alternatively, use super + W for the same option, or alt tab to go through all windows (there is also the shift switcher and ring switcher plugins, that look better doing the same. 

3. and 4. are bugs. 

8. Middle click the launcher icon to bring up a second instance of the program. when two instances are open, clicking the icon brings  the instances in a "scale" (expose) like view. 

the rest - otoh, I find that Gnome Shell has some potential, but is a horrible mess at the moment. Unity is 75 percent usable, but way too buggy.

### Post by Larkspur on 2011-05-19
> **decoherence said:**
> 2. I lose track of windows. The launcher tells you which windows are open - the little arrows are next to them - but to see all open windows, use Super + w. If you have more than one window open of any application, click on it's launcher to see just them 

> **decoherence said:**
> 3. putting some applications in full screen mode sometimes seems to force them to stay in the foreground. this smells like a bug and doesn't happen consistently

> **decoherence said:**
> 4. sometimes the dock won't disappear. again, doesn't happen consistently and smells like a bug.

I have seen this too; the only times I've had it has been when I've been running a beta program.  My workaround is to make the launcherbar Autohide.

> **decoherence said:**
> 5. the 'press windows key and start typing' is nice. that's one of my favourite features of gnome-shell so it's nice to see it in unity.

It is good, but would be better if it's search engine could except mispellings or parts of the name other than the start.

> **decoherence said:**

> **decoherence said:**
> 7. gnome-shell is prettier imho

It's certainly easier to theme.  

> **decoherence said:**
> 8. if I want to open up a second terminal window, i can't seem to do it by clicking the icon in the dock (rather, it takes me to the currently running instance. that's kind of a feature but it'd be nice to have a right-click menu option that will let me start a second instance). this is not a huge deal -- i just start it up using the windows key. it's just a bit weird how 'launchers' become 'switchers' when the application is already running. i guess that's how the OSX dock works but i don't much like that interface.

If you middle/shift click, you can run another instance.

### Post by ikt on 2011-05-21
> **jenkinbr said:**
> 'Won't Fix' = "This is how I want it and if you don't like it, bugger off."

Ok, then.
*Resumes distro-shopping*

Not really, it's a feature request, and not every feature request that ever gets made makes it into ubuntu, just look at ubuntu brainstorm.

As far as canonical is concerned the case is closed, Mark mentioned that implementing such a feature and bug testing it and supporting it is a lot harder than it looks.

However the source code is there, and if you're up for supporting the feature and bug testing, ubuntu can provide you with the tools you need.

source code = [https://code.launchpad.net/unity](https://code.launchpad.net/unity)
code hosting/bug reports = launchpad
release via ppa
test
release fixes
add checkbox to unity config tool

[http://www.jonobacon.org/2011/05/18/creating-an-ubuntu-power-user-community/](http://www.jonobacon.org/2011/05/18/creating-an-ubuntu-power-user-community/)

and you'll have a launcher that moves!

### Post by b00n on 2011-05-21
I switched back to Gnome.

I really *wanted* to like Unity -- I often use a small screen and it seemed like it would make better use of screen real estate.  More utility, less clutter.  After a few hours of haphazard use, it was driving me crazy.

Why I switched back:

* I could no longer use an external monitor.  The display was either party black or all garbage.

* I could no longer find a bunch of "obvious" stuff.  Like I wanted to switch from 2x2 virtual desktops to 1x3.  I poked around and while it was easy to find the switcher, it was not obvious how to get from there to configuring it.

* It seems to eat keystrokes -- or at east misdirect them.  For example, using Netscape, I Ctrl-W to close windows, and that seemed to work only part-time.  It worked fine until I switched to 11.04.

* Speaking of Netscape, I think putting shortcuts down low and tabs up high is a mistake.  It means I have to mouse a little further each time I want to use a tab.  I use shortcuts every once in a while in a given browsing session, but I use tabs all the time.  So making shortcuts more convenient at the expense of making tabs less convenient is a mistake.

* The Unity "menu down the left side" takes a long time come up when I mouse over, so it is time-consuming to bring stuff up.

* The Unity "menu down the left side" stacks up badly on a small display (in my case, wide but short).  It makes it just that much more time-consuming to navigate.

* I found it hard to find stuff.  I think this is partly "what I am used to", but it seemed like there was stuff which was fundamentally harder to find.

* When I clicked on Xterm, it took me to an Xterm I already had open, not a new one.  I wanted a new one.  Navigation and launching are fundamentally different.

In summary, I would love a UI making better use of screen real estate  and cleaning up the "little of this, little of that" spreading bits of  data here and there across my screen.  But Unity so far seems like more  harm than good for my usual use.

---

What I don't care for with Unity:
-Lack of customization - Gnome had lots of customizations you can do widgets, gnomenu, etc.
-Lack of uniformness - my Unity bar looks like someone just splatter squares of paint in random order.
-Size of icons - yes I know this is fixable, but still
-Global Menu - fixable yes, but still irritating, it takes me twice a long to perform certain tasks cause I have to first click on the window i need the file menu for, then click the menu options, horrible horrible horrible design
-Scrollbar overlays - fixable yes, but when you're trying to find a small sliver of orange and then try to get the scrollbar buttons to show up, yeah, that is not intuitive at all, and most new people to ubuntu will be like how do I scroll where's the bar? Half the time I couldn't get the buttons to appear.
-Unity Bar - unable to quickly add multiple icons, once you drag and drop one, you have to click back on dash, type in what your searching for, and do it all over again
-Trying to be like Mac OS X - I understand that the whole feel is supposed to be like Mac OS X and if that's refuted, they're lying, compare it to Mac OS X and you can see how similar if not exactly like Mac OS X it is. I like Ubuntu to be it's own thing.
-Slow ALT+TAB - The ALT+TAB is so slow, and when I'm working on things I need it quick, I need my system quick, if its slow to even show one thing, usually that program is gone and I find another one.

==Things they should do==
-Customize it while you install it. Allow options to choose what features you want and which you don't (overlay scrollbars for example), so that way people can try it while they install it and defer it if they don't want it.

==What they could learn from Gnome 3==
-Integrate Empathy so you can reply without having to switch to the chat window
-Not separate file menus and use hidden scrollbars.
-Preview in ALT+TAB without needing compiz.

---

### Post by Bandit on 2011-05-21
> **slooksterpsv said:**
> Get ready for a page and a half ..............
LOL all good. 

The scroll bars are a freaking pain in the arsh.. I dont like em, they are hard to find and then hard to actually use. There should be a uniformity with them, not some programs have half normall scroll bars and half overlay ones. Its not uniform at all. There needs to be a way to have them all overlay and work correctly and easily with the user, or convert back to traditional scroll bars that everyone is still accustomed to.

A suggestion would be to make the sliver bar thingy pulse brighter and dimmer when the mouse hovers over the area the scroll bar should be, helping the user find it. Then having the grip area of the scroll bar pull to the mouses location (not scroll the screen) just help the grip get closer to the mouse courser for the user.



I actually do like menus being moved off the application with the exception of firefox. Reason being Fx being an exception is that removing the menu option seems to have screwed up the Privacy option and also screws up the upper tab option and doesnt put the bookmarks icon at the right corner of the fx program like it does by default. 
One thing I dont like about the menus is the Program name in the upper left part of the screen and when you move your mouse to that spot, it sorta fades out to show the menu. This is aweful looking. It should how ever NOT show the program name, but show the program icon and always show the dialogs menu next to the program icon all the time. There should also be an option that lets the user decide if he or she wants the menu up in the left corner or back on the dialog itsself. This being a individual setting for each application.


Cheers,
Exo

---

### Post by SpookyAction on 2011-05-21
I tried to be open-minded and give Unity a try, but I found it just about unusable. Much of Unity I found to be "different for the sake of being different" and not because it was better. The UI is somewhat counter-intuitive and requires the user the learn the Unity way of doing things rather than presenting an experience that adapts to the user. I'm a long time Linux user with a technical background that's been using Linux as my primary desktop OS for about 12 years now, so it didn't take me to long to figure out how to get around in Unity, but I couldn't imagine non-technical people just being able to jump into Unity and be productive without relearning what they know about a desktop UI or without training. Plus I found the Unity UI to be an eye-sore. It just doesn't look that good, has no way to customize the UI and the fact that it requires 3D acceleration but still manages to look plain is just another strike against it but the most damning complaint I have about Unity (and Ubuntu/Canonical for even releasing Unity in this state) is its complete lack of stability. After a brief 40 minutes of use I managed to cause Unity to completely lock up. What herculean task was I doing to make Unity lock up? I tried adding an application to the toolbar. I found the app I wanted in the menu and dragged the icon to the toolbar and as a result crashed Unity. I rely on Linux as a productivity tool, I can't have my desktop crashing while trying to perform a basic task, which is why I'm running KDE now. Unity is just a step in the wrong direction and will do nothing to bring Linux to the masses. I really think Canonical dropped the ball on this one.

---

### Post by ventrical on 2011-05-23
> **W4l0ck said:**
> What, your saying ubuntu is built off of wincrap?

Back in the early 90's a lot of programmers and hackers (myself included) used to run BBSes (pre-windows,pre internet wave) using software running under Microsoft MSDOS 5.0.Programs such as Telix, Pegasus Software and WWIV BBS software allowed a lot of Linux people to use the FIDO network. (even Linux based echos were run in FIDO) and  most of the volunteer programmers were running DOS based comm programs. I was invited between 1993 and 1995 to work on the linux OS, I dabbled in it and my preliminary training is with Unix, but, I became so entrenched with malware removal (pre Windows) that all my time was spent researching malware , malware prevention and the removal thereof, so my time spent on LINUX was very nominal at that time.

  It is good to come back after all those years and see Linux progress as much as it had and no no crapware or malware to deal with.

  However, as for Linus Trevolds, I believe he used a UNIX compiler to compile the LINUX kernel, but most of the comm systems and the programmers involved in disemminating and sharing info were using MSDOS based programs.(I'd have to refresh my memory on this).

  Of course, I stand to be corrected :)

 My experience and testimonial, of course.

I'm holding on to Ubuntu 10.04 LTS and it's Gnome until 11.04 comes out. Then I'll see what Unity and Gnome 3 looks like at that point.  Afterwards I still have 1 year to make the decision/jump to whatever I decide on (Unity, Gnome 3, KDE, Ubuntu, openSUSE, Mint, Fedora, whatever).

No sense whining at this point.  If all else fails, there is always Windows.

### Post by daz4126 on 2011-05-24
I really wanted to like Unity and I did like some of the ideas (if not always the implementation). But I couldn't get over the lack of customization and a few other annoying things that didn't look like they were likely to change in the future.

So I bit the bullet and installed Xubuntu - brilliant decision. It took a while to familiarise myself to some of its differences, but now I've *customized* it and got it the way I like it. It's running with Cairo Dock which does far more than the Unity sidebar and is far more customizable.

I would definitely recommend trying Xfce to anybody who is disillusioned with Unity.

DAZ

I really like Gnome 3 better. They are a lot alike but there is just enough difference to make gnome better. I voted I don't like it be hope it gets better even though I would go with Gnome 3 if it worked better with 11.04, which it doesn't.

### Post by Bart_D on 2011-05-26
Tried Gnome3 Shell.

I can see why Ubuntu dumped Gnome. **Gnome3 is a power user desktop** experience. **Unity is for everyone, including** MOST IMPORTANTLY(in my opinion), **novices**.

But for those saying it is too netbookish, you are missing the point....desktop computing is heading in THAT direction. That's EXACTLY what Gnome3 Shell is! A tablet user would enjoy it.

Give Unity a bit more time....there will be improvements. The Applications Launcher HAS to be improved....a side selection pane is required, not an embedded one. The main launcher(top left of the screen) is just fine though.

Frankly, I see only minor modifications on the hirozon...not wholescale changes like so many people are asking for! Ha, I just don't get that.

### Post by Garthhh on 2011-05-26

For me it is the way Unity handles work spaces
I do different things on different workspaces
Unity takes that ability away
I need each workspace to be separate so I don't get distracted 
when I'm writing or editing a document, I'll have a few other things open as reference materials. I may have several instances of the same program open on different workspaces, when I need a break I switch to a different workspace.

I'm on the bugs that are apply

There is the learning curve problem
many users don't want to spend substantial amounts of time to learn how to set up & use a new DE

We'll see where it's at when we get to lts

### Post by daisylynn on 2011-05-26

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-05-26
> **Garthhh said:**
> For me it is the way Unity handles work spaces
I do different things on different workspaces
Unity takes that ability away

Having a graphical representation of your desktops instead of a pager is taking away your ability to do different things on different desktops how?

Bringing up the visual representation of your desktops, then double clicking the desktop you want is slower than having a pager and being able to just click the desktop you want, there's no argument about that, but to me personally having that visual representation and being able to drag windows from one desktop to another and being able to see the desktops before choosing which to go to, since I don't always remember what I have running on what desktop, is more beneficial to me than having the one extra second back (give or take half a second) that it takes each time I switch to another desktop when I know which desktop I want to switch to. 

> I need each workspace to be separate so I don't get distracted

When viewing a visual representation of an applications open windows I can understand the desire to have the windows grouped by desktop. I can understand wanting an option for the dock to only show a visual indicator or icon for apps that have a window on the current desktop depending on if the app has is pinned to the dock or not. I can understand wanting the scale plug-in to only show windows that are open on the current desktop.   

I don't really understand the 'so you don't get distracted' argument. 

I like less distracting in the sense of having less stuff pop up on the screen that I have to get rid of before I can continue with the task at hand, which the KDE/app-indicator way of handling notifications helps to a large degree.

I don't like less distracting if that means theoretical improvement in efficiency of working at a single task in front of you comes at the expense of actual overall efficiency when working with multiple tasks. 

So for me being able to switch to any application window no matter which desktop it is on is more beneficial than only seeing what is on the current desktop.

Not saying there shouldn't be an option to work in a more compartmentalized mode, just saying that's how it is for me.

Later, Seeker

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-27
> **decoherence said:**
> Thanks for the info! The indicators and the Super+W key combo are very useful. Unity is more usable for me now!

Re: gnome-shell, there were a couple of builds, fairly early in its development, that were solid enough for me to use as my primary desktop but lately whenever i've tried it there have been problems.

I'm looking forward to giving it a shot on Fedora 15. I figure if it runs properly anywhere, it'll be there since they're kinda banking on it as their default desktop. And if I can get a really solid gnome-shell, that might be enough to get me to switch... then again maybe not (i love debs too much!) I really hope we get some rock solid gnome-shell love on ubuntu... but again, this particular computer i'm on has issues with accelerated drivers so it may be hopeless for me. i understand that in the absence of nvidia binary blobs, 11.04 will use nouveau? maybe all i need to do is get rid of the blobs, then? hopefully it'll be accelerated.

anyway, thanks again for the info! very useful!

You really ought to give Debian 6.0 (Squeeze) a try. I was amazed at some of the ease to customize which has provided me ...
**A.** My system looks identical (better now) to my old Gnome2/Ubuntu 10.10 setup.
**B.** CCSM (Compiz) actually performs better for me on Debian.
**C.** My level of OS stability has increased as well, no kidding.
**D.** I swear I'm not making this up ... my system seems to be faster.
**E.** Firefox & Thunderbird settings can be transferred to Icedove & Iceweasel without a problem.
**F.** Most plugins like Firebug, Tab Previews, etc. also work on Icedove & Iceweasel.
**G.** Rock solid stability with rock solid stable releases every 18 to 24 months.
**H.** If you like deb, that's also part of Debian. Many distros, Ubuntu included, are built on Debian.
**I**. Over 31.000 software apps contained in the software center (sound familiar)?

Yes, I do believe the Debian _installation isn't quite as simple_ as with Ubuntu, but from my personal perspective, Mark Shuttleworth is ruining the true essence of Linux Freedom for **everyone** (and not just the code-jockeys). That attitude of "will be like that, period" is just not acceptable and way to Windows-Like for me. I feel that MS has turned his back on a lot of what Ubuntu actually stands/stood for and so I have no problem turning my back on Ubuntu. I haven't looked back since my switch and both, Son & Wife are happy with their new Debian setups too. Our 16 year old Son loves Debian ...

### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-27
> **Gh0zt36 said:**
> is that a Graphics card your suggesting? arnt graphics cards on laptops hardmounted to the board? thats what i was told but i dunno. 
Even if they arn't ive never taken a lappy apart before . desktop sure built a few but lappy no. i heard its a very specific process since the hardware is crammed in there i dunno if thats within my skillset

What you describe is exactly what happened to me with my Nvidia driver. Exactly the same symptoms. When I opened up my Nvidia control panel, the settings there for my display were set to "AUTO" (or 60, don't remember which) and so I changed the setting to the other available display refresh option (there were only two options), then I clicked on "APPLY" followed by acknowledging that I want to keep those settings, and voila .... everything started working smooth as silk.
Everytime that I rebooted, I had to make that switch in my Nvidia control panel.
Do you have a graphics control panel for your Intel chip?

If not, have you tried looking for updated drivers yet, to see if there's another display chip option available for your computer? Sorry, this is the extent of assistance that I can offer you.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ubuntu_%28philosophy%29](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ubuntu_%28philosophy%29)

There have also been millions of people who've been looking to get away from expensive/restrictive MS Windows, **or to help others with limited budgets** and zero computer know-how get into Linux as well. You know, the Ubuntu thing, the Human thing, to be kind and helpful. To many people, myself included, "Ubuntu" with what it was proclaiming itself to be "for the People" was _the one_ Linux distribution that "finally" brought forth such possibilities _more than any other_ Linux distro of the past. **Do you get it now ... ???**

First Ubuntu was marketed to the masses (no better advertising than word of mouth) years ago as being the free linux distro _for literally everyone_ **because** of its Ubuntuness, yet here we are, years later, with unyielding changes which clearly defy the meaning of Ubuntu.
Yeah, that's right, **Linux is not Windows ...**
**And Unity is not Ubuntu ...** (clear lack of Ubuntu collaboration)

I already switched to Debian, as did the rest of my family, and we're all very happy with it. You just don't seem to get the fact that there's a lot more to Linux Ubuntu than just another empty name like Fedora, Red Hat, Mint, and so on. Ubuntu actually meant something, something for absolutely everyone, regardless if you're a power user, a computer newbie, or a code-monster. Ubuntu i.e. Mark Shuttlworth had the "Ubuntu Responsibility" **to everyone** for keeping the meaning of that word alive. Unity so clearly defies that and that my friend happens to be a fact that there's no getting around.

### Post by Zero2Nine on 2011-05-27
> **WinRiddance said:**
> You really ought to give Debian 6.0 (Squeeze) a try. I was amazed at some of the ease to customize which has provided me ...
**A.** My system looks identical (better now) to my old Gnome2/Ubuntu 10.10 setup.
**B.** CCSM (Compiz) actually performs better for me on Debian.
**C.** My level of OS stability has increased as well, no kidding.
**D.** I swear I'm not making this up ... my system seems to be faster.
**E.** Firefox & Thunderbird settings can be transferred to Icedove & Iceweasel without a problem.
**F.** Most plugins like Firebug, Tab Previews, etc. also work on Icedove & Iceweasel.
**G.** Rock solid stability with rock solid stable releases every 18 to 24 months.
**H.** If you like deb, that's also part of Debian. Many distros, Ubuntu included, are built on Debian.
**I**. Over 31.000 software apps contained in the software center (sound familiar)?

Yes, I do believe the Debian _installation isn't quite as simple_ as with Ubuntu, but from my personal perspective, Mark Shuttleworth is ruining the true essence of Linux Freedom for **everyone** (and not just the code-jockeys). I feel that MS has turned his back on a lot of what Ubuntu actually stands/stood for and so I have no problem turning my back on Ubuntu. ### Post by WinRiddance on 2011-05-27
> **Joe of loath said:**
> I measure stability (unscientifically) by the amount of times I tut and push myself away from my desk on my swivel chair :lol: Basically, the amount of random lockups and freezes you experience.

Mouse freezes, Application lockups, Inexplicable xserver/screen crashes, CPU overheating, i.e. INSTABILITY.
I could literally make Ubuntu crash almost at will, in particular with some of the 3D related apps. on my dismal ATI HD3200 laptop with 4 GB of Ram and dual-core 64bit AMD processors ... but my Debian system so far appears much less buggy. Not a single crash or freeze yet, even with Compiz and 3D effects going. There's a lot to be said for once every 24 month releases VS. releases that come out every 6 months with loads of unresolved issues. Linux Mint is based directly on Ubuntu too and I've installed that on computers with smoother, more pleasant results as well. It's beyond me how that can be possible since I don't know anything about the guts of Linux/Ubuntu ... ???

As far as Ubuntu is concerned, I was real excited about an "every 6 months release" with new features, new software, latest hardware compatibility, which is why it took me a couple of years to realize just how buggy Ubuntu can really be if you feel like always installing the latest and greatest release. Once you install Ubuntu on dozens of machines, you really get to know the quirkyness of the latest and the greatest ... especially during upgrade times where suddenly something loses it's backward compatibility i.e. something that worked before without a problem suddenly no longer works (without a bunch of fiddling & forum visits).

Now I'm totally impressed by Debian. Never tried it seriously before because I always associated Web Servers with Debian since it runs on so many of them. I always figured that it would be far more technical and complex than it actually is. There's no doubt in my mind that if Debian was easier to install for many Noobs, Amateurs, or Windows switchers, [it would rank higher than (click here) just #4 on Distrowatch's]("http://distrowatch.com") download stats (scroll down, right side). **Alright, that's it, **I'm truly outta here now.[B] ):P
[/B]

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2011-05-28
Yeah, openSUSE is thinking of packaging it. It's not likely to come as a default option for installation for some time, though, if ever. Just like how openSUSE doesn't bother including an option for Xfce or LXDE in the install CD, I doubt they'll be going for Unity when Gnome 3 is around with surefire integration and support, as Gnome is the classic option. Of course, SUSEstudio may already have a Unity-based spin-off, and that's probably the most likely place to find it.

I really do hope Unity keeps ahead as all these improvements come to Gnome 3's environment. While most features from applications will make it into Ubuntu automatically, there are many extensions and features in the Shell already existing that won't be available for Unity in any portable fashion. I think it's a good option for Gnome users who might want something more like Unity, however, and I hope the Unity packages became stable and integrated enough to be shipped in the regular repositories on other distributions. (Arch, for example, known for its ease of package management, isn't exactly easy to install Unity in at the moment due to unpopularity, stability issues, and the fact that it has, until Oneiric, completely replaced certain Gnome applications/daemons, notifications in particular)

We went from suddenly having one modern option with it's share of issues (even when KDE became stable, and oh how I love it, it still isn't ideal for many users who don't like the 'KDE way') to having 3 very light, reasonably stable options for desktops, almost overnight it seems. Aside from making it difficult for new users to choose what to use, I think we can only improve with things like Unity.

I would like to see Unity, Gnome 3, and KDE 4 all improve their documentation and provide optional ways to get Linux users familiar with how things work on Linux, and in their particular DE.

### Post by kansasnoob on 2011-05-28
The "classic" gnome-panel DE is not dead and even in gnome3 it's not dieing anytime soon. Such rumors are just FUD!

[http://live.gnome.org/GNOME3Myths#GNOME_won.27t_support_the_current_panel_and_window_manager_anymore](http://live.gnome.org/GNOME3Myths#GNOME_won.27t_support_the_current_panel_and_window_manager_anymore)

> GNOME won't support the current panel and window manager anymore

While it is true that the GNOME 3 experience will not include the panel as it was in GNOME 2, the window manager used in GNOME 3 will use a window manager that behaves consistently with the GNOME 2 window manager (metacity). This is because it uses most of the same code.

It is important to emphasize that GNOME 2 technologies will still be available through the usual channels for some time.

**Downstream distributions such as Fedora, openSUSE and Ubuntu will have the option to include them in their distribution.** 

And there is already work underway on gnome-panel-3:

[https://code.launchpad.net/~jbicha/gnome-panel/gnome-panel-3](https://code.launchpad.net/~jbicha/gnome-panel/gnome-panel-3)

Even in the earliest Oneiric Ocelot testing images one can get a classic DE by installing the package 'gnome-session-fallback'. It certainly needs work yet but it's also nowhere near October :)

I'm also willing to bet there are no boogey men under your bed :lolflag:

### Post by Bazon on 2011-05-29
> **Garthhh said:**
> For me it is the way Unity handles work spaces
I do different things on different workspaces
Unity takes that ability away
I need each workspace to be separate so I don't get distracted 
when I'm writing or editing a document, I'll have a few other things open as reference materials. I may have several instances of the same program open on different workspaces, when I need a break I switch to a different workspace.
(....)


For me, it's exactly the same problem. Not being able to completely separate into different contexts makes unity impractical for really working with it.
Of course, that won't be a problem if you only use one workspace or only separate applications by workspaces like caribou here or Jorge Castro in his unity demonstration video. *(e.g. browser on workspace 1, chat on workspace 2.... or whatever their example was)* In that case, the unity launcher acts as a kind of replacement for workspace switcher and is fine doing so.

But if you want to use workspaces to separate windows from the SAME application, in unity you end up with just a big mess. I'll describe an example in my next post.

Just for the workspace reason, the Unity launcher is not acceptable for me. On my work netbook I moved to lubuntu and on my leisure computer the unity launcher is hidden and I use the fantastic configurable cairo-dock instead. *(I'm really happy with both. :-) )* On my other machines, I just didn't update to 11.04 and use good old gnome 2.

### Post by dirtyHANS on 2011-05-29
I said, uh, where's my stuff? new unity launch bar - it hides all my program"s menus!!!
- it is, eye candy, yes, but i searched and finall fond a way to fix it - 
Re: how to make Ubuntu 11.04 look like 10.10
If you are already in the Unity desktop environment, log out. At the log-in screen, when you click the user ID, a menu will appear at the bottom of the screen. Click the spin-button to the your right of the 'Ubuntu'; this will pop up a menu of environments. Select 'Ubuntu Classic' from it. Key in your password and click 'Logon'. The desktop you see will be the familiar Ubuntu 10.10 environment.

---

### Post by Bazon on 2011-05-29
Yesterday, my current work task made very clear why unity is just bad for workflow. Because of that, I want to tell you about that.

**The situation:**
I had to interchange information from a text document in libre office to a web form opened in firefox.
Both windows were opened on workspace 4 *(of 6, each workspace has its own context for me)*.
In workspace 4, there were some other libre office windows and on the other workspaces there were other libre office and firefox windows as well.

**Workflow in lubuntu:**
need information from libre office document for the web form in firefox
--> click firefox in the taskbar/window switcher
--> the firefox window minimizes and reveales the document window
--> use arrows + tab to navigate in the document, shift+arrows to select text, Ctrl+c to copy text
-->  click firefox in the taskbar/window switcher again
--> the firefox window maximizes 
--> use arrows + tab to navigate in the form, Ctrl+v to paste text

note that the mouse was not moved at all during that, all the time it was on the icon for the firefox window on the taskbar!
--> very fast, very efficient

**Workflow in unity:**
need information from libre office document for the web form in firefox
--> click firefox in the taskbar/window switcher
--> the firefox window _DOESN'T_ minimize and reveal the document window because of [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ayatana-design/+bug/733349](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ayatana-design/+bug/733349) which is a "design decision" and therefore "won't fix".
So instead you have to:
--> click the libre office icon in the unity launcher instead. 
--> an exposé window spread of all libre office writer windows will appear - in the worst case from all workspaces. [https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/689733](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/689733)
--> you have to select the right libre office writer window which is not easy because you can't read the title, nor the content as a minimized view is shown in the exposé window spread. [https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/734253](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/734253)
--> use arrows + tab to navigate in the document, shift+arrows to select text, Ctrl+c to copy text
-->  click firefox in the unity launcher
--> the firefox window maximizes 
--> an exposé window spread of all firefox windows will appear - in the worst case from all workspaces. [https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/689733](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/689733)
--> you have to select the right firefox window which is not easy because you can't read the title, nor the content as a minimized view is shown in the exposé window spread. [https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/734253](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/734253)
--> use arrows + tab to navigate in the form, Ctrl+v to paste text

note that there was significant more mouse move and useless wasted time for the exposé window spread animations and the selection of the right window.
--> compared to the workflow in lubuntu (or gnome2 before) not efficient at all.

--> the firefox window minimizes and reveales the document window
--> use arrows + tab to navigate in the document, shift+arrows to select text, Ctrl+c to copy text
-->  click firefox in the taskbar/window switcher again
--> the firefox window maximizes 
--> use arrows + tab to navigate in the form, Ctrl+v to paste text

note that the mouse was not moved at all during that, all the time it was on the icon for the firefox window on the taskbar!
--> very fast, very efficient

**Workflow in unity:**
need information from libre office document for the web form in firefox
--> click firefox in the taskbar/window switcher
--> the firefox window _DOESN'T_ minimize and reveal the document window because of [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ayatana-design/+bug/733349](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ayatana-design/+bug/733349) which is a "design decision" and therefore "won't fix".
So instead you have to:
--> click the libre office icon in the unity launcher instead. 
--> an exposé window spread of all libre office writer windows will appear - in the worst case from all workspaces. [https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/689733](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/689733)
--> you have to select the right libre office writer window which is not easy because you can't read the title, nor the content as a minimized view is shown in the exposé window spread. [https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/734253](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/734253)
--> use arrows + tab to navigate in the document, shift+arrows to select text, Ctrl+c to copy text
-->  click firefox in the unity launcher
--> the firefox window maximizes 
--> an exposé window spread of all firefox windows will appear - in the worst case from all workspaces. [https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/689733](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/689733)
--> you have to select the right firefox window which is not easy because you can't read the title, nor the content as a minimized view is shown in the exposé window spread. [https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/734253](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/734253)
--> use arrows + tab to navigate in the form, Ctrl+v to paste text

note that there was significant more mouse move and useless wasted time for the exposé window spread animations and the selection of the right window.
--> compared to the workflow in lubuntu (or gnome2 before) not efficient at all.
**Workflow in unity:**
need information from libre office document for the web form in firefox
--> Pull Firefox Window to one side, taking up half the screen
--> Pull LibreOffice Window to other side, taking up the other half of the screen
--> use arrows + tab to navigate in the document, shift+arrows to select text, Ctrl+c to copy text
--> use arrows + tab to navigate in the form, Ctrl+v to paste text

Oh look it's actually MORE efficient

---

### Post by kansasnoob on 2011-05-29
> Workflow in unity:
need information from libre office document for the web form in firefox
--> Pull Firefox Window to one side, taking up half the screen

What if you're old and can't see very well? I need maximized windows.

> --> Pull LibreOffice Window to other side, taking up the other half of the screen

See above question/comment.

> --> use arrows + tab to navigate in the document, shift+arrows to select text, Ctrl+c to copy text
--> use arrows + tab to navigate in the form, Ctrl+v to paste text

What if I'm used to using the mouse more than the keyboard?

> Oh look it's actually MORE efficient

For me it's simply not, but I'm willing to accept the label of "too stupid for Unity" :D

We, and I mean all of us, need to stop trying to convince others that their opinion of Unity (or any DE) is wrong, whether it's positive or negative.

There are many options available and each individual needs to use whatever suits them best. Ubuntu did offer a very easy "fallback" in Natty and I think that should have been "advertised" more prominently to stop the "freak-outs".

I continue to play with unity-2d when I feel like just playing but there is absolutely no reason to stress myself out, so I switch to Classic w/o effects when unity even begins to overwhelm me ;)

I think most importantly, as a community, we need to find a way to combat the divisiveness and be more supportive :D

This change seems to be devolving into a basic "playground" argument like, "my dad can beat up your dad", and it reflects negatively on each of us individually and also the community :(

---

### Post by NormanFLinux on 2011-05-29
Add your favorite programs to the Unity launcher and keep them there. Think of it like a favorites bar.

Then download and install cardapio from PPA and add it to the launcher. That will give you your classic start menu back if you're tired of typing and wading through the global menu to find something.

You can also add Show Desktop to the launcher. Once I got Unity set up the way I liked it, running Ubuntu was easier than with the old setup.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-05-29
> **kansasnoob said:**
> And BTW regarding this:



[ATTACH]193562[/ATTACH]

But I'm sure you'll tell me that I'm doing something wrong :(

One of the things that is a problem with Unity is that it does have features that are hard to discover, the compiz developer mentioned the fact  on the ubuntu-devel mailing list. Hopefully the Power Users Team will remedy this with better documentation.

Yes kansasnoob you are doing it wrong :), Unity has a feature similar to Windows 7 Aero Snap. You grab a window title bar and move it towards the right side of the screen, once the cursor hits the edge of the screen, it resizes to take up half the screen, then with the second window, grab the title bar and move it towards the left of the screen, once the cursor hits the left edge of the screen the window automagically resizes to take up the rest of the screen.

Check out my screen shot, for me the text in both windows is large enough to be readable.

**Note:** I'm using an Acer 17" @1280X1024 LCD, only because it looks better to me than any other LCD I've seen so far, including several pricey 24" models.

### Post by kansasnoob on 2011-05-30
> **cariboo907 said:**
> One of the things that is a problem with Unity is that it does have features that are hard to discover, the compiz developer mentioned the fact  on the ubuntu-devel mailing list. Hopefully the Power Users Team will remedy this with better documentation.

Yes kansasnoob you are doing it wrong :), Unity has a feature similar to Windows 7 Aero Snap. You grab a window title bar and move it towards the right side of the screen, once the cursor hits the edge of the screen, it resizes to take up half the screen, then with the second window, grab the title bar and move it towards the left of the screen, once the cursor hits the left edge of the screen the window automagically resizes to take up the rest of the screen.

Check out my screen shot, for me the text in both windows is large enough to be readable.

**Note:** I'm using an Acer 17" @1280X1024 LCD, only because it looks better to me than any other LCD I've seen so far, including several pricey 24" models.

Thanks, but in reality the only reason I even posted here when I did (beginning with post #3311) was to point out some actual facts regarding the future of the "classic" panelish gnome DE :D

I do still from time to time play around with unity-2d (now Ubuntu 2D in Oneiric) just to learn a bit more but the moment I begin to get frustrated I switch to classic w/o effects.

Compiz has always been a bit of a no-go for me because I am visually impaired, in fact my visual impairment combined with my age lend greatly to the fact that **familiarity is of vast importance**:D

There are a number of things about Unity that make it really unsuitable for me. The top panel is useless because I can't easily see it and Unity is "top of screen" heavy, so I'd really have to be able to see very well to differentiate between panel objects and/or window controls. This is why I've always used only a lower panel in gnome2.

IMHO routine daily computer usage should never be stressful, so if Unity is just not someones cup-of-tea then there are other DE options available, and *we really do need to stop fighting over who's opinion of Unity is right or wrong. It comes down to individual preference* ;)

Whether I remain with Ubuntu or switch to another variant like Lubuntu or the new "low-fat" Kubuntu depends greatly on how well gnome3 continues to integrate with gnome-panel. Regardless I'm sticking with some flavor of *ubuntu.

---

### Post by Paulgirardin on 2011-05-30
> **Bazon said:**
> 
Just for the workspace reason, the Unity launcher is not acceptable for me. On my work netbook I moved to lubuntu and on my leisure computer the unity launcher is hidden and I use the fantastic configurable cairo-dock instead. *(I'm really happy with both. :-) )* On my other machines, I just didn't update to 11.04 and use good old gnome 2.

I have a similar set-up.I use Unity launcher to launch apps and search but I also have a small Cairo dock on the right with only 3 icons on it: a shutdown button,a work space switcher and Tomboy Notes.
The shutdown button because it is easier to use than the native Natty one.
The workspace switcher because the Cairo version works with the scroll wheel on mouse over.
The Tomboy notes because the Cairo version opens sub-docks for all the notes on mouse over.
As well as these permanent icons in the dock,any open apps also place an icon in the dock and these make it easier than the Natty launcher to see what is running,the running apps icons allow me to go to the workspace the relevant app is placed on,they  also allow the app to be minimised/maximised and closed via the icon.

### Post by RonnieRoyce on 2011-05-31
Adapt to what ? Unity is not an interface... it's just a dumbed down gnome session with a uninstallable side panel that's been forced as a default because Mark Shuttleworth thinks he can reinvent the wheel.
The classic gnome session can do what "Unity" does and more:
1. I can have what ever side panel I want (AWN,Cairo) if I want:)
2. Unity Dash ?. Install the Linux Mint menu (it has a search too):P 
3. In classic gnome I have the beautiful cube, just one click away and other eye-candy.
4. I can add shortcut's in my top panel.
5. I can switch between metacity and composite without crashing the session.
.........

Should I continue ? The point is made.

### Post by FlameReaper on 2011-05-31

### Post by daz4126 on 2011-05-31
I agree with RonnieRoyce - my main problem with Unity was how it was so constrained to how the devs (read Mark Shuttleworth) thought I should do things. I quite like new things and new ideas, but if they don't suit me I want to be able to change it to suit me.

I switched to xfce because I wasn't convinced Gnome 2 will be supported for too much longer. 

I've ended up with a lighter system that is *very* customisable. Xfce also has a nice and simple window manager with basic compositing and some good default apps (I particularly like gMusicbrowser).

I'm also using GLX Dock as a launcher - it's great, does far more than the Unity dock ... and I can have it on the bottom! It also provides some useful desktop widgets and quick launchers.

Why the Ubuntu devs decided to waste so much time reinventing what was already out there is beyond me ... why not just include a pre-configured GLX Dock into the main release?

As an aside I've been reading a few interviews with Mark Shuttleworth recently and I don't think he understands the concept of default settings. I fully agree that Ubuntu should be easy to just pick up and use 'out of the box', so lots of time should be spent making the default settings the most sensible options for first time users. But the devs should also recognise that everybody is different and make it easy for users to configure the way they want it as they become more experienced. I really don't get why there must be one way that is the only way. But I digress....

I would suggest people give Xubuntu a try, you might be surprised at just how good it is!

DAZ

---

In case you are wondering what desktop manager comes with Unity it is Mutter. This is what the developer says about Mutter:
> Mutter is the clutter-based evolution of Metacity, which, as the
author says, is a "Boring window manager for the adult in you. Many
window managers are like Marshmallow Froot Loops; Metacity is like
Cheerios."

### Post by kansasnoob on 2011-06-03
While performing iso-testing I do use the Unity DE, of course very little multi-tasking is involved - maybe I'll use the screenshot tool, a browser, and the installer at the same time - that's about it.

But I did encounter another annoyance while testing the Oneiric Alpha1 candidate. I use gedit quite a bit, and gedit docs extend to the left edge of the screen so when I move the mouse pointer to the left edge in order to copy-n-paste pre-typed commands the Launcher opens over the doc slowing production to a crawl :(

As an example I use this to change my screen resolution so I can see:

```
xrandr --newmode "1440x900_60.00"  106.50  1440 1528 1672 1904  900 903 909 934 -hsync +vsync
xrandr --addmode VGA1 1440x900_60.00
xrandr --output VGA1 --mode 1440x900_60.00
```

If you paste that into a blank gedit doc you should see what I mean :)

I'll cross-post this at Ubuntu+1.

### Post by heyandy889 on 2011-06-04
Hey, bud. I'm pretty sure that, as you boot and log into Ubuntu you have the option to choose which "user interface" you want. I think you want the "classic" interface. The desktop with the toolbar on the left is called the "Unity" desktop.

So, you're not stuck with Unity.

### Post by kaldor on 2011-06-04
I really recommend that you adjust to the new UI. It's in its infancy right now, but it's going to be the only choice by default in the next release. I much prefer it to Panels, because I can do stuff much quicker via keystrokes. 

To get rid of the new Desktop, log out of your desktop. On the login screen, click your name but DO NOT enter password. On the bottom of the screen, change "Ubuntu Desktop" to "Ubuntu classic" and all will be well.

I really do recommend the new UI over the old one, though.

### Post by Kouki on 2011-06-05
I really liked the look of unity to start with and tried to persevere with it for two weeks.  However, it just didn't work in the way I wanted it to.  It was hard to get to the applications I wanted to, I hated the way the top of windows are incorporated into the top bar and the fact that the side bar wouldn't pop out when a window was maximised.

I didn't like having to keep asking for more applications to be shown and saw no point in taking up a third of my application view with applications that were not installed and which I had no intention of installing.

I had no idea what was meant to replace the 'system' drop down in unity, but without that, Ubuntu's usability was much worse.

Finally today, the buttons to maximise close and minimise windows disappeared, so I switched back to gnome.  It just works as it should do.  I won't be giving unity a go again for some time as for me it just didn't do what I wanted it to.

### Post by viking1au on 2011-06-09
> **slackthumbz said:**
> I finally got round to upgrading my netbook to 10.10 and I have to say I'm fairly unimpressed by the Unity desktop. 

First up, it feels kinda clunky, you have to wait for menus to load and I can't honestly see any advantage to the way it does things. It turns what could be a very small laptop into an appliancey consumer device and to me that just ruins a netbook.

Second, screen real estate, what a waste. Unity takes up a lot of space with that silly sidepane on the left and surely on a netbook you want to maximise your use of all available space? You could get rid of everything in the unity desktop and just provide a basic dock such as AWN or docky to act as the gnome-panel usually would or hell, just use a single gnome-panel at the top of the screen.

I know it's only two issues but they're annoying enough to make me use the normal gnome-session (oh yeah, in the netbook edition I had to manually install gnome-compiz and the various compiz fusion plugins just to get compositing working with desktop effects. WTF). 

Finally, and as far as I know this occurs in the normal desktop edition as well, you can't disable bluetooth via the panel without killing your wireless simultaneously. I've tested this on my netbook and my main machine (AMD64 box) and it occurs on both of them. Seriously annoying.

OK rant over :)
Got totally sick of 11.04 when I upgraded from 9.10, which was excellent. Finally spat the dummy, backed everything up & installed Linux Mint 11. -- Not totally happy, but certainly much more than the way Ubuntu has gone.-- Even got sick of the later Thunderbird so now use Evolution. Big shame all round. - Ubuntu have shot themselves in the foot.

---

"spat the dummy" - made me chuckle. :p

I have started trialling Mint 11 on a separate partition, myself. [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RIOiwg2iHio](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RIOiwg2iHio)

Actually, it is easy being green. I have been impressed by Mint's intuitive menu organisation and general simplicity, and being based on Ubuntu it is all familiar to me under the hood.

What concerns me about Canonical is its focus on Unity. In 11.10 I am sure Canoncial will be heavily focussed on Unity (unless a miracle happens and MS drops it) and I am sceptical that Gnome Shell will get much attention. However, my understanding is that Mint will not be supporting Unity but will be supporting Gnome Shell and since the Mint folk appear to put more care and attention into the user's needs I am wondering if I will get a much better Gnome Shell experience by riding the Mint train. As you say, Mint 11 is a better experience than Natty.

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2011-06-10
I now start off in UNITY ....... and its reasonably stable now after 2 months worth of fixes.
The dock on the left - I use for one thing when I can find it - Workspace Switcher .......
Other than that its hidden .....

On my desktop is one launcher for Gnome-Shell --replace and one launcher for Unity --replace

and I switch between the two ......

I still like the wobbly windows and the cube / 3D effects in UNITY and the gtk record my desktop works better in UNITY than in GNOME-SHELL .....

(But the organization of workspaces in GNOME-SHELL is so easy and just works ..... so I use that)

The Ring Switcher in UNITY sometimes it works sometimes it does not ...... that is a real pain still ...... usually happens after switching between SHELL and UNITY ...... but why I do not know.

Anyway thought I would just say that although I am still not impressed with SQUARES all looking the same on the left in UNITY ..... the desktop now seems to be stable-ish.

You cannot easily improve something based on block squares that all look the same
as they are hard to distinguish from one another ...... but thats what they seem to think
is needed for touch screen ...... to me it is not.

( If Android or any other tablet interface looked like this it would not sell .... square blocks - no design to quickly distinguish -so little visual help for the user )

Once they change the squares to something more distinguishable .... then it may just become easier to find things on it .......

___________________________________________

I have shown my desktop with cairo-dock and squares all over it now to keep the theme ......
at least with Cairo dock they change to cubes and they rotate ..... but still its hard to quickly make 
one thing out from another .......

[COLOR=Red][B][Full Size Link]("http://img600.imageshack.us/img600/6627/screenshot5u.jpg")

Try to Blend the dock on the left in with a THEME ..... best to just HIDE it .....
[/B][/COLOR]

### Post by beew on 2011-06-10
For those who are interested, I get my application menu back in Unity so I don't have to always use the dash to find and open my applications, it is a big improvement. Now Unity is actually quite usable.

[http://www.webupd8.org/2011/06/use-classic-menu-in-unity-classicmenu.html](http://www.webupd8.org/2011/06/use-classic-menu-in-unity-classicmenu.html)

I wish I can give you a screenshot but at this point it is still buggy, one bug being that I can't take screenshots when the menu is displayed, another is that it doesn't work if there are other tray icons of opened applications on the top panel (through whitlisting)

Another option (which I would actually prefer aesthetically) would be to put the menu launcher on the Unity bar, but it takes quite long to open the menu if you do it that way, that is another bug.

 I am sure these things will be sorted out eventually.

The beauty of open source. :)

### Post by beew on 2011-06-10
> **uRock said:**
> The Unity window switcher is much better looking and more productive that the Gnome2 panel windows switcher. I have never bothered with the Cube.

Which window switcher are you talking about? There is the gnome2 desktop switcher which is admittedly ugly but it  doesn't have an equivalent in Unity (Unity has expose desktop, not a switcher), in 10.10 I use the Cairo dock instead of the panel so I have a nice looking and functional desktop switcher ( a better looking dock too)

On the other hand there are several 3 d window switching mechanisms (within the same desktop) in Compiz, they worked for gnome 2 but none work with Unity anymore (probably not even with the Classic Desktop, though I can't say cause I don't use it)

### Post by Bazon on 2011-06-11
> **uRock said:**
> The Unity window switcher is much better looking and more productive that the Gnome2 panel windows switcher.

This is so not true. Because:

1. Window switching in Unity comes always with a useless animation. At least one, it could be more! E.g. when you use the launcher to get to another window: First, the launcher has to reveal again, then the actual window switching animation starts.
On my girlfriends netbook (she uses Unity, I think just to annoy me... ;-) ), only revealing the launcher can take seconds!
So window switching in Unity is slower and therefore less productive.

2. When I have multiple text documents open, the only way to switch between them is hitting the application symbol in the launcher. That opens an eposé like window spread, there all windows are zoomed quite small and the content is not readable any more.
See my screenshot with 6 pdf Files opened: [http://ubuntuone.com/p/hSh/](http://ubuntuone.com/p/hSh/)
So it is nearly impossible to distinguish them and directly select the right one.
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/unity/+bug/734253](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/unity/+bug/734253)
So window switching in Unity is harder and therefore less productive.

---

### Post by YesWeCan on 2011-06-11
> **viking1au said:**
> Yes, I am looking at Mint 11 also. -- Prob with Updater & Synaptic locking yp on me  'cos they are looking for a printer driver that I have put in several times. -- Printer working now but still have this other prob.
I have experienced several problems using Mint 11. I can't tell whether they are Mint specific or shared with Natty.
Before I rolled Compiz back I lost some windows: I had Chromium running an online radio show and I lost the window. Couldn't find it anywhere but I could still hear the radio. I also had the Skype panel icon missing. A couple of times at boot the audio applet crashed and pulse-audio was unresponsive. Also the desktop switcher applet not working properly due to a conflict between Gnome settings and Compiz settings.
Since then, I had flaky video performance with Skype and when I closed the call and then tried to close Skype it froze. I tried to kill it and ended up with a zombie process. Also had another zombie for something else I can't recall. This has never happened on my 10.04 installation.
I also cannot seem to play a CD using Banshee. Might be me but I had to revert to VLC, which is always a pleasure.

My impression so far is that Mint is nicely organised but has a lot of problems which may be common to Natty. So even putting Unity to one side for the moment, neither Mint 11 nor Natty are ready for shipment IMO. They should be called beta versions. Sigh.

Back to 10.04 for now :)

oh oh oh
Bazon & I are on the same page so I'll take a crack at it
I use different workspace for different jobs
I share computers with my wife, her stuff is always on workspace #1  she may have a browser a document & a calculator open in her area
On #2 I have a browser, & whatever I'm reading on calibre
On #3 a document I'm writing & a couple more I referring to
#4 a couple of other documents related to a different aspect of my writing project on #3  torrents I'm seeding
#5 a distro I'm testing 

I don't want all the documents together in a pile to pick through 
I can get up into the 20's in the number of windows, I'm generally switching between 3 or 4 windows on 2 workspaces

after I've worked enough, I pop back on to #2 for a few minutes of entertainment, work stays on 3&4

when I'm working I don't want any notifications from chats or email popping up I need each workspace to be it's own self contained entity

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-06-11

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-06-11
> **Garthhh said:**
> I share computers with my wife, her stuff is always on workspace #1 she may have a browser a document & a calculator open in her area

Don't know what the state of user switching is, but that's what it would be for, so each person can have their own login with their own stuff and switch between without having to log the other person out.

In KDE you have activities, so she could have her activity and you could have yours, but when you switch from one activity to another applications are closed and others started, depending on what was running in that activity the last time it was in use, so not good if you expect to be able to switch to your activity and leave her stuff running or switch to her activity and leave your stuff running.

> when I'm working I don't want any notifications from chats or email popping up I need each workspace to be it's own self contained entity

Wanting each workspace to be contained, relative to windows switching is one thing.

More than that sounds like a KDE thing, where you have more option for setting individual desktops up differently, not sure if that applies to the panel though. Even if it does apply to the panel in KDE, I'm pretty sure it would only apply to the widgets. If an actual application is running for email, instant messaging, etc... I'm pretty sure there will be a notification no matter which desktop you are on.

Even in Gnome Shell where working with less distraction seems to be a higher priority than working efficiently, I don't think notifications can be isolated by desktop.

Later, Seeker

### Post by BobSongs on 2011-06-12
Do I want Unity to be the default desktop?

Hmm.

I'm disappointed that Unity was made the default desktop.

One of Ubuntu's "selling" features was its tiny, subtle UI changes since 5.04. Unity breaks this tradition. Why this bothers me is that the Classic Ubuntu Desktop is still possible under 11.04. The time span that Canonical could reasonably offer the "Classic Ubuntu Desktop" should have determined when Unity would be the default desktop. Let me explain.

Let's say the "Classic" desktop could be offered with 11.04 and 11.11. From 12.04 on: no more Classic desktop. So:

11.04's setup would have given the user a choice box during setup asking which desktop is desired with Classic pre-checked: [x] Classic Ubuntu / [ ] Unity. Two small screenshots illustrate the difference, helping the user decide.

11.10's setup would give the user a choice box during setup  asking which desktop is desired with Unity pre-checked.: [ ] Classic Ubuntu / [x] Unity. Two  small screenshots still provided, continuing to help the user  decide.

12.04 is released the desktop choice option would simply be removed.

---

### Post by BobSongs on 2011-06-12
> **Joe of loath said:**
> So you're saying it would be a good idea for people to be running unsupported software, 30 months after it was discontinued?
From 11.04 to 12.04 is 30 months? Are you being deliberately provocative?

Ubuntu currently (11.04) supports the "Classic Desktop". So this "unsupported" software is getting 6 months support. And if the "Classic" desktop is offered again with 11.10 (at this point I'm unsure, but it's possible) then where is your argument?

We're assuming here that Gnome isn't going to lift a finger to give fixes and updates of 2.x. I think they realise there's a large user base still depending on those updates.

EDIT: With the amount of time Microsoft supported Windows XP, I think the Linux community will still want to support the older desktop until the new is established and accepted.

---

### Post by Joe of loath on 2011-06-12
> **BobSongs said:**
> From 11.04 to 12.04 is 30 months? Are you being deliberately provocative?

Ubuntu currently (11.04) supports the "Classic Desktop". So this "unsupported" software is getting 6 months support. And if the "Classic" desktop is offered again with 11.10 (at this point I'm unsure, but it's possible) then where is your argument?

No, 6 months between 11.04 and 11.10, plus 18 months of support thereafter. That's two years someone could potentially be using Gnome 2 after it's been EOL.

My argument is, if it were a two year old old Firefox, everyone would be up in arms about how outdated it is. Every other distro with a short release cycle (Fedora, opensuse etc) has, or will next release, drop Gnome 2 in favour of Gnome 3. Considering the Gnome devs probably won't be working on bugfixes and security patches. That's potentially a lot of zero day exploits out there that won't be fixed upstream. It simply isn't possible to keep Gnome 2 in the repos past 11.04, since everyone else will be using GTK3.

---

### Post by BobSongs on 2011-06-14
> **kansasnoob said:**
> You forget 10.04 altogether, being LTS it's supported until April 2013 ;)

That's a lot of time for a lot of people to tweak gnome3's forced fallback, which was already pretty good until they added gnome-panel version 3.

That was the change that convinced me to do most of my development testing using Lubuntu, but if all else fails Lucid is still a great viable option :D

@** Joe of loath**:

April, 2013. Canonical is committed to supporting the Gnome2 desktop environment for that long.

If this is the case, it's quite possible Canonical will include the Classic desktop in the 11.10 release as well. Which backs up my point nicely.

The poll at the beginning of this thread asked whether we wanted Unity as the desktop for the release of 11.04. It was just a poll. My response to that poll was basically saying that it isn't typical of Canonical to do such a push as to force all users to use Unity.

I can understand the argument that says Unity needs its beta testers. But Unity still feels rushed. And Ubuntu doesn't need (at this point in time or at any other point) a "Windows Vista-like" release.

I'd like to know where the documentation is that Gnome2/GTK2 is currently **unsupported**. My *impression* is that though Gnome3 is the latest desktop, Gnome2 will have a decent amount of future support. If Microsoft can support Windows XP for 5 years and then add a few more years on because of Vista's delayed release--making the total support time quite long, certainly Gnome isn't going to simply drop all future support for their very popular desktop because they launched a new product.

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2011-06-14
Here's Unity for the short-sighted. Take the bottom panel from Gnome 2 away and replace it with an intellihiding dock on the left, put a global menu up top, and allow the user to save more vertical screen space than any other mainstream OS.

How does this make it harder or less useful on a desktop. You can type a few letters to get to your applications if that's what you were thinking- if you think that's less efficient than scrubbing through a menu (although it may be less convenient if you don't know what you're looking for), then I guess you might be right. Xfce for you.

I actually kind of appreciate how Unity and Gnome 3 are dividing up the users out there. It's getting all those people who want more plain and simple configurations to look elsewhere, where it's most powerful and efficient, and now users who have been tweaking Gnome 2 to have more modern functionality finally have it built into one of two fabulous environments.

I'm sure Unity works great on netbooks, but I don't see anything inherent to the design that would make it unwieldy for desktop use. It's not Android people, wake up.

(this coming from a happy Gnome 3 user- Unity rocks, and it's probably my next choice)

---
