### Post by akelsall on 2011-06-15
Well, I decided to try Natty/Unity last night, and I hate to say that after 15 minutes of use, I was ready to chuck it out the window (and yes, 15 minutes IS enough time to know whether or not the interface makes "sense"). Here are things that annoyed me about Unity:

If you add too many items to the launcher bar, it begins squishing the icons at the bottom to make room for items you're adding. There's no way I could see to add "sub-folders" to group icons together on the launcher bar). 

When you initially install 11.04, you will find the Firefox has an add-on that causes the menu items to appear in the top panel of your window (NOT in the Firefox window, as you would expect to see). Not sure why the developers would think that it's more convenient to go to the very top panel on your desktop instead of having the menu items on the Firefox window itself. Just Google "Global Menu Bar Inegration" to see how to fix this. You'll see the same issue with the gedit window. 

Clicking the upper-left Ubuntu icon, it brings up shortcuts and a search bar. When I click on something with sub-folders, (such as Media Apps), there's no way that I can see to go back (up) one level to where I just was. Also, If I have this window open and click on anything else (e.g. the desktop), it disappears. My opinion is it should stay there until closed, but that's persoanl preference. 

Finding something as simple as a terminal window is not intuitive. The best way I found to get to it is to click on the Ubuntu icon in the upper left-hand corner, search for gnome-terminal, and drag it to the Launcher bar on the left. That's all fine and dandy, but come on. Can't they put something like gnome-terminal on the launcher bar, or somewhere that's easy to find?

I miss having the two panels (top and bottom) to pin stuff to. As far as I can tell, with Unity, all you can do is pin stuff to the Launcher bar on the left-hand side of the screen. 

Clicking on Libre Writer multiple times does NOT open up multiple windows (to open multiple windows, you must open Libre Writer, then open a new window (File | New or Window | New). 

I will definitely NOT be upgrading to 11.04 until I've had a chance to play with Gnome3 and see if it's "usable". I just don't get why people like Unity. Maybe someone can explain what's so "great" about it and enlighten me.

About the only thing I did like about 11.04 was the way they implemented the virtual desktops.

---

### Post by capink on 2011-06-15
Unity is still in its infancy, it is pretty much work in progress. However, it does show promise even though it might not be ready to use for some users in its current state. But to dismiss it entirely is premature.

I like Unity because it is simple yet beautiful. It tries to save as much screen space. It also allow the user to launch applications and switch between them using the keyboard in an effective manner.

For example, I have the launcher for the terminal pinned to bar on the left in position number 3. So if I need to launch it I just press  SUPER+3. If I switch to another application and then want to bring the terminal back to the foreground all I have to do is to press SUPER+3. And if I want to open a new terminal window, I press SUPER+shift+3.

Actually, I stopped opening new windows for applications that use tabs (browser, file manager and terminal). That way I can switch between the windows and tabs using the keyboard so easily.


> **akelsall said:**
> There's no way I could see to add "sub-folders" to group icons together on the launcher bar).

That is a valid point. Unity devs should take this into consideration. Unity is still work in progress.

> **akelsall said:**
> Not sure why the developers would think that it's more convenient to go to the very top panel on your desktop

I think the global menu was originally implented by apple if an not mistaken. It is meant to save screen space which important especially in tablets and netbooks. I personally like it. It is not probelm for me because I access the menus using the keyboard (ALT+F).

> **akelsall said:**
> When I click on something with sub-folders, (such as Media Apps), there's no way that I can see to go back (up) one level to where I just was.

Another valid point which could improve Unity further.

> **akelsall said:**
> Finding something as simple as a terminal window is not intuitive. The best way I found to get to it is to click on the Ubuntu icon in the upper left-hand corner, search for gnome-terminal, and drag it to the Launcher bar on the left.
 
I you press on SUPER key and just type 't' you will see that terminal is the first app to appear, you just press enter and it will launch


> **akelsall said:**
> Clicking on Libre Writer multiple times does NOT open up multiple windows (to open multiple windows, you must open Libre Writer, then open a new window (File | New or Window | New).

If you middle click the icon it will open a new window.Or as explained above, you can press SUPER+shift+<app number in the bar>


Edit: One more point. Unity gives ubuntu character and differentiates it from other OSs. I am happy Linux is finally trying to innovate and not just replicate other OSs.

Also everyone is trying to make their system run on a variety of form factors especially touch screens. The direction Microsoft is taking with Windows 8 is not much different. The interfaces are going to change and so will the users. When I first came to linux, I spent a great deal of time trying to make everything look and behave exactly like Windows, but linux eventually taught me to accept change and give it fair chance. It was an important lesson not just in computers but also in life.

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-06-15
> **Garthhh said:**
> I realize everything between LTS's is a beta
I'm distro & DE shopping on a VB to decide what's next for me

There is a difference between being beta and not being feature complete.

There is a development arc that goes from LTS to LTS, which includes the development cycles that produce each 6 month release, all of these are supported non-beta releases.

Any version of Ubuntu even the LTS releases may include something that is beta, it just depends on what fits the priorities relative to the development goals and support goals of each release.


If *your* priority is a stable environment, with less crashes, less regressions, etc... then you should stick with LTS releases, and it may be suggested to wait one to two months after an LTS has been released to update to it as well.

If *your* priority is keeping more up to date with recent developments, then you have to accept that with less stability (stability being determined by the rate at which new or changed software is introduced) the more there will be things that crash, don't work at all, or work but not as expected, which is independent of whether the included software is considered stable, rc, beta, etc....

New or changed software is introduced more in non-LTS releases, introduced less during the development cycle of an LTS release where higher priority is choosing versions of stuff that can be supported over the long haul, polishing existing features and finding more of the harder to find corner case bugs.

If *your* priority is a stable environment, with less crashes, less regressions, etc... then you should stick with LTS releases, and it may be suggested to wait one to two months after an LTS has been released to update to it as well.

Later, Seeker

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-06-15
> **Garthhh said:**
> We had this same discussion a few pages back
call em not feature complete if you want, it's still a Beta
from what you wrote, even the LTS is a Beta for a couple of months
we're all testers, which is fine

That's an over simplification at best.

If you are going to use that logic, then you would have to call all *.0 releases of commercial software beta releases too.

Gstreamer has been in development for quite a while now, having several stable releases, but still has not been given a 1.0 version number because it is not feature complete.

No matter if it is an Ubuntu LTS release or not, commercial software or open source, there will always be bugs that are not caught during testing because there are not enough people during testing running some combination of hardware and/or software and/or using some combination of features in the software to trigger the bug, or the bug gets triggered but not often enough to get the information necessary to figure out what is going on. There will be bugs considered high priority but not release critical so fixing can be pushed until after release if necessary. There will be bugs that are considered low priority so if they get fixed they get fixed if they don't it's OK to live with the resulting issues for an extended period of time.

During the first month or two after a release the bulk of the developer effort will be in stamping out the remaining high priority bugs that were pushed until after the release and prioritizing and dealing with new bugs that were not discovered during the development cycle because there is always stuff that is not discovered until the software is released to a larger audience.

All Linux distributions have to deal with the issues of how often to release, how long to support a release and how best to deal with the trade offs that result from the release and support schedules they have chosen or lack of schedules in the case of distributions that employ a 'release when ready' policy or that provide a rolling release. 

Some amount of the issues result from the nature of Linux distributions in providing a managed environment where the goals are to reduce the amount of stuff you have to go outside of official distribution channels to get and to maximize the use of shared stuff used by the software provided through the official distribution channels.

If Natty had been released with a fully working Gnome 3 development platform and desktop, there probably would have been just as many complaints as in the current situation because not only would there be a lot of people who don't like Gnome shell but some software that is not yet compatible would have to be omitted or specific features within software disabled because of compatibility issues. 

Distributions could employ a tactic of maximizing the use of shared stuff for a smaller set of core packages and packaging additional stuff in a more version neutral way, but that would have it's own set of trade offs that may or may not make things better.

Later, Seeker

### Post by cascade9 on 2011-06-18
> **Joe of loath said:**
> I'm going to say it again, if something is licensed at GPL, it *cannot* be made proprietary. Now, the MIT and BSD licenses are different, but Unity is mostly GPL.

Sorry, but wrong. GPLed code can be made proprietary. Since canonical is the only copyright holder for projects under the canonical contributor agreement, they legally can make a closed source version. 

> **So if I release something under the GPL, I can never take it back?**

  If you own the copyright on a piece of code, and you release it under the GPL, then you can never revoke this right you gave to others. You can choose to stop distributing the source code, but if someone obtained it from you, they can then choose to continue distribution without your ongoing permission. In that sense, you can't take it back.

  **However, if you are still the copyright holder, you can certainly choose to distribute the source code under a [B]*different*** license. [/B]



No one else can do that on your behalf! Only you, the copyright holder, can. This is how certain developers choose to release source code under a "double-license."  
[http://openacs.org/about/licensing/open-source-licensing](http://openacs.org/about/licensing/open-source-licensing)

*bolding in quoted text by me* 

I'm suprised that you havent run across this before, there are lots pages (some by fairly big names in FOSS) about contributor agreements in general, and the canonical contributor agreement in particular. Here is just one- 

[http://itmanagement.earthweb.com/article.php/3904526](http://itmanagement.earthweb.com/article.php/3904526)

It would be pointless (and laughable) to list 'Canonical could use your contributions  in a proprietary program' in the FAQ about the canonical contributor agreement if it wasnt possible.

### Post by Joe of loath on 2011-06-18
So why does it say this in the GPL?

> 5. Conveying Modified Source Versions.

You may convey a work based on the Program, or the modifications to produce it from the Program, in the form of source code under the terms of section 4, provided that you also meet all of these conditions:

    a) The work must carry prominent notices stating that you modified it, and giving a relevant date.
    b) The work must carry prominent notices stating that it is released under this License and any conditions added under section 7. This requirement modifies the requirement in section 4 to “keep intact all notices”.
**    c) You must license the entire work, as a whole, under this License to anyone who comes into possession of a copy. This License will therefore apply, along with any applicable section 7 additional terms, to the whole of the work, and all its parts, regardless of how they are packaged. This License gives no permission to license the work in any other way, but it does not invalidate such permission if you have separately received it.**
    d) If the work has interactive user interfaces, each must display Appropriate Legal Notices; however, if the Program has interactive interfaces that do not display Appropriate Legal Notices, your work need not make them do so.

A compilation of a covered work with other separate and independent works, which are not by their nature extensions of the covered work, and which are not combined with it such as to form a larger program, in or on a volume of a storage or distribution medium, is called an “aggregate” if the compilation and its resulting copyright are not used to limit the access or legal rights of the compilation's users beyond what the individual works permit. Inclusion of a covered work in an aggregate does not cause this License to apply to the other parts of the aggregate.

### Post by YesWeCan on 2011-06-18
*from: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gnu_public_license](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gnu_public_license)*
> **Copyright holders**

The text of the GPL is itself copyrighted, and the copyright is held by the Free Software Foundation. However, the FSF does not hold the copyright for a work released under the GPL, unless an author explicitly assigns copyrights to the FSF (which seldom happens except for programs that are part of the GNU project). Only the individual copyright holders have the authority to sue when a license violation takes place.
The FSF permits people to create new licenses based on the GPL, as long as the derived licenses do not use the GPL preamble without permission. This is discouraged, however, since such a license is generally incompatible with the GPL.[38] (See the GPL FAQ for more information.) It also causes license proliferation.
Other licenses created by the GNU project include the GNU Lesser General Public License and the GNU Free Documentation License.

The copyright holder has the power.
This is why Canonical needs contributors to sign their copyrights over.

> **Merk42 said:**
> .I know right? Unity cost me so much money, oh wait ["Ubuntu releases will always be free"]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/MarkShuttleworth").

That linked articale is exactly why I dont trust canonical. So you've got shuttleworth saying-

> I have no interest in taking Ubuntu to join the proprietary software industry

But then canonical will try legalistics with the canonical contributor agreement, and then explicitly state in the contributor agreement FAQ that they have the right to use contributions in a proprietary program

>  Canonical could use your contributions  in a proprietary program[http://www.canonical.com/contributors/faq](http://www.canonical.com/contributors/faq)

I can see various way that both statements can be true. I personally believe that a lot of the comments that mr. shuttleowrth makes are purely for mass consumption, misdirection and 'spin'. 

Not like it would be the 1st time that canonical has spun, sometimes to the point where its either lying or shows a total lack of knowledge about licencing and code- 

> Congratulations to Google on the open sourcing of[ Google Chrome OS ]("http://www.chromium.org/chromium-os/building-chromium-os")[http://blog.canonical.com/2009/11/19/google-chrome-os-and-canonical/](http://blog.canonical.com/2009/11/19/google-chrome-os-and-canonical/)

ChomeOS (and browser for that matter) is NOT 'open source' in any way. How somebody involved in open source can make a 'mistake' like that is beyond me....

### Post by BobSongs on 2011-06-20
This is how I see it.

Ubuntu -- from **Warty Warthog** to **Maverick Meerkat** -- went through slow but steady UI changes. Nothing drastic from release to release. Essentially this was because GNOME supported and developed GNOME 2.

With GNOME 3's pending release, Canonical expressed its UI disagreements while GNOME developers went ahead. Canonical decided the remedy was to produce their own desktop (based heavily on GNOME3).

[SIZE=3][COLOR=Gray]**result**[/COLOR][/SIZE]
GNOME3 on one side, Unity on the other with me (and many others like me) in the middle -- not convinced that either direction is helpful.

Perhaps these two Desktop Environments offer excellent UI benefits. And while those benefits sit there waiting for everyone to enjoy, GNOME 2 users express their frustration and reluctance, a reluctance similarly voiced by Windows XP users forced to wrestle with Vista's jarring changes.

Oh, it's easy to sit and pontificate Unity's amazing changes. However these papal statements often come from Linux users who can devote a greater amount of time to exploring a UI. GNOME 2 users (with less time to devote to such endeavors) simply "want it to work", and by "work" they mean "work like it always did, thank you."

Now we arrive at many crossroads.

[SIZE=3][COLOR=Gray]**canonical's**[/COLOR][/SIZE]

[LIST]
[*]Will they put their ear to the ground, listen to user frustrations and focus on a combination of stability and customization, giving Unity a GNOME 2 feel?
[*]Will they express a disdain for their disgruntled user-base by giving glowing reports about Unity's "amazing success" while plodding along with Unity's fine tuning? (Historians should recall how Microsoft considered the removal of Vista for XP as a Vista sale when an OEM downgraded, counting an XP sale as a part of the Vista success story.)
[/LIST]
We'll have to wait and see. The next release will tell all.

[SIZE=3][COLOR=Gray]**users**[/COLOR][/SIZE]

[LIST]
[*]Will they simply upgrade and "suck it up, princess"?
[*]Will they download and install an alternate desktop such as XFCE or KDE? Or they might possibly even test GNOME 3. (I've heard glowing reports of Ubuntu users testing Fedora 15 Live! CD)
[*]Will they step further away from Unity by turning to Linux Mint for temporary relief? (Eventually Mint will have to decide what to do with GNOME 3/Unity).
[*]Or will they simply give up on Linux altogether, opting for a less stable, poorly designed platform that may not be ready for the desktop... Like Windows 7, for example?
[/LIST]
[SIZE=3][COLOR=Gray]**blame**[/COLOR][/SIZE]
True: Canonical isn't to blame for all these crossroads. For that I look to GNOME's team for stripping away all user customization.


My decision? I have decided **against **Unity after testing it. Quite simply: I feel like I'm wrestling with the UI more than I'm effectively using it. Feel free to credit that to "muscle memory" -- like riding a bike or playing my guitar. (Telling me that my guitar would work better if the strings were reversed, and saying it with enthusiasm and a positive tone of voice might convince others you're being very helpful and proactive. But you'd still be wrong. And my growling disagreement would not make me a troll.)

[SIZE=3][COLOR=Gray]**conclusion**[/COLOR][/SIZE]
I will join the many who have tested Fedora 15 while waiting to see what Unity looks like in 11.10. If Canonical takes the Vista path by refusing to modify this Desktop Environment based on user reviews and complaints, then I'll examine other solutions.

[SIZE=3][COLOR=Gray]** plain and simple**[/COLOR][/SIZE]
This isn't about "loyalty to Ubuntu". Please, let's not make this the issue. Canonical has offered a desktop UI as an option. Not taking it and trying another flavour of Linux doesn't make someone a deserter. With hundreds of distributions of Linux available, moving from one to another should not constitute a crime.

### Post by Larkspur on 2011-06-20
> **Claus7 said:**
> Hello,

how can someone open a terminal from unity's interface? Step by step please, as some people in their machine were unable to find out how (they were newbies of course). And I was unable to say how just by searching the forums.

There are two ways to launch a terminal:

1) Press the super key and start typing "terminal"
2) Hold down Ctrl+Shift+T

When you've opened it, right-click the launcher icon in the bar and select "Keep in Launcher".  To open multiple Terminal windows either use Ctrl+Shift+T or middle-click the icon.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-06-20
> **Claus7 said:**
> Hello,

how can someone open a terminal from unity's interface? Step by step please, as some people in their machine were unable to find out how (they were newbies of course). And I was unable to say how just by searching the forums.

...Why do we need to have a MEGA thread about unity? Is this supposed to support the touch screen thing advertised in the new windows?

Sorry if my questions are off-topic. If they are, just ignore them. 

Regards!

Click the Applications icon in the Launcher and type **ter** to open a terminal. Once you have a terminal running, just right-click it's icon in the Launcher, and select **Keep in Launcher** 

Unity wasn't designed for a touch screen, this is just a rumor or assumption that has been perpetrated and many people have run with the assumption.

For those that still want to use the retro 2 panel interface, it is still available as a fallback mode in Gnome-shell, the Ubuntu implementation is broken in Oneiric at the moment, but it will be ready for usage by the time it is released.

### Post by Larkspur on 2011-06-20
> **Claus7 said:**
> Hello,thank you for your immediate responses. Just, out of curiosity: I cannot launch the terminal from...some menu or something?

Regards!

You've hit upon one of the reasons a lot of people dislike Unity.  The new "menu" is what appears when you hit the super key.  It's called the Dash, and instead of finding programs by going through conventional menus, you just type the name of the program and press enter.  Some people don't like to do this.  If you don't like it, you can either log out and choose the "Classic Gnome" option or run the command "gnome-panel" to get the old menu.

EDIT:

> eh... if I may ask: what retro 2 panel interface is supposed to do?

He means the panels from earlier versions of Ubuntu.

### Post by thejasper on 2011-06-22
1 minute. That is how long it took me to decide to get rid of unity. Actually it was probably faster. You may accuse me of not giving Unity a chance but for me there were two UI changes which are completely unacceptable. These choices are so basic that I cannot believe there is much good in the rest of the interface.

What two things do I hate so much that I won't even give Unity a try?

Menu's divorced from windows. This is an Apple heresy which I have always considered to be a major bug in their interface (and/or thought proces). My menu is part of my window. An application doesn't get to take over my whole machine or pretend it is the only thing running. Put the menu with window. There is no other choice.

Dock clone. Wow. Lots of HUGE icons so that all my nice small icons which I had on my top bar now scroll off the screen. Oh wait, I got all these other useless program icons stuffed in there as well. Not to mention that it hides the fact that there is a difference between a running process and a program. My process could be multiple windows. I could have multiple instances of the same program. I hate the dock on macs. On windows 7 the first thing I did was to get my quick launch bar back and to unpin everything.

I don't need to test anything more in Unity. These two issues are absolute deal killers. Don't need to know if these can or can't be disabled. I can use a decent window manager and not have these problems.

Oh, and somehow FF has become the default browser on my system. Don't knwo whether I have to blame Unity or Ubuntu for this but it certainly wasn't the case before I 'upgraded'.

### Post by MARP1961 on 2011-06-23
I am mystified as to why Ubuntu has developed it's own Unity shell after a long and successful partnership with Gnome. I realise that Ubuntu 11.10 will run on the new Gnome 3 but will use the Unity interface as default. 

After installing Ubuntu 11.04 I have grown quite used to Unity, but don't like the clumsy way it uses workspaces (after the simple panel switcher on Classic Gnome 2). I also dislike the appearance and behaviour of the side panel. Having said all this and not wanting to get left behind, I stuck with it and have been growing more used to everything....until two days ago! Curiosity made me burn a Fedora 15 CD. Wow! Is that really Gnome Shell? It instantly looked and felt more polished than Unity.

I wanted to stay with Ubuntu, so I added the Gnome Shell via the Gnome 3 Team ppa. This allows me to login as 'Gnome' and everything seems to work (except for autologin, shutdown option and other minor glitches). 

The much better appearance (I've managed to enable different themes) and the vastly superior way it handles workspaces in the background are a deal clincher for me. I look forward to 11.10 now, in the knowledge that Gnome Shell will be simpler to add (via the repositories rather than the ppa).

Why did Ubuntu go down the Unity route? If you're impatient, then try out Fedora 15 like I did. If you like what you see but want to stick with Ubuntu, then install Gnome Shell via ppa. The more cautious will wait for 11.10 and find the treat that awaits them if they ditch fusty old Unity for snappy new Gnome Shell. 

I'm sold! What about the rest of you?

---

### Post by sanderd17 on 2011-06-23
I also use gnome-shell on Ubuntu, but I used a separate install for it: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1754198](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1754198)

I like the shell a lot and I'm waiting for 11.10 to put it on some other machines.

btw, the not having a shutdown option is a feature, not a bug :P, the gnome team wants to get rid of "things that are technically different but serve the same purpose".

So they dropped the shut down button, because a user just wants to leave his computer, so shutdown, suspend and hibernate make no difference.
They dropped the minimize button because it is only used to get rid of a certain screen, so very similar to the close button.
...

But if someone wants it back, they are only a few clicks away.

EDIT: my response may look a bit weird here, but I responded to MARP1961 who created a separate thread. But now, the threads seem merged.

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-06-23
> **MARP1961 said:**
> Try 'new Gnome' that's Gnome 3 Shell. A live Fedora 15 disk should convince you that Unity is second rate.

I'm afraid I have to agree.  I just tried the Fedora 15 LiveCD and am still in shock.  At least now I can see what the Unity developers are aiming for.  

At times I've been fairly vocal about the shortcomings of Unity, and some of these things are addressed in Gnome 3:

* I love the way the workspace selector comes up with the launcher
* I was able to navigate workspaces, move windows between workspaces, change between applications, and generally rearrange things all intuitively and very quickly.  NO keyboard shortcuts required
* I love that however many workspaces you have, it always adds a free blank one at the end, ready to use.
* You don't need a keyboard shortcut to open a new instance of an application that is already running.
* No horrible Dash with one inch borders.  Instead, the same functions are provided seamlessly and with some extra functionality.
* It's probably more configurable going on what people are saying: that's not really important to me because apart from a few minor things that I'd like to tweak Gnome 3 is very nice as it is.  I don't really want to spend hours (or days) tweaking a system - there's more important things to be doing.
* Icons and graphics are all polished and nice to look at.

On the downside (in my opinion) both Unity and Gnome 3 have completely foregone the concept of the object oriented desktop.  I'm not convinced this is a logical or desirable move.  The OO Desktop works very well for many people, and for those who do not use their computer this way the fact that the desktop _can_ be used should have no bearing on how they work.


Chris

---

### Post by bulldog on 2011-06-23
If you're trying Gnome-Shell,take a look at the 'cheat-sheet',maybe there's something of use in it:
[http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell/CheatSheet](http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell/CheatSheet)

Also there are a lot of extensions,which can make live easier:
[http://www.fpmurphy.com/gnome-shell-extensions/](http://www.fpmurphy.com/gnome-shell-extensions/)

If you have questions about extensions or Gnome-Shell,or maybe even want to try a Ubuntu and UGR based ISO,both 32 and 64 bit versions available for download,which have Unity and Gnome-Shell both working nicely together,and even a gnome fallback mode,read this thread!
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1754198](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1754198)

There are tar.gz available with themes and extensions which let you make your Gnome-Shell experience easy and fast.

If you can spare the time and you're curious to know more about Ubuntu-UGR  Gnome-Shell ISO,made by cbowman57,come to this thread.

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-06-23
> **Larkspur said:**
> You've hit upon one of the reasons a lot of people dislike Unity.  The new "menu" is what appears when you hit the super key.  It's called the Dash, and instead of finding programs by going through conventional menus, you just type the name of the program and press enter.  Some people don't like to do this.  If you don't like it, you can either log out and choose the "Classic Gnome" option or run the command "gnome-panel" to get the old menu.

Of course if you prefer the menu type experience. 

When you click on 'More Apps' in the Dash or click the 'Applications' icon on the Launcher Panel, you can select 'Accessories --> Installed [See X More Results]' and click on the terminal icon there.

If you right click the Applications launcher icon you can go directly to any of the categories.

Later, Seeker

### Post by beew on 2011-06-24
> **collisionystm said:**
> Well I feel pretty guilty right now. I started using Fedora 15 with Gnome3. I dont think I am coming back any time soon. 

Ubuntu needs Gnome3 .

I have to take back everything bad I ever said about it. I just watned to let everyone know that is curious to try it....

Installing the Gnome3 PPA on natty just doesnt cut it. Use a Fedora 15 live cd and give it a shot. I truly enjoy it and it makes computing so much easier.

Oh and using YUM really isnt bad at all. Its not more different than apt-get

Just fire up a terminal and use

yum search ( search for a package ) 

sudo yum upgrade ( upgrade your packages ) same as sudo apt-get update

sudo yum install ( install packages ) same as sudo apt-get install


But anyways, Just thought I would share. 

I do like Unity, I just prefer to use Gnome 3  right now. I used Unity for the past month and I really like the breath of fresh air that Gnome brings.

Rock on Ubuntu! :guitar:

Thanks for the sharing. I actually have a partition running F15, but I have to say I prefer Unity, maybe because I have spent a lot more time on it so am more used to it. G3 is not as bad as I thought originally, but neither is Unity (after adding some third party tweaks)

And for those who want a nice package manager with GUI install yumex, it is much better than the default Add/Remove from Fedora, it is like Synaptic for Debian distros (but Synaptic is still better)

### Post by babuba on 2011-06-24
> **MARP1961 said:**
> 
After installing Ubuntu 11.04 I have grown quite used to Unity, but  don't like the clumsy way it uses workspaces (after the simple panel  switcher on Classic Gnome 2).
It`s subjective. For me in Unity workspaces become useful for the first time. I have not much use of workspaces  in Gnom 2, because using panel  switcher was disruptive to workflow. It is hard to concentrate on work,  and remember which windows on which workspaces are opened, and windows list  and windows selector simply provide poor representation of  many open  windows of the same program. On the contrary, in Unity+Compiz there is  nice preview of open windows. So I use workspaces switcher only for  rearranging windows on different workspaces. For example, if I need to  see simultaneously two or more windows, I put them side by side without  overlapping on separate workspace and forget on which workspace I put  them, since once windows are arranged, it is possible to simply use  launcher. Workspaces became working layers. Using shortcuts for  arranging windows in tiling manner it is possible for me to  avoid overlapping windows entirely. 
So I think it is subjective, that Unity uses workspaces in clumsy way, it depends from particular workflow.

### Post by wolfen69 on 2011-06-25
I wasn't crazy about it at first, but I revisited a month later and was pleasantly surprised. It's a departure from what we're used to, but it's not life altering, or enough to make me crazy. It's just a desktop environment. I take pride in knowing *all of them*. ;)

### Post by wolfen69 on 2011-06-25
> **thejasper said:**
> 1 minute. That is how long it took me to decide to get rid of unity. Actually it was probably faster. You may accuse me of not giving Unity a chance but for me there were two UI changes which are completely unacceptable. These choices are so basic that I cannot believe there is much good in the rest of the interface.

What two things do I hate so much that I won't even give Unity a try?

Menu's divorced from windows. This is an Apple heresy which I have always considered to be a major bug in their interface (and/or thought proces). My menu is part of my window. An application doesn't get to take over my whole machine or pretend it is the only thing running. Put the menu with window. There is no other choice.

Dock clone. Wow. Lots of HUGE icons so that all my nice small icons which I had on my top bar now scroll off the screen. Oh wait, I got all these other useless program icons stuffed in there as well. Not to mention that it hides the fact that there is a difference between a running process and a program. My process could be multiple windows. I could have multiple instances of the same program. I hate the dock on macs. On windows 7 the first thing I did was to get my quick launch bar back and to unpin everything.

I don't need to test anything more in Unity. These two issues are absolute deal killers. Don't need to know if these can or can't be disabled. I can use a decent window manager and not have these problems.

Oh, and somehow FF has become the default browser on my system. Don't knwo whether I have to blame Unity or Ubuntu for this but it certainly wasn't the case before I 'upgraded'.

WOW. Well said! ;)

---

### Post by Sven6210 on 2011-06-27
I using Ubuntu since 8.10 and very happy with it. With 10.04 I moved from Windows 7 to Ubuntu and never regretted it. I still have my Windows 7 as a dual boot available but I only start it once a month and then it takes quite some time just to get all necessary updates.

And yes, I also like 11.04 very much. It runs well on my master laptop and after one week I started to like the new unity interface a lot. But I also admit that not all about 11.04 is great.


[LIST=1]
[*]I would love to add the "window-picker-applet" to the top panel - I am really missing it there when working with several applications and I added the "window-picker-applet" to all my Gnome desktops before (I guess since 9.04).
[*]The WiFi causes problems with my EeePC 900a. While my EeePC worked perfectly with 10.04 the new 11.04 allows to switch off WiFi but not to switch it on again. I always need to reboot to get WiFi back
[/LIST]
Even though I have some difficulties I still like 11.04 and I am looking forward to 11.10 and the next improvements.

### Post by Bazon on 2011-06-29
I really liked Gnome2's usability and Ubuntu's graphical design, but knowing Gnome2 won't be supported any more removes the option keeping it.

So I did:

**On my netbook**:
Luntuntu with a Gnome2 like panel setup *(only the workspace switcher is in the upper panel so there's more space for the useful window-switcher in the bottom panel)* with ambiance theme.
Looks [awesome](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10856275&postcount=569) and is totally snappy fast. :-)

**On my sofa computer**:
Xubuntu with Compiz and Cairo-Dock as the only visible element on the blank desktop and ambiance theme. Cairo-Docks configurability is great and Compiz is so much faster without Unity, it's amazing! :-)

**On my laptop**:
Good old 10.04 with Gnome2. I reverted from 10.10 as sound didn't work after suspend there. 10.04 works still like a good reliable horse... :-)

### Post by BrokenKingpin on 2011-06-29

I am currently running:

- Main Desktop - Mint 11 (Gnome2)
- Media Server - Xubuntu 11.04 (Xfce)
- Netbook - Lubuntu 11.04 (LXDE)
- Laptop - Kubuntu 11.04 (KDE)

### Post by CraigPaleo on 2011-06-30
> **forrestcupp said:**
> I just installed Ubuntu for the first time in a couple of years. Up until now, I've scoffed at all of the people whining about Unity. Now that I've actually tried it, you can add me to the list of people who hate Unity. :)

I'm happy for the people who like Unity, but for my needs, it's crap. It's too hard to find things that aren't readily available, and having the app menus in the panel is not a great idea.

It didn't take me long to install Xubuntu-Desktop, and I'm liking it a lot.

That just cracks me up! Maybe because you're a long-time member and you're always posting screen shots of Win7 and saying your window manager is DWM. I never knew if you were really serious. 

> **oldos2er said:**
> I switched to KDE last year, when I saw the writing on the wall for Gnome2 and I wanted to avoid Unity. Very pleased with KDE.

 I came from Mac about three years ago. Everything I'd seen and read about KDE and its technology made it seem like the bees knees. BUT, It wasn't usable then so I used Gnome and became used to it. I switched to KDE at about 4.4. I do distro hop but KDE just feels like home to me. One of the biggest impacts that both KDE and Gnome had on me was the menus in the windows. Once I stopped instinctively clicking on a window and throwing the cursor to the top left of the screen and got used to it, it just made sense. Single clicking in KDE makes sense. 

I used to think docks were all the rage. How pretty... NO! Now that I can have everything on one panel at the top of the screen, where my pointer is closest to most of the time, I don't miss docks at all. 

I do like the idea of Ubuntu but they just need to think about the defaults for desktops and even laptops.

### Post by Santaji on 2011-06-30
My first impression of Unity was good, I felt it had some usability issues that could be fixed, But overall it was a improvement over the old Gnome 2 interface. But now after using Gnome 3/Shell i feel Unity is not as good, And Canonical is making a mistake by not using the default Gnome 3 shell. Unity feels like they just looked at Windows 7 and Mac OS X and copied the bits they liked and added some of their own touches to it. 
Gnome shell feels like they started from scratch and tried to design a "completely new computing experience"(Like they say on their website).
After using Gnome 3/Shell on Fedora 15 for about a week, I feel it is the best Desktop UI i have ever used, As good as, or maybe better then Mac OS X. It's just easy to use, and fast. You don't have to think much when using it, The UI does not get in your way at all, Launching apps and switching between open Windows is much better then in Unity and the old Gnome panels.

### Post by Nitrozzy7 on 2011-07-01
Customisation. And I'm not talking just about the colours, the icons, the size. I'm talking about dynamic shaping, positioning and functions.
I want to have the option to turn it into a wheel and place it at a random spot on screen or even link it to a declared shape in a wallpaper I just downloaded that make some pretty funny effects when I pass my cursor above it. Fixing the random cursor bug would also be nice.
I want it to have multiple functions instead of being just a luncher.
In addition I want easy tools to customise it and for my crappy laptop to run it just fine. I want it to provide me with the option to cycle wallpapers stored on my PC or right of the internet. I also want it to provide dynamic themes and the tools to create some of my own.

---

### Post by sumadartson on 2011-07-02
Ok, after trying out unity for a while, it just didn't grow on me. Culprit: the incomprehensible choice to go for the dock-like windows management model. I'm sorry, but try as I might, I just can't like it. Unity feels restrictive and I found myself thinking about my actions more than in any other desktop system. I just couldn't see myself do development work on it.

I've just switched to Xubuntu and am enjoying every second of my lightweight desktops, which actually shows windows in a taskbar, consumes less resources, and lets me do my work.

### Post by beew on 2011-07-02
> **sumadartson said:**
> Ok, after trying out unity for a while, it just didn't grow on me. Culprit: the incomprehensible choice to go for the dock-like windows management model. I'm sorry, but try as I might, I just can't like it. Unity feels restrictive and I found myself thinking about my actions more than in any other desktop system. I just couldn't see myself do development work on it.

I've just switched to Xubuntu and am enjoying every second of my lightweight desktops, which actually shows windows in a taskbar, consumes less resources, and lets me do my work.

After using Unity for a while I have no problem navigating around it, know enough short cuts and work arounds so I am quite comfortable with it myself and it isn't bad for me.

But I would not recommend  it to new users at this stage, too many inconsistencies, too many hidden things, too buggy especially when it comes to buttons, tray icons and so on (Yeah all because of the global menu, that has to be the most retarded idea). I know how to work around all these but I wouldn't expect that from a new user (hence the irony, Unity is supposed to be designed for new users and to hell with the experienced users :))

So instead of installing Ubuntu 11.04 on two of my friends' computers I am going to install Xubuntu  11.04 (slap on awn and activate the Compiz Cube and add the Ubuntu theme, it is very nice). Have been testing that for quite sometimes and I believe it would be a lot easier for them (The classical desktop is an alternative but Xubuntu is lighter so that is a plus)

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-07-02
> **cariboo907 said:**
> @beew, shouldn't you give them the choice, instead forcing your preference on them?

Not really; It doesn't end with recommending an OS, it has to be something you are comfortable supporting in the long term as well.  This is why I normally recommend the LTS release - I know that the support problems are going to be minimal once it is set up and running satisfactorily, and mostly educating people in the differences between Ubuntu and their old OS. Once people get to know their new OS and can be more independent they will be informed enough to make their own decisions.  I don't make any secret of the fact that I'm recommending the LTS release over the latest one.

Beew's approach is different in that he doesn't see the value in the LTS releases, which is fine.  Each to his own.  The most important thing is that new users are shown how *ubuntu can be a viable free and open source alternative.

As for apologising I understand Beew's position.  He is making recommendations that will drastically change how they use their computers which is going to affect people to greater degrees depending how they use the technology. I prefer not to put myself in the situation of having to apologise for any of my recommendations - hence the conscious decision to recommend the LTS release over any other.  I haven't had any cause to regret this.  

In my experience the average non-technical user just wants their computer to work.  They don't want to have to (or can't) use forums to fix problems and they'd prefer if support was required as little as possible, and certainly not due to problems in the OS or interface.

Chris.

### Post by babuba on 2011-07-03

> Customisation. And I'm not talking just about the colours, the icons, the size. I'm talking about dynamic shaping, positioning and functions.

### Post by BobSongs on 2011-07-05
> **bagatonovic said:**
> I have been using Ubuntu since Warty, and Linux since 1997.  This is by far the worst experience I've had with a Ubuntu, and any Linux distro for that matter.  The drastic change to this clunky interface is a terrible idea.  Did Canonical even test this with real users, or did they act like the Pidgin devs and force their opinions on users?

Anyway I know Ubuntu is free, but I'd gladly pay for MS Windows after seeing this crap.

Thanks,

Brian A
Cleveland, Ohio
Rage-quitting is never a good plan.

And leaving GNU/Linux for Windows to avoid such drastic UI changes is the height of irony. First, you'll have to wrestle with the Windows UI as opposed to GNOME3's. Then you'll have to face regular Windows UI changes with each Windows upgrade. After all, if a Windows user pays hundreds of dollars to upgrade and the new Windows looks and feels and works as well as the old, how on EARTH will he feel like he's gotten his money's worth? (Drastic UI changes and more frequent blue screens of death are a sure sign of a fresh, new OS for a Windows user.)

From Warty Warthog 4.10 to Maverick Meerkat 10.10, GNOME has undergone few and subtle changes. But even GNOME admits their code was ugly. And why not make more "modern" changes to the UI while starting afresh?

So. If we experienced little change from 4.10 to 10.10, then I think we can handle getting to know GNOME3 and living with it for the next 8 years while experiencing the same degree of subtle changes. 

Go to Windows if you want. But don't go there to **avoid** UI changes or as a way to shake your fist at GNOME/Unity. So before throwing a brick at Ubuntu, Brian, give GNOME3 a try on a Live Fedora 15 CD. Test it out as if Fedora 15 was the Windows you were going to go to for rescue. From many testimonies in this thread, GNOME3 has more to offer than we imagine.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-07-06
Nobody is denying that there are people that don't like Unity, it's just that most of them have had their say several times over. Then every week or so someone that's been living under a rock for the last year and a half comes up for air and notices that their Ubuntu has changed, and it starts all over again.

We have to face facts, Gnome has changed the way we use a computer, if Canonical hadn't come up with Unity, we'd probably see the same complaints about Gnome-shell, it's just that they have about a years head start on Unity, so the shell is a little more polished.

There will always be people that don't like the latest DE, just like there are people that didn't like Gnome 2.X, KDE or any of the other desktop environments available. 

Although I was a bit defensive of Unity when it first came out, I really don't care what desktop environment you use, as long as it's still a Linux desktop environment.

### Post by BobSongs on 2011-07-06
> **cariboo907 said:**
> Although I was a bit defensive of Unity when it first came out, I really don't care what desktop environment you use, as long as it's still a Linux desktop environment.
If I paid for Ubuntu like suckers pay for Windows then I'd complain long, loud and hard about Unity. Since it's free I haven't issued a complaint. I've said I'll wait for the next release.

But you're right. There is no **one** Linux. GNU/Linux is a set of tools combined into a distribution and maintained for free (usually) by some party. It can be a full system like Ubuntu, Fedora, Mandriva... or a tiny set of tools to get files off a corrupted PC.

So we're free to pick and choose.

My point is simple. I **like** Ubuntu. I think it's got a lot of class. I **don't like** Unity in its [COLOR=Blue]**current state**[/COLOR]. Since I've stayed with Ubuntu since 2005 -- six years now -- I'm pleased to wait for the 11.10 release. And when it arrives, I'll be one of many giving Unity a test run. If it proves head and shoulders above the current Unity, you can rest assured that I will add a post saying why I'm joining the Unity crowd.

:)

### Post by 82_28 on 2011-07-08
I'm about, in about three days switch to Mint or plain old Debian if Ubuntu doesn't figure this out.  I'm doing WORK on my machine and gnome under ubuntu worked for the last five years for me.  It WORKED!  Now nothing works, even booting into "ubuntu classic" sucks.  I can't change desktops (just a frozen quadrant of boxes that only one works), middle button cut and paste doesn't work anymore, and X crashes now!  How could I go from uptimes of nearly a year, to literally having to alt-f6 out of x to reboot with one "upgrade"?

I got my approaching elderly age parents to switch from windows to Ubuntu.  They were enjoying it.  But now my mom and my dad do not know what to do, because I told them they could "trust" all updates and upgrades from Ubuntu.  I sang the chorus of praise for all things Linux and esp Ubuntu.  Now I've gotta drive 300 miles to help my mom with her computer.  My dad doesn't wanna hear about it and prefers to not even turn his computer on anymore.  

Fix this, Canonical.  I don't want to get "under the hood" when I have work to do.  Unity is a total disaster in my book.  And has honestly screwed up my whole life a bit.

an option is to install them XFCE desktop (kinda looks like gnome they are used to) and then remove the Gnome.
 
First they do this: [http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/xfce](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/xfce)
Or they can also copy paste command line you prepare for them.
 
 
THen after logging in to Xubuntu they do this: [http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/purexfce](http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntu/purexfce)
Copy& paste first command to remove Ubuntu/gnome.

Still for inexperienced users 10.04 seems to be the way to go....

But yeah, I did tell them it's ok.  But do you think that the words "update" and "upgrade" mean a whole hell of a lot to someone who's 60?  No.  Of course they don't.  

I have updated and upgraded every single Ubuntu release since 2006ish -- used it exclusively.  I simply trusted it.  I want a gnome that I can trust and can allow others to use when I recommend it to them.  I would also like for "Ubuntu Classic" to work for me.  But it does not.  It blows and is the worst interface I have ever encountered for a laptop/desktop.  Tablets -- maybe. . .

### Post by Duncan Williams on 2011-07-08
In all fairness, It's also about hardware and the never ending variations in new computer technology.

As in:
If you have a recent computer with all the goodies, then as long as your graphic card is working 100% in 3D, it's all good with latest unity/compiz and any other recent software.

Older systems are fine with basic 2d linux setup but start falling over with all the latest stuff.

That's the same for windows > XP wil run on older systems, fine.
Windows 7 (Vista) start to fall over on older systems.

ram > supported video card (or lack off) > 32bit/64bit > etc...

So, really it's probably good that unity/compiz etc.. get sorted out now for next lot of > desktops/touchscreen/tablets, etc...

### Post by mpiter on 2011-07-08
> **Brushstroke said:**
> Grab a copy of 10.04 LTS and install it. A lot more stable and you'll have the familiar GNOME interface until 2013 when [hopefully] Canonical gets this crap with Unity sorted out. I'm with you, Unity is horribly buggy and they shouldn't have released it so early.

+1.  

Even Natty in Classic mode is not as stable as 10.04 or 10.10 and needs a lot of work to run without major problems.  Emerald from the synaptic tree is not compatible with Compiz anymore.  Emerald therefore needs to be compiled from sources.  No more possibilities to change the pointer size in Compiz, only the cursor size is supported. Sometimes my computer does not boot at the first power on, I do not know why. The Xdvi window in full screen mode slowly shifts to the left/top, I do not know why...

With Unity, it is a lot worst.  I gave it up after a couple of hours because I needed a working computer for my work.  I just do not understand why they released Unity while it was still so buggy.  I even understand less why they chose it as the default system when you reboot your computer after the upgrade because it just breaks your Compiz configuration.

I still think that Canonical has made a good work during many years.  I keep Ubuntu because everyone can make mistakes, and I make mistakes too.  I just hope that Canonical will learn from the user complaints that they have been a bit careless this time.  I still hope that the next releases will be better as they use to be.

### Post by motrengaw on 2011-07-08
My first reactions to the Unity interface were and are negative.  I use my computer as a tool, not a toy.  Part of using it effectively is of course investing the time to understand and quickly move through the GUI desktop.

Users like myself appreciate an incremental, opt-in approach to changes in their workspace.  Part of what keeps Open Source/Linux OS in the shade is exemplified by transitions like Ubuntu's Natty release, where the user is presented with a radically different GUI that comes out of nowhere, and requires a fairly extensive web search to find a way to revert to the 'Classic' GUI in which we've built our skill set.  In future releases, it seems, even this option will be eliminated or rendered even more obscure or tiresome.

This kind of paradigm shift should be presented as an option on installation, not crammed down people's throats in an attempt to justify development resources expended chasing 'innovation' by fiat.

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-07-09
> **Daremo_06 said:**
> So that's my rant.... which is probably falling on deaf ears.

Ubuntu is a big distribution with a lot of choices available so random threats to go to some other distribution probably fall on deaf ears since the easy solutions are one or more of... add the stuff you want to your current desktop, install a different desktop, use a different *buntu disc the next time around.

Currently the classic desktop is an option.

In future versions, when Unity and Unity-2d are the only OOB options on the default Ubuntu disc, it's not that big of a download to add the Gnome desktop and fallback session, for people who don't know how to change the option the option so recommends don't get treated as dependencies, it could be a bigger download depending on what the default list of apps will be relative to the default apps included on the Ubuntu disc.

There was some stuff posted about creating another *buntu that includes the Gnome desktop OOB, but what kind of group formed around this, how serious the effort is, and how long until they start getting stuff into the archive instead of just in a PPA and how long until they start producing an install disc are things I don't know.

With Oneiric actually including the full Gnome 3 desktop it should, theoretically, be easier work for some group that wants to feed and care for another *buntu disc that includes the Gnome desktop and the related *buntu stuff that would go into the archives to support it.

Don't forget that Ubuntu is not just an install disc with some small set of applications, it's also a distribution and a community.

Later, Seeker

### Post by autumnColors on 2011-07-09
I'm a few months in and have tried several other distros just because I don't like Unity at all. I liked the Classic Ubuntu (Gnome) DE much more, and KDE the best - which I installed on Ubuntu rather than switching to Kubuntu.

Unity looks like an environment if you have a touch screen I/F. The big icons would be easy to navigate by hand. The panels that jump out at you from the sides, to me anyway, are very annoying. Maybe if there was a voice I/F they would be ok because they could stay hidden if preferred. Other than touch screen or voice, I don't get how they came up with Unity. It's not keyboard friendly. It's just garish. I look forward to the LTS version of 11, but only if there's more options than Unity or Gnome 3.

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-07-10
> **uRock said:**
> I do recommend Ubuntu, but I am guilty of setting people up with the LTS, so that I don't get called to upgrade their system as often and I most of the bugs have been ironed out. 

There's nothing to be guilty of there, the LTS releases are there for a reason, namely the ones that you state.  

I also recommend LTS to Linux newcomers.  If they like it enough to stay with it, they will learn enough to make an informed decision about what version they would like to run in the future.  If they just use and don't learn, well I guess I'll hear from them sometime around 13.04 when it stops updating, and will probably upgrade them to the new LTS.

I set them all up with a separate /home partition and in some cases a separate /home drive altogether.  Barring hardware failures in the meantime I expect the upgrades will take about 20 minutes or so...

If my first experience of Ubuntu had been 11.04 I don't think I would have stuck with it - On most of my machines it crashes badly until updated - something that would be non-trivial to a linux newcomer.  I have trouble understanding why the default Canonical download is not the current LTS release, and why most people seem to recommend the latest "stable" release this far out from the next LTS.

Oh, I also use 10.04 LTS as my main OS on four systems.  I play with alphas, betas, new releases, kde, xfce, lxde etc and even Fedora. None of them have so far given me any reason to change from 10.04 though I keep hoping for a better upgrade.

Chris.

### Post by KillaB7 on 2011-07-11
> **thejasper said:**
> 1 minute. That is how long it took me to decide to get rid of unity. Actually it was probably faster. You may accuse me of not giving Unity a chance but for me there were two UI changes which are completely unacceptable. These choices are so basic that I cannot believe there is much good in the rest of the interface.

What two things do I hate so much that I won't even give Unity a try?

Menu's divorced from windows. This is an Apple heresy which I have always considered to be a major bug in their interface (and/or thought proces). My menu is part of my window. An application doesn't get to take over my whole machine or pretend it is the only thing running. Put the menu with window. There is no other choice.

Dock clone. Wow. Lots of HUGE icons so that all my nice small icons which I had on my top bar now scroll off the screen. Oh wait, I got all these other useless program icons stuffed in there as well. Not to mention that it hides the fact that there is a difference between a running process and a program. My process could be multiple windows. I could have multiple instances of the same program. I hate the dock on macs. On windows 7 the first thing I did was to get my quick launch bar back and to unpin everything.

I don't need to test anything more in Unity. These two issues are absolute deal killers. Don't need to know if these can or can't be disabled. I can use a decent window manager and not have these problems.


Couldn't have said it better myself. Been using Ubuntu Classic ever since I learned of the option. I love to stay current (TRIM support for my SSD, excellent IPv6 integration), but if the Classic login feature were to vanish, I'd have to find another distro, plain and simple.

### Post by FormatSeize on 2011-07-12
> **82_28 said:**
> But yeah, I did tell them it's ok. But do you think that the words "update" and "upgrade" mean a whole hell of a lot to someone who's 60? No. Of course they don't. 
Why are you doing this?
 
Linux, in general, is not for the average computer user. The types of users that want things to just work without having to tinker with things, edit system files, and become intimate with the terminal, really, should not be using Linux. I'm not saying that anyone is of lesser intelligence if they so choose to use some other OS, but they simply should not use something that requires maintainence if they are just doing day-to-day, run of the mill things.
 
There are two types of people that should use Linux: those that are super users that know how to build and maintain a system such that it is usable on most days, and those of us (like myself) that are bored and have the time to pull our hair out trying to figure things out. Those that want things to "just work" really shouldn't be introduced to Linux from 300 miles away. Things are going to break, and phone conversations generally aren't adequate. 
 
When I first started using GNU/Linux, I bloviated about how awesome it was and that I would introduce my girlfriend to it. Turns out that while I had no problem using it and working through issues, her situation was a train wreck inside of a hurricane. Over the years, I've come to realize what I am actually getting when I go to download iso files, and that's a CD or DVD full of broken packages that will become even more broken when I hit the "Update" button. It's up to me to wade through the wreckage that I boot my computer from to make a dependable system. 
 
Simply put, it's not the case that everyone can, or even wants to do this. I personally do it as a hobby, and I've learned a lot over the years, but I wouldn't expect for anyone that I mention Linux to to actually convert over from Windows or Mac. In general, even people that use a computer everyday don't have a single clue as to what is going on with that black box on their desk. And most people don't want to know. Even though Ubuntu is like a kiddie pool, it still requires some basic knowledge about what's going on inside the monitor, or they have to have a desire to care what's going on. This is not a characteristic that everyone is going to have. 
 
Not to say that it's a bad thing to recommend Linux to someone, but if either you or they aren't either already computer savvy, or ready to learn to maintain a system, work through crashes (this will happen) and problems ("Compiz" roughly translates to "broken windows"), then introducing them to Linux is throwing them into the water without and hope that they can learn to swim fast enough to not drown.

---

### Post by TBABill on 2011-07-12
To the OP, if I had a situation like yours with my own parents I would absolutely go with a LTS. Nothing wrong with other versions, but if they are like my parents they won't want a lot of things to change. The LTS 10.04 is supported till April 2013, longer than any current Ubuntu release. So I'd install that for them, set it to only advise them of new LTS versions available, and maybe setup a time to go help them with that upgrade when it becomes available (next year). That gives you a full year of 10.04 still being supported while they have the option to upgrade to 12.04. 
 
Whether or not they'd like Unity isn't so much the issue as keeping their system stable and consistent. Gnome is evolving and I'm sure we'll end up with a Gnome 3 desktop choice without the Unity or Gnome Shell required at some point. Mint is going to do it and I'll bet others will as well. Until it's sorted, 10.04 is an outstanding choice, as stable as anything I've used, and looks like what they're accustomed to using.

---

### Post by timgood on 2011-07-12
> **FormatSeize said:**
> Why are you doing this?
 
Linux, in general, is not for the average computer user. The types of users that want things to just work without having to tinker with things, edit system files, and become intimate with the terminal, really, should not be using Linux. I'm not saying that anyone is of lesser intelligence if they so choose to use some other OS, but they simply should not use something that requires maintainence if they are just doing day-to-day, run of the mill things.

I totally disagree. My mother, who is not computer literate and nearly 80 years old has had Ubuntu installed on her home computer for the last 4 years. She is now running 10.10 and the only reason I did not upgrade her to Natty was because of the problems I experienced with it.

When she was running Windows I was called to her house practically every week to sort out problems, including virus, spyware and driver issues. Since she has been running Ubuntu, I have not had to work on her machine (apart from performing upgrades) for years. For what she needs, internet surfing, email, word processing and playing music, Ubuntu is more than suitable and she loves it. The fact that it runs very well on hardware more than 6 years old is a plus. 

I have converted many other people,including business customers to Ubuntu. Natty is the only version that I have not been able to recommend for home and business use. I'm hoping that 11.10 will bring Ubuntu back into mainstream computing again.

### Post by BobSongs on 2011-07-12
> **timgood said:**
> I totally disagree. My mother, who is not computer literate and nearly 80 years old has had Ubuntu installed on her home computer for the last 4 years. She is now running 10.10 and the only reason I did not upgrade her to Natty was because of the problems I experienced with it.

When she was running Windows I was called to her house practically every week to sort out problems, including virus, spyware and driver issues. Since she has been running Ubuntu, I have not had to work on her machine (apart from performing upgrades) for years. For what she needs, internet surfing, email, word processing and playing music, Ubuntu is more than suitable and she loves it. The fact that it runs very well on hardware more than 6 years old is a plus. 

I have converted many other people,including business customers to Ubuntu. Natty is the only version that I have not been able to recommend for home and business use. I'm hoping that 11.10 will bring Ubuntu back into mainstream computing again.
Hear, hear! I agree with Tim on this one.

My neighbor bought a nice, fancy HP PC and Printer based on my recommendation. It came with Windows Vista and a companion Windows 7 upgrade DVD.

We sat down and started the upgrade to ensure running Win7 instead of that wretched Vista. The upgrade took so long, I threw up my hands, picked up my gear and walked home.

The next day, after Win7 was finally finished its long upgrade, it took me roughly an hour to partition her hard disk and quickly install Ubuntu with her sitting nervously by my side. Being wary about my encouragement to install Ubuntu side by side with Windows, she felt she needed to supervise the whole process.

Once installed, her printer appeared in the printer list once we turned it on. Scanner worked perfectly. She took the mouse and away she went.

Now? Well, it's been a year since this undertaking. I asked her to give me an estimate. How much time does she spend in Ubuntu... and how much time does she spend in Windows?

Answer: "99% of the time in Ubuntu, 1% Windows".

And this woman isn't a PC guru.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-07-13
> **Daremo_06 said:**
> So I have been rediculously busy and haven't even read responses to my thread, but now that I have I have to ask this.

I am not against changes, I just did not expect the upgrade to fully change the interface to the amount it did.  There are many things I have not liked in the time I have spent, that had I had the time to sit down and really look into might be possible to change, but I just havent had the time.  For example, I really dont like that closing out a window via the "X" menu is now stuck at the top instead of on the app.  That seemed more like a GNOME desktop kind of feature more than something hard-coded.  Another thing I don't like is not being able to see the active windows in a taskbar at the bottom or wherever I want it.  That is mainly why I want the desktop I had in 10.10 back.  At least for now.  When I have the time, I would gladly learn something new and cool if it was effcient and easy to use.

Semi-apology for rant ;)

You can select the classic desktop from the login screen, so if you have autologin enabled, you'll have to log out to make the selection. 

You shouldn't limit your self to the two panel interface though, as it is going away, and so far the only replacement is the gnome-fallback-session in Gnome 3, which is only meant for systems that don't have the resources to run Gnome-shell. Give some of the other desktop environments available in the repositories a try, you may find one that suits you better.

I would also suggest before doing an upgrade next time, read the release notes, so there aren't any surprises.

### Post by mastablasta on 2011-07-14
> **GSF1200S said:**
> ....or dual screen boxes..
 
whhat kind of a problem is there with dual screen in KDE?

---

### Post by GSF1200S on 2011-07-14
> **mastablasta said:**
> whhat kind of a problem is there with dual screen in KDE?
 
Heh linux has issues with dual screens while new ATI cards with eyefinity are supposed to run 6 screens :-)

Linux and Xorg doesnt have a problem with dual screens- nearly every desktop environment on Linux does. KDE will not work with the only supported form (aside from Nvidia Twinview which is basically one giant screen) of dual head support: Seperate X-session. While most window managers do OK at this task, nearly all have particular issues (systray only works on one screen, panel cannot have two instances, windows open on the wrong X-session, etc). In KDE's case, plasma has issues on the second screen, the menu will always launch applications on screen one (made worse by the fact that with Separate X sessions, you cant drag from screen to screen), etc..

Luckily, XFCE works absolutely perfect with dual-head/separate-X, so im content :)

### Post by idlemind324 on 2011-07-14
Seems we're leaving the main topic here with the thread. I think what people are looking for is some kind of survey of users to see if Unity is the right answer or if it needs to be removed. Not "Here install this alternative desktop".

The desktop interface is the first and primary application loaded for almost every Ubuntu users. If you change that part of the distro you're making a huge change in the product. Everything is designed around the desktop environment. Other programs are given love to look extra pretty in Unity or take advantage of different parts of Unity that benefit no other environment.

If all of your users load up your product and immediately install XFCE or something else all that extra work might as well get tossed out. If there are only a true handful of us that don't like Unity then it may be time for us to change focus. Otherwise it just seems like everyone is saying "Here take Unity, don't like it install something else". Maybe I missed the debate on it already but it seems like force-ware to me. Ubuntu is an excellent overall product and I understand that some developers really like to push the envelope but just because you have an idea doesn't always make it an awesome one.

---

### Post by cimh on 2011-07-20
I returned to Unity briefly again yesterday while installing 11.04 onto a new pc. After 10 exasperating minutes trying to find things I switched back to classic.

There is nothing wrong Unity it is just that nothing is where I expect it to be. I could adapt but there is a learning curve and for what purpose? - I suggest there is no purpose other than to be different. Unity doesnt do anything that classic cant do, but it does everything slower.

Its a bit like a car maker deciding to be different - lets put the gear shifter in the roof behind the rear view mirror, and why not have a joystick instead of a steering wheel and lets swap the pedals round and hide the gas filler cap under the hood and lets divert some of the engines power into creating pleasing (or annoying) visual effects. 

It would attract attention and it would probably work but it would put off many more people. I guess thats why most cars look the same - because the design works. 

Doesnt the fact that this thread is so long say something? 

I suggest that Ubuntu 11.10 should default to classic and keep Unity as the alternative option for those that like it.

CIMH
Ubuntu 11.04 classic
11.04 lubuntu
win 7 & vista

### Post by mpiter on 2011-07-21
> **cimh said:**
> ...There is nothing wrong Unity it is just that nothing is where I expect it to be...

I disagree.  There are design mistakes in Unity, it is not a question of liking it or not.  Just 2 examples.

First, the global menu is a design mistake in a Unix-based world.  It is a question of development philosophy.  With a global menu, you cannot use "focus under the pointer (mouse)", you are forced to use "click window to focus".  Many people use "focus under the mouse". Now, they cannot work anymore as they are used to do.  That is because with a global menu, you cannot easily reach the top of the screen with your pointer without touching another window if you have a lot of them open. If you touch a wrong window, you will have a wrong menu.  On top of this, it is still possible to find applications running with several open windows, each window of the application having its own menu.  With those programs, a global menu is objectively inefficient because you spend too much time to change focus just to enable the right menu which can be far from the window.  With Mac OSX, there is nothing wrong with the global menu because all third-parties developped their software with that menu philosophy in mind.  With Unix, more than 20 years of development in another philosophy just make the global menu a disease.

So, global menu within Unix is a mistake.  To enable it by default on the netbook version is very debatable.  To enable it by default on the desktop version is an obvious mistake.  Leaving the user without an easy and obvious way to choose local instead of global menus is careless.

Second, sliders are objectively less effective than scroll bars if your are not in despair for saving a few pixels on your screen.  Depending on the available space, sliders appear inside or outside the window. So you never know exactly where you can grab the slider.  On top of this, the margin indicator showing where the pointer can grab the slider is hardly visible on small screen.  In conclusion, one spend too much concentration and time to find where to catch the slider making it a very ineffective way in comparison to scroll bars.  Again, setting the sliders by default on netbook is very debatable.  Doing this on the desktop version is an obvious mistake. Again, leaving the user without an easy and obvious way to get rid of the sliders is careless.

There are other arguments showing that Unity contains real design mistakes in comparison to Gnome, but I do not want to clutter this thread with just one post.  You can easily find those arguments in discussions about that topic in many forums.

On the other hand, according to what people claim in forums, the advantages of Unity essentially boil down to 4 points:

  1. Unity saves screen space, which is good for netbooks;

  2. Unity gives a better management of keyboard shortcuts to find applications;

  3. Unity is supposed to contain a great dock;

  4. Unity brings changes and is supposed to be beautiful.

About the first point, Unity saves a little bit of screen space at the cost of incoherence as explain above.  The gain of screen space is so small, even on netbooks, that you hardly notice it and it does not change your life with your computer.  To me, it is clear that the advantage does not worth the cost.  About the second point, it is true that Unity brings an efficient way to search programs with few keystrokes when you know its name.  But unfortunately, they neglected the search for programs with the mouse, a task that has become tedious. I wonder why not simply adding a similar search tool to the old Gnome interface to have both the keyboard and mouse search performances.  About the third point, I have not understood why the dock is better than other ones such as Cairo Doc or Avant Window Navigator.  And about the fourth point, anyone experienced in object-oriented programming knows that you can change your object implementation as much as you want as long as you do not touch the user interface.  If you update a user interface, you should be very cautious to avoid bringing incoherence or you will end up into a disaster.  The rule is the same in real life. Regarding this aspect, they did a very very poor job with Unity.

Notice that I only wrote about flaws intrinsic to Unity.  I even did not mention the incredible high level of bugs leading to instability, all those features that were consciously abandoned such as saving a session on exit, all those features that disappeared due to bugs such as pointer size in Compiz, and some widely used but not maintained packages still in the Ubuntu repository such as Emerald that crashes!

So my conclusion is very simple.  There are design flaws in Unity that are objectively wrong such as the global menu.  Any object-oriented programmer knows from experience that changing so deeply a user interface without preparing the users is just plainly stupid. Even if I did not develop it here, Unity was put on the market too early because it is presently too buggy.  They should have kept working on it until Ubuntu 11.10 before releasing it.

If you consider that a computer is a toy such as a smartphone to play around during your leisure time, Unity can be fun to try.  But if you consider that a computer should be a working tool, Ubuntu 11.04 with Unity is just a disaster.

---

About the first point, Unity saves a little bit of screen space at the cost of incoherence as explain above.
