But unfortunately, they neglected the search for programs with the mouse, a task that has become tedious. I wonder why not simply adding a similar search tool to the old Gnome interface to have both the keyboard and mouse search performances.  About the third point, I have not understood why the dock is better than other ones such as Cairo Doc or Avant Window Navigator.  And about the fourth point, anyone experienced in object-oriented programming knows that you can change your object implementation as much as you want as long as you do not touch the user interface.  If you update a user interface, you should be very cautious to avoid bringing incoherence or you will end up into a disaster.  The rule is the same in real life. Regarding this aspect, they did a very very poor job with Unity.

Notice that I only wrote about flaws intrinsic to Unity.  I even did not mention the incredible high level of bugs leading to instability, all those features that were consciously abandoned such as saving a session on exit, all those features that disappeared due to bugs such as pointer size in Compiz, and some widely used but not maintained packages still in the Ubuntu repository such as Emerald that crashes!

So my conclusion is very simple.  There are design flaws in Unity that are objectively wrong such as the global menu.  Any object-oriented programmer knows from experience that changing so deeply a user interface without preparing the users is just plainly stupid. Even if I did not develop it here, Unity was put on the market too early because it is presently too buggy.  They should have kept working on it until Ubuntu 11.10 before releasing it.

If you consider that a computer is a toy such as a smartphone to play around during your leisure time, Unity can be fun to try.  But if you consider that a computer should be a working tool, Ubuntu 11.04 with Unity is just a disaster.

Thank you for this very insightful commentary. I completely agree with you on all accounts.

The issue is not the question of personally liking or disliking Unity (I actually like the overall idea very much) but the fact that there are some very significant flaws with the way the user interface works. 

The problems have again and again been described and shown by highly skilled people in comments, blog articles and youtube videos. 

What worries me immensely is that none of this seems to affect the development of Unity at all. 

I see no sign of the Unity team saying: "Hey guys! Wait a minute. These people actually have a few good points. There are some problems that we did not think about when we first designed the Unity interface. And these people have even shown us the solutions to those problems. I say, let's get these things fixed before pressing on."

Instead I have the impression that they all have read "The Secret" and are collectively thinking: "If we keep believing that Unity is perfect then everyone will eventually see that it is so. The people criticising Unity do it because they are afraid of change. They will criticise us no matter what we do. Their comments must be ignored."

Is there any sign at all that any of the criticism is being taken into consideration by the developers of Unity? (I am talking about the really analytic reviews not the "I hate/love it!" comments.)

What are the best channels to get an insight into what the Unity team is actually thinking and planning? E.g. re there any official responses from Canonical to the criticisms? (Again: The really analytic reviews not the "I hate/love it!" comments.)

### Post by mpiter on 2011-07-22
Thanks for that piece of information, but it does not solve the problem.  It just allows you to enable "focus under mouse", but it is not a workable option with a global menu.

To understand the problem, imagine a window of application Y covering the full half upper part of your screen (over the whole screen width).  Imagine another window of application Z below the upper window.  If you want to access the Z menu in a global menu context, you MUST go over window Y to reach the top bar containing the global menu. Going over window Y will enable the Y menu instead of the Z one.  As a consequence, you cannot access the Z menu in this configuration.

So my point was not that Unity hid the "focus under mouse" option, my point was that you CANNOT USE that option with a global menu.  Hence a global menu is a design mistake for a system that have been developed for more than 20 years with local menu in mind.  It is a question of development philosophy.

---

### Post by mikodo on 2011-07-22

I have remained with Gnome2 in 10.04 and am not familiar with Gnome3 or Unity shells.

### Post by Nicezia on 2011-07-22
I like the idea of it, but honestly it makes it difficult for me to get to things as easily as i used to, in fact overall Linux is starting to discourage me from liking it, as it strives to become a more "works-out-of-the-box" system all the ways i used to configure my system are becoming more and more difficult to maintain. For instance, with grub i used to be able to add my OS boots in a simple text file, and fine everything is ready to go... now it runs on bash scripting which i knew nothing about and never wanted to learn... but had to in order to get my systems whittled down the way i want it. now i don't mind the scripting they use for auto-detecting systems, but i think it should have a simple text file and run OS detection ONLY at user request... that way grub detects the OS' adds it in default ways to the text file,  and then the user can tweak those boot options. As it is now the user must accept the OSes added as is to grub, or learn bash scripting. 

that's just one example of the things that is getting less and less user friendly. It took me 3  weeks to realize that you can use shotwell to create wallpaper sideshows, where i believe i used to be able to just add a folder... still haven't found a way to configure my screensavers after selecting them...transmission no longer allows you to set the number of torrents allowed to be downloaded at one time ....etc.

In dumbing down Linux for "daily users" they are upping the bar for "used-to-be" power-users, and even managing to take away somethings that "daily users" may actually need.

in short i have a extreme love of the new concepts that appear in Linux, but an extreme hate for their introductory implementations... its like every time a new version comes out of an application, the developers seem to feel they have to "completely change the game" that's fine for gui/appearance but configuration options which i depended on are becoming more complicated, or completely disappearing. I feel like I'm dealing with Microsoft all over again now! In fact Wine and VirtualBox have become my best friends these days, where as by Ubuntu 9.04 i had completely abandoned Windows for home use and by 11.04 i almost am always using the a VirtualBox VM of WindowsXP as my main interface (of course Linux is still doing all my fileserving/webserving and other important tasks in the background. 

if you want to make things better make it better for all...

as far as Unity itself.. its a completely different management system, i understand that, but again its just to cumbersome for me at the moment... however its a beautiful and "almost-well-thought-out" interface and i look forward to it improving to the point where i can actually use it. but for now... Gnome 2.3 please!

---

### Post by cimh on 2011-07-23
> **cariboo907 said:**

On my 1024 x 600 screen, the unity panel looks larger than the gnome panel and doesnt give me a lot of info as havent used it for long enough to learn what the icons stand for. 

In gnome or lxde I only use one lower panel which in standard form is thinner than the unity one. I set it to auto hide. So I have the full sceen to use. The panel when it does pop up handles text better than a vertical one and its easier to see which programs are open.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-07-23
> **robert shearer said:**
> @stormelf 

Yes, from Sabdfl  
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/668415](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/668415)
Post #2 :(


Thank you for pointing us to that discussion.  It is the most insightful discussion I have read about the new Ubuntu orientation with Unity.  Unfortunately, it confirms what I had felt but refused to believe.

I have been working almost exclusively with Unix-based system since the beginning of 1988. I have little experience with Windows and Mac OS X, not that I hate those OS, but according to what I saw they do not offer what I like on computers.  If I had to summarize the main characteristics of the 3 OS, I would write:

[INDENT]1. [COLOR="Blue"]Mac OS X[/COLOR]: [COLOR="Green"]Very easy to use, rather stable[/COLOR], [COLOR="Red"]but close world poorly configurable and unable to offer many services[/COLOR]. It does very well what it is supposed to do, nothing more. If you want to use your computer like a car, i.e., buying it, starting it and using it without worries, go for a Mac.

2. [COLOR="Blue"]Windows[/COLOR]: [COLOR="Green"]Highly configurable, open to many services, hugest user base and industry support such as drivers development[/COLOR], [COLOR="Red"]but very unstable, terrible user interface with frequent deep changes that did not improve anything but are brought just for the very stupid argument "The previous one was old, a new one was needed."[/COLOR]  If you want a computer with many people around using the same OS or with the biggest third-party support, go for Windows.

3. [COLOR="Blue"]Unix-based[/COLOR]: [COLOR="Green"]Highly configurable, very stable and trustworthy, very easy to use once properly set by someone competent, very powerful for software development and server services of all kinds[/COLOR], [COLOR="Red"]but few users around to be physically on our computer in case of problems, poor support from industry such as driver development[/COLOR]. If you want a professional system very rational and easy to use once properly set, and if you are not afraid to rely only on forums and Internet help in case of trouble, go for a Unix-based system.[/INDENT]

Even though Ubuntu developers have my most sincere respect and admiration for the incredible work they have done for so many years, I cannot help myself being a bit afraid. Now I just want to join the people triggering an alarm in the hope that it will not be felt as an insult by the Ubuntu team but just the expression of a disappointment without anger nor hate.

To me, the discussion referenced by Robert Shearer (who I thank once again) confirms that Ubuntu is loosing its Unix-based identity getting closer to Mac OS X philosophy (limiting user choices for the sake of easiness of use) with Windows flaws (deep user interface changes in a version released to fast letting the users facing many bugs and an unstable system).

In other words, Ubuntu is loosing its Unix-based identity.  That includes professional features such as user experience stability, bug-free system, and system stability.

### Post by Amnykon on 2011-07-24
At first I did not like unity, but some features grow on you. Unity still needs improvements. 

overall:
Seems slower.
Needs to be more stable (it has froze my computer a few times).

Launcher:
I like the launcher.
I like the marks to inform how many windows of that program is open.

Desired changes:
Hover opens up a menu with each open menu (windows in other workspaces on bottom of list.
Clicking on an icon in that menu will open that specific window
Each item on the menu will also expand to a menu of all the open tabs of that window if any.
Clicking on an icon in the next window will open to that tab.

Right clicking opens a option menu
-pin/unpin to launcher
-move icon to different location

Double clicking: opens a new window.

Clicking on the open icon minimizes that icon.

Perhaps scrolling on the icon changes between windows?

The marks to inform how many windows are open should be gray for windows open on other workspaces.

needs built in options:
Icons size.
Side of the screen.

Dash:
I don't like the dash.
very slow.
When I want a program, I don't want to search for it.
Seems to be made for someone who does not know what they are looking for.
Icons are very large.

Desired changes:
primarily remove the dash. I like the organization of the old menu. With it one does not need to search to find the program they want, they just need to follow the logical path of the tree.

Scrollbar:
I am not sure if I like it.

Desired changes:
show the scrollbar when the mouse is on that edge of the window, not only on the orange scrolling area.

Combined menu bar:
Nice idea that saves space, but needs some minor changes.
I like how it combines with the top bar when a window is maximized.

Desired changes:
Place the menu bar on top of each window's title bar instead of at the top of the screen. by doing this, the menu bar can be assessed when not the active window, and will link better to the window that owns it.

Snap windows:
Makes multi tasking easier.

Desired changes:
When resizing a window that is snapped to an edge, the window that is snapped to the other edge is also resized to fill in the space.
When window snapped on right, window on left is consisted active window when determining to hide launcher.

Unity has some nice ideas, but needs to be further developed.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-07-25
> **Peter09 said:**
> Puzzled - why is gnome classic simpler? Or do you really mean - I know how to use it!

No, I do not think that it is a question of knowing Gnome.  The first time I used Gnome, I did not need to check manuals to find what I needed.  It was sufficiently close in philosophy to other desktop I had already used.  With Unity, I could not find my babies and I found in the first couple of hours several design flaws (I commented a couple of them above in this thread).

I spent a lot of time in forums to try to understand why Unity became the new Ubuntu standard.  I am still waiting for something else than "Gnome was too old and we needed something looking better" (my computer is a working tool, not a piece of art to hang on a wall) or "Unity saves a few pixels which is good for netbooks" (today netbooks are far less numerous than laptops and desktops, so why choosing the minority to define a standard?) or "Better keyboard shortcuts" (it can be implemented in Gnome as well, and plus if you work with the mouse without keyboard shortcuts with Unity it will be harder than in Gnome, so where is the simplicity?)

I also spent time in forums criticizing Unity and nobody answered my criticisms although some people took time to answer other one's criticisms.  I therefore think that the flaws I underlined are objective mistakes because it seemed that nobody wanted to argue on it.  So could you explain with rational arguments why Unity is better than Gnome?

> **VinDSL said:**
> Heh!  Let me guess...

WindowsXP in "Classic Mode" aka Win 3.1, yes?

I never got into XP.  Couldn't stand the Teletubby DE.   I'm still running a proxy server, on top of Win95, and my wife's machine is powered by W2K Pro.  No shame in that.

If you prefer XP, run it - that's all.

Unity will be perfected, in a few years...  No big deal!  ;)

That's the third time in these forums I've heard windows XP likened to Windows 3.1 with all the extras turned off when its more like windows9x which was a leap up from windows 3.1... Does anyone actually REMEMBER windows 3.1 cause i have a VM of it that i use SPECIFICALLY to remind myself of the torturous process of using it!

but no i don't want to run windows... I absolutely hate windows... its just in order to make some things work properly without having to figure out each release how they modified the configuration options (things like joystick, cell phones, etc...) its easier to connect it to a virtual machine of windows where it just works... I'm not by far complaining that i dislike Linux and would rather run windows, the fact that i don't even have a partition for a windows install and haven't even considered it, proves that point. But god, it gets annoying from one release to another to have to relearn everything!!!! which was one of the smaller reasons that i abandoned windows, to begin with... i could understand if there were major changes in technology... but honestly technology these days seems to be making only small steps, and the methods of interfacing with those technologies are almost at a standstill, so it just aggravates me. that's all, i just keep a VM running until i relearn how to do whatever it is i need to do... and by that time there's a new release with some feature i need that's going to require me to relearn the things i just learned... I'm just getting old and less patient :P

anyway, seriously though so as not to be a thread jack, yeah I'm looking forward to unity maturing, if it becomes a more intuitive interface, I'm definitely going to switch to it... but gnome is just fine for now, now if i can just manage to figure out how to disable my joystick from taking control of my mouse (and because the analogs don't center correctly driving my cursor batty) and how to keep my lirc devices from changing from lirc0 to lirc1 and vice versa. maybe i might be able to settle in for a few minutes before unity turns into something i want to have!!!

### Post by beew on 2011-07-27
> **uRock said:**
> So, from what you are telling me. You and many others have had a problem with the way Unity is designed, so therefore others can't be happy with it? Sorry, but just because you had a negative experience, does not mean others are not able to have a positive experience. 

Not sure what you think will come from arguing with everyone who had a good experience is going to do. 

Unity allows me to do all of the same things I can do in GNOME2. Nothing you say will change that.

No, it is your logic. Since you are happy with it and therefore others who complain they experience problems must be imagining it or something. 

I don't actually have too many problems myself. I use Unity in all my Natty installations (3 of them) and am quite happy with it, it is smooth and fast and I find Compiz actually uses less resources in 11.04 than in 10.10 despite the usual complaints. (It has come quite a long way since release, it wasn't like that just two months ago)

But I know my way around, I know the shortcuts and work arounds for  simple bugs and design oddities.  I can open applications very easily with the dash because I know exactly what I have installed (I have a classical menu from ppa installed but I seldom use it) But I can see that would be a huge problem for new users who don't know the names of the apps and what are installed because someone else set up the system for them. 

Honestly, even though I have gotten to quite like Unity, I would not recommend it to new users especially if I have to maintain their installations. I now recommend Xubuntu for my friends even though I don't use it myself. Why? It is easy and intuitive, just set it up and install Compiz for the eye candies then anyone would be able to use it quickly and efficiently, it is intuitive and there is no need to remember esoteric key combo and locate hidden options to do simple things, it is much better for beginners than Unity. New users should spend their time learning to actually use Linux programs than to fool around and getting frustrated with the UI.

Whether Unity is flawed in design and how "faulty" it is  depends on your level of experience and usage. I will not dismiss others' (probably) valid criticism just because it works fine in my own use case scenario.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-07-27

> So, from what you are telling me. You and many others have had a problem with the way Unity is designed, so therefore others can't be happy with it? Sorry, but just because you had a negative experience, does not mean others are not able to have a positive experience.

Your only way to get out of it is to imply that I made a very stupid deduction that I have never made ("so therefore others can't be happy with it?").  So your answer is totally out of target.

> Unity allows me to do all of the same things I can do in GNOME2. Nothing you say will change that.

Again, what you can do with Unity is totally beside the point. I never meant that nobody will be happy with Unity. My point is that with several respects, Unity is a step back in comparison to Gnome 2.  Global menu means no more "focus under pointer"... As a consequence, there are things that you cannot do with Unity while you can do them with Gnome 2. If you stop talking about what YOU can do with Unity but start considering the Ubuntu user community, you will soon realize that parts of that community is left on the side because Unity cannot satisfy their needs while Gnome 2 can.  So the real problem to me is not that people are developing Unity, specially because I also believe that this desktop interface will meet a market. To me the problem is making it the default Ubuntu desktop, specially when we consider that more than 2 users out of 3 seem to prefer Gnome to Unity: 

[[COLOR="Blue"]http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11090646&postcount=3770[/COLOR]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11090646&postcount=3770")

So keep Unity but not as the default desktop.  That is what I claim from the beginning.

> Not sure what you think will come from arguing with everyone who had a good experience is going to do.

Another comment totally out of topic.  But if you want an answer, ask yourself why you defend Unity, you will have part of the answer.  The main difference between you and I is that I discuss Unity features while you use arguments such as anti-Unity people are just "those who oppose change".

### Post by cariboo on 2011-07-27

Mr. Shuttleworth, has said several times, that advanced users, know enough about using Linux, that they can and will come up with a setup that works for them, which doesn't necessarily mean using Ubuntu as installed by default.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-07-27
> **uRock said:**
> Am I supposed to answer to you? I did not read your criticisms. Give me links to your bug reports and I'll read them. If I find them to be true, then I'll click the little "effects me" button.

I spoke about design, not bugs. I know that most bugs will be corrected.

If you are only interested in bug reports (which is fine of course), why do you involve yourself in discussions where design is involved without taking time to enlight yourself about the topic? (no offence, real question)


> You are trying to make me feel bad for being happy with Unity, because you aren't happy with it.

Certainly not.  I do not want that anyone happy with a system becomes unhappy with it, even with Windows :) If you like it and it fills your needs, keep it and be happy with it! I just tried a few times to raise the discussion at a technical level, and I have never received an answer about design or about a technical detail. When I take time to back up my claims with objective technical arguments, I really do not like to be called "who oppose change" without receiving a technical answer to my objections, specially from a Ubuntu forum staff member who is supposed to raise the discussion level.

> I defend Unity, because I have faith in Canonical to have it ironed out in time for the next LTS, which I believe is the reasoning behind making Unity the default. You are right, I am part of the answer, but not part of what you think is the answer.

I understand that your faith in Canonical motivates you to defend Unity.  But do you suggest that Unity was chosen to be the default because they have time to make it stable for the next LTS version?  But this is the same for Gnome. So why Unity instead of Gnome?

I read many things about the motivation of some Canonical developers, and it seems that they have other reasons.

If I take time to criticize Unity design, it is also because I have faith in Canonical.  If I did not trust them, I would already be on another distribution.  I just think that it is a mistake to impose Unity as the default desktop.  Presently, Mark Shuttleworth and others are so convinced that they know well what is good for the Linux community that they do not take user comments into account. They seem blind by their goal of having a uniform desktop on all computers, irrespective of their uses and irrespective of the user tastes and work habits.  If the user community stay silent, we may end up with a very poor Linux version sharing some Mac OSX limitations.  I just try to show by objective technical arguments what seems wrong with Unity in the hope either to be proved wrong or to be heard.  The more we yell everywhere, the higher the likelihood that we will be heard.

### Post by uRock on 2011-07-27
IMHO, design flaws are bugs, too. I am not sure where Canonical gets input, whether they actually have someone reading these threads or if they have hired folks to test and report on how the designs work. I am sure that if light is shown on good advice, then the design team will take it into consideration. If they do have someone with an open mind reading these forums for positive or, should I say, constructive input, then I would hope that having Unity as the default helped bring constructive suggestions to the table.

I have my theories as to why Canonical is creating Unity, while GNOME3 is implementing very similar interface, but I keep those to myself. I haven't used GNOME3 yet, since it wouldn't work in a VBox, but I am sure it will be easy for folks to install and implement by the time 12.04 releases.

I know there are flaws, I have clicked "effects me" on a couple of bugs which I felt would could use some tweaking, even though the bugs did not directly effect me.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-07-27
> **cariboo907 said:**
> If the majority of the people using Ubuntu had the type of setups the poster is talking about, I would agree, that where the launcher is, is a problem, but people with that type of setup, are not the type of user Unity is aimed at.

Since the majority of the people using Ubuntu prefer Gnome 2 to Unity (see the polls I have already cited), do you agree that making unity the default is a problem?

As far as the choice of the dock position is concerned, I would not be surprised if the majority of the Ubuntu users prefered to have it at the bottom of the screen.

> Unity is aimed at the average user, the type that used to run/ still runs windows, and the only change they make is the wallpaper, and the majority of their activities are internet related.

This market is already lost.  I do not remember the exact figure, but Unix-based system takes less than 5 % of the "grand-public" computer market if I remember well.  As computer industry worked a lot a few years ago to have the "grand-public" market replacing their desktops by laptops, they are now working on replacing the laptops by tablets for the usage you cite.  The smartphone and tablet markets are dominated by IOS and Android, specially the tablet market.  There are even plans to put android on laptops:
[[COLOR="Blue"]http://www.sleetherz.com/android-news/asus-is-working-on-a-13-inch-android-laptop/3957/[/COLOR]]("http://www.sleetherz.com/android-news/asus-is-working-on-a-13-inch-android-laptop/3957/")
Other manufacturers plan to do the same and Apple is slowly introducing IOS concepts in Mac OSX.  Their obvious goal is to have the same OS on your smartphone, your tablet, and your laptop.

Competing against the high-quality communication strategy of Apple, and competing against the high financial power of Google, and regarding the present position of IOS and Android, what do you think will be the market share of Ubuntu?

Ubuntu is tagged as the distribution for Linux users who do not want to mess around with a lot of system configuration. Canonical should keep doing what they are so good to do. Battling for the "grand-public" market is a lost war, specially when the average user you cite still believes that Linux is a system only manageable by technical staffs.  I think Canonical will just succeed in losing parts of its user base because if you need to tweak your desktop through direct system file manipulation, there are better options than Ubuntu.  I think that the number of people who have claimed in forums to have switched from Ubuntu to XUbuntu or Mint after the 11.04 release is just a testimony of Ubuntu future. It is sad :(

### Post by cariboo on 2011-07-27
> **mpiter said:**
> Since the majority of the people using Ubuntu prefer Gnome 2 to Unity (see the polls I have already cited), do you agree that making unity the default is a problem?

As far as the choice of the dock position is concerned, I would not be surprised if the majority of the Ubuntu users prefered to have it at the bottom of the screen.



This market is already lost.  I do not remember the exact figure, but Unix-based system takes less than 5 % of the "grand-public" computer market if I remember well.  As computer industry worked a lot a few years ago to have the "grand-public" market replacing their desktops by laptops, they are now working on replacing the laptops by tablets for the usage you cite.  The smartphone and tablet markets are dominated by IOS and Android, specially the tablet market.  There are even plans to put android on laptops:
[[COLOR="Blue"]http://www.sleetherz.com/android-news/asus-is-working-on-a-13-inch-android-laptop/3957/[/COLOR]]("http://www.sleetherz.com/android-news/asus-is-working-on-a-13-inch-android-laptop/3957/")
Other manufacturers plan to do the same and Apple is slowly introducing IOS concepts in Mac OSX.  Their obvious goal is to have the same OS on your smartphone, your tablet, and your laptop.

Competing against the high-quality communication strategy of Apple, and competing against the high financial power of Google, and regarding the present position of IOS and Android, what do you think will be the market share of Ubuntu?

Ubuntu is tagged as the distribution for Linux users who do not want to mess around with a lot of system configuration. Canonical should keep doing what they are so good to do. Battling for the "grand-public" market is a lost war, specially when the average user you cite still believes that Linux is a system only manageable by technical staffs.  I think Canonical will just succeed in losing parts of its user base because if you need to tweak your desktop through direct system file manipulation, there are better options than Ubuntu.  I think that the number of people who have claimed in forums to have switched from Ubuntu to XUbuntu or Mint after the 11.04 release is just a testimony of Ubuntu future. It is sad :(

If you are referring to the polls here in the Cafe, they don't really represent the forum users or Ubuntu users, polls are only available here in the Cafe, and the majority of our users aren't even aware there is a place like this in the forums. 

As Merk42 said:

> GNOME 2 is being discontinued by GNOME, so staying with GNOME 2 is not an option.

Development on Gnome 2.X has stopped, it's time to use a new DE, or continue using a Distro that still supports the 2 panel interface, or step up to the next release, and use gnome-session-fallback, which is the new version of the 2 panel interface. it isn't quite as configurable as the Gnome 2 version yet, but improvements are being made every day. Be sure to read the release notes, as the way things work has changed.

---

### Post by BobSongs on 2011-07-27
I'm not going to defend or put Unity/GNOME3 down. I'm going to say what I believe is going to happen. Simple as that.

1. Canonical and GNOME are not deaf. Yes, they have their development ideas. And they'll probably want to stick to their guns as to how a Desktop Environment should look. But it's one thing to be extremely determined. It's another to realize your user-base doesn't appreciate some of the changes your tool ships with. Chances are? They'll bring back a few of the things that were removed. And Ubuntu is released every 6 months. Don't like it? No problem. Wait a few more months and we'll see what ships.

2. Developers use Unity and GNOME3. Learning a whole new environment and losing beloved components are **not** going to fly well with them. Chances are? They'll have quite a number of patches & tweaks available to help Unity and/or GNOME3 look more like GNOME2. True, we'd be obliged to search for these tweaks using Google. <sarcasm>And we all know how Ubuntu users NEVER use Google</sarcasm>. 

I can't complain. I didn't pay for Ubuntu. I didn't shell out for Unity. I didn't write a cheque for GNOME. Complaining about a Windows I paid for? That makes sense. But I don't think it's right to complain about an Ubuntu & Unity I didn't pay for.

Mind you: I can **sympathize** with a person who struggles to understand Unity and/or GNOME3. A sudden UI change is tough to deal with regardless.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-07-28
> **lucazade said:**
> A change of philosophy?
Just because gnome2 shipped two anachronistic panels and a win95-like start menu
while unity comes with a top panel with a side dock and an icon based applications menu?

To me these are superficial details, nothing that could change my level of freedom using ubuntu.

I have not read yet a new Ubuntu manifesto, but I read somewhere that one of the present goals of Canonical is to have almost everyone with the same simple desktop on every platforms leaving the advanced users to tweak it differently.  That is a change in philosophy compared to Gnome 2.  Being unable to choose his dock position, or to replace the sliders by scrollbars, or to get rid of a global menu, etc. without manually changing system files (advanced-user task) may not be in itself a change of philosphy, but these are the consequences of the change of philosophy.

### Post by Motorhead Kaze on 2011-07-28
I don't like major changes to my OS when things are already working great. Case in point, while I have tried each upgrade since Feisty, I have only stayed with Feisty, Hardy and Lucid. Right away Unity ticked me off because there was no GOOD reason to move the menus that we have used for years and years. The "Search" box I did like, but my main reaction was that someone had broken into my house and rearranged all my belongings. I was really pleased to find that I could revert to the "Classic Desktop" (Gnome).

Reverting took care of problems I had with minimizing windows and having them NOT show up once I clicked the Unity icon, nor when clicking Super+W as I had read about. And reverting to Gnome also took care of the strange issue in Firefox where any time I attempted to right click on a bookmark, F.F. would respond as if I had left-clicked. Also, with Unity, you cannot click on icons to open new windows in that program, you can in Gnome.

Unity is an answer to a problem I didn't have.

---

### Post by Peter09 on 2011-07-28
Yes - well I think we all understand that Unity has some bugs - however bugs get fixed. 

The only real issue I have seen in Unity is 'focus follows mouse' - most of the rest seems to be about people not wanting to change their particular work flow.

 Many others seem to be bizarre


[LIST]
[*]It is not specifically designed for a touch interface
[*]It really does not look like MAC/Windows/Android etc etc
[*]It is not a dumbed down interface - it does make some things more available.
[/LIST]
Its ability to be configured to a particular user is less than other WM's - I hope it will change. However too much configuration present instability - choices to make.

### Post by yogiman.uk on 2011-07-28
I've been using Ubuntu for a good while now, since V6 and to be honest I was looking forward to seeing this new desktop.

Sadly the desktop we've been presented with is designed for those lacking in any computing skills what so ever and the developers should drop it.

This reminds me of the sort of ridiculous stupid thing that Microsoft did with office, removing the menus and replacing them with ribbons.

Look, people have been using drop down menus etc for a very long time, why mess with something that works.

I've had to re-install 10.04 to make my system 1. stable, but more importantly 2. Usable.

I am afraid it's a big NO! from me and if the dev team decide to continue along this course I will be moving to a different distro all together.

Which saddens me as I've been fighting the Ubuntu battle for a long time and recommending it to people, however now I find I can not.

Bad thing to do, what's the point in changing an interface which works just fine the way it is!

After all (no offence intended here) some of us are more than happy having a system you can do what you want with, making such a basic desktop and suggesting this is an improvement is a bonkers move in the wrong direction.

Cheers ;-)

---

### Post by Peter09 on 2011-07-28
:D Hi - discussion not argument.

The trouble with - why change something if it works - is that you stagnate while the world moves on.

My guess is that the evolution of the desktop in the next decade will move away from what we see currently towards an interface which requires less input and less skill by the user to do what's needed.

Many of the websites we see now already reflect that trend. Lots of back end processing to support a simple interface - look at this forum for instance.

### Post by WasMeHere on 2011-07-28
**Happy with Ubuntu, but not with Unity**

I run Lucid because of the long time support on my main desktop, 'my work-horse'. And I am happy with it. My daughter likes Linux Mint, my son likes Kubuntu. They are 'average users', not really interested in the systems (and use Windows for playing games).

I run Natty with Unity on 'my scout'. It installs and works well and I have found that there are advantages with newer versions of some programs. I realize that the GUI style of computers will come closer to the look-and-feel of hand-held devices, and I have to learn to swim in the new eddy currents. But I don't feel comfortable with it. I certainly like gnome much better. I like Openbox and KDE better too.

Suggestion

May I suggest, that you let the user choose between

1. Mobile phone & netbook desktop (Unity)
2. Traditional PC desktop (Gnome (or ...))
(default: Unity)

during the installation of Ubuntu.

Of course both user interfaces are available, but if the user can set the default behaviour during the installation, he/she will be happier. I think that old-timers like me don't want to stumble into something that feels like downgrading. New users (using mobile phones) will probably be happy with Unity. I would like that you go on maintaining Gnome, but if you have reasons to select another 'traditional desktop' it is OK for me.

Sometimes we don't see the distinction between complaining and constructive criticism.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-07-29
> **BobSongs said:**
> *"Because of the free nature of Ubuntu's release of Linux we are certainly free to offer constructive criticism. Good developers appreciate it to keep their work up to par. And Unity/GNOME3 will mature over time through such helpful input from their user-base."*

All those morale debates are culturally dependent.  I perfectly understand your point because I had lived for 2.5 years in Kingston, Ontario (had a great time in Canada :D).  But I grew up in Belgium, a social country where in my direct environment morale was considered above money considerations when I was a child. In our view, with leadership come responsibilities toward your followers, as I explained in my previous post with the tai ji example.

In 23 years of research and development on Unix-based systems, including several years of system management, I have never seen a Linux-based distribution as buggy as Ubuntu 11.04, not matter which criteria you choose to compare.  You can read from the top of Ubuntu home page ([[COLOR="Blue"]http://www.ubuntu.com/[/COLOR]]("http://www.ubuntu.com/")):
> Ubuntu is a fast, secure and easy-to-use operating system used by millions of people around the world.
In my view, millions of users means responsibilities from Canonical.  Also, from the middle of their home page:
> **Ubuntu for business**
Secure, [COLOR="Red"]reliable[/COLOR] and with no licence fees, businesses everywhere are using Ubuntu to reduce costs and boost performance.
I colored myself the word "reliable".  We know that at its release time, Natty was not reliable at all.  That update has costed millions of $ worldwide. Why?  Just because Canonical wanted to distribute Unity when it was not ready yet, so they released on OS which was still in beta state.  In my lab, we plan to give up Ubuntu because we cannot afford to place our trust in a company that makes promises and does not fulfill  them.  We are speaking about servers, productivity tools, not about toys.

With leadership comes responsibilities. We feel entitled to complain.

You are right. My mistake.  I like to have the same OS versions on all the computers I manage. This way, if I suspect something strange on a server, I make most tests on a desktop to avoid perturbating the server.  Since 1988, I have always done all the upgrades on my desktops and laptops as soon as they were available to have the last driver and software improvements for my daily work. So I have done the same for the servers. In all this time, I only met 3 incidents: 1993, 2003 (lack of hardware support unrelated to the Linux provider), and 2011. In other words, Ubuntu 11.04 was the first incident related to a Unix-based OS provider I have met during the last 18 years.  I naively assumed that no Unix-based OS provider would dare to do something like this.  This is what we call "Experience" :D

### Post by ankspo71 on 2011-07-30
@ ColeTurner

Natty doesn't have the option to install Gnome-Shell, I think because it was too incompatible with Unity or something, but the option of installing Gnome-shell is returning in Oneiric Ocelot. Natty does offer the traditional Gnome desktop (aka Ubuntu Classic) as a fallback option though, but that will not be available in Oneiric Ocelot because Ubuntu is moving on to Gnome3 libraries. 

If for some reason your computer can't run the Unity desktop... it is possible to install a 2-D version called "Unity-2D" in Natty (and I think Unity-2D will be already installed by default in Oneiric Ocelot). The 2D version was created for the people who don't have 3D capable graphics cards or drivers but want to use Unity.

So Natty has these Gnome based desktops/shells:
Unity (installed), Ubuntu Classic (installed as fallback), and Unity-2D is available for installation.

And Oneiric Ocelot (by the looks of it) will have these Gnome based desktops/shells:
Unity (installed), Unity-2D (installed as fallback), and Gnome Shell available for installation.

*fallback* means Ubuntu has been installing a secondary desktop for users to automatically 'fallback to' if their hardware and/or drivers don't support Unity's 3D effects.

### Post by blueridgedog on 2011-08-01
I still do not grasp the number of people upset with a PORTION of a desktop environment.  

I use Gnome as a DE, but am running Docky as my dock and cardapio as my menu system.  If you know Linux then you should be aware of the fact that you can run a variety of window managers and one of two leading DE's.  Within the Gnome DE, you can run a variety of docks and THREE overlay interfaces (gnome 2, gnome 3, unity).  If you don't know how window managers and window decorators work in Linux, then we should start a more productive thread teaching people how the DE, window manager and window decorator are related and can be user selected.

If you are not happy with it, then exercise your freedom to use what you are happy with, but don't expect the default install to be tailored to your preferences.  You should expect to be able to use your computer the way that brings you bliss, and you can do so with 11.04 just as can with most Linux distributions by taking the base install then adjusting it to suit your needs and desires.  Be careful to note that if you do use Linux, there is an expectation that you will put the effort needed to making it work for yourself and others.

My posting about this shows that I am willing to go out on a limb and do just that. No, I don't have the skills most here have and I am OK with that. As an end user, my opinion should count. 

As for what I don't like about Unity:
[LIST]
[*]Its clunky.
[*]Its hard to navigate.
[*]Its counter-intuitive.
[*]I don't "get it".
[*]Its not conducive to productivity. 
[/LIST]

### Post by NightwishFan on 2011-08-02
> **NetDoc said:**
> As for what I don't like about Unity:
> Its clunky
What does this mean..? This is non-specific.

> Its hard to navigate.
That is a bit better. Though this is mainly to do with just the dash launcher thingy?

> Its counter-intuitive.
This is something that requires more of a 'general' explanation to be fair than an individual one like WorksForMe or IHateIt! Ones experience and needs may not be the same for others.

> I don't "get it
This sounds like trolling.

> Its not conducive to productivity.
I hear people say this a lot about various window managers compared to others. Frankly I think it all boils down to 'do I use a single application or many at once?'. If you use a single application at a time the application handles much of your needs and thus complaining about the environment is less important or relevant. So by productivity you must mean the ease of working with more than one program at once.

As far as I have seen unity has features that make this easy such as an expose-like view of workspaces; Viewing two windows at once by dragging them to the edges, etc.. Those seem like easy to understand concepts.

Now if you mean launching and accessing programs; I am not sure what is faster than searching or pinning icons to the size panel. Navigating manually in the dash from what I have seen could be improved.

I think you are just making waves.

If you use a single application at a time the application handles much of your needs and thus complaining about the environment is less important or relevant. So by productivity you must mean the ease of working with more than one program at once.  All of us have gravitated to Linux and Ubuntu for various reasons. Personally, my business runs off of Linux, and a number of those servers run on Ubuntu. I felt that the best way to get a handle on the IT part of my business was to immerse myself into Ubuntu. Its been an awesome learning experience and I am happy with the progress thus far. Still, I have a business to run while I am learning the system, so I need to remain somewhat productive. I guess that's why I have one lone Windows PC here in the office. Its a fail safe of sorts. After all, as a former Network Consultant for Windows based systems, its what I knew best. Not so much anymore and I have become a real fan of Linux in general and of Ubuntu specifically. 

> **NightwishFan said:**

A person comes in asking about a product that the community has abandoned as being useful (Spare Air, for example) and many in the community erroneously label that person a troll or worse. Vilification of your "opponent" is a time honored tradition. Just be sure that the person is an actual opponent before you do. Calling them a troll is a sure fire way to produce a troll. I'm pretty immune to such shenanigans, but for many: them's fightin' words!

### Post by cariboo on 2011-08-02
With a quick search I found several links to pages telling you how to disable the global menu, this is the first link:

[http://www.webupd8.org/2011/03/disable-appmenu-global-menu-in-ubuntu.html](http://www.webupd8.org/2011/03/disable-appmenu-global-menu-in-ubuntu.html)

If you don't like the overlay-scrollbar, just purge the overlay-scrollbar package.

At the moment, Unity is very much a DIY interface, there are tools being built to modify Unity, but they aren't ready yet. If there is something you want to change, usually a quick google search will turn up a howto.

### Post by RiceMonster on 2011-08-03
> **YesWeCan said:**
> I thought the term "troll" implied ill-intentioned? Are there well-intentioned trolls too?

On here, "troll" is a weasel word that can mean whatever you want. Some examples are where the poster in question:
- has a different opinion than me
- said something that I deem incorrect
- likes what I don't like
- doesn't like what I like

A real troll would be someone who does or says things purely to get a reaction out of people.

---

### Post by YesWeCan on 2011-08-03
:P @ Ricemonster


Full disclosure:
I should point out that I think the whole linux thing is quite astounding. It is a movement to behold in terms of true cooperation among people for the good of all, and a refreshing change from the costly shackles of the computer corporations. Linux has transformed my computing experience and opened my eyes to what generous people can achieve together.

I separate this shining aspect from the objective assessment of linux products. I think it is vital for the future of linux that we linux advocates are at least as hard on linux as critics. Because this is the way to focus on improvement and avoid falling into self-satisfied complacency.

So I am quite tolerant, even welcoming, of new users or existing ones who sincerely express grievances. And lets not be in denial: there are loads of valid grievances. These expressions should not be suppressed but should be taken seriously and acted upon.

IMO Unity is clunky and awkward but it works. I personally don't see why Canonical is putting scarce resources into it when Gnome Shell is just as functional and when there are so many more important things (wrt usability for and uptake by normal humans) to fix and improve. There must be motivations at play that are out of alignment with the "linux for humans" motto but I am not privy to Canonical's business plan, which I assume has been well thought out.

---

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-08-03
> **blueridgedog said:**
> I would hope they would do the same thing most of us did...play, learn, break it, fix it.

Which ignores the fact that most people aren't in the slightest bit interested in breaking or fixing their computers.  They certainly don't expect their computer to be broken through no fault of their own and would consider it a major fail if it was.

If Ubuntu is to make any progress with Bug #1 this fact has to be taken into account.  The current perception (right or wrong) that Linux based systems are hard to install, maintain, and use is the single most important problem in overcoming bug #1.  If the system is broken on install (which Natty 1 was) it's really not helping this perception at all.

I think the current ratio of Linux users to users of other OS's probably closely resembles the ratio of geeks to "others". Ubuntu isn't going to break this ratio until it changes its image, and it won't do that by being an OS that is difficult to maintain or hard to use.  ie it's going to be stuck on a user base of a few percent despite the best efforts of everyone trying to promote it until such a time when this perception is changed.

Chris

---

### Post by cariboo on 2011-08-03
@fabricator4

Do you actually use Ubuntu, on well supported hardware, it is much easier to maintain than Windows, there are no AV updates to do, no malware scans to run, and the only updates to the LTS are for security reasons. If you are using one of the interim releases, there are bug fixes, but with only an effective life of six months even though they are supported for 18 months, most people will install the next version before the bugs are fixed. The one the thing you can be assured of is that there aren't any major show stoppers.

The problem with Ubuntu's reputation, is more because many users use a shotgun approach when trying to repair a problem, they try 5 or 6 different solutions without really understanding what they are doing. They copy the so called **code** from a post, and paste it into a terminal and press enter, if that doesn't seem to work, they try the next copy and paste solution, and so on and so on. Because they don't know what they are doing, they have no idea how to reverse the changes, to get back to where they started.

The biggest problem is education, but how do you educate someone that thinks they know what they are doing, because they've been running Windows for several years.

---

### Post by jtarin on 2011-08-03
> **cariboo907 said:**
> 
The biggest problem is education, but how do you educate someone that thinks they know what they are doing, because they've been running Windows for several years.
You don't. 
(Generalities)
People want familiar...People want comfortable.....People want zero stress. People expect one thing from their computing experience......the same thing every time they turn it on. People don't want to learn something new.....they either have no time or the inclination.Some people just do not have the ability. God did not create us all equal in all things.
I worked for an ISP at one time in the 90's and used Linux as my control console for accounts and administration. There was not one call-in in those days for help with Linux....everyone was using Windows. 90% were problems setting up their dial-up account. At that time it wasn't as intuitive in Windows as it was now, but nevertheless a piece of cake for someone that had done it thousands of times.I could walk a person through it without even looking at the computer or a schematic.But you would not believe what it took to get people to just find the "Start Menu". I kid you not. We assume a lot when we think people should "just get it". Most people have invested a lot in learning Windows...by their standards.....they don't want to have to go through that tortuous path again. Free or not.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-08-03
So you're saying that Ubuntu and the rest of the Linux distributions are doing it wrong? They should have a big Start button in the lower left corner?

Even the old two panel interface got it wrong with the menu in the upper left corner. It was more like the Windows 3.0 interface.

I know people like to use a familiar interface, just have a look at the screenshot thread, some people spend a lot of time making their desktop look like Windows.

That is all going to change, as even windows is going to change the desktop interface in version 8.

I know I don't belong to the right demographic that all these changes are aimed at, but I've learned to use Unity, and Gnome-shell. To me the two panel interface feels old and clunky, and Lubuntu feels like a throwback to 1995. I haven't tried Xubuntu in a couple of years, but I never did like the desktop. Kubuntu's menu felt to busy to me with way more choices than I need.

I like simplicity, and Unity fits my needs it gets out of the way and lets me get things done.

### Post by jtarin on 2011-08-03
> **cariboo907 said:**

> **cariboo907 said:**

I come in the house I put my keys in the same place everyday so I don't have to look for them the next morning....I have to many other things on my mind. I don't even think or want to learn to put them in a new place unless I am forced to.I do this with most things I use in everyday life....I expect things to be where I place them. My wife on the other hand is a hurricane looking for someplace to land. Her things are everywhere and she is in a constant state of stress due to not finding anything. Guess who has to remember where she scatters her things to? This is the way we are.....organized....disorganized. She has used a computer for more than 13 years, but anything beyond MS Word, e-mail and Facebook....forget it. This is what we're dealing with....two camps.....organize/disorganized.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-08-03
@capngloval

I'd suggest you stick with the LTS releases, by the time 12.04 is released most of the problems with Unity, Gnome-shell and gnome-session-fallback should be ironed out.

Unity will still be installed by default, but you will have the choice of installing gnome-shell, or just gnome-session-fallback, which is the new improved two panel interface.

Personally it makes sense to have all the controls on the left, as there is less mouse movement, when using the menus, starting programs or closing one program and opening another.

### Post by Bazon on 2011-08-05
> **cariboo907 said:**
> I agree criticism is good, but the Cafe is the wrong place to get things changed. There is a poster earlier on in the thread that complained about the focus follows mouse function not working anymore, the correct place for that problem is Launchpad, complaining about it here in the forum doesn't do a lot of good. Unfortunately most of the complaints about Unity sound more like entitlement issues rather than real problems.

The sad thing is:
Even on launchpad things aren't any better. **Many usability issues with big numbers of affected users get the status "won't fix".** 

Such as the here mentioned [focus follows mouse issue](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/674138) with 58 affected users.
Or the ["Minimize Application's Windows upon clicking its Launcher Icon"](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ayatana-design/+bug/733349) issue affecting 102 users.
Or the ["Movement of Unity launcher"](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/668415) issue with 112 affected users.


Other important issues such as ["cannot paste into dash (particularly relevant for alt+f2)"](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/736222) (with 119 affected users) get low priority or are threatened by expiration such as ["should be possible to display only windows from the current workspace in the launcher"](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/unity/+bug/683170) (31 affected users) or are simply ignored such as ["very hard to distinguish/select text documents in the expose window spread"](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/734253) (25 affected users).

*(Just for comparison: The bug with most affected users in Unity is [that compiz memory leak](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/720446) with 178 affected users)*.


All that really frustrated me and I'm really disappointed. Before Unity, I was an enthusiastic Ubuntu user and recommended it to everyone *(and helped with several Ubuntu installations)*.
Now, with Unity, I lost trust in Canonical and don't recommend it anymore.
Canonical just seems to not listen to the user community. *(and yes, there are real problems, especially the workspace issue makes Unity unusable for me.)*

For now, I'm using Xubuntu and Lubuntu, and you may so: So why are you complaining about Unity?
That's easy: When switching to Linux, I decided to follow the mainstream, because I assumed to meat the best documentation and user-community to help there. 
And in fact, that was right. And because of that, I can't recommend Xubuntu or Lubuntu to a new user.

---

### Post by Peter09 on 2011-08-05
**Only Icons in the active workspace are displayed**:

Yes - but there are others who do not want this sort of functionality:

When I click on the firefox icon I want to go to firefox, which ever workspace its in, I do not want to have to select a workspace and then select an application running in that workspace - it also means that while active in one workspace  I may not be aware of activities going on in another - there are two opinions here - yours is not necessarily the right one.

It also means that the Launcher summarises all thats going-on on my machine.

I guess the developers have to be the judges of this.

### Post by Bazon on 2011-08-05
> **Peter09 said:**
> **Only Icons in the active workspace are displayed**:

Yes - but there are others who do not want this sort of functionality:

When I click on the firefox icon I want to go to firefox, which ever workspace its in, I do not want to have to select a workspace and then select an application running in that workspace - it also means that while active in one workspace  I may not be aware of activities going on in another - there are two opinions here - yours is not necessarily the right one.

It also means that the Launcher summarises all thats going-on on my machine.

I guess the developers have to be the judges of this.


Therefore there is this nice thing called "options". But Unity developers unfortunately have a strong will against adding options...


And to mention why I desire the other behaviour:

1. I often have many pdfs open I need regularly, but in different contexts. For each context, I use one workspace. Everything I need in this context is only one click away in the window switcher.
2. I don't like being distracted by things going on on other workspaces. When I'm on a workspace I use for professional things, I don't want to see things happening on my private workspace, cause that helps me focusing on my actual work.

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-08-05
> **Bazon said:**
> 1. I often have many pdfs open I need regularly, but in different contexts. For each context, I use one workspace. Everything I need in this context is only one click away in the window switcher.

Spread was changed in Oneiric to only show windows on the current desktop, if only one window is open that is on another desktop, clicking the icon will still take you to that desktop. The down side, to me, is that now if an app has multiple windows on multiple desktops, but not the desktop you are on, clicking the icon on the launcher will take you to the desktop where the window of that app that had the most recent focus resides. 

> 2. I don't like being distracted by things going on on other workspaces. When I'm on a workspace I use for professional things, I don't want to see things happening on my private workspace, cause that helps me focusing on my actual work.

Kinda begs the question of why you have stuff running you don't want to be distracted by.

And to touch on an earlier post, I don't understand how someone can say [> * Whilst watching a TV programme in a large window, I wish to microblog about the TV programme to my followers without distracting myself from the programme. I can hover the mouse over twitter client dialog box without it obscuring the video window.](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/674138)

Isn't bloggin while watching a TV program by definition distracting you from the TV program?

And... Yes the mouse follow focus thing could use some work, but I think for many people there is a combination of settings that would work for them.

> As I understand it, with global appmenu enabled the menu for a window is placed at the top of the screen and changes as the window focus changes. If I am using focus follows mouse and wish to select a menu item for Window A, then as the mouse moves over Window B en-route to the menu, the menu content will change, making it challenging to select menu options for WIndow A. Under certain circumstances it may be possible to take a circuitous route to get the mouse to the appmenu so the menu content doesn't change, but this is suboptimal.

Don't know how many windows you have to have open before this becomes a problem, or how often people actually want to access menus of a window that is in the background.

But I do know you can uninstall the global menu stuff with 2 commands, 1 command if you already have aptsh installed.

```
sudo apt-get install aptsh
sudo aptsh purge appmenu* indicator-appmenu*
```

Which in my Oneiric installation indicates it would remove 5 packages if I said yes to the confirmation.

Later, Seeker

But whatever. Like I said, I'm liking it more than I thought I would.It probably helps that I waited a few months to upgrade, and they got some of the bugs worked out. (Bugs, not design flaws. Those are still there.)

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-08-06
> **capngloval said:**
> I don't understand the negativity towards offering people options.

All I want is the option to put screen elements where I want.

I am not as anti-unity as I am anti options. 

People went to gnome 2 to have a simple environment they could control, we just want that in Unity.

For me, all I want is that I can put the min - max window controls on the right, where they belong (for me and 98% of computers users today, save gui-less servers and macs).

All I am asking for is freedom to change my environment how I see fit, all you seem to want is to force me into your idea of what is good.

Let's all just get along, please.
I'm confused - I see this complaint all the time, but as far as I know, ever since the change was made to move the buttons to the left, it's been possible to change them back using gconf-editor.  It takes about 30 seconds to fix.  Surely people with this complaint have tried a Google search?
Feel free to try it yourself: Alt+F2, type gconf-editor (install from synaptic if necessary), open Apps --> Metacity --> General and go to the 'Button Layout' key.  Change it from close,minimize,maximize: to :minimize,maximize,close and you're done.  (You can even try out all sorts of different crazy layouts to your heart's content.)

### Post by ScionicSpectre on 2011-08-07
The funny thing is that people feel like they have to use Unity. Even in GNOME 3 there's gnome-panel and metacity if you want it, good ol' style (while I would recommend XFCE if you're into a GNOME 2-like environment).

I think the main gripe people have is that this is how Ubuntu will ship on hardware and be seen by new users, and they don't necessarily think it's setting a good impression for them. However, most people like Unity a lot, and everyone I've heard compare it to Windows (who wasn't already a Linux user) seemed to like it a lot better. So it's a good experience, and getting better, but it won't fit everyone. It doesn't fit me, for instance, but that doesn't mean it's bereft of potential or good features.

People who complain about how Unity isn't more like GNOME 2 kind of remind me of people who complain that Krita isn't more like the GIMP, or Firefox isn't more like Chrome, or KDE isn't more like OS X. Maybe we're done making each piece of software for everyone, and we're starting to encourage people to use software that suits their needs specifically. GNOME 2 was for everyone, but only a little. It was the greatest common factor, and it wasn't too bad, but developers aren't satisfied with 'not too bad'. They want to do the best they can in providing a slick and focused experience for users, one that's more powerful. And sometimes that leads to defaults that won't fit a few power users out there.

Big whoop, you can customize it anyway, and it's getting easier to do so. But there is a GNOME 2 fork called MATE and there are plenty of highly customizable environments/elements to choose from. I just feel like some users have been babied for so long with GNOME 2 that a little change makes them vomit on the carpet.

---

The only thing that throws me off is the global menu on a large screen - programs with small windows can end up very far from their menus.  Perhaps one day when I'm procrastinating I'll get around to disabling the global menu for certain programs on my desktop computer.

I don't know how to "disable" it but it is very easy to get rid of it, just remove the package indicator-appmenu from Synaptic, logout and log back in and it is gone. It is the first thing I do whenever I install Natty. :)


P.S. I can think of scenarios where you would need to maximize and unmaximize apps no matter what your screen size is. For example, I would not want vlc to maximize if I use it to listen to music, but I would want it to take up the whole screen for watching videos.

Classic Gnome (2.x) is no longer being developed, and I don't think it's supported either, hence the move to Gnome 3 and Unity - Many distributions will go the same way.  If you want to have a look at Gnome3 then the Debian distribution uses it.  It's very similar to the Unity concept but feels more polished and looks nicer IMO.

You should also be able to uninstall Unity and install Classic Gnome if you want to in the future, since it won't ship with 11.10 as far as I know.  To answer the question asked by the person you replied to however: Many people prefer to go with the mainstream distibution (ie Ubuntu) and asking them to install an alternate DE (ie Gnome 2) on that takes them out of their comfort zone, since any problems encountered are not likely to be supported by Canonical.

* I understand about leaving the comfort zone, but really, what's the point of doing thAT and stay there? I love the panels. They are quick, efficient, and the stay out of the way. No browsing or scrolling. Unity is BIG, ugly, cumbersome, and I want nothing of it.

When I install my system, I always start with the server version so when I install the rest of the programs I won't have wasted time installing the CD version first, and then the updates. If I could choose the menu system during that install and not have to REMOVE unwanted things afterwards, I'd be happy.

I'm happy with Linux, primarily because it's NOT Windows. I like change, but it must be FOR THE BETTER. In this case, in my opinion, it's just the opposite. I want something I can use. I don't mind so much it doesn't look so nice (Ubuntu Classic still does) but it must WORK. I was dead tired of spending half of my time with the PC fixing Windows problems. I'm happier now (I ditched Windows more than 3 years ago), spending time programming and solving real problems, and spending a minimum of time re-installing and rebooting. Going from Windows to Linux was like going from hell to heaven. Let's not go half way back. Let's go to the future. The future as I see it, is NOT Unity, at least for someone who wants to get things done.

### Post by ingeva on 2011-08-08
Surely a MEGA thread, this! :D
About 4 years ago I bought this computer, a Dell Vostro 400, with one
purpose in mind: To have an alternative to Windows, planning to migrate
to Linux. I had heard rumors that Dell computers would work with Ubuntu,
so that's what I bought. They wouldn't sell them with Ubuntu installed
however, so I had to do that myself, starting from almost complete scratch
(I had used Unix-family systems before).
It took a long time before I could make it work. Until 8.04 came out no
distribution would accept my hard disks, which were set up as IDE, and
when someone finally told me I would have to switch to RAID, things
started to roll. Or maybe it was the other way around.
Of course Windows didn't like that so I had to reconfigure the BIOS if I
wanted to boot Windows (which I kept installed to have a backup).

When I finally got Linux (Ubuntu 8.04) installed it took me about a week
to feel comfortable with it, and since then my visits to the Windows world
have become fewer and fewer, most of them occur when I'm called out
to help friends and neighbors with it. OMG, how much trouble they have,
and *how clumsy* their systems are - especially of course, to those who
have "upgraded" to the horrible Windows 7!

I really do feel like I'm in a comfort zone. I get things done. I never put
icons on my desktop. I mean **NEVER.** That would be counter-productive.
Instead I keep them in the side panels, so they are always there (No, I
don't even use autohide, because they are small and not a nuisance like
the dinosaur icons in the Unity panel.)

When I started the Natty 11.04 desktop the first time, I got stomach-ache.
I was shocked. I had no idea about what Linus Thorvalds meant about it, but
I somehow feel happy that we share the same opinion. Well, maybe [I]it's
because[/I] I have also been involved in writing operating systems, in an
ancient time when there was no graphics .... :modest:

I don't rule out the possibility that I might like Unity when it has gotten time
to evolve and become somewhat user friendly, but I think that will take a
long time. The fact that it won't even work on 3 of my 4 computers (Yes, I
have tried!) tells me that some changes need to be made anyway.
People don't want to go out and purchase a new game computer just to be
able to run Unity, or do they?

I haven't spent too much time reading about all the new stuff, because I've
been working on development projects of my own. It's hard to learn about
all the new stuff, and I know it's necessary to keep the axe sharp but I
really don't need another axe now that I have a gas driven chainsaw.

Oh, just a question: What's the difference between Unity and Gnome 3?

### Post by cariboo on 2011-08-08
In natty Unity was a shell running on top of Gnome 2.X, the devs are in the process of converting it to use the new Gnome 3 libraries (GTK3) for Oneiric (11.10), the Gnome developers have also created a new shell to run on Gnome 3 called Gnome-shell. They have stopped development on Gnome 2.X, and the intention was to not use the two panel interface anymore. It was found that many users still don't have the hardware to run Gnome-shell, so a new version of the two panel interface was created, that is supposed to be the fallback mode.

Unity was first created as a netbook interface running on mutter in Maverick (10.10) When development of Natty started it looked like Gnome 3 wouldn't be ready by the release date, so Unity, now based on compiz was and is still being devloped

Screenchots of Unity can be found [here]("http://www.webupd8.org/2010/05/taking-ubuntu-unity-interface-for-test.html") and gnome screenshots can be found [here]("http://www.techdrivein.com/2011/02/gnome-shell-review-its-starting-to-look.html")

### Post by bbrg548 on 2011-08-08
My first reaction?

Blech!  :-&

It looks like they took the worst part of MacOS/OSX (the global menu), and the worst part of Windoze 7 (the launcher thingy), and then made them even *worse*.

The global menu: Why separate the menus from the window they're supposed to control? How does that make any kind of sense whatsoever? And at least on Macs, the menu is visible all the time! What's the point of hiding it so that you have to move the mouse to the top bar to see if a window even *has* a menu? I have despised that arrangement on Macs for 27 years.

The launcher/dock/whatever: The little icons tell you nothing about what's open in a program, the little triangle indicators are hard to see, you can't move it to any other part of the screen, and clicking on an open program's icon won't minimize it.

The dock at least has some promise, as all of these issues are fixable, but there are other issues with the DE as a whole as well (the poor organization of the programs in the menu, how hard it is to find programs that aren't in the dock, the difficulty in opening multiple instances of the same program, etc.). Some are easily fixable, some appear to be more difficult to fix, but almost all appear to be poor design decisions.

Perhaps, since Gnome 2 was going away anyway, the devs would have been better off investing their time in creating a new DE that incorporated the best aspects of Gnome 2 (i.e., easily customizable panels) and integrating them more smoothly with Compiz and Metacity, rather than... this drek.

You can improve the wheel with new materials, designs, and processes, but it needs to stay round. Octagons just don't work.

Edit: Oh, yeah. My vote? I went back to the Gnome Classic, and was glad I had tried it on an older computer I don't use much before upgrading my daily use machine.

### Post by ScionicSpectre on 2011-08-08
I think usability and productivity don't ALWAYS go hand in hand. That is, GNOME 3 is usable and productive, while most other environments are equally productive but not necessarily very usable. Usability depends a lot on how a user expects to interact with their computer, of course, but for new computer users it seems GNOME 3 is a big winner (from my research with new computer users in my own family). The less you have to explain, the better, and many times that will translate well into usability for the people who know a lot about computers as well.

So there is a kind of usability that's good for you no matter what kind of user you are, and there's usability that's subjective. But if you have to switch windows so often that a minor change in window management is going to set you back significantly, you should probably reevaluate whether you need to multi-task so heavily. Most people who do will be using workspaces already.

I just think that there are a limited number of things you can do successfully in a desktop environment without alienating users and venturing too far from what's expected. I think GNOME 3 is a great example of how to pull this off successfully.

I think that Unity has a lot of good features from OS X, but I wouldn't say it's like OS X since it has many differences. If your options are between using big icons to identify applications or using smaller icons accompanied by text no one's really looking to read at first glance, I think people will more likely choose the larger icon these days. And if that means they're copying OS X, well too bad. Maybe they had a good idea that's worth sticking to.

There's plenty Unity has that OS X doesn't in functionality and appearance. I think it's just silly how hardcore technical users can be so short-sighted as to not understand that GNOME, for instance, takes accessibility very seriously, and that their user base is far broader than the people who sign up for Linux forum accounts.

They are listening to their users, and doing very serious research, and most of their users like it (Unity and GNOME both). If you don't like it, you don't have to use it, but you're more likely to be heard if you read a little bit of the research and reasoning behind the design of these interfaces first. Open source developers already know how to ignore the unhelpful trolling coming from Windows and OS X users who don't understand what they're trying to accomplish- a little understanding can go a long way, however.

---

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-08-10
While there are some complaints I have regarding lack of customization in Unity (I'm hoping we'll see new tools coming out in later versions to help with tweaking the settings), the one thing I've been quite happy with is the ability to do things efficiently from the keyboard - eg. the various Super shortcuts (Super-W, Super-S, etc) and Ctrl-Alt-Arrow, as well as still being able to use the mouse wheel to switch between desktops if I'm doing mouse-based activities like games or web browsing.
I installed Fedora 15 to an old hard drive in my desktop, and found Gnome shell harder to navigate - not just initially, but after a few weeks of practice, and with a printout of the cheat sheet for keyboard shortcuts.  Most of the time I found that switching programs/workspaces required either a lot of mouse movement, or a combination of mouse and keyboard.  (eg. since the workspace switcher displays down the right-hand side, I kept expecting to be able to expose it by moving the mouse to the right-hand edge, when in fact you have to go top-left, and then back to the right.)
A few clumsy things like this, along with the fact that there seemed to be all sorts of broken packages and software crashes (this was post-beta) led me to stop playing with it about a month ago.
