### Post by ScionicSpectre on 2011-08-11
My niece finds Unity confusing and GNOME Shell quite inviting. She's 3.

I'm a fairly technical user, but I find GNOME Shell easier to use for the same reasons as my niece. It's very clean and non-intrusive- there's a lot less to take in than in other environments. It's easier to get to the overview and manage your desktops/windows by wacking the corner with reckless abandon than aiming for a workspace button that changes position. Also, the relation of applications/windows/workspaces is very linear and easily understood in GNOME's interface. It's logical for a new user.

Also, it's more fun and stimulating to access windows in the overview than to shuffle them around by the title bars with a mouse. Much easier to do, and it gives you a better idea of what's running on your computer without any effort.

Of course, if you're an advanced user you've learned to deal with technicalities and anticipate the interface demanding more attention. But I don't think anything that makes technology easier to understand and work with is necessarily dumbing it down. There's nothing significant I can't do in the Shell that I could do in KDE or Unity.

That's not to say Unity is unpleasant or inferior. But for the purposes of appealing to people who have never used a computer, or don't want to put a lot of effort into using it, Gnome Shell is better tailored in my observations.

### Post by robert shearer on 2011-08-12
A small flicker of illumination down the rabbit hole .......

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/668415](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/668415)

> This bug has been made "don't fix" and is ignored by the Unity team.
However, it has been an important discussion forum for the (often
frustrated) community of users!

And so, I'd like to point everyone to a relevant change in Unity coming
for Oneiric: [B]the "BFB" in the top left corner is moving into the
launcher.[/B] More on this here:

[http://www.omgubuntu.co.uk/2011/08/revamped-dash-lands-unity-2d/](http://www.omgubuntu.co.uk/2011/08/revamped-dash-lands-unity-2d/)

This has lots of advantages in my view (no more clicking it by mistake
when you are trying to click on the "File" menu of an app). But, the
most important one for this "won't fix" bug is that it now makes more
sense to be able to move the launcher (to) other parts of the screen without
breaking the association with the BFB. The whole shell structure could
exist anywhere while staying 'unified'.

I don't know if there are plans to allow for this, but it's a move in
the right direction for us.

I also want to point out that apparently Unity's "vision" is not so much
a unified idea sprouting from Mark Shuttleworth's brain, but instead a
product of ongoing usability research by hired 3rd party teams. This is
good because it allows for evolution, but bad because it seems
arbitrary. Our "won't fix" bug might end up getting fixed because some
research intern point(s) out the obvious issues that have been filling
these 84 comments, not because a leader steps up and says "these guys
are right."


Context for those not subscribed....


> Mark **Shuttleworth (sabdfl) wrote on 2010-10-30**: Re: [Bug 668415] Re: Movement of Unity launcher	 #2
I think the report actually meant that the launcher should be movable to
other edges of the screen. I'm afraid that won't work with our broader
design goals, so we won't implement that. [B]We want the launcher always
close to the Ubuntu button.
[/B]
 status **wontfix**

Mark

Mark Shuttleworth (sabdfl) on 2010-10-30
Changed in unity:
status:	** Invalid &#8594; Won't Fix**

> **ScionicSpectre said:**
> My niece finds Unity confusing and GNOME Shell quite inviting. She's 3.

I'm a fairly technical user, but I find GNOME Shell easier to use for the same reasons as my niece. It's very clean and non-intrusive- there's a lot less to take in than in other environments. It's easier to get to the overview and manage your desktops/windows by wacking the corner with reckless abandon than aiming for a workspace button that changes position.

But you can set up corners using the Compiz settings plugin.  In fact, you can set up the corners to do different things in Compiz, and all four of them.


> **ScionicSpectre said:**
> Also, the relation of applications/windows/workspaces is very linear and easily understood in GNOME's interface. It's logical for a new user.

I find the workspace switcheroo with GNOME Shell awkward and I don't particularly care for automatic workspace creation.  Especially since I could not find a keyboard shortcut to see all my workspaces.  Maybe someone can enlighten me here?  I'd have to access the overview and then fling the mouse to the right to reveal the workspaces.   Or I can do WIN+S if I need in Unity + Compiz.  The only thing where GNOME 3 Shell has the right idea (kind of) is the combination of workspace + Scale.  

> **ScionicSpectre said:**
>  Also, it's more fun and stimulating to access windows in the overview than to shuffle them around by the title bars with a mouse. Much easier to do, and it gives you a better idea of what's running on your computer without any effort.

Look up the Scale plugin in Compiz.  And you can use your keyboard to switch around windows in scale, something not doable in GNOME 3 shell without a(n ugly) plugin.  Plus Scale is pretty darn customizable.    

> **ScionicSpectre said:**
> Of course, if you're an advanced user you've learned to deal with technicalities and anticipate the interface demanding more attention. But I don't think anything that makes technology easier to understand and work with is necessarily dumbing it down. There's nothing significant I can't do in the Shell that I could do in KDE or Unity.

I think GNOME 3 Shell is considerably less customizable and I couldn't get it to do all my tasks with a keyboard.  A list of shortcuts online seems to suggest some things are just not possible keyboard-only in GNOME 3.  That's a deal breaker.

> **ScionicSpectre said:**
> That's not to say Unity is unpleasant or inferior. But for the purposes of appealing to people who have never used a computer, or don't want to put a lot of effort into using it, Gnome Shell is better tailored in my observations.

Stock GNOME 3 Shell is prettier than Unity, but it doesn't take much to get Unity to look pretty nice.

### Post by sgage on 2011-08-18
> **Idefix82 said:**
> Isn't Ocelot supposed to some with gnome 3, along with unity?

Oneiric Ocelot is **built** on Gnome 3. The "official" UI for Gnome 3 is Gnome Shell, but Ubuntu has developed Unity, which is simply a different shell, and made that their default UI.

So whether you use Gnome Shell or Unity, the underlying "plumbing" is the Gnome 3 stack.

Gnome Shell is not installed by default in Oneiric, but is easily available in the repos - you simply do an 'apt-get install gnome-shell'.

### Post by jimmer321 on 2011-08-19
I have been using Ubuntu since the 8.04 release and truly feel it is the best linux out there. I do have some concerns and have been looking at other flavors of linux. From what I have have read in the next release of Ubuntu I understand they are going to be doing away with Gnome and force us into Unity. I tried Unity for about 3 month and it seems to work well and I think it be great for very basic computer users that want something that looks nice and handles everything they need but for a power user or even business user I find it clumbersome to get around in. The set up ubuntu has now works well where you can choose your desktop enviroment at the login screen. It would be a shame to remove that option from Ubuntu. This is what I give you this is what you get. It is all about freedom of choice. Both desktop enviroment have their place and are both very good options. I am for progress so in worse case situation if there was and option on the install to select up to 3 desktop enviroments and you could just check them off then have the user option on the log in screen that would be great and everybody would be happy. If you want to push Unity have it as the default desktop.
Hope the developement team is listening..

You heard wrong...  Next version of Unity will be built on GNOME 3 and installing the GNOME 3 Shell will be a breeze.  GNOME 2 and GNOME 2 shell will be gone, that's true, but nobody will be 'forcing' you into Unity.

As for productivity, I feel exactly the opposite about Unity as I've outlined elsewhere.  I am not able to use GNOME 3 Shell without resorting to the mouse (well, I *could* but it doesn't allow me to replicate all mouse behaviours via the keyboard), and therefore find it unfinished at present (unless you're comparing GNOME 2 shell to Unity -- in which case my commentary here is moot).

As for your choice of DE, why not do a minimal install and choose which environments you want?  GNOME 2 shell will be gone, but I think XFCE and its shell at this point are fine and worthwhile stand-ins.

### Post by Grams79 on 2011-08-25
I'm going to keep this short and sweet.

:popcorn:
I've been with Ubuntu since 4.10 Warty Warthog and I stopped having major issues by 8.10 Intrepid Ibex.

Now 11.04 brings back the dreaded "oh boy this stinks" thought to mind.

Unity stinks and it's not user friendly at all, even for new computer uses.
We can remove it any which way and still get crap loads of trouble.

Unity comes with a bulk load of extras that are not needed.

Now with 11.04 I can only run my computer at top performance and efficiency if I use the Ubuntu (fail safe) mode.
All other modes, including (no effects) only lockup my system at times.

The system load average for example goes off the charts to 5+ and that is not right.
I re-installed 10.10, 10.04 and even 9.10 to test.
And guess what? 
Everything runs great unless I install 11.04.

And for all my fellow computer gurus who may ask or think... no it is not my fault that this is happening.
I have checked the whole Internet and found nothing but posts complaining about 11.04.
I see nothing but complaints about the same lockups, freezes and crashes on 11.04.
There are little to no direct fixes for the whole problem... just minor workarounds such as disabling "Sync to Vblank" which does not help.

So to everyone in the community... 11.04 is crap and I think it is Unity's fault and all it's extra heavy weight saturated junk it requires.

### Post by jgestiot on 2011-08-27
Before going fishing with 11.04, the Ubuntu community had a fantastic and stable operating system. It was the kind of OS wrap that IT managers like myself recommends. We are in the process of pulling all the windows stuff at work and replacing it with Ubuntu.

I have over 35 years experience in IT. I saw the rise and beginning of the fall of windows and Ubuntu is starting to make the same mistake of developing WOW features to seduce bunnies and forgetting the established and rather large community of users out there that form the backbone to Ubuntu's success. Windows made the same mistake of trying to seduce beginners with resource-intensive interface rubbish. 

The new global menu is dodgy as hell. In fact, it is total rubbish. It is designed for messy people who are disorganised. They are the ones who always need to type the name of their applications into a search engine because they cannot follow a logical path like Apps -> Internet -> Skype.  

The Windows OS started to go wrong at windows 95 when they started to go all multimedia gun-ho. The last proper upgrade was the implementation of networking in their version 3.1.1 ... The rest has been rubbish aimed at boosting their marketing prospects and maintaining their market dominance by enslaving beginner users. 

Anyway, back to Ubuntu. The design of the global menu is **** and it does not work with some application. The selection of a clumsy interface should be optional. It should not be imposed on people especially when it does not work. There is a reason why traditional menus are still used everywhere. That's because they work. I don't know what stuff the makers of Ubuntu smoke but I certainly don't want any.

There are people out there you may think they are the new Steve Jobs, king of avant guarde design. They are forgetting something. No matter what novelty you come up with, it needs to work.

I have remove the upgrade 11.04 from all my computers and reverted to what works. Ubuntu is still a great environment for working but not that 11.04 nutty warhog.

Cheers

JG

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2011-08-27
The global menu is not "imposed" on you. It's a default setting in the system, which can be removed to bring back the old menus. It's not even difficult to do.

I will just point out that you're being a bit unfair to slag off the people who use the search engine rather than the mouse to find their programs. Typing "skype" and hitting Enter is a lot, lot quicker than moving your mouse around and having to read all the categories of programs, then all the names of the programs in that category.

The selection of the Unity interface IS optional. Change it at the login screen to regular Gnome 2. Easy. Takes all of ten seconds.

So really, you've not got much argument from me; you want the options to be available, and so do I. It's just that the options ARE available.

### Post by Velvet_Man on 2011-08-27
After all of the Unity bashing in this thread and elsewhere online, I was very hesitant to upgrade from 10.10. But curiosity eventually got the better of me, and I installed 11.04 two weeks ago.

I have to say, I'm pleasantly surprised with Natty and even Unity. I've been using Ubuntu since 8.04, installing every version along the way, and 11.04 was most painless install I've experienced yet. Everything that worked before still works, and in many cases, without any of the tweaking I had to do after installing 10.10. There were even some great unexpected pluses. It always drove me nuts how long Ubuntu took to re-establish a wifi connection after resuming from suspend. This could take up to 3 or 4 minutes in 10.10. In 11.04, it's nearly instant. This alone even won my wife over to Ubuntu. She used to reboot into Windows 7 every chance she got because she couldn't standing waiting forever for the wifi to reconnect. Now she's been using Ubuntu for two weeks straight with no complaints, and no thoughts of booting back into Windows.

Unity seemed a bit clunky at first, but a quick Google search for "Unity tweaks" turned up some simple improvements. After about half an hour, I had everything working more to my liking. I would still prefer to move the launcher to the bottom of the screen, but I've actually gotten quite used to it being on the left. Also, with all of the apps I use regularly pinned to the launcher, and the ones I don't use removed, I rarely ever have to use the awkward (and obviously incomplete) Applications lens. 

And as a laptop user, I absolutely LOVE the global menu. That, combined with the title bar and controls moving into the top panel on maximized windows, has really freed up a lot of screen space on my small 15-inch screen. I should mention that the global menus and left-hand control buttons were not much of a switch for me since I have to use Macs all day at work (it's one of the hazards of working in a creative field, they all seem to think any type of "creative" work simply can't be done without a Mac).

I also haven't experienced any of the so-call stability issues I keep hearing about. Despite a pretty heavy workload, I've yet to experience any type of crash of freeze. The only form of "instability" I've experienced at all has been when minimizing a maximized window and then restoring it. It occasionally restore with a gap of space between the top of the window and the top panel. This isn't really a problem, though, because it can be fixed by simply clicking the maximize button again.

There are some customizing options I hope to see in 11.10, but overall I'm quite happy with 11.04 and Unity. It's not only usable but extremely promising. I really don't get what all the bashing is about. I tend to think it has more to do with people resisting change than anything else.

---

### Post by ErkDemon on 2011-08-27
> **Roasted said:**
> If somebody tries out the interface for a decent  amount of time and truly doesn't like it, my hat goes off to you for  being mature and giving it a real shot. ...
To those who try it for 10 minutes and post on the forums about how bad  it is, I truly want to revoke your privileges to ever come within 100  yards of a computer. Ever. Again. You are absolutely unworthy of it if  you exhibited these actions.


Actually, it might not have taken some people much more than ten minutes  to realise that they didn't want to use this OS version. Some aspects  of it were pretty obviously dodgy. 
Application launchers are simple, spontaneous things ... at least  they're supposed to be. If they're not, they're probably not designed  right. People have been writing these things for twenty five years, and  they're not rocket science. So if someome presents you with an app  launcher and you have to be prepared to invest the time to "learn" it,  then to people who want an OS to basically run programs, and don't  expect an OS to be a hobby in itself ... then that's a fail.

I've been using Ubuntu for the last few years, and liked each version  better then the next, until 11.04. I'm not pro or anti any of the  underlying tools or technologies as long as the end-results work. And  the 11.04 release didn't work properly.

You right-clicked on the Ubuntu icon to see the version number and  release number ... and it didn't work. After a couple of hours, the  mouse pointer would get a rogue horizontal offset because of some window  metrics bug caused by the new vertical scrollbar code. The UI for  switching off Untity wasn't accessible if you'd selected auto-login when  you installed, because it was on the logon panel. 

I tried both 11.04 betas, and it was obvious that the project wasn't  going to yield a release-quality result in the available timeframe.  People were warning them about this months ahead of time, and were being  told not to worry, that there as plenty of time. There wasn't. 
The betas had unresolved issues that should have already been sorted out  before the beta stage, which meant that the beta testers were fixating  on those issues (like the correct behaviour of the global menu) instead  of focusing on bug-fixing. Wasting betatesters' time on things that  should have already been sorted out at the design stage when the release  date was looming was, I thought, not a great way to treat a valuable  human resource. 

I'm back to using 10.10 (oh, and the 10.10 installer refused to let me  remove 11.04, or even format the disk, so I had to resort to using a  Windows install disk to kill 11.04 ... irony).

My experience as a first-time betatester was that people simply refused  to accept that there were problems, which made me wonder why they were  asking for feedback if they were dismissing negative opinions as  belonging to people who were stupid, and who didn't deserve to use  Ubuntu. At times it all felt a bit like "You're with us or you're  against us".

Unity on 11.04 was, simply, a substandard release. 
Users were lethally critical about it because over the previous  releases, Ubuntu had successfully climbed out of the Linux ghetto and  become a proper, professional-quality "professional" home/pro operating  system on a par with Windows and OSX. You could recommend it to friends  without reservation or qualification, knowing that they wouldn't have to  go to you for product support. 
With 11.04, Ubuntu dropped back to being a hobbyist-standard OS with  flakey mouse-handling, things that didn't work, a supposedly  touchscreen-optimised interface that didn't seem to have been developed  or tested on an actual touchscreen. The most common reaction to problems  seemed to be "Sure it doesn't work properly *now*, but it's only a  prototype, the next versions will be much better", or, "If you don't  like it, you can go away and use something else", or "try if for a few  weeks or months, and it'll grow on you".

Now, to me, if you have something that doesn't quite work properly, you  either don't release it, or you put it out as a beta, or as an optional  addon. You don't make it the default interface for your flagship  mainstream desktop OS release, and then get surprised when people  splutter that its rubbish. That reaction was inevitable, and Canonical  made a calculated decision to risk getting that reaction because they  wanted to accelerate development, and they felt that if Unity wasn't the  default option, fewer people would install it and use it. They could  have avoided much of the criticism by making Unity the second-choice  option, but they deliberately cranked up the stress levels by letting  developers know that this was a live default release, to force the rate  of progress.

Whether that was a good decision, I guess we'll find out before too  long. But it's not the end-users' faults if they're annoyed. Some of  them had their time wasted by being encouraged to download something  that was supposed to be a finished operating system, only to find out  that they were effectively being used as betatesters.

As for me, I might give 11.10 a try, but I'm assuming that I'll be  leaving 11.x alone from now on, and probably having another look when  12.x comes long, to see if they've actually gotten the new design sorted  yet. 
10.10 is actually pretty good. Hell, my desktop machine's still running  XP, so while Ubuntu's six-month release schedule is reassuring in that  it gives regular development milestones, I don't feel an overwhelming  need to run the very *very* latest release, unless there's  something on that release that'd be useful to me (which was the case  with earlier releases, but not with 11.04).

---

### Post by jtarin on 2011-08-27
> **ErkDemon said:**
> Actually, it might not have taken some people much more than ten minutes  to realise that they didn't want to use this OS version. Some aspects  of it were pretty obviously dodgy. 
Application launchers are simple, spontaneous things ... at least  they're supposed to be. If they're not, they're probably not designed  right. People have been writing these things for twenty five years, and  they're not rocket science. So if someome presents you with an app  launcher and you have to be prepared to invest the time to "learn" it,  then to people who want an OS to basically run programs, and don't  expect an OS to be a hobby in itself ... then that's a fail.

+1 for a well thought out post.:P

### Post by jwbrase on 2011-08-29
I voted for "I have reverted", but in truth I've not yet upgraded from Lucid (though I have tried out Unity on a USB stick with Natty). However, from what I've seen so far, when I *do* eventually start using a version that defaults to Unity, I will most likely make reverting to GNOME 2 the first action I take after installing.

> **bbrg548 said:**
> My first reaction?

Blech!  :-&

It looks like they took the worst part of MacOS/OSX (the global menu), and the worst part of Windoze 7 (the launcher thingy), and then made them even *worse*.

The global menu: Why separate the menus from the window they're supposed to control? How does that make any kind of sense whatsoever?

I don't like it myself, but the logic is that the screen edges are among the fastest places on the screen for mouse access, since the mouse stops when it hits the edge of the screen, so you don't have to aim as much to hit a menu on a screen edge as a menu in a window (where you can overshoot).

> And at least on Macs, the menu is visible all the time! What's the point of hiding it so that you have to move the mouse to the top bar to see if a window even *has* a menu?

And of course, in doing this, they obliterate any advantage to be had speed wise from separating menus from windows and putting them on the top edge of the screen, thus making that particular feature useless.

The likely explanation for why they have done these two things (the global menu and *hiding* the global menu), is because Apple is considered to be the company that defines what is trendy and "modern" in GUI design (which is itself a travesty), and they have thus copycatted Apple (thus the global menu) without understanding the reasoning behind why Apple has designed its interface the way it has (thus *hiding* the global menu). 

However, I'm not very concerned by this as I've heard the global menu can be configured away.

> 
The launcher/dock/whatever: The little icons tell you nothing about what's open in a program, the little triangle indicators are hard to see, you can't move it to any other part of the screen, and clicking on an open program's icon won't minimize it.

Quite agreed. Plus, the dock is less useful for window switching as the dock icons are smaller targets than taskbar buttons. 

> 
The dock at least has some promise,

I disagree here: IMHO, the very concept of a dock is inherently broken as it tries to combine the functions of a taskbar and a set of quicklaunch icons (which are two different things with quite different optimal implementations) and nerfs the functionality of both in attempting to mash them together.

> Perhaps, since Gnome 2 was going away anyway, the devs would have been better off investing their time in creating a new DE that incorporated the best aspects of Gnome 2 (i.e., easily customizable panels) and integrating them more smoothly with Compiz and Metacity, rather than... this drek.

Indeed. Having seen what was being planned for GNOME 3, I was quite heartened when I heard that Canonical was refusing to go along with it. Then I found out that their idea was only superficially different from GNOME 3 (rather than being GNOME 2++). Talk about snatching defeat from the jaws of victory.

> **ninjaaron said:**
> My first reaction was that it was awesome.
My second reaction was that there was regression in mouse-accessibility.
My third reaction was that there is improvement in keyboard accessibility.
Keyboard accessibility is my main concern.

Hence: I love unity.

I suppose it could be said that there is a regression in mouse-accessibility.

It could also be said that temperatures in Antarctica are somewhat cool.

Mouse accessibility is my main concern.

Hence...

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-08-30
> **VinDSL said:**
> Why ppl get so emotional about an interface is a mystery to me...

Your car is now obsolete and you are being given a FREE (and unasked for) upgrade to the new control system.  The steering wheel has been replaced by two push buttons and the pedals have been changed so that the accelerator is on the left. In addition to this all the most often used controls are now on a new panel - the indicators are right next to the audible warning device (horn).  All the least used features like the hazard lights and the rear window de-mister can only be accessed by typing their name on a keyboard.

You start using this new interface and find that you can't access the controls for different things at the same time.  You have to select whether you are operating the radio, the GPS, the indicators, or looking at the speedo.

What do you do:

1) Complain loudly that the new interface doesn't work properly, and the designers have just thrown away 100 years of automotive design progress

2) Write angry emails to the car company telling them they've got it wrong

3) File bug reports against the control designers who obviously don't actually drive the thing themselves.

4) Demand that your old car is returned immediately

5) failing (4) Go out and by a sweet little 90's sports job and go back to enjoying your driving

6) All of the above

It's an emotional issue.  In a rather thoughtful post recently someone said that Unity was great if the DE was a hobby, but not much good if you just wanted it to work intuitively.

I'm all for intuitive.

Chris

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-08-31
> **uRock said:**
> I work intuitively just fine with Unity.

That's a definition of the word "intuitive" that I haven't previously come across.  

You might be _productive_ with Unity, after learning most of the keyboard shortcuts.  That alone negates the "intuitive" aspects of the interface since keyboard shortcuts are anything but intuitive, requiring effort and repetition to learn.

You might _like_ Unity, which is fine, but personally I've always found the application dock a limiting and stultifying paradigm that is best left as the dead end that it is.  Yes, this is personal opinion.

You might be able to recommend Unity, but I've only seen seen complete mystification and puzzlement from anyone who wants more than just an application launcher to do just one thing at a time. "How do I drag this data file into that minimised application"? ...  "Well, you see there's these keyboard shortcuts..."

In the time span in which Unity was developed it's a colossal achievement, but intuitive it is not.

Yes, Desktop environments will continue to evolve and change, but the successful ones will not do so at the expense of utility.  Unity has a long way to go, but I hope it will get there.

Chris

---

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-08-31
> **VinDSL said:**
> Lubuntu is the most promising, IMO.  You might want to try it.


I have tried it.  Also Fedora, Xubuntu, Kubuntu, and Slackware 13.37.  Slackware with Xubuntu was interesting, but Lubuntu was exciting from the point of view that it was an easy-install 'buntu experience with a very small footprint.

Kde I can't get running on anything here (at a decent resolution) since I'm using a KVM switch that doesn't pass EDID information to the video card.  The settings in xorg.conf that I've been using for years to get proper resolutions on my 21" monitor result in X crashing with a null pointer error.  I'm not bothered, since in low resolutions at least Kde has failed to win me over.

But I'm torn.  I like Ubuntu as a concept.  I like the Ubuntu community. I like using the mainstream 'buntu flavour because that's where 90%+ of the support is. Moving to another distro or even another 'buntu will require me to distance myself from a lot of what I like about Ubuntu.

I note that I never would have tried all those different things if it wasn't for Unity.  It's been an adventure, and at times a challenge.

We'll see what the next LTS brings us.

Chris.

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-08-31
>  But why after all this time is Synaptic being removed 

So far it's still installed by default in 11.10 - but the .iso is still too big to fit on a CD, so if it's removed, that's why.  And you know, it is possible to install other software not included in the default install.  If you want synaptic, chances are you know how to install it.  It's not like it's being banned from the repositories.

>  But why are there no launchers on the desktop 

Personally, I've never felt the need to try this one out.  Is it impossible?  I'm not sure why you would need launchers on the desktop, where they'll be covered by a window N% of the time, when you could have the same launcher 5mm away on the Unity launcher.

>  But why cant we find the things we need quickly 

Why can't you?  On my machine I can tap the super key and then start typing the name of the program/file I want, and there it is.  On those rare occasions that I don't know the name of what I'm looking for, the browsing might be a bit slower than before but it happens seldom enough to not really be an issue.

>  But why are we being told there is no other option than Global Menus 

Who on Earth is telling you this?  OK, they're installed by default, but people have been explaining how to remove them since 11.04 came out, and probably were explaining this while it was still in testing.  Just remove the indicator-appmenu package and the global menus are gone - like magic.

I get the impression that the argument here is that the default install should be how *I* want it - and of course everyone in the world will have the same tastes as me - why should I have to spend 5 minutes customising?

### Post by cariboo on 2011-08-31
Just a bit of a correction to what dsfitzpat wrote,

From the Software Center Wiki:

> Early versions of Ubuntu shipped many graphical utilities for installing and removing software: Add/Remove Applications, Synaptic Package Manager, Update Manager, Software Sources, apturl, GDebi, and Computer Janitor. This redundancy increased the amount of interface people had to learn, wasted space on the Ubuntu CD, fragmented development effort, and made people more likely to think that unsanctioned software installation methods were safe. Ubuntu Software Center replaces Add/Remove Applications, Synaptic, apturl, and GDebi, and acts as the main entry point to Software Sources.

So synaptic wasn't removed because of a lack of space on the CD, rather because it isn't needed any more.

I just did a fresh Oneiric iso testing install this morning, the first things I installed were aptitude, synaptic and gdebi. :)

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-08-31
I should also point out that although there is no longer a simple GUI to create desktop launchers, it remains possible to create them by hand.
The attached screenshot shows a launcher for Firefox on the Unity desktop of Ubutnu 11.10.  I'm strictly a Linux end-user - aside from dabbling I've only ever used Ubuntu, I've forgotten any programming I ever learned, and I have no expertise in scripting.  That said, it took about 5 minutes on Google to figure this one out, so although it's not point-and-click, I wouldn't consider this an experts-only deal.

FYI: if you want the how-to, here goes:
1. Create an empty file in your desktop folder, and open it with your favourite text editor.
2. Add the following contents (I'll use Firefox as the example; you can replace Firefox with the program of your choice - just find the path to the program in /usr/bin and find the icon, which is usually in /usr/share/pixmaps):

[Desktop Entry]
Name=Firefox
Exec=/usr/bin/firefox
Icon=/usr/share/pixmaps/firefox.png
Terminal=false
Type=Application
StartupNotify=false

3. Rename the file as firefox.desktop
4. Right-click on the file to access the Properties, and check of the box to make it executable.

Voila.

### Post by BuddyThirteen on 2011-09-01
I just right-clicked my desktop and the second option is "Create launcher..." and it brings up what appears to be an extremely simple little window with some input fields to create... well, a launcher.

Are you guys saying that's not supposed to be there anymore? I promise I haven't changed any settings to add that menu option (wouldn't even know how, or if that was possible).

Personally, I don't like a lot of desktop launchers. Been on Ubuntu long enough that when I must use Windows, I sorta cringe at all those messy icons on the desktop. Seems so disorganized.

I guess I'm not what you'd call a "power user", but Natty/Unity seems to be working for me rather well. And I was *super* skeptical. I hated docks before. But I've gotten used to it and I do find it aesthetically pleasing. It's got some problems that I, as a low-level user, have noticed. Like the global menus. Last I remember (okay, it's been years) Mac doesn't hide their dumb menus until you mouseover them. And Windows has them stuck right onto the window. So a new user is to find the menu how? A thought just occurred to me: If they're at all familiar with keyboard shortcuts, they might try hitting Alt. I tried it. What the hell? Who thought that behavior up? Alt is only handy if you already know the command. Because it doesn't open a menu, it just flashes the (dumb) global menu for as long as you've got the key held down. I don't know why we've gotta hide random things and then give no clues or cues as to where they are. That's bad design.

But anyway, what was I making this post for? Oh, right. Launcher. Mine works fine. Sorry I got sidetracked.

[edit]: Oh, is it gone for 11.10? Well, nevermind me, then. Tootles!

### Post by BuddyThirteen on 2011-09-04
As a lefty, I will say that handedness is a silly argument to use on a computer. Since when does what hand you use affect which side of the screen your cursor goes to? The whole point is that the mouse can cover the entire screen from wherever you happen to be using it. Right hand, left hand, you can reach the whole screen. And unlike paper, your hand doesn't cover half the screen when forced to use the "wrong" half of the screen. The launcher is not the equivalent of the wire spiral on a spiral-bound notebook, it doesn't interfere with the way your left hand operates.

And why would you have the mouse *in* the left hand anyway? Lefty or righty, the mouse belongs in the right hand, that's just how it works.

### Post by hzFVOW00pw on 2011-09-04
To get to the Screensaver I had to:

Click: Dash home
Click: More Applications
Click: See 105 more results
Scroll and search
Click: Screensaver

I automatically go to all programs because then I know everything will be there and it saves me from browsing through the categories.

Anyway, that's a lot of steps to get to a program. It seems to me that creating a Terminal shortcut on the desktop and typing xscreensaver is less of a hassle.

I now discovered I can drag 'n drop the menu entries to the desktop or launcher. So now my desktop is filling up quickly with icons because accessing them from the desktop is more convenient for me. So my desktop is in essence becoming my menu and will make Dash obsolete.

The main point of a menu system is that it should organize your programs and make them easily accessible. I think the Dash thing fails on that.

### Post by BuddyThirteen on 2011-09-05
I agree, the whole keyboard thing is utter <snip>. The whole point of a graphical interface is that we don't have to type esoteric commands to do simple tasks, and this seems like a huge step backwards.

Unlike your average windows user, I actually do use the keyboard to quicken my tasks. Like in Firefox, right-click then press "V" to save an image. Lots faster than reading each menu entry to find what I'm looking for. But I certainly don't want to use the keyboard exclusively. I might as well just disconnect the mouse, if Unity has its way.

And I'm really annoyed by these apologists who say "I just tap the super key and type bla blah" Well good for you. The point is that most people prefer using the mouse. And this assumes you know what you're looking for. Sometimes I just like looking at what I have installed and opening up something I haven't played with in a while.

And the lack of one-click access to things that should be simple is quite annoying. Especially workspace switching. Takes one click to open the switcher, then a double click or a right click to go to the workspace you want. But there's always the keyboard. Which is great when my hand is resting comfortably on the mouse, right? It's inconvenient to keep having to switch my right hand from mouse to keyboard and back.

Overall, I like Unity, but it does have serious design flaws that some people refuse to acknowledge (because anyone who doesn't want to do it the default way is inferior to them). It was not designed by anyone who actually intends to use it.

---

### Post by uRock on 2011-09-08
Off topic material removed. Please keep the thread on topic.

---

### Post by cariboo on 2011-09-08
> **azurehi said:**
> uRock's comment made no sense to me until cariboo907 explained to Some degree.  I am really confused now:  what do the Gnome 3 devs have to do with Unity?  I thought Unity was being developed independently.  In Natty, which I installed and tried, I could the "scr" and be taken to the screensaver settings, as I recall.

Will Oneric final have things like this corrected?

To explain a bit more, you seem to be confusing gnome-shell with gnome 3. Gnome 3 is the underlying libraries that the desktop needs to function, Gnome-shell is a desktop environment, like Unity. 

Ubuntu has always been a gnome based distribution, and with the release of gnome 3, the devs are busy converting Oneiric to run atop it.

Opening the dash and typing **scr** will give you 3 choices, none of which say screensaver, but the screen blanking and locking options are available through the screen icon.

See the screenshot.

### Post by spamhog on 2011-09-10
I am now convinced that Unity is the most perfect and wonderfully customizable user interface that was ever conceived on this planet.

I think developers should take advantage of it and poll the community as to the interface the community prefers, including KDE, Gnome 2/3, LXDE, XCFE, IceWM, and maybe even the default Unity.

It would also be a good idea to consider reaching out to the users who moved to other distros because of the switch to Unity. There is an excellent historical precedent: the Swedish Emigration Commission:
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Swedish_Emigration_Commission_1907–1913](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Swedish_Emigration_Commission_1907–1913)

This commission sent emissaries to the US to ask SWEDES WHO HAD ALREADY LEFT THE COUNTRY FOR GOOD what their preferences and reasons for leaving were.

They realized that asking those who were still in the country despite suboptimal conditions would have yielded a highly skewed and unscientific sample in favor of the status quo, and would have produced useless feedback data.

### Post by BuddyThirteen on 2011-09-10
> **uRock said:**
> You don't have to type. You can do everything with the mouse. I am not sure where people are getting the idea that they have to type.
And it takes twice as many clicks as it used to. Tell me how to switch workspaces with a single click. Until recently, I had to click the switcher, then double-click the workspace I wanted. That's three clicks. Used to be one. Through fiddling, I found that right-clicking once brings up the workspace, too. So that's down to two clicks. Still more than it used to be. And this is an "improvement"? And that's just one example. I also used to be able to open literally any application with a single click on the menu, and then move the mouse over the category and app I wanted, click to open. Two clicks total. Now I have to click the dash, then into applications (or just click straight on the app lens on the launcher -- what's the dash for again? It does nothing) and either expand the "installed" group or select a category on the upper-right, and try to find what I want. I could again reduce the number of clicks by right-clicking the app lens and selecting the category directly.

But in neither of those two examples is the right-click functionality expected or explained in any way. One must trial-and-error their way through Unity. Great design.

[edit]:
> **VinDSL said:**
> For instance, someone is trying to steal your  passwords and credit card numbers, via the keyboard, e.g. capturing your  keystrokes.  :wink:
I use KeePass, which also makes it harder for keyloggers to get my passwords. Credit card numbers, on the other hand... Well, there's not many places left where I have to type it manually. But yeah, that could be a problem.

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-09-10
I'll admit that this required me to go into CCSM and change a setting, but to change workspaces on my desktop I just put the cursor over a blank patch of desktop and use the mousewheel.  I know that's not so much of a click though...
Funny, one of the first things I did with Gnome 2 was to remove the workspace switcher because there were 3 or 4 easier ways to change workspaces that didn't involve squinting at tiny little boxes in the corner and trying to decide which one had the window I wanted.  But if that's your one 'I can't live without it' feature, why not just run a Gnome panel at the bottom (you can do this even with Gnome 3 by the way) and stick a workplace switcher in it?
I do admit though that it would be interesting to see the mythical desktop environment that's exactly what every person in the world wants without any need for customisation whatsoever.

### Post by jwbrase on 2011-09-10
> **uRock said:**
> You don't have to type. You can do everything with the mouse. I am not sure where people are getting the idea that they have to type.

You *can* use the mouse with Unity, it's just that Unity is a *huge* regression as far as mouse-only functionality compared to GNOME 2.

Frankly, though, my feelings are mixed on the dash. What really gets to me is the dock, which is a thoroughly broken concept to begin with, its unremovability, and the complete lack of ability to place *proper* launchers on the top panel. If I could place old-style launchers on the top panel, I could just run a gnome-panel instance to provide myself with a proper task bar, put launchers for frequently used programs across the top panel, and relegate the dock to an annoying but ignorable blemish on my desktop. (Even as is, there are probably ways that I could use gnome-panel to make the Unity desktop acceptable, but they'd still be a significant regression compared to GNOME 2, though only one on the order of switching to Windows, rather than one on the order of switching to OS X).

### Post by BuddyThirteen on 2011-09-10
I actually wouldn't mind so much if the switcher on the launcher was useful. I don't always need expo to see things. I'd love to be able to click on a region of the icon and have it take me to the corresponding workspace. There's no squinting involved, because I always know which apps I like on which workspace. It's not so much of a problem with running apps, because I just click the icon and it takes me to the correct workspace. But when I need to open new apps and want a blank workspace, it becomes more of a hassle than it should be.

By no means is this a dealbreaker for me, though. I've gotten used to it.

The problem is how esoteric this stuff is. There's no manual for Ubuntu, and at least Gnome was pretty intuitive. Unity intentionally hides things, which makes it have a very steep learning curve for people unfamiliar with it. It has *removed* features in favor of eyecandy.

I do look forward to the new dash in 11.10 -- It actually looks like it will have a purpose other than being a portal to stuff you could have accessed in easier ways.

Mine looks similar to that, I love the curve, but mine's only 6. And my curve is much more subtle. I don't hate expo, I love it. No problem.  Open Expo and start dragging n' dropping windows on other desktops.  LoL!

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-09-10
> **NightwishFan said:**
> There a few places such as Zareason, System76, and I think Dell. There was one other one but I do not remember it.

Asus were shipping some of their netbooks with Ubuntu.  I don't know if that is still the case - probably.

> 
Keyboard shortcuts are never counter productive. A mouse is what can be counter productive, just more natural and visual. Keyboard shortcuts are much faster than searching and navigating.


Keyboard shortcuts are not intuitive, therefore counter productive _for new users_.  Obviously they increase productivity for the more experienced.  If you take a new user working in isolation they are not going to know the shortcuts, may not know they exist, and probably won't know where to start looking.  Personally I like keyboard shortcuts, but that is only after the learning curve they necesitate. To put them forward as a primary feature that _must_ be learned to be productive seems to indicate that the DE is inefficient without them.

I'm speaking philosophically here, not trying to be argumentative.

> 
I/O buffer errors mean the CD download or burn was probably corrupted. There could be other reasons for the error but this is the main one.

Agreed.  I learned the hard way that it's necessary to check the MD5sum of a dowloaded ISO before committing it to a Bootaable USB or CD.

Chris

### Post by garvinrick4 on 2011-09-18
If you ever in any GUI cannot find an application: 
```
nautilus /usr/share/applications
```Will give you every application, If for want it in launcher open app and right click
on it in launcher and tell it to stay as a favorite in your launcher.

Believe you also still have Main Menu like you are used to in gnome-classic it
is called alacarte , always has been shows up as Main Menu (where you can add or
take away apps without removing from file system.) Might be installed at inception not sure now, do not use as much anymore.
```
sudo apt-get install alacarte
```

### Post by anewguy on 2011-09-19
Jeez, defaulting to Unity without the option of Classic is going to generate a lot of posts when it happens.  Why?  Many (including me) have ATI video cards for which the opendriver doesn't seem to work the best, plus I don't get the 3d that Unity seems to expect by default.  The result is having to use the propriety driver and even with it a lot of the eye candy doesn't work.

I myself am not against change - that's not a problem.  But if that change doesn't also bring an easy way (read no aborts on install or boot) to run a non-unity environment it could mean problems.  Perhaps they have improved Unity not to be quite so finicky as it seems to be now.

Time will tell.  I really don't feel like buying a new video card because my OS forces me to - that reminds me too much of other OS's I'd rather forget.

Dave ;)

The reason it's not going to be included is because GNOME 2 is out, GNOME 3 is in. That's what happens with development, old things get replaced. If you want to know why GNOME 2 is being replaced, talk to them. In my opinion, including Classic mode would be like including Amarok 1.4 with new releases of Kubuntu. It just doesn't make sense. For those interested, check out the GNOME 2 fork [MATE](http://matsusoft.com.ar/redmine/projects/mate). Thread about it [here](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1801892).

### Post by bbrg548 on 2011-09-20
Well, some irritating glitches and a bad update to 10.10 prompted me to do a clean install, so I decided to try 11.04, since I had the CD ready. I'm going to go ahead and give it a prolonged trial run, and may decide to go to 11.10 later (either the beta in another week or two, or once it's officially released), since it looks like they've made a few changes to Unity for 11.10. I'm still skeptical, but it's not *quite* as bad as I had feared, either.

> **jwbrase said:**
> I don't like it myself, but the logic is that the screen edges are among the fastest places on the screen for mouse access, since the mouse stops when it hits the edge of the screen, so you don't have to aim as much to hit a menu on a screen edge as a menu in a window (where you can overshoot).

I can see that there might be a slight speed increase with that, but not that it would be enough to counter traveling from a non-maximized window to the top of the screen, and "targeting" a traditional menu certainly wouldn't take any longer than clicking a link on a webpage - something nearly anyone who uses a computer does on a regular basis, and with little difficulty.

> **jwbrase said:**
> And of course, in doing this, they obliterate any advantage to be had speed wise from separating menus from windows and putting them on the top edge of the screen, thus making that particular feature useless.

The confusion caused by the "which window does this menu go with" issue (exacerbated by Unity's per-window implementation rather than Apple's per-application implementation) I think makes it even worse.

> **jwbrase said:**
> However, I'm not very concerned by this as I've heard the global menu can be configured away.

That's the very first thing I did after getting the graphics drivers settled, so I can confirm that it can be done, and fairly easily. :)

> **jwbrase said:**
> I disagree here: IMHO, the very concept of a dock is inherently broken as it tries to combine the functions of a taskbar and a set of quicklaunch icons (which are two different things with quite different optimal implementations) and nerfs the functionality of both in attempting to mash them together.

I don't *quite* agree that the *concept* is broken, but it's usefulness depends on a) your actual usage needs, and b) proper implementation. For instance, I found Cairo-dock to be quite useful, and it met my needs rather nicely - but then it's a *well-designed* and highly configurable dock.

Unity's dock (or are they calling that the launcher now? I'm a little confused), on the other hand, truly *did* nerf the functionality. My biggest two irritations right now are the difficulty in using the mouse for switching between multiple instances or windows of the same application (supposedly a double-click does it, but I haven't been able to get it to work consistently), and the inability to use it to open a second instance of an application. Cairo-dock has simple solutions (from the user's perspective) to both those issues - an overlay or sub-dock showing the additional windows pops up when you click on a program with more than one window open, and a left-click menu on a program's launcher gives an option to open a new window if it's already open. It's simple, it's expected (given that they are common in other dock systems), and it's fairly intuitive.

> **jwbrase said:**
> I suppose it could be said that there is a regression in mouse-accessibility.

It could also be said that temperatures in Antarctica are somewhat cool.

Mouse accessibility is my main concern.

Hence...

There is definitely a regression in mouse-accessibility. I want both mouse-accessibility and keyboard accessibility, because what works best for me depends on what I'm doing at the time. If I'm primarily web surfing, then I want good mouse-accessibility, because most web browsing is more easily done with the mouse. Good mouse-accessibility means I don't have to switch to the keyboard unless I actually have to type something, which makes things faster and more convenient. If I'm doing something that requires a lot of typing - like balancing my checkbook in KMyMoney, or paying bills - I want good keyboard accessibility, because I also have to switch between programs frequently (from KMyMoney to my bank's website in Firefox to an OpenOffice/LibreOffice spreadsheet and back), because, again, switching from the keyboard to the mouse and back slows down my workflow and is inconvenient.

I also miss the desktop cube, but at least I got the wall configured to work almost the same... just without the eye candy or being able to grab screen edges with the mouse to change workspaces (again, reduced mouse accessibility).

---

I'd suggest you actually watch the average user while they use their computers, without butting in and telling them a better way to do things, you'll see that for the most part if they use Unity with a mouse only, they won't be missing anything.

If you had read a little more carefully, you would have understood that surveying how average users used their computers was exactly what I did. May I suggest you to do the same and then to develop why average users would prefer keyboard shortcuts to mouse usage according to your survey?  B.T.W., I have just asked my girlfriend whether she uses keyboard shortcuts.  She does not.

I do not understand why you wrote that sentence: "...without butting in and telling them a better way to do things...". First it is a bit aggressive while I was not.  Second, why do you assume that I told them what to do? I just asked them how they worked, nothing more. May I suggest you to stick to the facts?

Finally, about the point which was discussed, it was not claimed in this small part of the thread that it is not possible to do certain things with the mouse in Unity.  It is just a question of efficiency. Many users agree, including in this thread, that the dash is a great tool to quickly access programs when you know their names. However, when you just want to browse the menus without knowing exactly what you are looking for, it is easier and faster with a classical menu organization than with the dash.

So I come back to the main question of my post that you have not address at all. Why favoring keyboard shortcuts in Unity detrimental to mouse usage (easiness and efficiency) when the target public is the average user?

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-10-09
What cariboo said was that using the mouse in Unity is no less efficient than most users' use of the mouse in Windows. Whether or not Unity is less efficient with the mouse than the default Gnome 2 interface or whether or not Unity is more efficiently managed from the keyboard doesn't come into that.

### Post by scratman on 2011-10-09
Shouldn't the question be "Why is there so much bias towards keyboard navigation over mouse navigation?"  The fact is that some people use primarily mouse, some primarily keyboard, some a bit of both. Shouldn't the aim of "Linux for human beings" to be flexible enough to be effortless for any users preference?

Telling people who prefer not to use the keyboard that the Dash is better for them will not necessarily get them to enjoy using the dash.  If they prefer a mouse oriented approach to desktop management and navigation, why spend so much development time and skill developing a DE that alienated so many users?  The answer is simple, make it better for both kinds of users.

### Post by seeker5528 on 2011-10-13
> **Garthhh said:**
> most people don't ever install an OS, so the process needs to be as simple as possible, even getting the file burned onto a disc or a USB is not all that easy on a windows machine, 
it's going to have to be google easy if there is any hope of 200mill

On older Windows burining to disk will depend on what burning software you have install, if it took over the association for .iso files, and if some archiving software to deal with .zip, .rar, etc... hijacked the association.

On Windows 7 using the built in option, you just right click the .iso and choose 'Burn image to CD/DVD' or something along those lines, assuming some other installed software didn't take over the association. Even if some other software did take over the extension you could always to the 'Open with' thing and choose 'Windows Disc Image Burner'.

For the common case, installation is easy enough. That's not to say it couldn't or shouldn't be improved, but it's more of a refinement process now rather than something that needs a big overhaul.

Later, Seeker

### Post by -RAX- on 2011-10-14
Hi all,

I am writing this post because I think that a feedback on this subject can be useful.

I am the CTO of a small IT company in Italy and for years we have used and promoted Ubuntu Desktop as clean and operative solution for software development.

However...

Since the introduction of UNITY things have changed, and Ubuntu is no longer "clean and operative". This new Unity interface is a big waste of time for those professional users which need NO eye-candies but only to work fast.

The effect of the UNITY is that my developers refuse to upgrade and that my customers/partners ask to switch to the previous LTR.

Considering that for business reason we use some of the newest libraries I cannot allow neither my developers or my partners to hold back to previous releases.

The conclusion is that I am looking for a different distribution which will replace Ubuntu for us. I know that it is possible with some effort to install the previous Gnome environment, but I have come to realize that the philosophy  of Ubuntu has changed in a direction which differs from the one my company needs. This latest release had no interesting updates, beside Unity. Even the kernel is the same....

I hope time will prove me wrong and that ubuntu will come back to be the most cutting edge distro and not a worthless eye-candy

---

### Post by IcyEyeG on 2011-10-14
Indeed I moved to Xubuntu 11.10 (I was going to use Ubuntu Studio, but unfortunately it isn't complete yet) and I'm happy with it, my hope is that the end of GNOME 2 will shift users to Xfce (which in turn increases its userbase and in the long run aids its development), as now it isn't simply a lightweight alternative, it is also an alternative for those who want the the old desktop paradigm.

My only complaint to Xfce is Thunar and GTK3 theming support. I understand its point of being a simple and lightweight file manager, but Nautilus is more flexible. However, almost all Xubuntu themes lack GTK3 support which makes my desktop experience inconsistent.
I'm pretty sure this will be fixed in the future, so I'm not mad.

---

### Post by 3Miro on 2011-10-14
> **IcyEyeG said:**
> Indeed I moved to Xubuntu 11.10 (I was going to use Ubuntu Studio, but unfortunately it isn't complete yet) and I'm happy with it, my hope is that the end of GNOME 2 will shift users to Xfce (which in turn increases its userbase and in the long run aids its development), as now it isn't simply a lightweight alternative, it is also an alternative for those who want the the old desktop paradigm.

My only complaint to Xfce is Thunar and GTK3 theming support. I understand its point of being a simple and lightweight file manager, but Nautilus is more flexible. However, almost all Xubuntu themes lack GTK3 support which makes my desktop experience inconsistent.
I'm pretty sure this will be fixed in the future, so I'm not mad.

This has nothing to do with the GTK2 themes. GTK2 themes have zero support for GTK3. Normally GTK3 themes will be handled by Gnome 3 (i.e. Unity or Gnome-shell), however, when Gnome 3 isn't working, GTK3 doesn't know what to do with the themes.

You can create a symlink to the GTK3 theme that you want to use:

[https://bbs.archlinux.org/viewtopic.php?id=118078](https://bbs.archlinux.org/viewtopic.php?id=118078)

Basically you need to run the following command:
```
ln -s /usr/share/themes/<some gtk3 theme>/gtk-3.0 ~/.config/gtk-3.0
```

Change "<some gtk3 theme>" to whatever theme you want. The thread has Adwaita, I am not sure if it is installed on Ubuntu. Do an
```
ls /usr/share/themes
```
to see the available themes. Make sure the one that you pick is a gtk3 theme indeed.

Also, look at the repos for plugins for Thunar. Some of the plugins like working with Archives or Samba are not installed by default. Overall Nautilus does have more plugins, but most of the extra plugins are not commonly used and/or can be handled by another program.

### Post by -RAX- on 2011-10-14
> **Larkspur said:**
> -RAX-, could you explain some of the specific issues your developers and customers have come up against when using Unity? That could be useful for this thread, especially if they are issues that can be resolved.

Sure, I was actually hoping for that.
1 - Unity was probably designed for a small screen or for a touchpad. Using it with a very large screen, HD or more, really slows you down. 
2 - Applications which are not in the launch bar are hard to find. A menu was less "cool" but more effective. I guess that this could easily be fixed by showing all the app that a user has installed instead of showing  "others that you may want to install".
3 - Systems files are hardly accessible. I noticed this myself as well. The file browser in Unity is designed to prevent users from messing with the system files. However sometimes you NEED to open a system file...
4 - while working I developers normally need to use 3 to 10 different app. They need to see 2 or 3  of them simultaneously. In Unity (and in the iPad) it is hard to use multiple apps simultaneously....

### Post by lento_ on 2011-10-14
I'm a developer and use 10.04 at work. I've had a bit of a play around with 11.04, but will not be upgrading to 11.10 for work.

The main problem is that I just don't like docks when I'm developing. Docks seem very *application-centric* rather than *document-centric*. That's fine if there's only one or two things open, but if I need to be rapidly switching between several terminals or several different instances of the same browser then it takes a lot longer with a dock than with the standard "window list" panel app. Instead of one click it'll be two clicks or a hover and a click - really tedious.

Add in the lack of ability to put a good system monitor on the panel (really important to me as a developer, and the indicator thingies don't seem quite there yet), the horrible dash, and a global menu which can put the menu a large distance on the screen away from the window it is applying to, and it all adds up to a bunch of changes which would make me *less* productive.

For using at home on a laptop where I'm just surfing the net, or playing music, it looks fine. For serious development on a dual screen PC with a lot of screen space I'm sticking with 10.04 until I need to upgrade, and then I'll probably move to Xubuntu or Kubuntu.

---

3 - Systems files are hardly accessible. I noticed this myself as well. The file browser in Unity is designed to prevent users from messing with the system files. However sometimes you NEED to open a system file...
4 - while working I developers normally need to use 3 to 10 different app. They need to see 2 or 3  of them simultaneously. In Unity (and in the iPad) it is hard to use multiple apps simultaneously....

1 - How does it slow you down? I never really understood this, but I haven't used Unity a whole lot (KDE fan here), so what do I know.
2 - Alt + F2 opens a search box. Type in what you want to launch and boom, it's right in front of you (you often don't have to type in the entire application name, just a few letters).
3 - How are they not accessible? They are just as easy to access as GNOME 2 (or any other desktop for that matter). You're Home folder is in the launcher by default and you can easily add links to other folders if you so desire. You can use terminal too, I don't know if it's in the launcher by default or not. If it isn't, you can easily add it.
4 - What's hard about it? Alt + Tab switches windows (I believe) and I think Compiz can support tilling.

### Post by 3Miro on 2011-10-14
> **lento_ said:**
> 
The main problem is that I just don't like docks when I'm developing. Docks seem very *application-centric* rather than *document-centric*. That's fine if there's only one or two things open, but if I need to be rapidly switching between several terminals or several different instances of the same browser then it takes a lot longer with a dock than with the standard "window list" panel app. Instead of one click it'll be two clicks or a hover and a click - really tedious.

Perfect explanation on the main problem with Unity. Gnome-shell has other issues, but for Unity this is the best summary of "what it wrong with it".

> 
Add in the lack of ability to put a good system monitor on the panel (really important to me as a developer, and the indicator thingies don't seem quite there yet), the horrible dash, and a global menu which can put the menu a large distance on the screen away from the window it is applying to, and it all adds up to a bunch of changes which would make me *less* productive.

The best indicators are not in the repositories for some reason. I installed indicators from here

[http://askubuntu.com/questions/30334/list-of-application-indicators](http://askubuntu.com/questions/30334/list-of-application-indicators)

At least for me those were good enough. IMO the best sensors and monitor applets are in XFCE, I don't like the Gnome ones as much.

> 
For using at home on a laptop where I'm just surfing the net, or playing music, it looks fine. For serious development on a dual screen PC with a lot of screen space I'm sticking with 10.04 until I need to upgrade, and then I'll probably move to Xubuntu or Kubuntu.

I keep Unity on my laptop because it looks cool for presentations and such. Otherwise I have xfce for work.

### Post by Dangertux on 2011-10-14
I tend to agree with the OP. In terms of professional use Unity, is less than convenient. From the perspective of a company they are going to be interested in the bottom line. If the bottom line goes up because they have to retrain their personnel they won't be interested in Ubuntu. 

While embracing the future may be what needs to happen in the long term, as it looks like Unity-like DE's are here to stay, the transition may have happened a little too quickly for the corporate world. Of course, this is one of the main reasons desktop installations in the corporate world are less than common. 

In my humble opinion if Canonical ever wants Ubuntu to be anything more than a hobby on the desktop front they need to provide the option of a more "traditional" gnome2 interface in addition to Unity, at least for a transition period longer than 6 months. Let's be realistic, the business world moves SLOWLY. 

I would also venture to say it's Canonical's "different" style of thinking that holds them back in the corporate world on the production server front. Ubuntu is not known for stability (yes I understand the cutting edge distro concept). That being said it will frequently not be  chosen in favor of more tried and true alternatives like CentOS, RHEL or BSD.

Ubuntu is a great operating system for the home user or enthusiast, not so much for businesses that require stability, and consistency. Two things which Canonical has yet to provide in any sort of abundance in terms of Ubuntu or Unity.

Just my 2 cents.

---

### Post by stinkeye on 2011-10-14
> **3Miro said:**
> Thank you, this was a very useful response. I wish people would give more of that rather than name-calling each other.
No problem. Friends??? :P

...and as to the OP's previous point about system files being less accessible,
there is a bug in 11.10 where nautilus-gksu no longer works but there is a fix.
ie no "Open as administrator" in the right click menu.
```
sudo cp /usr/lib/nautilus/extensions-2.0/libnautilus-gksu.so /usr/lib/nautilus/extensions-3.0/
```

### Post by moldaviax on 2011-10-14
I agree with the sentiments of the first post in this thread, and the excellent post 40 by [Dangertux]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=1322416"). When it comes to influencing customers to adopt linux (and in this case Ubuntu), it can be alarming for them to find release cycles of 6 months (some of which are percieved as necessary to fix bugs in a different version) and with 11.04 radical changes in the UI.

Granted there is xfce, other distros etc but for small IT  departments, its a struggle to support users on one OS, let alone users with different dialects.  

In mitigation I have also worked with people who have converted from WinXP to 11.04 and liked Ubuntu. However those are ancedotes - it would be far more interesting to get some data from the broader community on usage and experience. Is anyone collecting reliable stats on this?

In my humble opinion if Canonical ever wants Ubuntu to be anything more than a hobby on the desktop front they need to provide the option of a more "traditional" gnome2 interface in addition to Unity, at least for a transition period longer than 6 months. Let's be realistic, the business world moves SLOWLY.

Ubuntu is not known for stability (yes I understand the cutting edge distro concept). ### Post by DeMus on 2011-10-15
Hi all,

I have been a Ubuntu enthusiast from 2008 when Hardy was born. For a whole year I used Hardy with great satisfaction. Yes, there were issues, but that was also because I was new with Linux in general.

Then, April 2009, Jaunty came and I just had to install it, even though Hardy is an LTS version. Or better was an LTS version since its service has been stopped in April of this year.
Jaunty didn't let me down, it was even better than Hardy. I loved it.

In April of 2010 came Lucid and something was wrong. Somebody decided that the interface needed to be changed. I still don't know why. It took a while but I could change the GUI back to the way it should be, still wondering why I needed to do that since it always have been good. It should never have been changed.

Then, oh my god, April of this year brought us Natty and it was even worse than Lucid: now the complete interface was messed up: Unity was born.
It made me puke. How can somebody invent something so terrible? Yes, I know it is the interface on smartphones, but it doesn't have to be on a real computer. Smartphones are gadgets, useless items, computers are instruments people work on. It's something serious. An interface like Unity does not belong here.
Yes, I know I can change it back to the way it was but again I ask why do I need to do this? It used to be good, why change?
After having seen this disaster I decided to switch from Ubuntu to LinuxMint with a nice fresh interface. I love it. I sincerely hope Mint, a derivative from Ubuntu, stays far away from the Unity disaster and keeps using its own interface.

Now 11.10 has arrived and the forums are filled up with people complaining this or that does not work. It looks to me every year the amount of complaining people get more and more with every new issue of Ubuntu. I think every half year a new issue is just too much. The moment most of the bugs are gone it is time to install a new version again, getting all these same problems again. Why?
Wouldn't it be better to change to schedule? That way developers have more time to fix a version before it is released. Increasing the quality of their work.
At the present rate of new versions developers work (half of the time) on as much as 5 versions at the same time. Look at the top half of the sheet. During the rest of the time they work on 4 versions.
When the release schedule would be changed as in the bottom half of the sheet they work all the time on 3 different versions. Two changes:
one new version every year
LTS versions are maintained during 2 years instead of 3.
I am convinced the quality of the work will be much better when following this schedule:
less versions to worry about
more time to finish a new one.

Do we need a new version every half year? I don't. I'd rather have one new version every year which I know works, instead of having one every 6 months full of bugs.

That doesn't resolve the issue with the GUI, I know. That's something the developers really have to think about if they want to continue this or if they want to come to their senses. If it's the first, it's goodbye to Ubuntu for me, and for many others who absolutely don't like Unity. Is this what Ubuntu wants? Hard to imagine.

---

### Post by makitso on 2011-10-15
DeMus,
 

 My background with Ubuntu is similar to yours and I share some of your feelings as well.  I personally don't like Unity.  So, over the last three months I have been trying other distros and shells, Fedora, Mint, Xubunty and Lubuntu, xfce and LXDE.  I have also followed the posting to this forum as well as others on the Unity vs Gnome 3 wars.   
 

 It probably is a bit easier for me since I have used the Avant Window Navigator (AWN) dock since 10.04.  So, for my newer machines, I have settled on 11.10 running Gnome3 and AWN.  For my lower powered machines I use Lubuntu and the Cairo dock but with the 11.04 release.
 

 My personal concerns with the 11.10 release of ubuntu largely deal with performance and power.  This release took a major hit in the performance area.   
 

 Performance:
 When I upgraded my netbook from Lubuntu 11.04 to 11.10 there was a noticeable performance degredation.  So, I did a clean install of 11.10 and the same thing.  As such I have gone back to 11.04.
 

 Power:
 Lets face it, a lot of Ubuntu users have laptops.  Yet the power consumption of Ubuntu has grown over the last few releases forcing people to hack the system to try and compensate.  My concern is that Canonical directly, as well as the kernel developers, have little concern over this issue.
 

 I have seen many technical companies build a base and then abandon it in order to grow larger or attract a different following.  Many fail – so I hope that the end justifies Canonical means.
  :(

Now, i don't use unity or Gnome shell or unity, because i don't like the 'app' feel that they have. However, i have a feeling that new, casual users WILL like unity. I started with UNR in 9.10, and i can say that i hated GNOME when i first tried it after UNR's interface (which is a LOT like unity). I loved UNR at first, and it's what made me stay. as i grew, i chose more traditional DE's because they fit better with what i do. I'm thinking that those who come into ubuntu as linux-newbies will appreciate Something Different, and it'll make 'em stick around and check out what's going on. As they settle, many might stick with unity, or some might veer off and try XFCE, or something. Because of this, i tend to think of Unity as the 'hook' for newer users. Newer users (and experienced users who happen to like unity) will play with it gain experience, and people who DON'T like it will have the sense to install something else. 

Many have said it, and i'll repeat it: distro-hopping because you don't like the DE is a lot like buying a new car because your ash tray is full. 

Also in response to the release schedule; isn't it the whole idea of ubuntu to have a *reasonably* stable distro that stays up-to-date with new releases of things? If you're in need of a desktop that's stable at the expense of feature set, wouldn't you be running the LTS, or even Debian?

--Aaron

---

### Post by whitefort on 2011-10-15
BoxRec, just for what it's worth, you're not alone.  I first discovered Ubuntu in the days of Dapper Drake, and I've been updating to every new version since then.  Apart from a brief flirtation with Arch, Ubuntu has been my distro for all those years.

I've tried hard to get used to Unity, but it's just been a continual frustration - my computers feel brain-dead and less functional than they used to, and it takes longer to get everything done.

I think it's great that people are working independently to make Ubuntu more functional, but...  they shouldn't really have to.

I hate to say this (or even think it), but rather than the uphill slog to make Ubuntu act the way I'd like, for the first time I'm seriously considering switching to another distro.
(That's not a complaint  or a whine!  I know some people seem to love Ubuntu's new direction.  But it's just not for me.)
