```
### Post by irv on 2011-10-15
I  was getting ready to post about the bad taste I have in my mouth from Unity when I saw this thread so I will just add my thoughts on this.  Seeing others here have the same thoughts I have on Unity. 

When 11.04 came out, I downloaded it and installed it. Right from the get go I didn't like it, but to be fair about it I gave it time, but after some time I still didn't like it so I installed Gnome 3, and used it until 11.10 came out two days ago. (Didn't like it either, but felt it was a little better then Unity). 

I read about all the improvements in Unity with the release of 11.10 so I was going to give it a try again. To make a long story short, every time I made a change to Compiz it hosed up Unity and I had to re-install it. After five installs, I gave up and downloaded kubuntu.

Now I want everyone to know, I was a big fan of Gnome 2.3 and would be still using it, but I don't like where Gnome is going either. It seems like everyone wasts to go with the Dash (no more then a launch pad), which I do not like or want. 

It's been over 5 years since I have been using Ubuntu and Gnome but now that I have KDE with kubuntu I am going to go this way until ubuntu comes to realizes many people do not like Unity and and some that do use it, are using it because that is all that is being offered from Ubuntu. 

Just for the record, for the past year I have been using lubuntu with xfce desktop, and switching to Gnome 3. I also like Mint, but it has a few short falls.

If Ubuntu doesn't make some changes soon, it looks like I will stay with KDE. By the way KDE has come a long ways since I tried it a few years back. 

Thanks DeMus for the nice thread.

### Post by Atamisk on 2011-10-15
> **DeMus said:**
> About the release schedule:
What you are saying is that for Ubuntu quantity is more important than quality? Releasing a lot of releases very fast with lots of bugs is better than half of the releases which are (almost) bug free?
It's what you want, it's not what I want.
I want to switch on the computer and do those things I want and need to do, not worrying about installing and fixing bugs all the time. That's not why I have a computer.

But each its own. For now I am away from Ubuntu and use Mint with the perfect GUI. Again, each its own.

Actually, what i'm saying is that newer software comes with bugs and little niggles that go away with time. If these things weren't released until they were100% polished, we'd never use anything new here at Ubuntu. I think ubuntu realizes that some people are satisfied with slightly older architecture, so they offer the LTS, which is a release that is geared more to stability than features. If you're worried about bugs, run the LTS. it's there for that purpose. Ubuntu makes no secret of the fact that it's non-LTS versions are linked to Debian Unstable, and the LTS's are linked to Testing. 

It's not quality over quantity, it's development versus stagnation. If the newer versions of software don't get released to the GP to play with and use, the bugs never get worked out because despite their intentions, developers use software differently than normal users. .

### Post by cuppsy on 2011-10-15
> **quickmute said:**
> Thanks for Panel Tint2. That should help. With so much apparent hate toward Unity, hasn't Canonical got a hint? Although since there are so many articles praising it, I guess they'll opt to listen to editors over real user community. 

At this time, I'm giving Unity a chance for now. Here are some pending complaints:
Why is it that I can't dock windows to left/right/full like I could in the previous release?
Why is it that when I maximize, the minimize/return/close button disappears (double click still works on most apps - except LibreOffice)? Is this a bug or a feature?

For what it's worth, I represent someone in the user community as well, and after a hiatus after the initial release of 11.04, I've come back and grown to thoroughly enjoy Unity. As for some of your question, you should still be able to drag a window to the top/left/right to dock it accordingly. 

And the "disappearing" min/max button are just the like global menu: when maximized it disappears until you mouse over the menu bar. While I whole-heartedly agree you should be able to change that behavior for those that don't enjoy it, it hasn't really affected my workflow.

### Post by snowpine on 2011-10-15
While I am not personally a Unity user (since I'm not running Ubuntu) I did take it for a test-drive and I think it has some nice features. In particular it is much easier to use than Gnome 2 for people like me who prefer the keyboard to the mouse. For example to launch any applciation you simply tap the Windows key and type the first few letters of the app name, that is much easier to me than navigating through menus with the mouse. I predict a lot of "Unity sucks" complaints will dry up as users become more familiar with the interface and its shortcuts.

### Post by llanitedave on 2011-10-15
I've spent the last couple of days comparing Unity with Kubuntu.  There are some things I really like about Unity on my laptop.  It's visually more appealing (KDE doesn't display GTK+ apps nearly as attractively as GTK+ displays KDE apps!), and the interface, once you get used to it can indeed develop an intuitive appeal.  I've tried some of the shortcuts, and they are pretty smooth for the most part.

There are 2 1/2 reasons that I'm switching away from it, though.  The first and worst, is that it doesn't seem to detect my screen size properly, and some display windows spill off the bottom of the screen where it is impossible to manually resize them.  If there's a shortcut way to do this, I haven't learned it.  It makes some of my navigation, especially in the GIMP which I'm using extensively, very awkward and inefficient.

The second is the fact that I can't reposition the Dock.  As long as there's nothing on the bottom of the screen,  I'd like to put the Dock there.  On the left side, it constantly blocks my Firefox back button, as well as side menus on Geany.  To overcome this, I have to move my windows to the right a bit, but I have to do it each time I log in.  That cancels out the supposed added efficiency of extra screen space on the bottom.

And the 1/2 reason is that the desktop is simply not configurable.  I can't have different themes for each desktop, I don't even seem to be able to change the number of desktops.

I'm sure a lot of these issues will be addressed in the Pangolin, but for now it just isn't quite there.

For now, I'm on KDE, but I'm not real enamored of that either.  Parts of it are very nice, but parts are kludgy, and I've had some crashing in the System Settings application.  Even though KDE lets me work more efficiently, I have to admit that there are some nice touches in Unity that I'd like to see more widely adopted, like the 'Alt-tab' feature.

Tonight I'm going to set up Xfce and give it a try.  Maybe that's what I'm really looking for.

In all that, though, you aren't going to get any hate for Unity from me.  I think it has potential, and I plan to try it again for the next release at the latest.  It's just not quite there yet.

---

### Post by gdi2k on 2011-10-16
On the whole I like Unity for the vertical space I gain and general integrated and (mostly) smooth look and feel. Having said that, things need to get better. Current gripes: 
[LIST]
[*]Things are buggy and sometimes don't work as intended - frequently the launcher won't unhide and I have to move my window manually. Sometimes the minimise / maximise / close buttons are missing from libreoffice etc.
[*]It's SLOW on high resolutions. I have a laptop that I dock to two external monitors at home. When undocked, things are nice and fast, but once docked, things are horribly slow (Core2 Duo 2.4 GHz, integrated graphics). Gnome3 and Natty's Unity are no trouble...
[*]The task switcher needs love - I want to see pictures of the windows I have open, not fancy icons. Also: Switching between two windows of the same application is hasslesome - just make them separate icons! If I'd wanted them to be grouped, I would have opened a tab within the app itself.
[*]Why is 24 px the smallest size I can make my launcher icons? I would prefer 16 px personally. Why limit me? 
[*]Changing fonts and sizes is HARD! People have different screen sizes, resolutions, viewing distances, eyes etc. - let people change their font settings!
[*]Lack of consistency: Libreoffice still doesn't have the menus in the top bar like other apps, and the new scroll bars only work on some applications. I know this is being worked on, but I was expecting it to be done for Oneric. 
[/LIST]

Hopefully things will be perfect in 12.04...

---

To overcome this, I have to move my windows to the right a bit, but I have to do it each time I log in.  That cancels out the supposed added efficiency of extra screen space on the bottom.

And the 1/2 reason is that the desktop is simply not configurable.  I can't have different themes for each desktop, I don't even seem to be able to change the number of desktops.

I'm sure a lot of these issues will be addressed in the Pangolin, but for now it just isn't quite there.

For now, I'm on KDE, but I'm not real enamored of that either.  Parts of it are very nice, but parts are kludgy, and I've had some crashing in the System Settings application.  Even though KDE lets me work more efficiently, I have to admit that there are some nice touches in Unity that I'd like to see more widely adopted, like the 'Alt-tab' feature.

Tonight I'm going to set up Xfce and give it a try.  Maybe that's what I'm really looking for.

In all that, though, you aren't going to get any hate for Unity from me.  I think it has potential, and I plan to try it again for the next release at the latest.  It's just not quite there yet.

just found out you can change the number, and for me crucially, the configuration of the desktops in the compiz setting manager.  Go to the general settings and you can set up number of rows and columns of workspaces.

All in all unity just isn't winning me over, and although I've only been with ubuntu for about 4 years, the entirety of using linux as my main OS, I'm considering moving to another distro.  I'll see what mint does, and I may give some of the other ?buntus a try.  First I'm going to give this 30 days and see.

---

### Post by Atamisk on 2011-10-16
> **DeMus said:**
> So you are saying, if people want to continue with Ubuntu they should be prepared to have a buggy system the first couple of weeks/months?

** Absolutely. If this is unacceptable, stick with the LTS. ****There's even an option in the update manager to automatically ignore the non-LTS releases. **

What kind of nonsense is this? From a system like Ubuntu you may expect it to be working the day it leaves the factory. It should be tested more before they release it.

** In my experience, they spend 4 months almost solid testing the newest release. But, you could "test" for a year and still not iron out the bugs. Also, what factory?**

 But they have no time since they need to get a new one every 6 months. When do they finally come to their senses? Use more time for a release, work on less releases and make sure the product is finished when it is released. 

[B] Who decides when it's finished? If you wait for all of the packages to become 100% polished before the next release, we'll both be dead by the time the next release is ready. Remember that there of hundreds of packages and likely thousands of developers that are actively developing software Ubuntu uses. In *almost all* cases, Ubuntu staff have little to no control over the developments of the software. In very many cases (in my experiences) the development is extremely active in some of these softwares and *major* changes are made frequently. Therefore, expecting Ubuntu to guarantee a bug free experience is unrealistic no matter how long you wait between releases.

[tl;dr version: Linux is a fluid. by the time you've taken a picture of it, it's changed completely, so it's impossible to make a perfect system no matter how long you diddle with it between releases, 6 months or 6 years.][/B]

Then, and only then will you get fans. And when changing the GUI again (back to how it should be, and to how it was) all comment will be gone. There is no better advertisement than that.

** If you want the GUI to change, change it. it literally takes 5 seconds to type apt-get install (...)**. ** I should know, i did it. i run xfce because i'm not too fond of unity just yet. **



Anyway, since this has somehow wound up in the unity megathread, i believe we're officially off topic. I'd like to continue this discussion, so please feel free to contact me via PM or start a new thread ...somewhere...

EDIT: VinDSL, that screenshot makes Unity make sense. thank you!

### Post by chrisjoffe on 2011-10-16
> **critin said:**

Exactly.  Things should be easy to find and only one or two clicks away.  Also, having to take my focus off the window I'm working in to make menu selections for that application off the system bar is distracting.

### Post by Meelad on 2011-10-17
Oh come on!!!!!!

A few changes to the system.. BIG DEAL! You guys are acting like somebody who just lost his beloved one! Your old pen and paper are dead. Get over it.. I myself didn't like 11.04 and stayed with 10.10, but now it's time for change.. The way you whine and refuse even to give unity a chance or even try it is just like a child crying for some toy and doesn't want any other one..

Unity is better than gnome 2 and the evidence is that almost all of the new Ubuntu users prefer Unity to gnome 2.. So it's not a matter of Unity is bad, but rather a matter of you are too lazy to spend a week to get used to it..

Unity is still a young project and have a LOT of potential.. Imagine what it could be like in two years time.. They could even install it on tablets (it's clearly much more suitable for that than gnome 2), and imagine how great that would be to have your main system running seamlessly on your tablet too! Don't tie yourself to the past, because this way it will be difficult for you to get into the future.. 

So instead of "threatening to leave" (which is quite odd actually since we pay NOTHING for it, and we get MUCH more from Ubuntu than what we give back), we should all discuss calmly what can be improved to make the next version even better, because the work on the next version is already underway..

So cool - you want to use the bleeding edge then great - but you need to get upgrades for kernel and libraries but don't want the paradigm-shift then here's the other desktop you know.

So "default" Ubuntu provides a simple choice - big deal.  Maybe once that choice is made they just stay down that upgrade path - makes it even easier.

Isn't there a group of hardcore Ubuntu developers who want to support Gnome 3 and on?

I do not disagree that Unity is cool and has great promise - for certain uses. But one (default) shoe does not fit all.

### Post by Morbius1 on 2011-10-17
This is more of an FYI. I do not personally use KDE any more nor am I advocating any here does. I just thought I would share something I read at distrowatch: [http://distrowatch.com/weekly.php?issue=20111017](http://distrowatch.com/weekly.php?issue=20111017)
> The KDE team has announced the availability of Plasma Active One. [COLOR=Blue]While  the GNOME project has decided to rework their entire desktop in order to  address tablets and touch screens, the KDE project is taking a  different approach. The KDE4 desktop will remain as it is and a branch  of KDE is being designed specifically for mobile devices.[/COLOR] From the [release announcement]("http://www.kde.org/announcements/plasma-active-one/"): "Plasma  Active is compatible with the Plasma Desktop and Plasma Netbook  Workspaces. They are all based on the same framework, sharing more than  95% of code. This is a radically different approach to interface  commonality across devices. Most other tablet products are either unique  to themselves (sharing little or nothing in common with other device  interfaces), or attempts to use desktop interfaces on smaller displays.  But Plasma-based interfaces are crafted for specific device types, and  are able to do so while having nearly all their code and engineering  efforts in common."

### Post by BrunoThomas on 2011-10-18
It seems odd but this is the first time that Ubuntu has caused so much division amongst it's users.  Whether you are Unity supporters or not, the question remains why has this happened?

When I came to Ubuntu a few years ago, I tried a number of different linux distributions.  Ubuntu won on three accounts.  Firstly it installed without any bother, secondly they had obviously thought careful about which applications to include (most others just gave you a plethora of choice which daunted a newby), and thirdly it was easy to customise.  With the latest release, only one out of the three is still true.  

Ubuntu has been building up a steady base of supporters and new users.  The problem now is whether Unity will destroy this base of support and put off new users.  Unfortunately this latest release, after much criticism of the last release, seems to be heading that way. As such, I would ask Ubuntu to consider the following article very carefully: [http://jnd.org/dn.mss/gestural_interfaces_a_step_backwards_in_usability_6.html](http://jnd.org/dn.mss/gestural_interfaces_a_step_backwards_in_usability_6.html) (I realise it is about gestural computing but the concepts are very much relevant).

As for me, I have reverted back to 10.10, but am hoping that Ubuntu get their act together and produce a version of Unity in the next release that gives them the edge over the other distros once again.

---

### Post by rockney on 2011-10-19
Yes - but the circumstances just don't work for us. We need some library upgrades that are only supported on the 3.x kernel.

And it's not so much 'risk free' as giving us a straight-forward upgrade path without the Unity paradigm shift.

Fact of the matter is a Windows-like desktop is what most people can use without much trouble. Gnome has always been the best cross-over alternative. Some group will likely take up that combination. 

In fact, someone posted this (sorry, don't remember where I found it) but it looks interesting:  [Introducing Ubuntu-11-10 Without Unity]("http://news.softpedia.com/news/Introducing-Ubuntu-11-10-Without-Unity-228425.shtml")

### Post by ARooster on 2011-10-19
Unity is terrible in my opinion. As suggested, I am giving it a go, but I'm hardly encouraged to do so by the fact I am using more resources running Ubuntu 11.10 with Unity than on Windows XP. While it is true that Windows XP is indeed a decade old I have to say that 11.04 with classic desktop was not as greedy, not to mention it started up and closed considerably faster and it was not nearly as choppy as the various effects of Unity.

The Dash is less familiar than the Windows-like desktop GUI, it has a mobile phone look to it, however you hardly ever run more than a couple of apps on a mobile phone at the same time, whereas on a desktop computer I will typically run several instances of some program, coupled with several instances of another program and a few different applications at the same time. Finding things already open is faster on Windows or Gnome 2.

I would suggest adding some form of bar to group already open applications because the Dash just doesn't cut it. Plus it would perhaps be a good idea to at least give the option to keep Dash visible at all times or make it open and fold on command since if you are going to clutter the left part of my desktop with a pictogramme bar for illiterates, could you at least make it not pop up over my running application when I move the mouse too far left?

All in all, I'd say this is a rather stupid move. If I want eye candy I will use Windows. I went to Ubuntu for stability, ease of use and speed. And to top it off, with all the talk of a modern GUI, the desktop is empty and unused! Unless I decide to put loads of folders and files onto it or manually set up links (which given the limited amount of options you have on the desktop will have to be in a form of bash script, it seems like way too much work and as such quite unproductive) to applications which I don't want to include into my Dash that's already cluttered as it is, it will remain unused. Wouldn't a widget option be a good idea?

Since Ubuntu was seen as a serious upcoming competitor even by Microsoft a couple of years ago, I'd say Microsoft can now breathe a sigh of relief as apparently Windows 8 that is already being hyped up will be smaller and will use up less system resources than what I've seen from 11.10. Having a Linux distribution use up more resources than the newest Windows may sound like a bad joke now, however if things keep going this way I do believe Canonical will fall off the grid completely. They came to prominence when Vista was a Microsoft's failed project. Since Windows 7 is quite popular and since Windows 8 is going the other way (growing smaller, not larger) while still bringing a new and trendy interface it would be wise for Canonical to consider their direction slightly better. Perhaps at the start-up menu they could include various desktop options with the retarded dash for mobile devices and a choice of a more classic desktop environment for anything with more than 1024 x 768 pixels of screen space.

---

### Post by robert shearer on 2011-10-19
> **cmcanulty said:**
> I tried the gnomeshell remix with gnome classic option today and it is nowhere near as convenient as the real gnome desktop. No admin or prefs menus, poor font, a lot less convenient but it is better than unity

Look a bit more ;)
Applications>Other.
Applications>System Tools>System Settings.

For fonts install gnome-tweak-tool
```

sudo apt-get install gnome-tweak-tool
```

To modify panels **alt+right-click**.

### Post by idefixx on 2011-10-21
Unity doesn't work for me. Not even if I try really hard. 

Here's why:

I'm using nomachine to log into my Ubuntu server. I have the following problems with Unity:
- No way to change font size (*). It's way too big for me.
- The sidebar (called launcher?) disappears when windows are maximized. No way to change that behaviour (**). The sidebar reappears when the mouse is on the left edge of the screen but since I'm running Unity in a window I cannot easily position the mouse at the left edge of the Unity screen, only the host screen.
- I cannot change to an open but covered window. Really. If for example I have a terminal window open and it's covered by the browser, I have not found a way to bring the terminal window to front, using the mouse (only alt-tab works, but that doesn't count). 

Minor gripes:
- Menus are visible only when the mouse is hovering over the top panel. So when I have the mouse cursor in the application I'm actually working on I cannot see the menu for it. Really unhelpful.
- Menus are visible only when holding down the mouse button. This is a bug, I think since it is inconsistent.

Those are only the things I found in the short time I had with Unity until I was forced away to something else. 

You can argue that since I'm logging in via nomachine my concerns aren't really valid. Well that invalidates also virtual machine users and I really think it should not matter how I'm accessing my computer. Linux has and always will be a networked citizen. Why and how and where should be irrelevant.

I was really hoping for Ubuntu to finally take Linux to the desktop. I can't even use Unity and thus I can't recommend it to anyone else. That really hurts because Unity takes some hope away from me that Linux would finally make it to the desktop. Ubuntu is now a dead end for me and I really, really did not want that to happen.

Sincerely,
-- Henrik

(*) Only by installing gnome tweak tool. Completely unacceptable for such a basic feature. 
(**) I did try to change it by installing ccsm and configuring Unity but that didn't work due to bugs.

### Post by Morbius1 on 2011-10-21

### Post by jago25_98 on 2011-10-21
I was happy with Unity but now I've upgraded I don't have 3D at the moment (I'll fix it). What I'm finding now is that I don't have that things which snaps the windows to the edge of the screen. 

 I use tiling and the way I view it is very simple - only have 2 windows on screen at a time, vertically. 

 So now I'm looking for a 3rd party app to tile the windows for me. The only one I can find right now is laggy and the other (wmtile) has disappeared off the web. I don't want the hassle of switching to a new WM. 

 Other than that, I fixed the ability to browse what's installed quicker with classicmenu-indicator. 

 I also have to take my hand off the mouse to switch workspaces with ctrl+alt+arrowKey. So I'm looking for a fix for that. Why not a quick 3rd party app for that set to a hotkey. Anyone good with python?


p.s, it's all a result of not enough testing with different types of user

### Post by mpiter on 2011-10-21
> **jago25_98 said:**
> ...So now I'm looking for a 3rd party app to tile the windows for me. The only one I can find right now is laggy and the other (wmtile) has disappeared off the web. I don't want the hassle of switching to a new WM....

May I suggest you to try Gnome 3 (gnome-session).  I am among the users who cannot bare Unity for several reasons.  I however tried Unity once more for a while when Ubuntu 11.10 was out, then I switched to Gnome 3 because I like to test things.  I was positively abashed by the cleverness of Gnome 3 concept.  Even though Gnome 3.2 is obviously not fully finished and will keep evolving during the following months, it is already very nice and usable.  I am decided to quit Compiz + classical desktop ( Gnome 2 or XFCE4) for Gnome 3 as soon as it is available in Linux Mint this November.  I will see after a few weeks if I will keep it or come back to a more classical desktop.

If I were you, instead of spending a lot of time trying to fix something that will probably be fixed by Ubuntu team soon, I would try Gnome 3 for a couple of days.  According to me, both desktops share the idea of offering the user something simple, easy to use, not too configurable, that could also be used on tablets. I just think that when you need to manage many windows in several desktops, Gnome 3 is far superior to Unity.  If you like Unity, you might although like the new Gnome.  If you do not like it, you can come back to Unity, and maybe the bug that affects you would be fixed by then.

### Post by dummy910 on 2011-10-21
[http://www.linuxtoday.com/infrastructure/2011102100241OPUB](http://www.linuxtoday.com/infrastructure/2011102100241OPUB)
> Why does Unity suck so much? Because it assumes you're a complete retard  who can only click shiny buttons. It assumes you don't care what is  going on in any programs except the one you currently have focus on. It  assumes you want to run everything maximized. It assumes you don't need  proper notifications from certain applications. It puts the hideable  launch bar on the left, where you accidentally activate it every time  you go to the File menu in a program, or aim for the refresh button in a  browser.

Speaking of browsers, Unity hates Google Chrome (the browser I use  most) so much, it buried it three levels deep in a ridiculously  oversimplified, slow-loading, nonsensical menu system. It assumed I  wanted every LibreOffice program in my application dock; I use those  programs so infrequently that I may as well uninstall them, but somehow  Unity thinks they are more important to me than Chrome. Hell, Unity  thought I would prefer Chromium, and up until now I thought I'd  uninstalled it! 
I agree with these and so many other issues. I've already completely removed Ubuntu on every machine that I own because of these last 2 horrible 11 series releases.

I'm digging Mint Debian LMDE over the last couple of weeks. Not a single hiccup, and no Unity, no Gnome-Shell, no Zeitgiest DataHubs phoning home, no breakage after updates... no headaches in other words.

The Buntu's were fun while they lasted, but just don't cut the grade anymore. Sad it is.

Peace Out.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-10-21
> **oregonbob said:**
> Do you know the exact package names for a reasonably clean and useful version of Gnome 3?

```
sudo apt-get install gnome-shell
``` is sufficient to install Gnome 3.2.  I tested it for a few hours and it was rather stable on my computer.

> **oregonbob said:**
> I upgraded to 11.10 (big mistake), tried Unity again to confirm it is absolutely not ready for prime time. Then I search for a "gnome-session" and found it no longer exists.

As incredible as it can seem, Canonical does not provide Gnome anymore by default, you have to install it with the command shown above.  Without this, you only have Unity.

> **oregonbob said:**
> Then I loaded an Ubuntu supported Gnome that seemed broken or half-finished like Unity. Now when I boot I see choices "Gnome, Gnome Classic, Gnome (no extras), Ubuntu (unity) and maybe a few others. There is something significantly broken in every version option.

I also think that Gnome is not totally finished.  It however is functional enough on my computer to be used.  I did not dare to write "to be trustworthy used" because I have not used it enough to judge.  But it looks stable and easy to use to me.  I plan to use it as is very soon.

Be careful, Gnome Classic is not Gnome 2.32 anymore.  It is Gnome 3 with a screen organisation slightly looking as 2.32. I think that Gnome 2.32 is not available anymore on Ubuntu 11.10.

> **oregonbob said:**
> I would like to clean all those off and install one, clean, functional GUI that works like Ubuntu 10's. What a mess I have made with my main machine.

There are many options. You can try Gnome 3.2.  Just spend an hour in a tutorial in the Web to learn the basics.  It is really easy.  You can go back to Ubuntu 10.10 if you can convert some databases for the pieces of software that have changed their database format between Ubuntu 10.10 and 11.10.  It is the case of Evolution for example.  You can install XUbuntu or KUbuntu if you prefer a more classic experience.  You can give up Ubuntu and install Linux Mint instead.  That is what I am presently doing.

If you do not like present Canonical's attitude toward their users with their way to push Unity/Gnome 3 to the point of splitting the user community, take a look at how Linux Mint developers manage the Gnome transition:

> Linux Mint 12 “Lisa” will be released in November this year with continued support for Gnome 2 but also with the introduction of Gnome 3. The radical changes introduced by the Gnome project split the community. At the time of releasing Linux Mint 11 we decided it was too early to adopt Gnome 3. This time around, the decision isn’t as simple. Gnome 3.2 is more mature and we can see the potential of this new desktop and use it to implement something that can look and behave better than anything based on Gnome 2. Of course, we’re starting from scratch and this process will take time and span across multiple releases. Until then, it’s important we continue to support the traditional Gnome 2 desktop. We’re likely to release two separate editions, one for Gnome 2.32 and one for Gnome 3.2. We’re also working in cooperation with the MATE project (which is a fork of Gnome 2) at the moment to see if we can make both desktops compatible in an effort to let you run both Gnome 2 (or MATE) and Gnome 3 on the same system, either in Linux Mint 12, or for the future.

I copied this text from Linux Mint blog.  Linux Mint is Ubuntu and Debian based. It is the 4th OS more widespread in the world, just behind Ubuntu. It is very similar to Ubuntu but more user-oriented in its development and a bit less adventurous for more stability.  Ubuntu 11.04 was so disappointing to many experienced Linux users that it created a wave of Ubuntu users that gave up that OS for Linux Mint.  Ubuntu 11.10 has just created another wave of dissatisfied users who are installing Linux Mint now.

As far as I am concerned, I hesitated to give up Ubuntu when 11.04 was out.  I eventually decided to wait for Ubuntu 11.10 before taking a decision. Ubuntu 11.10 only confirmed the new direction taken by Canonical. I sincerely wish good luck to Mark Shuttleworth because he has created a great Linux world and has contributed a lot to dispatch Linux everywhere. But the new tendency does not correspond to my needs.  So I have just installed Linux Mint on one computer and I will check this week-end how it behaves.

### Post by hzFVOW00pw on 2011-10-22
> Linux Mint 12 “Lisa” will be released in November this year with continued support for Gnome 2 but also with the introduction of Gnome 3. The radical changes introduced by the Gnome project split the community. At the time of releasing Linux Mint 11 we decided it was too early to adopt Gnome 3. This time around, the decision isn’t as simple. Gnome 3.2 is more mature and we can see the potential of this new desktop and use it to implement something that can look and behave better than anything based on Gnome 2. Of course, we’re starting from scratch and this process will take time and span across multiple releases. Until then, it’s important we continue to support the traditional Gnome 2 desktop. We’re likely to release two separate editions, one for Gnome 2.32 and one for Gnome 3.2. We’re also working in cooperation with the MATE project (which is a fork of Gnome 2) at the moment to see if we can make both desktops compatible in an effort to let you run both Gnome 2 (or MATE) and Gnome 3 on the same system, either in Linux Mint 12, or for the future.

This sounds like a far more sensible approach than developing your own crappy desktop shell because of quarrels with the Gnome developers, thereby alienating a large part of your users in the process. As Canonical did.

Some months ago Linux Mint surpassed Ubuntu in popularity on distrowatch and was in first place for a few weeks. This will surely happen again in November.

I've seriously tried to use Unity several times now, I know how it works, but I still don't like it. To me Unity is already a thing of the past, I regard it now as an unwanted by-product of Ubuntu. And I am certainly not going to install this for my customers, because I think it won't be around for long.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-10-22
> **maxibuntu said:**
> This sounds like a far more sensible approach than developing your own crappy desktop shell because of quarrels with the Gnome developers, thereby alienating a large part of your users in the process. As Canonical did.

I also like Linux Mint upgrade model.  You have choice between two options: (i) Simple upgrade such as Ubuntu's; (ii) Fresh installation.  The second option boils down to[LIST=1]
[*]Keep [COLOR="Sienna"]/etc[/COLOR] in a repository using [COLOR="Sienna"]etckeeper[/COLOR] tool and update your repository before upgrading.
[*]Save your package list using Linux Mint backup tool.
[*]Make a new complete installation of the new Linux Mint version from the live DVD.
[*]Restore your packages from your backup list.
[*]Tweak [COLOR="Sienna"]/etc[/COLOR] with the help of your repository and [COLOR="Sienna"]etckeeper[/COLOR] tool.
[/LIST]
This schema clearly requests more work and someone competent, but the main advantage is that you start with a fresh installation that has been more thoroughly tested than the product of an upgrade of a personal configuration that often leads to incompatibilities between package versions or dependency problems that needs a few weeks to be resolved.  For those who do not have the knowledge or do not want to make the effort to go through that hassle, the first option is still available though not advised. See. [[COLOR="Blue"]http://community.linuxmint.com/tutorial/view/2[/COLOR]]("http://community.linuxmint.com/tutorial/view/2") if your interested by the motivations of the second model.

If you want support for a user interface specialized for a desktop or laptop today, Ubuntu might not be the first choice anymore.  That OS still lets choices for more computer-oriented interfaces, but if their support decreases and if the user community shifts to something else, what would be the point to keep Ubuntu when you need to carry out intensive work? ### Post by 3rdalbum on 2011-10-22
> **whatthefunk said:**
> Do you think that Unity is really increasing market share?  It seems to b alienating a lot of loyal users.

I'm not saying everyone is like this, but there have always been complaints about every change in every version of Ubuntu. I remember when the re-ordering of the window buttons was going to cause everyone to switch to Arch or Debian or Fedora. People like to grumble, but actual switching doesn't happen very often. People will get used to Unity and then they'll complain about the new sound theme in 12.04 or something.

> And why do you think that Win8 users will be disgruntled?  Maybe they wont like the tablet like interface for a desktop OS?  So is making an OS with a tablet like interface really the best way to attract these people?

Unity is not a tablet-like interface. It's a semi-transparent layer over a traditional desktop; or, if you like, it's a traditional desktop that's been re-organised a bit.

Metro is a tablet interface. It doesn't even run traditional desktop programs except by temporarily running a classic desktop for the non-Metro programs. Unity, of course, runs normal desktop programs.

If you look at the experience of using Metro with only Metro apps, it's truly like using a tablet - no window management, full-screen programs only, that sort of thing. If you look at the experience of using Metro with non-Metro apps, it's like using VNC to a desktop PC through your tablet, only worse. If you look at the experience of using Unity, it's like a normal desktop with window management and first-class keyboard and mouse support, etc.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-10-23
> **azurehi said:**
> Why can't the Dash in Unity be moved around...bottom, top, L, R?

Present goal of Mark Shuttleworth, the genius behind Canonical, is to provide a uniform interface for desktops, laptops, and tablets.  Unity is his means to reach that objective.  If I remember well, that was the motivation for global menu and overlay scrollbars: Saving as much pixels as possible for small screen devices.

About your question, if I remember well, Mark Shuttleworth motivation to lock the dash on the left-hand side of the desktop was to have it close to the Ubuntu button.  I do not remember the details but there was a huge passionate debate on that topic one year ago in this bug report [[COLOR="Blue"]https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/668415[/COLOR]]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/668415").  Several people complained as you about the lack of choice for the dash position. Mr. Shuttleworth himself explained his motivation.  Read it if you are interested in these details.

I think that is a great idea to have the same interface on all his devices.  Of course, some people prefer today to have a more computer-oriented interface for their computers and something else for their tablets and smartphones.  Ubuntu allows both options because you can choose to work with other desktops than Unity such as XFCE4 or KDE. If you prefer these more classical desktops, choose one of them and stay with Ubuntu (Xubuntu or Kubuntu or something else with your favorite desktop installed instead of Unity). If you fear that all efforts made by Canonical in Unity development will be done detrimental to more classical desktop maintenance, change distribution. For example Linux Mint is Ubuntu based and can be considered as a Ubuntu branch with the Ubuntu philosophy known till 10.10 while the real Ubuntu is Ubuntu with the new orientation known since 11.04.  Of course, Linux Mint is not maintained by Canonical, but it is nevertheless the most popular Linux distribution behind Ubuntu, so lack of support should not be feared.

In summary, if you do not like Unity and its way to lock the users with only a handful of configuration parameters, you can work with something else in the computing environment you know well.

### Post by Larkspur on 2011-10-23
> **mpiter said:**
> That is the best one of the recent posts.  Take an OS which does not suit your needs or taste, then pay someone to reprogram it :lolflag:.  Was it irony?  Are you really a Ubuntu forum staff member giving trustworthy pieces of advice to help people?

@azurehi, change distribution.  Usually forum staff members are more polite and more helpful than this.


Mpiter, you don't seem to be aware of one (if not the) central part of open source software. You do know there's a sizable part of Ubuntu is still written by volunteers, don't you?  I'm sure cariboo was being serious in his suggestion, and it was a good one; for example, if a coder had been motivated (either by their own preference or by being hired by an interested user) to write a patch allowing the bar to be moved, then we would have all benefited.  However, it is highly instructive to find that, despite that bug report, no such patch has appeared.

That said, your advice to azurehi is correct: if he is not happy with Unity, he could change the environment; he doesn't even have to go so far as to change distribution; Xubuntu and Lubuntu are available in the repositories, which are closer in feel to Gnome 2.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-10-23
> **Larkspur said:**
> Mpiter, you don't seem to be aware of one (if not the) central part of open source software. You do know there's a sizable part of Ubuntu still written by volunteers, don't you?  I'm sure cariboo was being serious in his suggestion, and it was a good one; if a coder had been motivated (either by their own preference or by being hired by an interested user) to write a patch allowing the bar to be moved, then we would have all benefited.  However, it is highly instructive to find that, despite that bug report, no such patch has appeared.

I am aware of everything you have written.  I myself in the past contributed to a few things to other software.

But cariboo's suggestion is totally out of place when you can use Cairo-dock that you can place where you want, when you can include in that dock classical menus, and according to other people (I have not tested it myself) it would be possible to hide the Unity dash.  Cariboo also knows that Mark Shuttleworth has strongly defended the idea of an unmovable dock locked to the left for the reading-left-to-right cultures and locked on the right-hand side for the other cultures. It is part of Unity philosophy to provide a unique experience independent of the device in use.  It is therefore very unlikely that a patch supported by Canonical permits the users to move the Unity dock. If you had read the bug report you mentioned, you would know it too.  In other words, instead of modifying Unity code to provide a patch against its philosophy, it would be quicker and more cost-effective for any user to use another dock in Unity or to switch Unity for another desktop.  I doubt that you would disagree on this. Cariboo is clever and enlightened enough to know all this.

Replying to someone who had answered a valid question (even though debated at length) to recode a part of the OS or pay someone to do it when there are so many better options is a clear lack of netiquette. It is unpleasant from anyone, but from a staff member forum team...

### Post by Larkspur on 2011-10-23
> **mpiter said:**
> It is part of Unity philosophy to provide a unique experience independent of the device in use.  It is therefore very unlikely that a patch supported by Canonical permits the users to move the Unity dock. If you had read the bug report you mentioned, you would know it too.

From the same report:

> **Mark Shuttleworth]Now, I know full well that the argument for being "on rails" can be
taken too far. It boils down to a judgement call, which has to be made
in the knowledge that wherever one draws the line (or *I* draw the line,
if you want to be personal about it) there will be some folks who never
use the flexibility offered, for whom it's confusing, and others for
whom it's not enough and who resent the line having been drawn there.
 

In all cases, it's not unreasonable to ask folks who want something
badly to implement at least a hacked up version of that capability. I
don't think it's needed, and I think it could in fact be detrimental
both to the user experience and the code itself, so I'm simply not going
to ask Canonical folks to spend any time on it. [B]But I can't stop, and
have no interest in stopping, someone from working up a patch which
implements the capability, which can then be tested and discussed.[/B]
Bleating or sulking don't inspire me to spend time and money helping out  said:**
> We may
be wrong, the best way to learn is to have others show that a better way
is possible. If so, we'll adapt quickly, we're not too proud to embrace
great work done elsewhere.[/B]
 

> **mpiter said:**
> In other words, instead of modifying Unity code to provide a patch against its philosophy, it would be quicker and more cost-effective for any user to use another dock in Unity or to switch Unity for another desktop.  I doubt that you would disagree on this. Cariboo is clever and enlightened enough to know all this.

There's no doubt it would be quicker and easier on the individual user, which is why a lot of people suggest this (both within and outside this thread); infact, on the above report, Shuttleworth suggests the same thing (I think he's suggested - ever since Unity was announced - that if the interface is not for you, there are alternatives within the 'buntu family). But Cariboo's suggestion is valid too; it just involves more work, yes, but the important part is that it benefits the community as well. Azurehi doesn't have to do it on his own; if the dislike of a static launcher bar is as widespread as that bug report suggests, then among that group must be a number of programmers who have the skills and wherewithal to create a patch.  You've no doubt read about Mate?  If there are enough people willing to work to maintain an entire UI, can't that level of expertise be directed to something as relatively minor as the placement of the bar?

> **mpiter said:**
> Replying to someone who had answered a valid question (even though debated at length) to recode a part of the OS or pay someone to do it when there are so many better options is a clear lack of netiquette. It is unpleasant from anyone, but from a staff member forum team...

azurehi doesn't seem to have taken it too badly.

### Post by dmoyes on 2011-10-24
My first reaction to Unity was that it has all the things that it has all the things that make Mac OS X and Windows horrible, and none of the things that make it good.

First, it's too different than anything out there, and it isn't immediately obvious how to use the system without exploration.  Yeah the buttons offer nice candy to look at (actually they're quite disgusting in my opinion and the Ubuntu color scheme has always made me think of barren dirt floors and the soup found in out houses), but these boxes fail provide any information on what the buttons actually do. No text, just some box with other meaningless colored boxes in it.

When you install Ubuntu you want to get to work right away. The first thought when I saw unity was "crap.. how do I start a terminal." I'm willing to bet that almost every Linux user out there has at least one terminal open at all times-- at least anyone who's been using Linux for awhile. If Canonical was smart, they would launch an x terminal right after the user logged in-- and please fix the default color scheme to the traditional Linux console colors... 

The second issue is it that it eats up screen real estate. Yes, many developers have huge screens or even multiple screens now, but I'd rather use what screen space I have to actually get work done rather than displaying a few huge buttons I'll use very infrequently.  Office 2007 when Microsoft went to ribbons had this same issue, as did earlier web browsers with gloppy function bars. Noticed a trend? Yeah they've killed the fluff and have compressed the navigation bars down significantly in recent years.

Sure, Canonical may have hired a bunch of interface design innovators, but what they really need is a bunch of usage innovators. Focus on making systems easy and FAST to use, not easy to learn to use. And of course any user interface should be highly customizable... I sorely miss FVWM and the days where you could change ANYTHING in your user interface to suit your needs.

I'll admit, I haven't bothered to even attempt to learn Unity. It simply eats up too much screen real estate and too "in your face" for me to bother with. And then there are the performance issues. Besides, I have work to do, and I'd rather not spend that time learning a completely alien interface if I don't have to.

Unity might have it's act together after 5 years of research development, but, as of right now, it is not suitable as the default desktop for Ubuntu or any distribution. There should be an option at install time to use Gnome, or Unity (ideally the installer would also give you a choice of KDE or XFCE). But that's not going to happen-- Unity is Canonical's ugly retarded baby, and they'll likely keep forcing it down our throats because they believe it is the most beautiful thing in the world. 

Until Canonical fixes their baby's genetic code and gives it some major plastic surgery, I'll be hitting CTRL-ALT-F1 and installing GNOME right after every new Ubuntu OS install... or I'll just head back to Fedora or even Slackware.

---

Something else that might make Canonical to think a little bit more wisely: [[COLOR="Blue"]http://distrowatch.com/index.php?dataspan=1[/COLOR]]("http://distrowatch.com/index.php?dataspan=1"). Move the page down a little bit till you find "Page Hit Ranking".  For all time spans of the last months, Ubuntu was the leader closely followed by Linux Mint.  Set the data span to 7 days, and you will find 3204 for Mint and 2262 for Ubuntu (not far from 50 % more for Mint).  Yesterday data was 3181 for Mint and 2337. Ubuntu is falling down day by day while Mint is doing the opposite.  Go to Linux Mint forums and see how many users claim that they have just migrated from Ubuntu to Mint, then you understand the shift in distribution hits.  It seems that since 11.10 confirming the new Ubuntu orientation, many users have been leaving Ubuntu for Mint, and the tendency is daily increasing.

### Post by shinkichi on 2011-10-28
I absolutely hate the side panel in unity--though I am biased, I have always hated menus on the side of the screen--I much prefer them on the top and the bottom. I actually don't mind the dash, though I wish it weren't the only way to get anything done. I would love it as just a searching tool--which is what it is.

The thing I dislike most about the unity interface it how it sort of attempts to make ubuntu look like an ipad, or an andoid phone or some other silly device. It's huge icons and fonts and glossy/fuzzy/transparent looks are really kind of silly--if I wanted to see that I would just get a tablet or ipad or something and be done with carting around a laptop.

Anyway, I am attempting to go back to ubuntu classic and re-customize everything: getting my cube back, and wobbly windows, and other fun pretty things, as well as making some customized themes. All the things I love most about ubuntu.

It's unfortunate Ubuntu committed such an infraction against their trusted user base by forcing them to Unity.

### Post by Clopper Almon on 2011-10-29
The Ubuntu “Unity” Disaster

Ubuntu 10.11 and previous releases provided a stable and useful desktop with space for about 100 normal-sized icon shortcuts, a panel at the top with highly useful “Applications  Places System” functions, small icons for frequently used programs, the clock, a drop-down list of available networks, the power button, and other items, while at the bottom was another panel that showed the open programs. It was a desktop for people who use computers for work. 

With 10.04, Ubuntu introduced the “Unity” desktop. It restricted the space available for shortcuts to about 10 icons and wiped out both the top and bottom panels. What did it offer instead? A button, called Dash, which enabled you to display icons (without description) for many programs, most of which are not even installed and which you certainly don't want to install. It offers a bright display of pretty icons, but fails to offer the tools and information for serious use. How anyone could think that it is an improvement I cannot imagine.

Personally, I feel betrayed. I have not only invested in learning Ubuntu Linux myself, I have also urged friends to do so. I have spent the better part of three days trying to make 11.10 usable and have failed. One might suppose that I am just an old fogey incapable of learning new tricks – I wrote machine-language code before Fortran was available – but I notice that Linus Thorwalds has the same low opinion of these latest insane user interfaces.  

I hope Ubuntu will have the good sense to return to something like the previous interface or at least to offer a choice. Otherwise, it will loose all its serious users. Until something like this is done, I will be very anxious about the future of computing.

Ubuntu could devote its considerable brainpower to urgent needs such as device support, getting up-to-date versions of programs into the repositories, or  helping the folks at LibreOffice make the many improvements which their users have asked for.  

But don't deprive us of the useful desktop we depend upon just to foist on us some glitzy plaything.

---

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2011-10-29
> **Clopper Almon said:**
> 
With 10.04, Ubuntu introduced the “Unity” desktop. It restricted the space available for shortcuts to about 10 icons and wiped out both the top and bottom panels.

Have you definitely used Unity? Firstly, the Unity desktop was introduced in 10.10, not 10.04. There is unlimited space for "shortcuts" because they stack up when there's more than fit into the available space. If you want more icons without stacking, you can make them smaller. The top panel still exists.

> I have spent the better part of three days trying to make 11.10 usable and have failed...I hope Ubuntu will have the good sense to return to something like the previous interface or at least to offer a choice.

To people unfamiliar with Unity, it takes more than three days to get your workflow optimal for Unity. Once you're used to Unity it's as quick or quicker than before.

Ubuntu DOES have the good sense to offer a choice. You can install gnome-session-fallback which is similar to the previous Gnome 2 interface. You can install KDE or Gnome Shell or XFCE or any one of half a dozen other desktop environments. As for actually going back to Gnome 2... well, I don't think going back to an obsoleted, unmaintained codebase is really a viable option.

Just because you can't get to grips with Unity in three days, doesn't mean that it doesn't work very well for other people. I for one like Unity. I like the extra space I get. I like the search functions. I like the look of it and the philosophy behind it. Don't begrudge me my choice by insisting that Ubuntu "go back to Gnome 2".

### Post by Perosteck on 2011-10-29
It's orange. And lilac. Oh please. And when you boot it up for the first time, you're greeted with large icons that are _objectively_ (I postulate this exists) ugly. Especially the LibreOffice icons situated right there in the middle of it all look pixelated and cheap. This is the first impression you get. If you make them smaller with CCSM and it's a bit less obvious. In 11.10 there is no ready way to change it. You can't install another set of icons just so, you have to copy paste and overwrite the icon folders which are hidden somewhere in the file system.

Looks are one of the most important things for human beings. If you want 200 million users, my suggestion is: Make the default more stylish. 

You can of course change the default icon set (Faenza here), but this is not the first impression.

For the record, I'm firmly in the pro-Unity camp.

---

### Post by drbisio on 2011-10-29
Hello everyone, 
I'd like to share my experience using unity with you.

At the time of its introduction, I was scared about the new interface, so initially I decided to keep my familiar gnome desktop.
Two weeks ago, I decided that I should have been more open minded, so I take courage and I decided to switch definitely to Unity.
After two weeks of evaluation I must tell that unity was not so bad in it's concept, but I've found terrible it's impossibility to be customized.
First I prefer the menu bars to be always visible in the applications windows, not hiding in the superior bar, and I think that there should be a way to decide this.
Furthermore I miss the "show desktop" shortcut, and I think that there should be a way to rearrange icons on the dash by dragging them and an easy way to add new ones by right clicking on the bar.
It's also impossible to add applets in the dash or in the desktop
At last I don't want to see in the launch menu applications that are not installed, I'd rather like the old way to group applications in many categories, as it was in the gnome menu.
I know unity is still a newborn baby, but I hope this post will help the ubuntu team to improve this new interface.

Thanks, bye,
Riccardo

### Post by mpiter on 2011-10-29

Yes, all my computers are running Mint now, probably as will the computers of my colleagues, IT, and students who are still running Ubuntu.  They usually follow my pieces of advise as far as Unix-based systems are concerned. 

We prefer to stick to the mainstream version of an OS for better support. Due to our heavy use of computers, we still need a computer-oriented interface, not an all-device-oriented one.  Furthermore, we are not beta-testers and we cannot afford to fight with our OS with each new release. These last two points led us to reject Ubuntu for Mint.  For other people such as some students who like new things to play around (like we have on smartphones) and who do not mind having a half-finished bugged interface just to explore new path, I still advise them to choose Ubuntu.

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-10-29
> **drbisio said:**
> Hello everyone, 
I'd like to share my experience using unity with you.

At the time of its introduction, I was scared about the new interface, so initially I decided to keep my familiar gnome desktop.
Two weeks ago, I decided that I should have been more open minded, so I take courage and I decided to switch definitely to Unity.
After two weeks of evaluation I must tell that unity was not so bad in it's concept, but I've found terrible it's impossibility to be customized.
First I prefer the menu bars to be always visible in the applications windows, not hiding in the superior bar, and I think that there should be a way to decide this.
Furthermore I miss the "show desktop" shortcut, and I think that there should be a way to rearrange icons on the dash by dragging them and an easy way to add new ones by right clicking on the bar.

At last I don't want to see in the launch menu applications that are not installed, I'd rather like the old way to group applications in many categories, as it was in the gnome menu.

I think most of your concerns have already been addressed in this thread, but it's HUGE, so I'll repeat some of it.  (There should probably be a more prominent user's guide - some are out there, but you have to look for them.)
The top-bar menus are controlled by the package indicator-appmenu.  Remove that package, and the old menus come back.  You can drag icons in the launcher to reorder.  You just have to drag them to the right, off of the launcher first, and then replace them where you want.  To add new programs, find it in the dash, and then just drag and drop onto the launcher.  There might be a show desktop shortcut but I don't recall it at the moment.

### Post by Mikeb85 on 2011-10-29

You just have to drag them to the right, off of the launcher first, and then replace them where you want.  To add new programs, find it in the dash, and then just drag and drop onto the launcher.  There might be a show desktop shortcut but I don't recall it at the moment.

Ctrl-Alt-D shows desktop (it was super-D in 11.04).  And you don't necessarily have to drag the launcher icons to the right, just click + hold and put them wherever..

### Post by beew on 2011-10-29
> **dsiddens said:**
> Here it is at the end of October 2011 and I'm feeling shame for installing 11.10 onto a friend's ThinkPad.  For my own it's Ubuntu 10.04 Lucid Lynx.  I guess I'm done with Ubuntu and need to start sampling other distro's.  Bummer; I've been an advocate for Ubuntu since Breezy.


Here it is only at the end of Oct so it is early. It always takes a month or so for the release to become stable and ppas to be set up etc. I wouldn't install 11.10 on anyone's machine at this stage. Given the drastic change from gnome 2 to gnome 3 I would give it another 2-3 months to iron out all the bugs. 11.04 however is solid, it has matured and is now at a very sweet spot.10.04 is just so outdated by now.

I remember when 10.04 and 10.10 first came out we heard the same hue and cry about buggy releases and people swore that they would be switching. Look what happened two months later? Everyone praised the releases as the best ever.

There is a trade off, if you want something new then there are risks that things may not work. This goes with all distros,--if you think Ubuntu releases are buggy in the first month or so wait til you try Fedora. It is just the way it is, Linux distros don't have tons of OEMS to test and fine tune their products before release and it has to get feedbacks from the users to get bugs fixed. So it is just stupid to trade in your working system for the newest right after it is released (and Canonical must share some blames for encouraging people to do just that starting with the update-manager popup, this is quite harmful to ubuntu's reputation)

 On the other hand you can always go with something like Debian stable and miss some great improvements and enhancements. It also probably won't work on newer hardware. I always stay 1/2 to 1 releases behind (meaning installing a new release 3 - 6 months later) , while installing the beta or fresh release on an external hd for testing, I never have a problem.

Lots of googling to try and get an enlarged mouse cursor and numerous other options that were available up until 11.10. Now it might as well be Windows, less choice, dumbed down interface.I am not a programmer but wouldn't it be relatively easy to update the gnome 2 desktop as an option? I run 14 machines but will be gone soon unless this changes.Nor will I encourage anyone to use Unity.I understand the goal of unity but releasing a half baked interface is really hurting the brand.I am planning to try a few more window managers to see if I can get a gnome 2 like desktop. Also in Unity the software center operates in super slow mo. I though Linux was proud of being less resource hungry than windows, is that goal gone too? If I find a solution I will post here. I know numerous people are going to mint but soon mint will go to gnome 3 also which is only slightly better than Unity and very resource hungry.

Mint will always support a fallback and never trash your setup on upgrades.  Their philosophy is if you want Gnome3, then you can choose it, but out the box is classic and will stay that way.  Look how many users have converted to Mint since Ubuntu went with Unity... they're not going to kill a good they thing (momentum).  I just went with Mint and happy as a lark now.  I'm particularly happy that Mint will always make Gnome a secondary choice and keep classic with the amazing Mint Menu as the default.

BTW, I like Gnome3.  I played around with it some, and it's cool.  I absolutely cannot stand Unity (unless I were using a Netbook or Tablet).  But for a desktop, Gnome3 wins.  But don't be confused by what I'm saying... I only played around with Gnome3.  Classic Mint Menu is my drug of choice.
```

```
### Post by cariboo on 2011-10-30
There a many posts in this thread that are infraction worthy, we decided to leave this thread open, so that everyone could have their say. If you find something offensive, report it, instead of trying to start something yourself.

As far as mint staying as it is, I wouldn't keep my hopes up, it will take a lot of resources to keep the old GTK-2 interface going, now that it is no longer in development. 

I'd much prefer seeing the effort put into keeping GTk-2 alive and kicking, put into improving the Gnome classic interface, it's quite similar to the older 2 panel interface, and with the newer GTK-3 libraries, there can be just as many if not more features added. Just because it's called gnome-session-fallback, doesn't mean it should be treated as a red-headed step child, like many users are doing.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-10-30
> **cariboo907 said:**
> As far as mint staying as it is, I wouldn't keep my hopes up, it will take a lot of resources to keep the old GTK-2 interface going, now that it is no longer in development. 

I'd much prefer seeing the effort put into keeping GTk-2 alive and kicking, put into improving the Gnome classic interface, it's quite similar to the older 2 panel interface, and with the newer GTK-3 libraries, there can be just as many if not more features added. Just because it's called gnome-session-fallback, doesn't mean it should be treated as a red-headed step child, like many users are doing.

I agree with you as Linux Mint developpers do.  From their blog ([[COLOR="Blue"]http://blog.linuxmint.com/?p=1845[/COLOR]]("http://blog.linuxmint.com/?p=1845")):
> Linux Mint 12 “Lisa” will be released in November this year with continued support for Gnome 2 but also with the introduction of Gnome 3. The radical changes introduced by the Gnome project split the community. At the time of releasing Linux Mint 11 we decided it was too early to adopt Gnome 3. This time around, the decision isn’t as simple. Gnome 3.2 is more mature and we can see the potential of this new desktop and use it to implement something that can look and behave better than anything based on Gnome 2. Of course, we’re starting from scratch and this process will take time and span across multiple releases. Until then, it’s important we continue to support the traditional Gnome 2 desktop. We’re likely to release two separate editions, one for Gnome 2.32 and one for Gnome 3.2. We’re also working in cooperation with the MATE project (which is a fork of Gnome 2) at the moment to see if we can make both desktops compatible in an effort to let you run both Gnome 2 (or MATE) and Gnome 3 on the same system, either in Linux Mint 12, or for the future.

Shuttleworth said, &#8220;This is a natural expansion of our idea as Ubuntu as Linux for human beings. As people have moved from desktop to new form factors for computing, it&#8217;s important for us to reach out to out community on these platforms. So, we&#8217;ll embrace the challenge of how to use Ubuntu on smartphones, tablets and smart-screens.&#8221;

Honestly, I have a hard time imagining it working on tablets, and especially on phones.  With no widgets and only a limited about of screen space, you'd essentially have to open an app for everything you do, the launcher wouldn't have enough real estate, and the menus would get annoying.  Dash would be painful to use without a keyboard.  

It would be nice to see a second Linux-based OS in the mobile space, but Android is so far ahead of everyone that catching up would be difficult.  Still, I wish Mr. Shuttleworth good luck.

Edit - then again just make a desktop that you can customize with shortcuts and widgets and you essentially have Android 4.0.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-10-30
> **KBD47 said:**
> [http://www.zdnet.com/blog/open-source/ubuntu-linux-heads-to-smartphones-tablets-and-smart-tvs/9834](http://www.zdnet.com/blog/open-source/ubuntu-linux-heads-to-smartphones-tablets-and-smart-tvs/9834)

I read the whole article and I really wonder where this strategy will lead. If you compare his speech to the facts of the 12 last months, there is a huge gap.

Looking at Unix-based system history, Canonical has probably created one the hugest division among its user base ever seen. This included creating a strong opposition between a non-negligible part of its user and the development team. Out of curiosity, just type "poll unity gnome" in Google.  Take the first 10 hits where there are real polls, and you will find that on average, more than 2/3 of the votes were against Unity.  After such a mess, how will Canonical really compete against IOS and Android, specially in 2014 when the phone and tablet markets will be set?  Counting on companies that will be tired of Google and Apple and that will be looking forward for something else than those two companies looks a very thin argument.

From the article:
> Shuttleworth added that Canonical can be very congenital to service partners and independent software vendors (ISV)s. With Ubuntu, “there’s plenty of room to share revenue with providers.

After Amazon scandal...
[[COLOR="Blue"]http://gburt.blogspot.com/2011/02/banshee-supporting-gnome-on-ubuntu.html[/COLOR]]("http://gburt.blogspot.com/2011/02/banshee-supporting-gnome-on-ubuntu.html")
[[COLOR="blue"]http://gburt.blogspot.com/2011/02/canonicals-new-plan-for-banshee.html[/COLOR]]("http://gburt.blogspot.com/2011/02/canonicals-new-plan-for-banshee.html")
If 25 % of the revenues for the developer and 75 % for the distributor is considered as a fair share, why not? But which developer will consider that his work worth only 25 % of the revenues?

> Finally, and this is telling, “Ubuntu has shown that we can hit deadlines and innovate. We can deliver a good plan and products.”
Such as Ubuntu 11.04 that was so bug free and so stable!

Between 2004 and 2010, Ubuntu earned a comfortable place in Linux world that was well deserved. In 2011... Do I really need to fill the blank here?

These things are said out of sadness, not out of anger as I was in June. I am not angry anymore.  We need strong Linux companies and I wish that Ubuntu will come back to something that unite people, not that divide them.

I hope Mr. Shuttleworth will consider that listeners are not all naive.  Some people analyze facts, and usually those who do it right are those who weigh in decisions.  After the analysis of the last 12 months, all computers of my labs will be washed out of Ubuntu for other distributions such as Linux Mint or Debian. We are not the only ones.  Let's hope that Mr. Shuttleworth will listen to the market and accordingly adapt his strategy.

### Post by KBD47 on 2011-10-30
I get that they want to eventually get into phones and tablets. That in itself is not a bad thing. But to expect their computer users to be willing to accept an interface that takes away features and ease of use and the ability to configure it and drink the kool-aid, and it is no surprise so much frustration.
  I think they could have handled it better. Why not be straightforward and say: We want to get into the tablet, smartphone, and other markets with our main distribution, but for those not happy with this new direction, we would encourage you to use our spin off distributions that are part of the Ubuntu family: Kubuntu, Lubuntu, Xubuntu.
  Instead they have managed to alienate users, and I cannot imagine many businesses wanting a phone app interface on their computers. You can put lipstick on this thing, you can kid yourself, but anyone doing work on a computer is going to quickly come to the conclusion that yeah, Unity on a tablet or phone might be fine, but for production on a computer it is not.
KBD47

---

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-10-30
cmcanulty, I notice that your concerns are primarily with accessibility issues.  This is probably more important (and more likely to be addressed) than the complains along the lines of 'I just don't like it'.
Since I don't make use of any accessibility features I don't have any useful suggestions, but if there are specific issues that represent a real barrier to using the interface, I would think that you'd be able to get results filing a bug report in Launchpad.  (I could be wrong, but I get the impression that the developers do care about this sort of thing.)
Have you tried going this route?  (Forgive me if you've mentioned that you have - this thread is way too long to go back and check!)  I would also suggest asking if you can enable the features you want on Ask Ubuntu.  I've found that the regulars there - people like Jorge Castro - are quite helpful, and genuinely concerned about addressing usability issues.

Why not be straightforward and say: We want to get into the tablet, smartphone, and other markets with our main distribution, but for those not happy with this new direction, we would encourage you to use our spin off distributions that are part of the Ubuntu family: Kubuntu, Lubuntu, Xubuntu.

KBD47

If you look at the bug report referenced a few pages back in this thread ('Community engagement is broken') you'll see that Mark Shuttleworth has posted a number of replies (which I think is already kind of cool - I've had Mark answer me directly on more than one report I've filed; I'm sure I'd never have received a personal reply from, say, Bill Gates, if I'd filed a Windows bug!) and he tries to explain some of the reasons for the design decisions for Unity as they affect the desktop PC.  One may disagree, but at least you can read the reasoning, and I (personally) can accept most of the reasons he gives.

I do agree however that Unity is not the best option for all use scenarios (it works well for my purposes but I can see that there are cases where it's not ideal).  In the bug report I suggested writing a page on the Ubuntu website outlining the pros and cons of each desktop environment, so that more people are aware of the choices that are available.  Maybe something like a table where various use cases are matched with the different desktop environments.  (eg: I want a traditional Windows-style setup; try Xubuntu.  My resources are limited; try Lubuntu.  I want all kinds of config options; try Kubuntu.  I want to be able to browse the web, check email, and create documents with the minimum of fuss; try Unity.)

### Post by JustinGordon on 2011-10-30
I experienced similar issues. At first, things seemed great, then it went severely bad when I kept trying to get the ati proprietary drivers to work, along with unity and compiz.

I figured, "this has got to work", and kept trying one more thing after another. I came to the following conclusions:
* Big problem having low and high power graphics from 2 vendors (intel and amd)
* Very poor HW support for the special laptop functions
* Difficult and unstable trying to configure unity on Oneiric

I've gotten to love unity on Natty, but Oneiric is a painful experience that has resulted in maybe 24 hours of work to be hard drive reformatted! It reminds me of trying to get things like laptop wireless cards and on-board sound to work 12 years ago with linux! back then the solution was to buy different HW!

I'm running Natty inside of VirtualBox and that seems far more usable than a dual boot on this laptop.

I did disable auto-switching of graphics cards. I'm hoping that was the cause of any instability with the VM.

The main disadvantage is that I can't seem to use 2 screens (laptop and external) with the virtual machine. I'm not sure of the performance difference of a VM as this 8 processor, 8gb, sandy bridge machine i7-2720M 2.20 Ghz machine is very fast.

And the ubuntu Oneiric release MUST remove the suggestion to install the proprietary ATI drivers before that causes more suffering and hatred of Ubuntu. The install process needs to do a better job of reading recent popular hardware and issuing warnings that prevent any of the driver madness related to these display drivers.

The worst part about this is having my girlfriend laugh at me, saying that Windows is so much better than Ubuntu, and how nothing works on Ubuntu!

Thanks in advance for any advice on the best setup for dual booting on this HP Pavilion dv7.

Justin

So I'm willing to believe that it's non-trivial to enable features like this.

Perhaps Vinux would be useful for you: 

Vinux is a remastered version of the Ubuntu Linux Distribution optimised for visually impaired users. It provides a screen-reader, full-screen magnification and support for Braille displays out of the box! It can be run from a Live CD without making any changes to your hard drive. If you like it you can install it to a USB pendrive or to your hard drive either alongside Windows using the Virtual Version, or as a complete replacement for windows. To find out more about the various versions of Vinux, please click on the relevant links.
We also have a wiki for many helpful articles, a support group you can join, and a Vinux blog.  These also can be accessed from the menus displayed.  We hope you enjoy your visit!

### Post by rg4w on 2011-10-31
> **robert shearer said:**
> Could be..:)
So I will repost that link you mentioned earlier....
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/882274](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/882274)
seemed to get some people thinking.
Thank you for posting that.

I read every comment there with great interest, and found most of them dismaying.  In fact, my first reaction was to simply be worried and move to Fedora; far too many posts displayed a tendency toward defensiveness that gave rise more frequently to false dichotomies than practical solutions.

But after digesting it for a while, it seemed more productive than simply giving up to instead see if there might be a way to move the discussion beyond mere opinion, by encouraging a greater role for usability testing as we all work toward honing the design for greater effectiveness.

We'll see what happens.  My C skills are merde so I can contribute little to the code base, but I would be happy to contribute what experience I can to a broader usability testing effort which takes full advantage of the human energy available in the Ubuntu community.

### Post by cmcanulty on 2011-11-02
```
