### Post by beew on 2011-11-02
> **cmcanulty said:**
> I am trying 11.10 on a junker computer with xfce desktop. More usable than gnome 3 or unity but a lot new to learn and again many loss of options. I can't figure out how to add menu apps or firefox to panel even with fiddling alot.

Install dconf-tools, invoke the dconf-editor by alt+f2, then go to desktop > Unity > panel and change the systray whitelist to ['all'] (with the brackets and quotations), then reboot.

EDIT: sorry, you were talking about xfce, I find xfce very crude. Desktop management is a joke comparing to gnome. But that is just me. I am not a panel -addict and my computers are less than 7 years old so I can run Unity comfortably. so after playing a bit with Xubuntu I say no thanks. :)

---

### Post by cmcanulty on 2011-11-04
Mint may not be an option soon either
"Linux Mint 12 “Lisa” will be released in November this year with continued support for Gnome 2 but also with the introduction of Gnome 3. The radical changes introduced by the Gnome project split the community. At the time of releasing Linux Mint 11 we decided it was too early to adopt Gnome 3. This time around, the decision isn’t as simple. Gnome 3.2 is more mature and we can see the potential of this new desktop and use it to implement something that can look and behave better than anything based on Gnome 2. Of course, we’re starting from scratch and this process will take time and span across multiple releases. Until then, it’s important we continue to support the traditional Gnome 2 desktop. We’re likely to release two separate editions, one for Gnome 2.32 and one for Gnome 3.2. We’re also working in cooperation with the MATE project (which is a fork of Gnome 2) at the moment to see if we can make both desktops compatible in an effort to let you run both Gnome 2 (or MATE) and Gnome 3 on the same system, either in Linux Mint 12, or for the future." and 
"Linux Mint 12 “Lisa” will be released in November this year with continued support for Gnome 2 but also with the introduction of Gnome 3. The radical changes introduced by the Gnome project split the community. At the time of releasing Linux Mint 11 we decided it was too early to adopt Gnome 3. This time around, the decision isn’t as simple. Gnome 3.2 is more mature and we can see the potential of this new desktop and use it to implement something that can look and behave better than anything based on Gnome 2. Of course, we’re starting from scratch and this process will take time and span across multiple releases. Until then, it’s important we continue to support the traditional Gnome 2 desktop. We’re likely to release two separate editions, one for Gnome 2.32 and one for Gnome 3.2. We’re also working in cooperation with the MATE project (which is a fork of Gnome 2) at the moment to see if we can make both desktops compatible in an effort to let you run both Gnome 2 (or MATE) and Gnome 3 on the same system, either in Linux Mint 12, or for the future. "

Gnbome 3 would be fine if they just made a useful desktop but so far it is worse than unity. Updates should add functionality not subtract.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-11-04
> **cmcanulty said:**
> Mint may not be an option soon either

My understanding as a new Mint user is that we will still have Gnome 2.32 for at least the next 6 months, maybe the next 12.  During that period, Gnome 3 is going to evolve including more user settings.  Just check the gnome-tweak-tool and how many grey options the program has.  I assume these grey unavailable options will be available soon.  Other options will probably be available too because the community works on it.  See how after a year, someone brought a tool to move the Unity dash, imperfect today but probably better in a few months. You can bet it will be the same with Gnome 3 which is already more configurable thanks to gnome-tweak-tool. So you can upgrade Mint till Gnome 2.32 becomes unavailable, then keep your current version till Gnome 3 satisfies your needs.  Since Mint evolution is more user-oriented than Ubuntu according to user claims, it can safely be assumed that user options will always be available. Migrating from Ubuntu to Mint is very easy today.  If it can keep you happy, just do it.  According to your previous posts, you would have saved time doing it earlier instead of trying to tweak a desktop which is not meant to be changed.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-11-04
> **beew said:**
> But now it seems that you have a bunch of panicky people who hate Unity just because it doesn't look like windows 95 and the tone of the recent critics has become quite irrational.

I have been using computers since the Sinclair Spectrum in 1982, and almost exclusively Unix-based systems since 1988.  This includes several years administrating Unix-based systems in the beginning of the 90's.  In Unix world, I do not remember a fuzz as big as what we have seen with Ubuntu switch to Unity.  And believe me, in 30 years I have seen changes much deeper than the Unity change.  But they were for better, not for worst. If what you said was true, no progress would be possible: No multiple workspace desktop, no Compiz, no Mac OSX, no IOS, no Android, no smartphones, no tablets...would have been possible. People can try to reassure themselves with the "afraid of changes" argument, it might explain a few things but not the tenth of what we have seen. Do not confuse change and regression.

---

### Post by beew on 2011-11-04
> **cmcanulty said:**
> No I hate it because many options are removed and many tasks take many more clicks. Also on our library public machines I need evereything to be easily visable and can't have min max etc buttons and menus that you have to screw around to find.

The global menu can be removed with one simple command > sudo apt-get remove indicator-appmenu Reboot after that the max/min buttons return to the  application windows like it was.This is the first thing I do when I install Ubuntu.

If you like point and click the gnome2 way you can simply install a classic menu, you even have two choices.
[http://www.webupd8.org/2011/06/use-classic-menu-in-unity-classicmenu.html](http://www.webupd8.org/2011/06/use-classic-menu-in-unity-classicmenu.html)

If you want the tray icons back, install dconf-tools, invoke the dconf-editor with alt+f2 (or use the classic menu thingy if you prefer) the command is simply "dconf-editor" (without quotes), go to desktop > Unity > panel and change the systray whitlist to ['all'] (with the brackets and quotations) 

Now you can even move the Unity launcher bar to the bottom
[http://www.webupd8.org/2011/10/how-to-move-unity-launcher-to-bottom-of.html](http://www.webupd8.org/2011/10/how-to-move-unity-launcher-to-bottom-of.html)

So all your problems are solved.

Now in 11.10 a lot of options are removed but that is the fault of gnome3, not Unity. Actually Canonical seems to have restore some of those features. You don't find them in Fedora's version of gnome shell (not without more work anyway)

### Post by beew on 2011-11-04
> **mpiter said:**
> People can try to reassure themselves with the "afraid of changes" argument, it might explain a few things but not the tenth of what we have seen. Do not confuse change and regression.

No, that is not an argument, just an observation. I used to hate people using that as an argument too (and still do) Certainly I would not classify everyone who has a problem with the new UI as such, but lately the complaints are getting ridiculous, I feel a difference in tone and substance in the recent wave of complaints comparing to those in the early days of 11.04. 

In those days many criticisms were well thought out and were made by people who had actually used Unity for a period of time and decided that some of their needs  were not met (now many of those complaints have been addressed, if by third party developers instead of Canonical)

Now you have many people who have not used Unity at any length and just complain, and complain hysterically because well.. they don't like a change of interface,--and that is basically all they tell you. So you have people stampeding to xfce and LXDE even though those DE are actually vastly inferior to Unity in terms of ease of use and configurability.--try something as simple as cutting and pasting into the desktop or hiding unused launcher icons in xfce, for example. The only reason they switch is because of the familiar windows 95-ish look.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-11-04
2. Unity's integration with the window manager gives it a number of perks over a simply dock and panel layout. Dragging and dropping between maximized windows, for instance, which no other arrangement is very good at, and the extra screen space saved by essentially making maximized windows run fullscreen, are major perks. Customization options are themselves arguable in value - when they conflict, they can lead to serious problems, and favoring a single, coherent workflow over a grab-bag of parts the user is expected to put together himself or herself is actually very valuable to the average user.

Honestly, however, I wouldn't use Unity in your case at all. As of 11.10, Cairo Dock installs a separate session all its own. It also includes an indicator tray (which you may need to activate, as I haven't tried it) and the dock itself.

1. If dragging and dropping icons to the desktop doesn't work, install gnome-tweak-tool and ensure that "Have file manager manage the desktop" is switched on under Desktop.

3. Under Unity, you'd use the Launcher, since it's the new taskbar. I find it slow and troublesome.

Under your Cairo Dock session, use Compiz to enable Scale as an upper-left hot corner for Activities-style task switching. You will not, obviously, be able to launch applications or switch workspaces from this view, but I've used it for years and found it extraordinarily useful for switching tasks.

4. The global menu's availability on the second monitor is a good idea for exactly the same reason that the Launcher's absence is infuriating. I'm not certain what to add to that. However, when I use a second monitor, I usually start everything on the main display and move specific windows over to the right. Obviously, Shell is much better designed for this. You might set a keystroke or right hotcorner to Scale's "Show Windows on Current Output" to help in task switching for the second display.

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-11-04
Another option for switching tasks in Unity with the mouse is to modify the scale plugin. By default, Super+W initiates the scale plugin for all windows (on all workspaces) and Shift+Alt+Up initiates it on the current workspace. I tend to use the keyboard (as I frequently have to type things, like this post) so that works well for me. But you can assign hot spots on the desktop for this as well, so that (for example) moving the cursor to the bottom right will show you all open windows and let you choose one.
I suggested bottom-right since that corner is least likely to be hit accidentally in the current setup. Whether you want to show all workspaces or the current one is entirely up to you.
As for the global menu, I'm afraid removing the package is the only way to do it. The same is true to some extent of most of the indicators in the panel: add/remove=install/uninstall. I suppose you can handle it by 'checking a box' if said box is the one in Synaptic next to the package name. :-)

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-11-05
Heh... ironically (given some of the complains in this thread) compiz has too MANY options! It probably needs to be cleaned up, and some of the plugins retired, to remove the possibility for conflicts that can break your display. (On the other hand, I like being able to turn off the default Alt+Tab switcher in Unity and replace it by the shift switcher. ### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-11-05
You can also tap Super, which I've honestly started to think of as the "Shell key." I've been using that for most of my window switching on the second display.

dsfitzpat, I agree that Compiz is overloaded with plugins, but even if there was some sense to what's included (instead of just a collection of every plugin someone's happened to write) the settings manager needs some work in sorting them out. For instance, I'd say that the Static Application Switcher really ought to be an option within the Application Switcher, even if they're still separate plugins, which they shouldn't be in the first place. Ditto Viewport Switcher and Wall, Scale and Scale Add-ons, Animations and Animations Add-ons, Cube, Cube Deformations, 3D Windows, and Rotate Cube, etc.

23dornot23d, while there's no way to *toggle* the global menu, I think you can disable it for individual apps. Try [this]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=11421231").

### Post by markbl on 2011-11-05
> **dsfitzpat said:**
> By the way, isn't a hot spot essentially how Gnome-shell handles window management? I haven't tried it in a few months (since it stopped working for anyone with AMD graphics) but as I recall, to do almost anything you had to move the cursor to the top-left.
As somebody says above, you just need to press the super key, very easy. What's nice about gnome-shell is that this key is overloaded. So you press the key (or hit the hot-spot) and you see your expose desktop, your workspace overview on the right, and your quick app launcher on the left. If you now start typing then gnome-shell assumes a "dash" search for applications. So that one simple quick key does all these things that Unity requires multiple contorted key presses for. Just makes more sense to me and easier for newbies to pick up. Since the launcher only appears when you press the super key, or the hot-spot, then you don't get the issues with auto disappear/popout of launcher , or it overlaying your application window, etc that annoy me in Unity.

---

### Post by mpiter on 2011-11-05
> **markbl said:**
> As somebody says above, you just need to press the super key, very easy. What's nice about gnome-shell is that this key is overloaded. So you press the key (or hit the hot-spot) and you see your expose desktop, your workspace overview on the right, and your quick app launcher on the left. If you now start typing then gnome-shell assumes a "dash" search for applications. So that one simple quick key does all these things that Unity requires multiple contorted key presses for. Just makes more sense to me and easier for newbies to pick up. Since the launcher only appears when you press the super key, or the hot-spot, then you don't get the issues with auto disappear/popout of launcher , or it overlaying your application window, etc that annoy me in Unity.

Even with my 23 years of experience with Unix-based systems, I needed a tutorial to do the basic with Unity.  Maybe I am just stupid. With Gnome Shell I configured in one hour what I wanted and I was able to satisfyingly use it without reading anything. Even after using Unity for a while with the help of tutorials and forum answers from Unity defenders, I think it clumsy and poorly designed in comparison to Gnome Shell when you have many windows spread through several workspaces. Of course, this is a personal experience coming from a personal way of thinking.  It does not mean that Unity is bad for everyone, but I think hard to consider it better designed than Gnome Shell for beginners.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-11-05
> **irv said:**
> By setting the Decoration plugins fixed the windows problem but not the workspace. I still can't get more then one workspace.
By setting the cube, and Rotate Cube I can flip one desktop. I do not have a cube. Am I missing something else?. This is in Mint that I am doing this.

Edit: I tried this in Unity and I have the same problem.

Check the Desktop Size value of the General Option plugin. The default value is strange considering the long cube tradition in Compiz since Beryl.  It is set to 2 for both Horizontal Virtual Size and Vertical Virtual Size. This suits a 2x2 framework organization, but not a cube organization.  If it is the case for you, change it to 4 for Horizontal and 1 for Vertical.  You will find your cube back.  Presently, the whole default setting for Compiz is just trash.  Disable Unity and enable your cube, then you lose all your settings and you cannot even move or resize a window.  I hope that it will solve your problem.

---

### Post by Mikeb85 on 2011-11-06
> **dgrafix said:**
> 
I mean who thought this was a nice out of the box interface? Where are -all- the system options? I managed to find half of them on a mammoth sized cog icon but where are the rest?.  Where is the terminal? (i had to launch it manually with alt-f2->gnome-terminal in the end).
Anyone who has been using Ubuntu for a long time should not even have to look for  this.


Mark Shuttleworth and Canonical thought it up.  Although it's similar to the direction the Gnome team went with Gnome 3.  

Open up dash (the top icon on the launcher, you can also use the -super- key), and type in either 'system' or 'settings'.  It will bring up all the settings for various programs and your system.  To find the terminal, either ctrl-alt-t or bring up the dash, and type 'terminal' (actually once you hit the t button it's the first option).  

Once you get familiar with dash, opening an app is as simple as hitting -super-, the first few letters of an app, then -enter-.  You don't ever need to touch a mouse, go through a menu, etc... to open an app.  For instance, to open Firefox, just type in -super- fir -enter-, and you're in Firefox.  For Chrome, I just -super- ch -enter-, and it's open.  

The idea is that you put your most used apps on the launcher (dock on the side), and the apps you don't need everyday (I doubt anyone wants to mess with system settings every single day) stay hidden.  Keeps things nice and clean, so you don't need to go through menus all the time.  Programs you want to run in the background (like music, weather app, notes, Skype, messaging apps, etc...) run in the global menu (bar up top) so you can always access them without having to have a window open.  

It's a huge change no doubt, but it works very well if you put the time in to learn it (also works for the newbies who only know how to click on a single icon on the launcher...).  It took me a good week to learn to effectively use it (I was used to Windows and spent some time on Gnome 2), but my girlfriend can use it without any instruction (although she only uses a few apps and doesn't do anything beyond that).

### Post by IWantFroyo on 2011-11-06
> **Megaptera said:**
> Hi dgrafix,
Why not just stick with 10.04 LTS and a familiar supported & regularly updated Ubuntu?
Less strssful and let things work themselves out....

Huge +1.

Ubuntu 10.04 will be supported until April 2013. It's nice, stable, purple, and has Gnome 2. Also, it doesn't spring any surprises on you.

```
sudo apt-get install ubuntu-fallback-session
```

or

```
sudo apt-get install gnome-shell
```

The second command will install Gnome. It won't be the Gnome you know and love, but it'll show you what Gnome is like now.

### Post by beew on 2011-11-06
> **Megaptera said:**
> Hi dgrafix,
Why not just stick with 10.04 LTS and a familiar supported & regularly updated Ubuntu?
Less strssful and let things work themselves out....

LTSes are not regularly updated unless you have a lot of ppas, but because of the recent changes many newer versions of softwares are not available to 10.04 even through ppas (and in general they dry up after a while as libraries become incompatible, maintainers move on to newer versions etc). So who cares if it is "supported" if your softwares are lacking in new features which could be important and if they are broken they will never get fixed? I am using Natty and Maverick now (will swap it for Natty soon when I get around to) Both are much faster, smoother and more reliable than 10.04.

I think there is absolutely no reason to recommend LTS over regular release unless you run a big company or something. It is the most over-rated concept around here. I use the LTS just like any other release, I add a lot of ppas and update to a newer version in a year or so and always stay 1/2 to 1 release behind to avoid buggy new releases.

@OP

I am not sure what to make of your post. If you are  doing a fresh install you would have to boot into a live usb or CD to install. Since you are an experienced user should you at least press "Try Ubuntu" just to check if the hardware works before you install right the way? If you did you would have seen the new UI before you actually install and that wouldn't even require any extra research.

If you don't like the new UI, and also for stability reasons I would recommend 11.04 with the classic desktop. 11.10 is too fresh regardless of UI, many things are broken, options have disappeared, softwares haven't been properly ported to gtk3 etc,  it is not ready yet and probably won't be for quite a while.

> **IWantFroyo said:**
> Huge +1.

Ubuntu 10.04 will be supported until April 2013. It's nice, stable, purple, and has Gnome 2. Also, it doesn't spring any surprises on you.

```
sudo apt-get install ubuntu-fallback-session
```

or

```
sudo apt-get install gnome-shell
```

```
The second command will install Gnome
```. It won't be the Gnome you know and love, but it'll show you what Gnome is like now.

it will install gnome shell, Gnome is already installed it is Gnome 3 with Unity as a shell by default.

The changes are due to Gnome not canonical.

### Post by sdf1984 on 2011-11-06
I think the biggest travesty that has come out of this Unity and GNOME 3 issue, is the people who have heard for years that Linux is a good alternative to Microsoft Windows, and finally after installing Ubuntu, they discover these new desktops are nothing even remotely similar to what they are used to, so they go back to Windows and tell their friends how crazy that Linux is, they can't make their way around in this completely foreign environment. Although Gnome 2 was different it was not so busy and tabletized. Ubuntu should have kept Unity for tablets and net-books like the started with.
I have gone back to Linux mint 11 with their great menu system and control tools, until 12 comes out. At least they are giving us a choice of environments. Linux Mint will be adding not only their own famous desktop improvements to Gnome 3 which will leave all of the good parts while adding a familiar look we are accustomed to, but they are adding as an option the Gnome 2 fork called Mate. Oh happy days.

---

### Post by bowens44 on 2011-11-06
Now that 11.10 has been released I decided to give it another try.

As as with 11.04 , I find it to be completely unusable. It seems as if the interface was designed to purposely prevent the computer from being used in a productive and intuitive manner. 

The buttons on left side of the window (granted this came about before unity), the global menus(by far the worst 'feature' of unity) ,the overlay scroll bars,  the menu system that is nearly impossible to navigate, the launcher stuck to the left hand side of the screen, the giant icons apparently designed for the visually impaired and next to no customization all work together to provide an irritating and frustrating user experience. 


Everything that was good about ubuntu up until 10.10 (10.10 was the closest thing to aa perfect operating system I have ever used) seems to have been taken away.

If unity was the only option available for linux, I would go back to windows without hesitation.

Right now I'm using linux Mint 11 on my primary machine. It is based on Ubuntu 11.04 but doesn't have unity (what a starnge name for an interface that is driving so many users away).

### Post by MarjaE on 2011-11-06
> **Mikeb85 said:**
> Install CompizConfig Settings Manager, then you'll find a bunch of options (you can resize the icons, autohide the launcher, etc...) for the launcher under 'Ubuntu Unity Plug-in'.

If something is so necessary, shouldn't it be included by default?

A few other issues:

Window switching. Gnome 2 makes this easy. Unity, in my experience, made this hard.

Visible-on-mouseover features. Sorry, these are bugs. At best, these are slow, and unreliable, but can be disabled. At worst, they can't be disabled.

Keyboard shortcuts. Okay, these have their place, but some users don't use keyboard shortcuts all the time, and some of us can't use the keyboard for everything due to arm/hand injuries, so keyboard shortcuts can be nice options, but they shouldn't be necessities.

And in Xubuntu I had to install three additional packages to disable the touchpad. In my experience, it's a good example of a system which seemed right for the big things, and worked smoothly on the live cd, but wouldn't install properly, and ended up with malfunctions on every little thing... it was just as frustrating as Unity and drove me back to Gnome 2.

### Post by MarjaE on 2011-11-06
> **Mikeb85 said:**
> Mark Shuttleworth and Canonical thought it up.  Although it's similar to the direction the Gnome team went with Gnome 3.

One of the more frustrating things about Ubuntu these days. I switched back to Classic in 11.04 because both Unity and Gnome 3 rely on features/bugs that just get in the way and drop features that I use all the time. Like the window-switching panel in Gnome 2. I tried Xubuntu and like some features, but the installation cd crapped out partway through, and although I managed to get some kind of installation, I never could fix the bugs. Also Xubuntu has no built-in touchpad support. *facepalm* I can't use the computer unless I can keep the touchpad from interfering with typing!

> Open up dash (the top icon on the launcher, you can also use the -super- key), and type in either 'system' or 'settings'.  It will bring up all the settings for various programs and your system.  To find the terminal, either ctrl-alt-t or bring up the dash, and type 'terminal' (actually once you hit the t button it's the first option).

What is this -super- key?

> Once you get familiar with dash, opening an app is as simple as hitting -super-, the first few letters of an app, then -enter-.  You don't ever need to touch a mouse, go through a menu, etc... to open an app.  For instance, to open Firefox, just type in -super- fir -enter-, and you're in Firefox.  For Chrome, I just -super- ch -enter-, and it's open.

In other words, as easy as jumping to the keyboard. I have an old arm injury and it hurts to type this reply.

> The idea is that you put your most used apps on the launcher (dock on the side), and the apps you don't need everyday (I doubt anyone wants to mess with system settings every single day) stay hidden.

And the apps I use every day weren't in the launcher when I tried Unity, and many apps I never use were there. It doesn't exactly work as intended in this regard.

> Keeps things nice and clean, so you don't need to go through menus all the time.  Programs you want to run in the background (like music, weather app, notes, Skype, messaging apps, etc...) run in the global menu (bar up top) so you can always access them without having to have a window open.

Except that the launcher itself is ugly, and hard to use, and it is visible-on-mouseover by default. Now maybe on some machines visible-on-mouseover works fine, but in this machine, it is too slow and too unpredictable.

> It's a huge change no doubt, but it works very well if you put the time in to learn it (also works for the newbies who only know how to click on a single icon on the launcher...).  It took me a good week to learn to effectively use it (I was used to Windows and spent some time on Gnome 2), but my girlfriend can use it without any instruction (although she only uses a few apps and doesn't do anything beyond that).

### Post by beew on 2011-11-06
> It seems as though for me and others the panels and menus are the huge  issue. I am not a programmer so excuse me if this is a dumb question. Is  it that hard to add panels and menus like in classic gnome to unity?I  would think they just need upgrading to run in gnome 3 as they do in  gnome 2?
What do you use the panel for? If it is just for system tray icons you can do it by whitelisting all applications in the dconf-editor. Many panel applets are available for Unity via ppas.

You can install a classical style menu in Unity.
[http://www.webupd8.org/2011/06/use-classic-menu-in-unity-classicmenu.html](http://www.webupd8.org/2011/06/use-classic-menu-in-unity-classicmenu.html)

There are two options, one is on the top panel, the other one is on the Unity launcher. See VinDSL's screenshots for the menu on the top panel.

So I really don't see why it is such a big deal. I am more concerned about hardware and software compatibility in the process of moving to gnome3 (not the shell) than the silly panel.

---

### Post by mpiter on 2011-11-07
> **cmcanulty said:**
> That is certainly good news. The other issues I have with Unity is the software center runs at about 10% of the speed it did in 11.04 and the black background and tiny disappearing max, min, close buttons. Also no way to enlarge cursor or make it black.That is in gnome tweak but doesn't work.I will continue to try Ubuntu Unity but lately Mint 11 looks so much easier to see and navigate.

I think very odd how people fight so hard to keep Ubuntu on their computers at such a high cost when other distributions fulfill their needs much better.  It reminds me of some Mac OSX users.

From [[COLOR="Blue"]http://www.zdnet.com/blog/open-source/ubuntu-linux-heads-to-smartphones-tablets-and-smart-tvs/9834?tag=nl.e539[/COLOR]]("http://www.zdnet.com/blog/open-source/ubuntu-linux-heads-to-smartphones-tablets-and-smart-tvs/9834?tag=nl.e539"), it is obvious that Ubuntu mainstream is going to be an OS running both on tablet/smartphone and computer worlds. Mark Shuttleworth believes (maybe rightly) that it will suit a large user majority.  The default settings have already been chosen for very small screen, i.e., the tablet/smartphone world though no distribution dedicated to that world will be available before 2014 at best. It is also obvious that Mark Shuttleworth wants to provide an OS with a basic interface not very configurable, leaving most user choices through poor interfaces such package managing or difficult-to-use tools such as dconf-editor. It is a philosophy choice to keep everyone in approximately the same easy-to-maintain interface independent of the device.

On the other hand, from [[COLOR="blue"]http://blog.linuxmint.com/?p=1851#comments[/COLOR]]("http://blog.linuxmint.com/?p=1851#comments"), it is obvious that Linux Mint has become the Ubuntu's cousin dedicated to user choices. You have already experienced yourself than less than a day is sufficient to migrate from Ubuntu to Linux Mint and to set it according to your needs to provide a working tool.  You have already been spending more time on trying to tweak Unity without being able to set a working tool for your needs because Unity is not meant to users such as you. So why do you keep trying to use a screwdriver to put a nail in a wall when you have a hammer at hand?

In summary, if you want an all-device OS with a minimum set of configuration tools to have the same thing on all your devices, stick to Ubuntu and support that distribution with the other market users aimed by Mr. Shuttleworth. If you do not belong to that category and if you prefer a computer-oriented OS dedicated to user choices, stick to Linux Mint and support that distribution. There will probably be two distinctive "Ubuntu" markets. Choose wisely which one you want to support.

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2011-11-07
> **dgrafix said:**
> You still just don't get it. 
It is not just about -me- it is because I care about how my favorite OS is received and for it's future, and in my opinion this is not intuitive or easy for beginners, very confusing for someone who wants to migrate from windows, and frustrating to people like me.

As a power user, it took me a few days to get used to it. This was when 11.04 was still an alpha. Unity has all the goodness of Compiz's keyboard shortcuts.

My girlfriend really isn't very computer literate. She can write a document in Microsoft Office, but until recently she had no idea how to use Powerpoint. She could never, say, set up a printer in Windows, or format a disk, without me showing her several times (she has a medical memory problem too).

But... she uses my computer. I don't know why, she's got a Windows 7 computer she uses for her assignments. But she finds her way around Unity well enough to play music and use Chromium. I'm not sure she could find programs that are not already in my Launcher, but that's because she's not familiar with the word "App" as used in the Dash Home.

Basically, new Ubuntu users who are somewhat familiar with Windows don't have much trouble with Unity.

I get somewhat annoyed when Ubuntu users complain about changes. Why use a fast-paced, six-month-cadence, cutting-edge distro and then complain when things change? Linux changes at a tremendous rate; since Ubuntu's appearance in 2004 things have changed very quickly, and probably even before then.

You could argue that the Unity change is the biggest change yet and warrants discussion. However, don't you remember the mass exodus that was going to happen when Ubuntu made the shocking decision to change the colour of the default wallpaper and move three buttons to the other side of the window? I do. There have been similar complaints against Ubuntu when **upstream** changed the Control-Alt-Backspace combination to Alt-SysRq-K, when Pulseaudio was included*, when Network Manager was included, when Ubuntu added social networking tools to the default install, when Ubuntu removed Gimp from the default install, when Ubuntu moved from Xsplash to Plymouth  for its splash screen, when **upstream Gnome** rewrote GDM, and when Software Center started selling software. Big complaints over tiny issues.

*It was a little half-baked in early Ubuntu implementations, I'll admit, but the complaints continued until long after the issues were fixed. Especially amongst people who got into the habit of removing Pulseaudio, and then started complaining that removing part of their sound system actually caused their sound to stop working. Gee, who would have thought?!

### Post by thatguruguy on 2011-11-07
There's some really simple math involved in the creation of Unity and Gnome Shell.

Linux usage on the desktop has hovered around 1% for the past several years.  You can claim that it's Microsoft's fault, or Apple's fault, or some basic failing in human nature, but the fact is that Linux (GNU/Linux, for those of you who insist upon using that) hasn't achieved significant market penetration.  Nothing that Canonical, the Gnome devs, Richard Stallman or Linus Torvalds does is going to change Apple, Microsoft or human nature.  What ***can*** be changed is the product that is being offered the public.

Usability testing has shown that people not familiar with Linux like the Unity interface.  They are the target audience.  And maybe they should be, since it's the only way to move beyond the 1% ceiling.

If you're a dev and you think that your personal preferences are more important than the preferences of the public, you need to reconsider your approach to what you're doing.

I get that it's possible that Ubuntu (as well as any distribution which uses either Unity or Gnome Shell) will lose some people who prefer the "old" way of doing things.  If, in exchange, they gain new people who never would have tried Linux otherwise, then that still scores as a win.

There's an old saying, frequently attributed to Albert Einstein, that "Insanity is doing the same thing, the same way, over and over and expecting different results." The way that things have been done hasn't achieved the desired results (namely, broader usage of Linux on the desktop).  And there's no reason to believe, after all this time, that continuing to do things the same way will suddenly achieve different results.

Unity might not be the ultimate answer. Gnome Shell may not be the ultimate answer. But there's no question that Gnome 2 and the desktops based on it weren't the answer, and it's time to try something new.

---

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-11-08
As mentioned a few times in this thread, both Cardapio and classicmenu-indicator provide the old style menus. You can also install the gnome fallback session, configure the panels in that environment to get only a bottom panel with the applets you want (menu, task manager, etc), and then run gnome-panel in a unity session.
Someone was working on a nested dash configured along the lines of the classic menu; I'm not sure where that's at these days. (i.e. first click on the dash would show the application categories, clicking on one of those would show the applications in that category.)

### Post by Crempel on 2011-11-09
OK, one way or the other this topic has been touched on, although never as a straight forward question:

Which of the two do you prefer, now that it is quite simple to install Gnome-shell in Oneiric and then just switch between it and Unity.

When Natty came along I was somewhat impressed with Unity, actually I got to like it. Couldn't really install Gnome-shell because of the conflict between Gnome 2 (Unity) and Gnome 3 (Shell).

Now it's all simple and Gnome 3 rules - and: after some two weeks of working with it, I prefer the Shell. Both desktops follow similar philosophies, but the Gnome-Shell is more elegant (ok, I love eye candy), more responsive, more intuitive IMHO, and with a few add-ons at least as configurable (or non-configurable compared with some other desktops as enemies would say), and it is the desktop that all other (non-KDE distros) will choose.

I DO NOT wish to start a flame war like KDE 4 vs Gnome; just some friendly exchange of opinions would be welcome.:)

---

### Post by HDave on 2011-11-09
OK -- I've recently upgraded from 10.04 to 11.10.  I've heard so many bad things about unity, but I was (and am) determined to give it a try.  I've been using Ubuntu exclusively for 4+ years and have watched is slowly getting better and better.  

I keep a list of tweaks for each release of Ubuntu and that list has been getting shorter and shorter over time. Version 7.04 was 7+ pages long...and 10.04 was barely 1 page.

No question but that 11.10 was a step backwards in reliability as my list of tweaks is back up to two pages.  Regardless I am determined to try it out for a long while and see what I really think.  So far I have to say that even though it is VERY different, I am starting to like it.  A big part of this attraction is because I was able to hack numerous changes that were critical to me (all of these issues have been file on launchpad by myself or others):

1) uninstall ubuntu one -- I use dropbox and found that UbuntuOne being everywhere was annoying and a distraction
2) make the launcher always be there -- the hiding launcher was very buggy and ended up wasting my time because I couldn't aim the mouse until after it appeared.  Making the launcher always be there was very tricky.
3) Unity 2d vs 3d -- its not obvious which one you are running and in my case I was running 2d even though I logged in as 3d.  My nVidia card was silently blacklisted.
4) remove global menu -- this part is the worst (IMO) and it was dissappointing that there is no easy way to remove this "feature"..though it can be removed.
5) button to see/switch workspaces - the launcher workspaces button is badly broken and underpowered
6) show desktop - lack of a show desktop button was a real problem, though I eventually found a great hack to do that.
7) weather and system monitor indicators -- took my forever to learn about these...had to believe they are not installed by default
8 ) removing my name - removing the session indicator with my name on it also took some figuring out

So on the one hand you could say "Nice success story...thats Linux...very customizable... you ended up with the perfect system."

But I say I am deeply concerned.  These issues I've listed above are not 'obscure' or 'edge cases'...its crazy that Unity doesn't have these things.  There is no way a regular computer user would have any chance of making any of these tweaks!!   It took me 3 days to sort these things out.  Three days spent screwing around instead of working productively.  It got me thinking long and hard about Fedora, Suse, Mint, etc.  

I manage dozens of these systems, I've put my company on Ubuntu, I've put numerous friends/family members on Ubuntu.  I can't do this anymore if Canonical is going in another direction.  Yes I can switch to Kubuntu, but I preferred the simplicity and stability of Gnome...and I feel like KDE will always be a second class citizen here. 

I'd like to believe that Ubuntu will always be focused on desktop computing, but the total lack of solid support for desktops, workspaces, and configurations...along with the reluctance to address core issues is concerning.  Really its this reluctance that is the worst part.

It amazes me how much push-back there is on adding an option for a simple "Show Desktop" button to the launcher.  I feel like this is a symptom of the fact that Android/iOS tablet PCs don't have a desktop and so Ubuntu won't have one soon either.  I honestly believe that Ubuntu is leaving the desktop market to go chase the emerging tablet PC market.  This leaves me out in the cold.

---

### Post by trivialpackets on 2011-11-09
I'm pretty sure this was asked directly at one point, but for me, it's such a tossup.  

Gnome-shell seems more responsive, but what I like about unity is that unity-2d exists.  I work remotely via NX and if I stick with unity, then I can have virtually the same interface in both places, which I like.  

I think that gnome-shell seems like it's closer to where it needs to be, but I think that the problem I have is that neither one seemlessly meshes with my workflow.  I'm working on that, as I really want to like them.  I like a lot of what ubuntu is doing.  I like the integration of social, plus the integration of ubuntuone.  I also like quickly for development, makes me want to give some focus to python when I get to release my rails app that I'm working on.  

They both have pros and cons, and for me, the jury is still out.

---

### Post by Handssolow on 2011-11-09
It's flimsy really but I don't like Unity for my desktop PC because I prefer drop down hierarchical menus rather then program launchers cluttering up my desktop on the left or on the bottom tool-bar. I want to preserve as much of my desktop area for my files rather than have it taken over by launchers which I don't frequently use, like the Libre suite for example. With the gnome-session-fallback package I can create small neat Firefox and Evolution launchers on the top toolbar, like I want. I own my desktop area.

So why all the fuss? It's about appearance isn't it. Why not make Unity so users can configure it the way they like instead of imposing a straight jacket, supposedly to make it more attractive to catch non-Ubuntu users with tablets and iPhones etc.

Or have I got it wrong?

---

### Post by robert shearer on 2011-11-09
Shuttleworth seems to view the future of the computer as being a truly personal and portable device...
from [https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/882274](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/882274)

Mark Shuttleworth (sabdfl) wrote : #74
> No. What's happening is that the new form factors are being integrated
into Ubuntu, just as they will be integrated into MacOS and Windows.
We'd just like to be ahead of the curve. Some day, you will be able to
carry a phone, and dock it to a keyboard, mouse and display and use it
as a full desktop with all your apps and data. Or use it as a tablet, in
a different dock.

Mark 

Thus a universal layout scaled to a smartphone that  scales to a tablet or desktop makes a lot of sense.
Why have to reconfigure a portable device each time you dock it just to get your preferred desktop when one universal one <Unity> will work for mobile,desktop,tablet and wherever you may be..?
Your computer will be with you 24/7 and becomes a desktop/tablet etc depending where you dock it. 
Multiple form factors as discrete units will then be superfluous.

Quite why the desktop is being locked for such a device so far ahead of the actual devices appearance **is** confusing though.

If this were Apple then I could envisage the device being worked on for release with the fully functional and tested O/S, but as Canonical don't seem to have a manufacturing capability then it seems both visionary and fingers crossed hopefulness.

Shrug, perhaps Canonical are in conversation with outside manufacturers and a device will arrive fully formed and explain the tortuous road we currently find ourselves on.

I certainly hope so and look forward to that eventuality.

### Post by Billisnice on 2011-11-09
The main grips i have with unity are:

1) no visual clue of what programs are open or closed. If i go to the left ribbon i have to scan up and down. It distracts my eyes. Lots of extra steps. Classic mode i could see in the bottom panel without thinking what is open and closed without all the ribbon icons distracting my thoughts. I could use my eyes with moving the mouse around. A more natural feel.

2) The dash home is so confusing. 

My 10.04 remax on my dell mini 9 is fantastic. I see the programs opens top left. If i have three programs open I see three icons and the ones not in use grayed out. Not all the left ribbon clutter. The upper left button open the menus without the grey out clutter or have to search and dig as much. A photo of the 10.04 remax is at [http://linux.softpedia.com/progScreenshots/Ubuntu-Netbook-Remix-Screenshot-42748.html](http://linux.softpedia.com/progScreenshots/Ubuntu-Netbook-Remix-Screenshot-42748.html)


I started with Ubuntu 4.10 (Warty Warthog)way back when.

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-11-10
> **Billisnice said:**
> The main grips i have with unity are:

1) no visual clue of what programs are open or closed. If i go to the left ribbon i have to scan up and down. It distracts my eyes. Lots of extra steps. Classic mode i could see in the bottom panel without thinking what is open and closed without all the ribbon icons distracting my thoughts. I could use my eyes with moving the mouse around. A more natural feel.


For better visual feedback (and I'm really surprised that this is not the default setting) go to the Unity plugin in compiz config settings manager and play around with the Backlight Mode settings under the Experimental tab. Backlight toggles will only colour in the backgrounds of running programs. Backlight and Edge Illumination toggles is cool: active programs on the current workspace will have a coloured background, and active programs on other workspaces will have a bit of highlighting around the border. Playing with this and the transparency gives you some pretty decent feedback, IMHO.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-11-10
> **cariboo907 said:**
> We having nothing against discussing other distributions in the Cafe. This is a thread about Unity, any discussion of page rankings on distrowatch  really has nothing to do with this thread.

I understand your point. I mistakenly distorted one of your comments posted about 1 week ago. You wrote that the Ubuntu forum team had decided to not moderate this thread anymore about netiquette (except after user's complaints about comments too chocking) to let people freely express their feelings about Unity, including anger. My vague souvenir combined with the fact that this thread could be called "Unity trash thread where every complaint about Unity is redirected to to avoid cluttering all forum topics with the same comments" made me wrongly believe that the thread was not moderated anymore at all, including topics.

> **cariboo907 said:**
> All of you that have jumped on the Mint bandwagon, have used distrowatch as proof that you are doing the right thing, but nobody has actually done some homework and shown us some real figures.

This is a wrong interpretation. Many people have claimed leaving Ubuntu as much as for Canonical's or Mark Shuttleworth's attitude as for Unity itself. The switch to Mint for many users, including me, was after a survey in the net about which distro was closer to Ubuntu easiness, but without what some consider as drawbacks regarding the new Ubuntu direction. Linux Mint just naturally emerges as a good candidate.

If you want figures, I spent two days in April on Unity and Ubuntu 11.04 trying to make it work according to my needs and without regular application crashes. Considering the slowness of my progress, I gave up and came back to 10.10. As stubborn as I can be, I switched to 11.04 a couple of months later.  It took me about two weeks (10 working days) to produce a system stable, fast, and that corresponded to my development needs. I am carrying out research in biomechanical face modeling and I am very demanding with my computers. It is my highest record of updating problems in about 20 years or research and development with Unix-based systems including Solaris, Red Heat, Fedora, Gentoo, and maybe some others that do not immediately come to my mind. Except the 32- to 64-bit step on PowerPC that was a mess, my previous record  was 4 days on Solaris. I have no other major updating problems to  report although I always have updated my computers as soon as the updates were available. After having recently done my homework about which distribution would suit my needs beside Ubuntu, It took me less than a day to switch from Ubuntu to Linux Mint.

Reading many things in the Internet about Mr. Shuttleworth's decisions and about the new Ubuntu direction, I understood that Ubuntu 11.04 was more or less in beta state when it was released. As far as I can understand, Canonical needed to have it soon on the market to have enough user feedback before releasing the LTS version planned one year later. So, although it was claimed on the main page of ubuntu.org that Ubuntu was safe, stable, and the latest release had everything we might need, a beta version was released (11.04) which was neither safe or stable! I use computers for professional purposes and I cannot afford to work with an OS provider that uses its user base as beta testers while upgrades are so easy with so many other distributions.

> **cariboo907 said:**
> Now that I've pushed this off topic again, lets either stop this discussion, our open another thread where it can be discussed without having to be deemed off topic.

As you wish :)

### Post by mpiter on 2011-11-10
> **robert shearer said:**
> Shuttleworth seems to view the future of the computer as being a truly personal and portable device...
from [https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/882274](https://bugs.launchpad.net/unity/+bug/882274)

Mark Shuttleworth (sabdfl) wrote : #74


Thus a universal layout scaled to a smartphone that  scales to a tablet or desktop makes a lot of sense...

Quite why the desktop is being locked for such a device so far ahead of the actual devices appearance **is** confusing though.

If this were Apple then I could envisage the device being worked on for release with the fully functional and tested O/S, but as Canonical don't seem to have a manufacturing capability then it seems both visionary and fingers crossed hopefulness.

Shrug, perhaps Canonical are in conversation with outside manufacturers and a device will arrive fully formed and explain the tortuous road we currently find ourselves on.

I certainly hope so and look forward to that eventuality.

from [[COLOR="Blue"]http://www.zdnet.com/blog/open-source/ubuntu-linux-heads-to-smartphones-tablets-and-smart-tvs/9834[/COLOR]]("http://www.zdnet.com/blog/open-source/ubuntu-linux-heads-to-smartphones-tablets-and-smart-tvs/9834"):

> Still, also like Windows 8, you won’t be seeing a production version of multi-device Ubuntu anytime soon. Shuttleworth said that he expects a fully-baked and ready to go Ubuntu for all devices will appear in Ubuntu 14.04-April 2014. In the meantime, there’s not even alpha code. They’re taking their time because they want to get it right. Shuttleworth wouldn’t say when the first code would appear.

We probably should assume April 2014 if everything works as planned, i.e., at best.

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2011-11-10
> **HDave said:**
> 
4) remove global menu -- this part is the worst (IMO) and it was dissappointing that there is no easy way to remove this "feature"..though it can be removed.

Meh, it only takes five days to get used to it.

> 5) button to see/switch workspaces - the launcher workspaces button is badly broken and underpowered

On 2D, yes. On 3D, no.

> 6) show desktop - lack of a show desktop button was a real problem, though I eventually found a great hack to do that...
It amazes me how much push-back there is on adding an option for a simple "Show Desktop" button to the launcher.

Probably because "Show Desktop" is silly when you have multiple workspaces. If you want to see your desktop, switch to a different workspace.

> I feel like this is a symptom of the fact that Android/iOS tablet PCs don't have a desktop and so Ubuntu won't have one soon either.  I honestly believe that Ubuntu is leaving the desktop market to go chase the emerging tablet PC market.

Trust me, that's not happening. Ubuntu will remain first and foremost a desktop distribution. Removing Show Desktop is an attempt at streamlining the user interface to get rid of redundant or little-used features. If you use Show Desktop, it's likely that you're using it just because you used to use it on Windows.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-11-10
[QUOTE=Billisnice]If i forgot they were open, i could have sensitive info still running in background for someone to see if i am away from my puter or loan someone my machine. There is no visual clues.[/QUOTE]
Chrome's all one session, man. If you closed those windows, they'd appear as recently closed tabs in the other ones, with all your cookies intact. If you're concerned about security, you don't let someone else use your login session, period.

The tickers on the Launcher show you exactly how many windows are open in which apps. Remembering where you put things is otherwise your own business. Gnome Shell is a little better at this, but even previews don't remind you which tabs are open in each individual browser window.

Gnome 2, incidentally, only shows windows from the present workspace on the taskbar by default, so there's no getting around the problem you're describing. It's also not really a problem.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-11-10
> **irv said:**
> I have a separate partition for each install, and I keep all my working files in Ubuntu One. So no matter what OS I am in my files are the same but my setting are different. 
[ATTACH]206848[/ATTACH]

Edit: the only thing bad about running so many OS' is when it comes to updating everything. I had to do this a little while ago. Plus I have a server running Ubuntu 10.04.

I am not sure I get it. I also have one OS per partition but only one home directory that I mount irrespective of the OS. So, do you mean that you have one different home directory in each system partition with a Ubuntu One copy in each home directory?  In this case, how do you manage a directory such as [COLOR="Green"]~/.local/share[/COLOR]?  Some of its subdirectories will be OS dependant (e.g., [COLOR="green"]deskbar-applet[/COLOR]) while other will be OS independent (e.g., [COLOR="green"]evolution[/COLOR]).

---

### Post by beew on 2011-11-10
> **mpiter said:**
> I am not sure I get it. I also have one OS per partition but only one home directory that I mount irrespective of the OS. So, do you mean that you have one different home directory in each system partition with a Ubuntu One copy in each home directory?  In this case, how do you manage a directory such as [COLOR=Green]~/.local/share[/COLOR]?  Some of its subdirectories will be OS dependant (e.g., [COLOR=green]deskbar-applet[/COLOR]) while other will be OS independent (e.g., [COLOR=green]evolution[/COLOR]).

You can have a separate /home partition for each OS to store the settings in that OS. All of them can be quite small except for the Ubuntu 1 /home partition that you also use for storing data. Alternatively you can have a separate shared data partition and a bunch of small /home partitions for the OSes. Either setup is fine if you only multiboot different flavours of Ubuntu (and Mint?) but if you have something like Fedora then there may be a permission problem for writting into the shared data partitions that need to be work around.

### Post by irv on 2011-11-10
More News from Mark Shuttleworth:
> By 14.04 LTS Ubuntu will power tablets, phones, TVs and smart screens from the car to the office kitchen, and it will connect those devices cleanly and seamlessly to the desktop, the server and the cloud.

Unity, the desktop interface in today’s Ubuntu 11.10, was designed with this specific vision in mind. While the interface for each form factor is shaped appropriately, Unity’s core elements are arranged in exactly the way we need to create coherence across all of those devices. This was the origin of the name Unity – a single core interface framework, that scales across all screens, and supports all toolkits.

For the whole story: [http://www.markshuttleworth.com/archives/820]("http://www.markshuttleworth.com/archives/820")

### Post by dgrafix on 2011-11-10
I have been using "Unity" for a few days now in a desperate attempt to like it. (In fact i feel I have uneccesarily wasted some valuable hours faffing about)

After a disastrous first install (I had no Dash Icon!) I was actually happy when i found out that is not how it is supposed to be and  that must have been why it was so bad!! (which as you can expect without a dash icon on unity was not good to start with!!)

But no. that was not the magic ingredient it needed (although it helped  :D and made it basically... useable.)

I have also been trying "pure" gnome 3 on fedora. It is much better and somehow more "sensical" than unity but they both suffer the same kind of idiocies to varying degrees. So what has unity bought that is new to the table?

-Nothing that i can see- 

Actually it has bought lots!... It has bought a whole large amount of LESS (unless you mean icon size in which case it rules!)

In almost every ("o.o.t.b.") video I have seen has been "all you have to do is search and type a few letters! blah blah blah..."  sorry, but you could always do that (just not as obviously) I do not see how that is "new". Even the command line can do that to some extent when you press tab! .... Ok not ideal to use the terminal, and gnome -should- have (an obvious) active search function.... I agree! 

-But- 

Windows Vista, 7, and the latest OSXs all have this (as a feature) but they have not taken away anything their power users expect! (except for some irritating moving about and renaming stuff).. On the contrary they have added more -and- made the interface easier and more attractive at the same time for beginners.  

Surely a simple search box on the top bar and a non intrusive search-adaptive menu would have been better than a full screen takeover with icons the size of houses!.... and in all that space it lists a fraction of your apps! It could list them all and some in that space!
**- I am not using a mobile phone!.**

Great so gnome 3 and unity has a -visible- active search!!! - a required evolution, i agree. However it certainly does not warrant the removal of fundamental stuff that all speed and power users use without even needing to think about it. Why have these thing been locked out? It is not as if they conflict with "ease of use" users anyway because the vast majority do not even know those methods are there.


Also what is with the "lockdown"?? If it was not for this it would not be half as bad IMO. My first thought was how can i make everything smaller.... but i can't... how can i move the bar to the other side? hmmm i cant...  Out of the box from a total "dummie's" point of view Ubuntu is now -less- customizable than Windows. 
 
I know I **-can-** change it and that in reality Ubuntu is 10x more customizable than Windows or OSX... that is... if i -really- wanted to get my hands dirty and go poking around under the hood,  but i have neither the time nor can be arsed to go looking for ways to force something that should at least have some freedom to start with. -This stuff should be obvious and more importantly present.


In short, it has been the most difficult transition of any Interface i have -ever- used , including stuff on Windows 2,3,NT,2K,XP,Vis,7, leopard, snow leopard, lion,  IOS, Android and any versions of Linux based systems I have used. 
These were all somehow intuitive and suitable for the scope of their purpose and I picked them all up quickly. (That is: -at least the transition you expect from the next generation of a system you have come to know like and respect)


Sure i can use Xfce(hmmm) or KDE (yuck) but I would rather use a (easily) customizable** ubuntu out of the box default** to be honest. Fine have the big easy interface but at least let me get rid of it within 5-15 minutes of switching it on without needing to do 'researching' if i want to. (and yes i know about fallback but how long is that going to be "tacked" on?)

Perhaps someone can make a branch of Gnome 2.3 and continue that along the proper path under another name - in the form of a proper DESKTOP OS. *(-and use skins or something for "easy peasy" looking tablet interfaces instead).... lets hope so :/

I** do not care **if people call me a "Unity troll" as some did when i ranted it off after first seeing it. Personally I do not think I am and my arguments above feel justified . I had not even heard about unity until a few days ago when i installed it and had a shock (been too busy to bother updating my 9.x) a but if that what it takes to add my voice to the MAJORITY  of users that DO NOT LIKE IT then all the better. Perhaps canonical or gnome or whoever needs to listen will listen.

 The Gnome-Ubuntu outlook has never looked so bleak IMO, the whole community is split over this issue.

---

Do you manage your hidden files (files starting with '.') of your home directory in a particular way.  Most of them should be shared between OS and cannot belong to the local system partition.  This includes browser, mail, dictionaries data, and many configuration files such as [COLOR="Green"].bashrc[/COLOR], [COLOR="green"].xmodmadrc[/COLOR]...

### Post by mpiter on 2011-11-11

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-11-11
On separate partitions - why not just put all content data on one separate partition organized like the home folder, symlink to Documents, Templates, Music, etc. from the home folders of each of the systems you have installed, and leave all the dotfiles on the same partition as the system? It seems quite a bit simpler. You can usually migrate config files forward to later versions of the software, but not backward to earlier ones, so sharing the config files themselves is just asking for pain.

Billisnice, that thread's been locked several times, now, and you already know that that stat doesn't really tell us anything interesting.

[quote=mpiter]In this case, what does make Unity more device independent than Gnome Shell?[/quote]
Nothing. They have the same goal. Canonical is just pushing it harder. Right now, Shell is more tablet-friendly than Unity.

> Icon size should be customizable. The present Unity desktop will not be manageable on a smartphone with a finger. Icons are too small.
They're not even talking about doing this until the next LTS. *After* Pangolin. Tubular Tamarin or whatnot. It's nothing to do with the current version of the interface, and Unity is likely to change quite a bit more than Shell does in the same amount of time. Almost certainly, the interface for the phone will only marginally resemble that for the desktop.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-11-11
> **Copper Bezel said:**
> On separate partitions - why not just put all content data on one separate partition organized like the home folder, symlink to Documents, Templates, Music, etc. from the home folders of each of the systems you have installed, and leave all the dotfiles on the same partition as the system?

Because if I work with Unity for a while doing my email with Evolution as usual, then if I reboot on another system partition with Gnome Shell, I will not have access to my email data because Evolution stores its data in [COLOR="Green"]~/.local/share/evolution[/COLOR]. Same problem with Chromium, Firefox, BMF, and other packages. Linux not only stores configuration files in dotfiles, but also important data to be restored between each session.

### Post by Nightstrike2009 on 2011-11-11
It is with regret i post this thread but I am sick on the Gnome 3 / Unity farce now, so much so I have returned to Windows 7.

I will miss the linux community which is second to none, but like MS Vista too much time and effort is being wasted on "eye candy" User interfaces in the Ubuntu World now, in my own opinion.

Hardware driver support and stable linux os enviroment should be paramount even if it means dvd sizes images like the modern Windows OS do.  

I left Windows because of Vista's over emphasis on "Eye candy" user interfaces at the cost of os stability and end user experience, now I find Ubuntu to be doing the same with 11.04 upwards.  I personally think that 10.04LTS was the best ubuntu version ever and Ubuntu would have larger support if more attention was paid to serious os bugs rather than flash interfaces.

I have the deepest respect for the Linux community but where Ubuntu is concerned enough is now enough for myself at least, I had acheived intermediate knowledge and have used ubuntu since v8.10 and was not a "Noob".

Its sadly ironic that Windows (Vista) drove me to Ubuntu, then Ubuntu (11.04 upwards) drove me back to Windows 7.  This UI farce is far from becoming equal to Microsoft its just letting the (Linux) side down and driving Windows converts back to "better the Devil they know".  Many of my friends have also gone back to the Windows side (from Ubuntu) too, if we have to put up with flashy "eye candy" UI's then at least Microsoft listens to its users these days, provides stable OS's and has excellent hardware support. 

I will miss the linux community loads, but ive had enough of the Gnome3 / Unity farce and have sadly "Returned to the darkside" has Ubuntu users would put it.  I may one day return to using a Linux os in the future, but that OS WILL NOT be ubuntu. :-(

Hope other Linux newcomers have an easier ride than I did, and Linux becomes the stable os it deserves to be not overshadowed by pointless, rushed, unfinished "eye candy" that hobbles OS stability.

Nightstrike out.

PS: all opinions expressed are my own, I don't wish any disrespect to other users I just feel I need to express my opinions and thank the other Ubuntu users who helped me to learn and use ubuntu better, cheers guys (& gals)!

---

### Post by digib on 2011-11-11
Did not post this in the help forum, since I am more into some general brainstorming, not a finished solution to a problem.

I used to enjoy Ubuntu 10.10. For me it had almost everything. Unfortunately it is too weak in the way it deals with flash. For example, I could be having one page open playing a streaming movie (on bbc iplayer for example), then when I load a page with many flash ads in another tab, the streaming would crash.

Then I upgraded to 11.10 and got so disappointed. First the hand holding Mac-like BS of unity, where you can't do what you want with the system, is a complete outrage. I then tried Gnome 3, which is crap as well. I also tried the classic desktop with 11.10, and that is what I am using now. I do feel however that it is not as good as 10.10, it just is not as pretty. One example, I can't move the plugins in the toolbar to where I want them. I also don't want right click functionality to have to be "unlocked" by the Alt-button. With the classic desktop it feels like I have gone back to some Ubuntu before version 9.04.

Guess what I want is something that has 11.10's stability with flash but looks exactly like 10.10. 

I think the stability  of 11.10 comes from the new Linux Kernel. Would I be right in that assumption?

Also thinking about switching to some other flavour. I am not sure I want to continue going in the direction Ubuntu is going. It's too bad really. I love Ubuntu because of the size of the userbase, which means that any problem is Googleable and easy to solve. Any ideas?

Sorry for being long winding. I am not really a geek, more of an intermediate user, so I write from that perspective. 

Grateful for any ideas or opinions. Have a great morning/day/night wherever you are you all.

### Post by snowpine on 2011-11-11
Flash is proprietary, "non-free" software from Adobe Corp. There is nothing Ubuntu can really do to improve/degrade Flash performance since they have no access to the source code. Did you try installing the latest Flash plugin in 10.10 to solve your flash-crashing problem?

### Post by snowpine on 2011-11-11
Regarding your question about other distros, honestly you are the first person I have ever heard call Ubuntu 11.10 "stable"! Personally I use Scientific Linux. It's a fork of Red Hat (super-stable) and will have support for the classic Gnome 2 desktop for approximately 6 more years. Debian and Slackware are also well-known for their stability. For Ubuntu the equivalent is 10.04 LTS.

### Post by Docaltmed on 2011-11-11
> **mpiter said:**
> Reading many things in the Internet about Mr. Shuttleworth's decisions and about the new Ubuntu direction, I understood that Ubuntu 11.04 was more or less in beta state when it was released. As far as I can understand, Canonical needed to have it soon on the market to have enough user feedback before releasing the LTS version planned one year later. So, although it was claimed on the main page of ubuntu.org that Ubuntu was safe, stable, and the latest release had everything we might need, a beta version was released (11.04) which was neither safe or stable! I use computers for professional purposes and I cannot afford to work with an OS provider that uses its user base as beta testers while upgrades are so easy with so many other distributions.

1. You're getting an OS and applications for free. The way I see it, your end of the bargain is to find and file bugs and put up with a little flakiness here and there if you're going to go with every release. Whimperin' and whamperin' on the forums is fun and everybody likes to read a good rant, but really...

2. Why on earth would you not be sticking with the LTS for your production machines? I'm not a computer professional, and even I'm not that naive. Everybody but me in the office has 10.04 and will continue to do so until several months after the new LTS is released. If you really are a computer professional, you should know at least that much.

### Post by 3Miro on 2011-11-11
Have you considered other alternatives to Windows. If you don't like Unity and Gnome-shell, and if Gnome-classic isn't cutting it for you, then you can always try XFCE or KDE or LXDE. There is so much choice under Linux, that if one DE doesn't work is hardly a reason to abandon the system altogether. Actually, with so much diversity, it is guaranteed that some of it will not work for you.

If you have Ubuntu installed, you can just install xfce4. It takes a bit longer to customize (as compared to Gnome 2), but xfce can do almost everything that Gnome 2 can plus more.

KDE is a great DE if you don't mind the fact it usually takes 100-200MB extra from the RAM. KDE is the most customizable DE out there.

Finally you have the young LXDE, while it is somewhat incomplete, LXDE gives a new definition to the expression "lightning fast".

### Post by mpiter on 2011-11-11
> **Docaltmed said:**
> 1. You're getting an OS and applications for free. The way I see it, your end of the bargain is to find and file bugs and put up with a little flakiness here and there if you're going to go with every release. Whimperin' and whamperin' on the forums is fun and everybody likes to read a good rant, but really...

A little flakiness here and there is not working about 2 weeks to work around bugs.  And about the end of the bargain, you may read ubuntu.com. The last version of their OS is always supposed to be secured, safe, and stable. Usually the bargain behind free OS is use it, spread it, and the OS provider will sell support to companies.

> **Docaltmed said:**
> 2. Why on earth would you not be sticking with the LTS for your production machines? I'm not a computer professional, and even I'm not that naive. Everybody but me in the office has 10.04 and will continue to do so until several months after the new LTS is released. If you really are a computer professional, you should know at least that much.

I will not use LTS versions for my work computers. I replace one computer per year on average (I have 3). I use biomechanical face models requiring intensive calculations for my work. So I always buy expensive recent hardware in search of computing performance. As a consequence, I meet only partial support for parts of my pieces of hardware from time to time. The solution is of course upgrading the software as soon as I can.

During more than 20 years I have always upgraded Linux-based systems as soon as the upgrades were available (Solaris, Red Hat, Fedora, Gentoo...). Except one exception with one Solaris upgrade taking me 4 days to work around bugs, I have never spent more than a couple of days for an upgrade till Ubuntu 11.04. So, I disagree that we should stick to LTS for production computers. I have never experienced a serious problem except with Ubuntu 11.04.

### Post by VinDSL on 2011-11-11
> **tartalo said:**
> Keep an eye on Linux Mint [...]

SOURCE:  [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=11336519#post11336519](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=11336519#post11336519)

> **Clement Lefebvre said:**
> Check out regexxer, you'll love it :)

Ok, jokes appart.. people touched on a couple of interesting topics. 

**The name**

I fully agree with the comments made here. The name won't help brand recognition, promotion or the project to get momentum. It's a common word in English, almost slangish, which does have a few inappropriate meanings. It's not too late to change it.

**The fallback mode**

The fallback mode is not here to stay. The plan is for Gnome Shell to work with a larger set of hardware and for the fallback mode to disappear. 

In terms of UI, the fallback mode is something nobody wants. Gnome 3 enthusiasts don't like its Gnome2-like design. Gnome 2 fans hate the fact that it simply doesn't work. Of course, it works.... but it looks like it doesn't work. And the reason for this is simple... it looks and gives people the impression they're running Gnome 2, only they're not. They're actually running Gnome 3 on top of GTK3 and without compatibility with Bonobo and panel applets. Once the user understands he can use the Alt key to interact with the panel, he gets a bit of false hopes... until he goes further and realize no Bonobo Gnome applet will run on this Fallback mode. To Mint users, basically, that means no mintMenu.

Why would we port mintMenu to GTK3 and DBUS just to make it work with fallback mode when this mode is set to disappear and we've got a stable 2.32 base which isn't going to change in the future? It would make no sense.

**Gnome 3 itself**

Mate and Gnome 3 can be run on the same system. If they can't right now, at least they will be in the future. 

Gnome 3 is a fantastic piece of technology and it's also a brand new desktop. It's something we can extend and improve with our own ideas, whether it's via extensions, configuration or even by porting applications such as mintMenu to work with Gnome Shell. 

As far as we're concerned we want to get started with Gnome 3, but we also want to continue to provide what is still the most popular Linux desktop out there: Gnome 2.32.

5 years from now do we plan on using Gnome 2? Well... what's the thing you want the most this month? Five years ago, were you even thinking about it? 

So 5 years from now, we'll be doing the exact same thing as we're doing now: We'll choose the best components for our users. Today this includes both Gnome 2 and Gnome 3.

Reading between the lines, it looks like Clem & Company is going to come up their own "Unity" and "Fallback". :)

Their "Unity" is going to be Gnome-Shell with a Mint Menu.  And, their "Fallback" is going to be Mate DE.
