### Post by 3rdalbum on 2011-11-12
I'm not sure what you mean by "you can't do what you want". The desktop is really just a way of managing files, running programs, and switching between running windows. In this way, Gnome 2 and Unity are different but equal. Perhaps Unity is even a little better in some ways.

If you're talking about customization, well, most software starts off being merely "functional" and then becomes more "customizable" over time. Unity is no different. There will be more options added to Unity.

You're free to move to something like XFCE if you really want, but be aware that Unity takes a few days to really get comfortable with; and once comfortable with Unity you can easily be comfortable with Gnome Shell. Unity has some excellent features that might not be apparent if you've only used it for a short time. Don't judge Unity so quickly; give it a few more days and see if you like it. And if you still don't, then try it in 12.04. It's still young and developing software.

---

### Post by expired_fox on 2011-11-12
> **3rdalbum said:**
> I'm not sure what you mean by "you can't do what you want". The desktop is really just a way of managing files, running programs, and switching between running windows. In this way, Gnome 2 and Unity are different but equal. Perhaps Unity is even a little better in some ways.
I'm not agree. The Gnome 2 + Synapse is superior without any doubt.
Unity is too full of bad design decisions, like all sorts of autohiding (autohiding is a pure evil, since it dramatically decreases predictability and prevent of forming behaviour reflexes). May be it's good for tiny laptop screens in order to save some screen estate, but only a few people need this with &#8805;20 inch displays.
Another aspect is Unity's general slowness, which annoys and prevent the building of reflexes too.

Gnome shell is better in this regard (forming reflexes), but the proposed workflow suffers from its universal tablet/traditional PC approach and nowhere near as effective as one of Gnome 2 + Synapse. It also has some very strange design decisions like switch widget, that is inferior and less recognizable than old good checkbox used on device with mouse.

---

### Post by Thad E Ginathom on 2011-11-12
Practical question, important to me: Is Gnome 2 not going to be a possibility with 11.10 and later?

If that is the case, then I have to make a note not to go there, and to check out the alternatives.

I have moved to 11.04. Not interested in Unity. I like my panels and my menus, and I intend to continue with them --- preferably as an unbroken succession of personal preferences and tweaks to the "eye candy". I miss the finesse of using Emerald with Compiz, but Compiz's own options, along with stuff like Gnome Colour Chooser still gives me plenty of options to decorate my desktop the way I like it. 

Equality is not in that different UIs all start and stop applications and manage files etc, etc, it is in *how* they do it. Unity is not equal, and, although I know less about it, I don't think Gnome 3 is either.

I don't work with my computer (any longer, thank goodness!) so productivity in the hard-line sense of the word is not important to me, but comfort, not tripping up on new *imposed *interfaces, and the personal pleasure of tweaking *my way* is what matters.

Honestly, this was never meant to be any sort of rant --- but I don't think the individual "my way" is part of the future for Ubuntu philosophy. I know there is still a choice. XFCE has been mentioned. There are several 'buntus. However, in making a desktop OS that is attractive and perfectly comprehensible to users of certain other commercial OSs, I feel that 10.04 hit a sweet spot, but after that, they completely lost the plot. Assuming, of course, that competing with those OSs was ever part of their aim at all: they might just be a bunch of people that enjoy doing weird things with desktops!

---

### Post by SeijiSensei on 2011-11-12
> **digib said:**
> So is there any flavour of Linux that is not going into a development direction of docks and absence of taskbars?

KDE uses taskbars which they call "panels."  By default there's one at the bottom, but you can reposition it on any screen edge, or have multiple panels, say on top and bottom.  KDE gives you control over most every aspect of your desktop.

I'd give [Kubuntu]("http://cdimage.ubuntu.com/kubuntu/releases/11.10/release/") a spin.  I'm on 10.10 which has been very stable with my hardware, but if you're just starting out, try 11.10 which has the current version of KDE, 4.7.

You'll be missing a couple of things like Firefox, since the default KDE browser is rekonq.  However it's easy enough to install Firefox and any other software you might need using the Software Management application, kpackagekit, or from the command line with apt-get.  I add firefox, thunderbird, gimp, and smplayer to any new installation.  You can also install synaptic if you prefer that.

### Post by Kalibur on 2011-11-12
I like Unity because of its Polished graphics and simple interface that has given me the confidence to give Ubuntu to a novice or someone who is not fond of computers.  I show them where all the stuff they need is, pin it to the launcher and that is just simple and straight.

For hardcore users such as myself I need to do a bit of tweaking to get some of the customizations that gnome and kde offer but then that is not new, I used to do it with a fresh gnome or kde installations.

The down side of Unity interface is the average clicks and time required to reach programs not pinned to the launcher has increased and that is why we hardcore users arean't fans of unity but why are some complaining if you still go ahead and tweak anything out of the box beit Gnome Kde or Unity.  

I for one don't miss the bottom panel I love the bigger workspace that Unity brings.  What I do miss are menus on the top of each window and not on the top unity panel going there up there and working out where focus is is a major pain.

In a nutshell Unity is great visual interface, simple and straight but its lacking on the side of browserability (efficient logic is required), clicks required.  

Its my default Display manager I love it and it has made it comfortable for me to handout a live CD to general novice computer users.  Keep the good work coming in because I think with unity we stand a very good chance to bring ubuntu to the masses.

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-11-12
> **MarjaE said:**
> I'm recovering from hand injuries, and really can't afford the extra clicks, extra typing, and visible-on-mouseover nonsense in Unity. I've done what I can to disable the visible-on-mouseover bugs in Gnome 2/Classic in 11.04.

I've voiced my concern about the accessibility issues in Unity before, quite early on in "that other thread".  With more focus on the keyboard and the use of the global menu Unity is not very friendly.

Those with some form of severe motor impairment can manage small movements with the mouse and single clicks. 

No I'm not impaired, but I've known a few people who have been over the years in my computer support roles.  They like launch icons on the desktop and a high degree of configurability so that they set things up to make it as easy as possible.  One guy I knew could manage combined keyboard and mouse operations by picking his left hand up with his right and placing the required finger onto the keyboard.  He would then operate the mouse with his right hand.  He absolutely hated sticky keys with a passion and refused to use them - he preferred to take his time with his own method rather race the sticky key.

I've lost touch with him now, but I have a fair idea what he would think of Unity.  He could be quite vocal on the subject of GUI design when frustrated by what he perceived as design stupidity.

Chris.

### Post by effenberg0x0 on 2011-11-12
It's a menu, much like what web developers call megamenu these days. See examples:
[http://designm.ag/inspiration/mega-menus/](http://designm.ag/inspiration/mega-menus/)

It's not innovative, it has no real possibilities to evolve in terms of API, additions (like lenses, etc), it has no real way to integrate applications with it, it changes absolutely nothing in the way people use PCs. It's a purely aesthetic change to a menu, that's it. 

Mint will call it "our Dash" or people will call it "Mint's Dash" because they're ignorant to the fact that it is just a menu, a few lines of code, not a real project, not something that was designed professionally and with a purpose of promoting change. 

Maybe they can paint a gnome-panel with a translucent color, put in the left part of the display and call it launcher too.

Well, anyway, it's a far from bold move, as we can always expect from Mint. It is perfect for people that don't like evolution, change. However, if no distro ever takes the lead to try and promote change, much of what we take for granted today in Linux wouldn't exist. We must not forget that big guys like Red Hat, Debian, promoted change in numerous ways in the past and they were discredited by ignorant users because of that. It's just the way it is.

EDIT: It will rain pigs the day someone takes real projects like Ayatana and Unity, which have real development, tons of code and a complex development environment and switch for a menu. It's like trading a solar car for a horse. Horses are good for some. But the world as a whole benefits from all the research, ideas, investment, etc of many professionals involved in such a complex project as designing a solar car.

---

### Post by beew on 2011-11-13
> **jerrrys said:**
> you cant compare an LTS to a nonLTS.  its just not fare :)  wait for 12o4

What is the difference between LTS and non LTS? 

Every 6 months Ubuntu rushes to release a buggy version with a lot of fanfare, features it on its download page and encourages users to "upgrade" (which causes extra problems) and now you are telling us that we should only expect LTS to work??? If that is so Canonical should make it clear on its website.

@OP,

It usually takes a month or more for a new release to become stable and the bugs to be squashed, that goes with both LTS and non LTS. So I generally wouldn't install a new release the moment it comes out, it is asking for troubles regardless what Canonical and some lucky people tell you. The 11.xx takes longer because of the great changes under the hood. It took about 3 months for 11.04 to become stable and solid because of the transition to Unity, I think it will take longer for 11.10 because of the drastic switch to gnome 3. I will wait at least 3 months to put 11.10 on any productive machine.

11.04 is now very solid and a joy to use, much faster than 10.10 and leaves 10.04 in the dust,--it also has better hardware support because of the new kernel. It has good and up to date software support as all the third party apps and ppas are in place (that also took a while, another reason not to rush to upgrade/update) I would recommend it over 10.04 any day, LTS is just that,--it hasn't had any feature update and non security related bug fixes for 2 to  3 years (with 10.04 it is 1.5 year now) and its softwares are all outdated,--hardly anything to desire after or celebrate about unless you are a company who wants "stability" above everything else. Horses and buggies are more "stable" than driving on a highway, that is the kind of "stability" you get from LTS,--at least if you use it as intended.

P.S. I think the reason why many people say that Mint is more stable than Ubuntu is that it is just Ubuntu with a different theme and some minor tweaks on the UI and some codecs preinstalled, but it is usually released a month after Ubuntu. By that time many of the bugs that turn the early upgraders off would have been fixed.

### Post by goneskiing on 2011-11-13
Seriously, we've been using linux since 1999 and it was getting better every year until about 2 yrs ago - Gnome 2 was becoming so productive and reliable that we had no problem recommending it to everyone.  Now Ubuntu 11.10 and Gnome 3 is so bad we will likely switch off it to Mint or Debian or ???. What the heck is going on ? It's like it's intentionally being sabotaged.  

1) Unity / gnome 3 interface is so terrible and unproductive that we've switched to xfce

2) gnome session-save is gone making it too unproductive for us

3) power management is gone - and the tab under screensaver has no selections for AC / Battery huh?

4) User & Group dialog is gone - huh? How the heck are we supposed to manage users - some post said to use gconf - huh ?  that's back to 2001 cryptic linux.

5) Synaptic package management is gone ?  Huh?  

6) running 11.10 Ubuntu w/ xfce desktop most of the icons are gone - it looks like crap.

7) suspend and resume are totally screwed up - screen gets darkened - unreadable, mouse freezes

8) usb-creator-gtk or otherwise is gone for making flash boots

9) mouse sensitivity / clicking while typing is completely screwed up

who is running gnome ?  Obviously someone who doesn't depend on it for day-day work.  Do they really think they are smarter and know better than the users ?  Where are the polls asking users about major gui or package switches ?  Seems like they don't care.

The situation is really bad and really sad.

### Post by Larkspur on 2011-11-13
> **beew said:**
> I agree with everything you say, but that begs the question. If the Mint Shell is just a few lines of code and that is enough to keep a lot of people happy, why doesn't Canonical do it? It wouldn't distract from Unity if it is just a few lines of code as you said. Then it would be able to have its cake and eat it too. If Canonical is concerned about user experience and the users can be satisfied by just a menu, I think it should just provide it as an option for the "fear change" crowd instead of digging in its heel to try to prove that it is right and all the pissed off users are wrong. What for? It just generates bad vibes and hurt feelings.

 Canonical wants to reach more users, the recommendation of existing users would play a big role especially since it doesn't have a big ad budget.

I have no vested interest in this, I use Unity quite happily, but just wondering.

Despite all Shuttleworth's riches, Canonical is actually a small company  with limited resources.  In order to make a change like Unity in a time  when Gnome 3 is introduced, they have to realistic in what they can  achieve.  This means a tightly defined set of goals with very little  derivation allowed.  Also, they were not just thinking in terms of a  single release, they had a final goal - the next LTS - and each version  of Unity we've seen was a step towards that.

Given their slim resources and limited manpower, they could only achieve  so much with each release.  The aim was not to provide a fully-matured,  feature-rich Unity right off the bat, the aim was to provide a Unity  that had all the features that made it suitable for most usercases.  It could not cover every eventuality.  

In fact, one could also ask the question why no one out of the group of Unity detractors got around to writing those "few lines" of code themselves?  I mean, aren't they all power users?  I'm sure it would take power users a few hours to get the code together, set up a PPA or submit it as a patch.  This is the essential freedom of Open Source, after all.

On the subject of PPA's, don't the Mint team offer one for MGSE?

### Post by Kapitän Rotbart on 2011-11-13
I actually really enjoy Unity. Granted it took me a bit of time storming forums and blogs to find tricks to configure my desktop to my likings, plus I still haven't figured out how to generate a custom cycle of background images, but it's not a big deal for me.

Posted a couple Unity notes [on my wiki]("http://dubroy.servehttp.com/pmwiki/pmwiki.php/Main/LinuxFixBugs#oneiricfeats"). I save a lot of time by adding quicklist shortcuts to the Unity launcher right-click menus. I find Dash relatively easy to navigate (I think I save time by launching Dash with the Super key and typing the name of the app rather than using a point-and-click mouse menu navigation). I really enjoy having all my configuration utilities as quicklist shortcuts on the System Settings launcher. In Compiz I was able to make the Unity bar launchers smaller, which helps because I have a small screen. I'm sure not everyone will like Unity, but Gnome Shell looks pretty slick as well, and I certainly am a fan of Xfce, so Ubuntu is still offering me plenty of enjoyable options. I have never been able to figure out KDE (it's just soooo confusing) and I've played around a bit with other distros, yet the Ubuntu family always seems to provide me with the latest software at fairly high stability.

My frustrations with Ubuntu 11.10 was that Evolution mail no longer works (had to migrate to Thunderbird, I certainly wasn't enthusiastic about this), Samba wasn't working at first, so I couldn't install a windaws printer over network (I know no one should use windaws) but fortunately that was fixed in the updates a week later. An update made Evolution almost work, it's still too buggy for serious use. I'm still confused about the "users and groups" migration in Gnome3. I got plenty of sources telling me how to add myself to the vboxusers because almost every Ubuntu user has an xp virtual machine in vbox, but I'm still confused about enabling a user to connect to the internets via modem is still a mystery to me (is it now enabled by default?), unfortunately I don't have a USB modem to try it myself.

Anyway, I think Ubuntu will be a winner with Unity, though in Ubuntu 12.04 I'll likely use Gnome Shell (hoping it will be more awesome by then).

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-11-13
For what it's worth, I prefer Gnome's approach of offering no real customization options but having a freely hackable extension base to add options on demand, versus Unity's approach of offering a couple of tweakable features and just hard-coding everything else. It's fairly easily assumed that if Unity took Shell's route, there'd be an extension to turn the Dash button into a Start Menu.

On the other hand, I don't think Canonical should be expected to lift a finger in writing code that they don't intend anyone to use, particularly when it replaces a big, central part of their project with a clunky imitation of someone else's. I also don't see why the classic menu is special, and why expecting Canonical to provide one is any different from expecting them to provide any other random feature.

A small amount of coding work to offer an option many users might appreciate is reasonable. That's not really what we're talking about here, though.

### Post by BobW on 2011-11-13
> **effenberg0x0 said:**
> Well, anyway, it's a far from bold move, as we can always expect from Mint. It is perfect for people that don't like evolution, change. However, if no distro ever takes the lead to try and promote change, much of what we take for granted today in Linux wouldn't exist. We must not forget that big guys like Red Hat, Debian, promoted change in numerous ways in the past and they were discredited by ignorant users because of that. It's just the way it is.

I've seen this attitude from several people and I don't think it's productive. There is a *big* difference between not liking a specific change and being against all change. Trying to claim that everyone who doesn't like like Unity is just a Luddite that hates all change is just not correct.

### Post by sunewbie on 2011-11-14
> **Primefalcon said:**
> Actually if all you care about is stability... Just use 10.04 and wait until extended support in 12.04 before switching to that release

I completely agree. 

If you want to stick to *buntu. XFCE is a good choice.

If you are planning to shift to another distro, I think you should consifer Pinguy OS 10.04/3 LTS. It has upgraded some softwares like shifting from Openoffice to Libreoffice, etc. 

here is the release announcement
[http://forum.pinguyos.com/Thread-10-04-3-Point-Release](http://forum.pinguyos.com/Thread-10-04-3-Point-Release)

so you will have to do less work

On the other hand, if you don't mind KDE, try SimplyMEPIS. It is based on Debian stable.

My personal favourite is Bodhi Linux which has 10.04 LTS core (solid and stable). By Default, It has only 3 app, Midori browser, synaptic, PCManFM, file manager. 

It has uses E17 as default DE. Software center is online and it has to be downloaded or installed (point and click)manually. So the choice is yours. which software to use.

It follows a sort of Semi-rolling release (till a new LTS i.e. 12.04) is released. So all you need to do is do a distribution upgrade, which only upgrades core components and latest E17 i.e. EFL libraries. So for 2 years you are at peace. This is a very lightweight distro and already gaining popularity (19 on distrowatch). 

If you have no experience with E17, it has a learning curve. Bodhi has excellent wiki and guide on using E17. 

It is very stable, and gives you freedom and you decide if you want to upgrade your system or not. If things are working fine, why to upgrade, except for new version of selected app likes browsers and Libreoffice. :)

Nothing is auto-upgraded by default, not even flash. In case of dist-upgrade, kernel upgrades are not done. Like Mint, Kernel upgrades, by default are off.

I suggest you give it a try :)

[www.bodhilinux.com](www.bodhilinux.com) 

Hope this helps :D


As an end user, it is best to stick with LTS version and wait for 5-6 months are the release. So that all bugs are solved. When 12.04.1 i.e. first update pack is released, download it and have a fresh install. Other releases are more like experimentation (I say april version as alpha, oct as beta and LTD version as final one - for end users ;) :D )

No need to download everything as soon as it is released. Alpha, beta and RC of Linux are different from those of proprietary apps of windoz. Everything is released as soon as it is developed, so that community can report bugs, which can be solved quickly. This is the nature of open source, i guess :)

Please let me know if I am wrong.

Regarding Flash, Linux Mint and Pinguy are not FOSS fanatics and incorporate prop drivers and give out-of-the-box OS.

If flash worked in 10.04 LTS , then it is better to revert back to it or install Pinguy OS 10.04 LTS.

cheers.

---

### Post by thatguruguy on 2011-11-14
> **Xenogears said:**
> 
for some reason the option to "Empty Rubbish Bin" is disable, when there are things to discard.
could be a bug.

There are a couple reasons that could happen.  

[LIST=1]
[*]If another user (including you, if you were using sudo) deletes something, it will show in the trash bin, but you won't be able to clear the trash using your normal user permissions.
[*]If you deleted something while using an external drive, and then failed to empty the trash bin when you disconnected the external drive, your trash bin will still continue to show that there is something in it, and you won't be able to empty it until you reconnect the external drive.
[/LIST]
You can look [here]("http://www.howtogeek.com/howto/ubuntu/how-to-empty-the-ubuntu-gnome-trash-from-the-command-line/") for instructions on how to empty the trash bin from the command line.  Make sure to read the comments, since there are some important points there.  You may also want to read [this thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5014310").

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-11-14
[QUOTE=Handssolow]I don't have a tablet but watching someone on TV using one, I can see that if you have a touch screen and use a finger rather than a mouse, having Unity makes some sense.[/QUOTE]
Actually, not true. Unity isn't yet ready for tablets. It depends a lot on the mouse's hover position, which isn't present in a tablet interface. While future releases should make a fairly smooth transition from desktop to tablet, it's basically unusable on a tablet as-is without some serious tweaking. It's very much designed for the desktop, and specifically netbooks, since it grew up from the interface of Ubuntu Netbook Remix.

Someone here at UF was messing with it on a netvertible, which is a tablet netbook (a netbook with a touchscreen that flips over the keyboard, as with older tablet notebooks before the slates became popular) and said its was something of a hassle. I haven't seen much beyond that.

Outside of slates, netbooks are slightly over-represented, but I don't think Ubuntu installs really favor any one form factor.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-11-14
[QUOTe=irv]I also like Gnome 3.[/QUOTE]
Well, I said I liked a part of the Gnome philosophy. (And I do mean a *part*. I don't care at all for the total disregard for modularity in this release.) I'm biased by the fact that I love Shell to death and the fact that it was made just for me in ways I can't fully express, but all I was really saying is that Gnome Shell's extensibility is such a unique and powerful approach that it's hard not to contrast it to Unity's limited built-in configurability, but also absurd to expect Unity to provide anything like the Shell extension base. It's apples to banana-strawberry-vodka parfait. 

I'm sure Mint's happy that the extension base is there, too, but I don't get what they're trying to do with it, because it looks like an attempt to fight the new interface back with anachronisms and put things back on the desktop that Gnome rightly moved to the Overview. I'm finding subtler extensions, like Zeitgeist support in the Favorites app icons and search, to be far more useful.

Anyway, if there's anything I'm not finding lacking in Gnome 3, it's the interface. I upgraded to Gnome 3 a little over two weeks ago, and my BFF commented yesterday that it was the first time she'd seen my desktop layout not change for two weeks straight. = )

Edit: And if I'm overly dismissive of Mint, good gravy, I *hope* I'm being just a *tiny* counterbalance to Mintyvangelists like the linked "Jim Lynch."

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-11-15
[QUOTE=goneskiing]3) power management is gone - and the tab under screensaver has no selections for AC / Battery huh?[/QUOTE]
Yes, this seems broken. I found the "real" power settings in dconf under org>gnome>settings-daemon, but they *don't take*. I can't change what the power button does, driving me batty having to use a menu to put my netbook to sleep, and I can't change the battery threshold, which, thanks to my low-quality third-party battery, means that if my battery dies in class, I get a hard unplug instead of a graceful suspend - and suspend-on-critical isn't even an option in the GUI anymore, anyway, so apparently the *option* of what to do on critical battery is "How would you like to lose your unsaved data?"

> 7) suspend and resume are totally screwed up - screen gets darkened - unreadable, mouse freezes
Both the "Dim Display when Idle" in Screen settings and Disable Trackpad when Typing settings are broken. They will respectively randomly disable your backlight and trackpad, often on resume from suspend but really any ol' time. Disabling them removes these problems (and the features, naturally.) Known bugs that no one has fixed.

> Do they really think they are smarter and know better than the users ?
Frankly, yes, they do. Sorry. But you're right that these real, legitimate bugs in Gnome are disappointing and don't reflect well on Gnome's reliability. I understand that it's a rough, crazy transition time for them, but I find it discouraging.

In any case, after all the ranting about Shell lately (and I know you don't like it, either) I'm glad to see someone upset for real reasons. = ) I'm a big fan of Shell, but it initially felt like a truly perfect, integrated UI from which to drive my poor, broken OS. I've obviously not yet sorted all of these problems. I'm hoping that the occasional *X crash on resume* I've experienced was *only* a result of a bad Shell extension (although this would *still* be a Gnome bug, since the extensions' little playpen is designed to prevent that sort of problem.)

Edit: Much later: Inexplicably, goneskiing, I just tried messing with the real PM settings I mentioned one last time, after seeing them stoically ignore my settings a dozen times before, and for no particular reason from the Unity side instead of Shell. The settings took, function, and persisted over reboot. I have no idea why.

### Post by Deltik on 2011-11-15
[CENTER][FONT=Arial Black][SIZE=4][COLOR=Green]**This post contains a potential solution to desktop environment problems.**[/COLOR][/SIZE][/FONT]
[/CENTER]

> **JustinGordon said:**
> I'm having only really annoying issue, which is that the title bar does not update unless the window is resized
I added that bug to my report.  Thanks.  I also added a [proposed solution to your forum topic]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=11458633#post11458633"), and I repeat it here below.

> **JustinGordon said:**
> I'm curious to know if you all think that 11.10 might be OK if unity is turned off.
In the past day, I found something called [MATE Desktop Environment]("http://matsusoft.com.ar/projects/mate/"), a fork of the classic GNOME 2.  The developer doesn't seem know English well, but [Linux Mint picked it up]("http://www.linuxmint.com/rel_lisa_whatsnew.php") and is working on it.

Linux Mint 12 "Lisa" is shipping with MATE, and according to [some sources]("http://www.ubuntubuzz.com/2011/11/install-linux-mint-12-application-in.html"), it's compatible with Ubuntu 11.10.

Here are the instructions to install Mint MATE on Ubuntu 11.10.  (I've tested the instructions up to installation, but I haven't actually used MATE before.)

[LIST=1]
[*]Load *Software Sources*.  This can be accessed by searching the dash or opening it from Ubuntu Software Center.
[*]Change to the tab "Other Software".
[*]Click "Add...".
[*]Add this APT line:
```
deb http://packages.linuxmint.com/ lisa main upstream import
```
[*]Try to close out of *Software Sources*.  You should get a dialog box with the bold text, "**The information about available software is out-of-date**".  Do not click "Reload".  Click "Close" instead.  If you have already clicked "Reload", do not panic about the error and skip step #7.
[*]Open a *Terminal*.  This can be accessed by searching the dash.
[*]Run this command:
```
sudo apt-get update
```
[*]Run this command:
```
sudo apt-get install linuxmint-keyring
```
[*]This is the command to install Mint MATE:
```
sudo apt-get install mint-meta-mate
```
[*]*(I have not tested this step.)* Log off and log on using "lightDM".
[/LIST]

### Post by Baldrick_NZ on 2011-11-15
I had reason to re-install Ubuntu just recently. I thought, 'what the heck' and installed 11.10. 

I quickly realised that it wasn't for me. The unmovable dock on the left-hand side, what were they thinking? Even Synaptic has to be installed from the repos! The whole look and feel on a large screen is too big. And it seems clunky in terms of finding the stuff you really need.  

So back to LTS 10.04 I go, consciously aware that this might be the last Ubuntu distro I use, unless the good folk at Canonical have a change a of heart regarding the design. 

The one thing I have always been selling people on regarding Ubuntu is the way it can be completely customisable. Well, I clearly can't say that anymore. They might as well stick with Vista/7, as that's just as customisable now!

I get that they are trying to dumb it down to get a greater appeal, but did it really have to be dumbed down as much as it has?

I really love Ubuntu, but unless they have a massive re-think before the 10.04 LTS dies, they will loose this punter and potentially many more. It'll be sad because I'll probably trip back to Windows, as no other Linux distro in my opinion can compare with this one. Even Mint isn't fully customisable. Just try installing macbuntu, you'll see.

I really love Ubuntu, but unless they have a massive re-think before the  10.04 LTS dies, they will loose this punter and potentially many more.  It'll be sad because I'll probably trip back to Windows, as no other  Linux distro in my opinion can compare with this one.

There's other customizable options besides returning to Windows. For example, have you tried [Xubuntu]("http://www.xubuntu.org/") or [Kubuntu]("http://www.kubuntu.org/"). The concept that returning to windows as the only other option (with all the available distros), seems extreme. I've been Ubuntu only since 7.04 and returning to windows would be my last choice.

I find it odd that when certain users gets frustrated, their immediate response is "I'm going to return to Windows". Historically, I can't count how many times I've read that here in the T&E after earlier releases. I always assumed they never really adopted Linux and at the first sign of frustration would immediately think of windows.

Let's keep in mind, that 10.04 is supported until 2013. Which leaves quite a while before any decision needs to be made. During which time one could easily investigate alternative options. However, if windows is your alternative OS of choice, then I wish you the best. As for re-thinking Ubuntu, you may wish to read [this]("http://www.markshuttleworth.com/"). I believe it confirms MartyBuntu response.

Good Luck

### Post by MartyBuntu on 2011-11-15
> **BigSilly said:**
> When Windows 7 passes, the desktop will have been killed off altogether.

No, it's that the desktop will be the *niche market* and the mobile platforms will be the main venue, in a reversal of historic roles.

There will be fewer, but concentrated offerings as far as desktop environments are concerned aimed towards "power workstation users".

More and more number-crunching/processing/rendering/storage is being done in the "cloud"...and less and less in front of your own personal workstation.

The cynic in me would say that this is a byproduct of the *computer-as-entertainment-kiosk* reality, with more and more people using devices to look at funny YouTube videos...giggle on Facebook...tweet random daily occurrences...etc...

### Post by rjs987 on 2011-11-15
> **Baldrick_NZ said:**
> The whole look and feel on a large screen is too big. And it seems clunky in terms of finding the stuff you really need.  

So back to LTS 10.04 I go, consciously aware that this might be the last Ubuntu distro I use, unless the good folk at Canonical have a change a of heart regarding the design. 

The one thing I have always been selling people on regarding Ubuntu is the way it can be completely customisable. Well, I clearly can't say that anymore. They might as well stick with Vista/7, as that's just as customisable now!


when I first installed 11.10 at work (since that was the current version JUST out when I needed it) I was thinking the same about the Launcher bar being way too big for a big screen. Then I found and installed CCSM and was able to make the launcher a reasonable size for the big screen. It was still strange to have that bar over there on the side for a while but now I am getting to like it as there are only a few programs I regularly use that I have on the bar now. I even implemented Unity with the adjustments from CCSM on my Ubuntu 11.04 at home. At work I have decided I like having the launcher on the side, the Ubuntu bar across the top, and my Windows 7 (mandatory at work, running in a vm in seamless mode) task bar across the bottom with plenty of screen space for working in programs. CCSM does provide much of the missing customization that I think should be standard in the install and I think it will be eventually. I also think Canonical should restore customization to the top bar.

As far as customization being a selling point... I agree to a point. It is very nice to have a well thought out standard setup when initially installed and then lots of options if desired. But there are a great many other reasons I mention to people why they should switch to Ubuntu before I even get into customization. Like all the stuff you really need to go to work on your computer is in the standard installation. Like you can install this OS on both new and old hardware and expect great performance over any Windows (and I've supported all versions of Windows over the years starting with version 1.0) with less hard disk space and less RAM. Like the fact that all this is free with free upgrades to new versions including the programs you use. Like how you can get support for any issue for free and WAY faster than any micro$oft support has ever been supplied in my experience (and I have to pay big buck$ to get the ms support). Like you get a complete office suite included at no extra charge that is compatible with the M$ Office suite that you have to pay extra big buck$ for. There is more but I really don't like to type long posts and this one is getting past my preferred limit.

I can overlook the initial growing pains of this new thing call Unity for now. As it is, this current implementation in 11.10 is way better than what I have at home in 11.04 already. Like I said, I am getting used to the launcher now. Even though it does remind me a lot of Apple's bar across the bottom of their desktops.

### Post by dsfitzpat on 2011-11-16
The issue is that Gnome Shell does not work properly with the fglrx proprietary driver for ATI/AMD cards. Things work fine with the open source driver, which is great unless you actually want to use some program with modest graphics requirements. (For example, try even playing a game like Super Tux Kart without fglrx. It's nowhere near as good.)

### Post by thatguruguy on 2011-11-16
> **JCMB1 said:**
> 
I also use get_iplayer and ffmpeg, both of which do no load in 11.10.


That's really weird, although I don't use iplayer, I do use ffmpeg for transcoding on a daily basis.  ffmpeg has itself changed; if you have applications which rely on the way that ffmpeg used to work (for instance, winff), you need to remove any old settings you have stored locally in your home folder when you upgrade so that those applications don't generate command-line arguments that are no longer supported by ffmpeg.

EDIT: I recognize that the person I responded to stated that he did a fresh install. However, what I wrote does apply to anyone who upgraded their systems (either by doing an upgrade through the update manager, or a fresh install over their root directory while leaving their home directory intact).  Also, if you are trying to use ffmpeg directly from the command-line with the old command-line arguments (e.g., using "croptop" and "cropbottom" to crop a video), the transcode will fail.  It's worthwhile to read up on the current command-line structure of ffmpeg, which is available [here]("http://ffmpeg.org/ffmpeg.html").

> **Copper Bezel said:**
> Depends on the job, then. I agree about Gnome Shell. I have it running on my desktop, and I switch between Unity 2D and Shell on my netbook. Frankly, I have found both of the DEs enormously productivity-enhancing compared to Gnome 2.

On any given day, this is what I am doing:

1. writing a presentation.
2. writing board meeting minutes.
3. interfacing with an EMR to enter patient notes, orders, check labs, etc.
4. Communicating via IM with my staff
5. Email with lotsofpeople
6. Skype with patients/friends
7. Fussing about with some damn part of the system on the terminal.
8. SSH into the practice server
9. Access both local and on-line reference sources
10. Transfer newspapers in and out of e-book reader.
11. Massage data from computer-based imaging system
12. Screw around and kill time on ubuntuforums :D



I'd say I use the wheels off the damn thing, and at any given time, I have multiple tasks going on simultaneously.Both Unity and Shell work brilliantly for me.

### Post by Dry Lips on 2011-11-16
> **Copper Bezel said:**
> Actually, not true. Unity isn't yet ready for tablets. It depends a lot on the mouse's hover position, which isn't present in a tablet interface. While future releases should make a fairly smooth transition from desktop to tablet, it's basically unusable on a tablet as-is without some serious tweaking. It's very much designed for the desktop, and specifically netbooks, since it grew up from the interface of Ubuntu Netbook Remix.
[...]
Outside of slates, netbooks are slightly over-represented, but I don't think Ubuntu installs really favor any one form factor.

This is what I wrote on April 30th, just after trying Unity for the very first time:
[I]
"I suspect that the **real** purpose of Unity is to attempt to create a OS 
specifically tailored for laptops/netbooks/tablet computers. The success 
of android must have convinced some heads at Canonical that they have
the opportunity of taking marked shares from MS Windows in this marked."[/I]

I still believe the point behind Unity has been to create an all in one interface for netbooks,
tablets, phones and desktop computers. I understand the logic behind this, I see what they
are trying to pull off. Since there's so much heat about this issue right now, I won't go into
much detail about my opinions on Unity as a *desktop* DE... However, I believe there can be 
no doubt about the fact that the main design consideration behind Unity, has been to make 
something that works well on small screens. 

[Shuttleworth: Ubuntu is heading to phones and tablets ]("http://arstechnica.com/open-source/news/2011/10/shuttleworth-ubuntu-is-heading-to-phones-and-tablets.ars")

[I]"Ubuntu&#8217;s new Unity shell will play a key role in Canonical&#8217;s plans to  bring the Ubuntu user 
experience to smaller screens. The platform  already has preliminary tablet support, including 
experimental  functionality for touchscreen-based window management. It seems likely  that 
the Qt-based Unity 2D experience will serve as the mobile  implementation. The Qt Quick user 
interface design framework is  well-suited for building touch-friendly mobile experiences."[/I]

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-11-16
[QUOTE=Dry Lips]I still believe the point behind Unity has been to create an all in one interface for netbooks, tablets, phones and desktop computers. I understand the logic behind this, I see what they are trying to pull off. Since there's so much heat about this issue right now, I won't go into much detail about my opinions on Unity as a *desktop* DE... However, I believe there can be no doubt about the fact that the main design consideration behind Unity, has been to make something that works well on small screens. [/QUOTE]
Yeah, there's no doubt there. I'm just getting very tired of seeing it referred to as a *tablet* interface, which is just misinformed. The most pigeonholed you can make it is to say that it's a netbook interface (Shuttleworth has stated outright that the Unity project *is* UNR) that makes concessions up and down, respectively, to full notebooks and to slates. As you say, it's also working toward better functionality on tablets, but it's not yet usable on them.

Any new OS needs touch usability baked in. It's still an an open question how ubiquitous touch interfaces are going to be in the near future and desktop OSs have to hedge their bets. Simultaneously, although touch is currently absent on the desktop, tablets are getting all the buzz, and everyone wants to be just a little more like Android and iOS to catch in on that buzz instead of being left behind by it.

I don't think Unity's concessions to tablets are any greater than those that are required for any viable desktop OS, at least without taking Apple's original route (and now KDE's) of providing a wholly separate DE for touch devices. There's nothing special about anything Unity is doing in relation to tablets.

And in the little touches, it's just a very desktoppy interface, particularly if you've used Shell for contrast. In Unity, if I press the Super key, what I get is the equivalent to the Windows Start Menu. If I press the Super key in Shell, I get the equivalent to the Android Home screen. Unity revamped Compiz's Alt+Tab; Gnome broke Metacity's. OS functions create dialogue windows in Unity; they create transparent overlays in Shell. Unity uses icons to switch between applications with the mouse; Gnome's core application switcher is a labeled card view like those of Blackberry and WebOS. Unity has a dock with a fixed size that can be scrolled, with Windows 7-style quicklists and keyboard shortcuts, and a handful of extra widgets worked in, which is a *panel*; Gnome has a simple mouse-only favorites list that rescales for the number of items, having much more in common with the call/contacts/web/whatever tray in Android.

Unity makes Gnome *less* tablety. *Substantially* less. The only really uniquely tablety thing that it does is the way maximized windows are handled - and that's a screen space concern, not a touch interaction one, and one that came from use on netbooks.

Edit: Two points of clarification. Why is the difference between dialogue windows and overlays significant? Because the former is a window drawn by an application under the traditional desktop metaphor. The latter is a baked-in function of the window manager that treats the desktop space as the *content area* of a unified interface for the *device*. And isn't Unity more tablety than Gnome 2? Yes, but it was being designed in response to Gnome Shell since its implementation in 10.10, when it was rebranded as Unity in the first place. Unity as it currently exists has always been a *reaction* to the push toward tablet-like interfaces, not a push of its own.

---

### Post by Baldrick_NZ on 2011-11-17
I guess I would have preferred Canonical started a 'mobile' fork, if that's the direction they want to head in.

The biggest bug-bear for me is that I have Ubuntu the way I like it. I've put some considerable time and effort into getting it just right for me, and now because a decision has been made outside of my control, I need to shift.

It wouldn't be so bad if they dealt with the unconfigurable dock on the left hand side. I'm sorry, but not everyone wants 'that' style of dock, if even one at all. Which leads me to one of the most enduring and endearing features of Ubuntu - total custom-ability. No more total. Then there's half the useful stuff that's been in previous releases that aren't in 11.10. No Synaptic out of box. What's that all about?

I too have been a long(ish) user (since 7.10) and have dabbled with other distros such as Mint, Opensuse and Mandriva as well. At one point I ditched Windows altogether and rode solo with Ubuntu. So my dedication to Linux should not be in question.

I've also grown accustomed to Macbuntu as my preferred theme, but sadly it just doesn't work on anything that isn't Ubuntu, including Mint and Studio. It does work under Unity - but just looks stupid with that unmovable monstrosity on the left of the screen. Don't start me on the next to useless mac4lin...

I recently tried the new opensuse 12.1, and like 11.10, it made a very quick departure from my puter. Too much of a learning curve from what I've grown accustomed to.

Ofcourse, I could try xfce, Ubuntu's poor cousin. But what guarantees does one have that it'll look and feel the same way as I have it now? None.

It will be a really sad day come 2013 when 10.04 is no longer supported, if some major development changes aren't made to future releases, and I find myself using Windows 7 or 8.

### Post by mpiter on 2011-11-17

It is worst than that.  The poll was obviously favoring Unity (that DOES NOT mean that it was done on purpose, it is just a fact).  The question was "Ubuntu 11.10: Which desktop environment are you using?".  That excludes people staying in 10.04 or 10.10 because they do not want Unity and the 11.04 crash, people that do not want to upgrade from 11.04 to 11.10 because they want to keep a true Gnome 2.32 (Classic mode), and people who have migrated from Ubuntu to a Ubuntu cousin such as Mint to avoid Unity.

If you want a clearer picture, the question should be rephrased to include all present Ubuntu laptop and desktop editions.  If you want an even clearer picture, you could include people using a Ubuntu cousin.  In other words, Unity gather certainly less than what the poll suggests because the poll was only addressed to the Linux community that was the most likely to use Unity.

### Post by thatguruguy on 2011-11-17
> **BobW said:**
> Strawman - no one has claimed that Unity doesn't work for some people. That would be just a wrong as claiming that Unity is perfect for everyone.

The fact that some people love Unity doesn't make the problems other  people have any less valid. Different people need the computer to do  different things.

Maybe you missed the point I was making.  I'll simplify it.  The majority of the anti-Unity rants do, indeed, state that Unity destroys productivity, with the strong implication that such a destruction of productivity is universal.  It is rare for the ranter to preface his or her comments with "in my personal experience" or "in my opinion".  Rather, they are making direct assertions of what they perceive as flaws in the interface (often, with the simple statement that "Unity is the worst DE ever", or some equivalent, without making any qualification for personal experience or opinion), which implies that their stated viewpoint is a statement of fact, rather than opinion.

My statement was made in the way it was to point out that such statements are somewhat over-reaching.  It requires those who have made such statements to acknowledge that their opinions and experiences may not be universal.  I hope that clears things up for you.

### Post by BobW on 2011-11-17
> **thatguruguy said:**
> Secondly, the point I made was not that Mythbuntu is lacking applications that I need.  I'm perfectly capable of installing any packages that I need that didn't come with the default installation. The statement I was making was in reference to the ***desktop environment***, which is a standard xfce4 shell, just like Xubuntu.

Again, I'll simplify my statement so you understand it.  I use Unity on two different computers and xfce4 (which has a menu structure very similar to Gnome 2) on another.  I use all three computers on a daily basis.  Now that I'm used to Unity, I personally find it to be a more efficient DE than xfce4. I hope that clarifies my point.  I'd be happy to elucidate further, if necessary.

The point I was making was just that it's unfair to criticize for Mythbuntu for being simple when that was what it's supposed to be. It needs to run on low end low power machines. It's like complaining that a compact car can't carry as much as a SUV.

Comparing against something that's intended to be a full featured desktop like Kubuntu or Mint is more of an even comparison.

---

### Post by thatguruguy on 2011-11-17
> **BobW said:**
> The point I was making was just that it's unfair to criticize for Mythbuntu for being simple when that was what it's supposed to be. It needs to run on low end low power machines. It's like complaining that a compact car can't carry as much as a SUV.

Comparing against something that's intended to be a full featured desktop like Kubuntu or Mint is more of an even comparison.

I'll simplify further. I'll be as explicit as possible, in order to try to avoid further confusion.


[LIST]
[*]Mythbuntu uses a fairly standard default xfce4 desktop. The primary difference between Mythbuntu and a standard Xubuntu installation is that there are less applications shipped with Mythbuntu (e.g., there is no word processor included, nor even a default text editor). The desktop itself (i.e., the use of text-based menus in a gnome 2-like panel) is standard xfce4.
[*]The standard xfce4 desktop is very much like the standard gnome 2 desktop.
[*]After using both Unity and the xfce4 desktop on a daily basis, I find that Unity is (for me, at least) easier and more productivity enhancing than xfce4.
[*]I wasn't referring to KDE4 in my statement, because that's not the relevant comparison. KDE4 is nothing like gnome 2.
[*]I wasn't referring to Linux Mint in my statement, because Linux Mint is heavily modified.
[*]People complain about the loss of gnome 2, because it was so much more productivity-enhancing than Unity (or Gnome Shell, for that matter).
[*]In my personal experience, Unity is more productivity-enhancing than a desktop that mirrors the default gnome 2 experience.
[/LIST]

lot's of people are using Unity, i think that's good, the more feedback, the better it gets or at least that's what i like to think.

Perhaps and in my opinion, those of us that are half way to like Unity is because we are used to work in a customizable environment, something that Unity is not having, and because is not as intuitive as one might wish, but i have to say that doesn't take you too long to figure out things, the system tray is important and i think it has to still exist in further versions, it also will save some icons for the side bar which sometimes proves to be a tall tower and in general, is not ready yet to be claimed as a solid Desktop for the daily tasks. 

My mayor concern about Unity is not it's looks or it's arrangement, but it's work flow, even with a good videocard and ram, applications which never would have problems and that indeed don't have in other desktops, present lags, drop of performance and in some cases stall, this regardless their current development status. i'm looking forward to future releases anyway.

### Post by cariboo on 2011-11-17
The poll at omgubuntu is interesting, but again, only people that are interested in the issue voted. I've seen many other polls, on other sites that show that the majority of 11.10 users are using Unity and happy with it.

What I would find interesting, is a poll of desktop environment usage, before the advent of Unity/Gnome-shell, I'm sure we'd find that there would be one desktop environment the the majority of users use, but there would be a large number that didn't use what was installed by default.

Now on to another topic, what I really find disturbing, is the small but vocal number of users that jump to another distribution, without even investigating the other desktop environment choices available in the repositories. There is currently a member exploring what can be done with the gnome classic interface (gnome-session-fallback) in the Precise testing & discussion sub-forum. He is finding that most of what was available using the old 2 panel interface is still available in gnome classic, and if as I suspect, that it will be available through the life of the LTS version, this gives users plenty of time to use it a a base DE, while the other newer environments have time to mature and become better.

---

### Post by fabricator4 on 2011-11-17
> **thatguruguy said:**
> Yes. Nothing is more misleading than asking the question "Ubuntu 11.10: Which Desktop Environment are you using?", and then providing all of the major DEs available on 11.10 as choices.

I mean, come on!  That question is so ambiguous!

It's ambiguous, because if you're using it as a measure of acceptance (or rejection) of Unity then it completely ignores several groups: Those that have not upgraded and are still using LTS or 10.10 or (shudder) 11.04; those that have changed distros to something else completely; and those that would prefer to not use Unity and either can't be bothered or do not have time to mess around with their computers.

If you ask me that question for example, I'd have to say "Gnome Shell", because the 11.10 machine I maintain for testing is a completely unmodified Ubuntu install.  I even upgraded the video card so I could test Gnome 3.  The fact is though, it's not my main machine, and it's not the one I want to do any serious work on (though I do try).

I've seen no recent polls that attempt to get user's thoughts on Unity itself.  Early polls all seemed to show a fairly clear 25-50-25 split and I've seen nothing that makes me think the figures are any different now.  If you've got anything to contrary I'd be very interested to see it (but the poll referenced above is not it)

Chris

### Post by BigSilly on 2011-11-17
Seeing as this is the Unity Mega Thread, I just thought I'd ask - will we be likely to see a couple of extra themes in the next release? We currently have both Ambiance and Radiance, and while they are both very lovely indeed and uniquely *Ubuntu*, I'd like to see some more options (unless of course, like a total numpty, there are some already available somewhere and I've missed them). Also, I've read somewhere (think it was OMG!Ubuntu) that the Unity dash will be losing the four Home buttons (Media Apps, Internet Apps, More Apps and Find Files) and will offer the main applications overview with the filters on the first click of the dash button. Is this true? I think this would be a great idea.

But otherwise I currently have to say that Unity is going great guns for me. The main issue for me is having to choose between it and Gnome 3/Shell!

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-11-17
[QUOTE=fabricator4]Those that have not upgraded and are still using LTS or 10.10 or (shudder) 11.04;[/QUOTE]

11.04 was quite stable by about halfway through its cycle. It never gave me any hassles. Give it a couple of months, and I imagine that 11.10 will be, too. There's no way to get bug-fixing feedback before the system has a user base. But the real point is, 11.04 is a very stable system now and doesn't require any shuddering.

And there are very valid reasons to use the LTS other than preferring the Gnome 2 interface - like the above stability issue. Folks using 10.04 don't have the option of using Unity.

No poll could possibly cover all the angles you're asking for. Any extra axis you want to add, there's another objection and another axis implied by that, and so on, recursively. The simplest way to get a good idea of which UI is getting preference is to ask the users who have access to all of them.

### Post by Copper Bezel on 2011-11-17
[QUOTE=cariboo907]I can understand why the question was ignored, but my feeling is that if someone is interested enough to make the effort to install Ubuntu themselves, they may also have enough interest to explore things further, if there aren't happy with the default desktop.[/QUOTE]

Yeah, but a user coming from that position is going to do his or her research and decide on a DE that way. Otherwise, Unity is a fine catch-all starting point, and once the user gets some idea of how Ubuntu and Linux work, he or she can experiment with other options with a few clicks in the Software Center. I just don't think most users are going to benefit from having the choices up front.

If a user is comfortable with the default software, there's no issue. If a user wants options, they're easy to find.

Of course, the same argument applies to Unity's "Available to Install" bit in the Dash, but that's another issue. = D

Edit:

I don't know what to say about the folks switching to Mint or anything else because they think it'll provide more options, but frankly, those are in most cases advanced / invested users who feel slighted by choices that Ubuntu has made. A new user isn't going to follow that track. Worst case scenario, you have new users who haven't been exposed to any of this stuff and "try Linux" but don't like it, and go back to Windows, because "Linux" has a "dock."

### Post by hzFVOW00pw on 2011-11-18

What are you talkin about? Look at this:

"LTS releases are now supported for a period of 5 years, which caters more to the needs of Corporate Customers."

Which, on a side note, makes the option to stay with Ubuntu more of a valid option.

Because now, if you want to strip off Unity and install and configure another DE, and write some scripts for bug-workarounds (yes, bug-workarounds: I have a postinstallscript I use on every new install of LTS Lucid, because there are quite a few long standing bugs that never get fixed (plymouth, ureadahead) EVEN IN AN LTS VERSION and despite the claim of LTS stability), now all your tinkering will be worth it for 5 years to come.

But then.. on the other hand... you might just as well install Debian Stable, because its faster and with less commercial frills like music stores and all that jive...

### Post by ankspo71 on 2011-11-18
> **maxibuntu said:**
> There is something called popularity-contest included in the distro isn't there? Where can we view that data?

On my Xubuntu 11.10 it is installed by default, but not turned on by default. 

Data collected can be viewed here: [http://popcon.ubuntu.com/](http://popcon.ubuntu.com/)

It can be turned on and off in the last tab of the Software Sources application (there should be an option in Software Center for this too). 


From the description of popularity-contest:

>  The popularity-contest package sets up a cron job that will
 periodically anonymously submit to the Ubuntu developers
 statistics about the most used Ubuntu packages on this system.
 .
 This information helps us making decisions such as which packages
 should go on the first CD. It also lets us improve future versions
 of Ubuntu so that the most popular packages are the ones which
 are installed automatically for new users.

they matter

### Post by Roasted on 2011-11-18
> **MartyBuntu said:**
> 
In Canonical's estimation, the "desktop is dead".


That doesn't sound accurate... That sounds like Apple, not Canonical. Apple has said before that the mobile/tablet market is *the* future, which I think is a little bold to suggest seeing as though I believe there will always be a need for the desktop market. The tablet market, while it has its place, isn't a one stop shop, nor will it ever be. Canonical on the other hand is trying to broaden their spectrum as much as possible so they can support everything, from tablets to phones to laptops to... wait for it... desktops. :guitar:

### Post by snowpine on 2011-11-18
> **Garthhh said:**
> what am I talking about?
1st impressions
they matter
there are 500million or so XP users who will be moving to another OS over the next few years, how many of these users will prefer to keep their old hardware or will be put off by W8's version of one size fits all [Unity]?

The thing about Open Source Software is that it's driven by talented and innovative developers working on the projects they are excited about. Creative people with years of interface design experience don't typically wake up in the morning and say "You know what I feel like working on today? I want to make a carbon copy of the interface Microsoft came up with 10 years ago and then abandoned, yeah, that will be fun!!" Rather, they are probably thinking "I have this interesting new idea nobody's tried before; I think I'll run it up the flagpole and see if anyone salutes it." 

I agree with you it will be interesting to see what happens when XP goes "end of life" but I think you are projecting your own goals onto the Ubuntu project. Nowhere on the Ubuntu homepage do I see "our mission: to be exactly like Windows XP." If you want to succeed in business then you have to believe your product is **better** than the competition and offers some feature that makes it stand out from the crowd. For Canonical in 2011, this "competitive edge" is Unity.

### Post by winjeel on 2011-11-18
Why was the classic desktop removed? I hate taking a long time to find things. I want things to work smoothly and without extra effort. Perhaps teenagers who like to tinker with what's under the bonnet and may like this new Unity, however, I don't have the time to mess around. I have work to do, and it includes using MS Word, which under Unity is a pain to use the search function to start up Word (please don't tell me to dump MS Word, it works so much better than Open Office and it's clones). Anyway, I've installed an icon that lets me have all the menu items I need (see here[ http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=11469760&posted=1#post11469760]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=11469760&posted=1#post11469760")). If I can't easily access menu items (including Wine) in the next Ubuntu I won't be updating or I'll be changing to a different brand of linux. For me and for the sake of efficiency, Unity really is a deal breaker.

---

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2011-11-18
> **winjeel said:**
> Why was the classic desktop removed?

Its developers are no longer maintaining it. Rather than put time and effort into maintaining a desktop that most people used to complain about (confusing notification area icons, disappearing panel applets, dated look, useless search etc), Ubuntu developed a new desktop shell on top of Gnome, that saves space and will be able to be adapted for touchscreen devices  and embedded devices (smart TVs, etc) in the near future.

> I hate taking a long time to find things. I want things to work smoothly and without extra effort. Perhaps teenagers who like to tinker with what's under the bonnet and may like this new Unity, however, I don't have the time to mess around. I have work to do, and it includes using MS Word, which under Unity is a pain to use the search function to start up Word

My Wine programs (not that I still have any, but the shortcuts remain in my home folder) appear in the Unity dash. Merely dragging them from there into the Launcher should do the trick, or typing to search would also work.

> The thing about Open Source Software is that it's driven by talented and innovative developers working on the projects they are excited about. Creative people with years of interface design experience don't typically wake up in the morning and say "You know what I feel like working on today? I want to make a carbon copy of the interface Microsoft came up with 10 years ago and then abandoned, yeah, that will be fun!!" Rather, they are probably thinking "I have this interesting new idea nobody's tried before; I think I'll run it up the flagpole and see if anyone salutes it." 

I agree with you it will be interesting to see what happens when XP goes "end of life" but I think you are projecting your own goals onto the Ubuntu project. Nowhere on the Ubuntu homepage do I see "our mission: to be exactly like Windows XP." If you want to succeed in business then you have to believe your product is **better** than the competition and offers some feature that makes it stand out from the crowd. For Canonical in 2011, this "competitive edge" is Unity.

I'm talking about the marketing & how to get a large number of new users in the door [low hanging fruit]
I didn't say abandon development or in any way alter the ultimate direction of the development

there is certainly a market for the latest & greatest, which is well served to the point of saturation

which distro is is geared towards  ease of installation [rules out Debian] & use?
lubuntu maybe, which is nowhere to be found on the home page

within the buntu family there are many different ways to go
pity the ubuntu beta [newest release] is exclusively featured

it is a common mistake for an organization to be stuck in what they think they do, preventing them from reaching their full potential

### Post by hzFVOW00pw on 2011-11-19

### Post by frank4360 on 2011-11-19
> **irv said:**
> This just show you that it is only a handful of users out here that are doing all the complaining about Unity. There are things I would like to see changed or added, but time will fix that.
The problem is a small number of users are too inpatient for Unity to develop. I feel it made some nice inroads from 04 to 10. I am waiting for 12.04. 

No doubt that Ubuntu developers can make any changes they want - but equally users can vote with their feet and move to another system.

It may be true that "only a handful of users" do all the complaining about Unity; most people cannot be bothered registering any opinion and only the numbers of lost users will matter in the end.  

One major problem with Ubuntu has been the 6 monthly schedule of new releases. This causes unnecessary problems for users who want to use the computer as a tool for their work, rather than something interesting to play with. For instance my wife hates the continual switching of packages (eg OpenOffice to Libre), whereas I find it interesting to see developments.

The Unity question is simply an extension of this desire for change for changes sake. It is no great advertisement for a major operating system to say ""time will fix" it, or to be waiting for the next release!

### Post by hzFVOW00pw on 2011-11-19
> **Garthhh said:**
> I used mint for a while, I didn't find the community to be very newbie friendly. I never did get GRSYNC to work across my home network & got told I was dumb for trying to figure it out.

That could be true about the Mint community. I installed Mint 11 on a laptop for a 12 year old kid. But I discovered that the software center kept asking for a password on every single package install (installed a lot of games for the kid). It would have been easier if the pw was kept in memory for 10 minutes or so. Looking for a solution I came across a thread in the Mint forums where lots of users had the same issue but no easy solution was given. I know how to fix this in sudoers, but the replies at the forum insisted that people just should not want to have the pw in memory for 10 minutes because it was a security breach or something. It made me scratch the back of my head.

> My comments are about what to recommend for other user which, just want something that works & they can understand easily

Well I have that same problem. I maintain several boxes for (rather computer illiterate) friends and keep some configuration and maintenance scripts synced with ssh/rsync over WAN. That means I cannot install another Ubuntu version every half year and I use the LTS versions only. But Unity is giving me a headache, because I don't think they are going to dig it and I don't want to lose them to Windows 7. So I'm seriously contemplating an alternative, and a rolling distro could be ideal, provided its stable and easy enough to use.

### Post by stalkingwolf on 2011-11-20
from a simple utilitarian view there are things that unity doesnt have or maybe as i have had limited success using it i havent found.

1. Ubuntu has redefined "never"  setting the monitor to go to sleep never results in it going to sleep after about 20 minutes.  even if there is activity , like a download say.  My solution the power inhibit applet.
not available in unity. nor are force quit, and the system monitor.

2. multiple desk top.  i use this a lot.  with out it just as well run windows.

3. top and bottom panels.  I use them for different things.  Bottom tells me
what i have open on that desktop.  Top gives me the things i want across all desktops.

In the words of My TI many many years ago.  IF IT WORKS DONT FIX IT.

### Post by Garthhh on 2011-11-20
> **philinux said:**
> [http://arstechnica.com/open-source/news/2011/09/ubuntu-technical-board-member-proposes-monthly-ubuntu-release-cycle.ars](http://arstechnica.com/open-source/news/2011/09/ubuntu-technical-board-member-proposes-monthly-ubuntu-release-cycle.ars)
You never know. It might or might not happen.

Here is my biggest take away from the article:

**Remnant addresses some similar issues in his blog entry. He points out that Ubuntu's culture of targeting individual features to specific releases works against the best practices of successful time-based release management. The problem is exacerbated, he explains, by Canonical's tendency to tie employee pay and bonuses to whether they deliver assigned functionality in time for feature freeze.**

explains the sorry state of unity
the product is going out the door whether or not it works

Unity seems to be a tantrum directed towards Gnome, for not sharing the same sensibility/sense of urgency

### Post by nothingspecial on 2011-11-20
From 2 different places.

There is no link because they are now here. To go over this again. 


This is not directed at Garthhh, rather a general point so everyone knows why stuff is being merged here.

First No one is censoring anyone, say what you like about Unity within the CoC, that is fine. The problem we have at the moment is that there are too many threads going round and round in circles flogging the same old dead horse. Yes some users don't like Unity (or Gnome Shell and Gnome 2 being depreciated). But why have the same conversation in 7 different sections of the forums in a bazillion different threads?

Go round and round in circles here, in the Unity mega thread. Then the staff can get on dealing with spam, profanity, trolling etc etc and generally making these forums a nice place to visit if you need help with Ubuntu or one of it's official derivatives, rather than policing all these threads that say the same thing over and over again. 


Gnome2 is deapreciated.

If you are unhappy about this please contact Gnome

[http://www.gnome.org/contact/](http://www.gnome.org/contact/)


If you are unhappy with the Unity interface please understand that no-one is forcing anyone to use it. Unity is merely another option shipped by default with Ubuntu
Feel free to try one of these alternative distributions.

Kubuntu - [http://www.kubuntu.org/](http://www.kubuntu.org/)

Xubuntu - [http://www.xubuntu.org/](http://www.xubuntu.org/)

Lubuntu - [http://lubuntu.net/](http://lubuntu.net/)

Edubuntu- [http://www.edubuntu.org/](http://www.edubuntu.org/)

or you may try one of the many other desktop environments that Ubuntu offers in their repositories including but not limited to

Gnome-shell - [http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell](http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell)

Openbox - [http://openbox.org/wiki/Openbox:Screenshots](http://openbox.org/wiki/Openbox:Screenshots)

Fluxbox - [http://fluxbox.org/screenshots/](http://fluxbox.org/screenshots/)

Enlightenment - [http://enlightenment.org/](http://enlightenment.org/)

If you have a question or need support with Unity (or any other desktop environment), please start a thread in the appropriate section. 
If you believe you have found a bug , please report it through launchpad.

[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/ReportingBugs](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/ReportingBugs)

If you wish to debate Unity please use the Unity mega-thread, we do not need any more threads debating Unity.

Ubuntu Forums is not the place to complain about Unity. Ubuntu developers do not read these forums nor should they be expected to.
If you would like to complain do it through launchpad or in a blog, these forums are for support.

### Post by Black_Sector on 2011-11-21
Do you really believe that everyone with something negative to say about designs flaws should just leave Ubuntu in Exodus towards other systems, or is it possible that criticism can sometimes be constructive? 

Even if people dont tell you how to improve something, just know WHAT about their design turns most people off is useful. Some companies PAY people to test their products and give feedback, but here you are given a large group of testers for FREE, who voluntarily give you bug fixes and improve your product....Its a two way street. The user base is not JUST a bunch of ungrateful leeches, but they create the community who indirectly funds this entire project. The devs should probably care what the users think because they are likely NOT doing this as a personal hobby just for their own amusement, but as a corporate money making venture where user satisfaction matters. Maybe its not huge profits compared to Mac/Windows/Android, but user opinions matter.


Its possible that Unity can turn into something that people like, but that wont happen if you just ban everyone who is upset by the latest changes.

---

### Post by mörgæs on 2011-11-21
Testing is always welcome. Launchpad is made for people to participate. 


Unity is a unique desktop environment, and a lot of users are happy with it. I don't like it, but I respect the users who do. 

Since there are lots of classic-looking distros to choose from but there is only one Unity (no pun intended), it would be a disadvantage to Linux in general to 'complain to Canonical' and try to roll Unity back, if that is the purpose. If the purpose is to strengthen and improve Unity in order to have an offer to the smartphone/tablet-inspired users, I support the idea.
