### Post by satanselbow on 2011-11-21
This is all so last April...


I have previously at times been quite vocal about my dislike of Unity - I have also, likewise, been quite open that Ubuntu is not my first choice distro.

Canonical have made it quite clear in recent interviews and press releases that they (read Mark Shuttleworth) have sunk way too many dev hours, and therefore benefactors cash, into Unity to pull out now.

Some people do like Unity - but only in the same way that some people do like Gnome3/Shell, some people like LXDE yada-yada - the list goes on.

Ask yourself this:

Is Canonical likely to drop Unity? No
Is Canonical likely to ship Ubuntu with an alternate default DE in the near future? No
Are there alternate DE readily available in the repo? Yes
Does Ubuntu actively prevent you using any of the alternate DE'? No

The best thing to come out of the last 2 Ubu releases has been the increased activity and development of DE/WM alternatives and arrival of new kids on the scene - which can only be good for Linux generally.

I personally think that Ubuntu should have shipped with Gnome2 as default for a couple more releases - with a developing Unity in the repos - but this was not technically possible. Canonical did too little too late with Unity. The release of Gnome3 was well publicised in advance - or more to the point the end of development of Gnome2 was well publicised and Unity was obviously underdeveloped at release in 11.04.

Is it here to stay? Most definitely! Canonical obviously see it as a valuable revenue stream (although it has yet to appear on any other distros or devices)

If you really cannot find an alternate desktop to you liking in the repos then maybe you should move on - problem being that you will find the same selection of DE in every other distro as well.

There is the MATE project that is attempting to keep Gnome2 alive but how long that will be sustainable remains to be seen.

If you have considered, intelligent design ideas to contribute to the Unity DE then go right ahead - if you want to hijack an official channel to whinge and whine... do us all a flavour and don't bother.

You think Unity stirred it up for Ubuntu? Wait for MS to unleash W8 on the flock... unfortunately the one most likely to benefit is Mac :(

---

### Post by LowSky on 2011-11-21
> **mörgæs said:**
> Rather than complaining about a distro which you dislike you should find one that suits your needs.

Ubuntu has a lot of things people do like, like easy setup, a simple to use installer, large active support forum, huge repository of applications, the list goes on. Hating one portion of the experience shouldn't force someone to leave. Maybe just suggest a another desktop environment. People are allowed to disagree and as a member of the forum staff you should have a more neutral stance on people's dislikes.

> **Docaltmed said:**
> This may absolutely fry your brain, but Unity already *is* something that people like.

Some may like it, but people are more likely to complain than praise. The forums has moved most of the complaints to the deep dark corners, because the company doesn't like the poor attention. I'm not saying everyone hates it, but the backlash is pretty well documented.

I'm one of those people who did move on. It was mostly because I got tired of the sweeping changes. It wasn't Unity. It was the lack of criticism allowed from the community. Every new change brought a new round of "tough". Every complaint is met with responses like, "If you don't like it become a developer or leave." That is basically telling a person we don't care about you. It's also the stance a company should never make to a customer if they pay for a product or not.

Be nice to people. Just think of it this way. Many people have used Ubuntu for years. They sang it glories to others. Then comes along something they don't like. These people have been dedicated users who are now not sure what to do. So they disagree, that shouldn't be enough to push them off the boat. Everything should be met with criticism, and it should be welcomed.

### Post by BobSongs on 2011-11-21
> **mörgæs said:**
> I will happily change my statement to 

Rather than complaining about a desktop environment which you dislike you should find one that suits your needs.
Mkay, that sounds like a reasonable suggestion. So, what's on the table before me?

[LIST]
[*] A freshly installed copy of Ubuntu 11.10 (like many others my experience went from 10.10 to 11.10).
[*] A side-by-side install of the GNOME3 desktop.
[*] A DVD of the latest release of Mint Linux, Debian's grandchild through Ubuntu.
[/LIST]
 [SIZE=3][COLOR=Plum]**what am I looking for?**[/COLOR][/SIZE]
Hmm. Let's start with what did I disliked most about Vista: Microsoft's desire to create a different environment because they **could**, not because it was **good**. Vista brought my attention back to the O/S instead of to my apps. Rather than being a familiar environment that's fine-tuned, fast and faultless ... it resulted in frustration.

[LIST]
[*]Is the "Start" button a fantastic and perfect idea? No.
[*]Is the "Start" button a familiar idea? I've used it since 1995, so... yes.
[/LIST]
The similarity between my Windows experience and Linux raises the following question:

[LIST]
[*]Are Unity/GNOME3 similar enough to my now-familiar GNOME2 experience to make the transition smooth and enjoyable?
[/LIST]
**No.** Because I now know what I am looking for: familiarity.

**[SIZE=3][COLOR=Plum]is it just me? am i being stubborn?[/COLOR][/SIZE]**
**distrowatch.com** reports that interest in Mint over the last **[12 months]("http://distrowatch.com/index.php?dataspan=52")** has displaced Ubuntu's #1 position proudly held since 2005 (threatened only once by PCLinuxOS in 2007).

While distrowatch may not be considered a perfect barometer or litmus test for people's opinions it is noteworthy that that we vote with our feet... or in this case, mouse clicks.

Now that Unity has had a fair trial on this desktop, its removal to test Mint 12 for the same duration awaits 12's official release.

[SIZE=3][COLOR=Plum]**final note**[/COLOR][/SIZE]
The challenge put forth here was "...you should probably find [a desktop environment] that suits your needs..." My need is for familiarity. When I'm in a rush and need to find something quickly, a shiny new and unfamiliar environment does not "suit" my "needs". And it's good to see that I do not stand alone in this matter.

---

### Post by snowpine on 2011-11-21
> **BobSongs said:**
> The challenge put forth here was "...you should probably find [a desktop environment] that suits your needs..." My need is for familiarity. When I'm in a rush and need to find something quickly, a shiny new and unfamiliar environment does not "suit" my "needs". And it's good to see that I do not stand alone in this matter.

I hear you completely, I need familiarity and stability on my work desktop, which is why I chose Scientific Linux (support for Gnome 2 through 2017 by the way, although that had nothing to do with my decision since I'm not really a big Gnome 2 fan).

Funny however to hear that you value familiarity above all else, yet you chose to go with the latest 11.10 release instead of 10.04 "long term support," a familiar, stable, comfortable option that will be supported through 2013/2015!

### Post by BobSongs on 2011-11-21
> **snowpine said:**
> I hear you completely, I need familiarity and stability on my work desktop, which is why I chose Scientific Linux (support for Gnome 2 through 2017 by the way, although that had nothing to do with my decision since I'm not really a big Gnome 2 fan).

Funny however to hear that you value familiarity above all else, yet you chose to go with the latest 11.10 release instead of 10.04 "long term support," a familiar, stable, comfortable option that will be supported through 2013/2015!
Excellent post and excellent point!

I do appreciate the "cutting edge" of Linux. I use Inkscape as a graphics tool and this tends to force my hand to the latest Ubuntu release. I also have some hardware in my PC that even 10.10 cannot properly handle. So stepping back to 10.04 (a much enjoyed release) is not in the cards.

I'm all for gtk3. Linux components should definitely move forward. But my suspicion is that the GNOME developers may not have put in as much time/money/effort into polling older GNOME users for their opinions (it took distrowatch to expose that). It smacks of MSFT's decision to put a ribbon into MS Office without the option to "flip back" to the previous style should an emergency arise and the ribbon fails to satisfy.

Hey: give me gtk5 with "Applications", "Places" and "System" on the top left hand side and I'll be happy.

Changing the user experience to "keep up with the pack" can sometimes be good. But it (and by "it", I mean GNOME3's new interface) doesn't accommodate

[LIST]
[*] MacOS users (their dock is on the bottom)
[*] Windows users (their task bar is on the bottom too)
[*] GNOME2 users (top and bottom)
[*] KDE users
[/LIST]
 I'm not upset with Ubuntu at all. In fact, my desire to test Mint is based primarily on it's lineage from Ubuntu. Is GNOME2 perfect? Not by a long shot. Is GNOME2 what I use most and can swing around in it like a kid on monkey bars? Ohhh yes!

I've never **ever** been upset with Ubuntu's UI change. All such frustration has always been pointed to the culprit: GNOME. And 11.10's attempt to find a middle ground between GNOME3 and GNOME2 is worthy of applause. But it's not similar enough to 2 for my taste and needs.

:)

---

### Post by MarjaE on 2011-11-21
> **mörgæs said:**
> I will happily change my statement to 

Rather than complaining about a desktop environment which you dislike you should find one that suits your needs.

Easier said than done.

I installed Xubuntu, but between the installer going bad, and the lack of touchpad support [I had a malfunctioning touchpad, and the computer was unusable until I could disable it for typing], and various other aggravations, I had to reinstall Ubuntu.

I understand that Gnome 2 was technically inelegant, but it had some touchpad support, and it was easy-to-use, relatively easy-to-customize [although I've still not figured out how to widen the scrollbars], and mostly free from visible-on-mouseover nonsense [after I disabled the new-style scrollbars]. In my view, that counts for an awful lot.
